blocks:
Ayahs

الٓمٓ (١)
Alif, Lam, Mim. (1)
ٱللَّهُ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ٱلْحَىُّ ٱلْقَيُّومُ (٢)
Allah—there is no god except Him—is the Living One, the All-sustainer. (2)
نَزَّلَ عَلَيْكَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ بِٱلْحَقِّ مُصَدِّقًا لِّمَا بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ وَأَنزَلَ ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةَ وَٱلْإِنجِيلَ (٣)
He has sent down to you the Book with the truth, confirming what was [revealed] before it, and He had sent down the Torah and the Evangel (3)
مِن قَبْلُ هُدًى لِّلنَّاسِ وَأَنزَلَ ٱلْفُرْقَانَ ۗ إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ شَدِيدٌ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَزِيزٌ ذُو ٱنتِقَامٍ (٤)
before as guidance for mankind, and He has sent down the Criterion. Indeed, there is a severe punishment for those who deny the signs of Allah, and Allah is all-mighty, avenger. (4)
إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَخْفَىٰ عَلَيْهِ شَىْءٌ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَا فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ (٥)
Nothing is indeed hidden from Allah in the earth or in the sky. (5)
هُوَ ٱلَّذِى يُصَوِّرُكُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْحَامِ كَيْفَ يَشَآءُ ۚ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (٦)
It is He who forms you in the wombs [of your mothers] however He wishes. There is no god except Him, the All-mighty, the All-wise. (6)

(Commentary)

The purpose of the surah is to call the believers to unity of word in religion (الدين), and to patience and steadfastness in protecting its sanctuary by alerting them to the precariousness of their position as they face enemies such as the Jews, the Christians, and the polytheists (المشركين), who have gathered their forces and resolved to extinguish the light of Allah, the Exalted, with their hands and their mouths.

It appears that this surah was revealed all at once, for its verses—numbering two hundred—exhibit a clear consistency and order from beginning to end; its verses are proportionate and its objectives are interconnected.

Therefore, the most likely view is that the surah was revealed to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) at a time when his authority had achieved some degree of stability, though not yet complete. This is because it mentions the Battle of Uhud, the Mubahala (المباهلة) — the ritual of mutual imprecation to invoke Allah's curse upon the liar — with the Christians of Najran, matters concerning the Jews, incitement against the polytheists, and a call to patience (الصبر), perseverance (المصابرة), and being stationed on the frontier (المرابطة). All of this supports the view that the surah was revealed during the days when the Muslims were afflicted with defending the sanctuary of religion with all their strength and every pillar of their community. On one hand, they were resisting the failure and apathy creeping into their ranks due to the tribulations (فتن) caused by the Jews and Christians, arguing with them and responding to them; on the other hand, they were fighting the polytheists, living in a state of war and lack of security. During those days, the fame of Islam had spread, and the world—comprising the Jews, the Christians, the Arab polytheists, and beyond them the Romans, the Persians, and others—rose up against it.

In this surah, Allah, the Glorified, reminds the believers of the realities of His religion to which He guided them, in order to soothe their souls and remove the stain of doubts, satanic whisperings, and the enticements of the People of the Book from their hearts. He explains to them that Allah, the Glorified, has not been heedless of the governance of His kingdom, nor has His creation frustrated Him. Rather, He chose His religion and guided a group of His servants to it according to the prevailing custom and the permanent sunnah (السنة) — the established divine way or law — which is the law of causes and effects (العلل والأسباب). Thus, the believer and the disbeliever both proceed according to the law of causes; a day for the disbeliever and a day for the believer. For this world is the abode of trial, today is the day of action, and the recompense is tomorrow.

His saying, the Exalted: اللَّهُ لَا إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ الْحَيُّ الْقَيُّومُ (Allah! There is no god except Him, the Living, the All-sustainer). The discussion regarding this has already passed in the commentary on the Verse of the Throne (آية الكرسي). What was concluded there is that the intent is to explain the absolute self-subsistence (القيام) of the Exalted over the matter of creation and governance. Thus, the system of existents, in their essences and effects, is under the self-subsistence (قيمومة) of Allah—not merely a self-subsistence of influence like that found in inanimate natural causes, but a self-subsistence of life (حياة) that necessitates knowledge (العلم) and power (القدرة). Divine knowledge penetrates them such that nothing of them is hidden from Him, and power dominates them such that nothing occurs from them except what He wills and permits. For this reason, He followed it two verses later by saying: إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَا يَخْفَىٰ عَلَيْهِ شَيْءٌ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَلَا فِي السَّمَاءِ هُوَ الَّذِي يُصَوِّرُكُمْ فِي الْأَرْحَامِ كَيْفَ يَشَاءُ (Indeed nothing is hidden from Allah in the earth or in the sky. It is He who forms you in the wombs as He wishes).

Since these six verses at the beginning of the surah serve as an introductory excellence (براعة الاستهلال), containing a summary of the details the surah holds—and the purpose of the surah has been mentioned—this verse serves as a preface to the discourse with a universal premise (البيان الكلي) from which the objective is derived. Similarly, the last two verses, namely His saying: "Indeed nothing is hidden from Allah..." etc., serve as the rationale (التعليل) after the explanation. Accordingly, the discourse that completes the introductory excellence consists of the two middle verses, namely His saying: "He has sent down to you the Book..." until His saying: "All-mighty, Avenger." Based on this, the meaning returns to the fact that it is incumbent upon the believers to remember that Allah, in Whom they have believed, is One in His divinity (الوهيته), presiding over creation and governance with a presiding of life; He is never overcome in His kingdom, and nothing occurs except what He wills and permits. If they remember this, they will know that He is the One who sent down the Book that guides to the truth, and the Criterion (الفرقان) — the decisive standard that distinguishes truth from falsehood — and that He proceeded in this according to the world of causes (عالم الأسباب) and the sphere of choice (ظرف الاختيار). So, whoever believes shall have his reward, and whoever disbelieves, Allah will surely recompense him, for He is All-mighty, Avenger. This is because He is Allah, besides Whom there is no other god to judge in these matters; their affairs are not hidden from Him, and their actions and disbelief do not escape His will and volition.

His saying, the Exalted: نَزَّلَ عَلَيْكَ الْكِتَابَ بِالْحَقِّ مُصَدِّقًا لِّمَا بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ (He has sent down to you the Book with the truth, confirming what was [revealed] before it). It has already been mentioned that "sending down" (التنزيل) indicates gradual descent, just as "sending down" (الإنزال) indicates a single, instantaneous descent.

This might be challenged by His saying: لَوْلَا نُزِّلَ عَلَيْهِ الْقُرْآنُ جُمْلَةً وَاحِدَةً (Why has not the Quran been sent down to him all at once?) (25:32), and by His saying, the Exalted: أَن يُنَزِّلَ عَلَيْنَا مَائِدَةً (that He should send down to us a table) (5:112), and His saying, the Exalted: لَوْلَا نُزِّلَ عَلَيْهِ آيَةٌ (Why has not a sign been sent down to him?) (6:37), and His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ إِنَّ اللَّهَ قَادِرٌ عَلَىٰ أَن يُنَزِّلَ آيَةً (Say, ‘Indeed Allah is able to send down a sign’) (6:37). Therefore, some commentators mentioned that it is better to say that the meaning of "He sent down (نزل) the Book to you" is that He sent it down descent after descent, to avoid this contradiction.

The response is: The intended meaning of "gradualness" (التدريج) in descent is not necessarily the intervention of a significant amount of time between the descent of each part of a thing and the next. Rather, composite things that exist through the existence of their parts have a relationship to the sum of their parts, and by that, the thing becomes a single, indivisible matter. Expressing it from this perspective uses "descent" (الإنزال), such as His saying, the Exalted: أَنزَلَ مِنَ السَّمَاءِ مَاءً (He sends down water from the sky) (13:17), referring to rain. Its relationship from the perspective of its existence through the existence of its parts one after another—whether a significant time intervenes between them or not—is "gradualness," and expressing it uses "sending down" (التنزيل), such as His saying, the Exalted: وَهُوَ الَّذِي يُنَزِّلُ الْغَيْثَ (It is He who sends down the rain) (42:28).

From here it becomes clear that the verses mentioned as contradictions do not actually contradict. The meaning of "Why has not the Quran been sent down (نزل) to him all at once" is: why is the Quran not sent down to him verse after verse in a single continuous time without the intervention of a significant period, as was the case with the descent of the Quran regarding various circumstances, events, and times. By this, the answer to the rest of the mentioned verses becomes clear.

As for what that commentator mentioned, it is a linguistic preference (استحسان) that is not permissible in the language at all, and it does not resolve any of the contradictions with the mentioned verses; rather, they remain as they were, which is obvious.

The speech of the Exalted has followed the course of expressing the bestowal of the Book upon the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) as "sending down" (التنزيل) and "descent" (النزول). Descent necessitates a high and lofty station or place (مقاماً أو مكاناً عالياً) from which the thing emerges in a certain way and heads toward another lower station or place where it settles. He has described Himself—Exalted is His Essence—with loftiness (العلو) and the raising of degrees, and He has described His Book as being from Him. The Exalted said: إِنَّهُ عَلِيٌّ حَكِيمٌ (Indeed He is all-exalted, all-wise) (42:51), and the Exalted said: وَلَمَّا جَاءَهُمْ كِتَابٌ مِّنْ عِندِ اللَّهِ مُصَدِّقٌ لِّمَا مَعَهُمْ (And when there came to them a Book from Allah, confirming what was with them) (2:89). Thus, it is correct to use the term "descent" in the context of the revelation (الوحي) settling in the heart of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم). Scholars have mentioned that "the truth" (الحق) is the report insofar as there is a fixed external reality corresponding to it, just as "truthfulness" (الصدق) is the report insofar as it corresponds to the external reality. Accordingly, the application of "truth" to external entities and actual matters—as it is said of Allah, the Glorified, that He is "Truth," and of external realities that they are "true"—is only because each of them is true from the perspective of the report about them. In any case, the meaning of "the truth" in the verse is the fixed matter (الامر الثابت) that does not accept falsehood.

It appears that the preposition "with" (بـ) in His saying "with the truth" (بالحق) denotes accompaniment (المصاحبة). The meaning is: He sent down the Book to you a descent that accompanies the truth and does not part from it. This accompaniment of the truth necessitates that no falsehood (باطل) can occur to it or mix with it; thus, it is secure from falsehood prevailing over it. In His saying "He sent down the Book to you with the truth," there is a metaphorical allusion (استعارة بالكناية). Other views have been mentioned regarding the meaning of the preposition "with," but they are not free from weakness.

"Confirmation" (التصديق) comes from "truthfulness" (الصدق). It is said: "I confirmed such a statement," meaning I affirmed it as true and acknowledged it as being the truth. "I confirmed so-and-so" means I acknowledged his truthfulness in what he reports.

What is meant by "what was before it" is the Torah (التوراة) and the Gospel (الإنجيل), as the Exalted said: إِنَّا أَنزَلْنَا التَّوْرَاةَ فِيهَا هُدىً (We sent down the Torah, containing guidance...) until He said: وَآتَيْنَاهُ الْإِنجِيلَ فِيهِ هُدًى (and We gave him the Gospel, containing guidance...) until He said: وَأَنزَلْنَا إِلَيْكَ الْكِتَابَ بِالْحَقِّ مُصَدِّقًا لِّمَا بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ مِنَ الْكِتَابِ (We have sent down to you the Book with the truth, confirming what was before it of the Book...) (5:44-48). The discourse is not without indication that what is in the hands of the Jews and Christians of the Torah and Gospel contains some of what Allah revealed to Moses and Jesus (peace be upon them), even if they are not free from omissions and corruption (التحريف). For what was circulating among them during the era of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) is the Torah existing today and the four famous Gospels. Thus, the Quran confirms the existing Torah and Gospel, but in part (في الجملة), not in their entirety (بالجملة), due to the verses speaking of corruption and omissions in them. The Exalted said: وَلَقَدْ أَخَذَ اللَّهُ مِيثَاقَ بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ (Allah certainly took a pledge from the Children of Israel...) until He said: وَجَعَلْنَا قُلُوبَهُمْ قَاسِيَةً يُحَرِّفُونَ الْكَلِمَ عَن مَّوَاضِعِهِ وَنَسُوا حَظًّا مِّمَّا ذُكِّرُوا بِهِ (and We made their hearts hard: they pervert the words from their contexts, and have forgotten a part of what they were reminded of...) until He said: وَمِنَ الَّذِينَ قَالُوا إِنَّا نَصَارَىٰ أَخَذْنَا مِيثَاقَهُمْ فَنَسُوا حَظًّا مِّمَّا ذُكِّرُوا بِهِ (And from those who say, ‘We are Christians,’ We took their pledge; but they forgot a part of what they were reminded of...) (5:12-14).

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَنزَلَ التَّوْرَاةَ وَالْإِنجِيلَ مِن قَبْلُ هُدًى لِّلنَّاسِ (and He sent down the Torah and the Gospel before, as guidance for mankind). "Torah" (التوراة) is a Hebrew word meaning "the Law" (الشريعة). "Gospel" (الإنجيل) is a Greek word—some say of Persian origin—meaning "glad tidings" (البشارة). A full discussion regarding these two books will come under His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّا أَنزَلْنَا التَّوْرَاةَ فِيهَا هُدًى وَنُورٌ (We sent down the Torah, containing guidance and light...) (5:44).

Among the things the Quran insists upon is naming the book of Jesus (peace be upon him) "the Gospel" in the singular form and stating that it was sent down from Allah, the Glorified, despite the fact that the gospels are many. The known ones—namely the four Gospels—existed before and during the revelation of the Quran, and their authorship is attributed to Luke, Mark, Matthew, and John. What we mentioned regarding the singular name and the description of "descent" is not without indication of corruption and omission. In any case, the mention of the Torah and the Gospel in this verse and at the beginning of the surah serves as an allusion to the Jews and Christians regarding what He will later mention of their affairs and the stories of the birth of Jesus, his prophethood, and his being raised up.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَنزَلَ الْفُرْقَانَ (and He sent down the Criterion). The Criterion (الفرقان) — the decisive standard that distinguishes truth from falsehood — is that by which truth is distinguished from falsehood, as stated in al-Sihah. The word, by its root, indicates something broader than that: it is everything by which one thing is distinguished from another. The Exalted said: يَوْمَ الْفُرْقَانِ يَوْمَ الْتَقَى الْجَمْعَانِ (the Day of Distinction, the day the two hosts met) (8:41), and the Exalted said: يَجْعَل لَّكُمْ فُرْقَانًا (He will provide you with a criterion) (8:29). Since the distinction sought by Allah regarding the meaning of guidance (الهداية) is the distinction between truth and falsehood in beliefs and knowledge, and between the duty of the servant and what is not his duty regarding the actions originating from him in the life of this world, its meaning applies to all fundamental and secondary knowledge that Allah, the Exalted, sent down to His prophets through revelation (الوحي), whether it be the Book or otherwise. The Exalted said: وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنَا مُوسَىٰ وَهَارُونَ الْفُرْقَانَ (Certainly We gave Moses and Aaron the Criterion) (21:48), and the Exalted said: وَإِذْ آتَيْنَا مُوسَى الْكِتَابَ وَالْفُرْقَانَ (And when We gave Moses the Book and the Criterion) (2:53), and the Exalted said: تَبَارَكَ الَّذِي نَزَّلَ الْفُرْقَانَ عَلَىٰ عَبْدِهِ لِيَكُونَ لِلْعَالَمِينَ نَذِيرًا (Blessed is He who sent down the Criterion to His servant that he may be a warner to all the worlds) (25:1).

The Exalted has expressed this meaning as "the Balance" (الميزان) in His saying: لَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا رُسُلَنَا بِالْبَيِّنَاتِ وَأَنزَلْنَا مَعَهُمُ الْكِتَابَ وَالمِيزَانَ لِيَقُومَ النَّاسُ بِالْقِسْطِ (Certainly We sent Our apostles with manifest proofs, and We sent down with them the Book and the Balance, so that mankind may maintain justice) (57:25). This is parallel to His saying: كَانَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً وَاحِدَةً فَبَعَثَ اللَّهُ النَّبِيِّينَ مُبَشِّرِينَ وَمُنذِرِينَ وَأَنزَلَ مَعَهُمُ الْكِتَابَ بِالْحَقِّ لِيَحْكُمَ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ فِيمَا اخْتَلَفُوا فِيهِ (Mankind were a single community; then Allah sent the prophets as bearers of good news and as warners, and He sent down with them the Book with the truth, that it may judge between the people concerning that about which they differed) (2:213). Thus, the Balance, like the Criterion, is the religion that judges between people with justice, along with the knowledge and duties of servitude (وظائف العبودية) joined to it; and Allah knows best.

It has been said: What is meant by the Criterion is the Quran. It has also been said: The evidence that separates truth from falsehood. It has also been said: The definitive proof of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) against those who argued with him regarding the matter of Jesus. It has also been said: Victory. It has also been said: Intellect (العقل). The correct view is what we have presented.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِآيَاتِ اللَّهِ (Indeed those who have faithlessly denied the signs of Allah...) until His saying: ذُو انتِقَامٍ (Avenger). Revenge (الانتقام), as it is said, is the punishment of the wrongdoer for his wrongdoing. It is not a necessary part of the meaning that it be for the sake of healing one's anger (التشفي), for that is a characteristic of the revenges that occur between us, since the wrongdoing of the wrongdoer causes us a deficiency or harm, which we remedy through severe punishment that heals our hearts. As for Him, the Exalted, His sanctuary is too exalted (أعز ساحة) to be benefited or harmed by any of the actions of His servants. However, He has promised—and His is the true promise—that He will judge between His servants with truth: if good, then good; and if evil, then evil. The Exalted said: وَاللَّهُ يَقْضِي بِالْحَقِّ (and Allah judges with the truth) (40:20), and the Exalted said: لِيَجْزِيَ الَّذِينَ أَسَاءُوا بِمَا عَمِلُوا وَيَجْزِيَ الَّذِينَ أَحْسَنُوا بِالْحُسْنَى (that He may requite those who do evil for what they have done, and reward those who do good with the best reward) (53:31). How could it be otherwise, when He is All-mighty (عزيز) in an absolute sense, His side being impregnable against His sanctities being violated. It has been said that the root meaning of "might" (العزة) is "impenetrability" (الامتناع).

And His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِآيَاتِ اللَّهِ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ شَدِيدٌ (Indeed those who have faithlessly denied the signs of Allah shall have a severe punishment). Insofar as the punishment (العذاب) is mentioned generally and not restricted to the Hereafter or the Day of Resurrection, it may include the threat of punishment in this world as well as in the Hereafter. This is one of the Quranic realities that researchers may have fallen short in fully exploring, and this is only because we do not consider something to be a punishment unless it involves some physical pain, or a deficiency or corruption in material blessings, such as the loss of wealth, the death of loved ones, or the weakness of bodies. However, what the Quran provides through its teaching is something beyond that.

A Discourse on the Meaning of Punishment (العذاب) in the Quran

The Quran considers the life of one who forgets his Lord to be "straitened" (ضنكاً), even if it appears to us to be completely expansive.

The Exalted said: وَمَنْ أَعْرَضَ عَن ذِكْرِي فَإِنَّ لَهُ مَعِيشَةً ضَنكًا (But whoever disregards My remembrance, his shall be a wretched life) (20:124). It also considers wealth and children to be a punishment, even if we consider them a pleasant blessing. The Exalted said: وَلَا تُعْجِبْكَ أَمْوَالُهُمْ وَأَوْلَادُهُمْ إِنَّمَا يُرِيدُ اللَّهُ أَن يُعَذِّبَهُم بِهَا فِي الدُّنْيَا وَتَزْهَقَ أَنفُسُهُمْ وَهُمْ كَافِرُونَ (So let not their wealth and their children impress you: Allah only desires to punish them with these in the life of this world, and that their souls should depart while they are faithless) (9:85).

The reality of the matter—as was briefly explained in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: "And We said, ‘O Adam, dwell with your mate in the paradise’" (2:35)—is that the grief, sorrow, joy, sadness, desire, fear, punishment, and bliss of a human being all revolve around what he perceives as felicity (سعادة) or wretchedness (شقاوة); this is the first point. Secondly, blessing (النعمة) and punishment (العذاب) and what relates to them differ according to what they are attributed to: the soul has its felicity and wretchedness, and the body has its felicity and wretchedness; likewise, the animal has its share of them, and the human has his share, and so on. The material, worldly human being who has not adopted the character of Allah, nor been disciplined by His discipline, sees material felicity as the only felicity and pays no heed to the felicity of the soul, which is spiritual felicity. Thus, he becomes obsessed with acquiring wealth, children, status, and the expansion of authority and power. Although he desires the very thing he has attained, he only desired the pure enjoyment and pleasure as his imagination depicted it to him. When he attains it, he finds a single pleasure surrounded by thousands of pains. As long as he has not attained what he desires, it remains a wish and a regret; and when he attains it, he finds it to be other than what he desired because of the deficiencies he sees in it and the pains he finds with it, and the failure of the causes he relied upon, as his heart was not attached to a matter above them in which there is tranquility of heart and consolation for every loss. Thus, it remains a regret. He continues to suffer from what he has found, turning away and seeking what is better than it, perhaps to quench the thirst of his breast; and in what he has not found, he fluctuates between pains and regrets. This is his state in what he has found, and that is his state in what he has lost.

As for the Quran, it views the human being as a composite of an eternal soul (روح خالد) and a changing, transforming material body. He remains in this state until he returns to his Lord, whereupon his eternity is completed without end. Thus, whatever contains pure felicity of the soul, such as knowledge and the like, is part of his felicity. Whatever contains the felicity of both his body and soul together, such as wealth and children—provided they do not distract from the remembrance of Allah nor cause him to cling to the earth—is also part of his felicity, and what a fine felicity it is. Likewise, whatever contains wretchedness of the body and deficiency in what pertains to the physical form, but contains the felicity of the eternal soul—such as being killed in the way of Allah or the loss of wealth and ease for the sake of Allah—is also part of his felicity, like enduring the bitterness of medicine for an hour to gain health for an age.

As for what contains felicity of the body but wretchedness of the soul, it is wretchedness for the human being and a punishment for him. The Quran calls the felicity of the body alone "a brief enjoyment" (متاعاً قليلاً) that should not be given any weight. The Exalted said: لَا يَغُرَّنَّكَ تَقَلُّبُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فِي الْبِلَادِ مَتَاعٌ قَلِيلٌ ثُمَّ مَأْوَاهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ وَبِئْسَ الْمِهَادُ (Never be misled by the bustle of the faithless in the towns. It is a brief enjoyment; then their abode shall be hell, and it is an evil resting place) (3:196-197).

Likewise, what contains wretchedness of both the body and the soul together is considered a punishment by the Quran, just as they consider it a punishment, but the perspective is different. It is a punishment according to the Quran because of the wretchedness of the soul, and a punishment according to them because of the wretchedness of the body. This is like the types of punishment that descended upon past nations. The Exalted said: أَلَمْ تَرَ كَيْفَ فَعَلَ رَبُّكَ بِعَادٍ إِرَمَ ذَاتِ الْعِمَادِ الَّتِي لَمْ يُخْلَقْ مِثْلُهَا فِي الْبِلَادِ وَثَمُودَ الَّذِينَ جَابُوا الصَّخْرَ بِالْوَادِ وَفِرْعَوْنَ ذِي الْأَوْتَادِ الَّذِينَ طَغَوْا فِي الْبِلَادِ فَأَكْثَرُوا فِيهَا الْفَسَادَ فَصَبَّ عَلَيْهِمْ رَبُّكَ سَوْطَ عَذَابٍ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَبِالْمِرْصَادِ (Have you not regarded how your Lord dealt with ‘Ad, [the people of] Iram, the ornate with columns, the like of whom was not created in any land, and Thamud, who hollowed out the rocks in the valley, and Pharaoh, the impaler—those who rebelled in the cities and caused much corruption in them? So your Lord poured on them a lash of punishment. Indeed your Lord is in ambush) (89:6-14).

Felicity and wretchedness for conscious beings are constituted by consciousness and perception (الشعور والإدراك). We do not consider a pleasant thing that we attained but did not feel to be felicity for ourselves, just as we do not consider an unfelt painful matter to be wretchedness. From here it becomes clear that this Quranic teaching, which follows a path in felicity and wretchedness other than the path of matter, must necessarily be followed by a type of training (تربية) through which the human being sees the true felicity that the Quran identifies as felicity, and the true wretchedness as wretchedness. And so it is, for it instills in its people that their hearts should not be attached to other than Allah, and that they should see their Lord as the Owner (المالك) who owns everything, such that nothing is independent except through Him, and nothing is intended except for Him.

Such a person sees nothing for himself in this world except felicity: between what contains the felicity of his soul and body, and what contains the pure felicity of his soul. As for anything else, he sees it as punishment and exemplary torment (نكالا). As for the person attached to the desire of the self (هوى النفس) and the matter of this world, although he may see what he has acquired of the adornment of the world as felicity for himself, good, and pleasure, he will soon discover his error in his path, and his presumed felicity will turn into wretchedness itself. The Exalted said: فَذَرْهُمْ يَخُوضُوا وَيَلْعَبُوا حَتَّىٰ يُلَاقُوا يَوْمَهُمُ الَّذِي يُوعَدُونَ (So leave them to indulge and play until they face their day which they are promised) (70:42), and the Exalted said: لَّقَدْ كُنتَ فِي غَفْلَةٍ مِّنْ هَٰذَا فَكَشَفْنَا عَنكَ غِطَاءَكَ فَبَصَرُكَ الْيَوْمَ حَدِيدٌ (You were certainly oblivious of this. We have removed from you your veil, and so your sight is acute today) (50:22), and the Exalted said: فأَعْرِضْ عَن مَّن تَوَلَّىٰ عَن ذِكْرِنَا وَلَمْ يُرِدْ إِلَّا الْحَيَاةَ الدُّنْيَا ذَٰلِكَ مَبْلَغُهُم مِّنَ الْعِلْمِ (So avoid those who turn away from Our remembrance and desire nothing but the life of the world. That is the ultimate reach of their knowledge) (53:30). Moreover, no life is pure for them except that it is marred by grief and anxiety that outweigh it.

From here it becomes clear that the perception and thought (الإدراك والفكر) present in the people of Allah and the people of the Quran are different from those in others, despite them all being of one species, which is the human being. Between the two groups are intermediaries from the people of faith who have not completed their divine teaching and training.

This is what is concluded from His speech regarding the meaning of punishment. Nevertheless, His speech does not refrain from naming physical wretchedness as "punishment," but ultimately it is a punishment at the level of the body rather than the soul. The Exalted said, narrating from Job (peace be upon him): أَنِّي مَسَّنِيَ الشَّيْطَانُ بِنُصْبٍ وَعَذَابٍ (Indeed Satan has visited me with hardship and punishment) (38:41), and the Exalted said: وَإِذْ أَنجَيْنَاكُم مِّنْ آلِ فِرْعَوْنَ يَسُومُونَكُمْ سُوءَ الْعَذَابِ يُقَتِّلُونَ أَبْنَاءَكُمْ وَيَسْتَحْيُونَ نِسَاءَكُمْ وَفِي ذَٰلِكُم بَلَاءٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ عَظِيمٌ (And when We delivered you from Pharaoh’s clan, who inflicted a terrible punishment on you: they would slaughter your sons and spare your women, and in that was a great test from your Lord) (7:141). Thus, He named what they did to them a "test" (بلاء) and a "trial" (امتحاناً) from Allah, and a "punishment" in itself, not from Him, the Glorified.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَا يَخْفَىٰ عَلَيْهِ شَيْءٌ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَلَا فِي السَّمَاءِ (Indeed nothing is hidden from Allah in the earth or in the sky) etc. The Exalted rationalized the punishment of those who disbelieve in His signs by stating that He is All-mighty, Avenger. However, since this rationale requires an addition to be joined to it for the objective to be complete—for the All-mighty, Avenger might be unaware of the disbelief of some who disbelieve in His blessings and thus not hasten with punishment and revenge—He followed that statement by saying: "Indeed nothing is hidden from Allah." Thus, He explained that He is All-mighty and nothing is hidden from Him, whether manifest to the senses or hidden from them. It is possible that what is meant by "in the earth" and "in the sky" are the manifest actions performed by the limbs and the hidden ones latent in the hearts, similar to what we pointed out regarding His saying, the Exalted: لِّلَّهِ مَا فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَمَا فِي الْأَرْضِ وَإِن تُبْدُوا مَا فِي أَنفُسِكُمْ أَوْ تُخْفُوهُ يُحَاسِبْكُم بِهِ اللَّهُ (To Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth. Whether you disclose what is in your minds or conceal it, Allah will bring you to account for it) (2:284).

His saying, the Exalted: هُوَ الَّذِي يُصَوِّرُكُمْ فِي الْأَرْحَامِ كَيْفَ يَشَاءُ (It is He who forms you in the wombs as He wishes). "Forming" (التصوير) is the casting of a form (الصورة) upon a thing. Form includes that which has a shadow, like a statue, and that which has no shadow. "Wombs" (الأرحام) is the plural of rahim, which is the place where the fetus settles in females.

This verse, in its meaning, is an escalation (الترقي) relative to the two verses preceding it. The conclusion of the two verses is that Allah, the Exalted, punishes those who disbelieve in His signs because He is the All-mighty, the Avenger, the Knower of the secret and the manifest; He is not overcome in His command, but rather He is the Overcomer. The conclusion of this verse is that the matter is greater than that. Whoever disbelieves in His signs and opposes His command is too lowly and insignificant to disbelieve through his own independence and reliance on his own power without Allah permitting that, thereby overcoming His command and nullifying the most perfect system (النظام الأحسن) upon which Allah, the Glorified, organized creation, such that his will prevails over the will of his Lord. Rather, Allah, the Glorified, is the One who permitted him that, in the sense that He organized matters in a way that leads to the existence of choice (الاختيار) in the human being. This is the attribute by which he is able to embark upon the path of faith and obedience or commit to the path of disbelief and disobedience, so that the matter of tribulation and trial (الفتنة والامتحان) may be completed. So, whoever wishes, let him believe, and whoever wishes, let him disbelieve; and they do not wish except that Allah, the Lord of all the worlds, wishes.

There is no disbelief, nor faith, nor anything else except by decree (تقدير), which is the organization of things in a way that facilitates for everything what it directs itself toward of its goals, which it will fulfill through its action, by His forming it with its specific form that paves the way for it to proceed toward what it proceeds to. Thus, Allah, the Glorified, is the Overcomer of His command, the Subduer in His will, the Dominant over His creation. The human being thinks that he does what he wishes and acts as he wants, and that he breaks that continuous order that Allah organized in the universe, thereby preceding the decree; yet this very thing is from the decree (القدر).

This is the meaning of His saying: "He forms you in the wombs as He wishes," meaning He organizes the parts of your existence at the beginning of the matter in a way that leads to what He wishes at its conclusion—a wish of permission (إذن), not a wish of necessity (حتم).

He restricted the discourse to the decree (التقدير) occurring in the human being and did not mention the general decree occurring in the entire world so that it would apply to the context, and because, as mentioned, there is an allusion in the verses to the Christians regarding what they say about the Messiah (peace be upon him). The verses conclude with the truth of his matter, for the Christians do not deny his being in the womb and that he did not bring himself into existence.

The generalization after the specification in the address—namely His saying "He forms you" after His saying "He sent down to you"—is to indicate that the faith of the believers, like the disbelief of the disbelievers, is not outside the rule of decree. Thus, their souls are soothed by the divine mercy and gift regarding themselves, and they are consoled by what they have heard of the matter of decree and the matter of revenge regarding what they find difficult of the disbelief of the disbelievers.

His saying, the Exalted: لَا إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ (There is no god except Him, the All-mighty, the All-wise). In this is a return to what the discourse began with in the verses regarding monotheism (التوحيد), and it serves as a summary of the proof for emphasis.

For these mentioned matters—namely: the guidance of creation after their origination, the sending down of the Book and the Criterion, and the perfection of governance by punishing the disbelievers—are matters that must be attributed to a God who governs them. Since there is no god except Allah, Exalted is His state, He is the One who guides people, He is the One who sends down the Book and the Criterion, and He punishes the disbelievers in His signs. He only does what He does of guidance, sending down, revenge, and decree through His might (عزته) and wisdom (حكمته).

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Majma', narrated from al-Kalbi, Muhammad ibn Ishaq, and al-Rabi' ibn Anas: The beginning of the surah, up to eighty-some verses, was revealed regarding the delegation of Najran. They were sixty riders who came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم), among whom were fourteen of their nobles. Among the fourteen were three men to whom their affairs returned: al-'Aqib, the leader of the people and their consultant, whose opinion they always followed, and his name was 'Abd al-Masih; al-Sayyid, their provider and the one in charge of their caravan, and his name was al-Ayham; and Abu Harithah ibn 'Alqamah, their bishop, scholar, leader, and the head of their schools. He was honored among them and had studied their books. The kings of the Romans had honored him, funded him, and built churches for him due to his knowledge and diligence. They came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) in Medina and entered his mosque when he had prayed the 'Asr prayer, wearing fine garments: cloaks and robes, with the beauty of the men of Balharith ibn Ka'b. Some of the companions of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) who saw them said: "We have never seen a delegation like them." The time for their prayer arrived, so they began to strike the bell and stood and prayed in the mosque of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم). The companions said: "O Messenger of Allah, this in your mosque?" The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) said: "Leave them." So they prayed toward the east. Then al-Sayyid and al-'Aqib spoke to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم). The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) said to them: "Submit (أسلما)." They said: "We have already submitted before you." He said: "You lie; what prevents you from Islam is your claim that Allah has a son, your worship of the cross, and your eating of swine." They said: "If he is not the son of Allah, then who is his father?" And they all argued with him regarding Jesus. The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) said to them: "Do you not know that there is no son but that he resembles his father?" They said: "Yes." He said: "Do you not know that our Lord is Living and does not die, and that Jesus is subject to extinction?" They said: "Yes." He said: "Do you not know that our Lord is the Sustainer over everything, protecting it and providing for it?" They said: "Yes." He said: "Does Jesus possess any of that?" They said: "No." He said: "Do you not know that nothing is hidden from Allah in the earth or in the sky?" They said: "Yes." He said: "Does Jesus know any of that except what he was taught?" They said: "No." He said: "Indeed, our Lord formed Jesus in the womb as He wished, and our Lord does not eat, nor drink, nor discharge waste." They said: "Yes." He said: "Do you not know that Jesus was carried by his mother as a woman carries, then she gave birth to him as a woman gives birth to her child, then he was nourished as a child is nourished, then he used to eat, drink, and discharge waste?" They said: "Yes." He said: "Then how can it be as you claimed?" They remained silent, so Allah revealed regarding them the beginning of Surah Al 'Imran up to eighty-some verses.

I say: Al-Suyuti narrated this meaning in al-Durr al-Manthur from Abu Ishaq, ibn Jarir, and ibn al-Mundhir from Muhammad ibn Ja'far ibn al-Zubayr, and from ibn Ishaq from Muhammad ibn Sahl ibn Abi Umamah. As for the story, its narration will come later. As for the revelation of the beginning of the surah regarding that, it seems to be their own deduction (اجتهاد), and it has already been mentioned that the apparent context indicates its revelation all at once.

From the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم): الشقي من شقى في بطن أُمه ، والسعيد من سعد في بطن أُمه — "The wretched is he who is wretched in his mother's womb, and the felicitous is he who is felicitous in his mother's womb."

In al-Kafi, from Imam al-Baqir (عليه‌السلام), who said: "When Allah intends to create the drop (النطفة) — the initial stage of the embryo — which is among those from whom the pledge (الميثاق) was taken from the loin of Adam, or what appears to Him regarding it, and places it in the womb, He moves the man for intercourse and inspires the womb: 'Open your door so that My creation, My penetrating decree (قضائي), and My measure (قدري) may enter you.' So it opens its door, and the drop reaches the womb. It remains there for forty days, then becomes a clot (علقة) for forty days, then becomes a lump (مضغة) for forty days, then becomes flesh in which intertwined veins run. Then Allah sends two creating angels who create in the wombs what Allah wishes. They enter the woman's belly through the woman's mouth and reach the womb, wherein is the ancient soul (الروح القديمة) transferred through the loins of men and the wombs of women. They breathe into it the soul of life and permanence (روح الحياة والبقاء), and they fashion for it hearing, sight, limbs, and all that is in the belly by the permission of Allah, the Exalted. Then Allah inspires the two angels: 'Write upon him My decree, My measure, and My penetrating command, and stipulate for Me the change of plan (البداء) — the manifestation of a divine decision after it appeared otherwise — in what you write.' They say: 'O Lord, what shall we write?' Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, inspires them: 'Raise your heads to the head of his mother.' They raise their heads, and behold, the Tablet (اللوح) is striking the forehead of his mother. They look into it and find in the Tablet his form, his adornment, his term, his pledge, whether he is felicitous or wretched, and all his affairs. One of them dictates to his companion, and they write all that is in the Tablet and stipulate the change of plan in what they write. Then they seal the book and place it between his eyes, then they set him upright in his mother's womb." He said: "Sometimes he rebels and turns over, and that only happens with every rebel or recalcitrant one. When the time for the child's exit arrives, whether complete or incomplete, Allah inspires the womb: 'Open your door so that My creation may emerge to My earth and My command may be executed upon it, for the time of its exit has arrived.' He said: 'So the womb opens the door for the child, and he turns over so that his feet are above his head and his head is at the bottom of the belly, so that Allah may make the exit easy for the woman and the child. Then Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, sends to him an angel called the Driver (زاجر), who drives him with a drive that startles the child. If he is delayed, the angel drives him with another drive and he is startled, so the child falls to the earth crying, startled by the drive.'"

I say: His saying: "When He intends to create the drop," means to make it a complete, well-proportioned human being. Restricting it with his saying: "which is among those from whom the pledge was taken," is an allusion to what will be explained later: that the human being in this worldly existence and his states are preceded in existence by another prior existence (نشأة) in which this one proceeds according to the path of that one. It is called in the language of the narrations "the World of Particles" (عالم الذر) and "the Pledge" (الميثاق). Thus, that from which the pledge was taken must necessarily be created in this worldly existence, and what is created in this existence is from that from which the pledge was taken, without accepting change or substitution; for that is from the necessitated decree (القضاء المحتوم). Therefore, he alternated the discourse between it and his saying: "or what appears to Him regarding it," meaning the change of plan (البداء) appears to Him regarding the completion of its creation, so it is not completed and becomes a miscarriage. Thus, the category opposite to it has no change of plan in it, as we mentioned. And his saying: "and places it in the womb," is conjoined to his saying: "create the drop."

His saying (عليه‌السلام): "They enter the woman's belly through the woman's mouth," it is possible that his saying "through the woman's mouth" is from the words of the narrator, as supported by the use of the manifest noun in place of the pronoun. Based on the apparent case of it being from the words of the Imam (عليه‌السلام), it is among the evidences that their entry and penetration into the woman's belly is not of the same nature as the entry of a body into a body, since there is no path to the womb other than the vulva except the veins, including the vein from which the menstrual blood flows into the womb. This opening is no easier for entry than the walls of the womb. Thus, for the entry from the mouth, there is a reason other than the ease of the path, and this is obvious.

His saying (عليه‌السلام): "wherein is the ancient soul transferred through the loins of men and the wombs of women," it is as if it is the vegetative soul (الروح النباتية) which is the principle for nutrition and growth.

His saying (عليه‌السلام): "They breathe into it the soul of life and permanence," its apparent meaning is that the pronoun refers to the "ancient soul." Thus, the soul of life and permanence is breathed into the vegetative soul. If we assume it refers to the lump (المضغة), for example, it would be breathed into the lump that is alive with the vegetative soul, so the vegetative lump becomes breathed into. In any case, the discourse indicates that the breathing of the human soul (الروح الإنساني) is a type of elevation (ترق) for the vegetative soul through intensification (الاشتداد) — according to what is necessitated by the theory of trans-substantial motion (الحركة الجوهرية).

By this, the meaning of the transfer of the ancient soul through the loins of men and the wombs of women becomes clear. The soul is united in existence with the body in a certain way, and it is the drop and what supplies it of menstrual blood, which is united with the bodies of the parents, and they with the drop, and so on. Thus, what happens to the human being is determined in general in the existence of his fathers and mothers, witnessed in the forms of their persons. It is, in a way, like the index taken from the book placed before it.

By this, the meaning of his saying (عليه‌السلام) becomes clear: "Then Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, inspires them—meaning the two angels—to raise your heads to the head of his mother." This is because what pertained to his father of the explanation of his decree and measure has been severed from him by the separation of the drop; thus, nothing remains connected to him except his mother. This is his saying (عليه‌السلام): "and behold, the Tablet is striking the forehead of his mother." The forehead is the gathering place of the human senses and the vanguard of his face. "They look into it and find in the Tablet his form, his adornment, his term, his pledge, whether he is felicitous or wretched, and all his affairs. One of them dictates to his companion," so their relationship is similar to the relationship of the agent (الفاعل) and the recipient (القابل). "And they write all that is in the Tablet."

His saying (عليه‌السلام): "and stipulate the change of plan in what they write," is due to the fact that his form does not contain all the causes of his future events. For the form, although it is a principle for all that happens to the human being of his states and the events specific to him, it is not the entire principle; rather, matters and events external to him have an involvement in that. Therefore, what appears from it of events is not inevitably occurring; thus, it is a place for the change of plan (البداء).

And know: that the attribution of the details of birth to Allah, the Glorified, moving the man, His inspiration to the womb, and the sending of the two creating angels and the driving angel, and so on, does not contradict the attribution of these events—including birth—to their natural causes (أسبابها الطبيعية). For these two types of causes—namely the spiritual causes (الأسباب المعنوية) and the material causes (الأسباب المادية)—are situated one in the longitudinal path (طول) of the other, not in the latitudinal path (عرض), such that one would nullify the other, or they would conflict and both be nullified, or the matter would return to the composition of the complete cause (العللة التامة) from the sum of the two causes. Rather, each of them is a complete cause, but in its own level.

For whoever Allah, the Glorified, has established to guide people to their spiritual felicity and their journey toward His pleasure—and they are the prophets (peace be upon them), and the path is the path of the inward (الباطن)—his function is to speak to people in a language that leads them through the path of the inward and reminds them of the station of their Lord in all his explanations. This involves the mediation of angels, the attribution of events to their actions, the attribution of felicity to their support, the attribution of wretchedness with its specifics to the devils and their enticements, and the attribution of everything to Allah, the Glorified, in a manner befitting the sanctuary of His holiness and the presence of His Lordship. This is to derive from that the forms of guidance and misguidance, profit and loss, and in short, all the affairs of the life of the Hereafter. Yet, they did not neglect the matter of natural causes nor did they waste their right, for they are one of the two pillars of human life and the foundation upon which the life of this world is based. It is necessary for the human being to know the summary of their matter just as it is necessary for him to know the summary of the matter regarding spiritual causes, so that his knowledge of himself may be complete, and thus he may know his Lord.

Ayahs

هُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنزَلَ عَلَيْكَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ مِنْهُ ءَايَـٰتٌ مُّحْكَمَـٰتٌ هُنَّ أُمُّ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ وَأُخَرُ مُتَشَـٰبِهَـٰتٌ ۖ فَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ فِى قُلُوبِهِمْ زَيْغٌ فَيَتَّبِعُونَ مَا تَشَـٰبَهَ مِنْهُ ٱبْتِغَآءَ ٱلْفِتْنَةِ وَٱبْتِغَآءَ تَأْوِيلِهِۦ ۗ وَمَا يَعْلَمُ تَأْوِيلَهُۥٓ إِلَّا ٱللَّهُ ۗ وَٱلرَّٰسِخُونَ فِى ٱلْعِلْمِ يَقُولُونَ ءَامَنَّا بِهِۦ كُلٌّ مِّنْ عِندِ رَبِّنَا ۗ وَمَا يَذَّكَّرُ إِلَّآ أُو۟لُوا۟ ٱلْأَلْبَـٰبِ (٧)
It is He who has sent down to you the Book. Parts of it are definitive verses, which are the mother of the Book, while others are metaphorical. As for those in whose hearts is deviance, they pursue what is metaphorical in it, courting temptation, and seeking its interpretation. But no one knows its interpretation except Allah and those firmly grounded in knowledge; they say, ‘We believe in it; all of it is from our Lord.’ And none takes admonition except those who possess intellect. (7)
رَبَّنَا لَا تُزِغْ قُلُوبَنَا بَعْدَ إِذْ هَدَيْتَنَا وَهَبْ لَنَا مِن لَّدُنكَ رَحْمَةً ۚ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ ٱلْوَهَّابُ (٨)
[They say,] ‘Our Lord! Do not make our hearts swerve after You have guided us, and bestow Your mercy on us. Indeed, You are the All-munificent. (8)
رَبَّنَآ إِنَّكَ جَامِعُ ٱلنَّاسِ لِيَوْمٍ لَّا رَيْبَ فِيهِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُخْلِفُ ٱلْمِيعَادَ (٩)
Our Lord! You will indeed gather mankind on a day in which there is no doubt. Indeed Allah does not break His promise.’ (9)

(Commentary)

His saying, the Exalted: هو الذي أنزل عليك الكتاب (It is He who has sent down to you the Book). The Exalted used the term "sending down" (الإنزال) — the revelation of the whole at once — rather than "gradual sending down" (التنزيل) because the objective is to explain some of the attributes and properties of the Book as a whole. This objective is to show that it contains verses that are "decisive" (محكمة) and others that are "metaphorical" (متشابهة), which refer back to the decisive ones and are clarified by them. Thus, the Book is viewed in this regard as a single entity without consideration of its multiplicity or gradual descent; hence, the use of (الإنزال) was more appropriate than (التنزيل).

His saying, the Exalted: منه آيات محكمات هن ام الكتاب وأُخر متشابهات (In it are verses that are decisive—they are the mother of the Book—and others [that are] metaphorical). The root of the word "decisiveness" (حكم) conveys the meaning of a thing being in a state that prevents the entry of what might corrupt it, fragment it, or disorder its affairs. From this root come "fortification" (الإحكام), "arbitration" (التحكيم), "judgment" (الحكم) in the sense of adjudication, and "wisdom" (الحكمة) in the sense of complete gnosis (المعرفة التامة) and certain, beneficial knowledge (العلم الجازم النافع). It also refers to the "bit" (الحكمة) of a horse's bridle. In all these usages, there is an element of the meaning of prevention (المنع) and precision (الإتقان). It is sometimes said that the root denotes the meaning of prevention coupled with rectification (إصلاح).

What is meant here by the "decisiveness" (إحكام) of the decisive verses (المحكمات) is the precision of these verses in terms of the absence of ambiguity (التشابه) within them, unlike the metaphorical ones. For although the Exalted described His Book as having decisive verses in His saying: كِتَابٌ أُحْكِمَتْ آيَاتُهُ ثُمَّ فُصِّلَتْ مِن لَّدُنْ حَكِيمٍ خَبِيرٍ (A Book whose verses have been made decisive and then elaborated from One who is All-wise, All-aware) [Hud: 1], the inclusion of "elaboration" (التفصيل) after "decisiveness" (الإحكام) in that verse is evidence that the decisiveness meant there refers to a state the Book possessed before its revelation. This state was its being a single unity that had not yet undergone division or fragmentation through the multiplicity of verses. Thus, it was its precision before the existence of fragmentation. This type of decisiveness is an attribute of the entire Book. This is in contrast to the attribute of decisiveness and precision (الإتقان) that belongs to some of its verses in relation to others, due to their immunity from ambiguity regarding their intended meaning.

In other words, since His saying: منه آيات محكمات هن ام الكتاب وأُخر متشابهات (In it are verses that are decisive—they are the mother of the Book—and others [that are] metaphorical) involves a division of the Book's verses into the two categories of "decisive" and "metaphorical," we know that the "decisiveness" meant here is different from the "decisiveness" with which the entire Book is described in the verse: "A Book whose verses have been made decisive..." Similarly, the "metaphorical" (التشابه) meant here is different from the "similarity" (التشابه) with which the entire Book is described in His saying: كِتَابًا مُّتَشَابِهًا مَّثَانِيَ (A Book self-consistent [in its parts] and repeated) [Az-Zumar: 23].

He described the decisive verses as being the "mother of the Book" (ام الكتاب). The word "mother" (الام), according to its primary linguistic meaning, is that to which a thing returns. This can only mean that the metaphorical verses (المتشابهة) return to them. Thus, one part of the Book—the metaphorical verses—returns to another part—the decisive verses. From this, it becomes clear that the genitive construction (الإضافة) in His saying "mother of the Book" (ام الكتاب) is not possessive (لامية), like saying "mother of the children," but rather carries the meaning of "from" (من), like saying "the women of the people" or "the early jurists" and the like. Thus, the Book contains verses that are the "mother" of other verses. The use of the singular "mother" (الام) rather than the plural indicates that the decisive verses are not discordant among themselves; rather, they are harmonious and unified.

The decisive verses are contrasted in the verse with His saying: وأُخر متشابهات (and others [that are] metaphorical). "Similarity" (التشابه) is the correspondence of different things and their unification in certain attributes or qualities. Allah, the Glorified, has described the entire Quran with this attribute where He said: كِتَابًا مُّتَشَابِهًا مَّثَانِيَ تَقْشَعِرُّ مِنْهُ جُلُودُ الَّذِينَ يَخْشَوْنَ رَبَّهُمْ (A Book self-consistent [in its parts] and repeated, whereat shiver the skins of those who fear their Lord) [Az-Zumar: 23]. What is meant by this, undoubtedly, is that the verses of the Book follow a single pattern in terms of the eloquence of their composition, the precision of their style, the exposition of realities and wisdoms, and the guidance toward the manifest truth, as indicated by the qualifiers mentioned in that verse. This "similarity" is an attribute of the entire Book.

As for the "metaphorical" (التشابه) mentioned in this verse—namely, His saying: وأُخر متشابهات (and others [that are] metaphorical)—its contrast with His saying: منه آيات محكمات هن ام الكتاب (In it are verses that are decisive—they are the mother of the Book), and the mention of those in whose hearts is "perversity" (زيغ) following them seeking "sedition" (الفتنة) and seeking its "interpretation" (التأويل), all of this indicates that what is meant by "metaphorical" is that a verse is such that its intended meaning is not determined for the listener's understanding by the mere hearing of it. Rather, the mind wavers between one meaning and another until it is referred back to the decisive verses of the Book, which then determine its meaning and clarify it with a full exposition. At that point, the metaphorical verse becomes decisive by means of the decisive verse, while the decisive verse is decisive in itself.

For example, His saying: الرَّحْمَٰنُ عَلَى الْعَرْشِ اسْتَوَىٰ (The All-beneficent, settled on the Throne) [Taha: 5]—its intended meaning is ambiguous to the listener upon first hearing it. But when it is referred back to a verse like His saying: لَيْسَ كَمِثْلِهِ شَيْءٌ (Nothing is like Him) [Ash-Shura: 11], the mind settles on the fact that what is meant is His dominance over the kingdom and His encompassment of creation, rather than physical sitting or relying on a place, which would imply "corporeality" (التجسم) — the attribution of a physical body to Allah — which is impossible for Allah, the Glorified. Similarly, His saying: إِلَىٰ رَبِّهَا نَاظِرَةٌ (looking at their Lord) [Al-Qiyamah: 23]—when referred back to a verse like: لَّا تُدْرِكُهُ الْأَبْصَارُ وَهُوَ يُدْرِكُ الْأَبْصَارُ (The sights do not perceive Him, and He perceives the sights) [Al-An'am: 103], it is known that the "looking" (النظر) meant is something other than looking with the physical eye. Likewise, when an "abrogated" (المنسوخة) verse is presented to the "abrogating" (الناسخة) verse, it becomes clear that the intended meaning of the former is a ruling limited by the boundary of the abrogating ruling, and so on.

This is what is gathered from the meaning of the "decisive" (المحكم) and the "metaphorical" (المتشابه), and what a simple understanding receives from the totality of His saying: هو الذي أنزل عليك الكتاب فيه آيات محكمات هن ام الكتاب وأُخر متشابهات (It is He who has sent down to you the Book. In it are verses that are decisive—they are the mother of the Book—and others [that are] metaphorical). For this very verse is undoubtedly decisive, even if one were to assume the rest of the Quran to be metaphorical.

If this verse itself were metaphorical, then all the verses of the Quran would become metaphorical, and the division indicated by His saying "In it are verses..." would be corrupted. Furthermore, the remedy indicated by His saying "they are the mother of the Book" would be nullified. His saying: كِتَابٌ فُصِّلَتْ آيَاتُهُ قُرْآنًا عَرَبِيًّا لِّقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ بَشِيرًا وَنَذِيرًا (A Book whose verses have been elaborated, an Arabic Quran for a people who have knowledge, as a bearer of good news and as a warner) [Fussilat: 3-4] would not be true. Nor would the argument contained in His saying: أَفَلَا يَتَدَبَّرُونَ الْقُرْآنَ وَلَوْ كَانَ مِنْ عِندِ غَيْرِ اللَّهِ لَوَجَدُوا فِيهِ اخْتِلَافًا كَثِيرًا (Do they not contemplate the Quran? Had it been from [someone] other than Allah, they would have surely found much discordance in it) [An-Nisa: 82] be complete—along with other verses indicating that the Quran is light, guidance, an exposition, a clarification, a manifest book, and a reminder.

Moreover, anyone who carefully examines the verses of the Quran from beginning to end will not doubt that there is no verse among them that has a meaning yet does not speak its meaning or remains lost in its intent. Rather, there is no verse except that it contains an indication of its meaning: either a single meaning about which one proficient in speech has no doubt, or multiple meanings that are confused with one another. These confused meanings must necessarily contain the true intended meaning; otherwise, the "indication" (الدلالة) would be nullified, as you have seen. This single meaning, which is undoubtedly the true intent, cannot be foreign to the established principles of the Quran, such as the existence of the Creator, His "monotheism" (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts —, the sending of prophets, the legislation of rulings, the "Resurrection" (المعاد), and the like. Rather, it is in agreement with them, and they necessitate it, produce it, and determine the true intended meaning from among the various possible meanings. Thus, some parts of the Quran clarify others, and some parts are a foundation to which other parts return.

Furthermore, if this observer, after such an examination, comes across His saying: منه آيات محكمات هن ام الكتاب وأُخر متشابهات (In it are verses that are decisive—they are the mother of the Book—and others [that are] metaphorical), he will not doubt that the "decisive verses" are those containing the established principles of the Quran, and the "metaphorical verses" are those whose meanings are determined and clarified by those principles.

If you say: The return of the "branches" (الفروع) to the "principles" (الاصول) is something beyond doubt wherever there are deep-rooted principles and branching derivatives, whether in Quranic sciences or elsewhere. But this does not necessitate the occurrence of "ambiguity" (التشابه); so what is the reason for it?

I say: The reason for it is one of two things. The knowledge that the Quran imparts is of two types:

First, there is high knowledge that lies beyond the realm of the senses and matter. Ordinary understandings cannot help but waver between a physical, sensory judgment and something else, such as His saying: إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَبِالْمِرْصَادِ (Indeed your Lord is in ambush) [Al-Fajr: 14] and His saying: وَجَاء رَبُّكَ (and your Lord comes) [Al-Fajr: 22]. To a mind accustomed to the sensible, meanings that are attributes and properties of physical bodies immediately occur. These are removed by referring back to the principles that involve the negation of the laws of matter and body from the subject. This is a phenomenon that occurs in all non-material sciences and subjects hidden from the senses. It is not exclusive to the Noble Quran but is found in other divine books containing high knowledge (without distortion), and it is also found in the "divine sciences" (المباحث الالهية) of philosophy. This is what the Quran refers to in another language in His saying: أَنزَلَ مِنَ السَّمَاءِ مَاءً فَسَالَتْ أَوْدِيَةٌ بِقَدَرِهَا (He sends down water from the sky whereat the valleys flow according to their capacity) [Ar-Ra'd: 17], and His saying: إِنَّا جَعَلْنَاهُ قُرْآنًا عَرَبِيًّا لَّعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ وَإِنَّهُ فِي أُمِّ الْكِتَابِ لَدَيْنَا لَعَلِيٌّ حَكِيمٌ (We have made it an Arabic Quran so that you may exercise your reason. And it is indeed in the Mother Book with Us, sublime and wise) [Az-Zukhruf: 3-4].

Second, there is that which pertains to social laws and "subsidiary rulings" (الأحكام الفرعية). The inclusion of the "abrogating" (الناسخ) and the "abrogated" (المنسوخ) in this category of knowledge—due to the changing interests that necessitate legislation on one hand, and the revelation of the Quran in stages on the other—causes ambiguity to appear in its verses. This ambiguity is removed by referring the metaphorical to the decisive, and the abrogated to the abrogating.

His saying, the Exalted: فأما الذين في قلوبهم زيغ فيتبعون ما تشابه منه ابتغاء الفتنة وابتغاء تأويله (As for those in whose hearts is perversity, they follow what is metaphorical in it, seeking to cause sedition and seeking its interpretation). "Perversity" (الزيغ) is a deviation from "uprightness" (الاستقامة). It necessitates the agitation and anxiety of the heart, as evidenced by its opposite at the end of the verse: والراسخون في العلم يقولون آمنا به كل من عند ربنا (and those firmly grounded in knowledge; they say, ‘We believe in it; all of it is from our Lord’). The verse describes the state of people regarding their reception of the Quran, with its decisive and metaphorical parts. Some have perverse, deviating, and agitated hearts; they follow the metaphorical seeking sedition and interpretation. Others are "firmly grounded in knowledge" (الراسخون في العلم), with stable hearts; they hold to the decisive, believe in the metaphorical without following it [independently], and ask Allah not to let their hearts deviate after having guided them.

From this, it becomes clear that "following the metaphorical" (اتباع المتشابه) means following it in practice, not in belief. This blameworthy following is the following of the metaphorical without referring it back to the decisive; for if it were referred back, the following would become a following of the decisive, in which there is no blame.

"Seeking sedition" (ابتغاء الفتنة) means seeking to misguide people, for "sedition" (الفتنة) is close to "misguidance" (الاضلال) in meaning. The Exalted says: They intend, by following the metaphorical, to misguide people regarding the verses of Allah, the Glorified, and another matter that is even greater: to attain and stand upon the "interpretation" (تأويل) of the Quran and the sources of the rulings of the lawful and the prohibited, so that they may dispense with following the decisive parts of the religion, thereby abrogating the religion of Allah from its root.

"Interpretation" (التأويل) is from (الأول), which means "returning" (الرجوع). Thus, the interpretation of the metaphorical is the "point of return" (المرجع) to which it returns, and the interpretation of the Quran is the source from which its knowledge is taken.

Allah, the Glorified, has mentioned the word "interpretation" (التأويل) in several places in His speech. He said: وَلَقَدْ جِئْنَاهُم بِكِتَابٍ فَصَّلْنَاهُ عَلَىٰ عِلْمٍ هُدًى وَرَحْمَةً لِّقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ هَلْ يَنظُرُونَ إِلَّا تَأْوِيلَهُ يَوْمَ يَأْتِي تَأْوِيلُهُ يَقُولُ الَّذِينَ نَسُوهُ مِن قَبْلُ قَدْ جَاءَتْ رُسُلُ رَبِّنَا بِالْحَقِّ (Certainly We have brought them a Book, which We have elaborated with knowledge, as a guidance and mercy for a people who have faith. Do they await anything but its interpretation? The day its interpretation comes, those who had forgotten it before will say, ‘Our Lord’s apostles had certainly brought the truth’) [Al-A'raf: 52-53]. That is, they brought the truth in what they informed and announced: that Allah is their true Guardian, that what they call upon besides Him is falsehood, that prophethood is true, that religion is true, and that Allah will resurrect those in the graves. In short, everything whose reality will manifest on the Day of Resurrection from the reports and news of prophethood.

From this, it has been said that "interpretation" in the verse refers to the "external reality" (الخارج) which the truthful report matches, such as the matters witnessed on the Day of Resurrection, which are the "referents" (مطابقات) of the reports of the prophets, messengers, and books.

This is refuted by the fact that "interpretation," according to this view, would be exclusive to verses reporting on attributes, certain acts, and what will occur on the Day of Resurrection. As for verses containing the legislation of rulings, since they involve "compositional speech" (الانشاء) — speech that initiates a reality rather than describing one —, they have no external matching reality outside of them. Likewise, verses indicating what the "plain intellect" (صريح العقل) judges, such as several ethical rulings, have their interpretation with them. Similarly, verses relating the stories of the prophets and past nations—their interpretation according to this meaning would precede them rather than being delayed until the Day of Resurrection. Yet the apparent meaning of the verse attributes "interpretation" to the entire Book, not to a specific category of its verses.

Similar to it is His saying: "This Quran could not have been fabricated..." until He says: "Or do they say, ‘He has fabricated it’?" until He says: بَلْ كَذَّبُوا بِمَا لَمْ يُحِيطُوا بِعِلْمِهِ وَلَمَّا يَأْتِهِمْ تَأْوِيلُهُ كَذَٰلِكَ كَذَّبَ الَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ فَانظُرْ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَاقِبَةُ الظَّالِمِينَ (No, they have impugned that which they do not comprehend, and whose interpretation has not yet come to them. Thus did those who were before them impugn [the apostles]. So observe how was the outcome of the wrongdoers!) [Yunus: 39]. These verses, as you see, attribute "interpretation" to the totality of the Book.

For this reason, some have mentioned that "interpretation" is the "objective external matter" (الأمر العيني الخارجي) upon which the speech relies. In the case of "reports" (الأخبار), it is the reported event that occurs in reality, whether in the past—like the stories of the prophets and past nations—or in the future—as in verses reporting on Allah's attributes, His names, His promises, and everything that will appear on the Day of Resurrection. In the case of "compositional speech" (الانشاء), such as the verses of rulings, it refers to the "interests" (المصالح) realized in external reality, as in His saying: وَأَوْفُوا الْكَيْلَ إِذَا كِلْتُمْ وَزِنُوا بِالْقِسْطَاسِ الْمُسْتَقِيمِ ذَٰلِكَ خَيْرٌ وَأَحْسَنُ تَأْوِيلًا (Fill the measure when you measure, and weigh with an even balance. That is better and more favorable in interpretation) [Al-Isra: 35]. The interpretation of filling the measure and establishing the weight is the interest resulting from them in society, which is the uprightness of the human social order.

Against this, it is argued: First, the apparent meaning of this verse is that "interpretation" is an external matter and an "objective effect" (أثر عيني) resulting from their external action—which is filling the measure and establishing the weight—not the legislative command contained in His saying: "Fill the measure when you measure..." Thus, interpretation is an external matter that is the point of return and the outcome of another external matter. Describing the verses of the Book as having an interpretation is because they "recount" (حكاية) external meanings (as in reports) or "relate" (تعلق) to external actions or matters (as in commands) that have an interpretation. Thus, the description is of the state of the thing's "adjunct" (متعلق), not the state of the thing itself.

Second, although "interpretation" is the point of return to which a thing returns and "reverts" (يؤول), it is a specific kind of return, not every return. A subordinate returns to his superior, but he is not his "interpretation." A number returns to the number one, but it is not its "interpretation." Thus, it is necessarily a point of return in a specific way, not absolutely. This is indicated by His saying in the story of Moses and Khidr (peace be upon them): سَأُنَبِّئُكَ بِتَأْوِيلِ مَا لَمْ تَسْتَطِع عَّلَيْهِ صَبْرًا (I will inform you of the interpretation of that which you could not bear patiently) [Al-Kahf: 78], and His saying: ذَٰلِكَ تَأْوِيلُ مَا لَمْ تَسْطِع عَّلَيْهِ صَبْرًا (That is the interpretation of that which you could not bear patiently) [Al-Kahf: 82]. What he informed Moses of were the "forms" (صور) and "titles" (عناوين) of what he had done in the three instances where Moses (peace be upon him) had been heedless of those forms and titles, perceiving instead other forms and titles that prompted his objection. The three instances were: حَتَّىٰ إِذَا رَكِبَا فِي السَّفِينَةِ خَرَقَهَا (until when they embarked on the ship, he made a hole in it) [Al-Kahf: 71]; حَتَّىٰ إِذَا لَقِيَا غُلَامًا فَقَتَلَهُ (until when they met a boy, he killed him) [Al-Kahf: 74]; and حَتَّىٰ إِذَا أَتَيَا أَهْلَ قَرْيَةٍ اسْتَطْعَمَا أَهْلَهَا فَأَبَوْا أَن يُضَيِّفُوهُمَا فَوَجَدَا فِيهَا جِدَارًا يُرِيدُ أَن يَنقَضَّ فَأَقَامَهُ (until when they came to the people of a town, they asked its people for food, but they refused to extend them any hospitality. There they found a wall which was about to collapse, so he set it up) [Al-Kahf: 77].

What Moses (peace be upon him) perceived of the forms and titles of these events was his saying: أَخَرَقْتَهَا لِتُغْرِقَ أَهْلَهَا لَقَدْ جِئْتَ شَيْئًا إِمْرًا (Did you make a hole in it to drown its people? You have certainly done a monstrous thing!) [Al-Kahf: 71]; his saying: أَقَتَلْتَ نَفْسًا زَكِيَّةً بِغَيْرِ نَفْسٍ لَّقَدْ جِئْتَ شَيْئًا نُّكْرًا (Did you kill an innocent soul who had not killed anyone? You have certainly done a dire thing!) [Al-Kahf: 74]; and his saying: لَوْ شِئْتَ لَاتَّخَذْتَ عَلَيْهِ أَجْرًا (Had you wished, you could have taken a wage for it) [Al-Kahf: 77].

The "interpretation" that Khidr announced was his saying: أَمَّا السَّفِينَةُ فَكَانَتْ لِمَسَاكِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ فِي الْبَحْرِ فَأَرَدتُّ أَنْ أَعِيبَهَا وَكَانَ وَرَاءَهُم مَّلِكٌ يَأْخُذُ كُلَّ سَفِينَةٍ غَصْبًا وَأَمَّا الْغُلَامُ فَكَانَ أَبَوَاهُ مُؤْمِنَيْنِ فَخَشِينَا أَن يُرْهِقَهُمَا طُغْيَانًا وَكُفْرًا فَأَرَدْنَا أَن يُبْدِلَهُمَا رَبُّهُمَا خَيْرًا مِّنْهُ زَكَاةً وَأَقْرَبَ رُحْمًا ، وَأَمَّا الْجِدَارُ فَكَانَ لِغُلَامَيْنِ يَتِيمَيْنِ فِي الْمَدِينَةِ ، وَكَانَ تَحْتَهُ كَنزٌ لَّهُمَا وَكَانَ أَبُوهُمَا صَالِحًا فَأَرَادَ رَبُّكَ أَن يَبْلُغَا أَشُدَّهُمَا وَيَسْتَخْرِجَا كَنزَهُمَا رَحْمَةً مِّن رَّبِّكَ (As for the ship, it belonged to some poor people who worked on the sea. I wanted to make it defective, for there was a king behind them who seized every ship by force. As for the boy, his parents were faithful [persons], and we feared he would overwhelm them with rebellion and unfaith. So we desired that their Lord should give them in exchange one better than him in purity and closer in affection. As for the wall, it belonged to two orphan boys in the city, and there was a treasure belonging to them under it. Their father had been a righteous man, so your Lord desired that they should come of age and take out their treasure as a mercy from your Lord) [Al-Kahf: 79-82]. Then he answered all of Moses's objections collectively by saying: وَمَا فَعَلْتُهُ عَنْ أَمْرِي (I did not do that of my own accord) [Al-Kahf: 82]. Thus, what is intended by "interpretation" in these verses, as you see, is the return of a thing to its [true] form and title—similar to the return of "striking" to "disciplining" or the return of "phlebotomy" to "medical treatment"—not similar to the return of our saying "Zayd came" to the actual coming of Zayd in external reality.

Close to this is the use of the word "interpretation" in several places in the story of Joseph (peace be upon him), such as His saying: إِذْ قَالَ يُوسُفُ لِأَبِيهِ يَا أَبَتِ إِنِّي رَأَيْتُ أَحَدَ عَشَرَ كَوْكَبًا وَالشَّمْسَ وَالْقَمَرَ رَأَيْتُهُمْ لِي سَاجِدِينَ (When Joseph said to his father, ‘O father! I saw eleven stars, and the sun and the moon: I saw them prostrating themselves before me’) [Yusuf: 4], and His saying: وَرَفَعَ أَبَوَيْهِ عَلَى الْعَرْشِ وَخَرُّوا لَهُ سُجَّدًا وَقَالَ يَا أَبَتِ هَٰذَا تَأْوِيلُ رُؤْيَايَ مِن قَبْلُ قَدْ جَعَلَهَا رَبِّي حَقًّا (And he seated his parents high upon the throne, and they fell down prostrate before him. He said, ‘O father! This is the interpretation of my dream of yore. My Lord has certainly made it come true’) [Yusuf: 100]. The return of what he saw in the dream to the prostration of his parents and brothers before him, while it is a "return," is of the nature of the return of the "symbol" (المثال) to the "symbolized" (الممثل).

Likewise, His saying: وَقَالَ الْمَلِكُ إِنِّي أَرَىٰ سَبْعَ بَقَرَاتٍ سِمَانٍ يَأْكُلُهُنَّ سَبْعٌ عِجَافٌ وَسَبْعَ سُنبُلَاتٍ خُضْرٍ وَأُخَرَ يَابِسَاتٍ ، يَا أَيُّهَا الْمَلَأُ أَفْتُونِي فِي رُؤْيَايَ إِن كُنتُمْ لِلرُّؤْيَا تَعْبُرُونَ ، قَالُوا أَضْغَاثُ أَحْلَامٍ وَمَا نَحْنُ بِتَأْوِيلِ الْأَحْلَامِ بِعَالِمِينَ ، وَقَالَ الَّذِي نَجَا مِنْهُمَا وَادَّكَرَ بَعْدَ أُمَّةٍ أَنَا أُنَبِّئُكُم بِتَأْوِيلِهِ فَأَرْسِلُونِ يُوسُفُ أَيُّهَا الصِّدِّيقُ أَفْتِنَا (The king said, ‘I saw seven fat cows being eaten by seven lean ones, and seven green ears and [seven] others dry. O members of the elite! Explain my dream to me, if you can interpret dreams.’ They said, ‘[These are] confused nightmares, and we do not know the interpretation of nightmares.’ The one of the two who had been delivered and who remembered after a long time, said, ‘I will inform you of its interpretation, so let me go.’ ‘Joseph, O truthful one! Explain to us...’) until He says: قَالَ تَزْرَعُونَ سَبْعَ سِنِينَ دَأَبًا فَمَا حَصَدتُّمْ فَذَرُوهُ فِي سُنبُلِهِ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا مِّمَّا تَأْكُلُونَ ثُمَّ يَأْتِي مِن بَعْدِ ذَٰلِكَ سَبْعٌ شِدَادٌ يَأْكُلْنَ مَا قَدَّمْتُمْ لَهُنَّ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا مِّمَّا تُحْصِنُونَ (He said, ‘You will sow for seven years consecutively. Then leave what you harvest in its ear, except a little that you eat. Then after that there will come seven hard [years] which will eat up what you have set aside for them, except a little that you preserve’) [Yusuf: 43-48].

Likewise, His saying: وَدَخَلَ مَعَهُ السِّجْنَ فَتَيَانِ قَالَ أَحَدُهُمَا إِنِّي أَرَانِي أَعْصِرُ خَمْرًا ، وَقَالَ الْآخَرُ إِنِّي أَرَانِي أَحْمِلُ فَوْقَ رَأْسِي خُبْزًا تَأْكُلُ الطَّيْرُ مِنْهُ نَبِّئْنَا بِتَأْوِيلِهِ إِنَّا نَرَاكَ مِنَ الْمُحْسِنِينَ (Two youths entered the prison with him. One of them said, ‘I saw myself [in a dream] pressing wine.’ The other said, ‘I saw myself carrying bread on my head from which the birds were eating. Inform us of its interpretation, for we see you to be a virtuous man’) until He says: يَا صَاحِبَيِ السِّجْنِ أَمَّا أَحَدُكُمَا فَيَسْقِي رَبَّهُ خَمْرًا وَأَمَّا الْآخَرُ فَيُصْلَبُ فَتَأْكُلُ الطَّيْرُ مِن رَّأْسِهِ قُضِيَ الْأَمْرُ الَّذِي فِيهِ تَسْتَفْتِيَانِ (O my two prison mates! As for one of you, he will serve wine to his master; and as for the other, he will be crucified and the birds will eat from his head. The matter about which you inquire has been decided) [Yusuf: 36-41].

Likewise, His saying: وَيُعَلِّمُكَ مِن تَأْوِيلِ الْأَحَادِيثِ (and He will teach you the interpretation of dreams) [Yusuf: 6]; His saying: وَلِنُعَلِّمَهُ مِن تَأْوِيلِ الْأَحَادِيثِ (and that We might teach him the interpretation of dreams) [Yusuf: 21]; and His saying: وَعَلَّمْتَنِي مِن تَأْوِيلِ الْأَحَادِيثِ (and You have taught me the interpretation of dreams) [Yusuf: 101]. "Interpretation" has been used in all these instances from the story of Joseph (peace be upon him) for the events to which a dream returns. It is what the sleeper sees in a corresponding form and symbol. Thus, the relation of the "interpretation" to the "object of interpretation" is the relation of the "meaning" (المعنى) to the "form" (الصورة) through which it appears, and the "reality" (الحقيقة) represented to its "symbol" (المثال) by which it is represented. This is exactly how the matter proceeded in the verses we cited from the story of Moses and Khidr (peace be upon them), and also in His saying: "Fill the measure when you measure..." until His saying: "and more favorable in interpretation."

Contemplation of the verses concerning the Resurrection reveals that this is also what is meant by the word "interpretation" in His saying: "No, they have impugned that which they do not comprehend, and whose interpretation has not yet come to them," and His saying: "Do they await anything but its interpretation? The day its interpretation comes..." For verses such as His saying: لَّقَدْ كُنتَ فِي غَفْلَةٍ مِّنْ هَٰذَا فَكَشَفْنَا عَنكَ غِطَاءَكَ فَبَصَرُكَ الْيَوْمَ حَدِيدٌ (You were certainly oblivious of this. We have removed your veil from you, and so your sight is acute today) [Qaf: 22] indicate that the witnessing of the occurrence of what the Book reported and the prophets announced on the Day of Resurrection is not of the same nature as the "sensory witnessing" (المشاهدة الحسية) we are familiar with in this world. Just as the occurrence itself and the system governing it are different from what we are accustomed to in this life. Further explanation of this will follow.
