Thus, the return of the reports of the Book and prophethood to their manifest contents on the Day of Resurrection is not like the return of a report about future events to the realization of their contents in the future.

From what has preceded, it has become clear: First, that a verse having an "interpretation" to which it returns is different from its being "metaphorical" and returning to a "decisive" verse.

Second, that "interpretation" is not exclusive to metaphorical verses; rather, the entire Quran has an interpretation. Thus, a decisive verse has an interpretation just as a metaphorical one does.

Third, that "interpretation" is not among the "concepts" (المفاهيم) that are the "signified meanings" (مداليل) of words; rather, it is among the "objective external matters" (الامور الخارجية العينية). Describing the verses as having an interpretation is a description of the state of the "adjunct" (المتعلق). As for using "interpretation" to mean a sense that contradicts the literal meaning of the word, this is a "neologism" (استعمال مولد) that arose after the revelation of the Quran. There is no evidence whatsoever that this is what is meant by His saying: "seeking its interpretation" and "no one knows its interpretation except Allah." Nor is there evidence for most of the meanings mentioned for "interpretation" which we shall soon cite.

His saying, the Exalted: وما يعلم تأويله إلا الله (and no one knows its interpretation except Allah). The apparent meaning of the speech is that the pronoun refers to "what is metaphorical" (ما تشابه), due to its proximity, as is also apparent in His saying: "seeking its interpretation." You have already seen that this does not necessitate that interpretation be restricted to metaphorical verses. It is also possible that the pronoun refers to "the Book," like the pronoun in His saying: "what is metaphorical in it" (ما تشابه منه).

The apparent meaning of the "restriction" (الحصر) is that the knowledge of interpretation is limited to Him, the Glorified. As for His saying: والراسخون في العلم (and those firmly grounded in knowledge), the apparent structure of the speech is that the "and" (الواو) is "disjunctive" (للاستيناف) — marking the start of a new sentence — in the sense of being a counterpart to the "division" (الترديد) indicated at the beginning of the verse: "As for those in whose hearts is perversity..." The meaning is: People are of two types in their reception of the Book: those who follow what is metaphorical in it, and those who say, when something in it is metaphorical to them: "We believe in it; all of it is from our Lord." They differ only because of the difference between "perversity of heart" and "firmness in knowledge."

Furthermore, if the "and" were "conjunctive" (للعطف) — joining two subjects in the same ruling — and the intent of the conjunction was to include those firmly grounded in knowledge in the knowledge of interpretation, then the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) would be among them, and indeed the best of them. How could it be conceived that the Quran was revealed to his heart while he did not know what was intended by it? It is the custom of the Quran, when mentioning the community or describing the state of a group that includes the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), to mention him individually first and distinguish him personally as an honor and exaltation for him, and then mention them all together. Examples include His saying: آمَنَ الرَّسُولُ بِمَا أُنزِلَ إِلَيْهِ مِن رَّبِّهِ وَالْمُؤْمِنُونَ (The Apostle has faith in what has been sent down to him from his Lord, and all the faithful [as well]) [Al-Baqarah: 285]; His saying: ثُمَّ أَنزَلَ اللَّهُ سَكِينَتَهُ عَلَىٰ رَسُولِهِ وَعَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ (Then Allah sent down His composure upon His Apostle and upon the faithful) [At-Tawbah: 26]; His saying: لَٰكِنِ الرَّسُولُ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا (But the Apostle and those who have faith with him...) [At-Tawbah: 88]; His saying: وَهَٰذَا النَّبِيُّ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا (and this Prophet and those who have faith) [Al-Imran: 68]; and His saying: لَا يُخْزِي اللَّهُ النَّبِيَّ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مَعَهُ (Allah will not disgrace the Prophet and those who have faith with him) [Al-Tahrim: 8], among others.

Thus, if the intent of His saying "and those firmly grounded in knowledge" was that they know the interpretation—and the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) is certainly among them—the proper way of speaking, as you have seen, would have been to say: "and no one knows its interpretation except Allah, His Messenger, and those firmly grounded in knowledge." This is so, even though it could be said that His saying at the beginning of the verse: "It is He who has sent down to you the Book..." indicates that the Prophet is knowledgeable of the Book, so there is no need to mention him a second time.

Therefore, it is apparent that the knowledge of interpretation is restricted in the verse to Him, the Exalted. This does not contradict the possibility of an "exception" (الاستثناء) being applied to it, just as verses indicate that the knowledge of the "Unseen" (الغيب) is restricted to Him, the Glorified, while an exception is mentioned in His saying: عَالِمُ الْغَيْبِ فَلَا يُظْهِرُ عَلَىٰ غَيْبِهِ أَحَدًا إِلَّا مَنِ ارْتَضَىٰ مِن رَّسُولٍ ([He is] the Knower of the Unseen, and He does not disclose His Unseen to anyone, except to an apostle He approves) [Al-Jinn: 26-27]. Nor does it contradict the possibility that the "excepted" (المستثنى) are the firmly grounded in knowledge themselves. For there is no contradiction between this verse indicating one of the states of those firmly grounded in knowledge—which is pausing at ambiguity and having faith and submission, in contrast to those with perverse hearts—and other verses indicating that they, or some of them, are knowledgeable of the reality of the Quran and the interpretation of its verses, as will be explained.

His saying, the Exalted: والراسخون في العلم يقولون آمنا به كل من عند ربنا (and those firmly grounded in knowledge; they say, ‘We believe in it; all of it is from our Lord’). "Firmness" (الرسوخ) is the utmost stability. The placement of "those firmly grounded in knowledge" in contrast to "those in whose hearts is perversity," and then describing them as saying "We believe in it; all of it is from our Lord," indicates their complete definition: that they have knowledge of Allah and His verses into which no doubt or uncertainty enters. Thus, the knowledge they have attained of the decisive verses is fixed and unshakable; they believe in it and follow it—that is, they act upon it. When a metaphorical verse comes to them, its ambiguity does not cause their hearts to waver in the firm knowledge they possess; rather, they believe in it and refrain from following it in practice [independently].

In their saying "We believe in it; all of it is from our Lord," they mention both the "evidence" (الدليل) and the "conclusion" (النتيجة) together. For the fact that both the decisive and the metaphorical are from Allah necessitates faith in all of it—both its decisive and its metaphorical parts. The clarity of the intent in the decisive necessitates following it in practice. Pausing at the metaphorical without rejecting it is because it is from Allah. It is not permissible to follow any of its metaphorical meanings that contradict the decisive, due to the brilliance of the exposition in the decisive. Therefore, one must follow from its multiple possible meanings that which agrees with the meaning of the decisive. This is precisely the "referring of the metaphorical to the decisive." Thus, His saying "all of it is from our Lord" serves as the evidence for both matters: faith and action in the case of the decisive, and faith alone in the case of the metaphorical, while referring back to the decisive for action.

His saying, the Exalted: وما يذكر إلا اولوا الألباب (and no one takes admonition except those who possess intellect). "Taking admonition" (التذكر) is the transition to the evidence of a thing to reach its conclusion. Since their saying "all of it is from our Lord" was, as mentioned, an act of "inference" (استدلال) and a transition to what indicates their action, Allah called it "taking admonition" and praised them for it.

"Intellects" (الألباب) is the plural of (لب), which is the "pure intellect" (العقل الزكي) free from impurities. Allah has praised them beautifully in several places in His speech, defining them as the people of faith in Allah, "turning" (الإنابة) to Him, and following the best of speech. He then described them as being in constant "remembrance" (ذكر) of their Lord, which resulted in them being the people of "taking admonition"—that is, transitioning to true knowledge through evidence—and the people of wisdom and gnosis. The Exalted said: وَالَّذِينَ اجْتَنَبُوا الطَّاغُوتَ أَن يَعْبُدُوهَا وَأَنَابُوا إِلَى اللَّهِ لَهُمُ الْبُشْرَىٰ فَبَشِّرْ عِبَادِ الَّذِينَ يَسْتَمِعُونَ الْقَوْلَ فَيَتَّبِعُونَ أَحْسَنَهُ أُولَٰئِكَ الَّذِينَ هَدَاهُمُ اللَّهُ وَأُولَٰئِكَ هُمْ أُولُو الْأَلْبَابِ (As for those who keep clear of the rebel [gods] lest they should worship them, and turn penitently to Allah, there is good news for them. So give good news to My servants who listen to the word [of Allah] and follow the best [sense] of it. They are the ones whom Allah has guided, and it is they who possess intellect) [Az-Zumar: 17-18]. And the Exalted said: إِنَّ فِي خَلْقِ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَاخْتِلَافِ اللَّيْلِ وَالنَّهَارِ لَآيَاتٍ لِّأُولِي الْأَلْبَابِ الَّذِينَ يَذْكُرُونَ اللَّهَ قِيَامًا وَقُعُودًا وَعَلَىٰ جُنُوبِهِمْ (Indeed in the creation of the heavens and the earth and the alternation of night and day, there are signs for those who possess intellect. Those who remember Allah standing, sitting, and lying on their sides...) [Al-Imran: 190-191]. This constant remembrance and the resulting humility and submission is the "turning" (الإنابة) that necessitates their taking admonition from the signs of Allah and their transition to true knowledge, as the Exalted said: وَمَا يَتَذَكَّرُ إِلَّا مَن يُنِيبُ (and no one takes admonition except him who turns [to Allah] penitently) [Ghafir: 13]. And He said: وَمَا يَذَّكَّرُ إِلَّا أُولُو الْأَلْبَابِ (and no one takes admonition except those who possess intellect) [Al-Baqarah: 269, Al-Imran: 7].

His saying, the Exalted: ربنا لا تزغ قلوبنا بعد إذ هديتنا وهب لنا من لدنك رحمة إنك أنت الوهاب (Our Lord, do not make our hearts swerve after You have guided us, and bestow on us a mercy from Yourself. Indeed You are the All-munificent). This is one of the effects of their being firmly grounded in knowledge. For when they knew the station of their Lord and understood from Allah, they became certain that the "Kingdom" (الملك) belongs to Allah alone, and that they possess nothing for themselves. Since it is possible for their hearts to swerve after the firmness of knowledge, they took refuge in their Lord, asking Him not to let their hearts swerve after He had guided them, and to bestow on them a mercy from Himself so that this blessing might remain with them and help them in traversing the "straight path" (صراط الهداية) and traveling through the "stages of proximity" (مراتب القرب).

As for the request to bestow mercy upon them after the request not to let their hearts swerve, it is because the absence of the heart's swerving does not necessitate the persistence of firmness in knowledge. It is possible that their hearts do not swerve, yet knowledge is withdrawn from them, leaving them "aimless" (سدى) — neither happy with knowledge nor wretched with swerving, but in a state of ignorance and "weakness" (الاستضعاف). They are in desperate need of the knowledge they possess. Moreover, their need does not stop at their current position; rather, they are travelers on a path in which they need types of mercy that no one knows or can count except Allah, the Glorified. They are conscious of this need of theirs, and the evidence for it is their subsequent saying: "Our Lord, indeed You will gather mankind for a day of which there is no doubt."

Thus, their saying "Our Lord, do not make our hearts swerve after You have guided us" is a "seeking of refuge" (استعاذة) from the descent of perversity into their hearts and its displacement of the firm knowledge within them. Their saying "and bestow on us a mercy from Yourself. Indeed You are the All-munificent" is a "seeking of rain" (استمطار) from the clouds of mercy so that the life of their hearts may endure through it. The use of the "indefinite" (تنكير) for "mercy" and describing it as being "from Himself" (من لدنه) is an expression of their ignorance of the nature of this mercy and how it ought to be, except that they know that were it not for a mercy from their Lord, and were it not from Himself, their affair would not be complete.

In seeking refuge from swerving in Allah alone and seeking the bestowal of mercy from Himself alone, there is an indication that they see the "totality of the Kingdom" (تمام الملك) as belonging to Allah alone, without turning toward the "intermediary causes" (الأسباب).

His saying, the Exalted: ربنا إنك جامع الناس ليوم لا ريب إن الله لا يخلف الميعاد (Our Lord, You will indeed gather mankind for a day of which there is no doubt. Indeed Allah does not break His promise). This serves as a "justification" (تعليل) for their request for mercy. This is because of their knowledge that the establishment of the system of creation, the call of religion, and the "striving" (كدح) of man in the course of his existence are all a prelude to their being gathered for the Day of Resurrection, a day on which no one will be of any avail or receive help except through mercy, as the Exalted said: إِنَّ يَوْمَ الْفَصْلِ مِيقَاتُهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ يَوْمَ لَا يُغْنِي مَوْلًى عَن مَّوْلًى شَيْئًا وَلَا هُمْ يُنصَرُونَ ، إِلَّا مَن رَّحِمَ اللَّهُ (Indeed the Day of Judgment is the tryst for them all, the day when a friend will not avail a friend in any way, nor will they be helped, except those on whom Allah has mercy) [Ad-Dukhan: 40-42]. Therefore, they asked for a mercy from their Lord and delegated its determination and specification to Him so that it might benefit them in their affair.

They described this day as being one "of which there is no doubt" so that their utmost concern in asking and supplicating might be directed toward it. They also justified this description by saying: "Indeed Allah does not break His promise," because their state is one of being firmly grounded in knowledge, and knowledge of a thing does not become firm nor does "assent" (تصديق) settle except with knowledge of its "productive cause" (علة). The cause of their lack of doubt in the realization of this day is Allah's promise of it; thus, they mentioned it.

A similar approach applies to their justification of their saying "and bestow on us a mercy from Yourself" with their saying "Indeed You are the All-munificent." For His being the All-munificent (الوهاب) is the reason for their request for mercy. Their use of the pronoun "You" (أنت) and the "definite predicate" (تعريف الخبر) with the "article" (اللام) — which implies "exclusivity" (الحصر) — justifies their saying "from Yourself" (من لدنك), which indicates "speciality" (الاختصاص). Likewise, a similar approach applies to their saying "Our Lord, do not make our hearts swerve," as they followed it with what serves as its cause: "after You have guided us." It was mentioned just now that their saying "We believe in it," in terms of its being followed by "all of it is from our Lord," is also of this nature.

These are men who believed in their Lord and remained firm upon it, so Allah guided them and perfected their intellects. Thus, they do not speak except from knowledge, and they do not act except from knowledge. Therefore, Allah called them "those firmly grounded in knowledge" and referred to them as "those who possess intellect." If you contemplate how Allah defined "those who possess intellect," you will find it perfectly applicable to what He mentioned of their state in these verses. The Exalted said: وَالَّذِينَ اجْتَنَبُوا الطَّاغُوتَ أَن يَعْبُدُوهَا وَأَنَابُوا إِلَى اللَّهِ لَهُمُ الْبُشْرَىٰ فَبَشِّرْ عِبَادِ الَّذِينَ يَسْتَمِعُونَ الْقَوْلَ فَيَتَّبِعُونَ أَحْسَنَهُ أُولَٰئِكَ الَّذِينَ هَدَاهُمُ اللَّهُ وَأُولَٰئِكَ هُمْ أُولُو الْأَلْبَابِ (As for those who keep clear of the rebel [gods] lest they should worship them, and turn penitently to Allah, there is good news for them. So give good news to My servants who listen to the word [of Allah] and follow the best [sense] of it. They are the ones whom Allah has guided, and it is they who possess intellect) [Az-Zumar: 17-18]. He described them with faith, following the best of speech, and turning to Allah, the Glorified—and He has described those firmly grounded in knowledge with these same attributes in these verses.

As for the "shift" (الالتفات) from "address" (الخطاب) to "absence" (الغيبة) in His saying "Indeed Allah does not break His promise," it is because this promise is not exclusive to them but encompasses them and others. Thus, it was more appropriate to change their saying "Our Lord" to the "Majestic Name" (لفظة الجلالة) — Allah — because the ruling of "Divinity" (الالوهية) is general and encompasses everything.

A Detailed Discussion on the Decisive, the Metaphorical, and Interpretation

What we have presented in the preceding discussion regarding the meaning of the "decisive" (المحكم), the "metaphorical" (المتشابه), and "interpretation" (التأويل) is what is gathered from contemplating His speech, the Glorified, and what is benefited from the "narrated traditions" (المأثور) from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام), which will follow in the "traditional discussion" (البحث الروائي).

However, people have differed on this subject, and the disagreement has become widespread and the deviation among them intense. The tail of this dispute and contention extends back to the first generation of the "exegetes" (مفسري) among the "Companions" (الصحابة) and the "Successors" (التابعين). Rarely is there found in what has been transmitted to us of their speech anything that approaches the aforementioned explanation, let alone being perfectly identical to it.

The primary reason for this is the confusion between the discussion on the decisive and the metaphorical and the discussion on the meaning of interpretation. This caused a strange disorder in the formulation of the problem, the method of investigation, and the resulting conclusion. We shall present a detailed account of each aspect of these discussions, what has been said regarding them, and what is the chosen truth, while distinguishing the subject of the discussion as much as possible within the following sections:

1 - The Decisive and the Metaphorical

"Decisiveness" (الإحكام) and "similarity/ambiguity" (التشابه) are among the words whose concepts are clear in the language. The Book has been described by both, as in His saying: كِتَابٌ أُحْكِمَتْ آيَاتُهُ (A Book whose verses have been made decisive) [Hud: 1], and His saying: كِتَابًا مُّتَشَابِهًا مَّثَانِيَ (A Book self-consistent [in its parts] and repeated) [Az-Zumar: 23]. In these instances, only the Book as a whole is described by them, in terms of the precision of its composition and exposition, and in terms of the similarity of its composition and exposition in reaching the peak of precision and decisiveness.

However, since His saying: هو الذي أنزل عليك الكتاب منه آيات محكمات هن ام الكتاب وأُخر متشابهات (It is He who has sent down to you the Book. In it are verses that are decisive—they are the mother of the Book—and others [that are] metaphorical) involves a division of the verses of the Book themselves into "decisive" and "metaphorical," we know that what is meant by decisiveness and similarity here is something other than what the entire Book is described by. It was thus appropriate to investigate their meaning and identify their "referents" (مصداقهما) among the verses. Regarding this, there are views that perhaps exceed ten:

First: That the "decisive verses" are His saying in Surah al-An'am: قُلْ تَعَالَوْا أَتْلُ مَا حَرَّمَ رَبُّكُمْ عَلَيْكُمْ أَلَّا تُشْرِكُوا بِهِ شَيْئًا (Say, ‘Come, I will recite what your Lord has forbidden you: do not ascribe any partners to Him...’) [Al-An'am: 151] until the end of the three verses. The "metaphorical verses" are those that were ambiguous to the Jews, namely the "disconnected letters" (الحروف المقطعة) revealed at the beginning of several Quranic surahs, such as Alif-Lam-Mim, Alif-Lam-Ra, and Ha-Mim. This is because the Jews interpreted them according to "alphanumeric calculation" (حساب الجمل), seeking to extract from them the duration of this community's existence and its lifespan, so the matter became ambiguous to them. This is attributed to Ibn Abbas among the Companions.

Against this: It is a claim without evidence. Even if it were accepted, there is no evidence for restricting the two categories to these instances. Furthermore, it would necessitate the existence of a third category that is neither decisive nor metaphorical, which the apparent meaning of the verse rejects.

However, the truth is that the attribution is misplaced. What is narrated from Ibn Abbas is that he said: "The three verses from the end of Surah al-An'am are among the decisive verses," not that the decisive verses are [only] those three verses. In al-Durr al-Manthur, it is recorded that Sa'id ibn Mansur, Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Hakim (who authenticated it), and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abdullah ibn Qays: "I heard Ibn Abbas saying regarding His saying 'In it are verses that are decisive': 'The three verses from the end of Surah al-An'am are decisive: "Say, Come...", and the two verses after it.'"

This is supported by what was also narrated from him regarding His saying "verses that are decisive": "From here: 'Say, Come...' to the end of three verses, and from here: 'Your Lord has decided that you shall not worship any but Him' to the end of three verses." Thus, the two narrations testify that he only mentioned these verses as examples of other decisive verses, not that he restricted them to these.

Second: The opposite of the first: that the "decisive verses" are the disconnected letters at the openings of the surahs, and the "metaphorical verses" are everything else. This is narrated from Abu Fakhtah, who mentioned regarding His saying "they are the mother of the Book": "They are the openings of the surahs; from them the Quran is extracted: 'Alif-Lam-Mim. This is the Book'—from it al-Baqarah was extracted; and 'Alif-Lam-Mim. Allah, there is no god but He, the Living, the Sustainer'—from it Al-Imran was extracted." A similar view is narrated from Sa'id ibn Jubayr regarding the meaning of His saying "they are the mother of the Book": "The origin of the Book, because they are written in all the books." This indicates that they both hold, regarding the meaning of the openings of the surahs, that what is meant by them are the "phonetic letters" (ألفاظ الحروف) with the consideration that the Book revealed to you is these disconnected letters from which words and sentences are composed—as is one of the views regarding the meaning of the openings of the surahs.

Against this: In addition to being based on something for which there is no evidence at all—namely, interpreting the disconnected letters at the openings of the surahs as you have seen—it does not apply to the verse itself. For then the entire Quran, except for the openings of the surahs, would become metaphorical. Yet Allah, the Glorified, has censured the following of the metaphorical and considered it a result of "perversity of heart," while He, the Exalted, has praised the following of the Quran and indeed considered it among the most obligatory of duties, as in His saying: وَاتَّبَعُوا النُّورَ الَّذِي أُنزِلَ مَعَهُ (and follow the light that has been sent down with him) [Al-A'raf: 157], and other verses.

Third: That the "metaphorical" is what is called "ambiguous/summary" (مجملاً) and the "decisive" is the "manifest/clear" (المبين).

Against this: What has been clarified of the attributes of the decisive and the metaphorical in the verse does not apply to the "summary" and the "manifest." To explain: The "summariness" (اجمال) of a word is its being such that some aspects of its meaning are mixed and merged with others, such that the intended aspect is not separated from the others. This causes the addressee or listener to be confused in identifying the intended meaning. The custom of the "people of the language" (أهل اللسان) in the context of communication is not to follow words of this nature; rather, they rely on another "manifest" (مبين) word that clarifies this summary one, which then becomes manifest and is followed. This is the state of the summary word with its clarifier. If the decisive and the metaphorical were exactly the summary and the manifest, then what is followed would be the metaphorical when referred back to the decisive, not the decisive itself. Such "following" would be something that the "nature of speech and communication" (قريحة التكلم والتفاهم) does not permit; thus, the people of the language—whether those with perversity or those firmly grounded in knowledge—would not engage in it. Nor would following the metaphorical be something that incurs censure and causes perversity of heart.

Fourth: That the "metaphorical verses" are the "abrogated" (المنسوخة) verses, because they are believed in but not acted upon; and the "decisive verses" are the "abrogating" (الناسخة) verses, because they are believed in and acted upon. This is attributed to Ibn Abbas, Ibn Mas'ud, and a group of the Companions. For this reason, Ibn Abbas used to think that he knew the interpretation of the Quran.

Against this: Even if it were correct, there is no evidence in it for restricting the metaphorical to the abrogated verses. For the properties Allah mentioned regarding the following of the metaphorical—seeking sedition and seeking interpretation—are present in many verses other than the abrogated ones, such as the verses of attributes and acts. Furthermore, this view would necessitate the existence of a "middle category" (الواسطة) between the decisive and the metaphorical.

In what is narrated from Ibn Abbas, there is an indication that his view on the decisive and the metaphorical is broader than what applies [only] to the abrogating and the abrogated, and that he only mentioned them as examples. In al-Durr al-Manthur: Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Ibn Abi Hatim narrated through the path of Ali from Ibn Abbas, who said: "The decisive verses are its abrogating [verses], its lawful, its prohibited, its 'legal punishments' (حدوده), its 'obligatory duties' (فرائضه), and what is believed in. The metaphorical verses are its abrogated [verses], its 'preceded' (مقدمه), its 'delayed' (مؤخره), its parables, its oaths, and what is believed in but not acted upon."

Fifth: That the "decisive verses" are those whose evidence is clear and manifest, such as the evidences for "Oneness" (الوحدانية), power, and wisdom; and the "metaphorical verses" are those that require reflection and contemplation to be known.

Against this: If what is meant by the evidence being "clear and manifest" or "requiring reflection and contemplation" is that the content of the verse has a "rational evidence" (دليل عقلي) close to "self-evidence" (البداهة) or is self-evident, and the absence of such, then its consequence would be that the verses of rulings, obligatory duties, and the like would be among the metaphorical due to their lack of a manifest, clear rational evidence. In that case, following them would be blameworthy, even though they are obligatory to follow. If what is meant is its having a clear, manifest evidence from the Book itself, and the absence of such, then all verses are on the same footing in this regard. How could it be otherwise? For it is a Book that is "self-consistent [in its parts] and repeated," a "light," and "manifest." This would necessitate that everything is decisive and that the metaphorical counterpart to it is removed from the Book, which is contrary to the assumption and contrary to the text.

Sixth: That the "decisive" is everything for which it is possible to attain knowledge through a manifest or hidden evidence; and the "metaphorical" is that for which there is no way to knowledge, such as the time of the onset of the Hour and the like.

Against this: "Decisiveness" and "similarity/ambiguity" are two attributes of a verse of the Book in terms of its being a "verse" (آية)—that is, indicating one of the divine knowledges. That which a verse of the Book indicates is not without a way [to know it], nor is it impossible to understand, either by itself or by joining it to something else. How could there be an intended matter from the wording of the verse that cannot be reached through the wording? Especially since He described His Book as "guidance," "light," and "manifest," and that it is in a position to be understood by the disbelievers, let alone the believers, where He said: تَنزِيلٌ مِّنَ الرَّحْمَٰنِ الرَّحِيمِ كِتَابٌ فُصِّلَتْ آيَاتُهُ قُرْآنًا عَرَبِيًّا لِّقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ بَشِيرًا وَنَذِيرًا فَأَعْرَضَ أَكْثَرُهُمْ فَهُمْ لَا يَسْمَعُونَ (A revelation from the All-beneficent, the Most Merciful. A Book whose verses have been elaborated, an Arabic Quran for a people who have knowledge, as a bearer of good news and as a warner. But most of them have turned away, so they do not listen) [Fussilat: 2-4]. And the Exalted said: أَفَلَا يَتَدَبَّرُونَ الْقُرْآنَ وَلَوْ كَانَ مِنْ عِندِ غَيْرِ اللَّهِ لَوَجَدُوا فِيهِ اخْتِلَافًا كَثِيرًا (Do they not contemplate the Quran? Had it been from [someone] other than Allah, they would have surely found much discordance in it) [An-Nisa: 82]. Thus, what a verse of the Book addresses is not impossible to understand, nor is standing upon it impossible. As for that which there is no way to stand upon, such as the time of the onset of the Hour and other things in the "Hidden Unseen" (الغيب المكنون), no verse among the verses has addressed its exposition with its wording such that it would be called "metaphorical."

Furthermore, in this view, there is a confusion between the meaning of the "metaphorical" and the "interpretation" of the verse, as previously mentioned.

Seventh: That the "decisive verses" are the verses of rulings, and the "metaphorical verses" are everything else that some parts of it "divert" (يصرف) others. This view is attributed to Mujahid and others.

And therein lies the critique: if the intended meaning of the "diversion" (الصرف) he mentioned is anything that aids in specifying the intended meaning of a word—such that it encompasses things like specification by a particularizer (التخصيص بالمخصص), qualification by a qualifier (التقييد بالمقيد), and all other situational context clues (القرائن المقامية)—then the verses of legal rulings (آيات الأحكام) would also be ambiguous (متشابهات) just like others. However, if it refers specifically to that which possesses no ambiguity in its indication of the intended meaning, nor a multiplicity of possibilities, such that the intended meaning is determined by the verse itself, and the meaning of others is determined through its mediation, then the necessary consequence of everything other than the verses of legal rulings being ambiguous is that no knowledge (العلم) would be attained regarding any of the Quranic sciences (معارف القرآن) besides the rulings. This is because, by definition, there would be no clear (محكمة) verse among them to which the ambiguous ones could be referred and through which their meanings could be clarified.

Eighth: That the clear (المحكم) among the verses is that which admits of only one aspect of interpretation (التأويل), while the ambiguous (المتشابه) is that which admits of many aspects of interpretation. This is attributed to al-Shafi‘i. It is as if the intended meaning is that the clear is what has no apparent meaning (ظهور) except in one sense—like the explicit text (النص) or that which is very strong in its apparent meaning—while the ambiguous is its opposite.

And therein lies the critique: this does nothing more than substitute one word for another. He has merely replaced the term "clear" (المحكم) with "that which has only one meaning," and "ambiguous" (المتشابه) with "that which admits of many meanings." Furthermore, he has taken interpretation (التأويل) to mean explanation (التفسير)—that is, the meaning intended by the word—and you have already learned that this is an error. If interpretation were identical to explanation, there would be no reason for its knowledge to be exclusive to Allah, or to Allah and those firmly grounded in knowledge (الراسخون في العلم). For the Quran explains itself, and in this regard, the believer, the disbeliever, those firmly grounded in knowledge, and those with perversity in their hearts are all equal.

Ninth: That the clear (المحكم) is what has been made firm and detailed regarding the accounts of the Prophets with their nations, while the ambiguous (المتشابه) is that whose wording regarding their stories has become similar through repetition in multiple chapters (سور). The necessary consequence of this view is that the division is restricted only to the verses of stories (آيات القصص).

And therein lies the critique: there is absolutely no evidence for this restriction. Moreover, the characteristic Allah mentioned for the clear and the ambiguous—namely, the seeking of discord (ابتغاء الفتنة) and the seeking of interpretation (ابتغاء التأويل) by following the ambiguous rather than the clear—does not apply to this definition. This characteristic is found in verses other than the stories just as it is found within them; it is found in a single story, such as the story of the appointment of vicegerency (الخلافة) on earth, just as it is found in repeated stories.

Tenth: That the ambiguous (المتشابه) is what requires clarification (بيان), and the clear (المحكم) is its opposite. This view is attributed to Imam Ahmad.

And therein lies the critique: the verses of legal rulings (آيات الأحكام) require the clarification of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم, despite being definitively among the clear verses (المحكمات) as has been explained repeatedly. Likewise, the abrogated verses (الآيات المنسوخة) are among the ambiguous, as previously mentioned, despite not requiring clarification because they are analogous to all other verses of legal rulings.

Eleventh: That the clear (المحكم) is what is believed in and acted upon, while the ambiguous (المتشابه) is what is believed in but not acted upon. This is attributed to Ibn Taymiyyah. Perhaps the intended meaning is that reports (الأخبار) are ambiguous while injunctions (الإنشاءات) are clear, as some have inferred; otherwise, it would not be a distinct opinion, as it could correctly fall under several of the aforementioned views.

And therein lies the critique: its necessary consequence is that everything other than the verses of legal rulings is ambiguous. This further implies that it would be impossible to attain knowledge of any divine sciences (المعارف الإلهية) outside of the rulings, since no action is realized in them, and there would be no clear verse among them to which the ambiguous could be referred. From another perspective, the abrogated verses (الآيات المنسوخة) are injunctions (إنشاءات), yet they are definitively not among the clear verses (المحكمات).

It appears that his intended meaning regarding belief and action for the clear, and belief without action for the ambiguous, is what the wording of the verse indicates: "As for those in whose hearts is perversity, they follow what is ambiguous of it..." and "those firmly grounded in knowledge say: 'We believe in it; all is from our Lord.'" However, since both matters—namely, belief and action together for the clear, and belief alone for the ambiguous—are duties for everyone who believes in the Book, one must first distinguish the clear from the ambiguous to fulfill one's duty. Therefore, knowing the clear and the ambiguous through these two [belief and action] is not sufficient to identify their specific instances (مصاديق), which is obvious.

Twelfth: That the ambiguous verses (المتشابهات) are specifically the verses of attributes (آيات الصفات), whether they pertain to the attributes of Allah, the Glorified—such as the All-Knowing (العليم), the All-Powerful (القدير), the All-Wise (الحكيم), and the All-Aware (الخبير)—or the attributes of His Prophets, such as His saying, the Exalted, regarding Jesus son of Mary (peace be upon them both): وَكَلِمَتُهُ أَلْقَاهَا إِلَىٰ مَرْيَمَ وَرُوحٌ مِّنْهُ — "and His Word that He cast toward Mary and a spirit from Him" (4:171), and similar verses. This is attributed to Ibn Taymiyyah.

And therein lies the critique: even if we concede that the verses of attributes are among the ambiguous, there is no evidence for restricting the ambiguous to them.

What appears from some of his quoted discourse, despite its length, is as follows: He takes "clear" (المحكم) and "ambiguous" (المتشابه) in their linguistic sense: that whose indication is made firm (احكمت دلالته) and that whose possibilities are similar (تشابهت احتمالاته). Both meanings are relative; thus, the indication of a verse might be ambiguous to one group, such as the common folk, while others—the scholars—know it through research. This meaning is most apparent in the verses of attributes, for their intended meanings are ambiguous to most people because their understandings are incapable of rising beyond the sensory realm. Consequently, they imagine that what Allah, the Exalted, has affirmed for Himself—such as knowledge, power, hearing, sight, pleasure, anger, hand, eye, and so on—are corporeal matters or meanings that are not the truth. Thus, discords (الفتن) arise, innovations (البدع) appear, and sects (المذاهب) are born. This, then, is the meaning of the clear and the ambiguous. Both are among those things for which knowledge can be attained. What cannot be reached or known is the interpretation (تأويل) of the ambiguous verses in the sense of the reality (حقيقة) of the meanings indicated by verses like those of the attributes. Suppose we know the meaning of His saying: "Indeed, Allah has power over all things," and "Indeed, Allah is All-Knowing of all things," and the like; we still do not know the reality of His knowledge, His power, the rest of His attributes, and the modality of His specific acts. This is the interpretation of the ambiguous verses which none knows but Allah, the Exalted. (End of summary). What pertains to his discourse will be discussed when we speak about interpretation (التأويل), Allah willing.

Thirteenth: That the clear (المحكم) is that to which the intellect (العقل) has a path, and the ambiguous (المتشابه) is the opposite.

And therein lies the critique: this is a claim without evidence. Although Quranic verses are indeed divided into those to which the intellect has a path and those to which it does not, this does not necessitate that the intended meaning of "clear" and "ambiguous" in this verse is to exhaustively cover this division. None of the descriptions of the clear and the ambiguous mentioned in the verse apply to this definition correctly. Furthermore, it is invalidated by the verses of legal rulings (آيات الأحكام), for they are clear (محكمة) yet the intellect has no path to them.

Fourteenth: That the clear (المحكم) is that by which its apparent meaning (ظاهر) is intended, and the ambiguous (المتشابه) is that by which something other than its apparent meaning is intended. This is a common view among later researchers, and upon it they base their technical definition of interpretation (التأويل): that it is the meaning that contradicts the apparent sense of the speech. It is also seemingly the intended meaning of those who said: "The clear is that whose interpretation is its revelation (تنزيله), and the ambiguous is that which is not grasped except through interpretation."

And therein lies the critique: this is a mere technical convention (اصطلاح) that does not fit the description of the clear and the ambiguous in the verse. The ambiguous is only ambiguous in terms of the similarity of its intended meaning and its referent. Interpretation (التأويل) is not the meaning intended from the ambiguous such that the ambiguous would be distinguished from the clear by having an interpretation. Rather, interpretation in the verse is a matter that encompasses all Quranic verses—both clear and ambiguous—as previously explained. Furthermore, there is no verse in the Quran in which something contradicting its apparent meaning is intended. Those verses that give such an illusion are intended for meanings provided to them by other clear verses; the Quran explains itself. It is well known that the meaning provided by context clues (القرائن)—whether connected or detached—to a word does not fall outside of its apparent meaning (ظهور), especially in the speech of a speaker who has explicitly stated that his habit is to speak in a way where parts are connected to one another and bear witness to one another, and every apparent difference or conflict is removed through contemplation (التدبر). The Exalted said: أَفَلَا يَتَدَبَّرُونَ الْقُرْآنَ وَلَوْ كَانَ مِنْ عِندِ غَيْرِ اللَّهِ لَوَجَدُوا فِيهِ اخْتِلَافًا كَثِيرًا — "Do they not contemplate the Quran? Had it been from [someone] other than Allah, they would surely have found much discordance in it" (4:82).

Fifteenth: What is narrated from al-Asamm: that the clear (المحكم) is that whose interpretation is agreed upon, and the ambiguous (المتشابه) is that which is disputed. It is as if the intended meaning of consensus (الإجماع) and disagreement (الاختلاف) is whether the referent of the verse is such that views differ regarding it or not.

And therein lies the critique: this would necessitate that the entire Book is ambiguous, which contradicts the division mentioned in the verse. For there is no verse in the Book except that there is some disagreement regarding it: either in its wording, its meaning, or whether it possesses an apparent meaning or not. Some have even gone so far as to claim the entire Quran is ambiguous, citing His saying, the Exalted: كِتَابًا مُّتَشَابِهًا — "a Book self-consistent" (39:23), neglecting the fact that this very deduction of theirs relies on the verse they cited being clear (محكمة), which contradicts their own claim. Others have gone so far as to say that the apparent sense of the Book is not a proof (حجة)—meaning it has no apparent sense.

Sixteenth: That the ambiguous (المتشابه) is that whose explanation (تفسير) is problematic due to its resemblance to something else, whether the problem arises from the wording or the meaning. This was mentioned by al-Raghib.

He said in Mufradat al-Quran: "The ambiguous (المتشابه) of the Quran is that whose explanation is problematic due to its resemblance to something else, either in terms of wording or meaning. The jurists said: The ambiguous is that whose apparent sense does not inform of its intended meaning. The reality of this is that verses, when considered in relation to one another, are of three types: absolutely clear (محكم على الإطلاق), absolutely ambiguous (متشابه على الإطلاق), and clear from one aspect while ambiguous from another.

The ambiguous, in general, is of three types: ambiguous from the aspect of wording only, ambiguous from the aspect of meaning only, and ambiguous from both aspects. The ambiguous from the aspect of wording is of two kinds: one refers to individual words, either due to their rarity—such as al-abb (الأبّ) and yaziffun (يزفّون)—or due to shared wording (homonymy), such as 'hand' (اليد) and 'eye' (العين). The second refers to the composite speech as a whole, which is of three types: a type due to brevity of speech, such as 'And if you fear that you may not deal fairly with the orphans, then marry such women as seem good to you'; a type due to expansiveness of speech, such as 'There is nothing like unto Him' (ليس كمثله شيء), for if it were said 'There is nothing like Him' (ليس مثله شيء), it would be clearer to the listener; and a type due to the arrangement of the speech, such as 'He sent down the Book to His servant and did not make in it any crookedness, [making it] upright' (أنزل على عبده الكتاب ولم يجعل له عوجاً قيماً), the intended order being 'the Book upright and did not make in it any crookedness,' and His saying: 'Were it not for believing men...' until His saying: 'had they been separate.'"

The ambiguous from the aspect of meaning includes the attributes of Allah, the Exalted, and the descriptions of the Day of Resurrection, for those attributes cannot be conceptualized by us, since no image occurs in our souls of what we have not sensed or what is not of the same genus as what we have sensed.

The ambiguous from the aspect of both meaning and wording together is of five types: First, from the aspect of quantity, such as the general (العموم) and the specific (الخصوص), like 'kill the polytheists.' Second, from the aspect of quality, such as obligation (الوجوب) and recommendation (الندب), like 'marry such as seem good to you.' Third, from the aspect of time, such as the abrogating (الناسخ) and the abrogated (المنسوخ), like 'be wary of Allah with the wariness due to Him.' Fourth, from the aspect of place or the matters regarding which they were revealed, such as 'It is not righteousness that you should come into houses from their backs,' and His saying: 'The postponement [of a sacred month] is only an increase in unfaith,' for one who does not know their customs in the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية) will find it impossible to know the explanation of this verse. Fifth, from the aspect of conditions by which an act is valid or invalid, such as the conditions of prayer and marriage.

When this whole is conceptualized, it becomes known that everything the commentators have mentioned in explaining the ambiguous does not fall outside these divisions—such as the view of those who said the ambiguous is Alif Lam Mim, the view of Qatadah that the clear is the abrogating and the ambiguous is the abrogated, and the view of al-Asamm that the clear is what is agreed upon and the ambiguous is what is disputed.

Furthermore, all ambiguous verses are of three types: a type for which there is no way to come to know it, such as the time of the Hour, the emergence of the Beast of the Earth, the modality of the Beast, and the like; a type for which man has a way to know it, such as rare words and obscure rulings; and a type that wavers between the two, which some of those firmly grounded in knowledge (الراسخون في العلم) may be specifically granted the knowledge of its reality while it remains hidden from those below them. This is the type referred to by his saying (peace be upon him) regarding ‘Ali (may Allah be pleased with him): 'O Allah, give him deep understanding of the religion and teach him the interpretation (التأويل),' and his similar saying to Ibn ‘Abbas." (End of his discourse). This is the most comprehensive of views regarding the meaning of the ambiguous, as he combined several of the aforementioned opinions within it.

And therein lies the critique: First: His generalization of the ambiguous to include instances of linguistic ambiguity—such as rarity of wording, obscurity of composition, generality and specificity, and the like—is not supported by the apparent sense of the verse. For the verse has made the clear verses (المحكمات) a reference point (مرجع) to which the ambiguous verses are returned. It is well known that the knot of a rare word and its like is not untied through the indication of the clear verses; rather, they have another reference point to which they return and through which they become clear.

Also, the verse describes the ambiguous verses as being such that they are followed for the seeking of discord (ابتغاء الفتنة). It is well known that following a general term without referring to its particularizer, or an absolute term without referring to its qualifier, or taking a rare word while turning away from what explains it in the language, is contrary to the method of the people of the tongue; their natural disposition does not permit it. Therefore, it would not naturally cause the stirring of discord because the language does not support it.

Second: His division of the ambiguous into what can be understood by the general public, what cannot be understood by anyone, and what can be understood by some but not others, clearly shows that he views interpretation (التأويل) as being exclusive to the ambiguous. You have already learned the contrary.

These are the well-known views regarding the meaning of the clear and the ambiguous and the distinction between their instances, and you have seen their flaws. You have also seen that what appears from the verse—in its clarity and the radiance of its light—is contrary to all of that. What the verse provides regarding the meaning of the ambiguous (المتشابه) is that the verse, while maintaining its status as a verse indicating a meaning, is doubtful (مريب) and wavering (مردد)—not from the aspect of wording such that it could be treated by the methods familiar to the people of the tongue, like referring the general and absolute to the particularizer and qualifier and the like—but rather from the aspect of its meaning being inconsistent with the meaning of another clear (محكمة) verse about which there is no doubt and which clarifies the state of the ambiguous one.

It is well known that the meaning of a verse does not possess this description unless the meaning being followed is familiar and intimate to common understandings, such that simple minds hasten to believe it, or the interpretation sought for the verse is closer to the acceptance of these understandings that are weak in perception and intellection.

If you trace the innovations (البدع), whims (الأهواء), and false sects (المذاهب الفاسدة) through which the Islamic factions deviated from the straight truth after the time of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم—whether in the sciences (المعارف) or the rulings (الأحكام)—you will find that most of their instances stem from following the ambiguous and interpreting the verses in a way that Allah, the Glorified, does not approve.

One faction clings to verses of the Quran for anthropomorphism (التجسيم), another for predestination (الجبر), another for delegation (التفويض), another for the lapses of the Prophets, another for absolute transcendence by denying attributes, and another for pure resemblance (التشبيه) and the addition of attributes, and so on. All of this is due to taking the ambiguous without referring it to the clear verse that governs it.

One group stated that religious rulings were only legislated to be a path to "attainment" (الوصول); thus, if there were a closer path, its pursuit would be mandatory for whoever embarks upon it, for the objective is merely attainment by whatever path happens to be available and easy. Another group said that religious obligation (التكليف) is only for reaching perfection, and there is no meaning for its remaining after perfection once attainment is realized; thus, there is no obligation for the "perfected" one.

The rulings, obligations, prescribed punishments (الحدود), and all other Islamic policies were established and implemented during the era of the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم, with none of them being neglected. Then, after his departure صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم, they did not cease to diminish and fall away, ruling by ruling, day by day, at the hands of Islamic governments. No ruling or punishment was abolished except that those who abolished it made the excuse that religion was only legislated for the welfare of the world and the reform of the people, and what they innovated was more beneficial for the state of the people today. This continued until it reached the point where it is said: "The sole purpose of religious laws is to reform the world by implementing them, and the world today does not accept religious policy nor can it digest it; rather, it requires the enactment of laws that today's civilization approves and implements." And it reached the point where it is said: "Engaging in religious acts is to purify the hearts and guide them toward righteous thought and will; hearts trained by social upbringing and souls dedicated to the service of creation have no need for purification through things like ablution (الوضوء), ritual bathing (الغسل), prayer (الصلاة), and fasting (الصوم)."

If you reflect on these and their like—and they are countless—and contemplate His saying, the Exalted: "As for those in whose hearts is perversity, they follow what is ambiguous of it, seeking discord and seeking its interpretation..." you will not doubt the correctness of what we have mentioned. You will judge that these discords and tribulations that have afflicted Islam and the Muslims did not take root except through the path of following the ambiguous and seeking the interpretation of the Quran.

This—and Allah knows best—is the reason for the Quran's severe emphasis on this matter and its extreme insistence on prohibiting the following of the ambiguous, the seeking of discord and interpretation, the deviation (الإلحاد) regarding the signs of Allah, speaking about them without knowledge, and following the footsteps of Satan. For it is the habit of the Quran to be excessive in its severity in matters through which a pillar of the religion might be breached, leading to the collapse of its structure—such as the severity regarding taking disbelievers as allies, the love for the near of kin, the staying of the Prophet's wives [in their houses], the dealing in usury, the unity of the word in religion, and so on.

Nothing washes the stain of perversity (الزيغ) from the hearts, nor blocks the path of seeking discord—both of which originate from leaning toward the world, clinging to the earth, and following whims—except the remembrance of the Day of Reckoning, as the Exalted said: وَلَا تَتَّبِعِ الْهَوَىٰ فَيُضِلَّكَ عَن سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَضِلُّونَ عَن سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ شَدِيدٌ بِمَا نَسُوا يَوْمَ الْحِسَابِ — "and do not follow desire, or it will lead you astray from the way of Allah. Indeed those who stray from the way of Allah—there is a severe punishment for them because they forgot the Day of Reckoning" (38:26). Therefore, you see those firmly grounded in knowledge (الراسخون في العلم)—who refuse to interpret the Quran in a way that their Lord does not approve—referring to this at the conclusion of their statement when they say: "Our Lord, indeed You are the gatherer of mankind for a Day about which there is no doubt; indeed Allah does not fail the appointment."

2 - What is the meaning of the clear verses being the "Mother of the Book"?

A group mentioned that the clear verses (الآيات المحكمة) being the Mother of the Book (أم الكتاب) means they are the foundation (الأصل) in the Book upon which the rules and pillars of the religion are built; thus, they are believed in and acted upon. Religion is nothing but the sum of belief and action. As for the ambiguous verses (الآيات المتشابهة), due to the instability of their intended meaning and the similarity of their referents, they are not acted upon; rather, they are only believed in with faith.

By reflecting on the aforementioned views, you will know that this is a necessary consequence of some of them—specifically those that hold that the ambiguous only became ambiguous because it contains an interpretation (تأويل) that is impossible to reach or understand, or that the ambiguous can be known and its ambiguity removed partially or totally by referring to the intellect, language, or a rational method relied upon to remove linguistic doubts.

Others said: The meaning of the motherhood of the clear verses is the return of the ambiguous verses to them. Their statements differ in explaining this "return." The apparent meaning of some is that the return means restricting the ambiguous verses to belief and practical following in their instances to the clear—like an abrogated verse (الآية المنسوخة) which is believed in, but in its instance, one returns to acting upon the abrogating verse (الناسخة). This view does not differ much from the first. The apparent meaning of others is that it means the clear verses clarify the ambiguous ones, removing their ambiguity.

The truth is the third meaning. The meaning of motherhood (الأمومة) indicated by His saying: "they are the Mother of the Book"—the verse—contains an additional nuance and is more specific than the meaning of "foundation" (الأصل) by which "Mother" was explained in the first view. In this word—that is, the word "Mother" (الأم)—there is a nuance of a return that involves emergence, derivation, and partiality. Thus, the word is not devoid of indicating that the ambiguous verses have referents that return to and branch out from the clear verses. Its necessary consequence is that the clear verses clarify the ambiguous ones.

Furthermore, the ambiguous was only ambiguous due to the similarity of its intended meaning, not because it possesses an interpretation (تأويل). For interpretation, as previously mentioned, exists for the clear just as it exists for the ambiguous. The Quran explains itself; thus, the ambiguous has an explainer, and it is the clear. An example of this is His saying, the Exalted: إِلَىٰ رَبِّهَا نَاظِرَةٌ — "looking at their Lord" (75:23). This is an ambiguous verse. By referring it to His saying, the Exalted: لَيْسَ كَمِثْلِهِ شَيْءٌ — "Nothing is like Him" (42:11), and His saying, the Exalted: لَّا تُدْرِكُهُ الْأَبْصَارُ — "The sights do not perceive Him" (6:103), it becomes clear that what is intended is a looking and a seeing that is not of the genus of sensory, physical sight. The Exalted has said: مَا كَذَبَ الْفُؤَادُ مَا رَأَىٰ أَفَتُمَارُونَهُ عَلَىٰ مَا يَرَىٰ until He said: لَقَدْ رَأَىٰ مِنْ آيَاتِ رَبِّهِ الْكُبْرَىٰ — "The heart did not belie what it saw. Will you then dispute with him about what he saw?... He certainly saw some of the greatest signs of his Lord" (53:11-18). Thus, He affirmed for the heart a vision specific to it. This is not "thought" (الفكر), for thought only pertains to assent (التصديق) and mental composition, whereas vision (الرؤية) pertains to the individual objective reality (المفرد العيني). It thus becomes clear that it is an orientation of the heart that is neither sensory-material nor intellectual-mental. The matter follows this pattern in all other ambiguous verses.

3 - What is the meaning of interpretation (التأويل)?

A group of commentators explained interpretation (التأويل) as explanation (التفسير)—which is the meaning intended by the speech. Since the intended meaning of some verses is known by necessity, the meaning of interpretation according to this view in His saying, the Exalted: "...and seeking its interpretation, and none knows its interpretation except Allah..."—the verse—is the meaning intended by the ambiguous verse. Thus, there is no path to knowledge of the ambiguous verses according to this view for anyone other than Allah, the Glorified, or for anyone other than Him and those firmly grounded in knowledge (الراسخون في العلم).

Another group said: The intended meaning of interpretation (التأويل) is the meaning that contradicts the apparent sense of the word. This meaning has become so common that the word has become a "second literal meaning" (حقيقة ثانية) for it, after it had been, according to the word's origin, a meaning of absolute "returning" (الإرجاع) or "the point of return" (المرجع).

In any case, this meaning is the one common among later scholars, just as the first meaning was common among the early commentators—whether those who said that interpretation is known only to Allah, or those who said that those firmly grounded in knowledge also know it, as is narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas that he used to say: "I am among those firmly grounded in knowledge, and I know its interpretation."

Another group went toward the view that interpretation (التأويل) is a meaning among the meanings of the verse that none knows but Allah, the Exalted, or none knows but Allah and those firmly grounded in knowledge, without it being contrary to the apparent sense of the word. The matter thus returns to the idea that the ambiguous verse has multiple meanings, one beneath the other: some are beneath the word and are grasped by all understandings, and some are further away and are not grasped except by Allah, the Glorified, or by Him and those firmly grounded in knowledge.

They differed in their views on how these meanings are connected to the word. It is certain that, insofar as they are intended by the word, they are not on the same horizontal plane; otherwise, it would necessitate using a word in more than one meaning, which is not permissible as established in its proper place. Therefore, they are inevitably meanings arranged vertically (في الطول). It was said: they are the implications (لوازم) of the word's meaning, but they are implications arranged such that the word has a literal meaning, which has an implication, which in turn has an implication, and so on. It was also said: they are meanings arranged one upon the other like the arrangement of the inner (الباطن) upon the outer (الظاهر). Thus, intending the customary, familiar meaning is an intention for the meaning of the word and an intention for its inner depth by the very same intention for itself. Just as when you say: "Give me a drink," you only seek the act of giving a drink, which is itself a seeking of quenching thirst, a seeking to fulfill an existential need, and a seeking for existential perfection. There are not four separate commands and objectives; rather, the single seeking related to giving a drink is related by its very nature to these matters, some of which are within the inner depth of others, and the act of giving a drink is connected to them and relies upon them.

There is a fourth view: that interpretation (التأويل) is not of the genus of meanings intended by the word; rather, it is the objective reality (الأمر العيني) upon which the speech relies. If the speech is an injunction (حكم إنشائي) like a command or prohibition, its interpretation is the benefit (المصلحة) that necessitates the initiation of the ruling and its legislation. Thus, the interpretation of His saying: "Establish the prayer," for example, is the external luminous state that arises in the soul of the one praying in the external world, which then forbids him from indecency and wrongdoing. If the speech is a report (خبري), then if it is a report about past events, its interpretation is the very event that occurred in the past, such as the verses containing the accounts of the Prophets and past nations; their interpretation is the very events that took place in the past. If it is a report about current or future events and matters, it is of two types: either the reported matter is something grasped by the senses or perceived by the intellects, in which case its interpretation is also what exists in the external world of the occurring event, such as His saying, the Exalted: وَفِيكُمْ سَمَّاعُونَ لَهُمْ — "and among you are some who listen to them" (9:47), and His saying, the Exalted: غُلِبَتِ الرُّومُ فِي أَدْنَى الْأَرْضِ وَهُم مِّن بَعْدِ غَلَبِهِمْ سَيَغْلِبُونَ فِي بِضْعِ سِنِينَ — "The Romans have been defeated in a nearby territory, but following their defeat they will be victorious in a few years" (30:2-4). Or, it is of the future matters of the Unseen (الغيب) that our worldly senses do not grasp and our intellects do not perceive their reality—such as matters related to the Day of Resurrection, the time of the Hour, the resurrection of the dead, the gathering, the questioning, the reckoning, and the flying of the books—or it is something outside the genus of time and the perception of intellects, such as the reality of His attributes and His acts, the Exalted; their interpretation is also their very external realities.

The difference between this category—namely, the verses clarifying the state of Allah's attributes and acts and what follows them of the states of the Day of Resurrection and the like—and the other categories is that the other categories can have their interpretation known, unlike this category, for none knows the reality of its interpretation but Allah, the Exalted. Yes, it is possible for those firmly grounded in knowledge (الراسخون في العلم) to attain some of it through Allah's teaching, to the extent that their intellects can encompass, but as for the reality of the matter which is the true interpretation, it is among that which Allah, the Glorified, has reserved for His own knowledge.

This is what is summarized from their schools of thought regarding the meaning of interpretation, and they are four.

There are other views they mentioned which are, in reality, branches of the first view, even if those who hold them shy away from accepting it.

Among them: that explanation (التفسير) is more general than interpretation (التأويل), and its use is more frequent regarding words and their individual components, while the use of interpretation is more frequent regarding meanings and sentences; and interpretation is most used regarding the Divine Books, while explanation is used regarding them and others.

Among them: that explanation (التفسير) is the clarification of the meaning of a word that admits of only one aspect, while interpretation (التأويل) is the identification of one of the possibilities of the word through evidence by way of deduction (استنباط).

Among them: that explanation (التفسير) is the clarification of the certain meaning of the word, while interpretation (التأويل) is the weighing of one of the possible meanings that are not certain; this is close to the previous one.

Among them: that explanation (التفسير) is the clarification of the evidence for the intended meaning, while interpretation (التأويل) is the clarification of the reality of the intended meaning. An example: His saying, the Exalted: "Indeed, your Lord is surely on the watch (لبالمرصاد)." Its explanation is that al-mirsad (المرصاد) is a noun of place from their saying: rasada yarsudu when he watched. Its interpretation is the warning against being negligent regarding the command of Allah and being heedless of Him.

Among them: that explanation (التفسير) is the clarification of the apparent meaning of the word, while interpretation (التأويل) is the clarification of the problematic meaning.

Among them: that explanation (التفسير) pertains to narration (الرواية), while interpretation (التأويل) pertains to deep understanding (الدراية).

Among them: that explanation (التفسير) pertains to following and hearing, while interpretation (التأويل) pertains to deduction and reflection. These seven views are, in reality, branches of the first view we quoted, and what applies to it applies to them. In any case, it is not correct to rely on any of these four views and what branches from them.

As for the summary: because you have already learned that the intended meaning of a verse's interpretation (تأويل) is not a concept among concepts indicated by the verse—whether it contradicts its apparent sense or agrees with it—rather, it is of the genus of external matters. Nor is it just any external matter such that the external instance (المصداق الخارجي) of a report would be its interpretation; rather, it is a specific external matter whose relationship to the speech is like the relationship of the symbolized to the symbol (الممثل إلى المثل) and the inner (الباطن) to the outer (الظاهر).

As for the detail: the first view is critiqued because the least that follows from it is that some Quranic verses would have their interpretation—that is, their explanation, that is, the intended meaning of their verbal indications—not grasped by general understandings. There are no such verses in the Quran; rather, the Quran explicitly states that it was only sent down as a Quran so that understandings might grasp it. The proponent of this view has no choice but to opt for the view that the ambiguous verses (الآيات المتشابهة) are only the openings of the chapters (فواتح السور) consisting of the disjointed letters (الحروف المقطعة), where general understandings do not grasp their meanings. This is critiqued by the fact that there is no evidence for it. The mere fact that interpretation (التأويل) contains the meaning of "returning" and that explanation (التفسير) is also not devoid of the meaning of "returning" does not necessitate that interpretation is explanation, just as a mother is a point of return for her children but is not an interpretation for them, and a leader is a point of return for the subordinates but is not an interpretation for them.

Furthermore, the seeking of discord (ابتغاء الفتنة) was counted in the verse as a specific, independent characteristic of ambiguity, and it exists in verses other than the openings of the chapters; for most of the discords innovated in Islam only occurred by following the rationales of the rulings, the verses of attributes, and others.

As for the second view, it is critiqued because its necessary consequence is the existence of verses in the Quran in which meanings are intended that are contradicted by their apparent sense—which causes discord in the religion due to its conflict with the clear verses. This returns to the idea that there is discordance (اختلاف) in the Quran between verses that cannot be removed except by diverting some of them from their apparent meanings to meanings that general understandings do not grasp. This invalidates the argument in His saying, the Exalted: أَفَلَا يَتَدَبَّرُونَ الْقُرْآنَ وَلَوْ كَانَ مِنْ عِندِ غَيْرِ اللَّهِ لَوَجَدُوا فِيهِ اخْتِلَافًا كَثِيرًا — "Do they not contemplate the Quran? Had it been from [someone] other than Allah, they would surely have found much discordance in it" (4:82). For if the removal of discordance between one verse and another were by saying: "something other than what the apparent sense indicates was intended by one or both of them—rather, an interpretative meaning according to their technical term which none knows but Allah, for example"—the argument of the verse would not succeed. For the negation of discordance through "interpretation" in their technical sense is possible in any collection of speech, even if it were from other than Allah. It is obvious that every speech—even that which is definitively false and nonsensical—can be returned to truth and reality through interpretation and diversion from its apparent sense. Thus, the removal of discordance in this sense from a collection of speech does not indicate that it is the speech of One who transcends the changing of states, the contradiction of opinions, oversight, forgetfulness, error, and perfection through the passage of time—as is the meaning of the argument in the verse. Thus, the verse, by the tongue of its argument, is explicit that the Quran is exposed to general understandings and is a field for research, reflection, and contemplation; there is no verse in it in which a meaning contradicting the apparent sense of Arabic speech was intended, nor is there in it any riddle or obfuscation.

As for the third view, it is critiqued because the Quranic verses containing meanings arranged one above the other and one beneath the other is something that none denies except one deprived of the blessing of contemplation. However, all of them—especially if we say they are implications of the meaning—are verbal referents that differ in terms of understanding based on the sharpness of the contemplating listener or his dullness. This does not suit His saying, the Exalted, in describing interpretation: "and none knows its interpretation except Allah," for high sciences and subtle issues do not differ in minds based on piety and purity of the soul, but rather based on sharpness and its lack—even if piety and purity of the soul are aids in understanding pure divine sciences, that is not by way of contingency and causality as is the apparent sense of His saying: "and none knows its interpretation except Allah."

As for the fourth view, it is critiqued because although he was correct in some of his discourse, he erred in another part. While he was correct in saying that interpretation (التأويل) is not exclusive to the ambiguous but exists for the entire Quran, and that interpretation is not of the genus of verbal referents but is an external matter upon which the speech relies, he erred in counting every external matter connected to the content of the speech—even the instances of reports narrating past and future events—as an interpretation of the speech, and in restricting the ambiguous whose interpretation is not known to the verses of attributes and the verses of the Resurrection.

To clarify: the intended meaning of interpretation (التأويل) in His saying, the Exalted: "...and seeking its interpretation..." etc., would then either be the interpretation of the Quran—by returning its pronoun to the Book—in which case His saying: "and none knows its interpretation except Allah..." etc., would not be sound. For much of the Quran's interpretation—which is the interpretations of the stories, and even the rulings and the verses of ethics—is among what can be known by other than Him, the Exalted, and other than those firmly grounded in knowledge, even those with perversity in their hearts according to his view. For the events indicated by the verses of stories are perceived equally by all people without some being deprived of them. Likewise, the ethical realities and the benefits created by acting upon the rulings—such as acts of worship, transactions, and other legislated matters.

If the intended meaning of interpretation in it is the interpretation of the ambiguous only, the restriction in His saying: "and none knows its interpretation except Allah" etc., would be sound, and it would imply that those other than Him, the Exalted, and other than those firmly grounded in knowledge, for example, should not seek the interpretation of the ambiguous, as it leads to discord and the misguiding of people. However, there is no reason to restrict the ambiguous whose interpretation is not known to the verses of attributes and the Resurrection. For discord and misguidance, just as they exist in their interpretation, exist in the interpretation of others among the verses of rulings, stories, and others. For example, one might say (and it has been said) that the purpose of legislating rulings is to revive human society by reforming its affairs with what corresponds to welfare; thus, if it were assumed that the welfare of society lay in something other than the legislated ruling, or that it did not correspond to the welfare of the time, it would be mandatory to follow it and abolish the legislated religious ruling. Or one might say (and it has been said) that the miracles of the Prophets mentioned in the Quran are ordinary matters, and they were only narrated with words whose apparent sense is contrary to the norm for the sake of winning the hearts of the common folk, so that their souls would be attracted and their hearts would submit to what they imagine to be supernatural and overpowering to the laws of nature. There is much of these sayings in the sects that branched out and were innovated in Islam, and all of them are undoubtedly among the interpretation of the Quran for the seeking of discord. Thus, there is no reason to restrict the ambiguous to the verses of attributes and the verses of the Resurrection.

Once you know what has passed, you will know that the truth in the explanation of interpretation (التأويل) is that it is the actual reality (الحقيقة الواقعية) upon which the Quranic statements—whether a ruling, an exhortation, or a wisdom—rely. It exists for all Quranic verses: the clear and the ambiguous. It is not of the genus of concepts indicated by words; rather, it is of the objective matters (الأمور العينية) that are too exalted to be encompassed by the nets of words. Allah, the Glorified, only bound it with the bond of words to bring it somewhat closer to our minds. They are like parables (الأمثال) struck so that objectives may be brought closer and clarified according to what suits the understanding of the listener, as the Exalted said: وَالْكِتَابِ الْمُبِينِ إِنَّا جَعَلْنَاهُ قُرْآنًا عَرَبِيًّا لَّعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ وَإِنَّهُ فِي أُمِّ الْكِتَابِ لَدَيْنَا لَعَلِيٌّ حَكِيمٌ — "By the Manifest Book! We have made it an Arabic Quran so that you may exercise your reason. And it is indeed in the Mother of the Book with Us, surely exalted and wise" (43:2-4). In the Quran, there are explicit statements and allusions to this meaning.

Furthermore, you have already learned in the previous explanation that the Quran did not use the word "interpretation" (التأويل) in the instances it used it—which are sixteen instances according to what has been counted—except in the meaning we have mentioned.

4 - Does anyone other than Allah, the Glorified, know the interpretation of the Quran?

This issue is also among the instances of intense disagreement among the commentators. Its origin is the disagreement occurring between them in explaining His saying, the Exalted: "...and those firmly grounded in knowledge say: 'We believe in it; all is from our Lord...'"—the verse—and whether the "and" (الواو) is for conjunction (عطف) or for the commencement of a new sentence (استئناف). Some of the early scholars, the Shafi‘is, and most of the commentators from the Shi‘a went toward the view that the "and" is for conjunction and that those firmly grounded in knowledge (الراسخون في العلم) know the interpretation of the ambiguous of the Quran. Most of the early scholars and the Hanafis among the Sunnis went toward the view that it is for commencement and that none knows the interpretation of the ambiguous but Allah, and it is among that which Allah, the Glorified, has reserved for His own knowledge. The first group argued for its school of thought with many points and some narrations. The second group argued with other points and several narrations stating that the interpretation of the ambiguous is among what Allah, the Glorified, has reserved for His own knowledge. Each group persisted in contradicting the other and opposing its arguments.

What the researcher in this position should be mindful of is that the issue has not been free from confusion and ambiguity from the moment it began to circulate among them and became a subject of research and scrutiny. Thus, the return of the ambiguous to the clear—or in other words, the meaning intended by the ambiguous—became confused with the interpretation (تأويل) of the verse, as indicated by how we titled the issue and established the disagreement and the statement of each of the two parties just now.

Therefore, we have omitted the mention of the arguments of both parties due to the lack of benefit in proving or denying them after they were built upon confusion. As for the narrations, they are contrary to the apparent sense of the Book. The affirming narrations—that is, those indicating that those firmly grounded in knowledge know the interpretation—took interpretation as a synonym for the meaning intended by the word of the ambiguous, and there is no interpretation in the Quran in this sense.
