As is narrated from the paths of the Sunnis: that the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم prayed for Ibn ‘Abbas, saying: "O Allah, give him deep understanding of the religion and teach him the interpretation (التأويل)." And what is narrated of the statement of Ibn ‘Abbas: "I am among those firmly grounded in knowledge and I know its interpretation," and his statement: "The clear verses are the abrogating verses and the ambiguous are the abrogated," for the necessary consequence of these narrations according to what they understood is that the meaning of the clear verse would be an interpretation for the ambiguous verse, which is what we pointed out—that interpretation in this sense is not the subject of the verse's focus.

As for the negating narrations—that is, those indicating that other than Him does not know the interpretation of the ambiguous—such as what is narrated: that Ibn ‘Abbas used to recite: "and none knows its interpretation except Allah, and those firmly grounded in knowledge say: 'We believe in it...'" and likewise Ubayy ibn Ka‘b used to recite. And what is narrated that Ibn Mas‘ud used to recite: "and its interpretation is only with Allah, and those firmly grounded in knowledge say: 'We believe in it...'" these are not fit to prove anything. Firstly: because these recitations (قراءات) have no authority (حجية). Secondly: because the extent of their indication is that the verse does not indicate the knowledge of those firmly grounded in knowledge regarding the interpretation, and the verse's lack of indication for it is different from its indication of its absence, as is claimed; for it is possible that another evidence indicates it.

And like what is in al-Durr al-Manthur from al-Tabarani from Abu Malik al-Ash‘ari that he heard the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم saying: "I do not fear for my nation except three traits: that wealth becomes abundant for them so they envy one another and kill one another; that the Book is opened for them so the believer takes it seeking its interpretation, and none knows its interpretation except Allah; and that their knowledge increases so they waste it and do not care for it. 'All is from our Lord, and none takes admonition except the possessors of intellect,' and that their knowledge increases so they waste it and do not care for it." This hadith, on the assumption of its indication of negation, only indicates its negation for the absolute believer, not for those firmly grounded in knowledge specifically, and only the latter benefits the one arguing.

And like the narrations indicating the obligation of following the clear and believing in the ambiguous. Their lack of indication of negation is something about which there is no doubt.

And like what is in the Tafsir of al-Alusi from Ibn Jarir from Ibn ‘Abbas in a marfu‘ narration: "The Quran was sent down according to four aspects: lawful and prohibited, for which no one is excused for ignorance; an explanation (تفسير) that the scholars explain; and ambiguous (متشابه) that none knows but Allah, and whoever claims its knowledge besides Allah is a liar." The hadith, besides being marfu‘ and contradicted by what was narrated from him regarding the Prophet's prayer for him and his own claim of knowledge of it, is contrary to the apparent sense of the Quran: that interpretation is other than the meaning intended by the ambiguous, as you have learned in what has passed.

What ought to be said is: the Quran indicates the permissibility of the knowledge of its interpretation for other than Him, the Exalted, but as for this verse, it has no indication of that.

As for the second aspect: because of what passed in the previous explanation: that the verse, by the context of its beginning, its end, and the verses that follow it, is only in the position of clarifying the division of the Book into the clear and the ambiguous, and the divergence of people in taking them. They are between those who lean toward following the ambiguous due to perversity in their hearts, and those who are firm in following the clear and believing in the ambiguous due to the firmness of their knowledge. The primary intent in mentioning those firmly grounded in knowledge is to clarify their state and their method in taking the Quran and to praise them for it, in contrast to what was mentioned of the state of the perverse and their method and their censure. Anything beyond this amount is outside the primary intent, and there is no evidence for their partnership in the knowledge of interpretation along with that except for the unsound points whose mention has passed. Thus, the restriction indicated by His saying, the Exalted: "and none knows its interpretation except Allah" remains without any nullifier to nullify it—whether by conjunction, exception, or otherwise. So, what the verse indicates is the restriction of the knowledge of interpretation to Him, the Exalted, and its exclusivity to Him.

However, it does not contradict the indication of a detached evidence that indicates the knowledge of other than Him, the Exalted, regarding it by His permission, as in its analogues like the knowledge of the Unseen (الغيب). The Exalted said: قُل لَّا يَعْلَمُ مَن فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ الْغَيْبَ إِلَّا اللَّهُ — "Say, ‘No one in the heavens and the earth knows the Unseen except Allah’" (27:65). And the Exalted said: إِنَّمَا الْغَيْبُ لِلَّهِ — "The Unseen belongs only to Allah" (10:20). And the Exalted said: وَعِندَهُ مَفَاتِحُ الْغَيْبِ لَا يَعْلَمُهَا إِلَّا هُوَ — "With Him are the treasures of the Unseen; no one knows them except Him" (6:59). All of this indicated the restriction. Then the Exalted said: عَالِمُ الْغَيْبِ فَلَا يُظْهِرُ عَلَىٰ غَيْبِهِ أَحَدًا إِلَّا مَنِ ارْتَضَىٰ مِن رَّسُولٍ — "Knower of the Unseen, He does not disclose His Unseen to anyone, except to an apostle He approves of" (72:26-27). Thus, He affirmed that for some who are other than Him, and it is the approved messenger. There are analogues for this in the Quran.

As for the first aspect—which is that the Quran indicates the permissibility of the knowledge of its interpretation for other than Him, the Exalted, in general—its explanation is: the verses, as you have learned, indicate that the interpretation (تأويل) of a verse is an external matter whose relationship to the referent of the verse is like the relationship of the symbolized to the symbol (الممثل إلى المثل). Although it is not a referent of the verse by virtue of its indication, it is narrated by it and preserved within it by a type of narration and preservation. This is similar to your saying: "In the summer you wasted the milk" to someone who sought a matter after having previously missed its causes. The concept indicated by the wording of the proverb—which is the woman's wasting of the milk in the summer—does not apply to the instance at all. Yet, it is a symbol (ممثل) for the state of the addressee, preserving it and depicting it in the mind with an image contained within the image that the speech provides by its referent.

Likewise is the matter of interpretation. The external reality (الحقيقة الخارجية) that necessitates the legislation of a ruling among the rulings, or the clarification of a science among the divine sciences, or the occurrence of an event that is the content of a story among the Quranic stories—even if it is not a matter indicated by correspondence (المطابقة) by the command, prohibition, clarification, or the specific event itself—nevertheless, because the ruling, clarification, or event each emerges from it and appears through it, it is its effect that narrates it by a type of narration and allusion. Just as the master's saying to his servant, "Give me a drink," emerges from the requirement of human nature for its perfection; for this external reality is what requires the preservation of existence and survival, which requires the replacement of what is decomposed from the body, which requires the necessary food, which requires quenching thirst, which requires the command to give a drink, for example. Thus, the interpretation of his saying, "Give me a drink," is the state of human external nature in its requirement for perfection in its existence and survival. If this external reality were to change into something else that contradicts the first, for example, the ruling—which is the command to give a drink—would change into another ruling. Likewise, the act that is recognized and thus performed, or unrecognized and thus avoided, in one of the human societies—despite their vast differences in manners and customs—only suckles from the breast of the "beauty and ugliness" (الحسن والقبح) that they possess, which in turn relies upon a united, agreed-upon set of temporal and spatial causes, and previous habits and customs rooted in the mind of the actor through inheritance from those who preceded him and the repetition of observation from those who observed him.

from among the people of his region. Thus, this cause, whose parts are harmoniously integrated, is the interpretation (التأويل) — the underlying reality and ultimate objective — of his action or omission, without being identical to the action or omission itself; rather, it is depicted, contained, and preserved within the act or its absence. If one were to suppose a change in the social environment, that which he performed of action or omission would necessarily change.

Therefore, the matter that possesses an interpretation—whether it be a ruling (حكماً), a narrative (قصة), or an event (حادثة)—inevitably changes with the change of the interpretation. For this reason, you see that the Exalted, in His saying: فأما الذين في قلوبهم زيغ فيتبعون ما تشابه منه ابتغاء الفتنة وابتغاء تأويله وما يعلم تأويله إلا الله (As for those in whose hearts is deviance, they pursue what is ambiguous in it, courting unrest and seeking its interpretation, and no one knows its interpretation except Allah...), when He mentioned that those with deviance in their hearts follow what is not intended from the ambiguous (المتشابه) seeking unrest, He mentioned that they thereby seek its interpretation—which is not its true interpretation. This is only because if the interpretation they adopted were the true interpretation, their following of the ambiguous would have been a rightful following, not a blameworthy one; and the matter indicated by the clear (المحكم) — the foundational verses that are clear in meaning — which is the intended meaning of the ambiguous, would have shifted to the unintended meaning they understood from the ambiguous and followed.

Thus, it has become clear: that the interpretation (التأويل) of the Qur’an consists of external realities (حقائق خارجية) upon which the verses of the Qur’an rely for their gnosis (معارفها), their laws (شرائعها), and everything else they explain, such that if any of those realities were supposed to change, the contents within the verses would be overturned.

If you reflect deeply, you will find that this corresponds perfectly to the saying of the Exalted: وَالْكِتَابِ الْمُبِينِ إِنَّا جَعَلْنَاهُ قُرْآنًا عَرَبِيًّا لَّعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ وَإِنَّهُ فِي أُمِّ الْكِتَابِ لَدَيْنَا لَعَلِيٌّ حَكِيمٌ (By the Manifest Book! We have made it an Arabic Qur’an so that you may exercise your reason. And indeed it is with Us in the Mother of the Book, sublime and wise.) [Az-Zukhruf: 2-4]. This indicates that the revealed Qur’an was, with Allah, a matter more sublime and more consolidated (أحكم) than could be reached by intellects or subjected to fragmentation and separation. However, the Exalted, out of His care for His servants, made it a recited Book and clothed it in the garment of the Arabic language so that they might reason (يعقلون) that which they had no way of reasoning or knowing as long as it remained in the Mother of the Book (أم الكتاب). This "Mother of the Book" is that which is indicated by His saying: يَمْحُو اللَّهُ مَا يَشَاءُ وَيُثْبِتُ وَعِندَهُ أُمُّ الْكِتَابِ (Allah effaces and confirms whatever He wishes, and with Him is the Mother of the Book) [Ar-Ra'd: 39], and by His saying: بَلْ هُوَ قُرْآنٌ مَّجِيدٌ فِي لَوْحٍ مَّحْفُوظٍ (Indeed it is a glorious Qur’an, in a Preserved Tablet) [Al-Buruj: 21-22].

The general content of the verse is also indicated by the saying of the Exalted: كِتَابٌ أُحْكِمَتْ آيَاتُهُ ثُمَّ فُصِّلَتْ مِن لَّدُنْ حَكِيمٍ خَبِيرٍ (A Book whose signs have been made firm and then articulated by One who is all-wise, all-aware) [Hud: 1]. Thus, the "making firm" (الإحكام) refers to its state with Allah such that there is no breach or separation in it, while the "articulation" (التفصيل) is its being made part by part and verse by verse, and its gradual revelation (تنزيله) upon the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم.

This second level, which relies upon the first, is indicated by the saying of the Exalted: وَقُرْآنًا فَرَقْنَاهُ لِتَقْرَأَهُ عَلَى النَّاسِ عَلَىٰ مُكْثٍ وَنَزَّلْنَاهُ تَنزِيلًا (A Qur’an which We have unfolded in stages so that you may read it to the people at intervals, and We have sent it down in successive revelations) [Al-Isra: 106]. Thus, the Qur’an was not divided into verses, then it was divided and sent down as a revelation and inspired in installments (نجوماً).

The meaning of this is not that it was a collection of verses with the chapters arranged in the state it is currently with us—as a composed book collected between two covers, for example—and then divided and sent down to the Prophet in installments to be read to the people at intervals, just as a teacher among us divides a text into segments and then teaches and recites it to his student, one segment each day according to the student's mental capacity.

This is because there is a clear difference between the revelation of the Qur’an in installments to the Prophet and its delivery piece by piece to a student. That difference lies in the involvement of the occasions of revelation (أسباب النزول) in the descent of the verse upon the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم, whereas nothing of that sort, nor anything resembling it, exists in the learning of a student. The various segments delivered to a student at different times can be collected and joined together at a single time. However, it is impossible to collect verses like the saying of the Exalted: فَاعْفُ عَنْهُمْ وَاصْفَحْ (So excuse them and forbear) [Al-Ma'idah: 13], and His saying: قَاتِلُوا الَّذِينَ يَلُونَكُم مِّنَ الْكُفَّارِ (Fight the faithless who are in your vicinity) [At-Tawbah: 123], and His saying: قَدْ سَمِعَ اللَّهُ قَوْلَ الَّتِي تُجَادِلُكَ فِي زَوْجِهَا (Allah has certainly heard the speech of her who pleads with you about her husband) [Al-Mujadilah: 1], and His saying: خُذْ مِنْ أَمْوَالِهِمْ صَدَقَةً (Take charity from their possessions) [At-Tawbah: 103], and the like, such that the occasion of revelation and its time are nullified, and then suppose their revelation occurred at the beginning of the Mission or at the end of the Prophet's صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم life. Therefore, the intended meaning of "Qur’an" in His saying: وقرآناً فرقناه is something other than the Qur’an in the sense of composed verses.

In summary, what is gathered from the noble verses is that behind what we read and reason of the Qur’an, there is a matter that stands in relation to the Qur’an as the soul (الروح) to the body (الجسد), or the symbolized to the symbol. This is what the Exalted calls the "Wise Book" (الكتاب الحكيم), and it is that upon which the gnosis of the revealed Qur’an and its contents depend and lean. It is not of the nature of separated, articulated words, nor the meanings indicated by them. This is precisely the interpretation (التأويل) mentioned in the verses containing the term, because its descriptions and attributes correspond to it. By this, the reality of the meaning of interpretation becomes clear, as does the reason why interpretation is inaccessible to ordinary understandings and unpurified souls.

Furthermore, the Exalted said: إِنَّهُ لَقُرْآنٌ كَرِيمٌ فِي كِتَابٍ مَّكْنُونٍ لَّا يَمَسُّهُ إِلَّا الْمُطَهَّرُونَ (This is indeed a noble Qur’an, in a protected Book, no one touches it except the pure) [Al-Waqi'ah: 77-79]. There is no doubt in the apparent meaning of the verses that the purified (المطهرون) among the servants of Allah are those who "touch" the noble Qur’an that is in the protected Book, which is guarded from change—and among the forms of change is the manipulation of minds by entering into it or departing from it. This "touching" (المس) is nothing other than the attainment of understanding and knowledge. It is also known that this protected Book (الكتاب المكنون) is the Mother of the Book (أم الكتاب) indicated by His saying: يمحو الله ما يشاء ويثبت وعنده ام الكتاب, and it is that mentioned in His saying: وإنه في ام الكتاب لدينا لعلي حكيم.

These are a people upon whose hearts purity (الطهارة) has descended, and none causes it to descend except Allah, the Glorified. For the Exalted has not mentioned it except in this manner—attributed to Himself—as in His saying: إِنَّمَا يُرِيدُ اللَّهُ لِيُذْهِبَ عَنكُمُ الرِّجْسَ أَهْلَ الْبَيْتِ وَيُطَهِّرَكُمْ تَطْهِيرًا (Indeed Allah desires to repel all impurity from you, O People of the Household, and purify you with a thorough purification) [Al-Ahzab: 33], and His saying: وَلَٰكِن يُرِيدُ لِيُطَهِّرَكُمْ (but He desires to purify you) [Al-Ma'idah: 6]. There is nothing in the Qur’an regarding spiritual purity (الطهارة المعنوية) except that it is attributed to Allah or by His permission. Purity is nothing but the removal of filth (الرجس) from the heart, and the heart of a human is nothing but that by which he perceives and by which he wills. Thus, the purity of the heart is the purity of the human soul in its belief (اعتقادها) and its will (إرادتها), and the removal of filth from these two aspects. This returns to the firmness (ثبات) of the heart in the true gnosis it believes in, without inclining toward doubt or wavering between truth and falsehood, and its firmness upon the requirements of the truth it has known, without leaning toward following whims or breaking the covenant of knowledge. This is "firmness in knowledge" (الرسوخ في العلم), for Allah, the Glorified, has not described those firm in knowledge (الراسخين في العلم) except as being guided, firm in what they know, their hearts not deviating toward seeking unrest. Thus, it has become clear that these purified ones are those firm in knowledge.

However, one should not mistake the conclusion yielded by this explanation. The extent established by this is that the purified know the interpretation (التأويل), and the necessary consequence of their purification is that they be firm in their knowledge, since the purification of their hearts is attributed to Allah, and He, the Exalted, is a cause that is never overcome. It does not mean that those firm in knowledge know it by virtue of being firm in knowledge—that is, that firmness in knowledge is the cause for knowing the interpretation—for the verse does not establish that. Rather, it may even appear from its context that they are ignorant of the interpretation where the Exalted says: يقولون آمنا به كل من عند ربنا (They say, ‘We believe in it; all of it is from our Lord’). Allah the Exalted has described certain men from the People of the Book as having firmness in knowledge and praised them for it, thanking them for their faith and righteous deeds in His saying: لَّٰكِنِ الرَّاسِخُونَ فِي الْعِلْمِ مِنْهُمْ وَالْمُؤْمِنُونَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِمَا أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكَ وَمَا أُنزِلَ مِن قَبْلِكَ (But as for those who are firmly grounded in knowledge among them, and the faithful, they believe in what has been sent down to you, and what was sent down before you...) [An-Nisa: 162], yet it was not established thereby that they were knowers of the interpretation of the Book.

Likewise, the verse—meaning the saying of the Exalted: لا يمسه إلا المطهرون—has not established for the purified anything except the "touching" of the Book in general. As for whether they know the entirety of the interpretation and are ignorant of none of it at any time, the verse is silent on that. If it were to be established, it would be by a separate proof.

5 - What is the Reason for the Book Containing Ambiguous Verses?

Among the objections raised against the noble Qur’an is the objection regarding its inclusion of ambiguous verses (المتشابهات). It is argued: "You claim that the obligations of creation until the Day of Resurrection are within it, and that it is a decisive word that distinguishes between truth and falsehood. Yet, we see every proponent of the various differing sects among Muslims clinging to it to prove his own doctrine. This is only due to the occurrence of ambiguity (التشابه) in its verses. Would it not have been closer to the desired purpose, and more effective in cutting off the source of disagreement and deviance, if He had made it entirely clear and free from these ambiguities?"

This has been answered with several views, some of which are clearly absurd, such as the answer that the existence of ambiguities necessitates difficulty in attaining the truth and the hardship of research, which leads to increased reward and recompense! Or the answer that if it contained nothing but explicit statements for one doctrine, it would have repelled the proponents of all other doctrines and they would not have looked into it; but because of the existence of ambiguity in it, it enticed them to examine it, and in that was the hope that they might stumble upon the truth and believe in it! Or the answer that its inclusion of the ambiguous necessitated seeking help from the guidance of reason, and in that is an exit from the darkness of imitation (التقليد) and an entry into the light of reflection and independent reasoning (الاجتهاد)! Or the answer that its inclusion of the ambiguous necessitated researching the various methods of interpretation, and in that is the benefit of becoming proficient in various arts such as linguistics, morphology, grammar, and the principles of jurisprudence (اصول الفقه)!

These are absurd answers, whose absurdity is apparent upon the slightest reflection. The answers that deserve presentation and investigation are three views:

First: That the inclusion of ambiguous verses in the noble Qur’an is to test the hearts in their belief in it. For if everything mentioned in the Book were rationally clear and beyond doubt for everyone, there would be no meaning of submission (خضوع) to the command of Allah and surrender to His messengers in faith.

The critique: Submission is a type of passivity and being affected by the weak in the face of the strong. A human only submits to that whose greatness he perceives, or to that which he cannot perceive due to its overwhelming greatness and the dazzlement of perception—such as the infinite power of Allah, His infinite greatness, and His other attributes which, when the intellect faces them, it retreats due to its inability to encompass them. As for matters that the intellect cannot reach but is deceived into believing it perceives, what is the meaning of its submission to them? Such are the ambiguous verses whose matter is confused for the intellect, so it thinks it understands them while it does not.

Second: That its inclusion of the ambiguous is only to incite the intellect toward research and investigation, so that it does not die through neglect by being presented only with clear matters that do not require the work of thought. For the intellect is the most precious of human faculties that must be nurtured through the nurturing of the human being.

The critique: Allah the Exalted has commanded people to employ the intellect and thought regarding the signs in the horizons and within themselves generally in several places of His speech, and specifically in other places—such as the creation of the heavens, the earth, the mountains, the trees, the animals, the human being, and the diversity of his languages and colors. He has urged reasoning, reflection, traveling through the land, and observing the states of those who passed before. He has incited toward the intellect and thought and praised knowledge with the most eloquent praise. In that, there is sufficiency from researching matters that are nothing but slippery slopes for feet and pitfalls for understandings.

Third: That the prophets were sent to people among whom were the commoners and the elite, the intelligent and the dull, the scholar and the ignorant. There were meanings that could not be expressed in a phrasing that reveals their reality and explains their essence such that everyone understands them equally. Thus, it was appropriate for such meanings to be delivered in a way that the elite understand them, even if by way of metaphor and allusion, while the commoners are commanded therein to surrender and delegate the matter to Allah the Exalted.

The critique: Just as the Book contains ambiguous verses, it also contains clear verses (المحكمات) which explain the ambiguous by referring back to them. The necessary consequence of this is that the ambiguous should not contain more than what the clear verses reveal. At that point, the question remains: "What is the benefit of the existence of ambiguous verses in the Book when there is no need for them given the existence of the clear verses?" The source of the confusion is that the respondent took the meanings to be two distinct types: meanings understood by all addressees, both commoners and elite—which are the significations of the clear verses—and meanings whose nature is such that only the elite receive them, consisting of high gnosis and subtle wisdom. Thus, the meanings of the ambiguous verses would not return to the clear verses. It has already been mentioned that this contradicts the explicit text of the verses indicating that the Qur’an explains itself, among other things.

What ought to be said is: that the existence of the ambiguous in the Qur’an is a necessity arising from the existence of the interpretation (التأويل) which necessitates that some of it explains others, in the sense we clarified for interpretation previously.

This becomes somewhat clear by reflecting deeply on the aspects of the Qur’anic exposition, the divine teaching, the matters upon which its gnosis is built, and the ultimate goal thereof, which are several matters:

Among them: That Allah, the Glorified, mentioned that His Book has an interpretation (تأويلا) — the ultimate reality to which it refers — around which the Qur’anic gnosis, the rulings, the laws, and all that the divine teaching contains revolve. This interpretation, which all these expositions face and toward which they are directed, is a matter that understandings fall short of reaching and intellects fail to ascend to, except for souls whom Allah has purified and from whom He has removed filth, for they specifically have the right to "touch" it. This is the ultimate goal He desires from the human being who responds to His call in the realm of knowledge: to be guided to the knowledge of His Book, which is an explanation of all things, and its key is divine purification. The Exalted said: مَا يُرِيدُ اللَّهُ لِيَجْعَلَ عَلَيْكُم مِّنْ حَرَجٍ وَلَٰكِن يُرِيدُ لِيُطَهِّرَكُمْ (Allah does not desire to put you to hardship, but He desires to purify you) [Al-Ma'idah: 6]. Thus, He made the goal of legislating the religion divine purification.

This human perfection, like all other perfections urged toward, is only fully attained by specific individuals, even if the call is related to everyone and directed toward all. Thus, the nurturing of people through religious education only bears the fruit of complete purification in specific individuals, and partial purification in others, varying according to the degrees of people. Just as Islam calls to the "truth of godwariness" (حق التقوى) in action—the Exalted said: اتَّقُوا اللَّهَ حَقَّ تُقَاتِهِ (Be wary of Allah with the wariness due to Him) [Al-Imran: 102]—yet its perfection is only achieved in certain individuals, and in those below them to a lesser extent, in the manner of the best then the next best. All of this is due to the difference of people in their natures and understandings. Such is the case with all social perfections in terms of nurturing and calling: the caller of society calls to the highest degree of every perfection, such as knowledge, craft, wealth, comfort, and others, but it is only attained by some, and by those below them to a lesser degree, according to the different levels of capacities.

In reality, such goals are attained by the society without fail, even if not by every individual within it.

Among them: That the Qur’an determined that the only way to bring the human being to this noble goal is the human being's recognition of his own self through nurturing in the two aspects of knowledge and action. As for the aspect of knowledge, it is by teaching him the realities connected to him—from the Beginning (المبدء) and the Return (المعاد) and the realities of the world between them—until he knows his self through what it is connected to of realities with a true knowledge. As for the aspect of action, it is by imposing social laws upon him such that they rectify the state of his social life and do not distract him from escaping to the world of knowledge and gnosis; then by imposing devotional obligations (تكاليف عبادية) whose performance and habitual practice necessitate the orientation of his soul and the sincerity of his heart toward the Beginning and the Return, and his oversight of the world of meaning and purity, and the avoidance of the filth and weight of material things.

If you reflect well on the saying of the Exalted: إِلَيْهِ يَصْعَدُ الْكَلِمُ الطَّيِّبُ وَالْعَمَلُ الصَّالِحُ يَرْفَعُهُ (To Him ascends the good word, and He elevates the righteous deed) [Fatir: 10], and join it to what you heard summarized in the saying of the Exalted: ولكن يريد ليطهركم (but He desires to purify you), and to His saying: عَلَيْكُمْ أَنفُسَكُمْ لَا يَضُرُّكُم مَّن ضَلَّ إِذَا اهْتَدَيْتُمْ (Take care of your own souls. He who goes astray cannot harm you if you are guided) [Al-Ma'idah: 105], and His saying: يَرْفَعِ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنكُمْ وَالَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْعِلْمَ دَرَجَاتٍ (Allah will raise those of you who have faith and those who have been given knowledge in rank) [Al-Mujadilah: 11], and similar verses, the divine purpose in legislating the religion and guiding the human being to it, and the path He took for that, will become clear to you. So understand.

An important result branches out from this explanation: that social laws in Islam are a prelude to devotional obligations and are intended for their sake, and devotional obligations are a prelude to the knowledge of Allah and His signs. Thus, the slightest disruption, distortion, or change in the social rulings of Islam necessitates the corruption of servitude (العبودية), and the corruption of servitude leads to the disruption of the matter of knowledge (المعرفة).

This result—besides being a clear branch of the explanation—is also supported by experience. For if you contemplate the course of events regarding the onset of corruption in the affairs of the Islamic religion among this nation, and look deeply into where it began and where it ended, you will find that the unrest (الفتنة) began with social matters, then mediated through devotional matters, then ended in the rejection of gnosis. We have already mentioned to you that the unrest began by following the ambiguous verses and seeking their interpretation, and the matter has remained so until today.

Among them: That religious guidance was built upon the negation of imitation (التقليد) from people and the anchoring of knowledge among them as much as possible, for that is what accords with its goal, which is knowledge (المعرفة). And how could it be otherwise? There is no book among the books of revelation, nor any religion among religions, that glorifies the matter of knowledge and incites toward it as much as what has come in the Qur’an and Islam!

This meaning is what necessitates that the Book explains to the human being the realities of gnosis first, and the connection of the practical rulings He legislated for him to those realities second. In other words, to make him understand:

That he is a created being, a creature of Allah the Exalted, whom He created with His hand and mediated in his creation and survival His angels and the rest of His creation—of heaven, earth, plants, animals, place, time, and others—and that he is traveling toward his Return and his Appointed Time by a compulsory journey, toiling toward his Lord with a constant toil until he meets Him, then he will be recompensed for what he did, either to Paradise or to the Fire. This is one category of gnosis.

Then He makes him understand: what are the deeds that lead him to the happiness of Paradise, and what are those that lead him to the wretchedness of the Fire? That is, He explains to him the devotional rulings and social laws. This is another category.

Then He explains to him: that these rulings and laws lead to happiness. That is, He makes him understand: that this second category is connected to the first category, and that their legislation and establishment for the human being is only to observe his happiness, because they contain the good of the human being in this world and the Hereafter. This is a third category.

It is apparent to you that the second category is in the position of the premise (المقدمة), the first category is in the position of the result (النتيجة), and the third category is in the position of the link (الرابط) that connects the second to the first. The indication of the verses for each of these mentioned categories is clear and there is no need to present them.

Among them: That since the understanding of the generality of people does not exceed the sensible (المحسوس) and their intellect does not ascend to what is above the world of matter and nature; and since those among them whose understanding has ascended through scientific exercises to the perception of meanings and universal rules and laws differ in their state according to the means that facilitated their entry into the world of meanings and universals—this necessitated a severe and wide-ranging difference among people in understanding meanings outside the realm of sense and the sensible, across various levels. This is a matter that no one denies.

It is impossible to deliver a meaning to a human being except through his mental information that was prepared for him during his life and living. If he is accustomed to the sensible, then it is through the sensible things to the extent of the sensory levels he has ascended—just as the pleasure of marriage is likened to a child by the sweetness of candy. If he has attained universal meanings, then it is by what he has attained and to the extent he has attained. Such a person perceives meanings from both sensory and intellectual exposition together, unlike the one accustomed only to the sensible.

Furthermore, religious guidance is not restricted to one group of people over another, but rather encompasses all groups and includes all classes, as is apparent.

This meaning—namely, the difference of understandings and the universality of the matter of guidance, along with what you have known of the existence of the interpretation (التأويل) for the Qur’an—is what necessitates that the expositions be conducted in the manner of parables (الأمثال). This means taking what the human being knows and what his mind is familiar with of meanings, and explaining by them what he does not know, due to a correspondence between them—similar to weighing goods with weights (المثاقيل) when there is no similarity between them in shape, form, size, or type, except for the correspondence between them in weight.

The Qur’anic verses mentioned previously, such as the saying of the Exalted: إِنَّا جَعَلْنَاهُ قُرْآنًا عَرَبِيًّا لَّعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ وَإِنَّهُ فِي أُمِّ الْكِتَابِ لَدَيْنَا لَعَلِيٌّ حَكِيمٌ [Az-Zukhruf: 3-4], and similar verses, although they explained this matter by way of allusion and metaphor, the Qur’an did not stop at that without explaining it by what it struck as a parable regarding the matter of truth and falsehood. The Exalted said: أَنزَلَ مِنَ السَّمَاءِ مَاءً فَسَالَتْ أَوْدِيَةٌ بِقَدَرِهَا فَاحْتَمَلَ السَّيْلُ زَبَدًا رَّابِيًا وَمِمَّا يُوقِدُونَ عَلَيْهِ فِي النَّارِ ابْتِغَاءَ حِلْيَةٍ أَوْ مَتَاعٍ زَبَدٌ مِّثْلُهُ كَذَٰلِكَ يَضْرِبُ اللَّهُ الْحَقَّ وَالْبَاطِلَ ، فَأَمَّا الزَّبَدُ فَيَذْهَبُ جُفَاءً وَأَمَّا مَا يَنفَعُ النَّاسَ فَيَمْكُثُ فِي الْأَرْضِ كَذَٰلِكَ يَضْرِبُ اللَّهُ الْأَمْثَالَ (He sends down water from the sky whereat the valleys are flooded to [the extent of] their capacity, and the flood carries a swelling scum. And from what they smelt in the fire for making ornaments or housewares [there is] a similar scum. Thus does Allah draw the parable of truth and falsehood. As for the scum, it leaves as dross, and as for what profits the people, it remains in the earth. Thus does Allah draw the parables) [Ar-Ra'd: 17].

He explained that the rule of the parable (المثل) applies to His acts, the Exalted, just as it applies to His words. His act, like His true word, is only intended for the truth they contain, and each of them is accompanied by unintended and non-beneficial matters that rise above them and swell, but they will vanish and become void, while the truth that benefits people remains. It only vanishes and perishes by another truth that is like it. This is like the ambiguous verse (الآية المتشابهة) which contains a true intended meaning, but is accompanied and overtaken in the mind by another false, unintended meaning; however, it will vanish by another truth that makes the first truth prevail over the falsehood that was rising above it, so that He may realize the truth by His words and invalidate falsehood, even if the guilty should be averse. The discussion regarding the application of this parable to His external acts established in the world of existence is like the discussion regarding His words, Exalted is He.

In summary: what is gathered from the noble verse is that the true divine gnosis is like the water Allah sent down from the sky; in itself, it is just water, without being restricted by quantity or quality. Then, like the flood flowing in the valleys, it is measured by different measures in terms of breadth and narrowness. These measures are fixed matters, each in its place, as is the case with the principles of gnosis and the legislative rulings, and the interests of the rulings (مصالح الأحكام) which we mentioned previously are links connecting the rulings to the true gnosis. This is their rule in themselves, regardless of the verbal exposition. In their course, they may be accompanied by what is like scum (الزبد), which appears for a moment and then quickly vanishes—such as the abrogated rulings (الأحكام المنسوخة) which are abrogated by the abrogating verses (النواسخ). For the abrogated, by its apparent nature, suggests it should endure, but the abrogating ruling nullifies its endurance and places another ruling in its stead. This is considering these gnoses themselves, regardless of their entry into the valley of verbal exposition.

As for the true gnoses in terms of their entry into the vessel of the word and signification, by their entry into the valleys of verbal significations, they are measured by their measures and take the forms of the intended meanings of speech after their absolute state. These are fixed statements in terms of the speaker's intent with his speech; however, they are nonetheless parables by which the original absolute, unmeasured meaning is symbolized. Then, by their passage through different minds, they carry unintended meanings like the scum in the flood, because minds—due to what they store of ingrained notions and familiarities—manipulate the meanings delivered to them. Most of this manipulation occurs in unfamiliar meanings, such as the original gnosis, and the interests and underlying reasons (ملاكات) of the rulings, as mentioned. As for the rulings and laws, there is no manipulation in them regardless of their underlying reasons, for they are familiar. From here, it becomes clear that the ambiguous verses (المتشابهات) are the verses in terms of their inclusion of the underlying reasons and gnosis, rather than the core text of the religious rulings and laws.

Among them: It has been gathered from the previous explanation that the verbal Qur’anic expositions are parables (أمثال) for the true divine gnosis, because the exposition in these verses descended to the level of common understandings which only perceive the sensible and only attain universal meanings in the mold of corporeal things. Since delivering universal meanings stripped of the accidents of bodies and corporeal things necessitated one of two problems: for if understandings, in receiving the intended gnosis, remained rigid at the level of sense and the sensible, the parables would turn into symbolized realities for them, and in that is the invalidation of realities and the loss of intended meanings and goals. And if they did not remain rigid and moved to abstract meanings by stripping the parables of the non-essential specifics, one could not be safe from addition or omission.

An example of this is if the common proverb were delivered to us: "At morning, the people praise the night-journey," or if we were symbolized by the saying of Sakhr:

I intend a matter of resolve but cannot do it, For the camel has been prevented from leaping.

Due to the mind's prior familiarity with the story or the matter symbolized, we strip the parable of the specifics surrounding the speech—such as the morning, the people, and the night-journey—and we understand from it that the intent is: that the goodness of an action's effect and the praise of its performance only appear when it is finished and its effect becomes manifest; but as long as the human is occupied with it, feeling the fatigue of his action, he does not appreciate its value. This stripping of what was symbolized by the poetry becomes manifest. But if we were not familiar with the symbolized and remained rigid upon the poetry or the proverb, the symbolized would be hidden from us and the parable would return to being just a piece of news. And if we did not remain rigid and moved generally to the fact that it is a parable, we would not be able to distinguish the amount that must be discarded through abstraction and what must be preserved for understanding, and this is apparent.

There is no escape from these two problems except by dividing the symbolized meanings into different parables and turning them into diverse molds until some explain others and some clarify the matter of others. Thus, by the conflict between them, it is known first: that the expositions are parables and have symbolized realities behind them, and their goals and intended meanings are not restricted to the word taken from the level of sense and the sensible. Second: after knowing they are parables, one knows thereby the amount that must be discarded of the specifics surrounding the speech, and what must be preserved of them to obtain the objective. This is only achieved by the fact that this one contains the negation of some specifics found in that one, and that one contains the negation of some of what is in this one.

Clarifying ambiguous goals and subtle demands by presenting multiple stories and many diverse parables and examples is a matter current in all tongues and languages, not restricted to one people over another or one language over another. This is only because the human being feels, by the instinct of exposition, the urgency of his need to negate the specifics that suggest the opposite of the intent in a single story or a single parable, by using the negating specifics found in another suitable story or another suitable parable.

Thus, it has become clear that it is necessary for the noble Qur’an to contain ambiguous verses, and that the ambiguity occurring in one verse be removed by the clarity (الإحكام) occurring in another verse. Thereby, the objection regarding the Qur’an's inclusion of ambiguities—as being detrimental to the purpose of guidance and exposition—is refuted.

From all the preceding lengthy investigations, several matters have become clear:

First: That the Qur’anic verses are divided into two types: clear (محكم) and ambiguous (متشابه), and that is due to the verse alone containing an ambiguous signification or not.

Second: That the entire Qur’an—its clear and its ambiguous—has an interpretation (تأويلاً). And that interpretation is not of the nature of verbal concepts, but rather of external matters; its relation to the explained gnosis and goals is like the relation of the symbolized to the symbol. All Qur’anic gnosis are parables struck for the interpretation that is with Allah.

Third: That the interpretation can be known by the purified (المطهرون), and they are those firm in knowledge (الراسخون في العلم).

Fourth: That the Qur’anic expositions are parables struck for its gnosis and goals, and this meaning is different from what we mentioned in the second point regarding its gnosis being parables, as we clarified previously.

Fifth: That it is necessary for the Qur’an to contain ambiguous verses, just as it is necessary for it to contain clear verses.

Sixth: That the clear verses are the "Mother of the Book" (ام الكتاب), to which the ambiguous verses return as an explanatory return.

Seventh: That clarity (الإحكام) and ambiguity (التشابه) are two descriptions that accept relativity and difference in aspects, meaning that a certain verse can be clear from one aspect and ambiguous from another; it would be clear in relation to one verse and ambiguous in relation to another. There is no instance of the "absolutely ambiguous" in the Qur’an, while there is no barrier to the existence of the "absolutely clear."

Eighth: That it is necessary for some of the Qur’an to explain others.

Ninth: That the Qur’an has different levels of meaning, arranged vertically (طولاً) without all of them being on a single horizontal plane (عرض واحد)—which would necessitate the use of a word in more than one meaning, or like the generality of metaphor—nor are they of the nature of multiple consequences for a single antecedent. Rather, they are corresponding meanings (معان مطابقية) indicated by the word through correspondence according to the levels of understanding.

To clarify this, we say: Allah, Blessed and Exalted, said: اتَّقُوا اللَّهَ حَقَّ تُقَاتِهِ (Be wary of Allah with the wariness due to Him) [Al-Imran: 102]. He informed that for godwariness (التقوى)—which is refraining from what Allah forbade and complying with what Allah commanded—there is a level which is the "truth of godwariness." It is known thereby that there is a godwariness below this true level. Thus, for godwariness, which is in one aspect righteous deeds, there are levels and ranks, some above others.

He also said: أَفَمَنِ اتَّبَعَ رِضْوَانَ اللَّهِ كَمَن بَاءَ بِسَخَطٍ مِّنَ اللَّهِ وَمَأْوَاهُ جَهَنَّمُ وَبِئْسَ الْمَصِيرُ هُمْ دَرَجَاتٌ عِندَ اللَّهِ وَاللَّهُ بَصِيرٌ بِمَا يَعْمَلُونَ (Is he who follows Allah’s pleasure like him who incurs Allah’s wrath and whose refuge is hell? It is an evil destination. They have [various] ranks with Allah, and Allah watches what they do) [Al-Imran: 162-163]. He explained that action (العمل) in general—whether righteous or wicked—consists of ranks and levels. The proof that the intended meaning is the ranks of action is His saying: والله بصير بما يعملون. Similar to this verse is the saying of the Exalted: وَلِكُلٍّ دَرَجَاتٌ مِّمَّا عَمِلُوا وَلِيُوَفِّيَهُمْ أَعْمَالَهُمْ وَهُمْ لَا يُظْلَمُونَ (For everyone there are ranks according to what they have done, so that He may pay them in full for their deeds, and they will not be wronged) [Al-Ahqaf: 19], and His saying: وَلِكُلٍّ دَرَجَاتٌ مِّمَّا عَمِلُوا وَمَا رَبُّكَ بِغَافِلٍ عَمَّا يَعْمَلُونَ (For everyone there are ranks according to what they have done, and your Lord is not oblivious of what they do) [Al-An'am: 132]. The verses on this meaning are many; among them are those indicating that the ranks of Paradise and the depths of the Fire are according to the levels and ranks of actions.

It is known that action of any kind is among the emanations of knowledge (رشحات العلم); it emanates from a heart-felt belief that suits it. The Exalted has argued for the disbelief of the Jews, the corruption of the polytheists' conscience, the hypocrisy of the hypocrites among the Muslims, and the faith of a number of prophets and believers by their actions and deeds in very many verses whose mention would be lengthy. Thus, action, whatever it may be, accompanies what suits it of knowledge and indicates it.

Conversely, every type of action necessitates what suits it of knowledge, producing it and anchoring it in the soul, as the Exalted said: وَالَّذِينَ جَاهَدُوا فِينَا لَنَهْدِيَنَّهُمْ سُبُلَنَا وَإِنَّ اللَّهَ لَمَعَ الْمُحْسِنِينَ (As for those who strive in Us, We shall surely guide them in Our ways, and Allah is indeed with the virtuous) [Al-Ankabut: 69], and the Exalted said: وَاعْبُدْ رَبَّكَ حَتَّىٰ يَأْتِيَكَ الْيَقِينُ (And worship your Lord until conviction comes to you) [Al-Hijr: 99], and He also said: ثُمَّ كَانَ عَاقِبَةَ الَّذِينَ أَسَاءُوا السُّوأَىٰ أَن كَذَّبُوا بِآيَاتِ اللَّهِ وَكَانُوا بِهَا يَسْتَهْزِئُونَ (Then the fate of those who committed misdeeds was that they denied the signs of Allah and they used to deride them) [Ar-Rum: 10], and He said: فَأَعْقَبَهُمْ نِفَاقًا فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ يَلْقَوْنَهُ بِمَا أَخْلَفُوا اللَّهَ مَا وَعَدُوهُ وَبِمَا كَانُوا يَكْذِبُونَ (So He caused hypocrisy to take root in their hearts until the day they will meet Him, because they went back on what they had promised Allah, and because they used to lie) [At-Tawbah: 77]. The verses on this meaning are also many, all indicating that action—whether righteous or wicked—gives birth to the types of gnosis and ignorance (which are sciences contrary to the truth) that suit it.

The Exalted said—and it is like a comprehensive word regarding righteous action and beneficial knowledge: إِلَيْهِ يَصْعَدُ الْكَلِمُ الطَّيِّبُ وَالْعَمَلُ الصَّالِحُ يَرْفَعُهُ (To Him ascends the good word, and He elevates the righteous deed) [Fatir: 10]. He explained that the function of the "good word" (الكلم الطيب)—which is true belief—is to ascend to Allah the Exalted and bring its possessor close to Him, and the function of the "righteous deed" is to elevate this knowledge and belief. It is known that the elevation of knowledge in its ascent is only by its sincerity from doubt and suspicion, the perfection of the soul's orientation toward it, and the heart not being divided between it and others (which is absolute polytheism). Thus, whenever its sincerity from doubt and hesitations is perfected, its ascent and elevation intensify.

The wording of the verse is not devoid of indication of that, for it expressed the "good word" with "ascent" (الصعود) and described the "action" with "elevation" (الرفع). Ascent is the opposite of descent, just as elevation is the opposite of lowering. These two—ascent and elevation—are descriptions by which the one moving from below to above is characterized in relation to the two sides: he is "ascending" (صاعد) in view of his aiming for the height and his proximity to it, and "elevated" (مرتفع) from the aspect of his separation from the bottom and his distance from it. Thus, action distances the human being and separates him from the world and from clinging to the earth, by turning his soul away from attachment to its distracting ornaments and the scattering and division caused by these vanishing, non-enduring pieces of information. Whenever the elevation and height increase, the ascent of the good word increases, and gnosis is purified from the taints of illusions and the filth of doubts. It is also known, as mentioned, that righteous action has ranks and degrees; thus, for every degree of righteous action, there is an elevation of the good word and a generation of true divine sciences and gnosis according to its state. The discussion regarding wicked action and its lowering of the human being is similar to the discussion regarding righteous action and its elevation, and some discussion on that has already passed in the interpretation of the saying of the Exalted: اهْدِنَا الصِّرَاطَ الْمُسْتَقِيمَ [Al-Hamd: 6].

Thus, it has appeared that people, according to the levels of their proximity and distance from Him, the Exalted, have different levels of action and knowledge. The necessary consequence is that what the people of one level and degree receive is different from what the people of another level and degree receive, whether above or below it. Thus, it has become clear that the Qur’an has different, arranged meanings.

Allah, the Glorified, has mentioned categories of His servants and specified each category with a type of knowledge and gnosis not found in the other category—such as the "sincere ones" (المخلصين), to whom He specified the knowledge of the attributes of their Lord with true knowledge. The Exalted said: سُبْحَانَ اللَّهِ عَمَّا يَصِفُونَ إِلَّا عِبَادَ اللَّهِ الْمُخْلَصِينَ (Clear is Allah of what they allege, except for Allah’s chosen servants) [As-Saffat: 159-160]. He specified for them other things of gnosis and knowledge whose explanation will come, Allah willing. And like the "possessors of certainty" (الموقنين), to whom He specified the witnessing of the kingdom (ملكوت) of the heavens and the earth. The Exalted said: وَكَذَٰلِكَ نُرِي إِبْرَاهِيمَ مَلَكوتَ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَلِيَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُوقِنِينَ (Thus did We show Abraham the dominion of the heavens and the earth, that he might be of those who possess certainty) [Al-An'am: 75]. And like the "penitent ones" (المنيبين), to whom He specified remembrance (التذكر). The Exalted said: وَمَا يَتَذَكَّرُ إِلَّا مَن يُنِيبُ (and no one takes admonition except him who turns [to Allah] penitently) [Al-Mu'min: 13]. And like the "knowers" (العالمين), to whom He specified the reasoning of the parables of the Qur’an. The Exalted said: وَتِلْكَ الْأَمْثَالُ نَضْرِبُهَا لِلنَّاسِ وَمَا يَعْقِلُهَا إِلَّا الْعَالِمُونَ (We draw these parables for mankind; but no one grasps them except the learned) [Al-Ankabut: 43]. And it is as if they are the "possessors of intellect" (اولوا الألباب) and those who reflect (المتدبرون) due to the saying of the Exalted: أَفَلَا يَتَدَبَّرُونَ الْقُرْآنَ أَمْ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبٍ أَقْفَالُهَا (Do they not contemplate the Qur’an, or are there locks on the hearts?) [Muhammad صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم: 24], and His saying: أَفَلَا يَتَدَبَّرُونَ الْقُرْآنَ وَلَوْ كَانَ مِنْ عِندِ غَيْرِ اللَّهِ لَوجَدُوا فِيهِ اخْتِلَافًا كَثِيرًا (Do they not contemplate the Qur’an? Had it been from [someone] other than Allah, they would have surely found much discrepancy in it) [An-Nisa: 82]. For the import of the three verses returns to a single meaning, which is the knowledge of the ambiguous of the Qur’an and its referral to its clear verses. And like the "purified ones" (المطهرون), whom Allah specified with the knowledge of the interpretation of the Book. The Exalted said: إِنَّهُ لَقُرْآنٌ كَرِيمٌ فِي كِتَابٍ مَّكْنُونٍ لَّا يَمَسُّهُ إِلَّا الْمُطَهَّرُونَ [Al-Waqi'ah: 77-79]. And like the "friends of Allah" (الاولياء), who are the people of infatuation and love for Allah, and He specified for them that they do not turn their attention to anything except Allah, the Glorified; therefore, they do not fear anything nor do they grieve for anything. The Exalted said: أَلَا إِنَّ أَوْلِيَاءَ اللَّهِ لَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ (Look! The friends of Allah will indeed have no fear nor will they grieve) [Yunus: 62]. And like the "drawn-near" (المقربين), the "chosen" (المجتبين), the "truthful" (الصديقين), the "righteous" (الصالحين), and the "believers" (المؤمنين); for each of them there are characteristics of knowledge and perception they are specified with, which we will discuss in their appropriate places.

Similar to these good stations are evil stations in opposition to them, which have wicked characteristics in the chapter of knowledge and gnosis, and they have possessors such as the disbelievers (الكافرين), the hypocrites (المنافقين), the transgressors (الفاسقين), the wrongdoers (الظالمين), and others. They have portions of ill-understanding and wicked perception of the signs of Allah and His true gnosis. We have omitted their mention for the sake of brevity, and we will address them during the investigations of this book, Allah willing.

Tenth: That the Qur’an has an expansiveness in terms of its application to instances (المصاديق) and the explanation of their state. A verse from it is not restricted to the occasion of its revelation, but rather runs in every instance that unites with the occasion of revelation in its underlying reason (ملاكاً)—like parables which are not restricted to their first instances, but rather extend to what suits them. This meaning is what is called the "running of the Qur’an" (جرى القرآن), and some discussion on it has already passed at the beginning of the book.

(Narrative Discussion)

In the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi: Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) was asked about the clear (المحكم) and the ambiguous (المتشابه). He said: "The clear is that which is acted upon, and the ambiguous is that which is ambiguous to the one ignorant of it."

I say: In it is an allusion that the ambiguous is among that which knowledge of it is possible.

In it also, from him (peace be upon him): "The Qur’an is clear and ambiguous: as for the clear, you believe in it, act upon it, and profess it as religion; as for the ambiguous, you believe in it but do not act upon it. It is the saying of Allah, Mighty and Majestic: وأما الذين في قلوبهم زيغ فيتبعون ما تشابه منه ابتغاء الفتنة وابتغاء تأويله وما يعلم تأويله إلا الله والراسخون في العلم يقولون آمنا به كل من عند ربنا. And those firm in knowledge (الراسخون في العلم) are the Progeny of Muhammad."

I say: A discussion will come regarding the meaning of his saying (peace be upon him): "And those firm in knowledge are the Progeny of Muhammad."

In it also, from Mus'adah ibn Sadaqah, who said: I asked Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) about the abrogating (الناسخ) and the abrogated (المنسوخ), and the clear (المحكم) and the ambiguous (المتشابه). He said: "The abrogating is the fixed that is acted upon; the abrogated is what used to be acted upon then that which abrogated it came; and the ambiguous is what is ambiguous to the one ignorant of it." He said: And in a narration: "The abrogating is the fixed, the abrogated is what has passed, the clear is what is acted upon, and the ambiguous is what resembles each other."

In al-Kafi, from al-Baqir (peace be upon him) in a hadith, he said: "The abrogated ones are among the ambiguous ones."

In al-Uyun, from al-Rida (peace be upon him): "Whoever refers the ambiguous of the Qur’an to its clear is guided to a straight path." Then he said: "Indeed, in our reports (أخبارنا) there is ambiguous like the ambiguous of the Qur’an, so refer their ambiguous to their clear, and do not follow their ambiguous lest you go astray."

I say: The reports, as you see, are close to each other in the interpretation of the ambiguous, and they support what we mentioned in the previous explanation: that ambiguity accepts removal, and that it is only removed by the clear verse's interpretation of it. As for the abrogated being among the ambiguous, it is so as previously mentioned; the aspect of their ambiguity is what appears from them of the continuation and endurance of the ruling, and the abrogating verse explains it by clarifying that its endurance is cut off. As for what he (peace be upon him) mentioned in the report of al-Uyun: "Indeed, in our reports there is ambiguous like the ambiguous of the Qur’an and clear like the clear of the Qur’an," widely transmitted (مستفيضة) narrations have been reported from them (peace be upon them) on this meaning. Reason supports it, for the reports only contain what the noble Qur’an contains, and they only explain what it addressed. You have already known previously: that ambiguity is among the descriptions of the meaning indicated by the word—which is its being such that it accepts application to the intended and to others—not among the descriptions of the word in terms of its indication of the meaning, like strangeness (الغرابة) or obscurity (الاجمال), nor among the descriptions of what is more general than the word and the meaning.

In other words: ambiguity only occurred to that which it occurred to of the verses because their expositions run in the manner of parables (الأمثال) in relation to the true divine gnosis. This very meaning exists in the reports; thus, in them is ambiguous and clear just as in the Qur’an. It has been reported from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم that he said: إنا معاشر الأنبياء نكلم الناس على قدر عقولهم — "We, the assembly of prophets, speak to people according to the capacity of their intellects."

In the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, from Ja'far ibn Muhammad, from his father (peace be upon them): A man said to the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him): "Will you describe our Lord for us so that we may increase in love for Him and knowledge of Him?" He became angry and addressed the people, saying among what he said: "O servant of Allah, adhere to what the Qur’an has directed you to of His description, and what the Messenger has preceded you in of His knowledge, and seek light from the light of its guidance; for it is a blessing and wisdom you have been given, so take what you have been given and be among the thankful. And what Satan has burdened you with of that which is not obligatory upon you in the Book, nor is its command in the Sunnah of the Messenger and the Imams of guidance, then delegate its knowledge to Allah, and do not measure the greatness of Allah. And know, O servant of Allah: that those firm in knowledge (الراسخون في العلم) are those whom Allah has enriched from plunging into the barriers struck before the Unseen (الغيوب), so they adhered to the acknowledgment of the entirety of what they were ignorant of its interpretation (تفسيره) from the veiled Unseen. They said: امنا به كل من عند ربنا (We believe in it; all of it is from our Lord). Allah has praised their confession of inability to reach what they did not encompass with knowledge, and He named their abandonment of deep-delving into what He did not burden them with researching as 'firmness' (رسوخاً). So restrict yourself to that, and do not measure the greatness of Allah according to the measure of your intellect, lest you be among the perished."

I say: His saying (peace be upon him): "And know, O servant of Allah, that those firm in knowledge..." is apparent in that he (peace be upon him) took the "and" (الواو) in the saying of the Exalted: والراسخون في العلم يقولون as a particle of commencement (للاستيناف) rather than a conjunction (العطف), as we inferred from the verse. The consequence of that is that the apparent meaning of the verse does not support those firm in knowledge being knowers of its interpretation (تأويله), not that it supports the impossibility of their knowledge of it. Thus, it does not contradict the existence of another exposition indicating it, as previously explained, and this is the apparent meaning of some reports from the Imams of the People of the Household, as will come. His saying (peace be upon him): "whom Allah has enriched from plunging into the barriers struck before the Unseen," is the predicate of "Indeed" (إن). The speech is apparent in inciting the addressee and encouraging him to adhere to the way of those firm in knowledge by confessing ignorance in what he is ignorant of, so that he may be among them. This is a proof for his (peace be upon him) interpretation of "those firm in knowledge" as anyone who adheres to what he knows and does not exceed it to what he is ignorant of. The intended meaning of the "Unseen veiled by barriers" is the meanings intended by the ambiguous verses which are hidden from common understandings; therefore, he followed it by saying second: "so they adhered to the acknowledgment of the entirety of what they were ignorant of its explanation (تفسيره)," and he did not say "of the entirety of what they were ignorant of its interpretation (تأويله)." So understand.

In al-Kafi, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "We are those firmly grounded in knowledge (الراسخون في العلم), and we know its interpretation (تأويله)."

I say: The narration is not devoid of an apparent indication that the saying of the Exalted, "and those firmly grounded in knowledge" (والراسخون في العلم), is conjoined to the exception in His saying: "No one knows its interpretation except Allah" (وما يعلم تأويله إلا الله). However, this appearance is dispelled by the explanation provided previously and the preceding narration. It is not entirely far-fetched that what is meant by interpretation (التأويل) here is the meaning intended by the ambiguous (المتشابه); for this sense of interpretation—which is synonymous with the explanation of the ambiguous—was common among the people during the first era of Islam.

As for his saying (peace be upon him): "We are those firmly grounded in knowledge," it has already been mentioned in a narration by al-Ayyashi from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) that he said: "Those firmly grounded in knowledge are the Progeny of Muhammad (آل محمد)." This sentence is also reported in other narrations. All of this falls under the principle of "flow and application" (الجري والانطباق) — the principle that Quranic verses apply to new instances and individuals over time beyond their original occasion of revelation — as evidenced by the preceding and forthcoming narrations.

In al-Kafi as well, from Hisham ibn al-Hakam, who said: "Abu al-Hasan Musa ibn Ja'far (peace be upon him) said to me..." until he said: "O Hisham, indeed Allah recounted concerning a righteous people that they said: ربنا لا تزغ قلوبنا بعد إذ هديتنا وهب لنا من لدنك رحمة إنك أنت الوهاب (Our Lord, do not make our hearts swerve after You have guided us, and bestow Your mercy on us from Yourself. Indeed You are the All-munificent). They realized that hearts swerve and return to their blindness and ruin. Indeed, he who does not intellectually comprehend (يعقل) from Allah does not fear Allah; and he who does not intellectually comprehend from Allah, his heart does not fasten upon a firm gnosis (معرفة ثابتة) that he beholds and whose reality he finds in his heart. No one can be like that unless his speech confirms his action, and his inner secret (سره) accords with his outward conduct (علانيته); because Allah, exalted is His Name (عز اسمه), did not point to the hidden inner dimension (الباطن الخفي) of the intellect except through an outward manifestation (ظاهر) from it and a speaker on its behalf."

I say: His saying (peace be upon him), "he who does not intellectually comprehend from Allah does not fear Allah," is in the sense of the saying of the Exalted: إِنَّمَا يَخْشَى اللَّهَ مِنْ عِبَادِهِ الْعُلَمَاءُ (Only those of Allah’s servants who possess knowledge fear Him). His saying (peace be upon him), "and he who does not intellectually comprehend from Allah..." etc., is the finest explanation of the meaning of being "firmly grounded in knowledge" (الرسوخ في العلم). This is because as long as a matter is not intellectually grasped with true comprehension, the paths of possibilities regarding it are not closed, and the heart remains agitated in its assent to it. But when the intellectual comprehension is complete and the heart is fastened upon it, it does not oppose it by following the whims (الهوى) that contradict it. Thus, what is in his heart becomes manifest in his limbs, and what he says is what he does. His saying, "No one can be like that..." etc., is an explanation of the sign of being firmly grounded in knowledge.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Jarir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and al-Tabarani narrated from Anas, Abu Umamah, Wathilah ibn al-Asqa', and Abu al-Darda' that the Messenger of Allah (may Allah bless him and his Progeny and grant them peace) was asked about those firmly grounded in knowledge, and he said: "He whose oath is pious, whose tongue is truthful, whose heart is upright, and he who guards his stomach and his private parts—such a one is among those firmly grounded in knowledge."

I say: It is possible to interpret this narration in a way that returns to the meaning of the previous hadith.

In al-Kafi, from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him): "Indeed, those firmly grounded in knowledge are those whose knowledge does not contain contradiction (لا يختلف)."

I say: This is applicable to the verse, for "those firmly grounded in knowledge" is contrasted therein with His saying: "those in whose hearts is swerving" (الذين في قلوبهم زيغ). Thus, the firmness of knowledge (رسوخ العلم) consists in the absence of contradiction and doubt in the scholar.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Abi Shaybah, Ahmad, al-Tirmidhi, Ibn Jarir, al-Tabarani, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Umm Salamah: "The Messenger of Allah used to frequently say in his supplication: 'O Allah, Turner of hearts, make my heart firm upon Your religion.' I said: 'O Messenger of Allah, do hearts indeed turn?' He said: 'Yes, Allah has not created any human being from the children of Adam except that his heart is between two fingers of the fingers of Allah; if He wills, He keeps it upright, and if He wills, He makes it swerve...'" (the hadith).

I say: This meaning has been narrated through numerous chains from several companions, such as Jabir, Nawwas ibn Sam'an, Abdullah ibn Umar, and Abu Hurayrah. The most famous in this regard is what is in the hadith of Nawwas: "The heart of the son of Adam is between two fingers of the fingers of the All-Beneficent (الرحمن)." Al-Sharif al-Radi (as I believe) narrated this phrasing in al-Majazat al-Nabawiyyah.

It is narrated from Ali (peace be upon him) that it was said to him: "Do you possess anything of revelation (الوحي)?" He said: "No, by Him who split the grain and created the soul, except that Allah grants a servant understanding (فهماً) of His Book."

I say: This is among the most brilliant of hadiths. At the very least, it indicates that the wonders of gnosis (أعاجيب المعارف) issued from his scholarly station—which dazzles the intellects—are derived from the Noble Quran.

In al-Kafi, from Imam al-Sadiq, from his father, from his ancestors (peace be upon them), he said: "The Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him) said: 'O people, you are in an abode of truce (دار هدنة), and you are on a journey, and the travel is swift for you. You have seen the night and the day, the sun and the moon, how they wear out every new thing, bring near every distant thing, and bring about every promised thing. So prepare the equipment for the long passage (المجاز).'" He said: "Then al-Miqdad ibn al-Aswad stood up and said: 'O Messenger of Allah, what is the abode of truce?' He replied: 'An abode of reaching and cutting off. So when trials (الفتن) confuse you like pieces of a dark night, then hold fast to the Quran, for it is an intercessor whose intercession is accepted (شافع مشفع), and a credible pleader (ماحل مصدق). Whoever puts it before him, it leads him to Paradise; and whoever puts it behind him, it drives him to the Fire. It is the guide that points to the best path. It is a book containing detail (تفصيل), clarification (بيان), and attainment (تحصيل). It is the Decisive Word (الفصل), not a jest (الهزل). It has an outward (ظهر) and an inward (بطن); its outward is judgment (حكم) and its inward is knowledge (علم). Its outward is elegant (أنيق) and its inward is deep (عميق). It has boundaries (تخوم), and upon its boundaries are boundaries. Its wonders cannot be counted, and its marvels do not wear out. In it are the lamps of guidance (مصابيح الهدى) and the lighthouse of wisdom (منار الحكمة), and a guide to gnosis (المعرفة) for one who recognizes the description. So let the observer cast his gaze and let his vision reach the description, that he may be saved from destruction and be freed from entanglement. For reflection (التفكر) is the life of the heart of the insightful one, just as the seeker of light walks in the darknesses. So you must seek a good exit and minimize hesitation.'"

I say: Al-Ayyashi narrated it in his tafsir up to his saying: "let the observer cast..."

In al-Kafi and the tafsir of al-Ayyashi as well, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), he said: "The Messenger of Allah (may Allah bless him and his Progeny and grant them peace) said: 'The Quran is guidance (هدى) from misguidance, a clarification (تبيان) from blindness, a pardon from stumbling, a light (نور) from darkness, a radiance from events, a protection (عصمة) from destruction, right-guidance (رشد) from error, a clarification from trials, and a reaching from this world to the Hereafter. In it is the perfection of your religion, and no one turns away from the Quran except toward the Fire.'"

I say: Narrations in this vein are numerous from the Prophet (may Allah bless him and his Progeny and grant them peace) and the Imams from his Progeny (peace be upon them).

In the tafsir of al-Ayyashi, from al-Fudayl ibn Yasar, who said: "I asked Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) about this narration: 'There is no verse in the Quran but that it has an outward (ظهر) and an inward (بطن), and there is no letter in it but that it has a limit (حد), and for every limit there is a lookout (مطلع).' What does he mean by 'outward' and 'inward'?" He said: "Its outward is its revelation (تنزيله) and its inward is its interpretation (تأويله). Some of it has passed and some of it has not yet come to be; it flows as the sun and the moon flow. Whenever something of it arrives, it occurs. Allah said: وَمَا يَعْلَمُ تَأْوِيلَهُ إِلَّا اللَّهُ وَالرَّاسِخُونَ فِي الْعِلْمِ (No one knows its interpretation except Allah and those firmly grounded in knowledge); we know it."

I say: The narration cited within the narration is what the group [of traditionists] narrated from the Prophet (may Allah bless him and his Progeny and grant them peace) with different wordings, though the meaning is one. As in Tafsir al-Safi from the Prophet (may Allah bless him and his Progeny and grant them peace): "Indeed, the Quran has an outward, an inward, a limit, and a lookout." And in it also from him (may Allah bless him and his Progeny and grant them peace): "Indeed, the Quran has an outward and an inward, and for its inward is an inward, up to seven inwards."

His saying (peace be upon him), "Some of it has passed and some of it is to come," apparently refers back to the Quran in consideration of its containing both the revelation and the interpretation. Thus, his saying, "it flows as the sun and the moon flow," applies to both together. In the case of revelation, it applies to the "flow" (الجري) which is a technical term in the reports for the application of the speech in its meaning to a referent (المصداق), such as the application of His saying: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اتَّقُوا اللَّهَ وَكُونُوا مَعَ الصَّادِقِينَ (O you who have faith! Be wary of Allah, and be with the truthful) [9:119], to every group of believers existing in ages subsequent to the time of the verse's revelation. This is one type of application. Another is the application of the verses of Jihad to the struggle against the self (جهاد النفس), and the application of the verses of the hypocrites to the transgressors among the believers; this is another type of application, more subtle than the first. Another is their application to those who practice self-watchfulness (المراقبة), remembrance (الذكر), and presence (الحضور) regarding their shortcomings and negligence in the remembrance of Allah the Exalted; this is another type, more subtle than what preceded it. And another is their application to them regarding their essential deficiency (القصور الذاتي) in fulfilling the right of Lordship (حق الربوبية); this is another type, the most subtle of all.

From here, it becomes clear, firstly: that the Quran has levels of intended meanings according to the levels of its people and their stations. Those who investigate the stations of faith (الإيمان) and sainthood (الولاية) have depicted meanings more subtle than what we have mentioned.

Secondly: that the outward (الظهر) and the inward (البطن) are relative matters. Every outward is an inward relative to what is more outward than it, and vice versa, as appears from the following narration.

In the tafsir of al-Ayyashi, from Jabir, who said: "I asked Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) about something regarding the interpretation of the Quran and he answered me. Then I asked him a second time and he answered me with a different answer. I said: 'May I be your ransom! You had answered this question with an answer other than this before today!' He said: 'O Jabir, indeed the Quran has an inward, and for the inward is an inward; and it has an outward, and for the outward is an outward. O Jabir, there is nothing further from the intellects of men than the interpretation of the Quran. Indeed, a verse may have its beginning concerning one thing, its middle concerning another, and its end concerning another, yet it is connected speech that can be directed toward various aspects.'"

In it also from him (peace be upon him) in a hadith, he said: "If a verse, when revealed concerning a people, were to die when those people died, nothing of the Quran would remain. However, the Quran flows from its beginning to its end as long as the heavens and the earth remain. For every people there is a verse they recite, and they are part of it, whether for good or for evil."

In al-Ma'ani, from Humran ibn A'yan, who said: "I asked Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) about the outward of the Quran and its inward. He said: 'Its outward is those concerning whom the Quran was revealed, and its inward is those who acted according to their deeds; what was revealed concerning the former flows into the latter.'"

In Tafsir al-Safi, from Ali (peace be upon him): "There is no verse but that it has four meanings: an outward (ظاهر), an inward (باطن), a limit (حد), and a lookout (مطلع). The outward is the recitation (التلاوة), the inward is the understanding (الفهم), the limit is the rulings of the lawful and the prohibited, and the lookout is what Allah intends from the servant through it."

I say: What is meant by "recitation" is the outward indication of the word, evidenced by the fact that he (peace be upon him) counted it among the meanings. Thus, what is meant by "understanding" in his explanation of the inward is the meaning that lies within the outward. What is meant by his saying, "it is the rulings of the lawful and the prohibited," is the outward gnoses received from the Quran in the initial or middle levels, in contrast to the "lookout" which is the highest level. Or, the limit and the lookout are relative, just as the outward and the inward are relative, as you have learned previously. Thus, every higher level is a lookout relative to the lower one.

The word muttala' (مطلع) is either with a damma on the mim, a doubled ta, and a fatha on the lam—a noun of place from "beholding" (الإطلاع)—or with a fatha on the mim and lam and a sukun on the ta—a noun of place from "rising" (الطلوع). It is what Allah intends from the servant through the verse, as he (peace be upon him) mentioned.
