They are the ones whose works have failed in this world and the Hereafter, and they will have no helpers. (22)
أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ نَصِيبًا مِّنَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ يُدْعَوْنَ إِلَىٰ كِتَـٰبِ ٱللَّهِ لِيَحْكُمَ بَيْنَهُمْ ثُمَّ يَتَوَلَّىٰ فَرِيقٌ مِّنْهُمْ وَهُم مُّعْرِضُونَ (٢٣)
Have you not regarded those who were given a share of the Book, who are summoned to the Book of Allah in order that it may judge between them, whereat a part of them refuse to comply and they are disregardful? (23)
ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ قَالُوا۟ لَن تَمَسَّنَا ٱلنَّارُ إِلَّآ أَيَّامًا مَّعْدُودَٰتٍ ۖ وَغَرَّهُمْ فِى دِينِهِم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَفْتَرُونَ (٢٤)
That is because they say, ‘The Fire shall not touch us except for a number of days,’ and they have been misled in their religion by what they used to fabricate. (24)
فَكَيْفَ إِذَا جَمَعْنَـٰهُمْ لِيَوْمٍ لَّا رَيْبَ فِيهِ وَوُفِّيَتْ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ مَّا كَسَبَتْ وَهُمْ لَا يُظْلَمُونَ (٢٥)
But how will it be [with them] when We gather them on a day in which there is no doubt, and every soul shall be recompensed fully for what it has earned, and they will not be wronged? (25)

(Commentary)

The verses address the state of the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب), who are the last of the three groups previously mentioned as subjects of discussion in this Surah. The most significant among them, in terms of the primary intent of the discourse, are the People of the Book from the Jews and the Christians; for it is regarding them and their affairs that the bulk of the Surah was revealed and to them it returns.

His saying, the Exalted: إن الدين عند الله الإسلام (Indeed, with Allah religion is Islam). The linguistic meaning of Islam (الإسلام) — submission and surrender — has already been discussed. It appears that this meaning is what is intended here, given the context of what He mentions regarding the disagreement of the People of the Book after knowledge had come to them, out of envy among themselves. Thus, the meaning would be: Indeed, religion with Allah, the Glorified, is one; there is no disagreement in it. He did not command His servants except with it, nor did He clarify for them in what He revealed of the Book to His prophets except it, nor did He establish the indicative signs (الآيات) except for it. This is Islam, which is submission (التسليم) to the truth—the truth of belief and the truth of action. In other words, it is submission to the clarification (البيان) issued from the station of Lordship (مقام الربوبية) regarding divine gnosis (المعارف) and legal rulings (الأحكام). Although it may vary in quantity and quality across the laws (شرائع) of His prophets and messengers—as Allah, the Glorified, relates in His Book—it is in reality nothing but a single matter. The differences between the laws are matters of perfection (الكمال) and deficiency (النقص), rather than contradiction (التضاد) or incompatibility (التنافي); the distinction between them is one of degrees. What unites them all is that they constitute submission and obedience to Allah, the Glorified, in what He desires from His servants as conveyed by the tongues of His messengers.

This, then, is the religion that Allah desired for His servants and clarified for them. Its necessary implication is that a human being should adopt what has been clarified to him of its gnosis with true clarity, and pause at ambiguities (الشبهات) with the stance of submission, without exercising personal discretion (تصرف) therein. As for the disagreement of the People of the Book—the Jews and the Christians—concerning religion despite the revelation of the Divine Book to them and His clarification of what constitutes religion with Him (which is submission to Him), it did not arise from ignorance on their part regarding the reality of the matter or the fact that religion is one. Rather, they were knowledgeable of that; it was only their envy (بغي) and injustice (ظلم) that drove them to it without excuse. This constituted disbelief (كفر) on their part in the signs of Allah (آيات الله) which clarified the truth and reality of the matter to them—not disbelief in Allah Himself, for they acknowledge Him. And whoever disbelieves in the signs of Allah, then indeed Allah is swift at reckoning (سريع الحساب); He reckons with him swiftly in his worldly life and the Hereafter. In this world, it is through disgrace and the stripping away of the happiness of life; in the Hereafter, it is through the painful punishment of the Fire.

The evidence for the general swiftness of reckoning encompassing both this world and the Hereafter is His saying, the Exalted, two verses later: أولئك الذين حبطت أعمالهم في الدنيا والآخرة وما لهم من ناصرين — "They are the ones whose works have come to naught in this world and the Hereafter, and they will have no helpers." (3:22)

From what has preceded, it becomes clear, firstly: that the meaning of religion being "with Allah" (عند الله) and its presence before Him, the Glorified, is a legislative presence (الحضور التشريعي). This means it is a single law (شرع) that does not differ except in degrees and according to the capacities of different nations, rather than it being one in terms of creation (التكوين)—meaning it being a single thing deposited in the human primordial nature (الفطرة) in a uniform manner.

Secondly: that the intended meaning of "the signs" (الآيات) is the signs of revelation (آيات الوحي) and the divine clarifications He bestowed upon His prophets, rather than the ontological signs (الآيات التكوينية) indicating Oneness (الوحدانية) and the accompanying divine gnosis.

The verse contains a threat to the People of the Book with that which is necessitated by envy (البغي), which is retribution (الانتقام), just as His saying, the Exalted, in the preceding verses: قل للذين كفروا ستغلبون وتحشرون إلى جهنم — "Say to the faithless: ‘You shall be overcome and mustered toward hell’" (3:12) contains a threat to the polytheists and disbelievers. Perhaps this is why He grouped the People of the Book and the polytheists together in the following verse in the address: قل للذين أوتوا الكتاب والأميين أأسلمتم — "Say to those who were given the Book and the unlettered: ‘Have you submitted?’" (3:20). This also carries an intimation of a threat.

His saying, the Exalted: فإن حاجوك فقل أسلمت وجهي لله ومن اتبعن (If they argue with you, say, ‘I have submitted my whole self to Allah, and [so has] he who follows me.’) The pronoun in "they argue with you" (حاجوك) refers to the People of the Book, which is apparent. What is intended is their arguing regarding the matter of disagreement, by saying: "Our disagreement is not due to envy on our part after clarification; rather, it is something our intellects, understandings, and our exertion (اجتهاد) in seeking knowledge of the realities of religion have led us to, without us abandoning submission to the side of the Truth, the Glorified. What you see and call toward, O Muhammad, is of this same nature," or they might say something similar. The evidence for this is His saying: "Say: I have submitted my whole self to Allah," and His saying: "And say to those who were given the Book and the unlettered: ‘Have you submitted?’" For these two sentences are arguments (حجة) brought forth to cut off their contention and disputation, not a turning away from arguing with them.

Its meaning, while maintaining its connection to what preceded it, is: Religion with Allah is Islam; the Books of Allah do not differ regarding it, nor does a sound intellect doubt it. It follows from this that there is no argument against you in your submission while you are a submitter (مسلم). If they argue with you regarding the matter of religion, then say: "I have submitted my whole self to Allah, and so has he who follows me." This is the religion, and there is no argument after religion in the matter of religion. Then ask them: Have they submitted? If they submit, they are rightly guided, and they must accept what Allah revealed to you and to those before you; there is no argument against them, and no contention between you after that. But if they turn away, do not contend with them or argue with them, for contention is not appropriate in a necessary (ضروري) matter—which is that religion is submission to Allah, the Glorified—and your duty is only to deliver the message.

The Glorified has joined the People of the Book and the unlettered (الاميين) in the verse by saying: "And say to those who were given the Book and the unlettered: ‘Have you submitted?’" because religion is common to them all, even if they differ in monotheism (التوحيد) and polytheism (التشريك).

Submission (الإسلام) was linked to the "face" (الوجه)—which is that part of a thing that meets you, or the face in the specific sense, because the submission of the face, as it contains most of the senses and perceptions, constitutes a submission of the entire body—to indicate the meaning of turning toward and humbling oneself (الخضوع) to the command of the Lord, the Exalted. The phrase "and he who follows me" (ومن اتبعن) was added to preserve the station of followership and as an honor for the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم.

His saying, the Exalted: وقل للذين أوتوا الكتاب والأميين أأسلمتم (And say to those who were given the Book and the unlettered: ‘Have you submitted?’) until the end of the verse. What is intended by "the unlettered" (الأميين) is the polytheists; they were called this because those placed in opposition to them were called "the People of the Book." Likewise, the People of the Book used to call them this, as related by the Exalted in His saying: ليس علينا في الأميين سبيل — "There is no blame upon us in respect of the unlettered" (3:75). The unlettered (الأمي) is one who neither writes nor reads.

In His saying, the Exalted: وإن تولوا فإنما عليك البلاغ والله بصير بالعباد — "But if they turn away, then your duty is only to communicate; and Allah sees best the servants" (3:20), there is an indication:

Firstly: of the prohibition against sophistry (المراء) and persistence in disputation; for arguing with one who denies a necessary truth (الضروري) is nothing but sophistry and obstinacy in discussion.

Secondly: that the judgment regarding people and the command (الأمر) belongs entirely to Allah, the Glorified. The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم is nothing but a messenger who delivers, not a dominating ruler, as the Exalted said: ليس لك من الأمر شيء — "You have no hand in the matter" (3:128), and He said: لست عليهم بمصيطر — "You are not a taskmaster over them" (88:22).

Thirdly: of a threat to the People of the Book and the polytheists; for concluding the speech with His saying: "and Allah sees best the servants," after His saying: "your duty is only to communicate," is not devoid of this. This is supported by the threat that occurs in a similar verse, which is His saying, the Exalted: قولوا آمنا بالله — "Say, ‘We have faith in Allah...’" until He says: ونحن له مسلمون فإن آمنوا بمثل ما آمنتم به فقد اهتدوا وإن تولوا فإنما هم في شقاق فسيكفيكهم الله وهو السميع العليم — "and to Him we submit. So if they believe in the like of what you believe in, then they are certainly guided; and if they turn away, then they are only [steeped] in defiance; so Allah will suffice you against them, and He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing" (2:136-137). The verse mentions that if the People of the Book turn away from Islam, they are persisting in opposition; then He threatens them with that which consoles the Prophet and puts his mind at ease. Thus, the verse—meaning His saying: "But if they turn away, then your duty is only to communicate"—is an allusion (كناية) to the command to leave them to their Lord and return their affair to Him; He sees best His servants and will judge them according to what their state necessitates and what the tongue of their capacity (لسان استعدادهم) asks of Him.

From here it becomes clear that what some commentators have mentioned—that the verse contains evidence for freedom of belief (حرية الاعتقاد) in the matter of religion and that there is no compulsion in it—is not well-founded; for the verse, as you have seen, is directed toward something else.

In His saying: "sees best the servants" (بصير بالعباد), where He employed the title of "servitude" (العبودية) and did not say: "sees best them" or "sees best the people" and the like, there is an intimation that His judgment is effective (نافذ) over them and will pass upon them; for they are His servants and subjects of His Lordship (مربوبون), whether they submit or turn away.

His saying, the Exalted: إن الذين يكفرون بآيات الله (Indeed, those who deny the signs of Allah) until the end of the verse. Although the speech in the verse is presented as a new beginning (استيناف), it is nevertheless not devoid of an intimation and clarification of the threat suggested by the end of the previous verse; for its content applies to the People of the Book, especially the Jews.

His saying: "they deny" (يكفرون) and "they kill" (يقتلون) in two places are in the present/continuous tense to indicate continuity (الاستمرار). They indicate that disbelief in the signs of Allah—which is disbelief after clarification—is envy (بغي), and the killing of prophets—which is killing without right—and the killing of those who call to equity (القسط) and justice and forbid injustice and envy, was a habit and a prevailing custom among them, as contained in the history of the Jews. They killed a great many and a vast multitude of their prophets and their servants who commanded the right and forbade the wrong; likewise, the Christians followed in their footsteps.

His saying: فبشرهم بعذاب أليم — "so give them the news of a painful punishment" (3:21) is an explicit statement of the encompassing of wrath (الغضب) and the descent of displeasure (السخط). It is not merely the punishment of the Hereafter, as evidenced by His saying, the Exalted, immediately following the verse: أولئك الذين حبطت أعمالهم في الدنيا والآخرة — "They are the ones whose works have come to naught in this world and the Hereafter" (3:22). Thus, they are given news of both worldly and otherworldly punishment. As for the otherworldly, it is the painful punishment of the Fire; as for the worldly, it is what they encountered of slaughter, expulsion, and the loss of wealth and lives, and Allah’s displeasure upon them by casting enmity and hatred among them until the Day of Resurrection, as the verses of the Mighty Book explicitly state.

In His saying, the Exalted: أولئك الذين حبطت أعمالهم في الدنيا والآخرة وما لهم من ناصرين — "They are the ones whose works have come to naught in this world and the Hereafter, and they will have no helpers" (3:22), there is an indication, firstly: of the nullification (حبط) of the deeds of one who kills a man because of his commanding the right or forbidding the wrong. Secondly: of the fact that intercession (الشفاعة) will not encompass him on the Day of Resurrection, due to His saying: "and they will have no helpers."

His saying, the Exalted: ألم تر إلى الذين أوتوا نصيباً من الكتاب (Have you not regarded those who were given a share of the Book?) until the end of the verse. This points toward the recording of envy (البغي) against the People of the Book according to what Allah the Exalted attributed to them: that they act with envy by adopting opposition and creating disagreement of word in religion. For when they are called to the judgment of the Book—the Book of Allah—between them, they do not submit to it, but turn away and disregard it. This is nothing but due to their being deluded by their saying: "The Fire shall not touch us..." and by what they fabricated against Allah in their religion.

What is intended by "those who were given a share of the Book" (الذين أوتوا نصيباً من الكتاب) is the People of the Book. It did not say: "were given the Book," but rather "were given a share of the Book" to indicate that what is in their hands of the Book is only a portion of it, not all of it. This is because their distortion (تحريف), alteration, and tampering with the Book of Allah caused many of its parts to vanish, as intimated by His saying at the end of the following verse: وغرهم في دينهم ما كانوا يفترون — "and they were deluded in their religion by what they used to fabricate" (3:24). In any case, the meaning—and Allah knows best—is that they turn away from the judgment of the Book of Allah, feeling empowered by what they said and deluded by what they fabricated from themselves, sufficing themselves with it over the Book.

His saying, the Exalted: ذلك بأنهم قالوا لن تمسنا النار (That is because they say, ‘The Fire shall not touch us...’) Its meaning is clear. Their being deluded by the fabrication that their own souls fabricated—even though a human being is not deceived by his own self when he knows it is a false deception—is because the deluded ones are other than the fabricators. Based on this, the attribution of the fabrication—which their predecessors resorted to—to these deluded ones among the successors is because they are a single nation, some of whom are pleased with the deeds of others.

Alternatively, it is because being deluded by the delusion of the self and being deluded by a false fabrication despite knowing it is a false fabrication—and the deluded one remembering that he is the one who fabricated the very lie he is deluded by—is not far-fetched for the People of the Book, and for the Jews in particular. Allah has related from them the like of this, indeed something even more amazing than that, where the Exalted said: وإذا لقوا الذين آمنوا قالوا آمنا وإذا خلا بعضهم إلى بعض قالوا أتحدثونهم بما فتح الله عليكم ليحاجوكم به عند ربكم أفلا تعقلون أولا يعلمون أن الله يعلم ما يسرون وما يعلنون — "When they meet the faithful, they say, ‘We believe,’ and when they are alone with one another, they say, ‘Do you discourse to them of what Allah has disclosed to you, so that they may use it as an argument against you before your Lord? Do you not exercise your reason?’ Do they not know that Allah knows whatever they hide and whatever they disclose?" (2:76-77).

Furthermore, a human being acts in his deeds and actions according to the states (الأحوال) or psychological traits (الملكات النفسانية) that have formed within him, and the images that his soul has beautified and adorned for him, rather than according to what he has acquired knowledge of. Just as one accustomed to using harmful substances like opium, tobacco, eating clay, and the like, uses them while knowing they are harmful and that using what is harmful is inappropriate; however, the form (الهيئة) established in his soul is pleasurable to him and attracts him toward use, leaving him no room for reflection or avoidance. Examples of this are many.

Thus, due to the entrenchment of pride (الكبر), envy (البغي), and the love of desires in their souls, they act according to what their fabrication calls them to. So their fabrication was what deluded them in their religion. Moreover, they repeatedly mentioned what they fabricated against Allah, the Glorified, and continued to repeat it and instill it into themselves until they became convinced of it—that is, they found tranquility and relied upon it through the instillation (التلقين) that produces the effect of knowledge, as explained by psychologists. Thus, the false fabrication, through repetition and instillation, came to delude them in their religion and prevent them from submitting to Allah and humbling themselves to the truth He revealed in His Book.

His saying, the Exalted: فكيف إذا جمعناهم ليوم لا ريب فيه (But how will it be when We gather them for a day in which there is no doubt?) until the end of the verse. The object of "how" (كيف) is elided, indicated by the context, such as "what will they do" or similar. The verse contains a threat to those who turned away when called to the Book of Allah to judge between them while they were disregarding it. However, since the intent was to clarify that they cannot frustrate Allah, the Glorified, the discourse took from their state on the Day of Resurrection—when they will be submissive—that which contrasts with their state in this world when called to the judgment of the Book of Allah while they were not submitting to it and were arrogant toward it. For this reason, the two statements were placed in parallel, and what will happen to them on the Day of Resurrection was expressed with the like of His saying: "when We gather them for a day in which there is no doubt," rather than saying: "when We give them life" or "resurrect them" or similar.

The meaning—and Allah knows best—is that they turn away and disregard when called to the Book of Allah to judge between them, out of delusion by what they fabricated in their religion and arrogance toward the truth. So how will they act when We gather them for a day in which there is no doubt—which is the Day of the Decisive Judgment and the True Decree—and every soul is paid in full for what it has earned, and the judgment is a judgment of justice and they shall not be wronged? If this is the case, it was incumbent upon them not to turn away and disregard, manifesting thereby that they can frustrate Allah or overcome His command; for all power belongs to Allah, and these are but days of respite and trial.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, narrated from Muhammad ibn Muslim, who said: "I asked him about His saying: إن الدين عند الله الإسلام (Indeed, with Allah religion is Islam). He said: 'That which contains faith (الإيمان).'"

And from Ibn Shahrashub, from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him) regarding His saying, the Exalted: إن الدين عند الله الإسلام (Indeed, with Allah religion is Islam), the verse; he said: "Submission (التسليم) to Ali ibn Abi Talib with the guardianship (الولاية)."

I say: This is of the nature of application (الجري); perhaps that is also what was intended by the previous narration.

And also from him, from Imam Ali (peace be upon him), who said: "I shall certainly attribute to Islam an attribution that no one before me has attributed, and no one after me shall attribute: Islam is submission (التسليم), and submission is certainty (اليقين), and certainty is confirmation (التصديق), and confirmation is affirmation (الإقرار), and affirmation is fulfillment (الأداء), and fulfillment is action (العمل). The believer takes his religion from his Lord; indeed, the believer is known by his faith in his action, and indeed the disbeliever is known by his disbelief through his denial.

O people! Your religion, your religion! For a bad deed (السيئة) within it is better than a good deed (الحسنة) in other than it. Indeed, a bad deed within it is forgiven, and indeed a good deed in other than it is not accepted."

I say: His (peace be upon him) saying: "I shall certainly attribute to Islam an attribution," what is intended by "attribution" (النسبة) is definition (التعريف), just as Surah al-Tawhid is called in the reports "the attribution of the Lord" (نسبة الرب). That which he defined it with is a definition by necessary consequence (اللازم) in other than the first, meaning his saying: "Islam is submission," for that is a verbal definition in which a term is defined by another term clearer than it. It is possible that what is intended by Islam is its technical meaning—which is this religion brought by Muhammad صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم—referring to His saying, the Exalted: "Indeed, with Allah religion is Islam," and by submission (التسليم) is meant humility and obedience in essence and action; thus everything returns to definition by necessary consequence.

The meaning is: This religion called Islam entails the human being's submission to Allah, the Glorified, in essence and action, and his placing himself and his deeds under His command and will—which is submission. Submission to Allah entails or necessitates certainty (اليقين) in Allah and the removal of doubt regarding Him. Certainty entails confirmation (التصديق) and manifesting the truth of the religion. Confirmation entails affirmation (الإقرار), which is the conviction of its stability and its being firm, not wavering in its place nor departing from its station. Its affirmation entails its fulfillment (الأداء), and its fulfillment entails action (العمل).

His (peace be upon him) saying: "And indeed a good deed in other than it is not accepted," what is intended by "non-acceptance" is the absence of reward in exchange for it in the Hereafter, or the absence of a beautiful, praised effect with Allah in this world through the happiness of life and in the Hereafter through the bliss of Paradise. This does not contradict what has been narrated that disbelievers are rewarded for their good deeds with something of the good things of this world; the Exalted said: فمن يعمل مثقال ذرة خيراً يره — "So whoever does an atom’s weight of good will see it" (99:7).

In al-Majma', from Abu Ubaydah al-Jarrah, who said: "I said: 'O Messenger of Allah, which of the people will have the most severe punishment on the Day of Resurrection?' He said: 'A man who killed a prophet, or a man who commanded the right or forbade the wrong.' Then he recited: الذين يقتلون النبيين بغير حق ويقتلون الذين يأمرون بالقسط من الناس (those who kill the prophets without right and kill those who call to equity from among the people). Then he said: 'O Abu Ubaydah, the Children of Israel killed forty-three prophets in one hour. Then one hundred and twelve men from the worshippers of the Children of Israel stood up and commanded those who killed them with the right and forbade them from the wrong, and they were all killed at the end of that day. That is what Allah mentioned.'"

I say: This meaning was narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from Ibn Jarir and Ibn Abi Hatim from Abu Ubaydah.

In al-Durr al-Manthur: Ibn Ishaq, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Ibn Abbas, who said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم entered the house of study (بيت المدراس) upon a group of Jews and called them to Allah. Nu'man ibn Amr and Harith ibn Zayd said to him: 'Upon what religion are you, O Muhammad?' He said: 'Upon the creed (ملة) of Abraham and his religion.' They said: 'But Abraham was a Jew.' The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم said to them: 'Then come to the Torah, for it is between us and you.' They refused him, so Allah revealed: ألم تر إلى الذين أوتوا نصيباً من الكتاب يدعون إلى كتاب الله ليحكم بينهم (Have you not regarded those who were given a share of the Book, being invited to the Book of Allah so that it may judge between them...) until His saying: وغرهم في دينهم ما كانوا يفترون (and they were deluded in their religion by what they used to fabricate)."

I say: Some have narrated that His saying, the Exalted: "Have you not regarded" (ألم تر) was revealed regarding the story of stoning (الرجم), and its mention will come in the discussion following His saying, the Exalted: يا أهل الكتاب قد جاءكم رسولنا يبين لكم كثيراً مما كنتم تخفون من الكتاب — "O People of the Book! Certainly Our Apostle has come to you, clarifying for you much of what you used to hide of the Book..." (5:15). Both narrations are solitary reports (آحاد) and are not of great strength.

Ayahs

قُلِ ٱللَّهُمَّ مَـٰلِكَ ٱلْمُلْكِ تُؤْتِى ٱلْمُلْكَ مَن تَشَآءُ وَتَنزِعُ ٱلْمُلْكَ مِمَّن تَشَآءُ وَتُعِزُّ مَن تَشَآءُ وَتُذِلُّ مَن تَشَآءُ ۖ بِيَدِكَ ٱلْخَيْرُ ۖ إِنَّكَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (٢٦)
Say, ‘O Allah, Master of all sovereignty! You give sovereignty to whomever You wish, and strip of sovereignty whomever You wish; You make mighty whomever You wish, and You degrade whomever You wish; all choice is in Your hand. Indeed You have power over all things.’ (26)
تُولِجُ ٱلَّيْلَ فِى ٱلنَّهَارِ وَتُولِجُ ٱلنَّهَارَ فِى ٱلَّيْلِ ۖ وَتُخْرِجُ ٱلْحَىَّ مِنَ ٱلْمَيِّتِ وَتُخْرِجُ ٱلْمَيِّتَ مِنَ ٱلْحَىِّ ۖ وَتَرْزُقُ مَن تَشَآءُ بِغَيْرِ حِسَابٍ (٢٧)
‘You make the night pass into the day and You make the day pass into the night. You bring forth the living from the dead and You bring forth the dead from the living, and You provide for whomever You wish without any reckoning.’ (27)

(Commentary)

The two verses are not devoid of a certain connection to the preceding discourse regarding the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب), specifically the Jews, as that discourse included warnings and threats of punishment in this world and the Hereafter. Among the forms of punishment is that Allah stripped them of sovereignty (الملك), and struck them with abasement (الذل) and destitution (المسكنة) until the Day of Resurrection, stifling them and stripping away their independence in leadership.

Furthermore, the objective of the Surah—as previously mentioned—is to clarify that Allah, the Glorified, is the Sustainer (القائم) of the creation of the world and its governance. Thus, He is the Master of Sovereignty (مالك الملك); He grants sovereignty to whom He wills and He honors whom He wills. In short, He is the Giver of good to whom He wills, and He is the Taker and Stripper of sovereignty, honor, and every good from whom He wills. Therefore, the content of the two verses does not fall outside the objective of the Surah.

His saying, the Exalted: قل اللهم مالك الملك (Say, ‘O Allah, Master of all sovereignty!) is a command to seek refuge in Allah, the Exalted, in Whose hand is absolute good and Who possesses absolute power, in order to be liberated from these illusory claims that have taken root in the hearts of the hypocrites and those among the polytheists and the People of the Book who rebel against the truth. They went astray and perished due to the sovereignty, honor, and wealth they presumed for themselves apart from Allah, the Glorified. The one seeking refuge presents himself to the emanation of the Bestower of good and the Provider for whom He wills without account.

Ownership (المِلك) — with a kasrah on the mim — is something we recognize among ourselves and are familiar with, having no doubt about its essence.

Of ownership (المِلك), there is that which is ontological (حقيقي) — pertaining to the actual state of being — which is for a thing, such as a human being for example, to be such that it is correct for him to dispose of something in any way possible according to creation and existence (التكوين والوجود). Just as it is possible for a human to dispose of his sight by employing it or neglecting it in whatever manner he wishes and intends, and likewise with his hand by clenching and spreading it, or by taking and leaving with it, and so on. Inevitably, between the owner and his property in this sense, there is an ontological bond (رابطة حقيقية) that is not subject to change, necessitating the subsistence of the owned through the owner in a manner of subsistence that is indispensable and does not depart from him except through annihilation, like sight and the hand when they depart from the human. Of this type is the ownership (المِلك) of the Exalted over the world and all its parts and affairs absolutely; thus, He may dispose of whatever He wills, however He wills.

And of ownership (المِلك), there is that which is conventional and conceptual (وضعي اعتباري) — pertaining to social constructs and legal fictions — which is for a thing, such as a human being, to be such that it is correct for him to dispose of something as he wishes according to the bond established by the rational members of society for the purpose of attaining social goals and objectives. This is merely an imitation by them of what they recognized in existence of ontological ownership and its effects; thus, they conceptualized its like within the context of their society through convention and claim, so that they might attain from these objects and goods benefits similar to what the ontological owner attains from his ontological, creative property.

Because the bond between the owner and the owned in this type of ownership is through convention and conceptualization (الوضع والاعتبار), we see in it what we see of the permissibility of change and transformation. Thus, it is permissible for this type of ownership to be transferred from one person to another through sale, gift, and other means of transfer.

As for sovereignty (المُلك) — with a dammah — although it is of the same genus as ownership (المِلك), it is the ownership of that which the collective of people owns. For the sovereign (المليك) is the owner of what his subjects own; he may dispose of what they own without their disposal opposing his, nor their will obstructing his will. Thus, it is in reality ownership over ownership, which we term "longitudinal ownership" (الملك الطولي), like the ownership of a master over a slave and what is in the slave's hand. For this reason, sovereignty (المُلك) has the same divisions we mentioned for ownership (المِلك).

Allah, the Glorified, is the Master of everything in an absolute sense. As for His being the Owner (مالك) of everything absolutely, it is because He possesses absolute Lordship (الربوبية) and absolute Self-Subsistence (القيمومة) over everything, for He is the Creator of everything and the God of everything. The Exalted said: ذَٰلِكُمُ اللَّهُ رَبُّكُمْ خَالِقُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ لَّا إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ فَأَنَّىٰ تُؤْفَكُونَ — "That is Allah, your Lord, the creator of all things, there is no god except Him. So where do you stray?" (40:62).

And the Exalted said: لَّهُ مَا فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَمَا فِي الْأَرْضِ — "To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth" (2:255), among other verses indicating that everything called a "thing" is subsistent in its essence through Him, needy in its essence for Him, and does not possess independence apart from Him. Thus, nothing prevents Him from what He intends regarding them or within them. This is ownership (المِلك) — with a kasrah — as previously mentioned.

As for His being the Sovereign (مليك) absolutely, it is a necessary consequence of His being the absolute Owner of all existents. For existents themselves own one another, such as causes (الأسباب) owning their effects (مسبباتها), and things owning their active powers, and active powers owning their actions—just as a human owns his limbs and his active powers of hearing, sight, and so on, and these powers own their actions. Since Allah, the Glorified, owns everything, He owns everyone who owns anything among them, and He owns what they own. This is sovereignty (المُلك) — with a dammah — so He is the Sovereign absolutely. The Exalted said: لَهُ الْمُلْكُ وَلَهُ الْحَمْدُ — "To Him belongs all sovereignty and to Him belongs all praise" (64:1), and the Exalted said: عِندَ مَلِيكٍ مُّقْتَدِرٍ — "in the presence of an all-powerful King" (54:55), among other verses. This is the ontological (الحقيقي) form of ownership and sovereignty.

As for the conceptual (الاعتباري) form, He, the Exalted, is the Owner (مالك) because He is the Giver to everyone who owns any wealth; had He not owned it, that would not be correct of Him, and He would be a giver of what He does not own to one who does not own. The Exalted said: وَآتُوهُم مِّن مَّالِ اللَّهِ الَّذِي آتَاكُمْ — "and give them out of the wealth of Allah which He has given you" (24:33).

And He, the Exalted, is the Sovereign (مليك) who owns what is in the hands of people because He is the Legislator and Judge (شارع حاكم) who disposes by His decree in what people own, just as kings dispose of the wealth held by their subjects. The Exalted said: قُلْ أَعُوذُ بِرَبِّ النَّاسِ مَلِكِ النَّاسِ — "Say, ‘I seek exulted refuge with the Lord of men, the King of men" (114:1-2). And the Exalted said: وَآتَاكُم مِّن كُلِّ مَا سَأَلْتُمُوهُ وَإِن تَعُدُّوا نِعْمَتَ اللَّهِ لَا تُحْصُوهَا — "He gave you all that you had asked Him. If you enumerate Allah’s blessings, you will not be able to count them" (14:34). And the Exalted said: وَأَنفِقُوا مِمَّا جَعَلَكُم مُّسْتَخْلَفِينَ فِيهِ — "and spend out of that in which He has made you successors" (57:7). And the Exalted said: وَمَا لَكُمْ أَلَّا تُنفِقُوا فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ وَلِلَّهِ مِيرَاثُ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ — "Why should you not spend in the way of Allah, when to Allah belongs the heritage of the heavens and the earth?" (57:10). And the Exalted said: لِّمَنِ الْمُلْكُ الْيَوْمَ لِلَّهِ الْوَاحِدِ الْقَهَّارِ — "‘To whom does the sovereignty belong today?’ ‘To Allah, the One, the All-paramount!’" (40:16). Thus, He, the Exalted, owns what is in our hands before us, owns it with us, and will inherit it after us—exalted is His sovereignty.

From reflection upon what has preceded, it becomes clear that His saying, the Exalted: اللهم مالك الملك (O Allah, Master of all sovereignty) is intended:

First: To clarify His ownership (المِلك) — with a kasrah — of every sovereignty (المُلك) — with a dammah — and the ownership of the Sovereign (المُلك) is ownership over ownership. Thus, He is the King of kings, Who is the Giver of sovereignty to every sovereign, as the Exalted said: أَنْ آتَاهُ اللَّهُ الْمُلْكَ — "because Allah had given him kingdom" (2:258), and the Exalted said: وَآتَيْنَاهُم مُّلْكًا عَظِيمًا — "and We have given them a great kingdom" (4:54).

Second: By placing the Majestic Name (لفظ الجلالة) first, it indicates the cause; for He, the Exalted, is the Master of Sovereignty because He is Allah—magnificent is His grandeur—and this is evident.

Third: That what is meant by sovereignty (الملك) in the noble verse—and Allah knows best—is that which is more general than the ontological and the conceptual. For what is mentioned of His command in the first verse—namely His saying: تؤتي الملك من تشاء وتنزع الملك ممن تشاء وتعز من تشاء وتذل من تشاء (You give sovereignty to whomever You wish, and take away sovereignty from whomever You wish; You exalt whomever You wish, and abase whomever You wish)—pertains to the affairs of conceptual sovereignty (الملك الاعتباري), as we shall clarify. And what He mentioned in the second verse pertains to the affairs of ontological sovereignty (الملك الحقيقي). Thus, He is the Master of Sovereignty absolutely.

His saying, the Exalted: تؤتي الملك من تشاء وتنزع الملك ممن تشاء (You give sovereignty to whomever You wish, and take away sovereignty from whomever You wish). Sovereignty (الملك), in its absolute sense, includes every sovereignty, whether rightful or false, just or tyrannical. For sovereignty—as previously explained regarding the verse: أَنْ آتَاهُ اللَّهُ الْمُلْكَ (2:258)—is in itself one of the gifts (مواهب) of Allah and a blessing (نعمة) suitable for producing good effects in human society. Allah has disposed souls toward the love of it and the desire for it. Sovereignty that is assumed by those unworthy of it is not blameworthy insofar as it is sovereignty; rather, the blame lies either in its assumption by one who is unfit—such as one who assumes it through tyranny and usurpation—or in his wicked conduct despite his ability for good conduct. This second point also returns, in a way, to the first.

In another sense, sovereignty is a blessing from Allah, the Glorified, for the one who is worthy of it, and a tribulation (نقمة) for the one who is unworthy. In any case, it is attributed to Allah, the Glorified, and is a trial (فتنة) by which He tests His servants.

It has been previously mentioned that the suspension [of an act] upon the Will (المشية) in the acts of the Exalted—as in this verse—does not mean that the act occurs haphazardly (جزافاً)—exalted is He above that. Rather, what is meant is that He, the Exalted, is not compelled in His act nor forced into it. Thus, He, the Exalted, does what He does by His absolute Will without anyone compelling or coercing Him, and His act always proceeds according to wisdom (المصلحة).

His saying, the Exalted: وتعز من تشاء وتذل من تشاء (You exalt whomever You wish, and abase whomever You wish). Honor (العز) is for a thing to be such that it is difficult to attain. Therefore, it is said of a thing that is rare in existence that it is "dear in existence" (عزيز الوجود), meaning difficult to find. And it is said "the dear one of the people" (عزيز القوم) for the one among them who is difficult to subdue or overcome; he is difficult to attain through subjugation and conquest, and difficult to attain in terms of his status among them and his possession of all they have without the reverse being true. Then it was used for every difficulty, as it is said: "This is dear (يعز) to me," meaning it is hard for me. The Exalted said: عَزِيزٌ عَلَيْهِ مَا عَنِتُّمْ — "grievous to him is your distress" (9:128), meaning it is hard for him. It is also used for every victory, as it is said: "Whoever conquers (عزّ) plunders," and the Exalted said: وَعَزَّنِي فِي الْخِطَابِ — "and he has overborne me in speech" (38:23), meaning he overcame me. The origin of its meaning is what has been mentioned.

Its opposite is abasement (الذل), which is the ease of being attained through actual or presumed subjugation. The Exalted said: ضُرِبَتْ عَلَيْهِمُ الذِّلَّةُ وَالْمَسْكَنَةُ — "and they were struck with vileness and destitution" (2:61). And the Exalted said: وَاخْفِضْ لَهُمَا جَنَاحَ الذُّلِّ — "and lower to them the wing of humility" (17:24). And the Exalted said: أَذِلَّةٍ عَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ — "humble towards the believers" (5:54).

Honor (العزة) is among the necessary concomitants of absolute sovereignty. Anyone other than Him, if they own something, it is the Exalted who has granted them that and made them its owner. If one rules over a people, it is the Exalted who gave him that. Thus, honor belongs purely to the Exalted, and whatever others possess of it is only through His granting and His grace. The Exalted said: أَيَبْتَغُونَ عِندَهُمُ الْعِزَّةَ فَإِنَّ الْعِزَّةَ لِلَّهِ جَمِيعًا — "Do they seek honour with them? Indeed all honour belongs to Allah" (4:139). And the Exalted said: وَلِلَّهِ الْعِزَّةُ وَلِرَسُولِهِ وَلِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ — "yet all might belongs to Allah and His Apostle and the faithful" (63:8). This is the ontological honor (العزة الحقيقية); as for anything else, it is merely abasement in the form of honor. The Exalted said: بَلِ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فِي عِزَّةٍ وَشِقَاقٍ — "Rather the faithless are in [the grip of] pride and defiance" (38:2). For this reason, He followed it with His saying: كَمْ أَهْلَكْنَا مِن قَبْلِهِم مِّن قَرْنٍ فَنَادَوا وَّلَاتَ حِينَ مَنَاصٍ — "How many a generation We have destroyed before them! Then they cried out [for help], but it was no longer the time for escape" (38:3).

Abasement (الذل), by contrast, follows the same rule as honor in reverse: everything other than the Exalted is abased in itself, except for those whom Allah has honored (تعز من تشاء وتذل من تشاء).

His saying, the Exalted: بيدك الخير إنك علی كل شيء قدير (All good is in Your hand. Indeed You have power over all things). The origin of the meaning of "good" (الخير) is selection (الانتخاب). We only call a thing "good" because we compare it to something else, wanting to choose one of them, so we select it and it is "good." We do not choose it except because it contains what we want and intend. Thus, what we want is the good in reality. Even if we wanted it for the sake of something else, then that other thing is the good in reality, and the first is good because of it. Therefore, the good in reality is that which is sought for itself; it is called "good" because it is the sought-after object when compared to others, and it is the one selected from among things when we want one of them and hesitate in choosing it from among them.

Thus, a thing—as you have learned—is only called "good" because it is selected when compared to something else, being preferred in relation to that other thing. Therefore, its meaning contains a relation to another, which is why it was said to be a superlative form (صيغة التفضيل), originally being akhyar (أخير). However, it is not the standard superlative (af'al al-tafdil); rather, it accepts the application of the meaning of preference to its instance, thus relating to others just as the standard superlative does. It is said: "Zayd is better (أفضل) than 'Amr," and "Zayd is the best (أفضلهما) of them." Similarly, it is said: "Zayd is better (خير) than 'Amr," and "Zayd is the best (خيرهما) of them."

If khayr (خير) were a standard superlative form, it would follow its rules, and one would say afdal, afadil, fudla, and fudlayat. But this does not occur with khayr; rather, one says khayr, khayrah, akhyar, and khayrat, just as one says shaykh, shaykhah, ashyakh, and shaykhat. Thus, it is an assimilative adjective (صفة مشبهة).

This is supported by its use in contexts where the meaning of a standard superlative would not be sound, such as the saying of the Exalted: قُلْ مَا عِندَ اللَّهِ خَيْرٌ مِّنَ اللَّهْوِ — "Say, ‘What is with Allah is better than diversion" (62:11). There is no "good" in diversion for the superlative meaning to be sound. They have excused this and its likes by saying that the meaning of preference is stripped from it in such cases, but this is as you see [weak]. The truth is that khayr only yields the meaning of selection, and the inclusion of some good in its opposite (the thing compared to) is a common characteristic in most instances.

It appears from what has preceded that Allah, the Glorified, is the Good (الخير) absolutely, because He is the One to Whom everything ends, to Whom everything returns, and Whom everything seeks and intends. However, the Noble Quran does not apply "the Good" (الخير) to Him as a proper name like His other Beautiful Names—magnificent are His names. Rather, it applies it to Him as a description, such as the saying of the Exalted: وَاللَّهُ خَيْرٌ وَأَبْقَىٰ — "and Allah is better and more lasting" (20:73), and the saying of the Exalted: أَأَرْبَابٌ مُّتَفَرِّقُونَ خَيْرٌ أَمِ اللَّهُ الْوَاحِدُ الْقَهَّارُ — "Are discrete lords better, or Allah, the One, the All-paramount?" (12:39).

Yes, it has been used as a name through genitive construction (الإضافة), such as the saying of the Exalted: وَاللَّهُ خَيْرُ الرَّازِقِينَ — "and Allah is the best of providers" (62:11); and His saying: وَهُوَ خَيْرُ الْحَاكِمِينَ — "and He is the best of judges" (7:87); and His saying: وَهُوَ خَيْرُ الْفَاصِلِينَ — "and He is the best of deciders" (6:57); and His saying: وَهُوَ خَيْرُ النَّاصِرِينَ — "and He is the best of helpers" (3:150); and His saying: وَاللَّهُ خَيْرُ الْمَاكِرِينَ — "and Allah is the best of devisers" (3:54); and His saying: وَأَنتَ خَيْرُ الْفَاتِحِينَ — "and You are the best of deciders" (7:89); and His saying: وَأَنتَ خَيْرُ الْغَافِرِينَ — "and You are the best of forgivers" (7:155); and His saying: وَأَنتَ خَيْرُ الْوَارِثِينَ — "and You are the best of inheritors" (21:89); and His saying: وَأَنتَ خَيْرُ الْمُنزِلِينَ — "and You are the best of those who bring [guests] ashore" (23:29); and His saying: وَأَنتَ خَيْرُ الرَّاحِمِينَ — "and You are the best of the merciful" (23:109).

Perhaps the reason for all this is the consideration of the meaning of selection (الانتخاب) inherent in the root of khayr. Thus, it was not applied as an absolute name to Him, the Exalted, to safeguard His presence from being compared to others in an absolute manner, while all faces have humbled themselves before His presence. As for naming through genitive construction and relation, as well as description in contexts that necessitate it, there is no objection to that.

The phrase—namely His saying, the Exalted: بيدك الخير (All good is in Your hand)—indicates the restriction (حصر) of good to Him, the Exalted, due to the presence of the definite article [in al-khayr] and the advancement of the prepositional phrase which is the predicate. The meaning is that the matter of every sought-after good belongs to You, and You are the Giver and Emanator of it.

Thus, the phrase is in the position of providing the reason (التعليل) for the preceding phrases—namely His saying: تؤتي الملك من تشاء (You give sovereignty to whomever You wish), etc. This is of the type of explaining the specific by that which encompasses it and others; meaning that the good which He, the Exalted, gives is more general than sovereignty and honor, and this is evident.

Just as it is correct to explain the granting of sovereignty and honoring by the good that is in His hand, it is also correct to explain the stripping of sovereignty and abasement by it. For although they are evils (شرين), evil is nothing but the absence of good. Thus, the stripping of sovereignty is nothing but the absence of honoring. Therefore, the termination of every good in Him, the Exalted, is what necessitates the termination of every deprivation of good in Him, the Exalted, in a certain way. Yes, what must be negated from Him, the Exalted, is being characterized by what is unsuitable for His holy presence, such as the deficiencies of the servants' actions and the ugliness of sins, except in the sense of forsaking (الخذلان) and the withholding of success (عدم التوفيق), as previously discussed.

In short, there are ontological (تكوينيان) good and evil, such as sovereignty and honor, and the stripping of sovereignty and abasement. Ontological good is an existential matter from the gift of Allah, the Exalted, while ontological evil is merely the non-granting of good. There is no harm in its attribution to Allah, the Glorified, for He is the Owner of good and none other owns it. If He gives someone some good, then to Him belongs the command and to Him belongs the praise. If He does not give or He withholds, no one else has a right over Him such that it would be binding upon Him; thus, His withholding of the gift would not be injustice (ظلم). Furthermore, His giving and His withholding are both coupled with general wisdoms (المصالح العامة) that play a role in the well-being of the system revolving among the parts of the world.

And there are legislative (تشريعيان) good and evil, which are the categories of acts of obedience and sins. These are the actions proceeding from the human being insofar as they are attributed to his choice (اختياره), and from this perspective, they are certainly not attributed to anyone other than the human. This attribution is the criterion for their beauty and ugliness; had choice not been presumed in their occurrence, they would not be characterized by beauty or ugliness. From this perspective, they are not attributed to Him, the Exalted, except in terms of His granting success or withholding it for wisdoms that necessitate that.

Thus, it has become clear that all good is in the hand of Allah, and by that, the order of the world is organized in its inclusion of every attainment and deprivation, and every good and evil.

Some commentators have mentioned that in His saying: بيدك الخير (All good is in Your hand), there is a conciseness through omission (إيجاز بالحذف), and the implied meaning is: "In Your hand is good and evil," just as it was said regarding the like of this in His saying, the Exalted: وَجَعَلَ لَكُمْ سَرَابِيلَ تَقِيكُمُ الْحَرَّ — "and He has made for you garments to protect you from the heat" (16:81), meaning "and the cold."

It seems the reason for this was to flee from Mu'tazilism, due to the Mu'tazilite view that evils are not attributed to Him, the Exalted. This is a strange audacity against His speech, the Exalted. Although the Mu'tazilites erred in negating the attribution absolutely—even through mediation—this strange implication is not permissible. The discussion on this and the clarification of the reality of the matter has already preceded.

His saying, the Exalted: إنك علی كل شيء قدير (Indeed You have power over all things) is in the position of providing the reason for good being in His hand. For absolute power over everything necessitates that no one is capable of anything except by His making them capable of it. If someone were capable of something without their power being attributed to His making them capable, then their object of power would be outside the scope of His power, and He would not be powerful over all things. Since His power has this scope, every presumed good is within His power; and every good emanated by another is attributed to Him, emanated from His hands. Thus, He is also its owner. Therefore, the genus of good—from which nothing escapes—is in His hand. This is the restriction indicated by His saying, the Exalted: بيدك الخير.

His saying, the Exalted: تولج الليل في النهار وتولج النهار في الليل (You make the night pass into the day and You make the day pass into the night). Al-Wuluj (الولوج) is entry. It is apparent, as they have mentioned, that what is meant by making the night pass into the day and the day into the night is what is observed of the variation of night and day throughout the year, according to the variation of the latitudes of regions and places on the surface of the earth, and the variation of the sun's inclination. Thus, the days begin to lengthen and the nights to shorten, which is the passing of the day into the night after the nights have reached their maximum length from the beginning of winter to the beginning of summer. Then the nights begin to lengthen and the days to shorten, which is the passing of the night into the day after the day has reached its maximum length from the beginning of summer to the beginning of winter. All this occurs in the northern regions, and the matter in the southern regions is the opposite of the northern ones. Thus, length on one side is shortness on the other. So He, the Exalted, makes the night pass into the day and the day into the night constantly. As for the equality at the equator and the poles, it is only according to perception; as for reality, the rule of change is constant and all-encompassing.

His saying, the Exalted: وتخرج الحي من الميت وتخرج الميت من الحي (and You bring forth the living from the dead and You bring forth the dead from the living). This is the bringing forth of the believer from the loins of the disbeliever, and the bringing forth of the disbeliever from the loins of the believer. For He, the Exalted, called faith life and light, and disbelief death and darkness, as the Exalted said: أَوَمَن كَانَ مَيْتًا فَأَحْيَيْنَاهُ وَجَعَلْنَا لَهُ نُورًا يَمْشِي بِهِ فِي النَّاسِ كَمَن مَّثَلُهُ فِي الظُّلُمَاتِ لَيْسَ بِخَارِجٍ مِّنْهَا — "Is he who was lifeless, then We gave him life and maintained for him a light by which he walks among the people, like one whose likeness is [that of one] in a manifold darkness from which he cannot emerge?" (6:122). It is possible that what is meant is more general than that, including the creation of living things like plants and animals from the insentient earth, and the returning of living things to the earth by causing them to die. For His speech, the Exalted, is explicit in that He changes the dead into the living and the living into the dead. The Exalted said: ثُمَّ أَنشَأْنَاهُ خَلْقًا آخَرَ فَتَبَارَكَ اللَّهُ أَحْسَنُ الْخَالِقِينَ ثُمَّ إِنَّكُم بَعْدَ ذَٰلِكَ لَمَيِّتُونَ — "then We produced him as another creation. So blessed is Allah, the best of creators! Then indeed you shall die after that" (23:14-15), among other verses.

As for the view held by some natural scientists—that life, which ends at its germs, follows a course from one living germ to another like it without ending at insentient matter—due to their denial of originated existence (الكون الحادث), it is invalidated by the perceptible death established by experience in the germs of life. Thus, the transformation of life into death reveals the connection between them. The rest of the discussion belongs elsewhere.

The verse—namely His saying, the Exalted: تولج الليل في النهار (You make the night pass into the day), etc.—describes His disposal, the Exalted, in ontological sovereignty (الملك الحقيقي التكويني), just as the preceding verse—namely His saying: تؤتي الملك من تشاء (You give sovereignty to whomever You wish), etc.—describes His disposal in conceptual, conventional sovereignty (الملك الاعتباري الوضعي) and its concomitants.

In each of the two verses, four types of disposal have been placed in a manner of correspondence (التقابل). In the first, the giving of sovereignty and its stripping were placed, and corresponding to them in the second is the passing of the night into the day and its reverse. The exalting and abasing were placed, and corresponding to them in the second is the bringing forth of the living from the dead and its reverse. In this, there is a wondrous elegance and a subtle suitability that is not hidden. For the giving of sovereignty is a type of subjugation of some individuals over others by removing a measure of their natural freedom and release, just as the night is subjugated over the day by the night removing some of what the day made manifest; and the stripping of sovereignty is the opposite of that. Likewise, the granting of honor is a type of giving life to one whose mention was extinguished and whose trace was hidden were it not for it, similar to bringing forth the living from the dead; and abasement is the opposite of that. In honor there is life, and in abasement there is death.

There is another perspective: Allah counted the day in His speech as a "visible sign" (آية مبصرة) and the night as a "blotted out sign" (آية ممحوة). The Exalted said: فَمَحَوْنَا آيَةَ اللَّيْلِ وَجَعَلْنَا آيَةَ النَّهَارِ مُبْصِرَةً — "then We blotted out the sign of the night, and made the sign of the day a visible one" (17:12). The manifestation of this affirmation and blotting out in human society is the appearance of sovereignty and dominion and its disappearance. He also counted life and death as the two sources of effects such as knowledge and power, as the Exalted said: أَمْوَاتٌ غَيْرُ أَحْيَاءٍ وَمَا يَشْعُرُونَ أَيَّانَ يُبْعَثُونَ — "Dead, not living; and they do not know when they will be resurrected" (16:21). He reserved honor for Himself, His Messenger, and the believers, saying: وَلِلَّهِ الْعِزَّةُ وَلِرَسُولِهِ وَلِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ — "yet all might belongs to Allah and His Apostle and the faithful" (63:8), and they are the ones whom He mentions with life. Thus, honor and abasement became manifestations in human society for life and death. For this reason, He corresponded what He mentioned in the first verse of giving sovereignty and its stripping, and exalting and abasing, with what is in the second verse of passing the night into the day and its reverse, and bringing forth the living from the dead and its reverse.

Then, a correspondence occurred between what He mentioned in the second verse: وترزق من تشاء بغير حساب (and You provide for whomever You wish without any reckoning), and what He mentioned in the first verse: بيدك الخير (All good is in Your hand), as will be explained.

His saying, the Exalted: وترزق من تشاء بغير حساب (and You provide for whomever You wish without any reckoning). The aforementioned correspondence implies that His saying: وترزق (and You provide), etc., is an explanation for what preceded it of giving sovereignty, honor, passing [the night into day], and others. Thus, the conjunction is an explanatory conjunction (عطف تفسير). It would then be of the type of explaining a specific rule by that which is more general than it, just as His saying: بيدك الخير (All good is in Your hand) is of this type in relation to what preceded it. The meaning is: You dispose in Your creation with these disposals because You provide for whom You wish without account.

The Meaning of Provision (الرزق) in the Quran

Provision (الرزق) is well-known. What is derived from its instances of usage is that it contains a trace of the meaning of "giving" (العطاء), such as the king providing for the soldier. What the king designates for his soldier of what he is given as a whole is called a rizqah (رزقة). It used to be specific to what one is nourished by and nothing else, as the Exalted said: وَعَلَى الْمَوْلُودِ لَهُ رِزْقُهُنَّ وَكِسْوَتُهُنَّ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ — "and their maintenance and their clothing, in accordance with honourable norms, shall be the father’s responsibility" (2:233). Thus, He did not count clothing as provision (رزق).

Then its meaning was expanded, so everything that reaches a human of nourishment was counted as provision, as if it were a gift according to fortune and luck even if its giver is unknown. Then it was generalized, so everything that reaches a thing from which it benefits was called provision, even if it is not nourishment, such as other advantages of life like wealth, status, kin, supporters, beauty, knowledge, and others. The Exalted said: تَسْأَلُهُمْ خَرْجًا فَخَرَاجُ رَبِّكَ خَيْرٌ وَهو خَيْرُ الرَّازِقِينَ — "Do you ask them for a tribute? But the tribute of your Lord is better, and He is the best of providers" (23:72). And He said, recounting from Shu'ayb: قَالَ يَا قَوْمِ أَرَأَيْتُمْ إِن كُنتُ عَلَىٰ بَيِّنَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّي وَرَزَقَنِي مِنْهُ رِزْقًا حَسَنًا — "He said, ‘O my people! Tell me, should I stand on a manifest proof from my Lord, who has provided me a good provision from Himself" (11:88), where what is meant is prophethood and knowledge, among other verses.

What is derived from the saying of the Exalted: إِنَّ اللَّهَ هُوَ الرَّزَّاقُ ذُو الْقُوَّةِ الْمَتِينُ — "Indeed it is Allah who is the All-provider, Lord of all might, the Ever-pitted" (51:58)—and the context is one of restriction (حصر)—is:

First: That provision, according to reality, is not attributed except to Him. What is attributed to others besides Him, the Exalted, of provision—as confirmed by the likes of His saying, the Exalted: وَاللَّهُ خَيْرُ الرَّازِقِينَ — "and Allah is the best of providers" (62:11), where He established providers and counted Himself the best of them; and His saying: وَارْزُقُوهُمْ فِيهَا وَاكْسُوهُمْ — "and provide for them out of it and clothe them" (4:5)—all of that is of the type of attribution through another. Just as sovereignty and honor belong to Allah for His essence, and to others through His giving and permission, so He is the Provider and none other.

Second: That whatever the creation benefits from in their existence of the good they attain is their provision, and Allah is its Provider. This is indicated—in addition to the verses of provision in their abundance—by many other verses, such as those indicating that creation, command, judgment, ownership (المِلك), will, disposal, and good belong purely to Allah—magnificent is His authority.

Third: That what a human benefits from in a prohibited manner, by it being a cause for sin, is not attributed to Him, the Exalted. For He, the Exalted, negated the attribution of sin to Himself from the perspective of legislation (التشريع). The Exalted said: قُلْ إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَا يَأْمُرُ بِالْفَحْشَاءِ أَتَقُولُونَ عَلَى اللَّهِ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ — "Say, ‘Indeed Allah does not enjoin indecency. Do you attribute to Allah what you do not know?’" (7:28). And the Exalted said: إِنَّ اللَّهَ يَأْمُرُ بِالْعَدْلِ وَالْإِحْسَانِ — "Indeed Allah enjoins justice and kindness" until He said: وَيَنْهَىٰ عَنِ الْفَحْشَاءِ وَالْمُنكَرِ — "and He forbids indecency and wrongdoing" (16:90). Far be it from Him, the Glorified, that He should forbid something and then command it, or forbid it and then restrict His provision to it.

There is no contradiction between a prohibited benefit not being provision according to legislation, and its being provision according to creation (التكوين). For there is no religious obligation (تكليف) in creation such that it would entail ugliness. What the Quran explains of the generality of provision is only according to the state of creation. The divine explanation is not limited to simple, common understandings such that it would turn away from addressing ontological truths (المعارف الحقيقية). In the Quran is a healing for all hearts, by which none are harmed except the losers. The Exalted said: وَنُنَزِّلُ مِنَ الْقُرْآنِ مَا هُوَ شِفَاءٌ وَرَحْمَةٌ لِّلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَلَا يَزِيدُ الظَّالِمِينَ إِلَّا خَسَارًا — "We send down in the Quran that which is a cure and mercy for the faithful, and it increases the wrongdoers only in loss" (17:82).

Furthermore, the verses attribute the sovereignty held by the likes of Nimrod and Pharaoh, and the wealth and finery in the hands of the likes of Korah, to the giving of Allah, the Glorified. This is only because all of that is by the permission of Allah; He gave it to them as a trial, to complete the proof, as a forsaking, as a gradual enticement (استدراج), and so on. All of these are legislative attributions. If the legislative attribution is correct without the objection of necessitating ugliness, then the correctness of the ontological attribution—in which there is no room for conventional beauty and ugliness—is even clearer.

Furthermore, He, the Exalted, mentioned that everything is created by Him, sent down from Him from the treasuries of His mercy, as He said: وَإِن مِّن شَيْءٍ إِلَّا عِندَنَا خَزَائِنُهُ وَمَا نُنَزِّلُهُ إِلَّا بِقَدَرٍ مَّعْلُومٍ — "There is not a thing but that its sources are with Us, and We do not send it down except in a known measure" (15:21). He also mentioned that what is with Him is good. The Exalted said: وَمَا عِندَ اللَّهِ خَيْرٌ — "and what is with Allah is better" (28:60). The combination of the two verses and those of similar meaning implies that everything a thing attains in the world and is clothed with throughout its existence is from Allah, the Glorified, and it is a good for it, from which it benefits and by which it is blessed. This is also yielded by the saying of the Exalted: الَّذِي أَحْسَنَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلَقَهُ — "[He] who perfected everything that He created" (32:7), along with the saying of the Exalted: ذَٰلِكُمُ اللَّهُ رَبُّكُمْ خَالِقُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ لَّا إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ — "That is Allah, your Lord, the creator of all things, there is no god except Him" (40:62).

As for some of the divine gifts attained by things being an evil (شراً) by which they are harmed, its being evil and harmful is only relative (نسبي), realized in relation to what it specifically afflicts, while being a beneficial good in relation to others and in relation to its causes and factors in the system of the universe. This was previously discussed, and it is pointed to by the saying of the Exalted: وَمَا أَصَابَكَ مِن سَيِّئَةٍ فَمِن نَّفْسِكَ — "and whatever misfortune may befall you is from yourself" (4:79). This meaning has been discussed previously.

In short, all that Allah emanates upon His creation of good—and all of it is good from which they benefit—is provision (رزق) according to the application of the meaning. For provision is nothing but the gift from which the provided thing benefits. Perhaps this is pointed to by the saying of the Exalted: وَرِزْقُ رَبِّكَ خَيْرٌ — "and the provision of your Lord is better" (20:131).

From here it becomes clear that provision (الرزق), good (الخير), and creation (الخلق), according to their instance (المصداق), are equal matters as explained by the Quran. Thus, every provision is good and created, and every creation is provision and good. The only difference is that provision requires the presumption of a "provided one" (مرزوق) who receives provision. Thus, nourishment is provision for the nutritive power because of its need for it; the nutritive power is provision for the individual human because of his need for it; and the individual human is provision for his parents because of their benefit from him. Likewise, the existence of the human is a good for the human, by presuming him devoid of this divine blessing. The Exalted said: الَّذِي أَعْطَىٰ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلْقَهُ — "[He] who gave everything its creation" (20:50).

And good (الخير) requires the presumption of a needy seeker who chooses from among what faces him that which is his sought-after object. Thus, nourishment is good for the nutritive power by presuming it needy of it and seeking it, selecting and choosing it when it attains it. The nutritive power is good for the human, and the existence of the human is good for him by presuming him needy and seeking.

As for creation (الخلق) and origination (الإيجاد), it does not require, in terms of the realization of its meaning, any fixed or presumed thing. Nourishment, for example, is created and originated in itself; likewise, the nutritive power is created, and the human is created.

Since every provision belongs to Allah, and every good belongs purely to Allah, whatever gift He, the Exalted, gives, whatever good He emanates, and whatever provision He provides, it occurs without compensation (عوض) and without anything taken in exchange. For everything we presume to be a thing belongs to Him, the Exalted, by right. There is no entitlement (استحقاق) there, for no one has a right over Him, the Exalted, except what He has placed upon Himself of right, as He placed it in the matter of provision. The Exalted said: وَمَا مِن دَابَّةٍ فِي الْأَرْضِ إِلَّا عَلَى اللَّهِ رِزْقُهَا — "There is no animal on the earth, but that its provision is due from Allah" (11:6). And the Exalted said: فَوَرَبِّ السَّمَاءِ وَالْأَرْضِ إِنَّهُ لَحَقٌّ مِّثْلَ مَا أَنَّكُمْ تَنطِقُونَ — "By the Lord of the heaven and the earth, it is indeed as real as your speaking [to one another]!" (51:23).

Thus, provision, while being a right upon Allah because it is a right established by Him, is a gift from Him without entitlement for the provided one from the perspective of himself, but rather from the perspective of the right He placed upon Himself.

From here it becomes clear that for the human who receives provision through prohibited means, there is a provision decreed from the lawful (الحلال) from the perspective of legislation. For His presence, the Exalted, is sanctified from making the provision of a human a fixed right upon Himself, then providing it to him through a prohibited way, then forbidding him from disposing of it and punishing him for it.

To clarify this with another explanation: Since provision is the divine gift of good, it is the mercy (الرحمة) that He has for His creation. Just as mercy is of two types: a general mercy that encompasses all creation—believer and disbeliever, pious and wicked, human and non-human—and a specific mercy, which is the mercy occurring in the path of felicity such as faith, godwariness, and Paradise; likewise, provision is of two types. There is a general provision, which is the general divine gift extending to every existent in the persistence of its existence. And there is a specific provision, which is that which occurs in the course of the lawful (الحل).

Just as general mercy and general provision are written and decreed—the Exalted said: وَخَلَقَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ فَقَدَّرَهُ تَقْدِيرًا — "He created everything and ordained it in a precise measure" (25:2)—likewise, specific mercy and specific provision are written and decreed. Just as guidance (الهدى)—which is a specific mercy—is written and decreed as a legislative decree for every human, whether believer or disbeliever—and for that reason He sent the messengers and sent down the books; the Exalted said: وَمَا خَلَقْتُ الْجِنَّ وَالْإِنسَ إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُونِ مَا أُرِيدُ مِنْهُم مِّن رِّزْقٍ وَمَا أُرِيدُ أَن يُطْعِمُونِ إِنَّ اللَّهَ هُوَ الرَّزَّاقُ ذُو الْقُوَّةِ الْمَتِينُ — "I did not create the jinn and the humans except that they may worship Me. I do not desire any provision from them, nor do I desire that they should feed Me. Indeed it is Allah who is the All-provider, Lord of all might, the Ever-pitted" (51:56-58); and the Exalted said: وَقَضَىٰ رَبُّكَ أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوا إِلَّا إِيَّاهُ — "Your Lord has decided that you shall not worship anyone except Him" (17:23). Thus, worship—which necessitates guidance and depends upon it—is decreed and ordained legislatively. Likewise, specific provision—which is through the course of the lawful—is decreed and ordained. The Exalted said: قَدْ خَسِرَ الَّذِينَ قَتَلُوا أَوْلَادَهُمْ سَفَهًا بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ وَحَرَّمُوا مَا رَزَقَهُمُ اللَّهُ افْتِرَاءً عَلَى اللَّهِ قَدْ ضَلُّوا وَمَا كَانُوا مُهْتَدِينَ — "They are certainly losers who kill their children foolishly without knowledge, and forbid what Allah has provided them, fabricating a lie against Allah. They have certainly gone astray and are not guided" (6:140). And the Exalted said: وَاللَّهُ فَضَّلَ بَعْضَكُمْ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ فِي الرِّزْقِ فَمَا الَّذِينَ فُضِّلُوا بِرَادِّي رِزْقِهِمْ عَلَىٰ مَا مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَانُهُمْ فَهُمْ فِيهِ سَوَاءٌ — "Allah has overspread some of you over others in provision; so those who have been overspread do not give over their provision to their slaves so that they may be equal in it" (16:71). The two verses, as you see, possess a definitive generality that includes the disbeliever and the believer, and the one who receives provision through the lawful and the one who receives it through the prohibited.

It is necessary to know that provision, as previously mentioned of its meaning, is that which one benefits from of the gift, according to the measure of the benefit. Thus, whoever is given much wealth but only eats a little of it, his provision is only what he ate. The remaining excess is not of the provision except from the perspective of the giving, not the eating. Thus, the expansion and constriction of provision is different from the abundance and scarcity of wealth, for example. The discussion on provision has a completion that will come to you regarding the saying of the Exalted: وَمَا مِن دَابَّةٍ فِي الْأَرْضِ إِلَّا عَلَى اللَّهِ رِزْقُهَا وَيَعْلَمُ مُسْتَقَرَّهَا وَمَا وَيَعْلَمُ مُسْتَقَرَّهَا وَمُسْتَوْدَعَهَا كُلٌّ فِي كِتَابٍ مُّبِينٍ — "There is no animal on the earth, but that its provision is due from Allah, and He knows its settling-place and its repository. Everything is in a manifest Book" (11:6).

Let us return to what we were discussing regarding the saying of the Exalted: وترزق من تشاء بغير حساب (and You provide for whomever You wish without any reckoning). We say: characterizing the provision as being "without account" is because provision from Him, the Exalted, in view of the state of the provided ones, is without compensation or entitlement. This is because whatever they possess of solicitation, seeking, or otherwise is purely owned by Him, the Exalted. Thus, nothing from them stands in exchange for His gift; therefore, there is no account for the provision of the Exalted.

As for the negation of account referring to the decree (التقدير)—in the sense of it being unlimited or unmeasured—this is refuted by the verses of decree, such as the saying of the Exalted: إِنَّا كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلَقْنَاهُ بِقَدَرٍ — "Indeed We have created everything in a [precise] measure" (54:49); and His saying: وَمَن يَتَّقِ اللَّهَ يَجْعَل لَّهُ مَخْرَجًا وَيَرْزُقْهُ مِنْ حَيْثُ لَا يَحْتَسِبُ وَمَن يَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى اللَّهِ فَهو حَسْبُهُ إِنَّ اللَّهَ بَالِغُ أَمْرِهِ قَدْ جَعَلَ اللَّهُ لِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدْرًا — "and whoever is God-conscious, He will make for him a way out, and provide for him from whence he does not reckon. And whoever puts his trust in Allah, He will suffice him. Indeed Allah carries through His command. Certainly Allah has ordained a measure for everything" (65:2-3). Thus, provision from Him, the Exalted, is a gift without compensation, but it is decreed according to what He, the Exalted, wills.

What has been derived from the two verses is:

First: That sovereignty (المُلك) — with a dammah — all belongs to Allah, just as ownership (المِلك) — with a kasrah — all belongs to Allah.

Second: That all good is in His hand and from Him, the Exalted.

Third: That provision is a gift from Him, the Exalted, without compensation or entitlement.

Fourth: That sovereignty, honor, and every conceptual good among the goods of society—such as wealth, status, power, and others—all of that is of the provision provided.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Kafi, narrated from 'Abd al-A'la, the client of the family of Sam, from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: I said to him: قل اللهم مالك الملك تؤتي الملك من تشاء وتنزع الملك ممن تشاء (Say, ‘O Allah, Master of all sovereignty! You give sovereignty to whomever You wish, and take away sovereignty from whomever You wish). Did Allah not give sovereignty to the Umayyads? He said: "It is not as you think. Indeed, Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, gave us the sovereignty, and the Umayyads took it, in the position of a man who has a garment and another takes it; so it does not belong to the one who took it."

I say: A similar report was narrated by al-'Ayyashi from Dawud ibn Farqad from him (peace be upon him). The giving of sovereignty, according to what has been previously explained, occurs in two ways: An ontological giving (إيتاء تكويني), which is the spreading of dominion over people and the effectiveness of power among them, whether that be through justice or tyranny, as the Exalted said regarding Nimrod: أَنْ آتَاهُ اللَّهُ الْمُلْكَ — "because Allah had given him kingdom" (2:258). Its effect is the effectiveness of the word and the execution of command and will. We shall discuss the meaning of its being ontological. And a legislative giving (إيتاء تشريعي), which is the decree that one is a king whose obedience is obligatory, as the Exalted said: إِنَّ اللَّهَ قَدْ بَعَثَ لَكُمْ طَالُوتَ مَلِكًا — "Allah has appointed Saul as king for you" (2:247). Its effect is the obligation of obedience and the establishment of authority (الولاية), and it can only be through justice. This is a praised station with Allah, the Glorified. What the Umayyads possessed of sovereignty was the first meaning and its effect. The matter became confused for the narrator of the hadith, so he took their sovereignty in the first sense and attached to it the effect of the second sense—which is the legislative station and religious praise. So he (peace be upon him) alerted him that sovereignty in this sense does not belong to the Umayyads; rather, it belongs to them [the Ahl al-Bayt] and to them belongs its effect. In other words: the sovereignty that the Umayyads held is only praised when it is in their [the Ahl al-Bayt's] hands (peace be upon them). As for being in the hands of the Umayyads, it is nothing but blameworthy because it is usurped. Based on this, it is not attributed to the giving of Allah except in the sense of a plot (مكر) and gradual enticement (استدراج), as in the sovereignty of Nimrod and Pharaoh.

The matter was also confused for these people themselves—namely the Umayyads—regarding this verse. In al-Irshad, in the story of Yazid ibn Mu'awiyah bringing the heads of the martyrs of al-Taff, al-Mufid said: When the heads were placed, and among them was the head of al-Husayn (peace be upon him), Yazid said:

We split the skulls of men who were dear to us, But they were more defiant and more unjust.

He said: Then he turned to those in his assembly and said: "This one used to boast over me, saying: 'My father is better than Yazid's father, my mother is better than his mother, my grandfather is better than his grandfather, and I am better than him.' As for his saying that his father is better than Yazid's father, my father contended with his father and Allah decided for my father over his father. As for his saying that his mother is better than Yazid's mother, by my life, he spoke the truth; indeed Fatimah, the daughter of the Messenger of Allah, is better than my mother. As for his saying that his grandfather is better than his grandfather, it is not for anyone who believes in Allah and the Last Day to say that anyone is better than Muhammad. But as for his saying that he is better than me, perhaps he did not read this verse: قل اللهم مالك الملك (Say, ‘O Allah, Master of all sovereignty!) the verse."

Zaynab, the daughter of 'Ali (peace be upon him and her), refuted his saying with the like of what al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) mentioned in the previous narration, as narrated by al-Sayyid ibn Tawus and others.
