She said in what she addressed him with: "Do you think, O Yazid, since you have closed against us the regions of the earth and the horizons of the heaven, so that we have become driven like captives are driven, that we are insignificant before Allah and that you are honorable before Him? And that this is due to your great importance with Him? So you turned up your nose in pride and looked at your side, joyful and happy, when you saw the world gathered for you and matters organized, and when our sovereignty and our authority became clear for you. Slowly, slowly! Have you forgotten the saying of Allah: وَلَا يَحْسَنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا أَنَّمَا نُمْلِي لَهُمْ خَيْرٌ لِّأَنفُسِهِمْ إِنَّمَا نُمْلِي لَهُمْ لِيَزْدَادُوا إِثْمًا وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ مُّهِينٌ — 'Let the faithless not suppose that the respite We grant them is good for their souls: We give them respite only that they may increase in sin, and there is a humiliating punishment for them' (3:178)?" — the sermon.

In al-Majma', regarding His saying, the Exalted: وتخرج الحي من الميت (and You bring forth the living from the dead) the verse, it was said its meaning is: "You bring forth the believer from the disbeliever and You bring forth the disbeliever from the believer." He said: "And that has been narrated from Abu Ja'far and Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon them)."

I say: Something close to it was narrated by al-Saduq from al-'Askari (peace be upon him).

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Mardawayh extracted through the path of Abu 'Uthman al-Nahdi from Ibn Mas'ud or from Salman from the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) regarding: يخرج الحي من الميت ويخرج الميت من الحي (He brings forth the living from the dead and He brings forth the dead from the living), he said: "The believer from the disbeliever and the disbeliever from the believer."

In it also, through the previous path from Salman al-Farsi, who said: The Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his family) said: "When Allah created Adam (peace be upon him), He brought forth his progeny. He took a handful with His right hand and said: 'These are the people of Paradise and I do not care.' And He took another handful with the other, and every wicked one came in it, and He said: 'These are the people of the Fire and I do not care.' So some of them were mixed with others. Thus, the disbeliever comes forth from the believer and the believer comes forth from the disbeliever. That is His saying: تخرج الحي من الميت وتخرج الميت من الحي (You bring forth the living from the dead and You bring forth the dead from the living)."

I say: This meaning has also been narrated from several of the masters of exegesis from Salman as well in a suspended form. The narration is among the reports of the Progeny (الذر) and the Covenant (الميثاق), and its explanation will come in a suitable place, if Allah wills.

In al-Kafi, from Muhammad ibn Yahya from Ahmad ibn Muhammad and several of our companions from Sahl ibn Ziyad from Ibn Mahbub from Abu Hamzah al-Thumali from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), who said: The Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his family) said in the Farewell Pilgrimage: ألا إن الروح الأمين نفث في روعي : أنه لا تموت نفس حتى تستكمل رزقها فاتقوا الله وأجملوا في الطلب ، ولا يحملنكم استبطاء شيء من الرزق أن تطلبوه بشيء من معصية الله فإن الله تعالى قسم الأرزاق بين خلقه حلالاً ، ولم يقسمها حراماً فمن اتقى الله وصبر اُتاه رزقه من حله ، ومن هتك حجاب ستر الله عز وجل وأخذه من غير حله قص به من رزقه الحلال ، وحوسب عليه — "Behold, the Trustworthy Spirit has breathed into my heart: that no soul shall die until it has completed its provision. So be God-conscious and be moderate in seeking. Let not the slowness of any provision lead you to seek it through something of disobedience to Allah. For Allah, the Exalted, has divided provisions among His creation as lawful, and He has not divided them as prohibited. So whoever is God-conscious and patient, his provision will come to him from his lawful [source]. And whoever tears the veil of Allah's protection, the Mighty and Majestic, and takes it from other than its lawful [source], he will be deprived of his lawful provision by that amount, and he will be held to account for it."

In Nahj al-Balaghah, he (peace be upon him) said: الرزق رزقان : رزق تطلبه ، ورزق يطلبك فإن لم تأته أتاك فلا تحمل هم سنتك يومك ، كفاك كل يوم ما فيه فإن تكن السنة من عمرك فإن الله تعالى جده سيؤتيك في كل غد جديد ما قسم لك ، وإن لم تكن السنة من عمرك فما تصنع بالهم لما ليس لك ، ولن يسبقك الى رزقك طالب ، ولن يغلبك عليه غالب ، ولن يبطیء عنك ما قد قدر لك — "Provision is of two types: a provision that you seek, and a provision that seeks you; so if you do not go to it, it comes to you. Do not carry the worry of your year on your day; each day is sufficient for what is in it. If the year is part of your life, then Allah—exalted is His majesty—will give you in every new tomorrow what He has apportioned for you. And if the year is not part of your life, then what will you do with the worry for what is not yours? No seeker shall precede you to your provision, no overcomer shall overcome you for it, and what has been decreed for you shall not be slow in reaching you."

In Qurb al-Isnad: Ibn Tarif from Ibn 'Alwan from Ja'far from his father (peace be upon them), who said: The Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his family) said: إن الرزق لينزل من السماء الى الأرض على عدد قطر المطر إلى كل نفس بما قدر لها ، ولكن لله فضول فاسألوا الله من فضله — "Indeed, provision descends from the heaven to the earth according to the number of raindrops to every soul, according to what has been decreed for it. But for Allah there are surpluses, so ask Allah from His grace."

I say: The narrations regarding these meanings are many, and the full investigation of the reports on provision will come in Surah Hud, if Allah, the Exalted, wills.

(Scientific Discourse)

It has been previously mentioned in some of the preceding discussions that the conceptualization of the origin of ownership (المِلك) — with a kasrah — is among the necessary conceptualizations (الاعتبارات الضرورية) that humanity cannot do without in any state, whether individual or social, and that its origin ends at the conceptualization of "exclusivity" (الاختصاص). This is the state of ownership (المِلك).

As for sovereignty (المُلك) — with a dammah — which is dominion over individuals, it is also among the necessary conceptualizations that a human cannot do without. However, what is needed primarily is society, from the perspective of its composition of many parts with different aims and divergent wills, rather than the individual as an individual. For individuals gathered together, due to the divergence of their wills and the difference of their aims, would not remain long before conflict occurs among them. Each would overcome the others in taking what is in their hands, transgressing the sanctuary of their boundaries, and undermining their rights. Thus, chaos and disorder (الهرج والمرج) would occur, and the society they adopted as a means to the felicity of life would become a pretext for misery and destruction, and the medicine would turn into a disease. There is no way to remove this occurring calamity except by establishing a force that is paramount over all other forces, dominating all the gathered individuals, until it returns the tyrannical, overbearing forces to the exact center, and raises the lowly, consumed ones to it as well. Thus, all forces become unified in terms of level, then it places each one of them in its specific place and gives every possessor of a right his right.

Since human nature has never, at any point, been devoid of the inclination toward exploitation (الاستخدام)—as previously elucidated—societies in bygone ages were never free from men who gained dominance over the realm (الملك). These individuals exalted themselves over the rest of the constituent members of society by extending their enslavement (الرقية) and ownership over souls and wealth. Some of the benefits of the realm (الملك) that we mentioned—namely, the existence of one who prevents the transgression of some individuals against others—were realized through the existence of this class of dominant, self-exalting rulers who manifested themselves under the title of "kingship" (الملك) in general terms. This remained true even if they themselves, along with their supporters and henchmen, were oppressive forces acting without any legitimate right. This occurred because they were compelled to keep the subjects in a state of subservience and suppression so that no one would gain enough power to seize the rights of other individuals, lest they one day seize power from the rulers themselves, just as the rulers had seized what was in the hands of others.

In short, the fact that the majority of individuals remained in a state of mutual peace out of fear of the kings who dominated them diverted people from thinking about the social construct of the realm (الملك). Instead, they occupied themselves with praising the conduct of these dominant rulers as long as their transgression did not reach the limit of their endurance; they would only lament and complain when their endurance was exhausted and the burden of transgression exceeded their capacity.

Indeed, they might sometimes lose one of these titled kings or leaders through death, assassination, or the like, and thus sense the onset of discord (الفتنة) and corruption (الفساد). Threatened by the breakdown of order and the occurrence of chaos (الهرج), they would hasten to put forward one of their men of influence and power, handing him the reins of the realm (الملك). He would then become a king holding the reins of affairs, only for the situation to eventually revert to its previous state of transgression and imposition.

Societies continued in this state period after period until they grew weary of the evil conduct of these titled kings—their injustices, their despotism (الاستبداد) in opinion, and their absolute license to do as they pleased. Consequently, laws were enacted to define the functions of the government operating among nations, and kings were compelled to follow them. Thus, the realm (الملك) became a constitutional monarchy (ملك مشروط) after having been absolute (مطلق), and people united to preserve this arrangement, though the kingship remained hereditary (موروث).

Later, some societies sensed the tyranny of their kings and their evil conduct, finding no way to hold them accountable once they had ascended the throne and established that kingship was an unchangeable, hereditary gift. They subsequently replaced the monarchy with a republic (رئاسة الجمهور), and thus the perpetual constitutional monarchy was transformed into a term-limited constitutional presidency (ملك مؤجل مشروط). Various forms of governance (الملك) may have emerged among different peoples and nations, driven by the desire to escape the injustices they witnessed from those who held the reins of their affairs. Perhaps in the future, developments will occur that our intellects have not yet grasped.

However, what can be gathered from all these efforts exerted by societies to reform this matter—namely, handing the reins of the nation to one who manages its affairs and unifies its scattered, conflicting wills and competing forces—is that human society cannot dispense with this station, which is the station of the realm (الملك), even if its names change and its conditions vary according to the differences between nations and the passage of time. For the occurrence of chaos and disorder (الهرج والمرج) and the breakdown of social life is, under all circumstances, an inevitable consequence of failing to unite the reins of wills and objectives into a single will of a single person or a single station.

This is what was mentioned at the beginning of the discourse: that the realm (الملك) is among the necessary social constructs (الاعتبارات الضرورية) in human society.

It is like all other conventional subjects (الموضوعات الاعتبارية) that society has continuously sought to perfect, reform, and rid of their deficiencies and effects that oppose human happiness.

Prophethood (النبوة) has the greatest share in this reform. It is an established principle in sociology that the spread of a particular idea among the masses—especially if it is linked to the innate disposition (الغريزة), approved by the natural intellect (القريحة), and brings tranquility to expectant souls—is the most powerful cause for unifying scattered inclinations and making divided groups a single hand that acts with a single will against which nothing can stand.

It is a matter of necessity that prophethood, since its earliest appearance, has called people to justice (العدل), forbidden them from injustice (الظلم), urged them toward the worship of Allah and submission to Him, and prohibited them from following oppressive Pharaohs and arrogant, dominant Nimrods. This call has persisted among nations for centuries, generation after generation and nation after nation, though it varied in breadth and narrowness according to the differences in nations and eras. It is impossible for such a powerful factor to remain among human societies for long centuries while being isolated from effect or devoid of action.

The Noble Quran has recounted much regarding the revelation sent down to the Prophets (peace be upon them), such as what it narrated from Nuh regarding his complaint to his Lord: رَّبِّ إِنَّهُمْ عَصَوْنِي وَاتَّبَعُوا مَن لَّمْ يَزِدْهُ مَالُهُ وَوَلَدُهُ إِلَّا خَسَارًا وَمَكَرُوا مَكْرًا كُبَّارًا وَقَالُوا لَا تَذَرُنَّ آلِهَتَكُمْ "My Lord! They have disobeyed me and followed someone whose wealth and children only add to his loss. They have plotted a tremendous plot, and said, 'Do not abandon your gods...'" (71:21-23). Likewise, the arguments that took place between him and the elites of his people as recounted by the Quran: قَالُوا أَنُؤْمِنُ لَكَ وَاتَّبَعَكَ الْأَرْذَلُونَ قَالَ وَمَا عِلْمِي بِمَا كَانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ إِنْ حِسَابُهُمْ إِلَّا عَلَىٰ رَبِّي لَوْ تَشْعُرُونَ "They said, 'Shall we believe in you when it is the lowliest who follow you?' He said, 'What do I know as to what they used to do? Their reckoning is only with my Lord, if you use your minds.'" (26:111-113). And the saying of Hud (peace be upon him) to his people: أَتَبْنُونَ بِكُلِّ رِيعٍ آيَةً تَعْبَثُونَ وَتَتَّخِذُونَ مَصَانِعَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَخْلُدُونَ وَإِذَا بَطَشْتُم بَطَشْتُمْ جَبَّارِينَ "Do you build a monument on every high ground for vain sport? And do you build castles as if you were to live forever? And when you seize [anyone], you seize [him] like tyrants." (26:128-130). And the saying of Salih (peace be upon him) to his people: فَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُونِ وَلَا تُطِيعُوا أَمْرَ الْمُسْرِفِينَ الَّذِينَ يُفْسِدُونَ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَلَا يُصْلِحُونَ "So be wary of Allah and obey me, and do not obey the bid of the profligate, who cause corruption in the land and do not rectify." (26:150-152).

Musa (peace be upon him) rose to defend the Children of Israel and oppose Pharaoh in his tyrannical and unjust conduct. Before him, Ibrahim (peace be upon him) rose to oppose Nimrod, and after him, Isa son of Maryam (peace be upon him) and the other Prophets of the Children of Israel rose to oppose the affluent elites (المترفين) of their times among kings and grandees, condemning their unjust conduct and calling the people to reject the obedience of the corrupt and the following of tyrants.

As for the Quran, its urging of the people to refrain from obeying corruption and to refuse oppression, and its informing of the consequences of injustice, corruption, enmity, and tyranny, is something that cannot be hidden. The Exalted says: أَلَمْ تَرَ كَيْفَ فَعَلَ رَبُّكَ بِعَادٍ إِرَمَ ذَاتِ الْعِمَادِ الَّتِي لَمْ يُخْلَقْ مِثْلُهَا فِي الْبِلَادِ وَثَمُودَ الَّذِينَ جَابُوا الصَّخْرَ بِالْوَادِ وَفِرْعَوْنَ ذِي الْأَوْتَادِ الَّذِينَ طَغَوْا فِي الْبِلَادِ فَأَكْثَرُوا فِيهَا الْفَسَادَ فَصَبَّ عَلَيْهِمْ رَبُّكَ سَوْطَ عَذَابٍ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَبِالْمِرْصَادِ "Have you not regarded how your Lord dealt with [the people of] ‘Ad, [and] Iram, [the city] of the columns, the like of which was not created in [any of] the lands, and Thamud, who hollowed out the rocks in the valley, and Pharaoh, the impaler—those who rebelled in the cities and caused much corruption in them? So your Lord poured on them the lash of punishment. Indeed your Lord is in ambush." (89:6-14), among other verses.

As for the fact that the realm (الملك) is among the necessities of human society, it is sufficient to note the most complete explanation in His saying, the Exalted, after recounting the story of Talut: وَلَوْلَا دَفْعُ اللَّهِ النَّاسَ بَعْضَهُم بِبَعْضٍ لَّفَسَدَتِ الْأَرْضُ وَلَٰكِنَّ اللَّهَ ذُو فَضْلٍ عَلَى الْعَالَمِينَ "Were it not for Allah’s repelling the people by means of one another, the earth would surely have been corrupted; but Allah is gracious to the world’s inhabitants." (2:251). The explanation of how this verse indicates this in a general sense has already passed.

In the Quran, there are many verses that address the realm (الملك), authority (الولاية), the obligation of obedience, and the like. Others count it as a blessing and a gift, such as His saying, the Exalted: وَآتَيْنَاهُم مُّلْكًا عَظِيمًا "...and We have given them a great kingdom." (4:54), and His saying, the Exalted: وَجَعَلَكُم مُّلُوكًا وَآتَاكُم مَّا لَمْ يُؤْتِ أَحَدًا مِّنَ الْعَالَمِينَ "...and made you kings, and gave you what He had not given to any other among the nations." (5:20), and His saying, the Exalted: وَاللَّهُ يُؤْتِي مُلْكَهُ مَن يَشَاءُ "...and Allah gives His kingdom to whomever He wishes." (2:247), among other verses.

However, the Quran only considers it an honor (كرامة) when it is coupled with godwariness (التقوى), because it restricts honor to godwariness (التقوى) — the state of being conscious of Allah and guarding oneself against His displeasure — among all the advantages of life in which one might imagine some honor to reside. The Exalted says: يَا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ إِنَّا خَلَقْنَاكُم مِّن ذَكَرٍ وَأُنثَىٰ وَجَعَلْنَاكُمْ شُعُوبًا وَقَبَائِلَ لِتَعَارَفُوا إِنَّ أَكْرَمَكُمْ عِندَ اللَّهِ أَتْقَاكُمْ "O mankind! Indeed We created you from a male and a female, and made you nations and tribes that you may identify with one another. Indeed the noblest of you in the sight of Allah is the most Godwary among you." (49:13). The reckoning of godwariness is with Allah; no one has the right to exalt themselves over another because of it. Thus, no one has cause for pride over another for anything; for if it is a worldly matter, there is no merit in worldly affairs, and value belongs only to religion (الدين). If it is an otherworldly matter, its affair belongs to Allah, the Glorified. In short, for a person clothed in this blessing—namely, the realm (الملك)—nothing remains in the view of a Muslim man except the endurance of effort and the hardship of shouldering the burden. Yes, he shall have a great reward and increased recompense with his Lord if he adheres to the path of justice and godwariness.

This is the spirit of the righteous conduct (السيرة الصالحة) that the guardians of the religion (أولياء الدين) adhered to. We shall, if Allah the Mighty wills, elaborate on this meaning in an independent discussion regarding the conduct of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and the pure ones from his family (الطاهرين من آله) as established by authentic reports—showing that they gained nothing from their realm except to rise against the tyrants in their corruption on earth and to oppose them in their transgression and arrogance.

For this reason, the Quran did not call people to unite for the purpose of establishing a kingdom or constructing the foundations of Caesarism or Khosrowism. Rather, it treated the realm (الملك) as one of the necessary affairs to be observed in human society, similar to education or the preparation of strength to deter the disbelievers.

Rather, it only called people to gather, unite, and agree upon the religion (الدين), and forbade them from division and schism within it, making it the foundation. The Exalted said: وَأَنَّ هَٰذَا صِرَاطِي مُسْتَقِيمًا فَاتَّبِعُوهُ وَلَا تَتَّبِعُوا السُّبُلَ فَتَفَرَّقَ بِكُمْ عَن سَبِيلِهِ "This is My straight path, so follow it, and do not follow [other] ways, for they will separate you from His way." (6:153). And the Exalted said: قُلْ يَا أَهْلَ الْكِتَابِ تَعَالَوْا إِلَىٰ كَلِمَةٍ سَوَاءٍ بَيْنَنَا وَبَيْنَكُمْ أَلَّا نَعْبُدَ إِلَّا اللَّهَ وَلَا نُشْرِكَ بِهِ شَيْئًا وَلَا يَتَّخِذَ بَعْضُنَا بَعْضًا أَرْبَابًا مِّن دُونِ اللَّهِ فَإِن تَوَلَّوْا فَقُولُوا اشْهَدُوا بِأَنَّا مُسْلِمُونَ "Say, ‘O People of the Book! Come to a common word between us and you: that we will worship no one but Allah, that we will not ascribe any partner to Him, and that none of us will take others as lords besides Allah.’ But if they turn away, say, ‘Be witnesses that we are Muslims.’" (3:64). Thus, the Quran—as you see—calls people only to submission (التسليم) to Allah alone, considers this the basis of the religious society, and refutes everything else, such as the worship of peers (الأنداد), submission to every lofty palace and high assembly, the kingdoms of Caesar and Khosrow, and division through the demarcation of borders and the separation of homelands, and so forth.

( Philosophical Discussion )

There is no doubt that the Necessary Being (الواجب), the Exalted, is the one to whom the chain of causality (العلية) in the world terminates, and that the relationship between Him and the world—in part and in whole—is the relationship of causality. It has been clarified in the discussions of cause and effect (العلة والمعلول) that causality pertains only to existence (الوجود); meaning that the real existence in the effect is what emanates (المترشح) from the existence of its cause. As for other than that, such as quiddity (الماهية), it is removed from emanation, issuance, and the need for a cause. This is inverted by contraposition to mean that what has no real existence is neither an effect nor terminates at the Necessary Being, the Exalted.

A problem arises regarding the attribution of purely conventional matters (الامور الاعتبارية) to Him, the Exalted, since they have no real existence at all; rather, their existence and fixity are conventional (اعتباري), not exceeding the context of convention, stipulation, and the domain of mental postulation (الفرض). The contents of the Sharia—commands, prohibitions, rulings, and stipulations—are all conventional matters, so their attribution to Him, the Exalted, is problematic. The same applies to things like the realm (الملك), might (العز), sustenance (الرزق), and so forth.

The solution that unties this knot is that although these matters are devoid of real existence, they possess effects (آثار) that preserve their names, as has been mentioned repeatedly. These effects are real matters (امور حقيقية) intended by the convention, and they have a relationship to Him, the Exalted. This relationship is what validates their attribution to Him. Thus, the realm (الملك) that exists among us as social beings, although it is a conventional and stipulated matter (أمر اعتباري وضعي) whose meaning has no share of real existence—being rather a conceptualized meaning (معنى متوهم) we have fashioned as a means to reach external effects that we could not reach without postulating and estimating this conceptual meaning—namely, the subduing of the dominant and those of power among the members of society who seize the rights of the weak and the obscure, placing each individual in their proper station, giving every rightful person their due, and so on.

However, since the reality of the meaning of the realm (الملك) and its name remains as long as these external effects remain and follow from it, the attribution of these external effects to their external causes is the very same as the attribution of the realm (الملك) to Him. The same is said regarding conventional might (العزة الاعتبارية), its external effects, and its attribution to its real causes. The matter is the same for others, such as command, prohibition, ruling, stipulation, and the like.

From here it becomes clear that they all have an attribution to the Necessary Being (الواجب), the Exalted, by virtue of the attribution of their effects to Him, in a manner befitting the presence of His sanctity and might.

Ayahs

لَّا يَتَّخِذِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ أَوْلِيَآءَ مِن دُونِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ ۖ وَمَن يَفْعَلْ ذَٰلِكَ فَلَيْسَ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ فِى شَىْءٍ إِلَّآ أَن تَتَّقُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ تُقَىٰةً ۗ وَيُحَذِّرُكُمُ ٱللَّهُ نَفْسَهُۥ ۗ وَإِلَى ٱللَّهِ ٱلْمَصِيرُ (٢٨)
The faithful should not take the faithless for allies instead of the faithful, and Allah will have nothing to do with those who do that, except when you are wary of them, out of caution. Allah warns you to beware of [disobeying] Him, and toward Allah is the return. (28)
قُلْ إِن تُخْفُوا۟ مَا فِى صُدُورِكُمْ أَوْ تُبْدُوهُ يَعْلَمْهُ ٱللَّهُ ۗ وَيَعْلَمُ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (٢٩)
Say, ‘Whether you hide what is in your hearts or disclose it, Allah knows it, and He knows whatever there is in the heavens and whatever there is in the earth; and Allah has power over all things.’ (29)
يَوْمَ تَجِدُ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ مَّا عَمِلَتْ مِنْ خَيْرٍ مُّحْضَرًا وَمَا عَمِلَتْ مِن سُوٓءٍ تَوَدُّ لَوْ أَنَّ بَيْنَهَا وَبَيْنَهُۥٓ أَمَدًۢا بَعِيدًا ۗ وَيُحَذِّرُكُمُ ٱللَّهُ نَفْسَهُۥ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ رَءُوفٌۢ بِٱلْعِبَادِ (٣٠)
The day when every soul will find present whatever good it has done; and as for the evil, it has done, it will wish there were a far distance between it and itself. Allah warns you to beware of [disobeying] Him, and Allah is most kind to [His] servants. (30)
قُلْ إِن كُنتُمْ تُحِبُّونَ ٱللَّهَ فَٱتَّبِعُونِى يُحْبِبْكُمُ ٱللَّهُ وَيَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ ذُنُوبَكُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٣١)
Say, ‘If you love Allah, then follow me; Allah will love you and forgive you your sins, and Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful.’ (31)
قُلْ أَطِيعُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱلرَّسُولَ ۖ فَإِن تَوَلَّوْا۟ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٣٢)
Say, ‘Obey Allah and the Apostle.’ But if they turn away, indeed Allah does not like the faithless. (32)

(Commentary)

The verses are not devoid of connection to what preceded them, based on what we mentioned regarding the previous verses: that the context is one of addressing the state of the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب) and the polytheists (المشركين), and alluding to them. Thus, if the term "disbelievers" (الكافرين) is meant to encompass the People of the Book, then these verses forbid taking them as allies (توليهم) and spiritual mingling with both the polytheists and them collectively. If the term refers only to the polytheists, then the verses are addressing them and calling for abandoning them and connecting with the party of Allah (حزب الله), the love of Allah, and obedience to His Messenger.

His saying, the Exalted: لا يتخذ المؤمنون الكافرين أولياء من دون المؤمنين — Faithful should not take the faithless for allies instead of the faithful. (3:28). The word awliya (أولياء) is the plural of wali (الولي), derived from wilayah (الولاية), which originally means the possession of the right to manage the affairs of a thing. Thus, the wali of a child, an insane person, or an imbecile is the one who possesses the management of their affairs and their wealth; the wealth belongs to them, but the management of its affairs belongs to their wali. Subsequently, it was used—and its usage became frequent—in the context of love (الحب), because love often necessitates that each of the two lovers intervenes in the affairs of the other, leading to proximity and being influenced by the beloved's will and other spiritual states. Thus, love is never free from the beloved's influence over the lover's affairs in their life.

Therefore, taking the disbelievers as allies (أولياء) means spiritual mingling with them in a way that leads to complying with them and being influenced by them in character and other affairs of life, and their intervention therein. This is indicated by the qualification of this prohibition with His saying: من دون المؤمنين — instead of the faithful, for it contains an indication of preferring their love over the love of the believers, and handing over the reins of life to them rather than the believers. It involves relying on them, connecting with them, and separating from the believers.

The prohibition against taking the disbelievers, the Jews, and the Christians as allies (أولياء) has been repeated in the Noble Verses, but the instances of prohibition contain that which explains the meaning of the forbidden alliance (التولي) and identifies the nature of the forbidden guardianship (الولاية). For example, this verse includes the phrase من دون المؤمنين after the phrase لا يتخذ المؤمنون الكافرين أولياء. Similarly, the saying of the Exalted: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لَا تَتَّخِذُوا الْيَهُودَ وَالنَّصَارَىٰ أَوْلِيَاءَ — O you who have faith! Do not take the Jews and the Christians for allies (5:51), is followed by His saying: بعضهم أولياء بعض — they are allies of each other. Also, the saying of the Exalted: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لَا تَتَّخِذُوا عَدُوِّي وَعَدُوَّكُمْ أَوْلِيَاءَ — O you who have faith! Do not take My enemy and your enemy for friends (60:1), is followed by His saying: لا ينهاكم الله عن الذين لم يقاتلوكم في الدين — Allah does not forbid you in regard to those who did not make war against you on account of religion (60:8), until the end of the verses.

Accordingly, the inclusion of these descriptions in His saying: لا يتخذ المؤمنون الكافرين أولياء من دون المؤمنين is to indicate the reason and cause (علة) of the ruling. This reason is that the attributes of disbelief (الكفر) and faith (الإيمان), with the distance and distinction between them, inevitably extend to those characterized by them. This creates a separation between them in knowledge (المعارف), ethics (الأخلاق), the path of journeying toward Allah the Exalted (طريق السلوك إلى الله تعالى), and other affairs of life. Their states are incompatible with guardianship (الولاية), for guardianship necessitates union and mingling, while these two attributes necessitate separation and distinction. If the guardianship becomes strong—as when it is "instead of the believers"—it leads to the corruption of the properties and effects of faith, and then to the corruption of its very essence. For this reason, He followed it with His saying: ومن يفعل ذلك فليس من الله في شيء — and whoever does that has nothing to do with Allah. Then He followed it further with His saying: إلا أن تتقوا منهم تقية — except when you are wary of them out of caution, thus excepting taqiyyah (التقية) — the practice of concealing one's faith or intentions for protection from harm. This is because taqiyyah only necessitates the outward form of guardianship, not its reality.

The word duna (دون) in His saying من دون المؤمنين is like an adverbial particle (ظرف) that conveys the meaning of "at" or "near" (عند) with a tinge of the meaning of inferiority and deficiency. The meaning is: starting from a place below (دون) the place of the believers, for they are in a higher station.

It appears that this is the original meaning of duna; it originally conveyed the meaning of proximity (الدنو) with the specific quality of being lower (الانخفاض). Thus, their saying "Zayd is dunaka" means he is in a place near yours but lower than it, like one step below another. Then it was used to mean "other than" (غير), as in His saying: إِلَٰهَيْنِ مِن دُونِ اللَّهِ — two gods besides Allah (5:116), and His saying: وَيَغْفِرُ مَا دُونَ ذَٰلِكَ لِمَن يَشَاءُ — and He forgives what is besides that to whomever He wishes (4:48); meaning what is other than that, or what is lower and lesser than that. It is also used as a verbal noun (اسم فعل), as in their saying: "dunaka Zaydan" meaning "stick to Zayd." All of this is due to its application to the context, not due to lexical homonymy (الاشتراك اللفظي).

His saying, the Exalted: ومن يفعل ذلك فليس من الله في شيء — and whoever does that has nothing to do with Allah. That is, whoever takes them as allies instead of the believers. The specific act was replaced by a general term to signal the speaker's extreme aversion to it, such that He does not mention it except with a general term, similar to using metonyms for foul things, which is common in language. For this reason also, He did not say: "and whoever among the believers does that," as if to protect the believers from having such an act attributed to them.

The preposition min (من) in His saying من الله is for beginning (للابتداء), and in such contexts, it conveys the meaning of affiliation or partisanship (التحزب). That is, he is not part of the party of Allah (حزب الله) in any way, as the Exalted said: وَمَن يَتَوَلَّ اللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا فَإِنَّ حِزْبَ اللَّهِ هُمُ الْغَالِبُونَ — Whoever takes for his guardians Allah, His Apostle and the faithful [should know that] the confederates of Allah are indeed the victorious (5:56). And as He narrated from Ibrahim (peace be upon him) in his saying: فَمَن تَبِعَنِي فَإِنَّهُ مِنِّي — so whoever follows me indeed belongs to me (14:36), meaning "is of my party." In any case, the meaning—and Allah knows best—is: he is not firmly established in the party of Allah in any of his states or effects.

His saying, the Exalted: إلا أن تتقوا منهم تقية — except when you are wary of them out of caution. Al-ittiqa’ (الاتقاء) originally means taking a shield or protection (الوقاية) out of fear. Then it was sometimes used to mean fear itself, using the effect in place of the cause. Perhaps taqiyyah (التقية) in this context is of this nature.

The exception is a disconnected one (منقطع), for drawing near to another out of fear by displaying the outward signs of alliance (التولي) without the heart's commitment to love and guardianship is not part of alliance at all. This is because fear and love are two heart-centered matters that are distinct and contradictory in their effects on the heart; how then could they be united? Thus, the exception of caution is a disconnected exception.

The verse contains a clear indication of the permissibility of taqiyyah (التقية), as narrated from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them). This is also indicated by the verse revealed regarding the story of ‘Ammar and his parents, Yasir and Sumayyah, which is the saying of the Exalted: مَن كَفَرَ بِاللَّهِ مِن بَعْدِ إِيمَانِهِ إِلَّا مَنْ أُكْرِهَ وَقَلْبُهُ مُطْمَئِنٌّ بِالْإِيمَانِ وَلَٰكِن مَّن شَرَحَ بِالْكُفْرِ صَدْرًا فَعَلَيْهِمْ غَضَبٌ مِّنَ اللَّهِ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ عَظِيمٌ — Any one who disbelieves in Allah after his belief—not him who is compelled while his heart is at rest with faith, but he who opens up his breast to unfaith—upon such shall be Allah’s wrath, and there is a great punishment for them (16:106).

In short, the Book and the Sunnah are in agreement regarding its general permissibility, and rational consideration (الاعتبار العقلي) confirms it. For religion has no aim, and its Legislator has no concern, except the manifestation of truth and its life. Sometimes, taqiyyah and going along with the enemies of religion and the opponents of truth result in preserving the interest of religion and the life of truth in a way that its abandonment does not. To deny this is mere obstinacy and arbitrariness. We shall provide a full discussion on this in the following traditional report section and in the commentary on the saying of the Exalted: مَن كَفَرَ بِاللَّهِ مِن بَعْدِ إِيمَانِهِ إِلَّا مَنْ أُكْرِهَ وَقَلْبُهُ مُطْمَئِنٌّ بِالْإِيمَانِ (16:106).

His saying, the Exalted: ويحذركم الله نفسه وإلى الله المصير — Allah warns you to beware of [disobeying] Him, and to Allah is the return. (3:28). Tahdhir (التحذير) is the intensive form of hadhar (الحذر), which is to guard against a fearsome matter. Allah has warned His servants of His punishment, as the Exalted said: إِنَّ عَذَابَ رَبِّكَ كَانَ مَحْذُورًا — Indeed the punishment of your Lord is a thing to be wary of (17:57). He also warned against the hypocrites and the temptation of the disbelievers, saying: هُمُ الْعَدُوُّ فَاحْذَرْهُمْ — They are the enemy, so beware of them (63:4), and: وَاحْذَرْهُمْ أَن يَفْتِنُوكَ — and beware of them lest they tempt you (5:49). He warned them of Himself, as in this verse and the one coming two verses later. This is only to indicate that Allah, the Glorified, is Himself the One to be feared and guarded against in this sin. That is, there is no other fearsome thing between this sinner and Him, the Exalted, such that one could protect oneself from it with something or take refuge in a fortress. Rather, it is Allah from Whom there is no protector. Nor is there any matter to be hoped for between him and Allah, the Glorified, to repel evil from him, whether a guardian (ولي) or an intercessor (شفيع). Thus, the speech contains the severest threat. Its severity is increased by its repetition twice in one context, and it is emphasized by its concluding first with His saying: وإلى الله المصير — and to Allah is the return, and secondly with His saying: والله رؤوف بالعباد — and Allah is most kind to [His] servants, as will be explained.

From another perspective, it appears from the folds of this verse and other verses forbidding the taking of non-believers as allies that it is a departure from the garb of servitude (العبودية), a rejection of the guardianship (ولاية) of Allah the Glorified, and an entry into the party of His enemies to corrupt the matter of religion. In short, it is rebellion (طغيان) and a corruption of the system of religion, which is more severe and harmful to the state of religion than the disbelief of the disbelievers and the polytheism of the polytheists. For the enemy whose enmity is manifest and whose path is distinct is repelled from the sanctuary and is easy to guard against and beware of. But the friend and intimate, if he becomes familiar with the enemies and their character and ways creep into him, his actions will soon destroy the sanctuary and its people from where they do not perceive. This is the destruction with which there is no hope for life or survival.

In short, it is rebellion (طغيان), and the matter of the rebel in his rebellion is referred to Allah the Glorified Himself. The Exalted said: أَلَمْ تَرَ كَيْفَ فَعَلَ رَبُّكَ بِعَادٍ إِرَمَ ذَاتِ الْعِمَادِ الَّتِي لَمْ يُخْلَقْ مِثْلُهَا فِي الْبِلَادِ وَثَمُودَ الَّذِينَ جَابُوا الصَّخْرَ بِالْوَادِ وَفِرْعَوْنَ ذِي الْأَوْتَادِ الَّذِينَ طَغَوْا فِي الْبِلَادِ فَأَكْثَرُوا فِيهَا الْفَسَادَ فَصَبَّ عَلَيْهِمْ رَبُّكَ سَوْطَ عَذَابٍ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَبِالْمِرْصَادِ — Have you not regarded how your Lord dealt with ‘Ad, [the people of] Iram, the city of many-columned buildings, the like of which was not created in [any of] the lands? And Thamud, who hollowed out the rocks in the valley? And Pharaoh, the impaler? Those who rebelled in the lands, and spread much corruption in them. So your Lord poured on them the lash of punishment. Indeed your Lord is in ambush. (89:6-14). Thus, rebellion leads the rebel on a path that brings him to the ambush (المرصاد) where there is none but Allah, majestic is His greatness (جلت عظمته), so He pours upon him the lash of punishment and there is no preventer.

From here it becomes clear that the threat of warning of Allah Himself in His saying: ويحذركم الله نفسه, is because the instance is one of the manifestations of rebellion against Allah by nullifying His religion and corrupting it.

What we have mentioned is indicated by the saying of the Exalted: فَاسْتَقِمْ كَمَا أُمِرْتَ وَمَن تَابَ مَعَكَ وَلَا تَطْغَوْا إِنَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ وَلَا تَرْكَنُوا إِلَى الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا فَتَمَسَّكُمُ النَّارُ وَمَا لَكُم مِّن دُونِ اللَّهِ مِنْ أَوْلِيَاءَ ثُمَّ لَا تُنصَرُونَ — So be steadfast as you have been commanded—[you] and whoever has turned [to Allah] with you—and do not rebel. Indeed He sees best what you do. And do not incline toward the wrongdoers, lest the Fire should touch you, and you will not have any friends besides Allah, then you will not be helped. (11:112-113). This is a verse about which the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said that it made him grow old—according to the narration. For the two verses—as is apparent to the contemplative—clearly show that inclining toward the wrongdoers among the disbelievers is a rebellion that entails being touched by the Fire in a way that leaves no helper. It is the divine vengeance from which there is no protector and no repeller, as previously explained.

From here it also becomes clear that in His saying: ويحذركم الله نفسه, there is an indication that the threat is of a punishment that has been decreed as an inevitable decree (قضاء حتماً), since the warning is linked to Allah Himself, which indicates that there is no barrier intervening, and no protector from Allah the Glorified. Since He has promised punishment, it results in the certainty of its occurrence, just as His saying in the two verses of Surah Hud indicates: فتمسكم النار وما لكم من ناصرين.

In His saying: وإلى الله المصير — and to Allah is the return, there is an indication that there is no escape for you from Him and no one to divert Him; thus, it contains an emphasis on the threat preceding it.

The verses—meaning the saying of the Exalted: لا يتخذ المؤمنون الكافرين أولياء and the verses following it—are among the prophecies (ملاحم) of the Quran, and the explanation will come, Allah willing, in Surah al-Ma'idah.

His saying, the Exalted: قل إن تخفوا ما في أنفسكم أو تبدوه يعلمه الله — Say, ‘Whether you hide what is in your hearts or disclose it, Allah knows it. (3:29). The verse is similar to the saying of the Exalted: وَإِن تُبْدُوا مَا فِي أَنفُسِكُمْ أو تُخْفُوهُ يُحَاسِبْكُم بِهِ اللَّهُ — Whether you disclose what is in your hearts or hide it, Allah will bring you to account for it (2:284). However, since it is more appropriate for knowledge (العلم) to be related to what is hidden, unlike accounting (الحساب) for which it is more appropriate to be related to what is manifest and apparent, the mention of hiding was prioritized in this verse over the mention of disclosing. The opposite was done in the verse of al-Baqarah, as has been said.

In the verse, He commanded His Messenger to convey this reality—which is His knowledge of what their souls hide or disclose—without addressing it Himself as in the preceding speech. This is only out of loftiness (ترفعاً) from addressing those whose state suggests they will oppose what He has enjoined, similar to what was mentioned regarding His saying: ومن يفعل ذلك.

In His saying, the Exalted: ويعلم ما في السموات والأرض والله على كل شيء قدير — He knows whatever there is in the heavens and whatever there is in the earth, and Allah has power over all things (3:29), there is a resemblance to what preceded in the verse of al-Baqarah, and the discussion on it has already passed.

His saying, the Exalted: يوم تجد كل نفس ما علمت من خير محضراً وما علمت من سوء — The day when every soul will find present whatever good it has done; and as for whatever evil it has done (3:30). It appears from the continuity of the context that this is a completion of the speech in the previous verse which the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was commanded to say. The adverbial phrase (الظرف) is related to an omitted word, i.e., "and remember the day when [every soul] finds," or it is related to His saying: يعلمه الله ويعلم. There is no harm in relating His knowledge, the Exalted, to what we shall witness of the states of the Day of Resurrection, for this day is a time for His knowledge, the Exalted, in relation to the manifestation of the matter to us, not in relation to its realization from Him, the Exalted. This is like the manifestation of His kingdom, power, and strength on that day. The Exalted said: يَوْمَ هُم بَارِزُونَ لَا يَخْفَىٰ عَلَى اللَّهِ مِنْهُمْ شَيْءٌ لِّمَنِ الْمُلْكُ الْيَوْمَ لِلَّهِ الْوَاحِدِ الْقَهَّارِ — The day when they will emerge [from their graves], nothing about them will be hidden from Allah. ‘To whom does the sovereignty belong today?’ ‘To Allah, the One, the All-paramount!’ (40:16). And He said: لَا عَاصِمَ الْيَوْمَ مِنْ أَمْرِ اللَّهِ — Today there is no protector from Allah’s decree (11:43). And He said: وَلَوْ يَرَى الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا إِذْ يَرَوْنَ الْعَذَابَ أَنَّ الْقُوَّةَ لِلَّهِ جَمِيعًا — That the wrongdoers may see, when they sight the punishment, that power, entirely, belongs to Allah (2:165). And He said: وَالْأَمْرُ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلَّهِ — and the command that day will belong to Allah (82:19). For it is known that Allah, the Glorified, possesses all sovereignty, power, strength, and command always—before the Resurrection, during it, and after it. The Day of Resurrection is only specified because of the manifestation of these matters to us, the assembly of creatures, a manifestation in which there is no doubt.

From this, it becomes clear that the relation of the adverbial phrase to His saying يعلمه الله does not imply that His knowledge, the Exalted, of the secrets of His servants—whether good or evil—is delayed until the Day of Resurrection.

Furthermore, in His saying, the Exalted: محضراً (brought present), rather than saying hadiran (present), there is an indication of this. For "bringing present" (الإحضار) only takes place with something that exists but is absent. Thus, deeds are existent and preserved from nullification; Allah, the Glorified, brings them present for His creation on the Day of Resurrection, and none preserves them except Allah, the Glorified. The Exalted said: وَرَبُّكَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ حَفِيظٌ — and your Lord is watchful over all things (34:21). And He said: وَعِندَنَا كِتَابٌ حَفِيظٌ — and with Us is a preserving Book (50:4).

His saying: تجد (will find), is from wijdan (finding), the opposite of fuqdan (losing). The preposition min (من) in His saying من خير and من سوء is for explanation (للبيان), and the indefiniteness (التنكير) is for generalization. That is, every soul will find all that it has done of good, even if it be little, and likewise of evil. His saying: وما عملت من سوء is coordinated (معطوف) with His saying ما عملت من خير, as is apparent from the context. The verse is among the verses indicating the embodiment of deeds (تجسم الأعمال), and the discussion on this has already passed in Surah al-Baqarah.

His saying, the Exalted: تود لو أن بينها وبينه أمداً بعيداً — it will wish there were a great distance between it and that [day]. (3:30). It appears that this is a predicate (خبر) for an omitted subject (مبتدأ), which is the pronoun referring to the soul. The word law (لو) is for wishing (للتمني), and its occurrence before the opened and doubled anna (أن) is frequent in the Quran. Therefore, no weight should be given to the claim that this is impermissible or to the forced interpretations of the instances where it occurs.

The word amad (الأمد) conveys the meaning of a temporal interval. Al-Raghib said in Mufradat al-Quran: "Al-amad and al-abad are close in meaning, but al-abad refers to a duration of time that has no defined limit and is not restricted; one does not say 'the abad of such-and-such.' Al-amad is a duration that has an unknown limit when used absolutely, but it may be restricted, such as saying 'the amad of such-and-such,' just as one says 'the time (zaman) of such-and-such.' The difference between zaman and amad is that amad is said in consideration of the end-point (الغاية), while zaman is general for the beginning and the end. For this reason, some have said: al-amad and al-mada are close in meaning." End quote.

In His saying: تود لو أن بينها وبينه أمداً بعيداً, there is an indication that the presence of the evil deed distresses the soul, just as it is sensed by contrast that the presence of the good deed gladdens it. It only wishes for a temporal interval between itself and the evil deed, rather than wishing it had never existed in the first place, because it witnesses its persistence through the preservation of Allah. Thus, it can only love its distance and its non-presence in the most distressing of states and during the greatest of terrors, just as one says to an evil companion something similar. The Exalted said: نُقَيِّضْ لَهُ شَيْطَانًا فَهُوَ لَهُ قَرِينٌ — We assign to him a devil who remains his companion... until He said: حَتَّىٰ إِذَا جَاءَنَا قَالَ يَا لَيْتَ بَيْنِي وَبَيْنَكَ بُعْدَ الْمَشْرِقَيْنِ فَبِئْسَ الْقَرِينُ — until when he comes to Us, he says, ‘I wish there were between me and you the distance between the east and the west! What an evil companion!’ (43:36-38).

His saying, the Exalted: ويحذركم الله نفسه والله رؤوف بالعباد — Allah warns you to beware of [disobeying] Him, and Allah is most kind to [His] servants. (3:30). The mention of the warning a second time conveys an importance of the matter and a reach in the threat that is not hidden. It is possible that this second warning looks toward the consequences of sin in the Hereafter, as is the focus of this verse, while the first warning looks toward its ill effects in this world, or in both this world and the Hereafter generally.

As for His saying: والله رؤوف بالعباد, while it reports His kindness and compassion (رأفته وحنانه) related to His servants—as indicated by the use of the description of servitude and slavery—it is another proof for the intensification of the threat. For such expressions in contexts of frightening and warning are only brought to establish the fear and to create the conviction that the speaker is a sincere advisor who desires nothing but good and rectitude. You might say: "Beware of opposing me in such-and-such a matter, for I have sworn not to be lenient with whoever opposes me in it; I only inform you of this out of kindness and compassion for you."

Thus, the meaning—and Allah knows best—resolves to something like saying: Allah, out of His kindness to His servants, forbids them beforehand from approaching such a sin, the ill consequence of which will inevitably occur without the intercession of any intercessor or the repelling of any repeller affecting it.

His saying, the Exalted: قل إن كنتم تحبون الله فاتبعوني يحببكم الله — Say, ‘If you love Allah, then follow me; Allah will love you (3:31). A discussion on the meaning of love (الحب) has already passed, and that it relates in its true meaning to Allah, the Glorified, just as it relates to others, in the commentary on the saying of the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَشَدُّ حُبًّا لِّلَّهِ — but those who have faith are stauncher in their love for Allah (2:165).

We add here: there is no doubt that Allah, the Glorified—as His speech proclaims—only calls His servant to faith in Him and worship of Him with sincerity (الإخلاص) for Him and avoidance of polytheism (الشرك), as the Exalted said: أَلَا لِلَّهِ الدِّينُ الْخَالِصُ — Behold, [only] exclusive devotion is worthy of Allah (39:3). And the Exalted said: وَمَا أُمِرُوا إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُوا اللَّهَ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ الدِّينَ — Yet they were not commanded except to worship Allah, dedicating their religion exclusively to Him (98:5). And the Exalted said: فَادْعُوا اللَّهَ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ الدِّينَ وَلَوْ كَرِهَ الْكَافِرُونَ — So supplicate Allah, dedicating your religion exclusively to Him, though the faithless should be averse (40:14), among other verses.

There is no doubt that sincerity in religion is only truly completed when the heart of the human being—who desires nothing and intends no matter except out of self-love and heart-attachment—is not attached to anything other than Him, the Exalted, whether a worshipped object or a sought goal, such as an idol, a peer, or a worldly aim, or even an otherworldly aim like attaining Paradise or escaping the Fire. Rather, the object of his heart's attachment must be Allah the Exalted in His worthiness of worship. Thus, sincerity to Allah in His religion is only through His love.

Furthermore, love, which is in reality the only means for the connection of every seeker to his sought object and every willer to his intended goal, only draws the lover to his beloved so that he may find him and complete through the beloved what the lover has of deficiency. There is no greater glad tidings for the lover than to be told that his beloved loves him. At that point, two loves meet, and two endearments reflect each other.

For man only loves food and is drawn to it so that he may find it and complete through it what he finds in himself of the deficiency brought by hunger. Likewise, he loves marriage to find what his soul seeks from it, the sign of which is lust. Likewise, he desires to meet a friend to find him and possess for his soul intimacy, for which his breast tightens. Likewise, the servant loves his master, and the attendant may become infatuated with his employer so that he may be a master to him with the right of mastership, and an employer to him with the right of being served. If you were to contemplate the instances of attachment and love, or read the stories of lovers and the infatuated in their diversity, you would not doubt the truth of what we have mentioned.

Thus, the servant who is sincere to Allah through love has no aim except that Allah, the Glorified, should love him just as he loves Allah, and that Allah should be for him just as he is for Allah, majestic is His Name. This is the reality of the matter. However, Allah, the Glorified, does not count every love for Him as love in His speech (and love is in reality the connecting bond that links one of two things to the other), according to what is dictated by the law of love governing existence. For the love of a thing necessitates the love of all that relates to it, and necessitates submission and surrender to all that is on its side. Allah, the Glorified, is the One, the Unique, upon Whom everything depends in all the affairs of its existence, and toward Whom it seeks a means of approach, and to Whom everything, small or great, returns. Therefore, it is necessary that His love and sincerity to Him through devotion to Him in the religion of monotheism (التوحيد) and the path of Islam be in proportion to what human perception and consciousness can bear. "Indeed, with Allah religion is Islam." This is the religion to which His envoys beckon, and to which His prophets and messengers call, especially the religion of Islam, in which there is a sincerity above which there is no sincerity. It is the primordial religion (الدين الفطري) with which the laws and paths of prophethood are sealed, just as the prophets (peace be upon them) are sealed with its proclaimer. This which we have mentioned is something in which the contemplator of His speech, the Exalted, does not doubt.

The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) identified his path, which he traversed, as the path of monotheism and the way of sincerity, according to what Allah, the Glorified, commanded him when He said: قُلْ هَٰذِهِ سَبِيلِي أَدْعُو إِلَى اللَّهِ عَلَىٰ بَصِيرَةٍ أَنَا وَمَنِ اتَّبَعَنِي وَسُبْحَانَ اللَّهِ وَمَا أَنَا مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ — Say, ‘This is my way. I summon to Allah with insight—I and he who follows me. Immaculate is Allah, and I am not one of the polytheists.’ (12:108). He mentioned that his path is the call to Allah with insight and sincerity to Allah without polytheism. Thus, his path is a call and sincerity, and following him and treading in his footsteps is only in that; so it is the attribute of whoever follows him.

Then Allah, the Glorified, mentioned that the Sharia (الشريعة) — the divinely ordained law and way of life — which He legislated for him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) is what represents this path, the path of call and sincerity. He said: ثُمَّ جَعَلْنَاكَ عَلَىٰ شَرِيعَةٍ مِّنَ الْأَمْرِ فَاتَّبِعْهَا — Then We set you on a law of the matter; so follow it (45:18). He also mentioned that it is submission (إسلام) to Allah when He said: فَإِنْ حَاجُّوكَ فَقُلْ أَسْلَمْتُ وَجْهِيَ لِلَّهِ وَمَنِ اتَّبَعَنِ — If they argue with you, say, ‘I have submitted my whole self to Allah, and [so has] he who follows me.’ (3:20). Then He attributed it to Himself and explained that it is His straight path (صراطه المستقيم), saying: وَأَنَّ هَٰذَا صِرَاطِي مُسْتَقِيمًا فَاتَّبِعُوهُ — ‘This is My straight path, so follow it’ (6:153). Thus, it became clear through all of this that Islam (which is the Sharia legislated for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), consisting of the foundational, ethical, and practical knowledge and his conduct in life) is the path of sincerity before Allah, the Glorified, which depends and is built upon love. Thus, it is the religion of sincerity, and it is the religion of love.

From all that has preceded, despite its length, the meaning of the verse we are interpreting becomes clear, namely His saying: قل إن كنتم تحبون الله فاتبعوني يحببكم الله. The meaning—and Allah knows best—is: if you wish to be sincere to Allah in your servitude by building upon love in reality, then follow this Sharia which is built upon love, represented by sincerity and Islam, and which is the straight path of Allah that leads its wayfarer to Him, the Exalted. If you follow me in my path, the nature of which is this, Allah will love you, and that is the greatest glad tidings for the lover. At that point, you will find what you desire. This is what a lover seeks through his love; this is what the Noble Verse necessitates by its absolute sense.

As for considering its occurrence after the verses forbidding the taking of disbelievers as allies and its connection to what preceded it, this guardianship (الولاية), because its realization requires the realization of love between the person and the one he takes as a guardian, as previously mentioned, the verse was looking toward calling them to follow the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) if they were truthful in their claim of the guardianship of Allah and that they are of His party. For the guardianship of Allah is not completed by following the disbelievers in their desires (and there is no guardianship except through following) and seeking the worldly gains they possess of honor and wealth. Rather, it requires following His Prophet in his religion, as the Exalted said: ثُمَّ جَعَلْنَاكَ عَلَىٰ شَرِيعَةٍ مِّنَ الْأَمْرِ فَاتَّبِعْهَا وَلَا تَتَّبِعْ أَهْوَاءَ الَّذِينَ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ إِنَّهُمْ لَن يُغْنُوا عَنكَ مِنَ اللَّهِ شَيْئًا وَإِنَّ الظَّالِمِينَ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلِيَاءُ بَعْضٍ وَاللَّهُ وَلِيُّ الْمُتَّقِينَ — Then We set you on a law of the matter; so follow it, and do not follow the desires of those who do not know. Indeed they will not avail you in any way against Allah. Indeed the wrongdoers are allies of one another, and Allah is the ally of the God-conscious. (45:18-19). Note the transition from the meaning of following (الاتباع) to the meaning of guardianship (الولاية) in the second verse.

Thus, it is mandatory for whoever claims the guardianship of Allah through His love to follow the Messenger until that leads to Allah's guardianship of him through His love.

He mentioned the love of Allah rather than His guardianship because it is the foundation upon which guardianship is built. He restricted it to the mention of the love of Allah alone because the guardianship of the Prophet and the believers returns in reality to the guardianship of Allah.

His saying, the Exalted: ويغفر لكم ذنوبكم والله غفور رحيم — and forgive you your sins, and Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (3:31). The vast divine mercy and the infinite spiritual and formal emanations (الفيوضات) He possesses are not restricted to a specific person or class of His servants. There is no exception there that governs the absolute nature of His emanation, nor is there a way that necessitates His withholding, except for the deprivation arising from the lack of readiness of the deprived recipient or a barrier he manifested through his evil choice. The Exalted said: وَمَا كَانَ عَطَاءُ رَبِّكَ مَحْظُورًا — and the bounty of your Lord is not confined. (17:20).

Sins (الذنوب) are what prevent one from attaining the honor of proximity and nearness (القرب والزلفى) and all the matters that are among its consequences, such as Paradise and what is in it. Removing their rust from the human heart and their forgiveness and concealment is the only key to opening the door of felicity and entering the abode of honor. For this reason, He followed His saying يحببكم الله with His saying ويغفر لكم ذنوبكم. For love, as previously mentioned, draws the lover to the beloved. Just as the servant's love for his Lord necessitates his seeking proximity through sincerity to Him and restricting servitude to Him, likewise His love, the Exalted, for His servant necessitates His proximity to the servant, and His lifting the veils of distance and the splendors of absence. There is no veil except sin, so that necessitates the forgiveness of sins. As for the honor and emanation following that, generosity (الجود) is sufficient for it, as mentioned just now.

Contemplating the saying of the Exalted: كَلَّا بَلْ رَانَ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِم مَّا كَانُوا يَكْسِبُونَ كَلَّا إِنَّهُمْ عَن رَّبِّهِمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ لَّمَحْجُوبُونَ — No indeed! Rather their hearts have been sullied by what they have been earning. No indeed! They will be veiled from their Lord on that day. (83:14-15), along with His saying in this verse: يحببكم الله ويغفر لكم ذنوبكم, is sufficient to support what we have mentioned.

His saying, the Exalted: قل أطيعوا الله والرسول — Say, ‘Obey Allah and the Messenger.’ (3:32). Since the previous verse called for following the Messenger, and following—which is treading in the footsteps—is not completed except when the one followed is traversing a path, and the path traversed by the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) is none other than the straight path (الصراط المستقيم) which belongs to Allah the Glorified, and it is the Sharia He legislated for His Prophet and made obedience to him therein mandatory, He repeated a second time in this verse the meaning of following the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in the form of obedience. This was to signal that the path of sincerity, which is the path of the Prophet, is itself the sum of commands, prohibitions, call, and guidance. Thus, following the Messenger in traversing his path is the obedience to Allah and His Messenger in the legislated Sharia. Perhaps His mention of Himself along with the Messenger was to signal that the matter is one, and the Messenger was mentioned with Him, the Glorified, because the discussion is about following him.

From here, the lack of soundness in what some have mentioned regarding the verse becomes clear: that the meaning is "Obey Allah in His Book and the Messenger in his Sunnah."

This is because it contradicts what is apparent from the context—that His saying قل أطيعوا الله والرسول etc., is like an explanation for His saying: قُلْ إِن كُنتُمْ تُحِبُّونَ اللَّهَ فَاتَّبِعُوني. Furthermore, the verse suggests that the obedience to Allah and the obedience to the Messenger are one, which is why the command was not repeated. If the object of obedience were different for Allah and His Messenger, it would have been more appropriate to say: "Obey Allah and obey the Messenger," as in the saying of the Exalted: أَطِيعُوا اللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ وَأُولِي الْأَمْرِ مِنكُمْ — Obey Allah and obey the Apostle and those vested with authority among you (4:59), as is not hidden.

Know that the discussion on this verse in terms of its absolute sense and its application to the context is similar to the discussion on the previous verse.

His saying, the Exalted: فإن تولوا فإن الله لا يحب الكافرين — But if they turn away, indeed Allah does not like the faithless. (3:32). This contains an indication of the disbelief (كفر) of the one who turns away from this matter, as indicated by other verses forbidding the alliance with disbelievers. It also contains a signal that this verse is like an explanation for the one before it, as it concluded with the negation of love for the disbelievers regarding the matter of obedience, while the first verse contained the affirmation of love for the believers who are submissive to the command of following. So understand that.

From the discussion on these Noble Verses, several matters have become clear:

First: The permissibility of taqiyyah (التقية) in general.

Second: That the accountability for taking disbelievers as allies and rebelling against the prohibition therein will never fail; it is of the inevitable decree (القضاء الحتم).

Third: That the divine Sharia represents sincerity (الإخلاص) to Allah, and sincerity to Him represents the love of Allah the Glorified. In other words, the religion—which is the sum of divine knowledge, ethical matters, and practical rulings in their vast breadth—does not end, according to analysis, except at sincerity alone. This is the human being placing his essence, the attributes of his essence (which are ethics), and the actions and deeds of his essence on the basis that they are for Allah, the One, the All-paramount. The mentioned sincerity is not analyzed except into love. This is from the perspective of analysis. From the perspective of synthesis, love ends at sincerity, and sincerity at the sum of the Sharia, just as religion, from another perspective, is analyzed into submission (التسليم) and submission into monotheism (التوحيد).

Fourth: That taking the disbelievers as allies is disbelief (كفر). What is meant is disbelief in the branches (الفروع) rather than the foundations (الأصول), like the disbelief of the one who withholds zakat or abandons prayer. It is possible that the disbelief of the one who takes them as allies is by virtue of what the matter of alliance leads to, as previously explained, and as will come in Surah al-Ma'idah.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, regarding the saying of the Exalted: لا يتخذ المؤمنون الكافرين أولياء the verse: Ibn Ishaq, Ibn Jarir, and Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas who said: "Al-Hajjaj ibn ‘Amr was an ally of Ka‘b ibn al-Ashraf, Ibn Abi al-Huqayq, and Qays ibn Zayd. They had become intimate with a group of the Ansar to tempt them away from their religion. Rifa‘ah ibn al-Mundhir, ‘Abdullah ibn Jubayr, and Sa‘d ibn Khaythamah said to that group: 'Avoid these individuals from the Jews, and beware of intimacy with them lest they tempt you away from your religion.' But that group refused, so Allah revealed: لا يتخذ المؤمنون الكافرين until His saying: والله على كل شيء قدير."

I say: The narration does not fit the outward sense of the verse because, as previously mentioned, the term "disbelievers" (الكافرين) in the Quran is not known to be applied absolutely to the People of the Book. Thus, it is more appropriate for the story to be the occasion of revelation for the verses forbidding the taking of Jews and Christians as allies, rather than these verses.

In al-Safi, regarding the saying of the Exalted: إلا أن تتقوا منهم تقية the verse: from the book al-Ihtijaj, narrated from the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) in a tradition: "And He commanded you to employ taqiyyah (التقية) in your religion, for Allah says: لا يتخذ المؤمنون الكافرين أولياء من دون المؤمنين until His saying: إلا أن تتقوا منهم تقية. Beware, and again beware, of exposing yourself to destruction and of abandoning the taqiyyah I have commanded you with, for you would be squandering your blood and the blood of your brothers, exposing your blessings and theirs to cessation, and humiliating them in the hands of the enemies of Allah's religion, while Allah has commanded you to honor them."

In the Tafsir of al-‘Ayyashi, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), who said: "The Messenger of Allah used to say: 'There is no religion for one who has no taqiyyah.' and he would say: 'Allah said: إلا أن تتقوا منهم تقية.'"

In al-Kafi, from al-Baqir (peace be upon him): "Taqiyyah is in everything that the son of Adam is forced into, and Allah has made it lawful for him."

I say: The reports regarding the legislated nature of taqiyyah from the paths of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt are very numerous, perhaps reaching the level of tawatur (التواتر) — successive transmission by so many narrators that it precludes any possibility of fabrication. You have seen the indication of the verse for it, an indication that is irrefutable.

In Ma‘ani al-Akhbar, from Sa‘id ibn Yasar, who said: "Abu ‘Abdillah said to me: 'Is religion anything but love? Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, says: قل إن كنتم تحبون الله فاتبعوني يحببكم الله.'"

I say: It was narrated in al-Kafi from al-Baqir (peace be upon him), and likewise by al-Qummi and al-‘Ayyashi in their two commentaries from al-Hadhdha’ from him (peace be upon him). Likewise, al-‘Ayyashi in his commentary from Burayd from him (peace be upon him), and from Rib‘i from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him). The narration supports what we clarified in the preceding commentary.

In al-Ma‘ani, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), who said: "He has not loved Allah who has disobeyed Him." Then he cited as an example the saying:

You disobey the God while you display His love, This, by my life, is a strange thing among deeds. If your love were sincere, you would have obeyed Him, For the lover is obedient to the one he loves.

In al-Kafi, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) in a tradition, he said: "Whoever is pleased to know that Allah loves him, let him act in obedience to Allah and let him follow us. Has he not heard the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, to His Prophet: قل إن كنتم تحبون الله فاتبعوني يحببكم الله ويغفر لكم ذنوبكم?" The tradition.

I say: The explanation of how following them is following the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) will come in the discussion on the saying of the Exalted: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَطِيعُوا اللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ وَأُولِي الْأَمْرِ مِنكُمْ (4:59).

In al-Durr al-Manthur, ‘Abd ibn Humayd narrated from al-Hasan who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'Whoever turns away from my Sunnah is not of me.' Then he recited this verse: قل إن كنتم تحبون الله فاتبعوني يحببكم الله until the end of the verse."

In it also, Ibn Abi Hatim, Abu Nu‘aym in al-Hilyah, and al-Hakim narrated from ‘Aishah who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'Polytheism (الشرك) is more hidden than the crawling of an ant on a smooth rock in a dark night. Its lowest form is to love based on something of injustice, or to hate based on something of justice. And is religion anything but love and hatred for the sake of Allah? Allah the Exalted said: قل إن كنتم تحبون الله فاتبعوني يحببكم الله.'"

In it also, Ahmad, Abu Dawud, al-Tirmidhi, Ibn Majah, Ibn Hibban, and al-Hakim narrated from Abu Rafi‘ from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) who said: "Let me not find one of you reclining on his couch, when a matter of my command comes to him—of what I have commanded or forbidden—and he says: 'We do not know; whatever we found in the Book of Allah, we followed it.'"

Ayahs

۞ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ ٱصْطَفَىٰٓ ءَادَمَ وَنُوحًا وَءَالَ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ وَءَالَ عِمْرَٰنَ عَلَى ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٣٣)
Indeed Allah chose Adam and Noah, and the progeny of Abraham and the progeny of Imran above all the nations; (33)
ذُرِّيَّةًۢ بَعْضُهَا مِنۢ بَعْضٍ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ (٣٤)
some of them are descendants of the others, and Allah is all-hearing, all-knowing. (34)

(Commentary)

This is the opening of the accounts concerning Jesus, son of Mary (عيسى بن مريم), and what pertains to them, including the mention of the truth regarding them and the argumentation against the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب) — those to whom the Divine Scriptures were previously revealed — concerning his matter. Through these two verses, what follows is linked to the preceding verses that addressed the condition of the People of the Book.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ اللَّهَ اصْطَفَىٰ آدَمَ وَنُوحًا (Indeed Allah chose Adam and Noah...) to the end of the verse. Selection (الاصطفاء), as previously explained in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: لَقَدِ اصْطَفَيْنَاهُ فِي الدُّنْيَا (We chose him in the world) [al-Baqarah: 130], means taking the purest part of a thing and refining it from whatever sullies it. Thus, it is close in meaning to "choosing" (الاختيار). In the stations of guardianship (الولاية) — the state of proximity and divine authority — it corresponds to the station of submission (الإسلام), which is the servant’s proceeding in the course of pure surrender to the command of his Lord in whatever He deems pleasing for him.

However, this is distinct from being "chosen over all the worlds" (الاصطفاء على العالمين). Had the intended meaning of selection here been that general sense, it would have been more appropriate to say "from the worlds" (من العالمين), which would have implied the exclusivity of submission to them and altered the meaning of the discourse. Thus, selection over the worlds is a type of choosing and prioritizing them over others in a matter or matters in which no one else shares.

Evidence for what we have mentioned regarding the difference in selection is His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذْ قَالَتِ الْمَلَائِكَةُ يَا مَرْيَمُ إِنَّ اللَّهَ اصْطَفَاكِ وَطَهَّرَكِ وَاصْطَفَاكِ عَلَىٰ نِسَاءِ الْعَالَمِينَ (And when the angels said, ‘O Mary, Allah has chosen you and purified you, and He has chosen you above the women of all the worlds’) [Al ‘Imran: 42]. Here, He distinguished between the two selections; thus, one selection is not the same as the other.

The Glorified has mentioned among these chosen ones Adam and Noah. As for Adam, he was chosen over all the worlds by being the first vicegerent (خليفة) — a representative or deputy — of this human species whom Allah placed on the earth. The Exalted said: وَإِذْ قَالَ رَبُّكَ لِلْمَلَائِكَةِ إِنِّي جَاعِلٌ فِي الْأَرْضِ خَلِيفَةً (When your Lord said to the angels, ‘Indeed I am going to set a viceroy on the earth’) [al-Baqarah: 30]. He was also the first for whom the door of repentance (التوبة) was opened. The Exalted said: ثُمَّ اجْتَبَاهُ رَبُّهُ فَتَابَ عَلَيْهِ وَهَدَىٰ (Then his Lord chose him, and turned to him [clemently], and guided him) [Taha: 122]. Furthermore, he was the first for whom the religion (الدين) was legislated. The Exalted said: فَإِمَّا يَأْتِيَنَّكُم مِّنِّي هُدًى فَمَنِ اتَّبَعَ هُدَايَ فَلَا يَضِلُّ وَلَا يَشْقَىٰ (Then if there comes to you any guidance from Me, whoever follows My guidance shall neither go astray nor be wretched) [Taha: 123]. These are matters in which no one else shares with him, and what a great merit (منقبة) this is for him (عليه السلام).

As for Noah, he is the first of the five "Arch-Prophets" (أولو العزم) — the messengers of strong resolve who brought new divine laws — who possessed a Book and a Law (الشريعة), as previously explained in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: كَانَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً وَاحِدَةً فَبَعَثَ اللَّهُ النَّبِيِّينَ (Mankind were a single community; then Allah sent the prophets...) [al-Baqarah: 213]. He is the second father of this species, and Allah, the Exalted, bestowed peace upon him among all the worlds. The Exalted said: وَجَعَلْنَا ذُرِّيَّتَهُ هُمُ الْبَاقِينَ وَتَرَكْنَا عَلَيْهِ فِي الْآخِرِينَ سَلَامٌ عَلَىٰ نُوحٍ فِي الْعَالَمِينَ (And made his offspring the survivors, and left for him [a good name] among posterity: ‘Peace be to Noah throughout the worlds!’) [as-Saffat: 77-79].

Then the Glorified mentioned the Progeny of Abraham (آل إبراهيم) and the Progeny of ‘Imran (آل عمران) among these chosen ones. The term "Progeny" (الآل) refers to the elite or specific associates of a person. Al-Raghib said in al-Mufradat: "It is said that al-Al (الآل) is a metathesis of al-Ahl (الأهل) — family or kin. Its diminutive form is Uhayl, except that it is specifically used in annexation to prominent rational beings, not to indefinite nouns, nor to times or places. One says 'the Al of so-and-so,' but one does not say 'the Al of a man,' nor 'the Al of such a time' or 'such a place.' Nor does one say 'the Al of the tailor'; rather, it is annexed to the most noble and virtuous. One says 'the Al of Allah' and 'the Al of the Sultan.' In contrast, Ahl is annexed to everything; one says 'the Ahl of Allah,' 'the Ahl of the tailor,' 'the Ahl of such a time,' and 'the Ahl of such a city.' It is also said that it is originally a name for the person himself, with the diminutive Uwayl, and it is used for those who have an essential specialty (اختصاص ذاتي) with a person, either through close kinship or through devotion (الموالاة)." This concludes the relevant portion of his words. Thus, what is meant by the Progeny of Abraham and the Progeny of ‘Imran are their elite from their families and those joined to them, as you have come to know.

As for the Progeny of Abraham, the apparent meaning of the term is the pure ones from his offspring, such as Isaac, Israel, and the prophets from the Children of Israel, as well as Ishmael and the pure ones from his offspring—their master being Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)—and those joined to them in the stations of guardianship (الولاية). However, the mention of the Progeny of ‘Imran alongside the Progeny of Abraham indicates that the term was not used in that broad sense. For this ‘Imran is either the father of Mary or the father of Moses (عليه السلام). In either case, he is from the offspring of Abraham, as is his progeny. Since they have been mentioned separately from the Progeny of Abraham, the intended meaning of "Progeny of Abraham" here is some of his pure offspring, not all of them.

Allah, the Exalted, said in what He said: أَمْ يَحْسُدُونَ النَّاسَ عَلَىٰ مَا آتَاهُمُ اللَّهُ مِن فَضْلِهِ فَقَدْ آتَيْنَا آلَ إِبْرَاهِيمَ الْكِتَابَ وَالْحِكْمَةَ وَآتَيْنَاهُم مُّلْكًا عَظِيمًا (Do they envy the people for what Allah has given them out of His bounty? We have certainly given the Progeny of Abraham the Book and wisdom, and We have given them a great kingdom) [an-Nisa: 54]. This verse is in the context of rebuking the Children of Israel and censuring them, as becomes clear by referring to its context and the surrounding verses. From this, it appears that the intended meaning of "Progeny of Abraham" therein is other than the Children of Israel—meaning other than Isaac, Jacob, and the descendants of Jacob (who are the Children of Israel). Thus, nothing remains for the "Progeny of Abraham" except the pure ones from his offspring through the line of Ishmael, among whom are the Prophet and his Progeny (آله).

Furthermore, we shall explain, Allah willing, that the intended meaning of "the people" (الناس) in that verse is the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and that he is included in the Progeny of Abraham by the indication of the verse.

This is also suggested by His saying, the Exalted, at the conclusion of these verses: إِنَّ أَوْلَى النَّاسِ بِإِبْرَاهِيمَ لَلَّذِينَ اتَّبَعُوهُ وَهَٰذَا النَّبِيُّ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا (Indeed the nearest of all people to Abraham are those who followed him, and this prophet and those who have faith...) [Al ‘Imran: 68], and His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذْ يَرْفَعُ إِبْرَاهِيمُ الْقَوَاعِدَ مِنَ الْبَيْتِ وَإِسْمَاعِيلُ رَبَّنَا تَقَبَّلْ مِنَّا إِنَّكَ أَنتَ السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ رَبَّنَا وَاجْعَلْنَا مُسْلِمَيْنِ لَكَ وَمِن ذُرِّيَّتِنَا أُمَّةً مُّسْلِمَةً لَّكَ وَأَرِنَا مَنَاسِكَنَا — until He said — رَبَّنَا وَابْعَثْ فِيهِمْ رَسُولًا مِّنْهُمْ يَتْلُو عَلَيْهِمْ آيَاتِكَ وَيُعَلِّمُهُمُ الْكِتَابَ وَالْحِكْمَةَ وَيُزَكِّيهِمْ (As Abraham raised the foundations of the House with Ishmael, [they prayed]: ‘Our Lord, accept it from us!
