Indeed You are the All-hearing, the All-knowing. Our Lord, make us submissive to You, and [raise] from our progeny a nation submissive to You, and show us our holy rites... Our Lord, raise amongst them an apostle from among themselves, who should recite to them Your signs, and teach them the Book and wisdom, and purify them...’) [al-Baqarah: 127-129].

Therefore, the intended meaning of the Progeny of Abraham is the pure ones from his offspring through the line of Ishmael. The verse is not in the context of exclusivity (الحصر); thus, there is no contradiction between its not mentioning the selection of Abraham himself, or Moses, and the other pure prophets from his offspring through the line of Isaac, and what many other verses establish regarding their merits, the loftiness of their status, and the height of their station. These are very numerous verses that need not be cited here, for the affirmation of one thing does not necessitate the negation of others.

Similarly, this does not contradict what has been revealed concerning the Children of Israel in His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنَا بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ الْكِتَابَ وَالْحُكْمَ وَالنُّبُوَّةَ وَرَزَقْنَاهُم مِّنَ الطَّيِّبَاتِ وَفَضَّلْنَاهُمْ عَلَى الْعَالَمِينَ (Certainly We gave the Children of Israel the Book, judgement and prophethood and We provided them with all the good things, and We preferred them over all the nations) [al-Jathiyah: 16]. All of this is evident.

Nor does their being preferred over the worlds contradict the preference of others over the worlds, nor the preference of others over them. For the preference of one people or various peoples over others only necessitates their precedence in a worldly or otherworldly virtue over those beneath them. If their preference over the people contradicted the preference of others, or if the preference of those mentioned in the verse—namely Adam, Noah, the Progeny of Abraham, and the Progeny of ‘Imran—over the worlds contradicted the preference of others over the worlds, it would necessitate a contradiction among those mentioned in the verse themselves; and this is obvious.

Furthermore, the preference of these over others does not contradict the occurrence of varying degrees of excellence (التفاضل) among themselves. Allah has preferred the prophets over all the worlds and preferred some of them over others. The Exalted said: وَكُلًّا فَضَّلْنَا عَلَى الْعَالَمِينَ (...and each We graced over all the nations) [al-An‘am: 86], and He also said: وَلَقَدْ فَضَّلْنَا بَعْضَ النَّبِيِّينَ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ (...and We have certainly preferred some of the prophets over others) [al-Isra: 55].

As for the Progeny of ‘Imran, it appears that the intended ‘Imran is the father of Mary, as suggested by the fact that these two verses are followed by verses mentioning the story of the wife of ‘Imran and Mary, the daughter of ‘Imran. ‘Imran, the father of Mary, is repeatedly mentioned by name in the Noble Qur’an, whereas ‘Imran, the father of Moses, is not mentioned even in a single place where it is certain that he is the one intended. This supports the view that the ‘Imran intended in the verse is the father of Mary (عليها السلام). Accordingly, the Progeny of ‘Imran refers to Mary and Jesus (عليهما السلام), or the two of them and the wife of ‘Imran.

As for the claim that the Christians do not recognize the name of Mary’s father as ‘Imran, the Qur’an does not follow their whims.

His saying, the Exalted: ذُرِّيَّةً بَعْضُهَا مِن بَعْضٍ (Offspring, one from the other). Offspring (الذرية) originally refers to young children, as mentioned by linguists, and was then used for children in general, which is the meaning intended in the verse. It is in the accusative case as an "explanatory apposition" (عطف بيان).

In His saying, "one from the other" (بعضها من بعض), there is an indication that every part assumed from them begins and ends from and to the other part. This necessitates that the whole is uniform in its parts (متشابه الأجزاء), where one part does not differ from another in its attributes and states. Since the discourse is about their selection, this implies that they are an offspring who do not differ in the attributes of virtue for which Allah chose them over the worlds; for there is no randomness (جزاف) or play (لعب) in Divine acts, among which is selection, which is the source of significant goods in the world.

His saying, the Exalted: وَاللَّهُ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ (And Allah is All-hearing, All-knowing), meaning He is All-hearing of their speech which indicates their inner thoughts, and All-knowing of their inner thoughts and what is in their hearts. Thus, the sentence serves as a justification (تعليل) for their selection, just as His saying, "offspring, one from the other," serves as a justification for the inclusion of this group in the gift of selection. The sum of the discourse is that Allah chose these over the worlds, and the selection extended to all of them because they are an offspring whose individuals are similar—some returning to others in the submission of hearts and the steadfastness of speaking the truth. He graced them with selection over the worlds because He is All-hearing and All-knowing; He hears their words and knows what is in their hearts.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Uyun, in the discourse of Imam al-Rida with al-Ma’mun: "Al-Ma’mun said: 'Has Allah preferred the Kinship (العترة) — the Prophet's immediate family and descendants — over the rest of the people?' Abu al-Hasan replied: 'Indeed, Allah has made clear the preference of the Kinship over the rest of the people in the decisive part of His Book.' Al-Ma’mun asked: 'Where is that in the Book of Allah?' Imam al-Rida (peace be upon him) said to him: 'In His saying: إِنَّ اللَّهَ اصْطَفَىٰ آدَمَ وَنُوحًا وَآلَ إِبْرَاهِيمَ وَآلَ عِمْرَانَ عَلَى الْعَالَمِينَ ذُرِّيَّةً بَعْضُهَا مِن بَعْضٍ (Indeed Allah chose Adam and Noah, and the Progeny of Abraham and the Progeny of ‘Imran above all the nations; offspring, one from the other)...'" the hadith.

In the Tafsir of al-‘Ayyashi, from Ahmad ibn Muhammad, from al-Rida, from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon them both): "Whoever claims that the matter is finished has lied, for the Will (المشية) belongs to Allah regarding His creation; He intends what He wills and does what He desires. Allah said: ذُرِّيَّةً بَعْضُهَا مِن بَعْضٍ وَاللَّهُ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ (Offspring, one from the other; and Allah is All-hearing, All-knowing). Its last is from its first, and its first is from its last. So if you are informed of something specific from it that it shall be, and it occurs in something else from it, then the report has indeed come to pass regarding what you were informed of."

I say: In this, there is an indication of what was mentioned in the preceding commentary regarding the meaning of His saying: ذُرِّيَّةً بَعْضُهَا مِن بَعْضٍ (Offspring, one from the other).

Also in it, from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him): He recited this verse and said: "We are from them, and we are the remnant of that Kinship (العترة)."

I say: His saying (peace be upon him), "and we are the remnant of that Kinship," refers to the term ‘Itrah (العترة), which according to its original meaning refers to the foundation upon which a thing relies. From this comes the use of ‘Itrah for children and the nearest relatives of those who have passed. In other words, it is the "preserved pillar" within a clan. From this, it appears that he (peace be upon him) derived from the saying of the Exalted, "offspring, one from the other," that they are a preserved kinship extending from Adam to Noah to the Progeny of Abraham and the Progeny of ‘Imran. Herein lies the subtle point in mentioning Adam and Noah alongside the Progeny of Abraham and ‘Imran; it is an allusion to the continuity of the chain of selection (الاصطفاء).

Ayahs

إِذْ قَالَتِ ٱمْرَأَتُ عِمْرَٰنَ رَبِّ إِنِّى نَذَرْتُ لَكَ مَا فِى بَطْنِى مُحَرَّرًا فَتَقَبَّلْ مِنِّىٓ ۖ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ (٣٥)
When the wife of Imran said, ‘My Lord, I dedicate to You in consecration what is in my belly. Accept it from me; indeed You are the All-hearing, the All-knowing.’ (35)
فَلَمَّا وَضَعَتْهَا قَالَتْ رَبِّ إِنِّى وَضَعْتُهَآ أُنثَىٰ وَٱللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا وَضَعَتْ وَلَيْسَ ٱلذَّكَرُ كَٱلْأُنثَىٰ ۖ وَإِنِّى سَمَّيْتُهَا مَرْيَمَ وَإِنِّىٓ أُعِيذُهَا بِكَ وَذُرِّيَّتَهَا مِنَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ ٱلرَّجِيمِ (٣٦)
When she bore her, she said, ‘My Lord, I have borne a female [child]’—and Allah knew better what she had borne, and the male [child she expected] was no match for the female [child she had borne] —‘and I have named her Mary, and I commend her and her offspring to Your care against [the evil of] the outcast Satan.’ (36)
فَتَقَبَّلَهَا رَبُّهَا بِقَبُولٍ حَسَنٍ وَأَنۢبَتَهَا نَبَاتًا حَسَنًا وَكَفَّلَهَا زَكَرِيَّا ۖ كُلَّمَا دَخَلَ عَلَيْهَا زَكَرِيَّا ٱلْمِحْرَابَ وَجَدَ عِندَهَا رِزْقًا ۖ قَالَ يَـٰمَرْيَمُ أَنَّىٰ لَكِ هَـٰذَا ۖ قَالَتْ هُوَ مِنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَرْزُقُ مَن يَشَآءُ بِغَيْرِ حِسَابٍ (٣٧)
Thereupon her Lord accepted her with a gracious acceptance, and made her grow up in a worthy fashion, and He charged Zechariah with her care. Whenever Zechariah visited her in the sanctuary, he would find provisions with her. He said, ‘O Mary, from where does this come for you?’ She said, ‘It comes from Allah. Allah provides whomever He wishes without any reckoning.’ (37)
هُنَالِكَ دَعَا زَكَرِيَّا رَبَّهُۥ ۖ قَالَ رَبِّ هَبْ لِى مِن لَّدُنكَ ذُرِّيَّةً طَيِّبَةً ۖ إِنَّكَ سَمِيعُ ٱلدُّعَآءِ (٣٨)
Thereat Zechariah supplicated his Lord. He said, ‘My Lord! Grant me a good offspring from You! Indeed You hear all supplications.’ (38)
فَنَادَتْهُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ وَهُوَ قَآئِمٌ يُصَلِّى فِى ٱلْمِحْرَابِ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُبَشِّرُكَ بِيَحْيَىٰ مُصَدِّقًۢا بِكَلِمَةٍ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَسَيِّدًا وَحَصُورًا وَنَبِيًّا مِّنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ (٣٩)
Then, as he stood praying in the sanctuary the angels called out to him: ‘Allah gives you the good news of John, as a confirmer of a Word of Allah, eminent and chaste, a prophet, and one of the righteous.’ (39)
قَالَ رَبِّ أَنَّىٰ يَكُونُ لِى غُلَـٰمٌ وَقَدْ بَلَغَنِىَ ٱلْكِبَرُ وَٱمْرَأَتِى عَاقِرٌ ۖ قَالَ كَذَٰلِكَ ٱللَّهُ يَفْعَلُ مَا يَشَآءُ (٤٠)
He said, ‘My Lord, how shall I have a son while old age has overtaken me and my wife is barren?’ Said He, ‘So it is that Allah does whatever He wishes.’ (40)
قَالَ رَبِّ ٱجْعَل لِّىٓ ءَايَةً ۖ قَالَ ءَايَتُكَ أَلَّا تُكَلِّمَ ٱلنَّاسَ ثَلَـٰثَةَ أَيَّامٍ إِلَّا رَمْزًا ۗ وَٱذْكُر رَّبَّكَ كَثِيرًا وَسَبِّحْ بِٱلْعَشِىِّ وَٱلْإِبْكَـٰرِ (٤١)
He said, ‘My Lord, grant me a sign.’ Said He, ‘Your sign is that you will not speak to people for three days except in gestures. Remember Your Lord much, and glorify Him morning and evening.’ (41)

(Commentary)

His saying, the Exalted: إذ قالت امرأة عمران رب إني نذرت لك ما في بطني محرراً فتقبل مني إنك أنت السميع العليم (When the wife of ‘Imran said, ‘My Lord, I have dedicated to You what is in my belly, to be devoted [to Your service]. So accept it from me; indeed You are the All-hearing, the All-knowing’): Vow (النذر) — the act of a human being making obligatory upon themselves that which was not originally obligatory. Devoted (التحرير) — the act of releasing from a bond; from this comes the manumission of a slave from servitude (الرقية), and the "editing" or "liberating" of a book (تحرير الكتاب) as if it were a release of meanings from the confines of the mind and thought. Acceptance (التقبل) — the act of receiving with desire and pleasure, such as the acceptance of a gift or the acceptance of a prayer and the like.

In her saying: قالت امرأة عمران رب إني نذرت لك ما في بطني (the wife of ‘Imran said, ‘My Lord, I have dedicated to You what is in my belly’), there is an indication that she uttered this while she was pregnant, and that her pregnancy was from ‘Imran. The discourse is not devoid of an intimation that her husband, ‘Imran, was not alive at that time; otherwise, she would not have been able to independently dedicate what was in her womb with such autonomy. This is also indicated by what follows in His saying, the Exalted: وَمَا كُنتَ لَدَيْهِمْ إِذْ يُلْقُونَ أَقْلَامَهُمْ أَيُّهُمْ يَكْفُلُ مَرْيَمَ — "You were not with them when they were casting their pens [to see] which of them would take charge of Mary" (3:44), as will be explained later.

It is well known that the "liberation" (تحرير) of a child by a father or mother is not a liberation from slavery (الرقية), but rather a liberation from the bond of guardianship (الولاية) that parents hold over a child regarding their upbringing, their employment for parental purposes, and the obligation of obedience. Through this liberation, the child exits the dominion of the parents in terms of service. When this liberation is vowed to Allah, the Glorified, the child enters the guardianship of Allah to worship Him and serve Him—meaning, to serve in the synagogues, churches, and places designated for His worship at a time when the child would have otherwise been under parental guardianship. It has been said that they used to dedicate the child to Allah, so the parents would not employ the child for their own benefit or use them for their needs; rather, the child would be placed in the temple to sweep and serve it, not leaving until reaching puberty. Then, the child would be given the choice between staying or leaving; if they wished to stay, they stayed, and if they wished to leave, they went about their business.

The discourse indicates that she believed what was in her womb was a male, not a female, as she supplicates her Lord with certainty and decisiveness, without condition or contingency, saying: نذرت لك ما في بطني محرراً (I have dedicated to You what is in my belly, to be devoted), without saying, for example, "if it be a male," or the like.

The masculine form of His saying: محرراً (devoted/liberated), is not because it is a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for the relative pronoun "what" (ما)—which is used for both masculine and feminine—because if she had vowed the liberation of what was in her womb whether it be male or female, there would be no reason for her to speak with such grief and regret when she delivered it: رب إني وضعتها انثى (My Lord, I have delivered a female). Nor would there be an apparent reason for His saying, the Exalted: والله أعلم بما وضعت وليس الذكر كالانثى (And Allah knew better what she had delivered, and the male is not like the female), as will be explained.

In the Exalted's narration of her speaking with certainty, there is an indication that her belief was not based on conjecture or reliance on intuitive signs (القرائن الحدسية) that often occur to women through experience and the like; for all of that is mere opinion, and opinion avails nothing against the truth. His speech, the Exalted, does not contain falsehood except to nullify it. He, the Exalted, has said: اللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ مَا تَحْمِلُ كُلُّ أُنثَىٰ وَمَا تَغِيضُ الْأَرْحَامُ وَمَا تَزْدَادُ — "Allah knows what every female carries, and what the wombs diminish and what they swell" (13:8), and He said: عِندَهُ عِلْمُ السَّاعَةِ وَيُنَزِّلُ الْغَيْثَ وَيَعْلَمُ مَا فِي الْأَرْحَامِ — "With Him is the knowledge of the Hour, and He sends down the rain, and He knows what is in the wombs" (31:34). Thus, He made the knowledge of what is in the wombs part of the Unseen (الغيب) — that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge — which is exclusive to Him. He also said: عَالِمُ الْغَيْبِ فَلَا يُظْهِرُ عَلَىٰ غَيْبِهِ أَحَدًا إِلَّا مَنِ ارْتَضَىٰ — "Knower of the Unseen, He does not disclose His Unseen to anyone, except to an apostle He approves" (72:26-27). Thus, He made the knowledge of the Unseen by others contingent upon revelation (الوحي). Therefore, His narration of her decisiveness in speech regarding what is exclusive to the knowledge of Allah, the Glorified, indicates that her knowledge of the masculinity of what was in her womb was ultimately derived from revelation. For this reason, when it became clear that the child was female, she did not despair of a male child; she spoke a second time with certainty and decisiveness: وإني اعيذها بك وذريتها من الشيطان الرجيم (and I commend her to Your protection, plus her offspring, from the outcast Satan), thus affirming offspring for her, though there was no apparent way to know this.

The object of her saying: فتقبل مني (so accept from me), although omitted, could potentially be her vow—insofar as it is a righteous deed—or it could be her liberated child. However, His saying, the Exalted: فتقبلها ربها بقبول حسن (So her Lord accepted her with a gracious acceptance), is not devoid of an intimation or indication that her intent was the acceptance of the liberated child.

His saying, the Exalted: فلما وضعتها قالت : رب إني وضعتها انثى (But when she delivered her, she said, ‘My Lord, I have delivered a female’): In placing the feminine pronoun in place of "what was in her belly," there is a subtle brevity. The meaning is: when she delivered what was in her belly and it became clear it was a female, she said: "My Lord, I have delivered a female." This is a statement intended to express regret and grief rather than mere reporting, as is evident.

His saying, the Exalted: والله أعلم بما وضعت وليس الذكر كالانثى (And Allah knew better what she had delivered, and the male is not like the female): These are two parenthetical sentences, both of which are the speech of the Exalted, not the wife of ‘Imran; nor is the second her speech and the first Allah's.

As for the first, it is evident. However, since her saying: رب إني وضعتها انثى (My Lord, I have delivered a female), was intended to show regret, the apparent meaning of His saying: والله أعلم بما وضعت (And Allah knew better what she had delivered), is that it was intended to clarify: "We know she is a female, but We intended by that to fulfill what she hoped for in the best possible way and the most pleasing path. Had she known what We intended by making what was in her womb a female, she would not have felt that regret and grief. For the male she was hoping for could never have become like this female We have granted her, nor could there have resulted from him what will result from the creation of this female. The utmost of his affair would have been to become like Jesus—a prophet healing the blind and the leper and raising the dead—but this female shall be the one through whom the Word of Allah is completed; she shall give birth to a child without a father, and she and her son shall be made a sign for all the worlds. He shall speak to people in the cradle, and he shall be a Spirit and a Word from Allah; his likeness with Allah is as the likeness of Adam, among other dazzling signs in the creation of this pure, blessed female and the creation of her son, Jesus (peace be upon them both)."

From here it becomes clear that His saying: وليس الذكر كالانثى (and the male is not like the female), is the speech of the Exalted, not the wife of ‘Imran. Had it been her speech, the proper phrasing would have been: "and the female is not like the male," not the reverse. This is evident because one who hopes for something noble or a high station and is then granted something lesser or inferior says in their regret: "This which I found is not what I was seeking," or "What I was granted is not like what I hoped for." One never says: "What I hoped for is not like this which I was granted." Thus, it becomes clear that the definite article (اللام) in both "the male" (الذكر) and "the female" (الانثى), or in "the female" alone, is for reference (للعهد) — referring to the specific male she hoped for and the specific female she delivered.

Most commentators have taken His saying: وليس الذكر كالانثى (and the male is not like the female), as a continuation of the wife of ‘Imran's speech, and they have struggled to justify the precedence of "the male" over "the female" in a way that yields no real result; whoever wishes may refer to their books.

His saying, the Exalted: وإني سميتها مريم وإني أُعيذها بك وذريتها من الشيطان الرجيم (I have named her Mary, and I commend her to Your protection, plus her offspring, from the outcast Satan): The meaning of Mary (مريم) in their language is "the worshipping woman" or "the servant," as has been said. From this, the reason for her haste in naming the newborn at birth becomes known, as does the reason for the Exalted mentioning her naming her so. For when she despaired of the child being a male liberated for worship and the service of the temple, she hastened to this naming, preparing her through the name for worship and service. Thus, her saying: وإني سميتها مريم (I have named her Mary), is equivalent to her saying: "I have made what I delivered devoted to You." The evidence that this statement of hers carries the meaning of a vow is His saying, the Exalted: فتقبلها ربها بقبول حسن وأنبتها نباتاً حسناً (So her Lord accepted her with a gracious acceptance and made her grow up a goodly growth).

Then she commended her and her offspring to Allah's protection from the outcast Satan (الشيطان الرجيم) so that worship and service might be upright for her, and so the name might match the named.

The discussion regarding her saying: وذريتها (and her offspring), insofar as it is an absolute statement without condition or restriction, is that it is not something one would utter in the presence of discourse if they had no knowledge of it, especially since the future state of a human being is part of the Unseen (الغيب) which none knows but Allah, the Glorified. This is similar to the discussion regarding her saying: رب إني نذرت لك ما في بطني محرراً (My Lord, I have dedicated to You what is in my belly, to be devoted), as previously explained. It can only be that she knew she would be granted a righteous male child from ‘Imran; then when she became pregnant and ‘Imran died, she did not doubt that what was in her womb was that promised child. Then when she delivered her and her intuition appeared mistaken, she became certain that she would be granted that child from the lineage of this newborn girl. So she transferred her vow from the son to the daughter, named her Mary (the worshipper, the servant), and commended her and her offspring to Allah from the outcast Satan. This is what reflection upon His speech, the Exalted, yields.

His saying, the Exalted: فتقبلها ربها بقبول حسن وأنبتها نباتاً حسناً (So her Lord accepted her with a gracious acceptance and made her grow up a goodly growth): Acceptance (القبول), when qualified by "gracious" (الحسن), is semantically equivalent to "receiving with pleasure" (التقبل). Thus, the discourse is in the meaning of our saying: "So her Lord received her with pleasure (تقبلاً)." The term "receiving with pleasure" was analyzed into "gracious acceptance" to indicate that the excellence of the acceptance is intended in the discourse, and because of the manifest honor in explicitly stating the graciousness of the acceptance.

Since these two sentences—His saying: فتقبلها (So He accepted her) to His saying: حسناً (goodly)—correspond to the two sentences in her saying: وإني سميتها (And I have named her) to her saying: الرجيم (the outcast), the requirement of correspondence is that His saying: فتقبلها ربها بقبول حسن (So her Lord accepted her with a gracious acceptance), is an acceptance of her saying: وإني سميتها مريم (I have named her Mary), and His saying: وأنبتها نباتاً حسناً (and made her grow up a goodly growth), is an acceptance and response to her saying: وإني اعيذها بك وذريتها من الشيطان الرجيم (and I commend her to Your protection, plus her offspring, from the outcast Satan). Therefore, the meaning of "accepting her with a gracious acceptance" is not acceptance in the sense of accepting the wife of ‘Imran's seeking of proximity through the vow and granting the otherworldly reward for her deed—for the acceptance is attributed to Mary, not to the vow, as is evident—but rather the acceptance of the daughter insofar as she is named Mary and is devoted. Thus, its meaning returns to her being chosen (الاصطفاء) — and it has been mentioned that the meaning of being chosen is total submission to Allah, the Glorified — so understand that.

The meaning of "making her grow up a goodly growth" is granting maturity and purity to her and her offspring, and bestowing life upon her and those who grow from her offspring—a life untouched by the breath of Satan or the filth of his enticement and whispering; and that is purity (الطهارة).

These two—namely, the gracious acceptance referring to being chosen (الاصطفاء), and the goodly growth referring to purification (التطهير)—are what His saying, the Exalted, at the end of these verses refers to: وإذ قالت الملائكة يا مريم إن الله اصطفاك وطهرك (And when the angels said, ‘O Mary, Allah has chosen you and purified you’), and We shall clarify this in the commentary, Allah the Almighty willing.

It has thus become clear that the choosing of Mary and her purification were a response to her mother's supplication, just as her being chosen over the women of the worlds in the birth of Jesus, and her and her son being a sign for the worlds, is a confirmation of His saying, the Exalted: وليس الذكر كالانثى (and the male is not like the female).

His saying, the Exalted: وكفلها زكريا (and [He] put her in the care of Zechariah): He only took charge of her through the falling of the lot (القرعة) when they disputed over her care and then agreed among themselves on the lot, and the lot fell to Zechariah, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: وما كنت لديهم إذ يلقون أقلامهم أيهم يكفل مريم وما كنت لديهم إذ يختصمون (You were not with them when they were casting their pens [to see] which of them would take charge of Mary, nor were you with them when they were contending) (3:44).

His saying, the Exalted: كلما دخل عليها زكريا المحراب وجد عندها رزقاً (Whenever Zechariah visited her in the sanctuary, he would find provisions with her...): The sanctuary (المحراب) — the place designated for worship in a mosque or house. Al-Raghib said: "The sanctuary (محراب) of the mosque: it is said it was named so because it is the place of warring (محاربة) against Satan and passion. It is also said it was named so because it is the right of a person therein to be stripped (حريباً) of worldly preoccupations and distracted thoughts. It is also said that originally the sanctuary of a house was the head of the assembly; then when mosques were established, their front part was named so. It is also said that the sanctuary is originally in the mosque, a name specifically for the head of the assembly, and the head of a house was named sanctuary by way of comparison to the sanctuary of the mosque, and this seems more correct. He, the Almighty and Majestic, said: 'They made for him whatever he wished of sanctuaries and statues' (34:13)." End quote.

Some have mentioned that the sanctuary here is what the People of the Book refer to as the altar (المذبح), which is a chamber in the front of the temple with a door reached by a ladder of few steps, and whoever is in it is veiled from those in the temple.

I say: To this the practice of adopting a private chamber (المقصورة) in Islam traces back.

In the indefiniteness of His saying: رزقاً (provisions), there is an intimation that it was an unaccustomed provision, as it has been said: he used to find winter fruit with her in summer, and summer fruit in winter. This is supported by the fact that if it were accustomed provision, and its indefiniteness merely meant that he never found her sanctuary empty of provision but rather there was always some provision with her, Zechariah would not have been satisfied with her saying: هو من عند الله إن الله يرزق (It is from Allah. Indeed Allah provides...) in response to his question: يا مريم أنى لك هذا (O Mary, from where does this come to you?), because it would be possible that some people who frequented the mosque for a good or bad purpose were bringing it to her.

Furthermore, His saying, the Exalted: هنالك دعا زكريا ربه (Thereupon Zechariah supplicated his Lord...), indicates that Zechariah received the presence of this provision with her as a miraculous divine favor (كرامة إلهية خارقة), which necessitated that he ask Allah to grant him from His presence a goodly offspring. Thus, the provision was a provision whose very existence indicated it was a favor from Allah, the Glorified, to the pure Mary. This is intimated by His saying, the Exalted: قال يا مريم (He said, ‘O Mary...’), as will be explained.

His saying: قال يا مريم أنی لك (He said, ‘O Mary, from where...’), as a detached sentence without being conjoined to His saying: وجد عندها رزقاً (he would find provisions with her), indicates that he (peace be upon him) only said that to her once, and she answered with that which satisfied him, and he became certain that it was a favor for her; and thereupon he supplicated and asked his Lord for a goodly offspring.

His saying, the Exalted: هنالك دعا زكريا ربه قال رب هب لي من لدنك ذرية طيبة (Thereupon Zechariah supplicated his Lord. He said, ‘My Lord! Grant me from Your presence a goodly offspring...’): The "goodliness" (طيب) of a thing is its suitability for its owner in what he intends for it. Thus, a "goodly land" (البلد الطيب) is one that suits the life of its people in terms of water, air, and provision; the Exalted said: وَالْبَلَدُ الطَّيِّبُ يَخْرُجُ نَبَاتُهُ بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِ — "As for the good land, its vegetation comes out by the permission of its Lord" (7:58). A "goodly living" and a "goodly life" are those whose parts suit one another and in which the heart of its owner finds rest; from this comes "perfume" (الطيب) for a pleasant scent. Thus, "goodly offspring" (الذرية الطيبة) is the child who is righteous for his father, for example, whose attributes and actions suit the father's hopes and wishes. So Zechariah's (peace be upon him) saying: رب هب لي من لدنك ذرية طيبة (My Lord! Grant me from Your presence a goodly offspring), since the motive for it was what he witnessed of Mary's affair and her specific favor with Allah, and his heart being filled with her status, he could not help but ask Allah to grant him the like of her in significance and favor. Thus, his offspring being "goodly" meant having what Mary had of favor with Allah and personal nobility. For this reason, he was answered in the very thing he asked of Allah, and he was granted John (يحيى), who was the prophet most resembling Jesus (peace be upon them both), and the one who most combined the attributes of perfection and favor found in Jesus and his mother, the truthful Mary. From here, the Exalted named him John (يحيى), made him a confirmer of a Word from Allah, a master, chaste, and a prophet from among the righteous. These are the closest ways a human being can resemble Mary and her son Jesus (peace be upon them both), as We shall explain, Allah the Almighty willing.

His saying, the Exalted: فنادته الملائكة وهو قائم يصلي في المحراب أن الله يبشرك بيحيى (Then the angels called out to him, as he stood praying in the sanctuary: ‘Allah gives you the good news of John...’): The third-person and second-person pronouns refer to Zechariah. Good news (البشرى) — the act of informing of that which gladdens a person by its existence.

His saying: أن الله يبشرك بيحيى (that Allah gives you the good news of John), is evidence that his naming as John was from the side of Allah, the Glorified, as indicated by the parallels of these verses in Surah Maryam: يَا زَكَرِيَّا إِنَّا نُبَشِّرُكَ بِغُلَامٍ اسْمُهُ يَحْيَىٰ لَمْ نَجْعَل لَّهُ مِن قَبْلُ سَمِيًّا — "O Zechariah! Indeed We give you the good news of a son whose name is ‘John.’ We have not named anyone with this name before" (19:7).

His being named John and the naming being from Allah, the Glorified, at the very start of what Zechariah was given news of—before John's birth and creation—supports what We mentioned just now: that what Zechariah requested from his Lord was to be granted a child whose status would be like that of Mary. Mary and her son Jesus (peace be upon them both) were a single sign, as the Exalted said: وَجَعَلْنَاهَا وَابْنَهَا آيَةً لِّلْعَالَمِينَ — "and We made her and her son a sign for all the worlds" (21:91).

Thus, what was observed in John was what was observed in them both from Allah, the Glorified. In Jesus was observed the completion of what was observed in Mary. So what was observed in John was the complete resemblance and perfect alignment with Jesus (peace be upon them both) insofar as that was possible. Jesus has complete precedence in all of that because his existence was decreed before the answering of Zechariah's supplication regarding John. For this reason, Jesus preceded him in being among the "Arch-Prophets" (اولي العزم) — the possessors of resolve — the possessor of a law and a book and other things; yet they resembled each other and their affairs resembled each other insofar as possible.

If you wish for confirmation of what We have mentioned, reflect upon what Allah, the Exalted, mentioned of their story in Surah Maryam. He said regarding John: يَا زَكَرِيَّا إِنَّا نُبَشِّرُكَ بِغُلَامٍ اسْمُهُ يَحْيَىٰ لَمْ نَجْعَل لَّهُ مِن قَبْلُ سَمِيًّا — until He said — يَا يَحْيَىٰ خُذِ الْكِتَابَ بِقُوَّةٍ وَآتَيْنَاهُ الْحُكْمَ صَبِيًّا وَحَنَانًا مِّن لَّدُنَّا وَزَكَاةً وَكَانَ تَقِيًّا وَبَرًّا بِوَالِدَيْهِ وَلَمْ يَكُن جَبَّارًا عَصِيًّا وَسَلَامٌ عَلَيْهِ يَوْمَ وُلِدَ وَيَوْمَ يَمُوتُ وَيَوْمَ يُبْعَثُ حَيًّا — "‘O John! Hold on with strength to the Book.’ And We gave him judgment while still a child, and a compassion from Us and purity. He was God-conscious, and good to his parents, and was not self-willed or rebellious. Peace be on him the day he was born, and the day he dies, and the day he is raised alive" (19:7-15). And He said regarding Jesus (peace be upon him): فأرسلنا إليها روحنا — until He said — إِنَّمَا أَنَا رَسُولُ رَبِّكِ لِأَهَبَ لَكِ غُلَامًا زَكِيًّا — until He said — قَالَ رَبُّكِ هُوَ عَلَيَّ هَيِّنٌ وَلِنَجْعَلَهُ آيَةً لِّلنَّاسِ وَرَحْمَةً مِّنَّا — until He said — فَأَشَارَتْ إِلَيْهِ قَالُوا كَيْفَ نُكَلِّمُ مَن كَانَ فِي الْمَهْدِ صَبِيًّا قَالَ إِنِّي عَبْدُ اللَّهِ آتَانِيَ الْكِتَابَ وَجَعَلَنِي نَبِيًّا وَجَعَلَنِي مُبَارَكًا أَيْنَ مَا كُنتُ وَأَوْصَانِي بِالصَّلَاةِ وَالزَّكَاةِ مَا دُمْتُ حَيًّا وَبَرًّا بِوَالِدَتِي وَلَمْ يَجْعَلْنِي جَبَّارًا شَقِيًّا وَالسَّلَامُ عَلَيَّ يَوْمَ وُلِدتُّ وَيَوْمَ أَمُوتُ وَيَوْمَ أُبْعَثُ حَيًّا — "Then We sent to her Our Spirit... He said, ‘I am only a messenger of your Lord that I may give you a pure son.’ ... He said, ‘So shall it be. Your Lord says, ‘‘It is easy for Me.’’ And [We will create him] that We may make him a sign for mankind and a mercy from Us.’ ... Thereat she pointed to him. They said, ‘How can we speak to one who is yet a baby in the cradle?’ He said, ‘Indeed I am a servant of Allah! He has given me the Book and made me a prophet. He has made me blessed, wherever I may be, and He has enjoined me to [maintain] the prayer and to [pay] the zakat as long as I live, and to be good to my mother, and He has not made me self-willed and wretched. Peace be on me the day I was born, and the day I die, and the day I am raised alive’" (19:17-33). The verses of this Surah which we are currently in are close to these in terms of indicating the proximity of their affairs when compared.

In short, Allah, the Glorified, named him John (يحيى) and named the son of Mary Jesus (عيسى), which means "he lives" (يعيش) as has been said. He made him a confirmer of a Word from Him—which is Jesus—as the Exalted said: بِكَلِمَةٍ مِّنْهُ اسْمُهُ الْمَسِيحُ عِيسَى — "with a Word from Him whose name is the Messiah, Jesus" (3:45). He gave him judgment and taught him the Book while a child, as He did with Jesus. He counted him as a compassion from His presence, a purity, and good to his parents, not self-willed, as Jesus was likewise. He sent peace upon him in the three stations as He did for Jesus. He counted him a master (سيداً) as He made Jesus distinguished (وجيها) with Him. He made him chaste (حصوراً), a prophet, and from among the righteous like Jesus. All of this was a response to Zechariah's request and supplication when he asked for a goodly offspring and a pleasing guardian when his heart was filled with what he witnessed of Mary's affair and her wondrous status and favor with Allah, as previously explained.

In His saying: مصدقاً بكلمة من الله (confirming a Word from Allah), there is an indication that he was among those who called to Jesus. The "Word" (الكلمة) is Jesus the Messiah, as the Exalted mentioned at the end of these verses in the Spirit's good news to Mary.

The master (السيد) is one who takes charge of the affairs of the masses and their community in their life and livelihood, or in a virtue among the virtues praised by them; then its usage became dominant for the noble of a people because the aforementioned taking charge necessitates nobility through judgment, wealth, or another virtue.

The chaste (الحصور) is one who does not approach women. What is intended by it in the verse, by the context of the passage, is one who refrains from that by turning away from the desires of the soul out of asceticism (زهداً).

His saying, the Exalted: قال رب أني يكون لي غلام وقد بلغني الكبر وامرأتي عاقر (He said, ‘My Lord, how shall I have a son, when old age has already overtaken me and my wife is barren?’): This is an inquiry of wonder and a seeking of the reality of the situation, not an expression of improbability or deeming it too great, especially with the explicit good news of it and that Allah, the Glorified, would grant him the child he asked for—even though he mentioned these two attributes, which he made the source of wonder and inquiry, within his request as found in Surah Maryam: رَبِّ إِنِّي وَهَنَ الْعَظْمُ مِنِّي وَاشْتَعَلَ الرَّأْسُ شَيْبًا وَلَمْ أَكُن بِدُعَائِكَ رَبِّ شَقِيًّا وَإِنِّي خِفْتُ الْمَوَالِيَ مِن وَرَائِي وَكانَتِ امْرَأَتِي عاقِراً فَهَبْ لِي مِنْ لَدُنْكَ وَلِيًّا — "‘My Lord! Indeed my bones have become feeble, and my head has flared up with hoariness, and I have never been disappointed in supplicating You, my Lord! Indeed I fear my kinsmen after me, and my wife is barren. So grant me from Your presence an heir’" (19:4-5).

However, the station represents another meaning. It is as if he (peace be upon him), when his state was transformed by witnessing Mary's affair and remembering the cutting off of his lineage, did not realize it until he had asked his Lord what he asked, mentioning in his prayer that which had a large share in his being affected and saddened—namely, the reaching of old age and his wife being barren. Then, when his supplication was answered and he was given the good news of a son, it was as if he woke up and recovered from the state he was in, and began to wonder at that while he had reached old age and his wife was barren. Thus, that which used to stir the dust of despair and the signs of grief upon his face changed into a look of wonder mixed with joy.

Furthermore, mentioning the deficiencies of the matter after the good news of the fulfillment of the primary need, and inquiring into the manner of removing each one of them, is merely a request to understand the specifics of the bestowal and favor, taking pleasure in the overflowing blessing after blessing. This is similar to what occurred in the good news to Abraham of offspring: وَنَبِّئْهُمْ عَن ضَيْفِ إِبْرَاهِيمَ إِذْ دَخَلُوا عَلَيْهِ فَقَالُوا سَلَامًا قَالَ إِنَّا مِنكُمْ وَجِلُونَ قَالُوا لَا تَوْجَلْ إِنَّا نُبَشِّرُكَ بِغُلَامٍ عَلِيمٍ قَالَ أَبَشَّرْتُمُونِي عَلَىٰ أَن مَّسَّنِيَ الْكِبَرُ فَبِمَ تُبَشِّرُونَ قَالُوا بَشَّرْنَاكَ بِالْحَقِّ فَلَا تَكُن مِّنَ الْقَانِطِينَ قَالَ وَمَن يَقْنَطُ مِن رَّحْمَةِ رَبِّهِ إِلَّا الضَّالُّونَ — "And inform them about Abraham’s guests. When they entered his presence and said, ‘Peace!’ He said, ‘Indeed we are afraid of you.’ They said, ‘Do not be afraid. Indeed we give you the good news of a wise son.’ He said, ‘Do you give me good news though old age has come upon me? What [sort of] good news do you give me?’ They said, ‘We have given you good news in truth; so do not be among the despondent.’ He said, ‘And who despairs of his Lord’s mercy except the astray?’" (15:51-56). He mentioned in response to the angels' forbidding him from despair that his inquiry was not out of despair—how could it be, when he was not astray and despair is misguidance? Rather, when a master turns toward his servant with a turning that signals proximity, intimacy, and honor, it necessitates an expansion and joy from the servant that calls for his taking pleasure in every conversation and enjoyment in every matter.

In His saying: وقد بلغني الكبر (when old age has already overtaken me), there is an observance of etiquette that is not hidden, for it is an allusion (كناية) to the fact that he finds no desire for marriage within himself due to reaching old age and senility. Both old age and barrenness had combined in his wife, for that is the apparent meaning of His saying: وكانت امرأتي عاقراً (and my wife was barren), and He did not say: "and my wife is barren."

His saying, the Exalted: قال كذلك الله يفعل ما يشاء (He said, ‘So it is: Allah does whatever He wishes’): The subject of "He said" (قال), although it is Allah, the Glorified—whether it was without the mediation of angels through revelation (وحياً) or through the mediation of the angels who were calling him—the speech in any case is His speech, the Exalted. However, it is apparent that it is attributed to Him, the Exalted, through the mediation of the angel; so the speaker is the angel, and it is attributed to Him, the Exalted, because it is by His command. The evidence for this is His saying, the Exalted, in Surah Maryam regarding the story: قَالَ كَذَٰلِكَ قَالَ رَبُّكَ هُوَ عَلَيَّ هَيِّنٌ وَقَدْ خَلَقْتُكَ مِن قَبْلُ وَلَمْ تَكُ شَيْئًا — "He said, ‘So shall it be. Your Lord says, ‘‘It is easy for Me, and I created you before when you were nothing’’’’" (19:9).

From this it appears, firstly: that he heard the voice from where he was hearing it initially. Secondly: that His saying: كذلك (So it is), is the predicate of a suppressed subject, and the estimation is: "The matter is so," meaning that which you were given good news of regarding the gift is thus, inevitably coming to be. In it is an indication that it is part of the decisive decree (القضاء المحتوم) whose occurrence is beyond doubt, similar to what the Spirit mentioned in response to Mary as narrated by Allah, the Exalted: قَالَ كَذَٰلِكِ قَالَ رَبُّكِ هُوَ عَلَيَّ هَيِّنٌ — until He said — وَكَانَ أَمْرًا مَّقْضِيًّا — "He said, ‘So shall it be. Your Lord says, ‘‘It is easy for Me...’’’ ... and it is a matter [already] decided" (19:21). Thirdly: that His saying: الله يفعل ما يشاء (Allah does whatever He wishes), is a detached statement in the position of providing the reason (التعليل) for the content of His saying: "So it is," etc.

His saying, the Exalted: قال رب اجعل لي آية قال آيتك أن لا تكلم الناس ثلاثة أيام إلا رمزاً (He said, ‘My Lord, appoint a sign for me.’ He said, ‘Your sign is that you may not speak to the people for three days except by gestures...’): It is said in al-Majma‘: Gesture (الرمز) — the act of signaling with the lips; it may also be used for signaling with the eyebrow, the eye, and the hand, but the first is more dominant. End quote. Evening (العشي) — the latter part of the day; it is as if it is taken from "dimness" (العشوة), which is a darkness occurring in the eye preventing sight, so they took that as a description for the time because of its moving toward darkness. Morning (الإبكار) — the beginning of the day and its early part; the root of its meaning is haste.

The occurrence of this sign in the birth of John is one of the aspects of correspondence between him and Jesus, for it corresponds to Jesus's saying to Mary after his birth: فَإِمَّا تَرَيِنَّ مِنَ الْبَشَرِ أَحَدًا فَقُولِي إِنِّي نَذَرْتُ لِلرَّحْمَٰنِ صَوْمًا فَلَنْ أُكَلِّمَ الْيَوْمَ إِنسِيًّا — "And if you see any human, say, ‘‘Indeed I have vowed a fast to the All-beneficent, so I will not speak to any human today’’’’" (19:26).

His (peace be upon him) request from his Lord to appoint for him a sign (الآية) — and the sign is the mark indicating a thing — was it to use it as evidence that the good news was indeed from his Lord—in other words, that it was a divine angelic address, not a satanic one? Or was it because he wanted to use it as evidence for his wife's pregnancy and to know the time of conception? There is a disagreement among the commentators.

The second view is not devoid of being far-fetched from the context of the verses and the flow of the story. However, what caused the group to shy away from the first of the two views—namely, that the request for a sign was to distinguish that the address was divine—is what they mentioned: that the prophets, due to their infallibility (العصمة), must know the difference between the speech of the angel and the whispering of Satan, and it is not permissible for Satan to play with them such that the path of conveying understanding becomes confused for them.

This is a true statement, but it must be known that their recognition is only through Allah's making it known to them, not from themselves or the independence of their essences. If that is so, why is it not permissible for Zechariah to seek from his Lord that He appoint for him a sign by which he would know that? What is the objection in that? Yes, if his supplication had not been answered and Allah had not appointed a sign for him, the objection would be in its place.

Furthermore, the specific nature of the sign itself—which is the inability to speak for three days—supports, rather indicates, this. For although it is possible for Satan to touch the prophets in their bodies or by causing destruction or corruption in what they hope for of the results of their actions in the spread of religion and the reception of people or the weakening of the enemies of religion—as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: وَاذْكُرْ عَبْدَنَا أَيُّوبَ إِذْ نَادَىٰ رَبَّهُ أَنِّي مَسَّنِيَ الشَّيْطَانُ بِنُصْبٍ وَعَذَابٍ — "And remember Our servant Job, when he called out to his Lord, ‘The Devil has visited me with hardship and torment’" (38:41), and His saying, the Exalted: وَمَا أَرْسَلْنَا مِن قَبْلِكَ مِن رَّسُولٍ وَلَا نَبِيٍّ إِلَّا إِذَا تَمَنَّىٰ أَلْقَى الشَّيْطَانُ فِي أُمْنِيَّتِهِ فَيَنسَخُ اللَّهُ مَا يُلْقِي الشَّيْطَانُ ثُمَّ يُحْكِمُ اللَّهُ آيَاتِهِ — "We did not send any apostle or prophet before you but that when he had a desire, Satan cast [doubts] into his desire. But Allah abrogates what Satan casts, and then Allah confirms His signs" (22:52), and His saying, the Exalted: فَإِنِّي نَسِيتُ الْحُوتَ وَمَا أَنسَانِيهُ إِلَّا الشَّيْطَانُ — "I forgot the fish, and none but Satan made me forget to mention it" (18:63).

However, these and their like of Satan's touch and interference only result in harming the prophet. As for his touching the prophets in their souls, the prophets are infallible from that, and the proof of their infallibility (peace be upon them) has already passed in previous discussions.

That which Allah made a sign for Zechariah—as indicated by His saying: آيتك أن لا تكلم الناس ثلثة أيام إلا رمزاً واذكر ربك كثيراً وسبح بالعشي والإبكار (Your sign is that you may not speak to the people for three days except by gestures, and remember your Lord much, and glorify Him morning and evening)—is that he was unable for three days to speak to anyone, his tongue being bound except for the remembrance of Allah and His glorification. This is a sign occurring upon the prophet's own self and tongue, and a specific disposal (تصرف) over him that Satan is not capable of due to the station of infallibility; thus it can only be divine. This sign, as you see, is consistent with the first view rather than the second.

If you say: If the matter were so, what is the meaning of His saying: قال رب أنى يكون لي غلام وقد بلغني الكبر وامرأتي عاقر قال كذلك يفعل الله ما يشاء (He said, ‘My Lord, how shall I have a son, when old age has already overtaken me and my wife is barren?’ He said, ‘So it is: Allah does whatever He wishes’)? For its apparent meaning is that he addressed his Lord and asked what he asked, then was answered with what he was answered. What is the meaning of this address if he were in doubt about the call? And if he were not in doubt then, what is the meaning of the request for distinction?

I say: The levels of reliance and belief are different. It is possible that his soul had found rest in the call being divine from the side of Allah, then he asked his Lord about the manner of the birth at which his noble soul was wondering, as previously mentioned. Then he was answered by another angelic call in which his soul found rest, then he asked his Lord for a sign that would necessitate certainty that it was divine, thereby increasing in trust and tranquility.

This is supported by His saying, the Exalted: فنادته الملائكة (Then the angels called out to him), for calling (النداء) is only from a distance. For this reason, the use of "calling" is frequent in the case of speaking loudly, as it is among us one of the concomitants of distance. It is not a necessity according to the root meaning of the word, as witnessed by His saying, the Exalted, in what He narrated of Zechariah's prayer: إِذْ نَادَىٰ رَبَّهُ نِدَاءً خَفِيًّا — "when he called out to his Lord with a secret cry" (19:3). "Calling" was applied to it by way of considering Zechariah's self-abasement and humility before Allah's might, loftiness, and transcendence; then He described the call as "secret." Thus, the discourse is not devoid of an intimation that Zechariah did not see the angel himself, but rather heard a voice calling out to him.

Some commentators have mentioned: that the meaning of Allah making the inability to speak a sign was His forbidding him from speaking to people for three days, and devoting himself therein to the remembrance of Allah and His glorification, without his tongue being bound. He said: "The correct view is that Zechariah loved, by the requirement of human nature, that the time in which he would receive that divine grant be specified for him so that his heart might find rest and he might give the good news to his family. So he asked about the manner, and when he was answered with what he was answered, he asked his Lord to specify for him an act of worship by which he might hasten his gratitude, and the completion of which would be a sign and mark of the attainment of the objective. So He commanded him not to speak to people for three days, but rather to devote himself to remembrance and glorification evening and morning for a period of three days; if he needed to address people, he would signal to them with gestures. According to this, his giving the good news to his family would be after the passing of the three nights." End quote.

You are well aware that there is no trace or sign in the verse for what he mentioned (of his asking for an act of worship as gratitude for the grant, its ending in the attainment of the objective, the ending being the sign, His saying "that you may not speak" being intended as a legislative prohibition, and likewise his intention to give the good news to his family).

(A Discourse on Angelic and Satanic Thoughts and the Speech Associated with Them)

It has been mentioned repeatedly that words are coined for their meanings in terms of their inclusion of the purposes intended from them. Speech (القول) or discourse (الكلام), for example, is only so named for the sound because of its conveying an intended meaning upon which it is correct to remain silent. Thus, that by which this is conveyed is discourse and speech, whether its conveyor is a single sound, multiple composed sounds, or something other than sound like signaling and gestures. People do not hesitate to name a sound that conveys a complete benefit "discourse," even if it does not emerge from the opening of a mouth; likewise, they name signaling "speech" and "discourse," even if it does not include a sound.

The Quran also names the meanings cast into the hearts by Satan as "discourse" from him and "speech" from him. He, the Exalted, said, narrating from Satan: وَلَآمُرَنَّهُمْ فَلَيُبَتِّكُنَّ آذَانَ الْأَنْعَامِ — "and I will surely command them and they will surely cut the ears of the cattle" (4:119). And He said: كَمَثَلِ الشَّيْطَانِ إِذْ قَالَ لِلْإِنسَانِ اكْفُرْ — "Like the case of Satan when he says to man, ‘Disbelieve!’" (59:16). And He said: يُوَسْوِسُ فِي صُدُورِ النَّاسِ — "who whispers into the breasts of mankind" (114:5). And He said: يُوحِي بَعْضُهُمْ إِلَىٰ بَعْضٍ زُخْرُفَ الْقَوْلِ — "some of them inspiring others with tawdry speech" (6:112). And He also said, narrating from Iblis: إِنَّ اللَّهَ وَعدَكُمْ وَعْدَ الْحَقِّ وَوَعَدتُّكُمْ — "Indeed Allah made you a true promise, and I [too] made you a promise" (14:22). And He said: الشَّيْطَانُ يَعِدُكُمُ الْفَقْرَ وَيَأْمُرُكُم بِالْفَحْشَاءِ وَاللَّهُ يَعِدُكُم مَّغْفِرَةً مِّنْهُ وَفَضْلًا وَاللَّهُ وَاسِعٌ عَلِيمٌ يُؤْتِي الْحِكْمَةَ مَن يَشَاءُ وَمَن يُؤْتَ الْحِكْمَةَ فَقَدْ أُوتِيَ خَيْرًا كَثِيرًا — "Satan frightens you of poverty and prompts you to indecency. But Allah promises you His forgiveness and bounty, and Allah is all-bounteous, all-knowing. He gives wisdom to whomever He wishes, and he who is given wisdom, is certainly given an abundant good" (2:268-269). It is clear that these are the thoughts (الخواطر) occurring to the hearts, attributed to Satan, and named "command," "speech," "whispering," "revelation," and "promise"; all of them are speech and discourse, yet they did not emerge from the opening of a mouth or the movement of a tongue.

From here it is known: that what the last verse contains of His promise, the Exalted, of forgiveness and bounty in opposition to Satan's promise is the angelic speech (الكلام الملكي) in opposition to the whispering from Satan. He, the Exalted, has named it wisdom (الحكمة). Similar to it is His saying, the Exalted: وَيَجْعَل لَّكُمْ نُورًا تَمْشُونَ بِهِ — "and He will give you a light to walk by" (57:28), and His saying: هُوَ الَّذِي أَنزَلَ السَّكِينَةَ فِي قُلُوبِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ لِيَزْدَادُوا إِيمَانًا مَّعَ إِيمَانِهِمْ وَلِلَّهِ جُنُودُ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ — "It is He who sent down composure into the hearts of the faithful that they might enhance in their faith. To Allah belong the hosts of the heavens and the earth" (44:4). The explanation of this has already passed in the discourse on composure (السكينة) — the divine tranquility that brings peace to the heart — under His saying, the Exalted: فِيهِ سَكِينَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ — "in which there is composure from your Lord" (2:248). Likewise His saying: فَمَن يُرِدِ اللَّهُ أَن يَهْدِيَهُ يَشْرَحْ صَدْرَهُ لِلْإِسْلَامِ وَمَن يُرِدْ أَن يُضِلَّهُ يَجْعَلْ صَدْرَهُ ضَيِّقًا حَرَجًا كَأَنَّمَا يَصَّعَّدُ فِي السَّمَاءِ كَذَٰلِكَ يَجْعَلُ اللَّهُ الرِّجْسَ عَلَى الَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ — "Whomever Allah desires to guide, He opens his breast to Islam, and whomever He desires to lead astray, He makes his breast narrow and straitened as if he were climbing into the sky. Thus does Allah lay abomination on those who do not have faith" (6:125). He named the whispering "defilement" (رجزاً), saying: رِجْزَ الشَّيْطَانِ — "the defilement of Satan" (8:11). From all of this, it appears that the satans and the angels speak to the human being by casting meanings into their heart.

Here is another category of speech that is exclusive to Him, the Exalted, as He mentioned in His saying: وَمَا كَانَ لِبَشَرٍ أَن يُكَلِّمَهُ اللَّهُ إِلَّا وَحْيًا أَوْ مِن وَرَاءِ حِجَابٍ — "It is not [given] to any human that Allah should speak to him except by revelation or from behind a veil" (42:51). He named it "speaking" and divided it into revelation (الوحي) — that in which there is no veil between Him and the servant spoken to — and speaking from behind a veil. These are categories of speech for Allah, the Glorified, and for the angels and satans.

As for the speech of Allah, the Glorified, named revelation (الوحي), it is distinguished and defined by its essence, for Allah, the Glorified, placed a contrast between it and speaking from behind a veil. Thus, it is a speaking where there is no veil between the human being and their Lord, and it is impossible for confusion to occur there; this is evident. As for other than it, it requires a confirmation that ultimately traces back to revelation.

As for angelic and satanic speech, the aforementioned verses are sufficient to distinguish between them. For the angelic thought (الخاطر الملكي) is accompanied by the opening of the breast, calls to forgiveness and bounty, and ultimately leads to what accords with the religion of Allah made clear in His Book and the Sunnah of His Prophet. The satanic thought (الخاطر الشيطاني) is concomitant with the narrowing of the breast and the stinginess of the soul, calls to following passion, promises poverty, and commands indecency; and ultimately leads to what does not accord with the Book and the Sunnah, and contradicts the primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being.

Furthermore, the prophets and those who follow them may find it possible to witness the angel and the satan and recognize them, as Allah, the Exalted, narrated regarding Adam, Abraham, and Lot; this obviates the need for using a distinguisher. As for when there is no witnessing, then it is necessary to use it like other believers, and it ultimately traces back to the distinction of revelation; this is evident.

(Narrative Discussion)

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: إذ قالت امرأة عمران (When the wife of ‘Imran said...), narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), who said: "Allah revealed to ‘Imran: 'I am granting you a well-proportioned, blessed male who will heal the blind and the leper and raise the dead by the permission of Allah, and I shall make him a messenger to the Children of Israel.' So ‘Imran told his wife, Hannah, of that—and she was the mother of Mary. When she became pregnant, she thought in her soul it was a boy. When she delivered her, she said: 'My Lord, I have delivered a female, and the male is not like the female; a girl cannot be a messenger.' Allah says: 'And Allah knew better what she had delivered.' When Allah granted Mary Jesus, he was the one of whom ‘Imran had been given the good news and promised. So if we say something regarding a man among us and it occurs in his child or his grandchild, do not deny that."

I say: A similar narration is found in al-Kafi from him (peace be upon him), and in the Tafsir of al-‘Ayyashi from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him).

In the Tafsir of al-‘Ayyashi, regarding the verse, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "The 'devoted' (المحرر) would be in the temple and not leave it. When she delivered her, she said: 'My Lord, I have delivered a female, and the male is not like the female.' For the female menstruates and thus must leave the mosque, while the 'devoted' does not leave the mosque."

In the same source, from one of the two [Imams]: "She vowed what was in her womb to the temple to serve the worshippers, and the male is not like the female in service." He said: "So she grew up and used to serve them and hand things to them until she reached puberty; then Zechariah commanded that a veil be placed for her apart from the worshippers."

I say: The narrations, as you see, accord with what We presented in the previous commentary, except that their apparent meaning is that His saying: وليس الذكر كالانثى (and the male is not like the female), is the speech of the wife of ‘Imran, not His, the Exalted. This would leave the reason for the precedence of "the male" over "the female" in the sentence unexplained, even though the requirements of Arabic grammar are contrary to it. Likewise, the reason for her naming her Mary would remain, and it has already been mentioned that it carries the meaning of "liberation" (التحرير), unless a distinction is made between liberation and making her a servant; so let it be considered.

In the first narration, there is an indication that ‘Imran was a prophet to whom revelation was sent. This is supported by what is in al-Bihar from Abu Basir, who said: "I asked Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon both of them) about ‘Imran: was he a prophet? He said: 'Yes, he was a prophet sent to his people...'" the hadith.

The narration also indicates that the name of ‘Imran's wife was Hannah, which is the famous view. In some narrations, it is "Marthar," and the investigation of that does not concern us.

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, in the continuation of the previous narration: "When Mary reached puberty, she stayed in the sanctuary and drew a curtain over herself, and no one would see her. Zechariah would enter the sanctuary to her and find summer fruit with her in winter and winter fruit in summer. He would say: 'From where does this come to you?' and she would say: 'It is from Allah. Indeed Allah provides for whomever He wishes without account.'"

In the Tafsir of al-‘Ayyashi, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), who said: "When Zechariah supplicated his Lord to grant him a child and the angels called out to him with what they called out, he loved to know that that voice was from Allah. So it was revealed to him that the sign of that was that he should hold his tongue from speaking for three days. When he held his tongue and did not speak, he knew that none but Allah was capable of that. And that is the saying of Allah: 'My Lord, appoint a sign for me.'"

I say: A similar narration was recorded by al-Qummi in his Tafsir, and you have already seen that the context of the verses does not preclude that.

Some commentators have strongly denounced what these narrations contain, such as the revelation to ‘Imran, the presence of fruit in Mary's sanctuary out of its season, and Zechariah's request for a sign being for distinction. He said: "These are matters for which there is no way to prove; neither did He, the Glorified, mention them, nor did His Messenger say them, nor are they among what is known by opinion, nor has any reliable history established them. There are only Israelite and non-Israelite narrations, and there is no reason to struggle in obtaining the meaning of the Quran and making it carry such views that are far from understanding."

This is a statement from him without proof. Although the narrations are solitary (آحاد) and not free from weakness in their chains, the researcher is not obliged to accept them or argue with what is in them; however, reflection upon the verses brings the mind close to them. That which is narrated from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them) does not contain anything impermissible according to reason.

Yes, in some of what is narrated from the early commentators are matters that are not reasonable, such as what is narrated from Qatadah and ‘Ikrimah: that Satan came to Zechariah and made him doubt whether the good news was from Allah, saying: "If it were from Allah, He would have whispered to you in His call just as you whispered to Him in your call," and so on. These are meanings that there is no justification for accepting, just as it occurs in the Gospel of Luke: that Gabriel said to Zechariah: "And behold, you will be silent and unable to speak until the day that these things take place, because you did not believe my words, which will be fulfilled in their time" (Gospel of Luke 1:20).

(Another Narrative Discussion)

In al-Kafi, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "There is no heart except that it has two ears: upon one of them is a guiding angel, and upon the other is a tempting satan. This one commands him, and that one restrains him. The satan commands him to acts of disobedience, and the angel restrains him from them. And that is the saying of Allah, the Almighty and Majestic: 'He utters no word but there is with him a vigilant observer' (50:18), 'on the right and on the left, seated' (50:17)."

I say: The narrations with this meaning are many; a portion of them will follow. The Imam's (peace be upon him) application of the verse to the angel and the satan in this narration does not contradict his application of it to the two angels who record good and evil deeds in another narration. For the verse does not indicate more than the existence of a vigilant observer (رقيب عتيد) with the human being who observes them in all that they speak, and that they are multiple, on the right and left of the human being. As for whether they are purely from the angels or an angel and a satan, the verse is not explicit in that and is capable of being applied to both possibilities.

In the same source, from Zurarah, who said: "I asked Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) about the Messenger (الرسول), the Prophet (النبي), and the Muhaddath (المحدث) — one who is spoken to by angels. He said: 'The Messenger is he who beholds the angel who comes to him with the message from his Lord, saying: He commands you such and such. The Messenger is a prophet along with the message. The Prophet does not behold the angel; the matter—the news—descends upon his heart, so he becomes as if in a faint and sees in his sleep.' I said: 'What is his knowledge that what is in his sleep is the truth?' He said: 'Allah makes it clear until he knows that it is the truth, and he does not behold the angel...'" the hadith.

I say: His saying: "The Messenger is a prophet," is an indication of the possibility of the two descriptions combining. The discussion on the meaning of messengership (الرسالة) and prophethood (النبوة) has already passed in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: كَانَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً وَاحِدَةً فَبَعَثَ اللَّهُ — "Mankind were a single community; then Allah sent..." (2:213).

His saying: "so he becomes as if in a faint," is an explanation of the meaning of his seeing in sleep—that its meaning is absence from the senses, not the well-known sleep. His saying: "Allah makes it clear," etc., is an indication of the distinction between angelic and satanic casting through what Allah has made clear of the truth.

In al-Basa’ir, from Burayd, from Imam al-Baqir and Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon both of them), in a hadith where Burayd said: "So what is the Messenger, the Prophet, and the Muhaddath?" He said: "The Messenger is he to whom the angel appears and speaks to him. The Prophet sees in sleep, and sometimes prophethood and messengership combine for one person. The Muhaddath is he who hears the voice but does not see the form." He said: "I said: 'May Allah set you right, how does he know that what he saw in sleep is the truth and that it is from the angel?' He said: 'He is given success for that until he recognizes it. Allah has sealed the Books with your Book and the prophets with your Prophet...'" the hadith.

I say: This is in the same vein as the previous hadith. The Imam's (peace be upon him) explanation is sufficient for the Muhaddath to distinguish what he hears of the voice of the caller. In his saying: "Allah has sealed," etc., there is an indication of that. The discussion regarding the Muhaddath will follow under the subsequent verses.

Ayahs

وَإِذْ قَالَتِ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ يَـٰمَرْيَمُ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ ٱصْطَفَىٰكِ وَطَهَّرَكِ وَٱصْطَفَىٰكِ عَلَىٰ نِسَآءِ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٤٢)
And when the angels said, ‘O Mary, Allah has chosen you and purified you, and He has chosen you above the world’s women. (42)
يَـٰمَرْيَمُ ٱقْنُتِى لِرَبِّكِ وَٱسْجُدِى وَٱرْكَعِى مَعَ ٱلرَّٰكِعِينَ (٤٣)
O Mary, be obedient to your Lord and prostrate and bow down with those who bow [in worship].’ (43)
ذَٰلِكَ مِنْ أَنۢبَآءِ ٱلْغَيْبِ نُوحِيهِ إِلَيْكَ ۚ وَمَا كُنتَ لَدَيْهِمْ إِذْ يُلْقُونَ أَقْلَـٰمَهُمْ أَيُّهُمْ يَكْفُلُ مَرْيَمَ وَمَا كُنتَ لَدَيْهِمْ إِذْ يَخْتَصِمُونَ (٤٤)
These accounts are from the Unseen, which We reveal to you, and you were not with them when they were casting lots [to see] which of them would take charge of Mary’s care, nor were you with them when they were contending. (44)
إِذْ قَالَتِ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ يَـٰمَرْيَمُ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُبَشِّرُكِ بِكَلِمَةٍ مِّنْهُ ٱسْمُهُ ٱلْمَسِيحُ عِيسَى ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ وَجِيهًا فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ وَمِنَ ٱلْمُقَرَّبِينَ (٤٥)
When the angels said, ‘O Mary, Allah gives you the good news of a Word from Him whose name is Messiah, Jesus son of Mary, distinguished in the world and the Hereafter and one of those brought near [to Allah]. (45)
وَيُكَلِّمُ ٱلنَّاسَ فِى ٱلْمَهْدِ وَكَهْلًا وَمِنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ (٤٦)
He will speak to the people in the cradle and in adulthood, and will be one of the righteous.’ (46)
قَالَتْ رَبِّ أَنَّىٰ يَكُونُ لِى وَلَدٌ وَلَمْ يَمْسَسْنِى بَشَرٌ ۖ قَالَ كَذَٰلِكِ ٱللَّهُ يَخْلُقُ مَا يَشَآءُ ۚ إِذَا قَضَىٰٓ أَمْرًا فَإِنَّمَا يَقُولُ لَهُۥ كُن فَيَكُونُ (٤٧)
She said, ‘My Lord, how shall I have a child seeing that no human has ever touched me?’ He said, ‘So it is that Allah creates whatever He wishes. When He decides on a matter He just says to it ‘‘Be!’’ and it is. (47)
وَيُعَلِّمُهُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَٱلْحِكْمَةَ وَٱلتَّوْرَىٰةَ وَٱلْإِنجِيلَ (٤٨)
He will teach him the Book and wisdom, the Torah and the Evangel, (48)
وَرَسُولًا إِلَىٰ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ أَنِّى قَدْ جِئْتُكُم بِـَٔايَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ ۖ أَنِّىٓ أَخْلُقُ لَكُم مِّنَ ٱلطِّينِ كَهَيْـَٔةِ ٱلطَّيْرِ فَأَنفُخُ فِيهِ فَيَكُونُ طَيْرًۢا بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَأُبْرِئُ ٱلْأَكْمَهَ وَٱلْأَبْرَصَ وَأُحْىِ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَأُنَبِّئُكُم بِمَا تَأْكُلُونَ وَمَا تَدَّخِرُونَ فِى بُيُوتِكُمْ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً لَّكُمْ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ (٤٩)
and [he will be] an apostle to the Children of Israel, [and he will declare,] “I have certainly brought you a sign from your Lord: I will create for you the form of a bird out of clay, then I will breathe into it, and it will become a bird by Allah’s leave. I heal the blind and the leper and I revive the dead by Allah’s leave. I will tell you what you have eaten and what you have stored in your houses. There is indeed a sign in that for you, should you be faithful. (49)
وَمُصَدِّقًا لِّمَا بَيْنَ يَدَىَّ مِنَ ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةِ وَلِأُحِلَّ لَكُم بَعْضَ ٱلَّذِى حُرِّمَ عَلَيْكُمْ ۚ وَجِئْتُكُم بِـَٔايَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُونِ (٥٠)
And [I come] to confirm [the truth of] that which is before me of the Torah, and to make lawful for you some of the things that were forbidden you. I have brought you a sign from your Lord; so be wary of Allah and obey me. (50)
إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ رَبِّى وَرَبُّكُمْ فَٱعْبُدُوهُ ۗ هَـٰذَا صِرَٰطٌ مُّسْتَقِيمٌ (٥١)
Indeed Allah is my Lord and your Lord; so worship Him. This is a straight path.” ’ (51)
۞ فَلَمَّآ أَحَسَّ عِيسَىٰ مِنْهُمُ ٱلْكُفْرَ قَالَ مَنْ أَنصَارِىٓ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ ۖ قَالَ ٱلْحَوَارِيُّونَ نَحْنُ أَنصَارُ ٱللَّهِ ءَامَنَّا بِٱللَّهِ وَٱشْهَدْ بِأَنَّا مُسْلِمُونَ (٥٢)
When Jesus sensed their faithlessness, he said, ‘Who will be my helpers [on the path] toward Allah?’ The Disciples said, ‘We will be helpers of Allah. We have faith in Allah, and you be witness that we have submitted [to Him]. (52)
رَبَّنَآ ءَامَنَّا بِمَآ أَنزَلْتَ وَٱتَّبَعْنَا ٱلرَّسُولَ فَٱكْتُبْنَا مَعَ ٱلشَّـٰهِدِينَ (٥٣)
Our Lord, we believe in what You have sent down, and we follow the apostle, so write us among the witnesses.’ (53)
وَمَكَرُوا۟ وَمَكَرَ ٱللَّهُ ۖ وَٱللَّهُ خَيْرُ ٱلْمَـٰكِرِينَ (٥٤)
Then they plotted [against Jesus], and Allah also devised, and Allah is the best of devisers. (54)
إِذْ قَالَ ٱللَّهُ يَـٰعِيسَىٰٓ إِنِّى مُتَوَفِّيكَ وَرَافِعُكَ إِلَىَّ وَمُطَهِّرُكَ مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَجَاعِلُ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّبَعُوكَ فَوْقَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۖ ثُمَّ إِلَىَّ مَرْجِعُكُمْ فَأَحْكُمُ بَيْنَكُمْ فِيمَا كُنتُمْ فِيهِ تَخْتَلِفُونَ (٥٥)
When Allah said, ‘O Jesus, I shall take you[r soul], and I shall raise you up toward Myself, and I shall clear you of [the calumnies of] the faithless, and I shall set those who follow you above the faithless until the Day of Resurrection. Then to Me will be your return, whereat I will judge between you concerning that about which you used to differ. (55)
فَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ فَأُعَذِّبُهُمْ عَذَابًا شَدِيدًا فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ وَمَا لَهُم مِّن نَّـٰصِرِينَ (٥٦)
As for the faithless, I will punish them with a severe punishment in the world and the Hereafter; and they will have no helpers.’ (56)
وَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ فَيُوَفِّيهِمْ أُجُورَهُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٥٧)
But as for those who have faith and do righteous deeds, He will pay them in full their rewards, and Allah does not like the wrongdoers. (57)
ذَٰلِكَ نَتْلُوهُ عَلَيْكَ مِنَ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ وَٱلذِّكْرِ ٱلْحَكِيمِ (٥٨)
These that We recite to you are from the signs and the Wise Reminder. (58)
إِنَّ مَثَلَ عِيسَىٰ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ كَمَثَلِ ءَادَمَ ۖ خَلَقَهُۥ مِن تُرَابٍ ثُمَّ قَالَ لَهُۥ كُن فَيَكُونُ (٥٩)
Indeed the case of Jesus with Allah is like the case of Adam: He created him from dust, then said to him, ‘Be,’ and he was. (59)
ٱلْحَقُّ مِن رَّبِّكَ فَلَا تَكُن مِّنَ ٱلْمُمْتَرِينَ (٦٠)
This is the truth from your Lord, so do not be among the skeptics. (60)

(Commentary)

His saying, the Exalted: وإذ قالت الملائكة يا مريم إن الله اصطفاك وطهرك (And when the angels said, ‘O Mary, Allah has chosen you and purified you’); this sentence is coordinated with His saying: إذ قالت امرأة عمران (When the wife of ‘Imran said), thus serving as an explanation, similar to it, of the "choosing of the family of ‘Imran" contained in His saying, the Exalted: إن الله اصطفى (Indeed Allah chose...); the verse.

The verse contains evidence that Mary was a muhaddathah (محدثة) — one who is spoken to by angels while hearing their speech — just as it is also indicated by His saying in Surah Maryam: فأرسلنا إليها روحنا فتمثل لها بشراً سوياً (Then We sent to her Our Spirit, and he embodied himself for her as a flawless human) until the end of the verses; the discussion regarding the muhaddath (المحدث) will follow.

It has already been mentioned regarding His saying, the Exalted: فتقبلها ربها بقبول حسن (So her Lord accepted her with a gracious acceptance); the verse: that this is an exposition of the answering of the prayer of Mary’s mother: وإني سميتها مريم (and I have named her Mary) and: وإني أعيدها بك وذريتها من الشيطان الرجيم (and I confide her and her offspring to Your protection from the outcast Satan); the verse. Furthermore, the angels' saying to Mary: إن الله اصطفاك وطهرك (Indeed Allah has chosen you and purified you) is an informing of her regarding the dignity (الكرامة) and station (المنزلة) she holds with Allah, the Glorified; so refer back to that section.

Thus, her "choosing" (اصطفاؤها) was her being accepted for the worship (عبادة) of Allah, and her "purification" (تطهيرها) was her protection through the impeccability (عصمة) of Allah; she is therefore chosen and impeccable (معصومة). It has been suggested that her purification refers to her being made a batul (بتولاً) — one who does not menstruate — so that she would not be compelled to leave the temple, and there is no harm in this view, though what we have mentioned is more in accord with the context of the verses.

His saying, the Exalted: واصطفاك على نساء العالمين (and chosen you above the women of all the worlds). It was previously mentioned regarding His saying, the Exalted: إن الله اصطفى (Indeed Allah chose) up to His saying: على العالمين (above all the worlds) that "choosing" (الاصطفاء) when transitive with the preposition "above" (على) implies the meaning of precedence (التقدم), and that it is distinct from absolute choosing (الاصطفاء المطلق) which implies the meaning of submission (التسليم). Accordingly, her being chosen above the women of all the worlds means her being given precedence over them.

Is this precedence from every aspect or from some? The apparent meaning of His saying, the Exalted, in the verse following this: إذ قالت الملائكة يا مريم إن الله يبشرك (When the angels said, ‘O Mary, Allah gives you the good news...’); the verse, and His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّتِي أَحْصَنَتْ فَرْجَهَا فَنَفَخْنَا فِيهَا مِن رُّوحِنَا وَجَعَلْنَاهَا وَابْنَهَا آيَةً لِّلْعَالَمِينَ — And her who guarded her chastity, so We breathed into her of Our spirit, and made her and her son a sign for all the worlds (Al-Anbiya: 91), and His saying, the Exalted: وَمَرْيَمَ ابْنَتَ عِمْرَانَ الَّتِي أَحْصَنَتْ فَرْجَهَا فَنَفَخْنَا فِيهِ مِن رُّوحِنَا وَصَدَّقَتْ بِكَلِمَاتِ رَبِّهَا وَكُتُبِهِ وَكَانَتْ مِنَ الْقَانِتِينَ — And Mary, daughter of ‘Imran, who guarded her chastity, so We breathed into it of Our spirit, and she confirmed the words of her Lord and His Books, and she was one of the obedient (Al-Tahrim: 12); since these verses do not mention anything unique to her among women except her wondrous state in the birth of the Messiah (عليه السلام), it indicates that this is the aspect of her being chosen and given precedence over the women of all the worlds.

As for what the verses contain regarding her story—such as purification, confirming the words and Books of Allah, obedience (القنوت), and her being a muhaddathah—these are matters not exclusive to her but found in others. As for the claim that she was chosen over the women of the worlds of her own time, the absolute nature (إطلاق) of the verse refutes it.

His saying, the Exalted: يا مريم اقنتي لربك واسجدي واركعي مع الراكعين (O Mary, be obedient to your Lord and prostrate and bow down with those who bow [in prayer]). Obedience (القنوت) is the persistence in worship out of humility (خضوع), as has been said, and prostration (السجدة) is well-known. Bowing (الركوع) is the act of inclining or absolute self-abasement (تذلل).

Since a call (النداء) necessitates drawing the attention of the one called (المنادى) and directing their understanding toward the caller (المنادي), the repetition of the call in this context is as if she is being told: "We have for you news after news, so listen to both and harken to them." The first is the station with which Allah has honored you, which is what you have with Allah. The second is the duty of servitude (وظيفة العبودية) that is required of you in return, which is what Allah, the Glorified, has from you. Thus, this would be a fulfillment of servitude and a gratitude for the station. Consequently, the meaning of the speech results in His saying: يا مريم اقنتي (O Mary, be obedient...) being a derivation (تفريع) from His saying: يا مريم إن الله اصطفاك (O Mary, indeed Allah has chosen you...), meaning: since it is so, then be obedient, prostrate, and bow with those who bow. It is not improbable that each of the three traits mentioned in this verse is a derivation from one of the three traits mentioned in the previous verse, though this is not without some subtlety; so let it be reflected upon.

His saying, the Exalted: ذلك من أنباء الغيب نوحيه إليك (These are accounts of the Unseen, which We reveal to you). Counting this among the accounts of the Unseen (أنباء الغيب) — the reports of events beyond sensory perception — is similar to how the story of Joseph (عليه السلام) was counted among the accounts of the Unseen revealed to the Messenger of Allah; the Exalted said: ذَٰلِكَ مِنْ أَنبَاءِ الْغَيْبِ نُوحِيهِ إِلَيْكَ وَمَا كُنتَ لَدَيْهِمْ إِذْ أَجْمَعُوا أَمْرَهُمْ وَهُمْ يَمْكُرُونَ — These are accounts of the Unseen which We reveal to you, and you were not with them when they conspired their plan and were plotting (Yusuf: 102).
