As for what exists of this among the People of the Book, it is of no consequence due to its lack of safety from the distortions of the distorters, just as many of the details narrated in the stories of Zechariah are not found in the books of the Two Testaments as Allah described them in the Quran.

This view is supported by His saying, the Exalted, at the end of the verse: وما كنت لديهم إذ يلقون (and you were not with them when they cast...).

Furthermore, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and his people were unlettered (أميين), not knowing these stories, nor had they read them in books, as the Exalted mentioned after narrating the story of Noah: تِلْكَ مِنْ أَنبَاءِ الْغَيْبِ نُوحِيهَا إِلَيْكَ مَا كُنتَ تَعْلَمُهَا أَنتَ وَلَا قَوْمُكَ مِن قَبْلِ هَٰذَا — These are accounts of the Unseen which We reveal to you; neither you nor your people knew them before this (Hud: 49). The first view is more in accord with the context of the verse.

His saying, the Exalted: وما كنت لديهم إذ يلقون أقلامهم أيهم يكفل مريم (and you were not with them when they were casting their pens [to see] which of them would take charge of Mary...); the "pen" (القلم) here refers to the arrow (القدح) used for drawing lots (القرعة), also called a "shaft" (سهماً), and its plural is aqlam (أقلام). Thus, His saying: يلقون أقلامهم (they were casting their pens) means they were casting their arrows to determine by lot which of them would take charge of Mary.

This sentence indicates that the "contention" (الاختصام) indicated by His saying: وما كنت لديهم إذ يختصمون (and you were not with them when they were contending) was their contention and competition over the guardianship of Mary, and that they did not cease until they agreed to draw lots among themselves. They cast the lots, and the arrow came out for Zechariah, so he took charge of her, as evidenced by His saying: فكفلها زكريا (so Zechariah took charge of her); the verse.

Some have entertained the possibility that this contention and drawing of lots occurred after she grew up and Zechariah became unable to care for her. The origin of this view seems to be the mention of this lot-casting and contention after the completion of the story of her birth and choosing, while the mention of Zechariah’s guardianship occurred in the middle of it, suggesting they were two separate events.

However, there is no harm in repeating some details of the story, or what is equivalent to repetition, to establish the claim, just as occurred in the story of Joseph where the Exalted said after the completion of the story: ذَٰلِكَ مِنْ أَنبَاءِ الْغَيْبِ نُوحِيهِ إِلَيْكَ وَمَا كُنتَ لَدَيْهِمْ إِذْ أَجْمَعُوا أَمْرَهُمْ وَهُمْ يَمْكُرُونَ — These are accounts of the Unseen which We reveal to you, and you were not with them when they conspired their plan and were plotting (Yusuf: 102), referring thereby to the meaning of His saying, the Exalted, at the beginning of the story: إِذْ قَالُوا لَيُوسُفُ وَأَخُوهُ أَحَبُّ إِلَىٰ أَبِينَا مِنَّا وَنَحْنُ عُصْبَةٌ (When they said, ‘Joseph and his brother are dearer to our father than we are, though we are a group...’) until He said: لَا تَقْتُلُوا يُوسُفَ وَأَلْقُوهُ فِي غَيَابَتِ الْجُبِّ يَلْتَقِطْهُ بَعْضُ السَّيَّارَةِ إِن كُنتُمْ فَاعِلِينَ (‘Do not kill Joseph, but throw him into the recess of the cistern; some caravan will pick him up, if you are to do [anything]’) (Yusuf: 10).

His saying, the Exalted: إذ قالت الملائكة يا مريم إن الله يبشرك (When the angels said, ‘O Mary, Allah gives you the good news...’). It appears that this good news (البشارة) is the same as that contained in His saying, the Exalted, in another place: فَأَرْسَلْنَا إِلَيْهَا رُوحَنَا فَتَمَثَّلَ لَهَا بَشَرًا سَوِيًّا قَالَتْ إِنِّي أَعُوذُ بِالرَّحْمَٰنِ مِنكَ إِن كُنتَ تَقِيًّا قَالَ إِنَّمَا أَنَا رَسُولُ رَبِّكِ لِأَهَبَ لَكِ غُلَامًا زَكِيًّا (Then We sent to her Our Spirit, and he embodied himself for her as a flawless human. She said, ‘I seek the protection of the All-beneficent from you, should you be God-conscious!’ He said, ‘I am only a messenger of your Lord that I may give you a pure son’) (Maryam: 17-19). Thus, the good news attributed to the "angels" here is the same as that attributed to the "Spirit" alone there.

It has been said regarding this that the intended meaning of "the angels" is Gabriel, using the plural to refer to the singular out of veneration (تعظيماً) for his affair, just as it is said: "So-and-so traveled and rode the beasts and rode the ships," when he only rode one beast and one ship; or it is said: "The people said such-and-such to him," when only one person said it, and so on. A parallel to this verse is His saying in the previous story of Zechariah: فنادته الملائكة (Then the angels called out to him), followed by His saying: قال كذلك الله يفعل ما يشاء (He said, ‘So it is: Allah does whatever He wishes’); the verse.

It has also been suggested that Gabriel was accompanied by others who shared in calling out to her.

However, what is yielded by reflection (التدبر) on the verses mentioning the state of the angels is that among the angels there is precedence and subsequence in terms of the station of proximity (مقام القرب), and that the subordinate has absolute following (التبعية) of the commands of the superior, such that the act of the subordinate in rank is the very act of the superior, and his speech is the very speech of the superior. This is similar to what we observe and acknowledge regarding the acts of our faculties and limbs being the very acts of ourselves without any duality. You say: "My eyes saw it and my ears heard it," and "I saw it and I heard it." It is said: "My limbs did it, my hand wrote it, and my fingertips drew it," and "I did it and I wrote it." Likewise, the act of the followed among the angels is the very act of those following him and obeying his command, and his speech is their speech without difference, and vice versa. Just as the act of all is the act of Allah, the Glorified, and their speech is His speech, as the Exalted said: اللَّهُ يَتَوَفَّى الْأَنفُسَ حِينَ مَوْتِهَا — Allah takes the souls at the time of their death (Al-Zumar: 42), attributing the taking of souls to Himself; and He said: قُلْ يَتَوَفَّاكُم مَّلَكُ الْمَوْتِ الَّذِي وُكِّلَ بِكُمْ — Say, ‘The angel of death, who has been given charge of you, will take your souls’ (Al-Sajdah: 11), attributing it to the Angel of Death; and He said: حَتَّىٰ إِذَا جَاءَ أَحَدَكُمُ الْمَوْتُ تَوَفَّتْهُ رُسُلُنَا — until when death comes to one of you, Our messengers take him (Al-An'am: 61), attributing it to a group of angels.

Similar to this is His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّا أَوْحَيْنَا إِلَيْكَ — We have indeed revealed to you (Al-Nisa: 163), and His saying: نَزَلَ بِهِ الرُّوحُ الْأَمِينُ عَلَىٰ قَلْبِكَ — The Trustworthy Spirit has brought it down to your heart (Al-Shu'ara: 193-194), and His saying: مَن كَانَ عَدُوًّا لِّجِبْرِيلَ فَإِنَّهُ نَزَّلَهُ عَلَىٰ قَلْبِكَ — Whoever is an enemy to Gabriel [should know that] it is he who has brought it down to your heart (Al-Baqarah: 97), and His saying: كَلَّا إِنَّهَا تَذْكِرَةٌ فَمَن شَاءَ ذَكَرَهُ فِي صُحُفٍ مُّكَرَّمَةٍ مَّرْفُوعَةٍ مُّطَهَّرَةٍ بِأَيْدِي سَفَرَةٍ كِرَامٍ بَرَرَةٍ — No indeed! It is a reminder—so let him who wishes remember it—in honored scriptures, exalted and purified, by the hands of envoys, noble and pious (Abasa: 11-16).

Thus, it becomes clear that the good news of Gabriel is the very good news of those under his command from the assembly of angels, and he is among the masters of the angels and those brought near to Him, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّهُ لَقَوْلُ رَسُولٍ كَرِيمٍ ذِي قُوَّةٍ عِندَ ذِي الْعَرْشِ مَكِينٍ مُّطَاعٍ ثَمَّ أَمِينٍ — it is indeed the speech of a noble messenger, powerful and established with the Lord of the Throne, obeyed there and trustworthy (Al-Takwir: 19-21). Further clarification of this discourse will follow in Surah Fatir, if Allah, the Exalted, wills.

What we have mentioned is supported by His saying, the Exalted, in the following verse: قال كذلك الله يفعل ما يشاء (He said, ‘So it is: Allah does whatever He wishes’), for its apparent meaning is that the speaker is Allah, the Glorified, even though this speech was attributed in Surah Maryam in the same story to the Spirit; the Exalted said: قَالَ إِنَّمَا أَنَا رَسُولُ رَبِّكِ لِأَهَبَ لَكِ غُلَامًا زَكِيًّا قَالَتْ أَنَّىٰ يَكُونُ لِي غُلَامٌ وَلَمْ يَمْسَسْنِي بَشَرٌ وَلَمْ أَكُ بَغِيًّا قَالَ كَذَٰلِكِ قَالَ رَبُّكِ هُوَ عَلَيَّ هَيِّنٌ (He said, ‘I am only a messenger of your Lord that I may give you a pure son.’ She said, ‘How shall I have a child seeing that no human has ever touched me, nor have I been unchaste?’ He said, ‘So it is: your Lord says, ‘‘It is easy for Me’’’) (Maryam: 19-21).

The speaking of the angels and the Spirit with Mary is evidence of her being a muhaddathah; indeed, His saying, the Exalted, in Surah Maryam regarding the very same story: فأرسلنا إليها روحنا فتمثل لها بشراً سوياً (Then We sent to her Our Spirit, and he embodied himself for her as a flawless human) indicates her visual witnessing (معاينة) of the angel in addition to hearing his voice. The full discussion of this meaning will follow in the upcoming traditional discourse, if Allah wills.

His saying, the Exalted: بكلمة منه اسمه المسيح عيسى بن مريم (with a Word from Him whose name is the Messiah, Jesus son of Mary). The discussion regarding the meaning of His "speech" (كلامه) has already passed in the commentary on His saying: تِلْكَ الرُّسُلُ فَضَّلْنَا بَعْضَهُمْ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ — These are the apostles, some of whom We gave an advantage over others (Al-Baqarah: 253).

The "word" (الكلمة) and "words" (الكلم) are like "date" (التمرة) and "dates" (التمر) in terms of being a single instance and a genus. A "word" is applied to a single utterance indicating a meaning, and to a sentence, whether it is complete enough to stop upon—such as "Zayd is standing"—or not—such as "If Zayd is standing." This is according to the language. As for what is conventional in the Quran—meaning the "Word" attributed to Allah, the Exalted—it is that through which what Allah intended of an affair becomes manifest, such as the Word of Origination (كلمة الإيجاد), which is His saying to a thing He intends: "Be" (كن), or the Word of Revelation (كلمة الوحي) and inspiration, and the like.

As for what is intended by "the Word" here, it has been said that it refers to the Messiah (عليه السلام) because the prophets who preceded him, or specifically the prophets of the Children of Israel, gave good news of him as the savior of the Children of Israel. In similar contexts, it is said: "This is my word that I used to say." A parallel is His saying, the Exalted, regarding the appearance of Moses (عليه السلام): وَتَمَّتْ كَلِمَتُ رَبِّكَ الْحُسْنَىٰ عَلَىٰ بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ بِمَا صَبَرُوا — And the gracious word of your Lord was fulfilled for the Children of Israel because of their patience (Al-A'raf: 137). However, while this might be supported by the books of the Two Testaments, the Noble Quran is devoid of this; rather, the Quran considers Jesus son of Mary to be a bringer of good news, not merely the subject of it. Furthermore, the context of His saying: اسمه المسيح (his name is the Messiah) does not suit this, for the "Word" according to this view would be the appearance of Jesus who was previously announced, not Jesus himself, whereas the apparent meaning of His saying: اسمه المسيح is that "the Messiah" is the name of the Word, not the name of the one regarding whom the Word was previously given.

It has also been suggested that he is called "the Word" because of his clarification of Allah’s intent in the Torah, and his exposition of the distortions of the Jews and the matters of religion they differed upon, as Allah, the Exalted, narrated from him in his address to the Children of Israel: وَلِأُبَيِّنَ لَكُم بَعْضَ الَّذِي تَخْتَلِفُونَ فِيهِ — and to clarify for you some of the things you differ about (Al-Zukhruf: 63). While this is a point that could justify this expression, it lacks supporting evidence from the context.

It has also been suggested that "a Word from Him" refers to the good news itself—that is, the informing of her pregnancy with Jesus and his birth. Thus, the meaning of His saying: يبشرك بكلمة منه (gives you the good news of a Word from Him) would be: "gives you good news which is that you will give birth to Jesus without the touch of a human." However, the context of the end of the verse—namely His saying: اسمه المسيح (his name is the Messiah)—does not harmonize with this, as is evident.

It has also been suggested that it refers to Jesus (عليه السلام) because of his being the Word of Origination (كلمة الإيجاد)—that is, the saying: "Be" (كن). Jesus (عليه السلام) was specifically characterized by this, despite every human—indeed every thing—existing by the creative Word "Be," because the birth of other individuals of humanity proceeds according to the customary, familiar causes of conception from the entry of the male's fluid into the female's ovum and the action of the accompanying factors. Therefore, conception is attributed to it just as other effects are attributed to their causes. But since the conception of Jesus did not proceed in this manner and lacked some of the gradual, customary causes, his existence was by the mere Word of creation without the intervention of customary causes; thus, he was the Word itself. This is supported by His saying, the Exalted: وَكَلِمَتُهُ أَلْقَاهَا إِلَىٰ مَرْيَمَ وَرُوحٌ مِّنْهُ — and His Word that He cast toward Mary and a spirit from Him (Al-Nisa: 171), and His saying at the end of these verses: إِنَّ مَثَلَ عِيسَىٰ عِندَ اللَّهِ كَمَثَلِ آدَمَ خَلَقَهُ مِن تُرَابٍ ثُمَّ قَالَ لَهُ كُن فَيَكُونُ — Indeed the case of Jesus with Allah is like the case of Adam: He created him from dust, then said to him, ‘Be,’ and he was (Al-Imran: 59). This is the best of the views.

"The Messiah" (المسيح) means the "anointed one" (الممسوح). Jesus (عليه السلام) was named this because he was anointed with auspiciousness and blessing (اليمن والبركة), or because he was anointed with purification from sins, or anointed with blessed olive oil with which the prophets used to be anointed, or because Gabriel anointed him with his wing at his birth to be a protection from Satan, or because he used to wipe (يمسح) the heads of orphans, or because he used to wipe the eye of the blind with his hand and they would see, or because he would not wipe one afflicted with an ailment with his hand except that they were healed. these are views they have mentioned regarding his being named the Messiah.

However, what can be relied upon is that this term was part of the good news with which Gabriel gave glad tidings to Mary (عليها السلام), as narrated by the Exalted in His saying: إِنَّ اللَّهَ يُبَشِّرُكِ بِكَلِمَةٍ مِّنْهُ اسْمُهُ الْمَسِيحُ عِيسَى ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ (Indeed Allah gives you the good news of a Word from Him whose name is the Messiah, Jesus son of Mary). This very term is the Arabicized form of "Mashiha" (مشيحا) found in the books of the Two Testaments.

What is gathered from them is that it was the custom of the Children of Israel that when a king among them rose to the affair of kingship, the priests would anoint him with holy oil so that he might be blessed in his kingdom; thus he was called "Mashiha," meaning either "the king" or "the blessed."

It appears from their books that he (عليه السلام) was only named "Mashiha" because his good news included his kingship, and that he would appear among the Children of Israel as a king over them and a savior for them, as hinted at in the Gospel of Luke regarding the good news to Mary. It says: "And the angel came in unto her, and said, Hail, thou that art highly favoured, the Lord is with thee: blessed art thou among women... And the angel said unto her, Fear not, Mary: for thou hast found favour with Allah. And, behold, thou shalt conceive in thy womb, and bring forth a son, and shalt call his name JESUS. He shall be great, and shall be called the Son of the Highest: and the Lord Allah shall give unto him the throne of his father David: And he shall reign over the house of Jacob for ever; and of his kingdom there shall be no end" (Luke 1:28-33).

For this reason, the Jews make excuses for rejecting his prophethood by claiming that the good news, because it includes his kingship, does not apply to Jesus (عليه السلام) since he did not attain kingship during the days of his mission and his life. Consequently, the Christians—followed by some Muslim commentators—have interpreted his kingship as spiritual kingship rather than formal kingship.

I say: It is not improbable to say that his being named the Messiah in the good news means his being "blessed" (مباركاً), for anointing among them was only for seeking blessing. This is supported by His saying, the Exalted: قَالَ إِنِّي عَبْدُ اللَّهِ آتَانِيَ الْكِتَابَ وَجَعَلَنِي نَبِيًّا وَجَعَلَنِي مُبَارَكًا أَيْنَ مَا كُنتُ — He said, ‘Indeed I am a servant of Allah! He has given me the Book and made me a prophet. He has made me blessed, wherever I may be’ (Maryam: 30-31).

"Jesus" (عيسى) is originally "Yeshua" (يشوع), which they interpreted as "the savior" (المخلص) or "the rescuer" (المنجي). In some narrations, its interpretation is "Ya'ish" (يعيش) — he lives — which is more appropriate in terms of naming the son of Zechariah "Yahya" (يحيى) — he lives — based on the complete similarity between these two prophets as previously mentioned.

The restriction of Jesus as "son of Mary" (ابن مريم), despite the address in the verse being to Mary, is to draw attention to the fact that he was created without a father, and that he would be known by this attribute, and that Mary is his partner in this sign, as the Exalted said: وَجَعَلْنَاهَا وَابْنَهَا آيَةً لِّلْعَالَمِينَ — and made her and her son a sign for all the worlds (Al-Anbiya: 91).

His saying, the Exalted: وجيهاً في الدنيا والآخرة ومن المقربين (honoured in this world and the Hereafter, and one of those brought near [to Allah]). "Honour" (الوجاهة) is acceptability (المقبولية). His (عليه السلام) being accepted in this world is not hidden, and likewise in the Hereafter by the explicit text of the Quran.

The meaning of "those brought near" (المقربين) is clear; he is brought near to Allah, included among the ranks of the friends (الأولياء) and the angels brought near in terms of proximity, as the Exalted mentioned in His saying: لَّن يَسْتَنكِفَ الْمَسِيحُ أَن يَكُونَ عَبْدًا لِّلَّهِ وَلَا الْمَلَائِكَةُ الْمُقَرَّبُونَ — The Messiah would never disdain to be a servant of Allah, nor would the angels brought near [to Him] (Al-Nisa: 172). The Exalted has defined the meaning of "proximity" (التقريب) in His saying: إِذَا وَقَعَتِ الْوَاقِعَةُ (When the Imminent Event befalls) up to His saying: وَكُنتُمْ أَزْوَاجًا ثَلَاثَةً (and you are three groups) up to His saying: وَالسَّابِقُونَ السَّابِقُونَ أُولَٰئِكَ الْمُقَرَّبُونَ — And the Foremost Ones are the foremost ones: they are the ones brought near [to Allah] (Al-Waqi'ah: 1-11). The verse, as you see, indicates that this proximity—which is proximity to Allah, the Glorified—is in reality the precedence (سبق) of a human over other individuals of his species in traversing the path of return to Allah, the traversing of which is prescribed for every human, indeed every thing. The Exalted said: يَا أَيُّهَا الْإِنسَانُ إِنَّكَ كَادِحٌ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ كَدْحًا فَمُلَاقِيهِ — O man! You are laboring toward your Lord with [great] labor, and you will encounter Him (Al-Inshiqaq: 6), and the Exalted said: أَلَا إِلَى اللَّهِ تَصِيرُ الْأُمُورُ — Look! To Allah do all matters return (Al-Shura: 53).

If you reflect on "those brought near" being an attribute of individuals among humans and individuals among angels, you will know that it does not necessarily have to be an acquired station (مقاماً اكتسابياً), for the angels do not secure the station they have secured with Allah, the Glorified, through acquisition (الكسب). Perhaps it is a station attained by the angels brought near through a divine gift (هبة إلهية), and by the humans brought near through action (العمل).

His saying: وجيهاً في الدنيا والآخرة (honoured in this world and the Hereafter) is a circumstantial qualifier (حال), as is what is coordinated with it: ومن المقربين (and one of those brought near), ويكلم (and he will speak...), ومن الصالحين (and one of the righteous), ويكلمه (and He will speak to him...), رسولاً (and an apostle...).

His saying, the Exalted: ويكلم الناس في المهد وكهلاً (and he will speak to the people in the cradle and in adulthood). The "cradle" (المهد) is the bed prepared for the infant. "Adulthood" (الكهل) refers to the stage of kahulah (الكهولة) — the period between youth and old age — when a person is a complete and strong man. Thus it is said: the kahal is one whose hair is streaked with gray. It has also been said that the kahal is one who has reached thirty-four years.

In any case, it contains an indication that he will live until he reaches the age of adulthood; thus it is another good news for Mary.

In the explicit mention of this, while the Gospels indicate that he did not live on earth for more than thirty-three years, there is a point of consideration that should be examined deeply. For this reason, it has been suggested that his speaking to people in adulthood will only be after his descent from heaven, as he did not remain on earth long enough to reach the age of adulthood. It has also been suggested that what history yields after verification is that Jesus (عليه السلام) lived for about sixty-four years, contrary to what appears in the Gospels.

What appears from the context of His saying: في المهد وكهلاً (in the cradle and in adulthood) is that he does not reach the age of old age (الشيخوخة), but rather ends at the age of adulthood. Accordingly, the exposition has taken his speech at the two ends of his life: infancy and adulthood.

The customary practice of placing an infant in the cradle is to place him there in the early part of his life while he is in swaddling clothes, before he crawls or walks, which is usually in the second year or less. This is the age of [beginning] speech. Thus, the speech of an infant in the cradle, while not in itself a miraculous event (خوارق العادة), the apparent meaning of the verse is that he speaks to people in the cradle with complete speech that is given heed by the rational among people, just as they give heed to the speech of an adult. In other words, he speaks to them in the cradle just as he speaks to them as an adult. Speech from an infant with this quality is a miraculous sign (آية خارقة).

Furthermore, the story in Surah Maryam clarifies that his speaking to people occurred at the very first hour Mary brought him to the people after his birth, and the speech of an infant on the first day of his birth is undoubtedly a miraculous sign. The Exalted said: فَأَتَتْ بِهِ قَوْمَهَا تَحْمِلُهُ قَالُوا يَا مَرْيَمُ لَقَدْ جِئْتِ شَيْئًا فَرِيًّا يَا أُخْتَ هَارُونَ مَا كَانَ أَبُوكِ امْرَأَ سَوْءٍ وَمَا كَانَتْ أُمُّكِ بَغِيًّا فَأَشَارَتْ إِلَيْهِ قَالُوا كَيْفَ نُكَلِّمُ مَن كَانَ فِي الْمَهْدِ صَبِيًّا قَالَ إِنِّي عَبْدُ اللَّهِ آتَانِيَ الْكِتَابَ وَجَعَلَنِي نَبِيًّا وَجَعَلَنِي مُبَارَكًا أَيْنَ مَا كُنتُ (Then she brought him to her people, carrying him. They said, ‘O Mary, you have certainly come up with a strange thing! O sister of Aaron! Your father was not an evil man, nor was your mother unchaste.’ Thereat she pointed to him. They said, ‘How can we speak to one who is yet a baby in the cradle?’ He said, ‘Indeed I am a servant of Allah! He has given me the Book and made me a prophet. He has made me blessed, wherever I may be’) (Maryam: 27-31).

His saying, the Exalted: قالت رب أنى يكون لي ولد ولم يمسسني بشر (She said, ‘My Lord, how shall I have a child seeing that no human has ever touched me?’). Her addressing her Lord, despite the one speaking to her being the embodied Spirit, is based on what was previously mentioned: that the address of the angels and the Spirit and their speech is the speech of Allah, the Glorified. She knew that the one speaking to her was Allah, the Glorified, even if the address was directed to her from the aspect of the embodied Spirit or the angels; therefore, she addressed her Lord.

It is also possible that the speech is of the same nature as His saying, the Exalted: قَالَ رَبِّ ارْجِعُونِ — He says, ‘My Lord! Send me back’ (Al-Mu'minun: 99), being a plea (استغاثة) interspersed in the speech.

His saying, the Glorified: قال كذلك الله يفعل ما يشاء إذا قضى أمراً فإنما يقول له كن فيكون (He said, ‘So it is: Allah creates whatever He wishes. When He decides on a matter, He just says to it, ‘‘Be!’’ and it is’). Reference has already been made to the fact that applying this answer to what is in Surah Maryam—namely His saying: قَالَ كَذَٰلِكِ قَالَ رَبُّكِ هُوَ عَلَيَّ هَيِّنٌ وَلِنَجْعَلَهُ آيَةً لّلنَّاسِ وَرَحْمَةً مِّنَّا وَكَانَ أَمْرًا مَّقْضِيًّا (He said, ‘So it is: your Lord says, ‘‘It is easy for Me; and We shall make him a sign for mankind and a mercy from Us, and it is a matter [already] decided’’’) (Maryam: 21)—yields that His saying here: كذلك (So it is) is a complete statement, its implied meaning being: "The matter is so," and its sense is that what you have been given good news of is a decided matter (أمر مقضي) that cannot be averted.

As for the wonderment at this matter, it would only be valid if this matter were something Allah, the Glorified, were not capable of or if it were difficult. As for power (القدرة), His power is unlimited; He does whatever He wishes. As for difficulty and hardship, hardship and difficulty are only conceived if the matter is something reached through causes (الأسباب); thus, whenever the premises and causes increase and become scarce or distant in attainment, the matter becomes more difficult. But Allah, the Glorified, does not create what He creates through causes; rather, when He decides on a matter, He just says to it, "Be," and it is.

Thus, it has become clear that His saying: كذلك (So it is) is a complete statement intended to remove Mary’s agitation and the hesitation of her soul. His saying: الله يخلق ما يشاء (Allah creates whatever He wishes) removes the incapacity (العجز) suggested by wonderment. And His saying: إذا قضى (When He decides) removes the illusion of hardship and difficulty.

His saying, the Exalted: ويعلمه الكتاب والحكمة والتوراة والإنجيل (And He will teach him the Book and wisdom, the Torah and the Evangel). The definite article (اللام) in "the Book" (الكتاب) and "wisdom" (الحكمة) is for the genus (للجنس). It has already passed that "the Book" is the revelation (الوحي) that removes the differences among people, and "wisdom" is the beneficial knowledge (المعرفة النافعة) related to belief or action. Accordingly, the coordination of the Torah and the Evangel with the Book and wisdom—despite their being two books containing wisdom—is of the nature of mentioning the particular after the general due to an importance in its specific mention. The definite article in "the Book" is not for exhaustiveness (للاستغراق) because of His saying, the Exalted: وَلَمَّا جَاءَ عِيسَىٰ بِالْبَيِّنَاتِ قَالَ قَدْ جِئْتُكُم بِالْحِكْمَةِ وَلِأُبَيِّنَ لَكُم بَعْضَ الَّذِي تَخْتَلِفُونَ فِيهِ فَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُونِ — When Jesus brought the manifest proofs, he said, ‘I have certainly brought you wisdom, and to clarify for you some of the things you differ about. So be God-conscious and obey me’ (Al-Zukhruf: 63), and its explanation has already passed.

As for the Torah (التوراة), what the Quran intends by it is that which Allah revealed to Moses (عليه السلام) at the appointed time (الميقات) in tablets, as Allah narrates in Surah Al-A'raf. As for the scrolls (الأسفار) held by the Jews, they themselves admit to the interruption of the chain of transmission (انقطاع اتصال السند) between Nebuchadnezzar, among the kings of Babylon, and Cyrus, among the kings of Persia. However, the Quran confirms that the Torah in their hands during the time of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was not entirely contrary to the original Torah, even if the hand of distortion (التحريف) had played with it; the indication of the Quranic verses to this is clear.

As for the Evangel (الإنجيل) — meaning "the good news" — the Quran indicates that it was a single book revealed to Jesus; it is the revelation specific to him. The Exalted said: وَأَنزلَ التَّوْرَاةَ وَالْإِنجِيلَ مِن قَبْلُ هُدًى لِّلنَّاسِ — and He sent down the Torah and the Evangel before, as guidance for mankind (Al-Imran: 3-4). As for these gospels attributed to Matthew, Mark, Luke, and John, they are books composed after him (عليه السلام).

It also indicates that the laws (الأحكام) are only in the Torah, and that the Evangel does not contain except some abrogating (النواسخ) [laws], as in His saying in these verses: مصدقاً لما بين يدي من التوراة ولأحل لكم بعض الذي حرم عليكم (confirming what is before me of the Torah, and to make lawful to you some of the things that were forbidden to you); the verse, and His saying: وَآتَيْنَاهُ الْإِنجِيلَ فِيهِ هُدًى وَنُورٌ وَمُصَدِّقًا لِّمَا بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ مِنَ التَّوْرَاةِ وَهُدًى وَمَوْعِظَةً لِّلْمُتَّقِينَ وَلْيَحْكُمْ أَهْلُ الْإِنجِيلِ بِمَا أَنزَلَ اللَّهُ فِيهِ — and We gave him the Evangel, in which there is guidance and light, and confirming what was before it of the Torah, and as guidance and an admonition for the God-conscious. Let the people of the Evangel judge by what Allah has sent down in it (Al-Ma'idah: 46-47). It is not improbable to gather from the verse that it contains some affirmative laws.

It also indicates that the Evangel contains the good news of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) just like the Torah. The Exalted said: الَّذِينَ يَتَّبِعُونَ الرَّسُولَ النَّبِيَّ الْأُمِّيَّ الَّذِي يَجِدُونَهُ مَكْتُوبًا عِندَهُمْ فِي التَّوْرَاةِ وَالْإِنجِيلِ — those who follow the Apostle, the uninstructed prophet, whose mention they find written with them in the Torah and the Evangel (Al-A'raf: 157).

His saying, the Exalted: ورسولاً إلى بني إسرائيل (and an apostle to the Children of Israel). Its apparent meaning is that he (عليه السلام) was sent to the Children of Israel specifically, as is evident from the verses regarding Moses (عليه السلام). It has already passed in the discussion on prophethood (النبوة) under His saying, the Exalted: كَانَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً وَاحِدَةً فَبَعَثَ اللَّهُ النَّبِيِّينَ — Mankind were a single community; then Allah sent the prophets (Al-Baqarah: 213), that Jesus (عليه السلام), like Moses, is among the "Arch-Prophets" (أولي العزم) — the possessors of steadfastness — and they are sent to all the people of the world.

However, the knot is untied by what we mentioned there regarding the difference between the "Apostle" (الرسول) and the "Prophet" (النبي): that prophethood is the office of being sent and conveying (التبليغ), while apostleship (الرسالة) is the specific embassy (السفارة الخاصة) that entails judgment and decreeing with truth among the people—either with survival and blessing, or with destruction—as yielded by His saying, the Exalted: وَلِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ رَّسُولٌ فَإِذَا جَاءَ رَسُولُهُمْ قُضِيَ بَيْنَهُم بِالْقِسْطِ — There is an apostle for every community; so when their apostle comes, judgement is made between them with justice (Yunus: 47).

In other words, the Prophet is the human sent to clarify the religion to the people, and the Apostle is the one sent to deliver a specific clarification whose rejection entails destruction and whose acceptance entails survival and happiness, as supported—indeed indicated—by what Allah, the Glorified, narrated of the addresses of the apostles to their communities, such as Noah, Hud, Salih, Shu'ayb, and others (عليهم السلام).

If this is so, apostleship to a specific people does not necessitate being sent only to them; it was possible for the Apostle to a specific people to be a Prophet sent to them and to others, like Moses and Jesus (عليهما السلام).

There are evidences for this from the Noble Quran, such as the apostleship of Moses to Pharaoh; the Exalted said: اذْهَبْ إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ إِنَّهُ طَغَىٰ — Go to Pharaoh, for he has indeed rebelled (Taha: 24), and the faith of the magicians in Moses and the manifestation of the acceptance of their faith, though they were not from the Children of Israel; the Exalted said: قَالُوا آمَنَّا بِرَبِّ هَارُونَ وَمُوسَىٰ — They said, ‘We believe in the Lord of Aaron and Moses’ (Taha: 70), and the invitation to the people of Pharaoh; the Exalted said: وَلَقَدْ فَتَنَّا قَبْلَهُمْ قَوْمَ فِرْعَوْنَ وَجَاءَهُمْ رَسُولٌ كَرِيمٌ — Certainly We tried the people of Pharaoh before them, and a noble apostle came to them (Al-Dukhan: 17). Similar to this was the matter of people's faith in Jesus, for before the mission of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), the Romans and great nations of the Westerners—such as the Franks, Austrians, Prussians, and English—and nations of the Easterners—such as Najran—believed in him, and none of them were from the Children of Israel. The Quran does not restrict—in what it mentions of the Christians—the Christians of the Children of Israel specifically, but generalizes its praise or blame to all.

His saying, the Exalted: أني قد جئتكم بآية من ربكم أني أخلق لكم من الطين (I have come to you with a sign from your Lord: I will create for you out of clay...) up to His saying: وأحيي الموتى بإذن الله (and I will revive the dead by Allah’s leave). "Creation" (الخلق) is the assembling of the parts of a thing. In this is the attribution of creation to other than Him, the Exalted, as also suggested by His saying, the Exalted: فَتَبَارَكَ اللَّهُ أَحْسَنُ الْخَالِقِينَ — So blessed is Allah, the best of creators (Al-Mu'minun: 14).

The "blind" (الأكمه) is the one born with sightless eyes; it is also said of one whose eyes have failed. It is said: "his eyes became blind (كمهت) until they turned white," as stated by al-Raghib. The "leper" (الأبرص) is one afflicted with leprosy (البرص), which is a well-known skin disease.

In His saying: وأحيي الموتى (and I will revive the dead), where He linked revival to "the dead" (الموتى) in the plural, there is an indication—or at least a hint—of frequency and multiplicity.

Likewise, His saying: بإذن الله (by Allah’s leave) was brought to indicate that the issuance of these miraculous signs (الآيات المعجزة) from him (عليه السلام) is dependent upon Allah, the Exalted, without Jesus (عليه السلام) being independent in any of that. It was repeated with an insistence because it was expected that people might go astray regarding him and believe in his divinity (ألوهيته), arguing from the miraculous signs issuing from him (عليه السلام). Therefore, he restricted every sign he announced about himself that they might go astray by—such as creation and reviving the dead—with "by Allah’s leave," then concluded the speech by saying: إن الله ربي وربكم فاعبدوه هذا صراط مستقيم (Indeed Allah is my Lord and your Lord; so worship Him. This is a straight path).

The apparent meaning of His saying: أني أخلق لكم (I will create for you...) is that these signs were issuing from him as external occurrences, not that the speech was brought merely for argumentation and challenge (التحدي). If it were merely a statement to sever excuses and complete the proof, it would have been proper for the speech to be restricted by a condition implying that, such as saying: "If you ask" or "If you wish" or the like.

Furthermore, what Allah, the Glorified, narrates of His addressing Jesus on the Day of Resurrection indicates the occurrence of these signs with the utmost clarity. He said: إِذْ قَالَ اللَّهُ يَا عِيسَى ابْنَ مَرْيَمَ اذْكُرْ نِعْمَتِي عَلَيْكَ وَعَلَىٰ وَالِدَتِكَ (When Allah will say, ‘O Jesus son of Mary, remember My blessing upon you and upon your mother’) up to His saying: وَإِذْ تَخْلُقُ مِنَ الطِّينِ كَهَيْئَةِ الطَّيْرِ بِإِذْنِي فَتَنفُخُ فِيهَا فَتَكُونُ طَيْرًا بِإِذْنِي وَتُبْرِئُ الْأَكْمَهَ وَالْأَبْرَصَ بِإِذْنِي وَإِذْ تُخْرِجُ الْمَوْتَىٰ (and when you would create out of clay the form of a bird, with My leave, and you would breathe into it and it would become a bird, with My leave; and you would heal the blind and the leper, with My leave; and when you would raise the dead...) (Al-Ma'idah: 110).

From here, the falsity of what some have mentioned becomes clear: that the most the verse indicates is that Allah, the Glorified, placed this secret in Jesus son of Mary, and that he argued against the people with it and completed the proof against them such that if they had asked him for any of that, he would have brought it; but as for whether all or some of it occurred, there is no indication in it of that.

His saying, the Exalted: وأنبئكم بما تأكلون وما تدخرون في بيوتكم (and I will inform you of what you eat and what you store up in your houses). This is an informing of the Unseen (الغيب) which is exclusive to Allah, the Exalted, and those of His messengers He specifically chooses for revelation. It is another sign and an informing of an Unseen whose realization is explicitly clear, leaving no room for doubt or suspicion, for a person does not usually doubt what they have eaten nor what they have stored in their house.

He did not restrict this sign with "by Allah’s leave," even though the sign is not realized except by leave from Him, the Exalted, as He said: وَمَا كَانَ لِرَسُولٍ أَن يَأْتِيَ بِآيَةٍ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ اللَّهِ — An apostle may not bring a sign except by Allah’s leave (Ghafir: 78), because this sign was expressed as "informing" (الإنباء), which is a speech subsisting in Jesus (عليه السلام) and considered an act of his; thus it is not appropriate to attribute it to the Presence of Sanctity (ساحة القدس), unlike the two previous signs—namely creation and revival—for they are in reality the act of Allah and are not attributed to another except by His leave.

Furthermore, the two mentioned signs are not like informing, for the straying of people regarding them is faster than in informing. Simple hearts might accept the divinity of the creator of birds and the reviver of the dead with the slightest whisper or fallacy, unlike the divinity of one who informs of the Unseen, for they do not acknowledge the exclusivity of the Unseen to Allah, the Glorified, but rather consider it a common matter attainable by any ascetic or soothsaying sorcerer. Thus, it was necessary when addressing them to restrict the two mentioned signs with "leave" but not the last one, nor the healing; it was sufficient for them to merely mention that it is a sign from Allah, especially when the address is directed to a people who claim to be believers. Therefore, he concluded the speech by saying: إن في ذلك لآية لكم إن كنتم مؤمنين (There is indeed a sign in that for you, should you be believers), meaning: if you are truthful in your claim of faith.

His saying, the Exalted: ومصدقاً لما بين يدي من التوراة ولأحل لكم بعض الذي حرم عليكم (confirming what is before me of the Torah, and to make lawful to you some of the things that were forbidden to you). This is coordinated with His saying: ورسولاً إلى بني إسرائيل (and an apostle to the Children of Israel). The fact that the coordinated part is based on the first person (متكلم) while the part it is coordinated with is based on the third person (غيبة)—namely that Jesus (عليه السلام) in His saying: ومصدقاً لما بين يدي is a speaker, and in His saying: ورسولاً إلى بني إسرائيل is absent—does not harm the coordination after interpreting His saying: ورسولاً إلى بني إسرائيل as the speech of Jesus: أني قد جئتكم (I have come to you). The mode of speech thus changes from absence to presence, making the coordination sound.

His confirmation of the Torah that was before him is only a confirmation of what Allah taught him of the Torah according to what the previous verse yields, which is the original Torah revealed to Moses (عليهما السلام). Thus, his being a confirmer of the Torah in his time is not an indication of its being undistorted, just as our Prophet Muhammad’s (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) confirmation of the Torah before him is not an indication of its being undistorted.

His saying, the Exalted: ولأحل لكم بعض الذي حرم عليكم (and to make lawful to you some of the things that were forbidden to you). For Allah, the Exalted, had forbidden them some of the good things (الطيبات); the Exalted said: فَبِظُلْمٍ مِّنَ الَّذِينَ هَادُوا حَرَّمْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ طَيِّبَاتٍ أُحِلَّتْ لَهُمْ — Due to the wrongdoing of the Jews, We prohibited them certain good things that were permitted to them (Al-Nisa: 160).

The speech is not devoid of an indication of his (عليه السلام) endorsement of the laws of the Torah except for what Allah, the Exalted, abrogated by his hand of the burdensome laws (الأحكام الشاقة) prescribed for the Jews. Therefore, it has been said that the Evangel does not contain a [complete] law (الشريعة). His saying: ولأحل (and to make lawful) is coordinated with His saying: بآية من ربكم (with a sign from your Lord), and the "lam" (اللام) is for purpose (للغاية). The meaning is: "I have come to you to abrogate some of the forbidden laws prescribed for you."

His saying, the Exalted: وجئتكم بآية من ربكم (And I have come to you with a sign from your Lord). It appears that this is to clarify that His saying: فاتقوا الله وأطيعون (so be God-conscious and obey me) is a derivation from the bringing of the sign, not from the making lawful of the forbidden things; thus it is to repel an illusion. It is possible that this is what was intended by those who said that the repetition of the sentence is to distinguish between what is before it and what is after it, for mere distinction is not among the merits of speech.

His saying, the Exalted: إن الله ربي وربكم فاعبدوه (Indeed Allah is my Lord and your Lord; so worship Him). In this is a severing of the excuse of those who believed in his divinity due to his (عليه السلام) perceiving that from them or his knowing it through revelation, as we mentioned regarding the restriction of His saying: فيكون طيراً (and it would become a bird) and His saying: وأحيي الموتى (and I will revive the dead) with His saying: بإذن الله (by Allah’s leave). However, what appears from His saying, the Exalted, in narrating the speech of Jesus (عليه السلام): مَا قُلْتُ لَهُمْ إِلَّا مَا أَمَرْتَنِي بِهِ أَنِ اعْبُدُوا اللَّهَ رَبِّي وَرَبَّكُمْ — I did not say to them [anything] except what You had commanded me [to say]: ‘‘Worship Allah, my Lord and your Lord’’ (Al-Ma'idah: 117), is that this was by a command from his Lord and a revelation from Him.

His saying, the Exalted: فلما أحس عيسى منهم الكفر قال من أنصاري إلى الله (When Jesus sensed their faithlessness, he said, ‘Who will be my helpers [on the way] to Allah?’). Since the good news given to Mary included the general accounts of Jesus (عليه السلام) from the time of his conception to the time of his mission and invitation, He restricted Himself to them as a concise narration in the speech, and derived from it the completion of the account of his story—which is his selection of his disciples (حوارييه), the plotting of his people against him, and Allah’s plotting against them in purifying him from them and taking him and raising him to Himself—which is the completion of the story.

The story has taken into account the amount that was important to deliver to the Christians at the time of the revelation of the verses—namely the Christians of Najran, the delegation who came to Medina for discussion and argumentation. Therefore, some of the details contained in his stories mentioned in other Quranic surahs—such as Al-Nisa, Al-Ma'idah, Al-Anbiya, Al-Zukhruf, and Al-Saff—were omitted from it.

In the use of the term "sensing" (الإحساس) in the case of faithlessness (الكفر), despite it being an internal matter of the heart, there is a hint of its manifestation from them such that sensing became attached to it, or that they intended to harm and kill him because of their faithlessness, so he sensed it. Thus, His saying: فلما أحس عيسى (When Jesus sensed) means he perceived and became certain of faithlessness from them—that is, from the Children of Israel whose name was mentioned in the good news. He said: من أنصاري إلى الله (Who will be my helpers [on the way] to Allah?). He only intended by this question to distinguish a number of men from his people so they might devote themselves to the truth—thus the strength of the religion would be established in them, and its power would be centered in them, and then his invitation would spread from them. This is the state of every natural or social power: when it begins to act and spread its influence and broadcast its work, it must take for itself a center (كانوناً) in which it gathers, upon which it relies, and from which it draws support. Were it not for that, it would not be established in action and would go to waste, yielding no benefit.

Similar to this in the invitation of Islam was the Pledge of al-Aqabah and the Pledge of the Tree; the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) intended by them the anchoring of power and the gathering of strength so that the affair of the invitation might be established.

When Jesus (عليه السلام) became certain that his invitation would not be successful among all or most of the Children of Israel, and that they were inevitably faithless toward him, and that if they extinguished his breath the invitation would perish and the trial would intensify, he prepared this preparation for the survival of his invitation. He sought help from them for the journey toward Allah, the Glorified, and the disciples answered him in that. Thus they were distinguished from the rest of the people by faith, and that was the foundation for the distinction of faith from faithlessness and its triumph over it through the spreading of the invitation and the establishment of the proof, as the Exalted said: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا كُونُوا أَنصَارَ اللَّهِ كَمَا قَالَ عِيسَى ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ لِلْحَوَارِيِّينَ مَنْ أَنصَارِي إِلَى اللَّهِ قَالَ الْحَوَارِيُّونَ نَحْنُ أَنصَارُ اللَّهِ فَآمَنَت طَّائِفَةٌ مِّن بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ وَكَفَرَت طَّائِفَةٌ فَأَيَّدْنَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا عَلَىٰ عَدُوِّهِمْ فَأَصْبَحُوا ظَاهِرِينَ — O you who have faith! Be Allah’s helpers, as Jesus son of Mary said to his disciples, ‘Who will be my helpers [on the way] to Allah?’ The disciples said, ‘We will be Allah’s helpers.’ So a group of the Children of Israel believed, and a group disbelieved. Then We strengthened those who believed against their enemies, and they became the ones who prevailed (Al-Saff: 14).

He restricted the "helpers" in His saying: من أنصاري (Who will be my helpers) with His saying: إلى الله (to Allah) to complete the meaning of encouragement and incitement for which this question was brought, similar to His saying, the Exalted: مَّن ذَا الَّذِي يُقْرِضُ اللَّهَ قَرْضًا حَسَنًا — Who is it that will lend Allah a good loan (Al-Baqarah: 245).

The prepositional phrase is linked to His saying: أنصاري (my helpers) by implying within "help" (النصرة) the meaning of traversing (السلوك) and going, or what resembles them, as narrated from Abraham (عليه السلام) in his saying: إِنِّي ذَاهِبٌ إِلَىٰ رَبِّي سَيَهْدِينِ — ‘I am going to my Lord; He will guide me’ (Al-Saffat: 99).

As for what some commentators have entertained—that "to" (إلى) means "with" (مع)—there is no evidence for it, nor does the etiquette of the Quran support making another in the same rank as Him, counting another besides Allah as a helper just as He is counted as a helper. Nor does the etiquette of Jesus (عليه السلام), which is evident from what the Quran narrates of his speech, support this. Furthermore, His saying, the Exalted: قال الحواريون نحن أنصار الله (The disciples said, ‘We will be Allah’s helpers’) also does not support it, for it would have been necessary in that case for them to say: "We are your helpers with Allah"; so let it be reflected upon.

His saying, the Exalted: قال الحواريون نحن أنصار الله آمنا بالله واشهد بأنا مسلمون (The disciples said, ‘We will be Allah’s helpers. We have faith in Allah, and bear witness that we are muslims’). The "disciples" (الحواريون) of a person are those among the people who are specifically close to him. It has been said that its origin is from hawar (الحور) — intense whiteness. The Quran has not used this term except for the elite of Jesus’s (عليه السلام) companions.

Their saying: آمنا بالله (We have faith in Allah) serves as an explanation for their saying: نحن أنصار الله (We will be Allah’s helpers). This is among what supports His saying: أنصاري إلى الله (my helpers to Allah) proceeding as an implication (تضمين), as previously mentioned, for it yields the meaning of traversing the path to Allah, and faith (الإيمان) is a path.

Was this their first faith in Jesus (عليه السلام)? It might be gathered from His saying, the Exalted: كَمَا قَالَ عِيسَى ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ لِلْحَوَارِيِّينَ مَنْ أَنصَارِي إِلَى اللَّهِ قَالَ الْحَوَارِيُّونَ نَحْنُ أَنصَارُ اللَّهِ فَآمَنَت طَّائِفَةٌ (as Jesus son of Mary said to his disciples, ‘Who will be my helpers [on the way] to Allah?’ The disciples said, ‘We will be Allah’s helpers.’ So a group... believed) (Al-Saff: 14) that it was faith after faith, and there is no harm in this, as becomes clear by referring to what we clarified regarding faith (الإيمان) and submission (الإسلام) having different levels (مراتب), some above others.

Indeed, His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذْ أَوْحَيْتُ إِلَى الْحَوَارِيِّينَ أَنْ آمِنُوا بِي وَبِرَسُولِي قَالُوا آمَنَّا وَاشْهَدْ بِأَنَّنَا مُسْلِمُونَ — And when I inspired the disciples, [saying], ‘‘Have faith in Me and My Apostle,’’ they said, ‘‘We have faith, and bear witness that we are muslims’’ (Al-Ma'idah: 111) might indicate that their response was by a revelation from Allah, the Exalted, to them, and that they were prophets. Thus, the faith with which they responded would be faith after faith.

Furthermore, their saying: وأشهد بأننا مسلمون ربنا آمنا بما أنزلت واتبعنا الرسول (and bear witness that we are muslims. Our Lord, we have faith in what You have sent down, and we follow the Apostle)—and this submission (الإسلام) is the absolute surrender (التسليم المطلق) to all that Allah, the Exalted, intends from them and in them—also indicates this. For this submission is not attained except by the sincere among the believers, not by everyone who merely bears witness to monotheism and prophethood. The explanation of this is that it has already passed in the discussion on the levels of faith and submission: that every level of faith is preceded by a level of submission, as indicated by their saying: آمنا بالله وأشهد بأنا مسلمون (We have faith in Allah, and bear witness that we are muslims), where they used the verb for faith and the attribute for submission. The first level of submission is the surrender and witnessing to the foundation of the religion in general, followed by the heart's acknowledgment (الإذعان القلبي) of this formal witnessing in general. This is followed by (and it is the second level of submission) the heart's surrender to the meaning of faith, where internal resentment and objection cease regarding all that Allah and His Messenger command; this is practical following (الاتباع العملي) in the religion. This is followed by (and it is the second level of faith) the sincerity of action and the establishment of the attribute of servitude (العبودية) in all acts and deeds. This is followed by (and it is the third level of submission) the surrender to the love of Allah and His will, such that one does not love or will anything except through Allah, and nothing occurs there except what Allah loves and wills, with no report of the servant's own love and will in himself. This is followed by (and it is the third level of faith) the prevalence of this servile surrender in all actions.

If you recall what we have mentioned, and reflect on his (عليه السلام) saying in what was narrated of his invitation: فاتقوا الله وأطيعون إن الله ربي وربكم فاعبدوه هذا صراط مستقيم (so be God-conscious and obey me. Indeed Allah is my Lord and your Lord; so worship Him. This is a straight path); the verse, you will find that he (عليه السلام) first commanded the godwariness of Allah and the obedience of himself, then justified that by saying: إن الله ربي وربكم (Indeed Allah is my Lord and your Lord), meaning: "Indeed Allah is your Lord, O assembly of the community, and the Lord of His Messenger whom He sent to you." Therefore, it is obligatory upon you to be conscious of Him through faith, and to obey me through following. In short, it is obligatory upon you to worship Him through godwariness and obedience to the Messenger—that is, faith and following. This is what is gathered from this speech. Therefore, he replaced godwariness and obedience in the justification with His saying: فاعبدوه (so worship Him). He only did that to clarify the connection of the command to Allah, due to the manifestation of the connection to Him in worship. Then he mentioned that this worship is a straight path (صراط مستقيم), making it a way that leads its traveler to Allah, the Glorified.

Then, when he perceived their faithlessness (الكفر) and the signs of despair regarding the faith of the masses became apparent, he said: "Who will be my helpers toward Allah?" Thus, he sought helpers to traverse this straight path (الصراط المستقيم) — the direct and unerring way to Allah — to which he was calling, which is servitude (العبودية), meaning godwariness (التقوى) and obedience (الإطاعة). The disciples (الحواريون) responded to him with exactly what he sought, saying: "We are the helpers of Allah." Then they mentioned what serves as an explanation for this, saying: "We have believed in Allah, and bear witness that we are muslims (مسلمون)." Their intent by "Islam" was obedience to him and following him; therefore, when they addressed their Lord in a tone of humility and refuge, mentioning what they had promised Jesus (عليه السلام), they said: "Our Lord, we have believed in what You have sent down and we have followed the Apostle." Thus, they substituted "Islam" with "following" (الاتباع), and they broadened the scope of faith (الإيمان) by conditioning it upon everything that Allah had sent down.

This indicated that they believed in all that Allah had sent down—which Jesus son of Mary had taught them of the Book, wisdom, the Torah, and the Gospel—and they followed the Apostle in that. This, as you can see, is not the first degree of faith, but rather among its highest and most sublime degrees.

They only asked Jesus (عليه السلام) to bear witness to their submission (الإسلام) and their following, rather than saying, "We believe in Allah and we are muslims," or something to that effect, so that they might have a proof when presenting their state before their Lord, as they said: "Our Lord, we have believed in what You have sent down and followed the Apostle." It is as if they said: "Our Lord, this is our state, and Your Apostle bears witness to it."

His saying, the Exalted: رَبَّنَا آمَنَّا بِمَا أَنزَلْتَ وَاتَّبَعْنَا الرَّسُولَ فَاكْتُبْنَا مَعَ الشَّاهِدِينَ — "Our Lord, we believe in what You have sent down, and we follow the Apostle, so write us down among the witnesses" (3:53). This is the speech of the disciples; the word "saying" (القول) was omitted from the phrasing to indicate the narration of the event itself, which is one of the subtle styles of the Noble Quran, as previously explained. They asked their Lord to write them among the witnesses (الشاهدين), and they predicated this upon their faith and submission together, because the Apostle's delivery of his message is only realized through his explanation of what Allah sent down to him in word and deed—that is, by his teaching the landmarks of the religion and his acting upon them. Thus, witnessing the delivery (التبليغ) only occurs by learning it from the Apostle and following him in action, until it is witnessed that he is a practitioner of what he calls toward, neither overstepping nor transgressing it.

It appears that this witnessing is what is alluded to in His saying, the Exalted: فَلَنَسْأَلَنَّ الَّذِينَ أُرْسِلَ إِلَيْهِمْ وَلَنَسْأَلَنَّ الْمُرْسَلِينَ — "We will surely question those to whom [Our messengers] were sent, and We will surely question the messengers" (7:6). This is the witnessing of the delivery. As for His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذَا سَمِعُوا مَا أُنزِلَ إِلَى الرَّسُولِ تَرَىٰ أَعْيُنَهُمْ تَفِيضُ مِنَ الدَّمْعِ مِمَّا عَرَفُوا مِنَ الْحَقِّ يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنَا آمَنَّا فَاكْتُبْنَا مَعَ الشَّاهِدِينَ — "When they hear what has been sent down to the Apostle, you see their eyes shed tears because of the truth they have recognized. They say, ‘Our Lord, we believe, so write us down among the witnesses’" (5:83), it is a witnessing to the truthfulness of the Apostle's message rather than the delivery itself, and Allah knows best.

It may be possible to infer from their saying, "so write us down among the witnesses," after asking the Apostle to bear witness to their submission, that what was requested was for Allah to write them among the witnesses of deeds (شهداء الأعمال), as is suggested by what Allah narrated in the prayer of Abraham and Ishmael (عليهما السلام): رَبَّنَا وَاجْعَلْنَا مُسْلِمَيْنِ لَكَ وَمِن ذُرِّيَّتِنَا أُمَّةً مُّسْلِمَةً لَّكَ وَأَرِنَا مَنَاسِكَنَا — "Our Lord, make us submissive to You, and [raise] from our progeny a nation submissive to You, and show us our holy rites" (2:128). One should refer to what we mentioned in the commentary on that verse.

His saying, the Exalted: وَمَكَرُوا وَمَكَرَ اللَّهُ وَاللَّهُ خَيْرُ الْمَاكِرِينَ — "Then they plotted [against Jesus], and Allah also devised, and Allah is the best of devisers" (3:54). The "devisers" (الماكرون) are the Children of Israel, based on the context of His saying: "When Jesus perceived their faithlessness." The discussion regarding the meaning of "devising" (المكر) attributed to the Exalted has already passed in the commentary on His saying: وَمَا يُضِلُّ بِهِ إِلَّا الْفَاسِقِينَ — "and He misleads by it only the transgressors" (2:26).

His saying, the Exalted: إِذْ قَالَ اللَّهُ يَا عِيسَىٰ إِنِّي مُتَوَفِّيكَ — "When Allah said, ‘O Jesus, I will take you [fully]...’" (3:55). Taking (التوفي) is to take a thing in its entirety; hence it is used for death because Allah, at the time of death, takes the human soul (نفس) from the body. The Exalted said: تَوَفَّتْهُ رُسُلُنَا — "Our messengers take him" (6:61), meaning they caused him to die. And the Exalted said: وَقَالُوا أَإِذَا ضَلَلْنَا فِي الْأَرْضِ أَإِنَّا لَفِي خَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ... قُلْ يَتَوَفَّاكُم مَّلَكُ الْمَوْتِ الَّذِي وُكِّلَ بِكُمْ — "They say, ‘When we have been lost in the dust, shall we be in a new creation?’ ... Say, ‘The angel of death, who has been given charge of you, will take you [fully]’" (32:10-11). And the Exalted said: اللَّهُ يَتَوَفَّى الْأَنفُسَ حِينَ مَوْتِهَا وَالَّتِي لَمْ تَمُتْ فِي مَنَامِهَا فَيُمْسِكُ الَّتِي قَضَىٰ عَلَيْهَا الْمَوْتَ وَيُرْسِلُ الْأُخْرَىٰ — "Allah takes the souls at the time of their death, and those who have not died, in their sleep. Then He retains those for whom He has decided death and releases the others" (39:42).

Contemplation of the latter two verses reveals that "taking" (التوفي) is not used in the Quran to mean death itself, but rather with the connotation of taking and preserving. In other words, "taking" is used for what occurs at the time of death in terms of "taking" to indicate that the human soul does not perish or vanish with death—which the ignorant person thinks is annihilation—but rather Allah preserves it until He resurrects it to return to Him. Otherwise, the Exalted expresses Himself in instances where this connotation does not apply using the word "death" (الموت) rather than "taking" (التوفي), as in His saying: مَا مُحَمَّدٌ إِلَّا رَسُولٌ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِن قَبْلِهِ الرُّسُلُ أَفَإِن مَّاتَ أَوْ قُتِلَ انقَلَبْتُمْ عَلَىٰ أَعْقَابِكُمْ — "Muhammad is but an apostle; [other] apostles have passed before him. If he dies or is slain, will you turn back on your heels?" (3:144), and His saying: لَا يُقْضَىٰ عَلَيْهِمْ فَيَمُوتُوا — "it shall not be finished with them so that they might die" (35:36), among many other verses. This even includes what was mentioned regarding Jesus (عليه السلام) himself, such as His saying: وَالسَّلَامُ عَلَيَّ يَوْمَ وُلِدتُّ وَيَوْمَ أَمُوتُ وَيَوْمَ أُبْعَثُ حَيًّا — "Peace be to me the day I was born, and the day I die, and the day I am raised alive" (19:33), and His saying: وَإِن مِّنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتابِ إِلَّا لَيُؤْمِنَنَّ بِهِ قَبْلَ مَوْتِهِ وَيَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ يَكُونُ عَلَيْهِمْ شَهِيدًا — "There is none among the People of the Book but will surely believe in him before his death; and on the Day of Resurrection he will be a witness against them" (4:159). From this perspective, "taking" (التوفي) is not explicit in meaning death.

Furthermore, His saying, the Exalted, in refuting the claim of the Jews: وَقَوْلِهِمْ إِنَّا قَتَلْنَا الْمَسِيحَ عِيسَى ابْنَ مَرْيَمَ رَسُولَ اللَّهِ وَمَا قَتَلُوهُ وَمَا صَلَبُوهُ وَلَٰكِن شُبِّهَ لَهُمْ وَإِنَّ الَّذِينَ اخْتَلَفُوا فِيهِ لَفِي شَكٍّ مِّنْهُ مَا لَهُم بِهِ مِنْ عِلْمٍ إِلَّا اتِّباعَ الظَّنِّ وَمَا قَتَلُوهُ يَقِينًا بَل رَّفَعَهُ اللَّهُ إِلَيْهِ وَكَانَ اللَّهُ عَزِيزًا حَكِيمًا وَإِن مِّنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتَابِ إِلَّا لَيُؤْمِنَنَّ بِهِ قَبْلَ مَوْتِهِ وَيَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ يَكُونُ عَلَيْهِمْ شَهِيدًا — "and for their saying, ‘We killed the Messiah, Jesus son of Mary, the apostle of Allah’—though they did not kill him nor did they crucify him, but so it was made to appear to them. Indeed those who differ concerning him are surely in doubt about him: they do not have any knowledge of that beyond following conjectures, and they certainly did not kill him. Rather, Allah raised him up toward Himself, and Allah is all-mighty, all-wise. There is none among the People of the Book but will surely believe in him before his death; and on the Day of Resurrection he will be a witness against them" (4:157-159). This supports that view, for the Jews claimed they killed the Messiah, Jesus son of Mary (عليه السلام), and the Christians likewise thought the Jews killed Jesus son of Mary (عليه السلام) by crucifixion, except they believed that Allah raised him from his grave to heaven after his killing, according to the Gospels. The verses, as you see, explicitly deny the story of the killing and crucifixion.

What the apparent meaning of His saying, "There is none among the People of the Book..." provides is that he is alive with Allah and shall not die until the People of the Book believe in him. Accordingly, his "taking" (توفيه) (عليه السلام) would be his being taken from among the Jews. However, the verse is still not explicit in this; it is merely the apparent meaning (الظهور). The full discussion on this will come at the end of Surah al-Nisa.

His saying, the Exalted: وَرَافِعُكَ إِلَيَّ وَمُطَهِّرُكَ مِنَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا — "...and raise you up toward Myself, and purify you from those who are faithless" (3:55). Raising (الرفع) is the opposite of lowering (الوضع), and purity (الطهارة) is the opposite of filth (القذارة). The discussion on the meaning of purity has already passed.

Since the "raising" was restricted by His saying, "toward Myself" (إلي), it indicates that what is meant is a spiritual raising (الرفع المعنوي) rather than a physical raising (الرفع الصوري), as the Exalted has no place of the same nature as physical places (الأمكنة الجسمانية) which bodies and physical entities occupy by indwelling (الحلول) or by being near or far from them. Thus, it is of the same nature as His saying at the end of the verse: "Then to Me is your return." This is especially true if "taking" (التوفي) is meant as seizing (القبض), for it is then apparent that what is meant is the raising of rank and proximity to Allah, the Glorified, similar to what He mentioned regarding those killed in His way: أَحْيَاءٌ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ — "alive with their Lord" (3:169), and what He mentioned regarding Idris (عليه السلام): وَرَفَعْنَاهُ مَكَانًا عَلِيًّا — "And We raised him to a station sublime" (19:57).

It is sometimes said that what is meant by raising him toward Himself is raising him with his soul and body alive to the heaven, as the apparent meaning of the Noble Quran suggests that the heaven—meaning the physical one—is the station of proximity to Allah, the Glorified, the place of the descent of blessings, and the dwelling of the honored angels. Perhaps we shall be successful in discussing the meaning of "heaven" (السماء) in what is to come, if Allah wills.

The "purification from the faithless," since it followed the "raising toward Allah," indicates a spiritual purification rather than a physical or apparent one. It is his being distanced from the disbelievers and protected from mixing with them or falling into their society, which is defiled by the filth of disbelief and denial.

His saying, the Exalted: وَجَاعِلُ الَّذِينَ اتَّبَعُوكَ فَوْقَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ الْقِيَامَةِ — "...and make those who follow you above those who are faithless until the Day of Resurrection" (3:55). This is a promise from Him to him (عليه السلام) that He will make the followers of Jesus (عليه السلام) superior to his opponents who disbelieved in his prophethood, and that this superiority will last until the Day of Resurrection. In defining those who are superior to others, the Exalted only mentioned that the superior ones are "those who follow you" and that the others are "those who are faithless," without saying they are the Children of Israel, or the Jews who adhere to the law of Moses (عليه السلام), or otherwise.

However, since the Exalted used "disbelief" (الكفر) to define his opponents, it becomes clear that what is meant by "following him" is following based on truth—that is, the following pleasing to Allah. Thus, those who followed him would be his upright followers among the Christians before the appearance of Islam and its abrogation of the religion of Jesus, and the Muslims after the appearance of Islam, for they are his followers upon the truth. Accordingly, the "superiority" (التفوق) would be superiority in terms of proof (الحجة) rather than sovereignty and dominion. The summary of the meaning of the sentence would be: your followers among the Christians and Muslims will have their proof prevail over the proof of those who disbelieve in you among the Jews until the Day of Resurrection. This is what the commentators have mentioned and accepted regarding the meaning of the verse.

In my view, the verse does not support this, neither in its wording nor its meaning. The apparent sense of His saying, "I will take you, and raise you toward Myself, and purify you from those who are faithless, and make those who follow you..." is an announcement of the future; that a "taking," "raising," "purification," and "making" will be realized in the future relative to the time of speaking. Furthermore, His saying, "and make those who follow you," is a good promise and glad tidings, and such things only pertain to what is to come. It is well known that the proof of the followers of Jesus (عليه السلام) is nothing but the proof of Jesus himself, which Allah mentioned within the verses of the glad tidings—the glad tidings to Mary. These proofs were superior during the presence of Jesus before the raising, and after his raising; in fact, before his raising (عليه السلام), they were more decisive in cutting off the excuses of the disbelievers and uprooting their contention, and clearer in removing their doubts. So what is the meaning of promising him (عليه السلام) that the proof of his followers will surpass the proof of his opponents in the future? Furthermore, what is the meaning of restricting this victory and superiority with His saying, "until the Day of Resurrection," when proof, in its victory, does not accept restriction by time or day? Moreover, the superiority of proof over proof remains as it is on the Day of Resurrection, as the Quran informs within the reports of the Resurrection.

If you say: Perhaps what is meant by the superiority of the proof is its superiority in terms of acceptability, such that people are more attentive to the proof of the followers and more obedient to it, thereby making them greater in number, firmer in pillar, and stronger in power.

I say: This returns either to the superiority of his true followers in terms of sovereignty and power—and the reality is contrary to that, and the possibility of it being a report of a manifestation for the followers and a superiority from them that will be realized at the end of time is not supported by the wording of the verse—or it returns to the greatness of number, meaning that his followers (عليه السلام) will surpass the disbelievers, i.e., the people of truth after Jesus will be more numerous than the people of falsehood. In addition to the fact that reality does not support this—for the people of falsehood have never ceased to grow and increase over the people of truth from the time of Jesus until our day, a gap of twenty centuries—the wording of the verse does not support it either. Superiority (الفوقية) in the verse, especially since the context is one of announcing the descent of divine displeasure upon the Jews and the encompassing of wrath upon them, only suits subjugation and dominance: either in terms of the conclusive proof (الحجة البالغة) or in terms of authority and power. As for greatness of number, it does not suit the context, as is apparent.

What ought to be said is that what was taken in the verse as a definition for the two groups is His saying, "those who follow you" and His saying, "those who are faithless." The verb only indicates realization and occurrence, rather than the state of being characterized (التلبس) which is indicated by the description (الوصف), such as "the followers" (المتبعين) and "the disbelievers" (الكافرين). The mere issuance of an act from some individuals of a nation, with the approval of the rest and the subsequent generations following the path of the predecessors and proceeding upon their way, is sufficient to attribute that act to them. Similarly, the Quran rebukes the Jews and censures them for many of the acts of their predecessors, such as killing the prophets, harming them, being arrogant toward obeying the commands of Allah and His messengers, and distorting the verses of the Book, and so on.

Accordingly, it is correct that "those who are faithless" refers to the Jews, and "those who followed" refers to the Christians, because of what issued from their leaders and predecessors in terms of faith in Jesus (عليه السلام) and following him—and it was a pleasing faith and a true following—even if Allah did not approve of their following him (عليه السلام) after the appearance of Islam, nor the following of the people of the Trinity among them before the appearance of the Islamic call.

Thus, the intent is making the Christians—who are those whose predecessors followed Jesus (عليه السلام)—above the Jews, who are those who disbelieved in Jesus (عليه السلام) and plotted against him. The purpose in this context is to explain the descent of divine displeasure upon the Jews, the occurrence of the "devising" against them, and the intensification of punishment upon their nation. This does not contradict what we mentioned—that the intent of "following" is following upon the truth—as we inferred at the beginning of the discussion, as is not hidden.

This meaning is supported by the change of style in the subsequent verse, namely His saying: "As for those who have faith and do righteous deeds..." For if the intent of "those who followed" were only the people of truth and salvation among the Christians and Muslims, it would have been more appropriate to say: "As for those who followed you, He will pay them their rewards in full," without changing the context, as is not hidden.

There is another view: that the intent of "those who followed" is the Christians and Muslims collectively, and the verse is informing that the Jews will be under the humiliation of whoever acknowledges the necessity of following Jesus until the Day of Resurrection. The reasoning is the same, and this is the best of views in directing the verse upon reflection.

His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ إِلَيَّ مَرْجِعُكُمْ فَأَحْكُمُ بَيْنَكُمْ فِيمَا كُنتُمْ فِيهِ تَخْتَلِفُونَ — "Then to Me will be your return, and I will judge between you concerning that about which you used to differ" (3:55). The Glorified has gathered in this address Jesus, those who followed him, and those who disbelieved in him. This is the ultimate end of their affair on the Day of Resurrection. With this, the matter of Jesus and his report is concluded, from the time of the glad tidings of him until the end of his affair and news.

His saying, the Exalted: فَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فَأُعَذِّبُهُمْ عَذَابًا شَدِيدًا فِي الدُّنْيَا وَالْآخِرَةِ — "As for the faithless, I will punish them with a severe punishment in this world and the Hereafter" (3:56). Its apparent sense is that it is predicated upon His saying, "and I will judge between you," as the branching of detail from the general. Thus, it would be an explanation of the divine judgment on the Day of Resurrection regarding the punishment for the Jews who disbelieved and the full payment of reward for the believers.

However, the inclusion of "in this world" (في الدنيا) in the branching indicates that it is predicated upon the entirety of His saying: "and make those who follow you above those who are faithless... then to Me is your return," etc. Thus, it indicates that the result of this "making" and "return" is the intensification of punishment upon them in this world at the hands of those whom Allah made superior to them, and in the Hereafter by the Fire, and they shall have no helpers in that.

This is one of the evidences that the intent of "superiority" (التفوق) in the previous verse is dominance through control and power, rather than support through proof.

In His saying, "and they shall have no helpers," there is an indication of the negation of intercession (الشفاعة) — the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah — that would prevent the descent of punishment upon them; it is a finalized decree, as previously mentioned.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ فَيُوَفِّيهِمْ أُجُورَهُمْ — "But as for those who have faith and do righteous deeds, He will pay them their rewards in full" (3:57). This is a good promise of fair recompense for those who followed. However, since the mere truth of "following" does not necessarily entail the entitlement to a great reward—because "following," as you have known, is a description true of the nation by its mere realization and issuance from a number of its individuals—it only produces a beautiful effect and a great reward for the one who is personally characterized by it, rather than the one who is merely attributed to it by name. Therefore, He substituted "those who followed you" with the like of His saying: "those who have faith and do righteous deeds," so that the meaning remains sound. For felicity and the good end revolve around reality rather than the name, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَالَّذِينَ هَادُوا وَالنَّصَارَىٰ وَالصَّابِئِينَ مَنْ آمَنَ بِاللَّهِ وَالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ وَعَمِلَ صَالِحًا فَلَهُمْ أَجْرُهُمْ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ وَلَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ — "Indeed the faithful, the Jews, the Christians and the Sabaeans—those of them who have faith in Allah and the Last Day and act righteously—they shall have their reward from their Lord, and they will have no fear, nor will they grieve" (2:62).

This is the reward of those who have faith and do righteous deeds among those who followed Jesus (عليه السلام): that Allah will pay them their rewards in full. As for others, they have nothing of that, and this was alluded to in the verse by His saying: "and Allah does not like the wrongdoers."

From here, the secret becomes apparent in concluding the verse—which is a verse of mercy and Paradise—with the like of His saying: "and Allah does not like the wrongdoers," even though it is customary in verses of mercy and blessing to conclude with names of mercy and forgiveness, or with praise for the state of those in whose favor the verse was revealed, similar to His saying, the Exalted: وَكُلًّا وَعَدَ اللَّهُ الْحُسْنَىٰ وَاللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ — "To each Allah has promised the best [reward], and Allah is well aware of what you do" (57:10), and His saying: إِن تُقْرِضُوا اللَّهَ قَرْضًا حَسَنًا يُضَاعِفْهُ لَكُمْ وَيَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ وَاللَّهُ شَكُورٌ حَلِيمٌ — "If you lend Allah a good loan, He will multiply it for you and forgive you, and Allah is all-appreciative, all-forbearing" (64:17), and His saying: وَمَن يُؤْمِن بِاللَّهِ وَيَعْمَلْ صَالِحًا يُكَفِّرْ عَنْهُ سَيِّئَاتِهِ وَيُدْخِلْهُ جَنَّاتٍ تَجْرِي مِن تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهَارُ خَالِدِينَ فِيهَا أَبَدًا ذَٰلِكَ الْفَوْزُ الْعَظِيمُ — "Whoever has faith in Allah and acts righteously, He will absolve him of his misdeeds and admit him into gardens with conduits running through them, to remain in them forever. That is the great triumph" (64:9), and His saying: فَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ فَيُدْخِلُهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ فِي رَحْمَتِهِ ذَٰلِكَ هُوَ الْفَوْزُ الْمُبِينُ — "As for those who have faith and do righteous deeds, their Lord will admit them into His mercy. That is the manifest triumph" (45:30), among other verses.

Thus, His saying, "and Allah does not like the wrongdoers," is intended to explain the state of the other group among those who attributed themselves to Jesus (عليه السلام) by following, but who are not those who have faith and do righteous deeds.

His saying, the Exalted: ذَٰلِكَ نَتْلُوهُ عَلَيْكَ مِنَ الْآيَاتِ وَالذِّكْرِ الْحَكِيمِ — "This which We recite to you is of the signs and the Wise Remembrance" (3:58). This is an indication of the conclusion of the story. What is meant by the Wise Remembrance (الذكر الحكيم) is the Quran, which is a remembrance of Allah, firm (محكم) in its verses and explanations; falsehood does not enter it, nor does jest penetrate it.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ مَثَلَ عِيسَىٰ عِندَ اللَّهِ كَمَثَلِ آدَمَ خَلَقَهُ مِن تُرَابٍ ثُمَّ قَالَ لَهُ كُن فَيَكُونُ — "Indeed the case of Jesus with Allah is like the case of Adam: He created him from dust, then said to him, ‘Be,’ and he was" (3:59). This is a summary of the point of necessity from what He mentioned of the story of Jesus regarding his birth in detail. Conciseness after prolixity—especially in the context of argumentation and reasoning—is among the merits of speech. The verses were revealed for argumentation and addressed the state of the Christian delegation, the Christians of Najran. It was most appropriate to summarize the explanation of his creation after the detailed account of his story, to indicate that the manner of his birth does not indicate more than his being a created human, similar to Adam (عليهما السلام). Thus, it is not permissible to say regarding him more or greater than what was said regarding Adam, which is that he is a human whom Allah created without a father.

The meaning of the verse is: the likeness of Jesus with Allah—that is, his description existing with the Exalted, meaning what Allah knows of the manner of Jesus's creation which proceeded by His hand—is that the manner of his creation resembles the manner of Adam's creation. The manner of his creation was that He gathered his parts from dust, then said to him "Be," and he became a human becoming without a father.
