Accordingly, His saying: 'Say, by the grace of Allah'—'and were it not for the grace of Allah...'" End quote.

Accordingly, His saying: "Indeed grace is in the hand of Allah" is a form of conciseness by sufficing with the major premise of the syllogistic exposition. The estimation is: Say, indeed this revelation and divine granting—which you plot to restrict to yourselves by feigning belief and disbelief, and by advising the concealment of the matter—is not something we, the assembly of people, deserve from Allah; rather, it is from grace. Grace is in the hand of Allah, to whom belongs the kingdom and the judgment; so He has the right to give it to whom He wills, and Allah is all-bounteous, all-knowing.

In the speech, there is a negation of what their words and actions indicate regarding the restriction of divine blessing to themselves in all its possible aspects. For the blessing of some people with Allah's grace to the exclusion of others—like the Jews being blessed with the religion and the qiblah and others being deprived—would either be because grace from Him, the Exalted, can fall under the influence of another, thereby interfering with the divine will, withholding His grace from one side and directing it to another; but it is not so, for grace is in the hand of Allah, He gives it to whom He wills.

Or it would be because grace is little and insufficient, while those to be graced are many; thus, its granting to some to the exclusion of others would require the addition of a preponderant factor (مرجح), so one plots to establish a factor to restrict it to the part that is blessed; but it is not so, for Allah, the Glorified, is all-bounteous (واسع) in grace and power.

Or it would be because grace, though bounteous and in Allah's hand, the one to be graced could be hidden from Him, the Exalted, due to ignorance on His part, so he does not attain the grace; thus, one plots to hide him and veil his state from Him, the Exalted, so he is deprived of His grace; but it is not so, for Allah, the Glorified, is all-knowing (عليم), and no ignorance occurs to Him.

His saying, the Exalted: يختص برحمته من يشاء والله ذو الفضل العظيم — "He singles out for His mercy whomever He wishes, and Allah is the dispenser of a great grace" (3:74): Since grace is in the hand of Allah, He gives it to whom He wills, and He is all-bounteous and all-knowing, it is possible for Him to single out some of His servants for some of His blessings. For He has the right to dispose of His kingdom as He wills. It is not the case that if He is not prevented from disposing of His grace and giving it to His servants, He must give all His grace to everyone; for this too would be a kind of restriction in disposal. Rather, He has the right to single out for His grace whom He wills.

He concluded the speech with His saying: "and Allah is the dispenser of a great grace," which is in the position of a reason for all the preceding meanings. For the necessity of the absolute greatness of grace is that it be in His hand, He gives it to whom He wills; that He be all-bounteous in His grace; that He be all-knowing of the state of His servants and what grace is suitable for their state; and that He have the right to single out for His grace whom He wills.

In the substitution of "grace" with "mercy" (الرحمة) in His saying: "He singles out for His mercy whomever He wishes," there is an indication that grace—which is the non-obligatory gift—is a branch of mercy. The Exalted said: وَرَحْمَتِي وَسِعَتْ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ — "...but My mercy embraces all things" (7:156). And He said: وَلَوْلَا فَضْلُ اللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ وَرَحْمَتُهُ مَا زَكَىٰ مِنكُم مِّنْ أَحَدٍ أَبَدًا — "Were it not for Allah’s grace and His mercy upon you, not one of you would ever be pure" (24:21). And the Exalted said: قُل لَّوْ أَنتُمْ تَمْلِكُونَ خَزَائِنَ رَحْمَةِ رَبِّي إِذًا لَّأَمْسَكْتُمْ خَشْيَةَ الْإِنفَاقِ — "Say, ‘If you possessed the treasuries of my Lord’s mercy, you would surely withhold [them] for fear of spending’" (17:100).

His saying, the Exalted: ومن أهل الكتاب من إن تأمنه بقنطار يؤده إليك — "Among the People of the Book is he who, if you entrust him with a quintal, will restore it to you..." (3:75) until His saying: "...any way [to blame us]": This is an indication of their vast difference in the preservation of trusts and covenants, a difference that reaches the extremes of contradiction. Although this is in itself a harmful national vice, it arose among them and spread in their community from another doctrinal vice, which is what their saying contains: "There is no way [to blame us] concerning the unlettered (الاميين)." For they used to call themselves "the People of the Book" and others "the unlettered." Their saying: "There is no way [to blame us] concerning the unlettered" means the negation of any right for a non-Israelite over an Israelite. They attributed this word to the religion, and the evidence for it is His saying, the Exalted: ويقولون علی الله الكذب وهم يعلمون بلى — "...and they tell lies against Allah while they know. Yes..." (3:75-76).

They used to imagine—as they do today in their imagination—that they are the ones specifically chosen for divine honor, which does not pass from them to others, since Allah, the Glorified, placed among them prophethood, a book, and kingship. Thus, they have sovereignty and precedence over others. They concluded from this that the rights legislated among them, which they are required to observe—such as the prohibition of taking usury, consuming the wealth of others, and encroaching upon people's rights—are only among themselves, the assembly of the People of the Book. Thus, what is forbidden is an Israelite consuming the wealth of another like him, and what is prohibited is encroaching upon the rights of a Jew by one of his own faith. In short, the "way" (السبيل) is only for the People of the Book against the People of the Book; as for those other than the People of the Book, they have no "way" against the People of the Book. Thus, they may judge others as they wish and do to those below them as they desire. This leads to treating others as if they were dumb animals, whoever they may be.

Although this is not found in the books they possess that are attributed to revelation, such as the Torah and others, it is a matter they took from the mouths of their rabbis and followed them in it. Then, since the Mosaic religion did not pass beyond the Children of Israel to others, they made it a nationality among them. From this was born the idea that this honor and sovereignty is a national matter specifically for the Children of Israel. Thus, Israelite lineage is the substance of honor and the element of sovereignty, and the one related to Israel has absolute precedence over others. This transgressive spirit, when it creeps into the mold of a people, drives them to corrupt the earth and kill the human spirit and its governing effects in human society.

Yes, the root of this word—which is the stripping of general rights from some individuals and communities—is something unavoidable in human society. However, what the righteous human society considers is the stripping of rights from those who wish to nullify rights and destroy society. What Islam considers in the establishment of rights is the religion of monotheism, whether through Islam or the status of protected minority (الذمة). Thus, he who has neither Islam nor dhimmah has no right to life, which corresponds to the primordial law (الناموس الفطري) that you heard is considered in general by human society.

Let us return to our discussion of the verse. His saying, the Exalted: "And among the People of the Book" (ومن أهل الكتاب)—it would have been apparent to say: "And among them" (ومنهم). This is a case of placing the explicit noun in the place of the pronoun, and the reason for it is to prevent the illusion that these are some of the group mentioned in the two previous verses who said: "Believe in what has been sent down..." Therefore, when the mentioned illusion was dispelled, it was said in the following verse: وإن منهم لفريقاً يلوون ألسنتهم بالكتاب — "And there is indeed a group of them who twist their tongues with the Book..." (3:78).

There is another perspective, which is that mentioning the description—namely, their being from the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب)—suggests a kind of causal explanation (تعليل). This is because the issuance of such a statement and action from them—meaning their saying: "There is no way against us concerning the unlettered (الاميين)," and their consuming people's wealth based on that—would not have been so far-fetched or strange had they been unlettered (اميين) with no knowledge of prophecy or revelation. However, they are the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب), and they possess the Book containing the judgment of Allah (حكم الله), and they know that the Book does not judge in their favor in this regard, nor does it permit them the wealth of others simply because they are "others." Thus, what they said and subsequently did, while being the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب), is stranger and more far-fetched coming from them, and the rebuke and condemnation against them are more appropriate and binding.

The qintar (القنطار) — a large weight of gold or wealth — and the dinar (الدينار) — a single gold coin — are well-known. The contrast between them—notwithstanding the rhetorical beauty (المحسنات البديعية) it contains—in a context where trust (الأمانة) is mentioned, indicates that He used them as metaphors for a large amount and a small amount. The meaning is that among them are those who do not betray a trust even if it is vast and of heavy value, and among them are those who betray it even if it is meager and light.

Similarly, the address employed in the speech, in His saying: "if you entrust him with a qintar, he will pay it back to you," is not directed at a specific addressee. Rather, it is a metaphorical use (تكنية) for any addressee who could potentially be addressed with this speech, to indicate that the ruling is general and not restricted to one person over another. The speech carries the meaning of our saying: "If any trustee (مؤتمن), whoever he may be, entrusts him with a qintar, he will pay it back to him."

The particle ma (ما) in His saying: "unless you keep standing over him," is a gerundive particle (مصدرية) according to what has been said, and the estimation is: "except for your continuous standing over him." The mention of "standing over him" (القيام عليه) is to indicate persistence and urgency, for the claimant's standing on his feet during the demand without sitting down is evidence of that. It has been suggested that ma is adverbial (ظرفية), but that is of no consequence.

Regarding His saying: ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ قَالُوا لَيْسَ عَلَيْنَا فِي الْأُمِّيِّينَ سَبِيلٌ "That is because they say, ‘We have no obligation toward the gentiles,’" the apparent context is that "that" (ذلك) refers to the collective content derived from the preceding statement. That is, the fact that some of them fulfill the trust even if it is significant and important, while some do not fulfill it even if it is contemptible and disregarded, is only because of their saying: "There is no way against us concerning the unlettered (الاميين)." This necessitated a divergence among them in spiritual qualities, such as preserving trusts and guarding against wasting people's rights, and being deluded by a sense of nobility, despite their knowing that Allah did not legislate that for them in the Book, nor did He approve of such actions from them.

It is possible that "that" (ذلك) refers to the state of the second group mentioned in His saying: "and among them is he who, if you entrust him with a dinar, will not pay it back to you." In this case, the mention of the first, trustworthy group would be to complete all categories and to maintain fairness (النصفة). It is then permissible for the plural pronouns in His saying: "and they say" (ويقولون) and in His saying: "and they know" (وهم يعلمون) to refer back to the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب), or to refer back to His saying: "he who, if you entrust him with a dinar," according to the meaning. Likewise, under the second possibility, it is permissible for the first-person pronoun in His saying: "against us" (علينا) to refer to all the People of the Book or specifically to some of them. The meaning varies according to these possibilities, but all of them are correct and sound; you should reflect upon them.

His saying, the Exalted: وَيَقُولُونَ عَلَى اللَّهِ الْكَذِبَ وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ "and they attribute lies to Allah, and they know [it]" is a refutation of their claim that "there is no way against us concerning the unlettered," and evidence that they used to attribute that to heavenly revelation and religious legislation, as previously mentioned.

His saying, the Exalted: بَلَىٰ مَنْ أَوْفَىٰ بِعَهْدِهِ وَاتَّقَىٰ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ يُحِبُّ الْمُتَّقِينَ "Yes, whoever fulfills his pledges and is God-conscious—Allah indeed loves the God-conscious" is a rebuttal of their words and an affirmation of what they denied by their saying: "There is no way against us concerning the unlettered." Fulfilling the pledge (إيفاء العهد) is to complete it by guarding against excuses and deficiency. Tawfiyah (التوفية) is to bestow and give in full, and istifa’ (الاستيفاء) is to take and receive in full.

What is meant by the pledge (العهد) is the covenant (الميثاق) that Allah took from His servants that they should believe in Him and worship Him, as suggested by His saying in the following verse: "Indeed those who sell the covenant of Allah and their oaths for a small price." Or it refers to the absolute pledge (العهد), of which the pledge of Allah (عهد الله) is a part.

His saying: "for Allah indeed loves the God-conscious (المتقين)" is an instance of placing the major premise (الكبرى) in the position of the minor premise (الصغرى) for the sake of conciseness. The estimation is: "for Allah loves him because he is God-conscious (متق), and Allah loves the God-conscious (المتقين)." The meaning is that the honor (الكرامة) from Allah for His God-conscious servants is His love for them, not what you claimed regarding the "negation of the way" (نفي السبيل).

The import of the speech is that divine honor (الكرامة الإلهية) is not something cheap or easily attained such that anyone who has a mere affiliation to Him can reach it, or that every deceiver or arrogant person can consider it a racial or national honor. Rather, the condition for attaining it is the fulfillment of Allah's pledge and covenant, and godwariness (التقوى) in religion. When the conditions are met, the honor is achieved, which is the divine love and guardianship (الولاية الإلهية) that does not bypass His God-conscious servants. Its effect is divine assistance and a felicitous life (الحيوة السعيدة) that prospers the world, rectifies the state of its people, and raises the degrees of the Hereafter.

This is the divine honor; it is not that He should carry one people upon the shoulders of His other servants—righteous and wicked alike—and set them free to do as they wish and act as they please, so they say one day: "There is no way against us concerning the unlettered," and another day: "We are the friends of Allah to the exclusion of all people," and another day: "We are the sons of Allah and His beloved ones," leading them thereby to spread corruption on earth and destroy crops and progeny.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَشْتَرُونَ بِعَهْدِ اللَّهِ وَأَيْمَانِهِمْ ثَمَنًا قَلِيلًا "Indeed those who sell the covenant of Allah and their oaths for a small price" is a causal explanation (تعليل) for the ruling mentioned in the previous verse. The meaning is that divine honor is exclusive to those who fulfill their pledge and are God-conscious, because others—those who sell the covenant of Allah and their oaths for a small price—have no honor.

Since the breaking of Allah's covenant and the abandonment of godwariness (التقوى) are only for the sake of enjoying the vanities of the world and preferring the desires of the first life over the last, it involved placing the enjoyment of the world in the place of fulfilling the pledge and godwariness, and exchanging the pledge for it. Therefore, He likened their action to a transaction, making the covenant of Allah the commodity sold for enjoyment, and He called the enjoyment of the world—which is meager—a "small price" (ثمناً قليلاً). "Buying" (الاشتراء) here is selling (البيع), so it was said: "they buy a small price with the covenant of Allah and their oaths," meaning they exchange the covenant and oaths for the enjoyment of the world.

His saying, the Exalted: أُولَٰئِكَ لَا خَلَاقَ لَهُمْ فِي الْآخِرَةِ وَلَا يُكَلِّمُهُمُ اللَّهُ "they shall have no share in the Hereafter, and Allah will not speak to them..." to the end of the verse.

Khalaq (الخلاق) is the portion or share (النصيب). Tazkiyah (التزكية) — purification — is to cause something to grow in a wholesome manner. Since the description used to define this group of people is the opposite of the description used for the other group mentioned in His saying: "whoever fulfills his pledge and is God-conscious," and since the consequences mentioned for their description were negative matters, this implies:

First: That the use of the demonstrative pronoun ula’ika (اولئك) — those — which indicates distance, is to signify the distance of these people from the Presence of Proximity, just as those who fulfill their pledge and are God-conscious are brought near (مقربون) due to Allah's love for them.

Second: That the effects of the love of Allah, the Glorified, are the share (الخلاق) in the Hereafter, the speaking (التكليم) and the looking (النظر) on the Day of Resurrection, and the purification (التزكية) and forgiveness, which is the removal of the painful punishment.

The traits that Allah mentioned for these breakers of Allah's covenant and their oaths are three:

One: That they have no share (نصيب) in the Hereafter. What is meant by the Hereafter (الآخرة) is the Abode of the Hereafter (الدار الآخرة) — the description standing in place of the thing described — meaning the life after death, just as the world (الدنياً) refers to the Abode of the World (الدار الدنيا), which is the worldly life before death.

The negation of a share for them in the Hereafter is due to their choosing the share of the world over it. From here it becomes clear that what is meant by the "small price" (الثمن القليل) is the world. We previously interpreted it as the "enjoyment of the world" because of His describing it as "small," and He described the enjoyment of the world as such in the saying of the Mightiest of Speakers: قُلْ مَتَاعُ الدُّنْيَا قَلِيلٌ "Say, ‘The enjoyment of this world is little’" (4:77); moreover, the enjoyment of the world is the world.

Two: That Allah will not speak to them nor look at them on the Day of Resurrection. This has been set in parallel to the divine love for the God-conscious, insofar as love necessitates the lover taking delight in the beloved by freely looking and speaking during presence and union. Since Allah does not love them, He will not speak to them nor look at them on the Day of Resurrection, which is the day of summoning and presence. The progression from speaking to looking is due to the presence of strength and weakness between them, for freedom in speaking is more than in looking; it is as if it were said: "We shall not honor them, neither much nor little."

Three: That Allah will not purify them and they shall have a painful punishment. The absolute nature of the speech implies that what is meant by both is that which encompasses purification and punishment in both this world and the Hereafter.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنَّ مِنْهُمْ لَفَرِيقًا يَلْوُونَ أَلْسِنَتَهُمْ بِالْكِتَابِ لِتَحْسَبُوهُ مِنَ الْكِتَابِ وَمَا هُوَ مِنَ الْكِتَابِ "And there is a group of them who twist their tongues while [reading] the Book, so that you may think it is from the Book, but it is not from the Book." Layy (الليّ) is the twisting of a rope; the twisting of the head or tongue is to incline them. The Exalted said: لَوَّوْا رُءُوسَهُمْ "they twist their heads" (63:5), and He said: لَيًّا بِأَلْسِنَتِهِمْ "twisting with their tongues" (4:46). It appears that what is meant by this is that they read the discourse they have fabricated against Allah, the Glorified, with the same melodies and intonations with which they read the Book, to deceive people into thinking it is from the Book, while it is not from the Book.

The repetition of the word "the Book" (الكتاب) three times in the speech is to prevent confusion. What is meant by the first "Book" is that which they wrote with their own hands and attributed to Allah, the Glorified. By the second is the Book that Allah sent down through revelation. By the third is the second one; its mention was repeated to prevent confusion and to indicate that the Book, insofar as it is the Book of Allah, is of a status too high to contain such fabrications. This is due to the meaning of the description in the word "the Book" which suggests causality.

Similar to this is the repetition of the Majestic Name (لفظ الجلالة) in His saying: وَيَقُولُونَ هُوَ مِنْ عِنْدِ اللَّهِ وَمَا هُوَ مِنْ عِنْدِ اللَّهِ "and they say, ‘It is from Allah,’ but it is not from Allah." The meaning is: "and it is not from Allah, who is truly God and says nothing but the truth." The Exalted said: وَالْحَقَّ أَقُولُ "and I speak the truth" (38:84).

As for His saying: وَيَقُولُونَ عَلَى اللَّهِ الْكَذِبَ وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ "and they attribute lies to Allah, and they know [it]," it is a refutation after a refutation of their attributing their fabricated revelation to Allah, the Glorified. They used to obscure the matter for people through the "twisting of speech" (لحن القول), so Allah nullified it by His saying: "but it is not from the Book." Then they used to say with their tongues: "It is from Allah," so Allah belied them: first, by His saying: "but it is not from Allah," and second, by His saying: "and they attribute lies to Allah." He added to the benefit, first, that lying is their habit and custom, and second, that this is not a lie issued from them due to a confusion in the matter, but rather they are knowledgeable of it and intentional in it.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, regarding His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ يَا أَهْلَ الْكِتَابِ تَعَالَوْا إِلَىٰ كَلِمَةٍ سَوَاءٍ "Say, ‘O People of the Book! Come to a common word...’" [3:64], it is narrated—meaning by Ibn Jarir—from al-Suddi, who said: "Then the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم called them—meaning the delegation of the Christians of Najran—and said: 'O People of the Book! Come to a common word...'"

I say: This meaning is also narrated therein from Ibn Jarir from Muhammad ibn Ja'far ibn al-Zubayr. The apparent sense of the narration is that the verse was revealed concerning them. We have already presented the narration at the beginning of the Surah indicating that the beginning of the Surah, up to eighty-odd verses, was revealed concerning the Christians of Najran, and this verse is among them as it occurs before the completion of that number.

It has been mentioned in some narrations that the Messenger of Allah called the Jews of Medina to the "common word" until they accepted the jizyah (الجزية) — the protection tax paid by non-Muslims under Islamic rule — and that does not contradict the verse being revealed concerning the delegation of Najran.

In Sahih al-Bukhari, with its chain of transmission from Ibn Abbas from Abu Sufyan in a long tradition in which he mentions the letter of the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم to Heraclius, the Great of Rome. Abu Sufyan said: "Then he—meaning Heraclius—called for the letter of the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم and read it, and in it was: 'In the Name of Allah, the All-Beneficent, the Most Merciful. From Muhammad, the Messenger of Allah, to Heraclius, the Great of Rome. Peace be upon him who follows the guidance. To proceed: I invite you with the invitation of Islam. Submit and you will be safe; submit and Allah will give you your reward twice over. But if you turn away, then upon you is the sin of the peasants (الأريسيين). And O People of the Book! Come to a common word between us and you: that we worship none but Allah...'—to his saying—'...bear witness that we are Muslims.'" (The tradition).

I say: It was also narrated by Muslim in his Sahih, and by al-Suyuti in al-Durr al-Manthur from al-Nasa'i, Abd al-Razzaq, and Ibn Abi Hatim from Ibn Abbas.

It has been said that the letter of the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم to the Muqawqis, the Great of the Copts, also included His saying, the Exalted: "O People of the Book! Come to a common word between us and you." There is a manuscript attributed to him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم written in Kufic script that resembles his letter صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم to Heraclius, and copies of it have recently been made through photography and are in the possession of many.

In any case, historians have mentioned that the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم only wrote the letters and sent the messengers to the kings—Caesar, Khosrow, and the Negus—in the sixth year of the Hijrah. This necessitates that the verse was revealed in the sixth year or before it. Historians such as al-Tabari, Ibn al-Athir, and al-Maqrizi have mentioned that the Christians of Najran only came as a delegation to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم in the tenth year of the Hijrah. Others, such as Abu al-Fida in al-Bidayah wa al-Nihayah and similarly in al-Sirah al-Halabiyyah, mentioned that it was in the ninth year of the Hijrah. This would necessitate the revelation of this verse in the ninth or tenth year.

It has sometimes been said that the verse was among those revealed at the beginning of the Hijrah, as suggested by the following narrations. It has also been said that the verse was revealed twice, as reported by Hafiz Ibn Hajar.

What supports the continuity of the Surah's verses in context—as previously indicated at the beginning of the Surah—is that the verse was revealed before the ninth year, and that the story of the delegation occurred in the sixth year of the Hijrah or before it. It is unlikely that he صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم would write to the greats of Rome, the Copts, and Persia while overlooking Najran despite its proximity.

In the narration, there is another subtle point, which is the beginning of the letter with "In the Name of Allah, the All-Beneficent, the Most Merciful." From this, the issue with some of the narrations we quoted regarding the story of the Najran delegation becomes apparent, such as what is reported from al-Bayhaqi in al-Dala'il: "That the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم wrote to the people of Najran before Ta Sin of Solomon [Surah al-Naml] was revealed to him: 'In the Name of Allah, the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. From Muhammad, the Messenger of Allah, to the Bishop of Najran. If you submit, then I praise to you Allah, the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. To proceed: I invite you to the worship of Allah instead of the worship of servants, and to the guardianship of Allah instead of the guardianship of servants. If you refuse, then the jizyah. If you refuse, then I declare war upon you. Peace.'" (The tradition).

This is because Surah al-Naml is among the Meccan Surahs, and the contents of its verses are explicit in that they were revealed before the Hijrah of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم. How can that be reconciled with the story of Najran? Furthermore, the letter contains other matters that cannot be justified, such as the mention of jizyah and the declaration of war, and so on. Allah knows best.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, al-Tabarani narrated from Ibn Abbas: "That the letter of the Messenger of Allah to the disbelievers was: 'Come to a common word between us and you...'"

Also in al-Durr al-Manthur, regarding His saying, the Exalted: يَا أَهْلَ الْكِتَابِ لِمَ تُحَاجُّونَ "O People of the Book! Why do you argue..." [3:65], Ibn Ishaq, Ibn Jarir, and al-Bayhaqi in al-Dala'il narrated from Ibn Abbas, who said: "The Christians of Najran and the rabbis of the Jews gathered before the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم and disputed in his presence. The rabbis said: 'Abraham was nothing but a Jew,' and the Christians said: 'Abraham was nothing but a Christian.' So Allah revealed concerning them: 'O People of the Book! Why do you argue about Abraham, while the Torah and the Evangel were not sent down until after him...'—to His saying—'...and Allah is the Guardian of the faithful.' Then Abu Rafi' al-Qurazi (from the Jews of Banu Qurayzah) said: 'Do you want us, O Muhammad, to worship you as the Christians worship Jesus son of Mary?' A man from the people of Najran said: 'Is that what you want, O Muhammad?' The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم said: 'I seek refuge in Allah from worshipping other than Allah, or commanding the worship of other than Him. I was not sent for that, nor did He command me with it.' So Allah revealed concerning their words: 'It is not for a human that Allah should give him the Book, judgment and prophethood, and then he should say to the people, "Be my servants instead of Allah"’—to His saying—'...after you have been Muslims.' Then He mentioned what He took from them and their forefathers of the covenant to believe in him when he comes to them, and their acknowledgment of it against themselves, saying: 'And when Allah took the covenant of the prophets...'—to His saying—'...of those who bear witness.'"

I say: The verses—meaning His saying: "It is not for a human that Allah should give him the Book, judgment and prophethood..." to the end of the verses—are more consistent in context and more easily applicable to Jesus son of Mary (peace be upon him) than to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم, as will be discussed in the commentary on those verses. Perhaps what is in the narration regarding the revelation of the verses concerning the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم is an inference and application by Ibn Abbas. Moreover, it is the customary practice of the Quran to address this type of statement in the form of question and answer or narration and rebuttal.

In the Tafsir of al-Khazin, al-Kalbi narrated from Abu Salih from Ibn Abbas, and Muhammad ibn Ishaq narrated it from Ibn Shihab with his chain, the tradition of the migration to Abyssinia. He said: "When Ja'far ibn Abi Talib and a group of the companions of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم migrated to the land of Abyssinia and settled there, and the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم migrated to Medina, and the event of Badr took place, the Quraysh gathered in the Dar al-Nadwa. They said: 'We have a blood-revenge against those with the Negus from the companions of Muhammad for those of you killed at Badr. So collect wealth and gift it to the Negus; perhaps he will hand over to you those of your people who are with him. Let two men of sound judgment be sent to him.'"

"So they sent Amr ibn al-As and Umarah ibn Abi Mu'ayt with gifts: leather and other things. They traveled by sea until they reached Abyssinia. When they entered upon the Negus, they prostrated to him and greeted him. They said to him: 'Our people are sincere advisers to you, grateful, and lovers of your companions. They have sent us to you to warn you against these who have come to you, for they are the people of a lying man who has emerged claiming to be a messenger of Allah, and none of us followed him except the foolish. We had restricted them and forced them into a mountain pass in our land where no one could enter upon them, so hunger and thirst killed them. When the matter became severe for him, he sent his cousin to you to corrupt your religion, your kingdom, and your subjects. So beware of them and hand them over to us so we may deal with them for you.' They said: 'The sign of that is that when they enter upon you, they do not prostrate to you, nor do they greet you with the greeting people greet you with, out of aversion to your religion and tradition.'"

"He said: So the Negus summoned them. When they arrived, Ja'far shouted at the door: 'The party of Allah (حزب الله) seeks permission to enter upon you.' The Negus said: 'Tell this shouter to repeat his words.' Ja'far did so. The Negus said: 'Yes, let them enter under the protection and covenant of Allah.' Amr looked at his companion and said: 'Do you not hear how they jargon about the "party of Allah" and how the King answered them?' That distressed them."

"Then they entered upon him but did not prostrate to him. Amr ibn al-As said: 'Do you not see that they are too arrogant to prostrate to you?' The Negus said to them: 'What prevented you from prostrating to me and greeting me with the greeting with which those who come to me from the horizons greet me?' They said: 'We prostrate to Allah who created you and gave you kingdom. That greeting was for us when we worshipped idols, but Allah sent among us a truthful prophet, and he commanded us with the greeting that Allah is pleased with, which is al-Salam (السلام) — peace — the greeting of the people of Paradise.' The Negus recognized that this was the truth and that it was in the Torah and the Evangel. He said: 'Which of you was the caller: "The party of Allah seeks permission to enter upon you"?' Ja'far said: 'I am.' He said: 'You are a king among the kings of the earth from the People of the Book; much talk and injustice are not appropriate in your presence. I only wish to answer on behalf of my companions. Command these two men that one of them speaks and the other remains silent so you may hear our dialogue.' Amr said to Ja'far: 'Speak.'"

"Ja'far said to the Negus: 'Ask these two men: are we slaves or free men? If we are slaves who have fled from our masters, then return us to them.' The Negus said: 'Are they slaves or free men?' He said: 'Rather, noble free men.' The Negus said: 'They have escaped slavery.' Ja'far said: 'Ask them: have we shed any blood without right such that retaliation should be taken from us?' Amr said: 'No, not a drop.' Ja'far said: 'Ask them: have we taken people's wealth without right such that we must repay it?' The Negus said: 'If it were a qintar, I would be responsible for its repayment.' Amr said: 'No, not even a qirat (قيراط) — a tiny fraction of a coin.' The Negus said: 'Then what do you seek from them?' He said: 'We and they were upon one religion, the religion of our forefathers, but they left it and followed another. Our people sent us so you may hand them over to us.' The Negus said: 'What is this that you were upon and the religion they followed?' Ja'far said: 'As for the religion we were upon, it was the religion of Satan; we used to disbelieve in Allah and worship stones. As for that which we turned to, it is the religion of Allah, Islam. A messenger came to us with it from Allah, with a Book like the Book of the son of Mary, in agreement with it.' The Negus said: 'O Ja'far, you have spoken of a great matter.'"

"Then the Negus ordered the bell to be rung, and it was rung, and every priest and monk gathered to him. When they gathered, the Negus said: 'I adjure you by Allah who sent down the Evangel to Jesus, do you find between Jesus and the Day of Resurrection a sent prophet?' They said: 'O Allah, yes, he gave us glad tidings.' He said: 'Whoever believes in him has believed in me, and whoever disbelieves in him has disbelieved in me.' The Negus said to Ja'far: 'What does this man say to you? What does he command you and what does he forbid you?' He said: 'He reads to us the Book of Allah, commands us with the right (المعروف) and forbids us from the wrong (المنكر), and commands us with good neighborliness, joining of kinship, and kindness to the orphan. He commands us to worship Allah alone without any partner.' He said to him: 'Read to me from what he reads to you.' So he read to him Surah al-Ankabut and al-Rum, and the eyes of the Negus and his companions overflowed with tears. They said: 'Give us more of this pleasant discourse.' So he read to them Surah al-Kahf. Amr wanted to anger the Negus, so he said: 'They insult Jesus and his mother.' The Negus said: 'What do you say about Jesus and his mother?' So he read to them Surah Maryam. When he reached the mention of Mary and Jesus, the Negus picked up a piece of his toothstick (سواك) the size of a speck in the eye and said: 'By Allah, the Messiah did not exceed what you say by this much.' Then he turned to Ja'far and his companions and said: 'Go, for you are suyum (سيوم) in my land'—meaning safe from being insulted or harmed—'and whoever harms you shall be fined.' Then he said: 'Rejoice and do not fear, for there is no dahurah (دهورة) — humiliation or destruction — today upon the party of Abraham.' Amr said: 'O Negus, and who is the party of Abraham?' He said: 'This group and their companion from whom they came, and whoever follows them.' The polytheists denied that and claimed the religion of Abraham. Then the Negus returned to Amr and his companion the wealth they had brought and said: 'Your gift is but a bribe, so take it back, for Allah gave me kingdom and did not take a bribe from me.' Ja'far said: 'So we departed and were in the best of neighborliness, and Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, revealed concerning that to His Messenger صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم regarding their dispute over Abraham while he was in Medina: "Indeed the nearest of people to Abraham are those who followed him, and this Prophet and those who believe, and Allah is the Guardian of the faithful."'"

I say: This story is narrated through other paths and through the paths of the People of the House (عليه السلام). We have quoted it at length only because it contains important benefits regarding the trials of the Muslims among the first migrants, and it is not strictly an "occasion of revelation" (سبب النزول).

In the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding His saying, the Exalted: مَا كَانَ إِبْرَاهِيمُ يَهُودِيًّا وَلَا نَصْرَانِيًّا "Abraham was neither a Jew nor a Christian" [3:67], he said: "The Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) said: 'Neither a Jew praying toward the West, nor a Christian praying toward the East, but he was a hanif (حنيفاً) — one naturally inclined toward monotheism — a Muslim upon the religion of Muhammad صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم.'"

I say: The meaning of his being upon the religion of Muhammad (peace be upon them both and their progeny) has been previously explained. The narration considers the facing of the Kaaba, as the qiblah (القبلة) — the direction of prayer — was turned toward it in Medina, and the Kaaba is approximately at its southern point. The refusal of the Jews and Christians to accept it caused them to deviate from it toward the West, where Jerusalem is, and the East, which the Christians face. Thus, that was considered a deviation from the exact center for both groups. This notion is supported by the wording of the verse: "And thus We have made you a middle nation..." [2:143]. In short, it is a subtle point and nothing more.

In al-Kafi, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "Pure and sincere, containing nothing of the worship of idols."

In al-Majma', regarding His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ أَوْلَى النَّاسِ بِإِبْرَاهِيمَ "Indeed the nearest of people to Abraham..." [3:68], the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) said: "Indeed the nearest of people to the prophets are those who act most in accordance with what they brought." Then he recited this verse and said: "Indeed the friend (ولي) of Muhammad is he who obeys Allah, even if his kinship is distant; and indeed the enemy of Muhammad is he who disobeys Allah, even if his kinship is near."

In al-Kafi and the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "They are the Imams and those who follow them."

In the Tafsirs of al-Qummi and al-Ayyashi, from Umar ibn Udhaynah from him (peace be upon him), who said: "By Allah, you are from the family of Muhammad." I said: "From their very selves, may I be your ransom?" He said: "Yes, by Allah, from their very selves," three times. Then he looked at me and I looked at him, and he said: "O Umar, indeed Allah says in His Book: 'Indeed the nearest of people...'" (The verse).

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَقَالَتْ طَائِفَةٌ مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتَابِ آمِنُوا "A group of the People of the Book say, ‘Believe...’" [3:72], from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him): "When the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم came to Medina and was praying toward Jerusalem, that pleased the people. When Allah turned him from Jerusalem to the Sacred House, the Jews were upset by that. The turning of the qiblah occurred during the Dhuhr prayer. So they said: 'Muhammad prayed in the morning facing our qiblah, so believe in what was revealed to Muhammad at the beginning of the day and disbelieve at its end'—meaning the qiblah when the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم faced the Sacred Mosque."

I say: The narration, as you see, makes the phrase "the beginning of the day" (وجه النهار) an adverb for "revealed" (انزل), rather than for "believe" (آمنوا). This was previously discussed in the preceding commentary.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Jarir and Ibn Abi Hatim narrated through the path of al-Awfi from Ibn Abbas regarding His saying: "A group said..." (The verse). He said: "A group of the Jews said: 'When you meet the companions of Muhammad at the beginning of the day, believe; and when it is the end of it, pray your prayer; perhaps they will say: "These are the People of the Book and they are more knowledgeable than us," so perhaps they will turn away from their religion.'"

I say: It was also narrated therein from al-Suddi and Mujahid.

In al-Kafi (regarding His saying, the Exalted: "Indeed those who sell the covenant of Allah..."), from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him), who said: "It was revealed concerning the pledge: 'Indeed those who sell the covenant of Allah and their oaths for a small price, they shall have no share in the Hereafter, and Allah will not speak to them nor look at them on the Day of Resurrection, and they shall have a painful punishment.' Khalaq (الخلاق) is the share (النصيب). So whoever has no share in the Hereafter, by what thing shall he enter Paradise?"

In the Amali of the Shaykh, with his chain from Adi ibn Adi from his father, who said: "Imru' al-Qays and a man from Hadramawt disputed before the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم regarding a land. He said: 'Do you have evidence?' He said: 'No.' He said: 'Then [it is decided] by his oath.' He said: 'Then, by Allah, he will take my land.' He said: 'If he takes your land by his oath, he will be among those whom Allah will not look at on the Day of Resurrection, nor will He purify him, and he shall have a painful punishment.' He said: The man was terrified and returned it to him."

I say: The narration, as you see, does not indicate that the verse was revealed specifically for the occasion of the story. It has been narrated through Sunni paths in several narrations that the verse was revealed regarding this matter, but they are conflicting regarding the details of the story. In some, the dispute was between Imru' al-Qays and a man from Hadramawt, as in the previous narration. In some, it was between al-Ash'ath ibn Qays and a Jewish man regarding a land of his. In some, it was revealed regarding a man from the disbelievers who had displayed his merchandise in the market and swore by Allah that he had been offered for it what he had not been offered, in order to entrap a man from the Muslims; so the verse was revealed.

You have already seen in the preceding commentary that the apparent sense of the verse is that it serves as a causal explanation (تعليل) for the content of the verse preceding it. Thus, the proper approach is to treat the narrations—if possible—as an explanation of the verse's applicability to the occasion of the story, rather than as an "occasion of revelation" in the customary sense.

Ayahs

مَا كَانَ لِبَشَرٍ أَن يُؤْتِيَهُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَٱلْحُكْمَ وَٱلنُّبُوَّةَ ثُمَّ يَقُولَ لِلنَّاسِ كُونُوا۟ عِبَادًا لِّى مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ وَلَـٰكِن كُونُوا۟ رَبَّـٰنِيِّـۧنَ بِمَا كُنتُمْ تُعَلِّمُونَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَبِمَا كُنتُمْ تَدْرُسُونَ (٧٩)
It does not behoove any human that Allah should give him the Book, judgement and prophethood, and then he should say to the people, ‘Be my servants instead of Allah.’ Rather [he would say], ‘Be a godly people, because of your teaching the Book and because of your studying it.’ (79)
وَلَا يَأْمُرَكُمْ أَن تَتَّخِذُوا۟ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةَ وَٱلنَّبِيِّـۧنَ أَرْبَابًا ۗ أَيَأْمُرُكُم بِٱلْكُفْرِ بَعْدَ إِذْ أَنتُم مُّسْلِمُونَ (٨٠)
And he would not command you to take the angels and the prophets for lords. Would he call you to unfaith after you have submitted [to Allah]? (80)

(Commentary)

The occurrence of these verses immediately following those related to the matter of Jesus (عليه السلام) indicates that they serve as a second chapter in the argument establishing the innocence of the Messiah from what the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب) among the Christians believe concerning him. The discourse is as if to say: He is not as you claim; he is neither a Lord (رب), nor did he claim Lordship (الربوبية) for himself. As for the first point: it is because he was a created human being (مخلوق بشري) whom his mother carried, gave birth to, and raised in the cradle, except that he had no father, just like Adam (عليهما السلام); thus, his likeness with Allah is as the likeness of Adam. As for the second point: it is because he was a prophet (نبي) who was given the Book (الكتاب), judgment (الحكم), and prophethood (النبوة). A prophet of such standing does not exceed the bounds of servitude (العبودية) nor divests himself of the raiment of slavery (الرقية). How then could it be possible for him to say to the people, "Take me as a lord and be servants to me instead of Allah"? Or how could such a thing be permissible for any other servant of Allah, whether angel (ملك) or prophet, such that he would grant a servant of Allah what is not his by right, or deny a prophet what Allah has established for him of the Message (الرسالة), thereby taking from him a right that belongs to him?

His saying, the Exalted: ما كان لبشر أن يؤتيه الله الكتاب والحكم والنبوة ثم يقول للناس كونوا عباداً لي من دون الله — It is not for a human that Allah should give him the Book, judgement and prophethood, and then he should say to the people, ‘Be my servants instead of Allah.’ (3:79). "Human" (البشر) is a synonym for "man" (الإنسان), and it is applied to both the individual and the collective; thus, a single person is a human (بشر) just as a group of them is human (بشر).

His saying, "It is not for a human" (ما كان لبشر), the letter lam (اللام) denotes possession (الملك), meaning: he does not possess that right, or it is not his by right. This is similar to His saying, the Exalted: مَّا يَكُونُ لَنَا أَن نَّتَكَلَّمَ بِهَٰذَا — It is not for us to say such a thing (24:16), and His saying: وَمَا كَانَ لِنَبِيٍّ أَن يَغُلَّ — A prophet may not illicitly take from the spoils (3:161).

His saying, the Exalted, "that Allah should give him the Book, judgment, and prophethood" (أن يؤتيه الله الكتاب والحكم والنبوة) is the subject of "was" (كان), yet it serves as a preamble for what follows in His saying, "then he should say to the people" (ثم يقول للناس). Mentioning this preamble—despite the meaning being apparently sound without it—serves another facet of the meaning of "It is not for a human." For if it were said, "It is not for a human to say to the people," it would mean that this right was not legislated for him, even if it were possible for him to say it out of wickedness (فسقاً) or rebellion (عتواً). However, when it is said: "It is not for a human that Allah should give him the Book, judgment, and prophethood, then he should say," it means that Allah’s granting him knowledge (العلم) and understanding (الفقه) from His presence, and His nurturing him with a Lordly upbringing (تربية ربانية), would never allow him to exceed the bounds of servitude (العبودية), nor would it permit him to dispose of what he does not own or have a right to. This is as the Exalted recounts from Jesus (عليه السلام) in His saying: وَإِذْ قَالَ اللَّهُ يَا عِيسَى ابْنَ مَرْيَمَ أَأَنتَ قُلْتَ لِلنَّاسِ اتَّخِذُونِي وَأُمِّيَ إِلَٰهَيْنِ مِن دُونِ اللَّهِ قَالَ سُبْحَانَكَ مَا يَكُونُ لي أَنْ أَقُولَ مَا لَيْسَ لِي بِحَقٍّ — And when Allah will say, ‘O Jesus son of Mary, did you say to the people, ‘‘Take me and my mother for two gods besides Allah’’?’ He will say, ‘Immaculate are You! It does not behoove me to say what I have no right to [say].’ (5:116).

From here, the subtle point (النكتة) in His saying, "that Allah should give him..." (أن يؤتيه الله) becomes clear, rather than saying, "It is not for a human to whom Allah gave the Book... to say..." The second phrasing would imply the meaning of the origin of legislation (أصل التشريع) as previously mentioned, whereas His saying, "that Allah should give him..." implies that such a thing is absolutely impossible. That is, the Lordly upbringing and Divine guidance (الهداية الالهية) never fail to achieve their purpose, as the Exalted said: أُولَٰئِكَ الَّذِينَ آتَيْنَاهُمُ الْكِتَابَ وَالْحُكْمَ وَالنُّبُوَّةَ فَإِن يَكْفُرْ بِهَا هَٰؤُلَاءِ فَقَدْ وَكَّلْنَا بِهَا قَوْمًا لَّيسُوا بِهَا بِكَافِرِينَ — They are the ones whom We gave the Book, judgement and prophethood. So if these [people] disbelieve in them, We have certainly entrusted them to a people who will not disbelieve in them. (6:89).

The sum of the meaning is that it is not possible for a human to combine these Divine blessings with calling people to worship himself—by being given the Book, judgment, and prophethood, and then saying to the people, "Be my servants instead of Allah." Thus, the verse, according to its context (السياق), is in one respect like His saying, the Exalted: لَّن يَسْتَنكِفَ الْمَسِيحُ أَن يَكُونَ عَبْدًا لِّلَّهِ وَلَا الْمَلَائِكَةُ الْمُقَرَّبُونَ — The Messiah would never disdain to be a servant of Allah, nor would the angels brought near [to Him]. — until He said — وَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ اسْتَنكَفُوا وَاسْتَكْبَرُوا فَيُعَذِّبُهُمْ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا وَلَا يَجِدُونَ لَهُم مِّن دُونِ اللَّهِ وَلِيًّا وَلَا نَصِيرًا — As for those who disdain and are arrogant, He will punish them with a painful punishment, and they will not find besides Allah any guardian or helper. (4:172-173). What is understood from the verse is that the Messiah, as well as the angels brought near, are too exalted in status and too high in rank to disdain the worship of Allah, for disdaining His worship necessitates a painful punishment; and far be it from Allah to punish His noble prophets and His near angels.

If you say: The use of "then" (ثم), which denotes a delay (التراخي), in His saying, "then he should say to the people," contradicts the combination (الجمع) you mentioned.

I say: What we mentioned regarding the meaning of combination is the summarized meaning. Just as it is correct to consider the gathering and accompaniment between things united in time, it is also correct to consider it between things that are ordered and sequential; thus, it is a type of combination (الجمع).

As for His saying: "Be servants to me instead of Allah" (كونوا عباداً لي من دون الله), the word "servants" (العباد) like "slaves" (العبيد) is the plural of "servant/slave" (عبد). The difference between them is that "servants" (العباد) is predominantly used when attributed to Allah, the Glorified—one says "servants of Allah" (عباد الله)—and one does not say "servants of people" (عباد الناس), but rather "slaves of people" (عبيد الناس). The qualification of His saying, "servants to me" with His saying, "instead of Allah" (من دون الله) is a necessary qualification, for Allah, the Glorified, does not accept any worship except that which is sincere for His noble countenance, as the Exalted said: أَلَا لِلَّهِ الدِّينُ الْخَالِصُ وَالَّذِينَ اتَّخَذُوا مِن دُونِهِ أَوْلِيَاءَ مَا نَعْبُدُهُمْ إِلَّا لِيُقَرِّبُونَا إِلَى اللَّهِ زُلْفَىٰ إِنَّ اللَّهَ يَحْكُمُ بَيْنَهُمْ فِي مَا هُمْ فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَا يَهْدِي مَنْ هُوَ كَاذِبٌ كَفَّارٌ — Now, surely, sincere religion belongs to Allah. As for those who take guardians besides Him, [saying,] ‘We only worship them so that they may bring us near to Allah,’ Allah will indeed judge between them concerning that about which they differ. Indeed Allah does not guide someone who is a liar and an ingrate. (39:3). Thus, He rejected the worship of anyone who worships another alongside Him, even under the pretext of seeking nearness, mediation, or intercession (الاستشفاع).

Furthermore, the reality of worship (العبادة) is not realized except by granting some degree of independence (استقلال) to the object of worship, even in the form of polytheism (الإشراك); for a partner, insofar as he is a partner, is a participant possessing some independence. Allah, the Glorified, possesses absolute Lordship (الربوبية المطلقة), so His Lordship is not complete nor is His worship sound except with the negation of independence from everything in every respect. Therefore, worshipping other than Allah is worshipping him "instead of Allah," even if Allah is worshipped alongside him.

His saying, the Exalted: ولكن كونوا ربانيين بما كنتم تعلمون الكتاب وبما كنتم تدرسون — Rather [he would say], ‘Be Lordly (ربانيين) — those intensely devoted to the Lord in knowledge and worship — because of your teaching the Book and because of your studying it.’ (3:79). The "Lordly" (الرباني) is one attributed to the Lord (الرب); the letters alif and nun were added to denote magnification (التفخيم), just as one says "bearded" (لحياني) for one with a large beard, and the like. Thus, the meaning of "Lordly" is one who is intensely specialized in the Lord and frequently occupied with His servitude and worship. The "by" (الباء) in His saying, "because of what you were" (بما كنتم) denotes causality (السببية), and "what" (ما) is an infinitive particle (مصدرية). The speech implies a suppressed "he says," and the meaning is: "But he says: Be Lordly because of your teaching the Book to the people and your studying it among yourselves."

"Studying" (الدراسة) is more specific than "teaching" (التعليم), as it is mostly used for what is learned from a book by reading it. Al-Raghib said: "The house was studied (درس)" means its traces remained, and the remaining of traces implies their eventual effacement in themselves; therefore, durūs (الدروس) was explained as effacement. Likewise, "he studied the book," and "I studied the knowledge" means I took its traces by memorization. Since taking that occurs through constant reading, constant reading was expressed as memorization. The Exalted said: وَدَرَسُوا مَا فِيهِ — and they have studied what is in it (7:169), and He said: بِمَا كُنتُمْ تُعَلِّمُونَ الْكِتَابَ وَبِمَا كُنتُمْ تَدْرُسُونَ — because of your teaching the Book and because of your studying it (3:79), and وَمَا آتَيْنَاهُم مِّن كُتُبٍ يَدْرُسُونَهَا — We have not given them any books that they might study (34:44). [End of quote].

The sum of the discourse is that a human of this standing only calls you to clothe yourselves with faith (الإيمان) and certainty (اليقين) in what is in the Book—which you teach and study—of the principles of Divine gnosis (المعارف الإلهية), and to be characterized by and realize the virtuous traits and ethics it contains, and to act upon the righteous deeds (الصالحات) to which you call the people, so that through this you may devote yourselves entirely to your Lord and become Lordly scholars (علماء ربانيين) through Him.

His saying, "because of what you were" (بما كنتم), since it contains the past tense denoting realization (التحقق), is not devoid of some indication that the discourse in the verse is intended as an allusion (التعريض) to the Christians among the People of the Book regarding their claim that Jesus informed them that he was His son and His word—according to the differences in interpreting sonship. This is because the Children of Israel were the ones who held a Heavenly Book in their hands, teaching it and studying it, yet they differed concerning it with a disagreement accompanied by alteration (التغيير) and distortion (التحريف). Jesus (عليه السلام) was only sent to clarify for them some of that wherein they differed, and to make lawful some of what had been forbidden to them; in short, to call them to fulfill the obligatory duties of teaching and studying, which is to be Lordly in their teaching and studying of the Book of Allah, the Glorified.

Although the verse does not resist being applied to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in some respect—as his call also concerned the People of the Book who were teaching and studying the Book of Allah—Jesus (عليه السلام) is more prior in its application to him, and his mission was specifically for the Children of Israel, unlike the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

As for the other great prophets among the "Possessors of Resolve" (أولو العزم) and the Book, such as Noah, Abraham, and Moses, the content of the verse does not apply to them [in this specific context of allusion], and this is evident.

His saying, the Exalted: أو يأمركم أن تتخذوا الملائكة والنبيين أرباباً — Nor would he command you to take the angels and the prophets for lords. (3:80). This is conjoined to His saying, "he says" (يقول), according to the well-known recitation which puts "command you" (يأمركم) in the subjunctive case. This is because a group of the People of the Book, like the Sabians, used to worship the angels and attribute that to a religious call; and like the Arabs of the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية) who used to say that the angels are the daughters of Allah, while claiming they were following the religion of Abraham (عليه السلام)—this concerns taking the angels as lords.

As for taking the prophets as lords, it is like the saying of the Jews: "Ezra is the son of Allah," as recounted by the Quran, though Moses (عليه السلام) never permitted that for them, nor does anything occur in the Torah except the monotheism (التوحيد) of the Lord. Had he permitted that for them, it would have been a command to it—far be it from him to do so.

The two verses—namely His saying, "then he should say to the people, ‘Be my servants instead of Allah’" and His saying, "Nor would he command you to take the angels and the prophets for lords"—differ in two respects regarding their context: First, the one commanded in the first ("then he should say to the people") is "the people," while in the second, it is those addressed by the verse. Second, the thing commanded in the first is servitude (العبودية) to him, while in the second, it is taking them as lords (أرباباً).

As for the first: since the discourse was intended as an allusion to the Christians in their worship of Jesus and their explicit claim of his divinity (ألوهيته), attributing that to his call, it was an attribution from them to him that he said: "Be servants to me." This is unlike taking the angels and prophets as lords in the sense said of others besides Jesus, for that contradicts divinity by its implication (بلازمه), not by its explicit statement (بصريحه); therefore, it was said "lords" (أرباباً) and not "gods" (آلهة).

As for the second: the reason is that both expressions ("Be servants to me" and "he commands you to take") are commands that, if they were to pertain to anyone, would pertain to those addressed by these verses among the People of the Book and the Arabs. However, since the expression in the first verse used "saying" (القول), and saying necessitates direct address (المشافهة), and those present at the time of the verse's revelation were not present then, it was necessarily said: "then he should say to the people," and it was not said: "then he should say to you." This is unlike the word "command" (الأمر) used in the second verse, for it does not necessitate direct address but is complete even with absence (الغيبة); for a command pertaining to the ancestors pertains to the descendants while maintaining national unity. As for "saying," because it conveys—according to its common usage—the hearing of a voice, it necessitates direct address and presence, unless it is intended to mean merely the sense of conveying understanding (التفهيم).

Accordingly, the basis in the context of these verses is presence and collective address, as followed in His saying, the Exalted: "Nor would he command you..." to the end of the verse.

His saying, the Exalted: أيأمركم بالكفر بعد إذ أنتم مسلمون — Would he command you to disbelieve after you have submitted [to Allah]? (3:80). The apparent address is that it pertains to all who affiliate themselves with prophethood among the People of the Book or those claiming a connection to the prophets, as the Arabs of the Age of Ignorance claimed they were Hunafā’ (حنفاء). The discourse is based on a hypothetical premise (الفرض والتقدير); the meaning is that, assuming you respond to this human who was given the Book, judgment, and prophethood, you would be submitters (مسلمين) to Allah, adorned with the ornament of Islam and dyed with its hue. How then could it be possible for him to command you to disbelieve (الكفر) and lead you astray from the path to which he guided you by the permission of Allah, the Glorified?

From here, it becomes clear that what is meant by Islam (الإسلام) is the religion of monotheism (التوحيد), which is the religion of Allah according to all the prophets. This is also indicated by the surrounding verses carrying this meaning of Islam—namely His saying, the Exalted, previously: إِنَّ الدِّينَ عِندَ اللَّهِ الْإِسْلَامُ — Indeed, with Allah religion is Islam (3:19), and His saying, the Exalted, afterwards: أَفَغَيْرَ دِينِ اللَّهِ يَبْغُونَ — Do they seek a religion other than the religion of Allah? — until He said — وَمَن يَبْتَغِ غَيْرَ الْإِسْلَامِ دِينًا فَلَن يُقْبَلَ مِنْهُ وَهُوَ فِي الْآخِرَةِ مِنَ الْخَاسِرِينَ — Should anyone follow a religion other than Islam, it shall never be accepted from him, and he will be among the losers in the Hereafter. (3:85).

Some exegetes have mentioned that what is meant by His saying, the Exalted, "It is not for a human that Allah should give him..." to the end of the two verses, is the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)—based on what was narrated regarding the occasion of revelation (شأن النزول). Its summary is: Abu Rafi‘ al-Qurazi and a man from the Christians of Najran said to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): "Do you want us to worship you, O Muhammad?" So Allah sent down: "It is not for a human that Allah should give him..." to the end of the two verses. Then he supported this with His saying at their end, "after you have submitted [to Allah]," for Islam is the religion brought by Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

The flaw in this is that it confuses Islam in the usage of the Quran—which is the religion of monotheism (التوحيد) with which all prophets were sent—and Islam as a technical term (الاصطلاح) that arose among Muslims after the era of revelation; the discussion on this has already preceded.

(A Conclusion Containing Several Sections)

1 - What is the story of Jesus and his mother in the Quran?

The mother of the Messiah was Mary, the daughter of ‘Imran. Her mother conceived her and vowed to make what was in her womb, when she delivered it, consecrated (محرراً) to serve the Temple, assuming that what was in her womb was male. When she delivered her and it became clear to her that it was female, she was saddened and felt regret. Then she named her Mary, meaning "the servant." Her father ‘Imran had died before her birth, so she brought her to the Temple to hand her over to the priests, among whom was Zechariah. They disputed over her guardianship, then they agreed on drawing lots. The lot fell to Zechariah, so he took charge of her until she reached maturity. Then she struck a veil between herself and them; she used to worship Allah, the Glorified, therein, and no one entered upon her except Zechariah. Whenever Zechariah entered the sanctuary (المحراب) to see her, he found provision with her. He said, "O Mary, from where does this come to you?" She said, "It is from Allah; indeed Allah provides for whomever He wishes without stint." She (peace be upon her) was a truthful woman (صديقة), and she was protected by the protection (عصمة) of Allah, pure (طاهرة), chosen (مصطفاة), and spoken to (محدثة)—the angels spoke to her: that Allah had chosen her and purified her, and she was of the obedient (القانتين) and one of the signs of Allah for all the worlds (العالمين). (Surah Al ‘Imran 3:35-44, Surah Maryam 19:16, Surah al-Anbiya’ 21:91, Surah al-Tahrim 66:12).

Then Allah the Exalted sent the Spirit (الروح) to her while she was veiled, and he appeared to her as a harmonious man (بشراً سوياً). He mentioned to her that he was a messenger from her Lord to grant her, by Allah’s permission, a son without a father. He gave her glad tidings of the manifest miracles that would appear from her son, and informed her that Allah would support him with the Holy Spirit (روح القدس), and teach him the Book, wisdom, the Torah, and the Gospel, and make him a messenger to the Children of Israel with clear signs. He informed her of his status and story, then he breathed the Spirit into her, and she conceived him as a woman conceives her child. (Verses from Al ‘Imran: 35-44).

Then Mary withdrew with him to a distant place, and the birth pangs brought her to the trunk of a palm tree. She said, "I wish I had died before this and been a thing forgotten, out of mind!" Then he called her from below her, "Do not grieve! Your Lord has made a rivulet below you. Shake the trunk of the palm tree toward yourself; it will drop fresh ripe dates upon you. So eat and drink and be consoled. Then if you see any human, say, ‘Indeed I have vowed a fast to the All-Beneficent, so I will not speak to any human today.’" Then she brought him to her people, carrying him. (Surah Maryam 19:20-27). His gestation, birth, speech, and all other affairs of his existence were of the same nature as those of other human individuals.

When her people saw her—in this state—they rose against her with slander and blame, as evidenced by the state of a woman who has conceived and given birth without a husband. They said, "O Mary, you have certainly brought a monstrous thing! O sister of Aaron, your father was not an evil man, nor was your mother unchaste." She pointed to him. They said, "How can we speak to one who is a child in the cradle?" He said, "Indeed I am a servant of Allah! He has given me the Book and made me a prophet. He has made me blessed wherever I may be, and He has enjoined upon me prayer and charity as long as I live, and to be dutiful to my mother, and He has not made me a tyrant, wretched. Peace be to me the day I was born, and the day I die, and the day I am raised alive." (Surah Maryam 19:27-33). This speech from him (peace be upon him) was like a brilliant prelude (براعة الاستهلال) to what he would undertake in opposing transgression and injustice, reviving the law of Moses (عليه السلام) and rectifying it, renewing what had been effaced of its gnosis, and clarifying that wherein they differed regarding its signs.

Then Jesus (عليه السلام) grew up and reached youth. He and his mother followed the customary course of human life, eating and drinking, and within them was what is in all other people of the accidents of existence (عوارض الوجود) until the end of their lives.

Then Jesus (عليه السلام) was given the Message (الرسالة) to the Children of Israel, so he set out calling them to the religion of monotheism (التوحيد), saying: "I have come to you with a sign from your Lord: I will create for you out of clay the form of a bird, then I will breathe into it, and it will become a bird by Allah’s permission. And I will heal the blind and the leper and I will revive the dead by Allah’s permission. And I will inform you of what you eat and what you store in your houses. Indeed in that is a sign for you. Indeed Allah is my Lord and your Lord, so worship Him."

He used to call them to his new law (شريعة), which was a confirmation of the law of Moses (عليه السلام), except that he abrogated some of what had been forbidden in the Torah as a hardship upon the Jews. He used to say: "I have come to you with wisdom and to clarify for you some of that wherein you differ." And he used to say: "O Children of Israel, indeed I am the messenger of Allah to you, confirming what is before me of the Torah and bringing glad tidings of a messenger who will come after me, whose name is Ahmad."

He (peace be upon him) performed what he had mentioned to them of miracles, such as creating the bird, reviving the dead, healing the blind and the leper, and informing of the unseen (المغيبات) by Allah’s permission.

He continued to call them to the monotheism of Allah and his new law until he despaired of their faith, having witnessed the rebellion of the people, their stubbornness, and the arrogance of the priests and rabbis toward that. So he chose from the small group that believed in him the Disciples (الحواريين) as his helpers toward Allah.

Then the Jews rose against him, intending to kill him, but Allah took him (توفاه الله) and raised him to Himself. It was made to appear so to the Jews: some claimed they killed him, and some claimed they crucified him, but it was only made to appear so to them. (Al ‘Imran 3:45-58, al-Zukhruf 43:63-65, al-Saff 61:6 and 14, al-Ma’idah 5:110 and 111, al-Nisa’ 4:157 and 158). This is the summary of what the Quran recounts concerning Jesus son of Mary and his mother.

2 - The status of Jesus with Allah and his position in himself:

He (peace be upon him) was a servant of Allah and he was a prophet (Surah Maryam 19:30). He was a messenger to the Children of Israel (Al ‘Imran 3:49). He was one of the five "Possessors of Resolve" (أولو العزم), the possessor of a law and a Book, which is the Gospel (al-Ahzab 33:7, al-Shura 42:13, al-Ma’idah 5:46). Allah named him the Messiah, Jesus (Al ‘Imran 3:45). He was a word from Allah and a spirit from Him (al-Nisa’ 4:171). He was an Imam (al-Ahzab 33:7). He was among the witnesses over deeds (al-Nisa’ 4:159, al-Ma’idah 5:117). He was a bringer of glad tidings of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) (al-Saff 61:6). He was distinguished in this world and the Hereafter and among those brought near (Al ‘Imran 3:45). He was among the chosen (Al ‘Imran 3:33). He was among the elect, and he was among the righteous (al-An‘am 6:85-87). He was blessed wherever he was, and he was pure (زكياً), and he was a sign for the people and a mercy from Allah, and dutiful to his mother, and peace was upon him (Maryam 19:19-33). He was among those whom Allah taught the Book and wisdom (Al ‘Imran 3:48). These are twenty-two characteristics from the stations of guardianship (مقامات الولاية) which comprise the sum of how Allah described this noble prophet and raised his rank. They are of two types: acquired (اكتسابية), such as servitude, nearness, and righteousness; and specialized (اختصاصية). We have explained each of them in its appropriate place in this book to the extent of our understanding, so let one refer to their respective places.

3 - What did Jesus (peace be upon him) say? And what was said about him?

The Quran mentions that Jesus was a servant and a messenger, and that he did not claim for himself what they attributed to him, nor did he speak to them except with the Message; as the Exalted said: وَإِذْ قَالَ اللَّهُ يَا عِيسَى ابْنَ مَرْيَمَ أَأَنتَ قُلْتَ لِلنَّاسِ اتَّخِذُونِي وَأُمِّيَ إِلَٰهَيْنِ مِن دُونِ اللَّهِ قَالَ سُبْحَانَكَ مَا يَكُونُ لِي أَنْ أَقُولَ مَا لَيْسَ لِي بِحَقٍّ إِن كُنتُ قُلْتُهُ فَقَدْ عَلِمْتَهُ تَعْلَمُ مَا فِي نَفْسِي وَلَا أَعْلَمُ مَا فِي نَفْسِكَ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ عَلَّامُ الْغُيُوبِ ، مَا قُلْتُ لَهُمْ إِلَّا مَا أَمَرْتَنِي بِهِ أَنِ اعْبُدُوا اللَّهَ رَبِّي وَرَبَّكُمْ وَكُنتُ عَلَيْهِمْ شَهِيدًا مَّا دُمْتُ فِيهِمْ فَلَمَّا تَوَفَّيْتَنِي كُنتَ أَنتَ الرَّقِيبَ عَلَيْهِمْ وَأَنتَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ شَهِيدٌ إِن تُعَذِّبْهُمْ فَإِنَّهُمْ عِبَادُكَ وَإِن تَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ فَإِنَّكَ أَنتَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ ، قَالَ اللَّهُ هَٰذَا يَوْمُ يَنفَعُ الصَّادِقِينَ صِدْقُهُمْ — And when Allah will say, ‘O Jesus son of Mary, did you say to the people, ‘‘Take me and my mother for two gods besides Allah’’?’ He will say, ‘Immaculate are You! It does not behoove me to say what I have no right to [say]. Had I said it, You would certainly have known it: You know whatever is in my self, and I do not know what is in Your Self. Indeed You are the Knower of the Unseen. I did not say to them [anything] except what You had commanded me [to say]: ‘‘Worship Allah, my Lord and your Lord.’’ And I was a witness to them so long as I was among them. But when You had taken me, You were Yourself the watcher over them, and You are witness to all things. If You punish them, they are indeed Your servants; but if You forgive them, You are indeed the All-mighty, the All-wise.’ Allah will say, ‘This is a day when the truthful will benefit from their truthfulness.’ (5:116-119).

This wondrous speech, which contains the very essence of servitude (العبودية) and includes the most eloquent of manners (الأدب), expresses what Jesus the Messiah (عليه السلام) saw as his own position regarding the Lordship of his Lord, and toward the people and their deeds. He mentioned that he saw himself, in relation to his Lord, as a servant who has no affair except compliance; he does not refrain except by command, nor does he act except by command. He was not commanded except to call to the worship of Allah alone, and he did not say to them except what he was commanded: "Worship Allah, my Lord and your Lord."

He had nothing to do with the people except to bear witness (الشهادة) over their deeds; as for what Allah does with them and to them on the day they return to Him, he has no part in that, whether He forgives or punishes.

If you say: Then what is the meaning of what preceded in the discussion on intercession (الشفاعة): that Jesus (عليه السلام) is among the intercessors on the Day of Resurrection, who will intercede and whose intercession will be accepted?

I say: The Quran is explicit, or nearly explicit, in that. The Exalted said: وَلَا يَمْلِكُ الَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِهِ الشَّفَاعَةَ إِلَّا مَن شَهِدَ بِالْحَقِّ وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ — Those whom they invoke besides Him do not possess [the power of] intercession, except those who bear witness to the truth and they know [it]. (43:86). And the Exalted said concerning him: وَيَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ يَكُونُ عَلَيْهِمْ شَهِيدًا — and on the Day of Resurrection he will be a witness against them (4:159). And the Exalted said: وَإِذْ عَلَّمْتُكَ الْكِتَابَ وَالْحِكْمَةَ وَالتَّوْرَاةَ وَالْإِنجِيلَ — and when I taught you the Book and wisdom, the Torah and the Evangel (5:110). The discussion on the meaning of intercession has already been exhaustively presented. This is different from the "atonement" (التفدية) which the Christians believe in—which is the nullification of recompense through ransom and compensation—for that nullifies the absolute Divine sovereignty (السلطنة المطلقة الإلهية), as will be explained. The verse only negates that [atonement]. As for intercession, the verse does not address its matter, neither by affirmation nor negation; for if it were in the process of affirming it—despite its incompatibility (1) with the context—the appropriate speech would have been: "and if You forgive them, indeed You are the All-Forgiving, the All-Merciful." And if it were in the process of negating it, there would be no reason to mention the witness over the people. This is a summary of what will come in the detailed interpretation of the verses, Allah willing.

As for what people said concerning Jesus (عليه السلام), although they split into various sects after him and differed in their paths—to the point that they may have exceeded seventy in terms of the general categories of their differences, while the specific sects and opinions are very many indeed.

However, the Quran only concerns itself with what they said regarding the matter of Jesus himself and his mother, because of its connection to the foundation of monotheism (التوحيد), which is the sole purpose of what the Quran and the upright primordial religion (الدين الفطري) call toward. As for some specifics like the issue of distortion (التحريف) and the issue of atonement (التفدية), it did not concern itself with them to that same extent.

What the Quran recounts from them or attributes to them is in His saying, the Exalted: وَقَالَتِ النَّصَارَى الْمَسِيحُ ابْنُ اللَّهِ — and the Christians say, ‘The Messiah is the son of Allah.’ (9:30), and what is in its meaning, like His saying: وَقَالُوا اتَّخَذَ الرَّحْمَٰنُ وَلَدًا سُبْحَانَهُ — And they say, ‘The All-beneficent has taken a son.’ Immaculate is He! (21:26). And in His saying: لَقَدْ كَفَرَ الَّذِينَ قَالُوا إِنَّ اللَّهَ هُوَ الْمَسِيحُ ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ — They are certainly faithless who say, ‘Allah is the Messiah, son of Mary.’ (5:72). And in His saying: لَّقَدْ كَفَرَ الَّذِينَ قَالُوا إِنَّ اللَّهَ ثَالِثُ ثَلَاثَةٍ — They are certainly faithless who say, ‘Allah is the third [person] of a trinity.’ (5:73). And in His saying: وَلَا تَقُولُوا ثَلَاثَةٌ — and do not say, ‘[God is] a trinity.’ (4:171).

These verses, although they apparently contain different words with varying contents and meanings—and therefore have sometimes been interpreted (2) as referring to the differences of sects, such as the Melkites who believe in literal sonship, the Nestorians who believe that the descent and sonship are like the radiance of light upon a transparent body like crystal, and the Jacobites who believe it is by way of transformation (الانقلاب), and that the Deity, the Glorified, transformed into flesh and blood.

(1) For the context is one of self-abasement (التذلل) rather than free expression (الاسترسال). (2) As al-Shahrastani did in al-Milal wa al-Nihal.

However, it is apparent that the Quran does not concern itself with the specifics of their various sects, but rather concerns itself with a single word common to all of them: "sonship" (البنوة), and that the Messiah is of the same nature as the Deity, the Glorified, and what branches from it of the talk of the Trinity (التثليث), even if they differed greatly in its interpretation and became deeply entrenched in dispute and conflict. The proof for this is the unity of the argument (الاحتجاج) brought against them in the Quranic language.

To clarify: the Torah and the current Gospels all explicitly state the monotheism of the Deity, the Exalted, on one hand, while the Gospel explicitly states sonship on the other, and states that the Son is the Father and none other.

They did not interpret the sonship found therein as being for honor (التشريف) or blessing (التبريك), despite there being instances of explicit statement of that, such as his saying: "But I say to you, love your enemies, bless those who curse you, do good to those who hate you, and pray for those who persecute you and mistreat you, so that you may be sons of your Father who is in heaven; for He makes His sun rise on the evil and on the good, and sends rain on the just and on the unjust.
