For if you love those who love you, what reward do you have? Do not even the tax collectors do the same? And if you greet only your brothers, what more are you doing than others? Do not even the Gentiles do the same? Be perfect, therefore, as your heavenly Father is perfect." (End of the fifth chapter of the Gospel of Matthew) (1).

And his saying also: "Let your light shine before others, so that they may see your good works and give glory to your Father who is in heaven." (Gospel of Matthew - Chapter Five).

And his saying also: "Beware of practicing your righteousness before other people in order to be seen by them, for then you will have no reward from your Father who is in heaven."

And his saying also in prayer: "Pray then like this: Our Father in heaven, hallowed be your name," etc.

And his saying also: "For if you forgive others their trespasses, your heavenly Father will also forgive you." All of this is in the sixth chapter of the Gospel of Matthew.

And his saying: "Be merciful, even as your Father is merciful." (Gospel of Luke - Chapter Six).

(1) The Arabic version printed in 1811 AD, from which we quote all that we quote in this research from the Arabic books of the Testament.

And his saying to Mary Magdalene: "Go to my brothers and say to them, 'I am ascending to my Father and your Father, to my God and your God.'" (Gospel of John - Chapter Twenty).

These and similar passages from the Gospels apply the term "Father" to Allah, the Exalted and Sanctified, in relation to Jesus and everyone else, as you see, by way of honor and the like.

Even if what is in some instances gives the impression that this sonship and fatherhood are a type of perfection leading to union (الاتحاد), such as his saying: "When Jesus had spoken these words, he lifted up his eyes to heaven, and said, 'Father, the hour has come; glorify your Son that the Son may glorify you.'" Then he mentioned his prayer for his messengers among his disciples, then said: "I do not ask for these only, but also for those who will believe in me through their word, that they may all be one, just as you, Father, are in me, and I in you, that they also may be in us, so that the world may believe that you have sent me. The glory that you have given me I have given to them, that they may be one even as we are one, I in them and you in me, that they may become perfectly one, so that the world may know that you sent me and loved them even as you loved me." (Gospel of John - Chapter Seventeen).

However, there occurred in them sayings whose apparent meanings resist being interpreted as honor and the like, such as his saying: "Thomas said to him, 'Lord, we do not know where you are going. How can we know the way?' Jesus said to him, 'I am the way, and the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me. If you had known me, you would have known my Father also. From now on you do know him and have seen him.' Philip said to him, 'Lord, show us the Father, and it is enough for us.' Jesus said to him, 'Have I been with you so long, and you still do not know me, Philip? Whoever has seen me has seen the Father. How can you say, "Show us the Father"? Do you not believe that I am in the Father and the Father is in me? The words that I say to you I do not speak on my own authority, but the Father who dwells in me does his works. Believe me that I am in the Father and the Father is in me.'" (Gospel of John - Chapter Fourteen).

And his saying: "For I came from God and I am here. I came not of my own accord, but he sent me." (Gospel of John - Chapter Eight).

And his saying: "I and the Father are one." (Gospel of John - Chapter Ten).

And his saying to his disciples: "Go therefore and make disciples of all nations, baptizing (1) them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit." (Gospel of Matthew - Chapter Twenty-eight).

(1) Baptism (التعميد) is a type of washing among Christians by which the one washed is purified from sins, and it is one of the obligations of the Church.

And his saying: "In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God. He was in the beginning with God. All things were made through him, and without him was not any thing made that was made. In him was life, and the life was the light of men." (Gospel of John - Chapter One).

These words and their like that occurred in the Gospel are what called the Christians to the belief in the Trinity (التثليث) within unity.

What is meant by it is to preserve "that the Messiah is the son of Allah" while maintaining the monotheism (التوحيد) which the Messiah stipulated in his teaching, as in his saying: "The most important is, 'Hear, O Israel: The Lord our God, the Lord is one.'" (Gospel of Mark - Chapter Twelve).

The summary of what they believe (even if it does not result in a rational conclusion) is: that the Essence (الذات) is one substance (جوهر) having three persons (أقانيم). What is meant by "person" (الاقنوم) is the attribute (الصفة) which is the mode of a thing's appearance, manifestation, and self-disclosure to another, and the attribute is not other than the described. The three persons are: the person of Existence (الوجود), the person of Knowledge (العلم)—which is the Word—and the person of Life (الحياة)—which is the Spirit.

These three persons are: the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit. The first is the person of Existence, the second is the person of Knowledge and the Word, and the third is the person of Life. Thus, the Son—who is the Word and the person of Knowledge—descended from his Father—who is the person of Existence—accompanied by the Holy Spirit—who is the person of Life by which things are illuminated.

Then they differed in the interpretation of this summary with a great difference that necessitated their fragmentation and branching into many sects and paths exceeding seventy, and the news of them will come to you to the extent that suits the nature of this book.

If you reflect on what we have presented, you will know: that what the Quran recounts from them, or attributes to them in His saying: "and the Christians say, ‘The Messiah is the son of Allah,’" and His saying: "They are certainly faithless who say, ‘Allah is the Messiah, son of Mary,’" and His saying: "They are certainly faithless who say, ‘Allah is the third [person] of a trinity,’" and His saying: "and do not say, ‘[God is] a trinity.’ Desist!"—all of that returns to one meaning (which is the Trinity of Unity) that is common among all the modern sects in Christianity, and it is what we presented in the meaning of the Trinity of Unity.

He restricted Himself to this common meaning because that which refutes their sayings regarding the Messiah (عليه السلام) specifically—despite their number and fragmentation—is one matter that refutes them in a single manner, as will become clear.

4 - The Quran's argument against the doctrine of the Trinity

The Quran, in its argument, refutes the claim of the Trinitarians through two paths: First, the general path, which is the demonstration of the impossibility of a son (الابن) for Him, the Exalted, in Himself—that is, whether Jesus was the son or someone else. Second, the specific path, which is the demonstration that Jesus son of Mary is not a divine son, but rather a created servant.

As for the first path, its clarification is that the reality of sonship (البنوة) and procreation (التولد) is that one of these living material beings—like humans, animals, or even plants—separates a part of its own substance, then makes it, through gradual nurturing, another individual of its kind similar to itself, upon whom the properties and effects that were upon the source part are established. For example, the animal separates from itself the sperm, and the plant separates from itself the pollen, then it begins its gradual nurturing until it becomes another animal or plant similar to itself. It is well-known that this is impossible for Allah, the Glorified: Firstly, because it necessitates material corporeality (الجسمية المادية), and Allah, the Glorified, is transcendent above matter and its needy concomitants such as motion (الحركة), time (الزمان), space (المكان), and so on. Secondly, because Allah, the Glorified, due to the absolute nature of His Divinity and Lordship, possesses absolute self-subsistence (القيومية المطلقة) over all else; everything besides Him is needy in its existence to Him, subsisting in its existence through Him. How then can one assume something other than Him that matches Him in kind, is independent of Him in itself, and possesses the essence, attributes, and laws that He possesses without being needy of Him? Thirdly, because the permissibility of begetting and procreation for Him, the Exalted, would necessitate the permissibility of gradual action (الفعل التدريجي) for Him, the Exalted, which would necessitate His falling under the law of matter and motion, which is a contradiction; rather, what occurs by His will and volition, the Exalted, occurs without delay or gradualness.

This demonstration is what is conveyed by His saying, the Exalted: وَقَالُوا اتَّخَذَ اللَّهُ وَلَدًا سُبْحَانَهُ بَل لَّهُ مَا فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ كُلٌّ لَّهُ قَانِتُونَ بَدِيعُ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَإِذَا قَضَىٰ أَمْرًا فَإِنَّمَا يَقُولُ لَهُ كُن فَيَكُونُ — And they say, ‘Allah has taken a son.’ Immaculate is He! Rather, to Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth. All are obedient to Him, the Originator of the heavens and the earth. When He decides on a matter, He just says to it, ‘Be!’ and it is. (2:116-117). According to what we have explained, His saying, "Immaculate is He" (سبحانه) is a proof; His saying, "to Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth. All are obedient to Him" is another proof; and His saying, "the Originator of the heavens and the earth. When He decides..." is a third proof.

It is possible to consider His saying, "the Originator of the heavens and the earth" (بديع السموات والأرض) as the addition of an attribute to its agent, and to understand from it that His creation, the Exalted, is without a prior model; thus, procreation is impossible from Him because it would be creation according to the model of Himself—since their assumption is identity (العينية)—so this phrase would be another proof.

Even if we assume their saying, "Allah has taken a son" was spoken not literally but metaphorically regarding the meaning of son and child—meaning the separation of something from something that matches it in reality without material division or temporal gradualness (and this is what the Christians aim for in their saying, "the Messiah is the son of Allah" after refining it)—to escape thereby the problem of corporeality, matter, and gradualness, the problem of similarity (المماثلة) remains.

To clarify: establishing the Son and the Father is necessarily establishing number, which is establishing real multiplicity (الكثرة الحقيقية), even if specific unity (الوحدة النوعية) is assumed between the Father and the Son—like the father and son among humans are one in human reality, yet many insofar as they are two individuals of man. Accordingly, if the unity of the Deity is assumed, everything besides Him—including the Son—would be other than Him, owned by Him, and needy of Him; thus, the assumed Son would not be a Deity like Him. And if a Son matching Him is assumed, not needy of Him but independent like Him, monotheism (التوحيد) regarding the Deity, exalted is His Name, would be nullified.

This demonstration is what is indicated by His saying, the Exalted: وَلَا تَقُولُوا ثَلَاثَةٌ انتَهُوا خَيْرًا لَّكُمْ إِنَّمَا اللَّهُ إِلَٰهٌ وَاحِدٌ سُبْحَانَهُ أَن يَكُونَ لَهُ وَلَدٌ لَّهُ مَا فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَمَا فِي الْأَرْضِ ، وَكَفَىٰ بِاللَّهِ وَكِيلًا — and do not say, ‘[God is] a trinity.’ Desist! It is better for you. Indeed Allah is but one God. Immaculate is He that He should have a son. To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth, and Allah suffices as trustee. (4:171).

As for the second path, which is the demonstration that the person of Jesus son of Mary (عليه السلام) is not a son of Allah participating with Him in the Divine reality, it is because of what was in him of humanity (البشرية) and its concomitants.

To clarify: the Messiah (عليه السلام) was carried by Mary, she nurtured him as a fetus in her womb, then she delivered him as a woman delivers her child, then she raised him as a child is raised in the care of his mother. Then he began to grow and pass through the stages of life and progress in the levels of age from childhood, youth, and middle age. In all of that, his state was the state of a natural human in his life; he was subject to the accidents and states that subject a human: hunger and satiety, joy and sorrow, pleasure and pain, eating and drinking, sleep and wakefulness, fatigue and rest, and so on.

This is what was witnessed of the state of the Messiah (عليه السلام) during his stay among the people. No rational person doubts that one whose state is this is a human like other humans of his kind. If he is so, then he is a created, made being (مخلوق مصنوع) like all other individuals of his kind. As for the occurrence of wonders and the realization of miracles at his hand—such as reviving the dead, creating the bird, and healing the blind and the leper—as well as the realization of wonders of signs in his existence—such as his formation without a father—these are only matters that break the familiar habit and the current law in nature; for they are rare in existence, not impossible. Here is Adam, whom the Heavenly Books mention was created from dust and had no father; and here are the prophets of Allah like Salih, Abraham, and Moses (عليهم السلام), at whose hands many miraculous signs occurred, mentioned in the records of revelation, without that necessitating divinity in them, nor a departure from the bounds of humanity.

This method is the one followed in His saying, the Exalted: لَّقَدْ كَفَرَ الَّذِينَ قَالُوا إِنَّ اللَّهَ ثَالِثُ ثَلَاثَةٍ وَمَا مِنْ إِلَٰهٍ إِلَّا إِلَٰهٌ وَاحِدٌ — They are certainly faithless who say, ‘Allah is the third [person] of a trinity,’ while there is no god except the One God. — until He said — مَّا الْمَسِيحُ ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ إِلَّا رَسُولٌ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِن قَبْلِهِ الرُّسُلُ وَأُمُّهُ صِدِّيقَةٌ كَانَا يَأْكُلَانِ الطَّعَامَ انظُرْ كَيْفَ نُبَيِّنُ لَهُمُ الْآيَاتِ ثُمَّ انظُرْ أَنَّىٰ يُؤْفَكُونَ — The Messiah, son of Mary, is but an apostle. Certainly [other] apostles have passed before him. His mother was a truthful woman. Both of them used to eat food. Behold how We clarify the signs for them, then behold how they are perverted! (5:73-75). He specifically mentioned "eating food" from among all actions because it is among the best of them in indicating materiality (المادية) and necessitating need and poverty (الحاجة والفاقة) which are incompatible with divinity. For it is well-known that one who hungers and thirsts by his nature, then is satiated by a meal or has his thirst quenched by a drink, possesses nothing but need and poverty which none but another can remove. What is the meaning of divinity for one of this standing? For he who is surrounded by need and requires something external to himself to remove it is deficient in himself, managed by another; he is not a God, self-sufficient in His essence, but rather a managed creature (مخلوق مدبر) under the Lordship of the one to whom his management returns.

To this, His saying, the Exalted, can return: لَّقَدْ كَفَرَ الَّذِينَ قَالُوا إِنَّ اللَّهَ هُوَ الْمَسِيحُ ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ قُلْ فَمَن يَمْلِكُ مِنَ اللَّهِ شَيْئًا إِنْ أَرَادَ أَن يُهْلِكَ الْمَسِيحَ ابْنَ مَرْيَمَ وَأُمَّهُ وَمَن فِي الْأَرْضِ جَمِيعًا وَلِلَّهِ مُلْكُ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا يَخْلُقُ مَا يَشَاءُ وَاللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ — They are certainly faithless who say, ‘Allah is the Messiah, son of Mary.’ Say, ‘Who can avail anything against Allah should He wish to destroy the Messiah, son of Mary, and his mother, and everyone upon the earth?’ To Allah belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth, and whatever is between them. He creates whatever He wishes, and Allah has power over all things. (5:17).

Likewise, His saying, the Exalted, at the end of the previously quoted verse (verse 75), addressing the Christians: قُلْ أَتَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ اللَّهِ مَا لَا يَمْلِكُ لَكُمْ ضَرًّا وَلَا نَفْعًا وَاللَّهُ هُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ — Say, ‘Do you worship besides Allah what has no power to bring you any benefit or harm, while Allah is the All-hearing, the All-knowing?’ (5:76).

For the criterion in this type of argument is that what was witnessed of the Messiah's affair is that he lived according to the law current in human life, characterized by all the specific human attributes, actions, and states—such as eating, drinking, and all other human needs and human properties. This being characterized and characterized was not according to the outward sense or the deception of imagination only, but was in reality; the Messiah (عليه السلام) was a human possessing these attributes, states, and actions. The Gospels are filled with his naming himself a human and the Son of Man, and full of stories speaking of his eating, drinking, sleeping, walking, traveling, fatigue, speaking, and the like, such that none of them accepts diversion or interpretation. With the admission of these matters, what applies to others applies to the Messiah; he does not possess anything from another, just like others, and he can be destroyed just like others.

Likewise, the matter of his worship and his prayer is such that there is no doubt that what he performed of worship was only for nearness to Allah and submission to the sanctity of His presence, not for teaching the people or for other purposes similar to that.

To the matter of worship and arguing with it, His saying, the Exalted, points: لَّن يَسْتَنكِفَ الْمَسِيحُ أَن يَكُونَ عَبْدًا لِّلَّهِ وَلَا الْمَلَائِكَةُ الْمُقَرَّبُونَ وَمَن يَسْتَنكِفْ عَنْ عِبَادَتِهِ وَيَسْتَكْبِرْ فَسَيَحْشُرُهُمْ إِلَيْهِ جَمِيعًا — The Messiah would never disdain to be a servant of Allah, nor would the angels brought near [to Him]. And whoever disdains His worship and is arrogant, He will soon muster them all toward Him. (4:172). The worship of the Messiah is the first proof that he is not a God, and that divinity belongs to another in which he has no share. For what is the meaning of a thing placing itself in the station of servitude and being owned by itself? And a thing being self-subsistent from the very same respect by which it subsists itself? The matter is clear. Likewise, the worship of the angels reveals that they are not the daughters of Allah, the Glorified, nor is the Holy Spirit a God, since they were all worshippers of Allah, obedient to Him, as the Exalted said: وَقَالُوا اتَّخَذَ الرَّحْمَٰنُ وَلَدًا سُبْحَانَهُ بَلْ عِبَادٌ مُّكْرَمُونَ لَا يَسْبِقُونَهُ بِالْقَوْلِ وَهُم بِأَمْرِهِ يَعْمَلُونَ يَعْلَمُ مَا بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَمَا خَلْفَهُمْ وَلَا يَشْفَعُونَ إِلَّا لِمَنِ ارْتَضَىٰ وَهُم مِّنْ خَشْيَتِهِ مُشْفِقُونَ — And they say, ‘The All-beneficent has taken a son.’ Immaculate is He! Rather, they are [His] honored servants. They do not precede Him in speech, and they act by His command. He knows that which is before them and that which is behind them, and they do not intercede except for him with whom He is pleased, and they are apprehensive for fear of Him. (21:26-28).

Furthermore, the Gospels are filled with the fact that the Spirit is obedient to Allah and His messengers, complying with the command and subject to the judgment; and there is no meaning to a thing commanding itself, nor to its obedience to its own essence, nor to its submission and compliance to its own creature.

Similar to the Messiah's worship of Allah, the Glorified, in indicating the distinction (المغايرة) is his calling the people to the worship of Allah, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: لَقَدْ كَفَرَ الَّذِينَ قَالُوا إِنَّ اللَّهَ هُوَ الْمَسِيحُ ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ ، وَقَالَ الْمَسِيحُ يَا بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ اعْبُدُوا اللَّهَ رَبِّي وَرَبَّكُمْ إِنَّهُ مَن يُشْرِكْ بِاللَّهِ فَقَدْ حَرَّمَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ الْجَنَّةَ وَمَأْوَاهُ النَّارُ وَمَا لِلظَّالِمِينَ مِنْ أَنصَارٍ — They are certainly faithless who say, ‘Allah is the Messiah, son of Mary.’ But the Messiah said, ‘O Children of Israel! Worship Allah, my Lord and your Lord. Indeed whoever ascribes partners to Allah, Allah shall forbid him [entry into] paradise, and his abode shall be the Fire, and the wrongdoers shall not have any helpers.’ (5:72). The path of the verse and its argument is manifest.

The Gospels are also replete with his call to Allah, the Glorified. Although they do not contain this comprehensive expression: اعبدوا الله ربي وربكم — "Worship Allah, my Lord and your Lord," they do include the call to the worship (العبادة) of Allah, and his acknowledgment that He is his Lord in Whose hand is the rein of his affairs, and his acknowledgment that He is the Lord of the people. His call does not explicitly include the worship of himself even once, despite what they contain of his saying: "I and my Father are one" (Gospel of John, Chapter 10). It is necessary, assuming its authenticity, to interpret it as meaning: "My obedience is the obedience of Allah," as the Exalted said in His Noble Book: مَّن يُطِعِ الرَّسُولَ فَقَدْ أَطَاعَ اللَّهَ — Whoever obeys the Apostle has certainly obeyed Allah (4:80).

5 — The Messiah is among the intercessors with Allah and is not a redeemer:

The Christians claimed that the Messiah redeemed them with his noble blood, and for this reason, they titled him "the Redeemer" (الفادي). They said: When Adam disobeyed Allah by eating from the forbidden tree in Paradise, he erred thereby and sin (الخطيئة) became attached to him; likewise, it became attached to his progeny after him as they procreated and reproduced. The recompense for sin is punishment in the Hereafter and eternal perdition (الهلاك الأبدي) from which there is no escape. Yet Allah, the Glorified, was Merciful and Just.

At that point, a difficult and insoluble problem arose: if He were to punish Adam and his progeny for their sin, it would contradict His mercy (الرحمة) for which He created them; and if He were to forgive them, it would contradict His justice (العدل), for the requirement of justice is to punish the criminal sinner for his crime and sin, just as its requirement is to reward the virtuous obedient one for his goodness and his [avoidance of] evil. (1)

This difficulty remained as it was until it was resolved through the blessing of the Messiah. This occurred by the Messiah (who is the Son of God, and is God Himself) entering a single womb from the progeny of Adam—that of Mary the Virgin—and being born from her as a human is born. Thus, he was a perfect man because he was the son of a man, and a perfect God because he was the Son of God—and the Son of God is God (Exalted is He)—infallible (معصوماً) from all sins and errors.

After he lived among the people for a brief period of time, socializing and mingling with them, eating and drinking with them, speaking to them and finding comfort in them, and walking among them, he was surrendered to his enemies so they might kill him with the most evil

(1) This is the view of most of them, though it appears from some, such as Priest Mar Isaac, that failing to penalize a crime or sin—in other words, breaking a threat (خلف الوعيد)—is permissible, unlike breaking a promise (خلف الوعد).

of deaths, which is the death of crucifixion (الصلب), the victim of which is cursed in the Divine Book. Thus, he bore the curse and the crucifixion with its attendant restraint, harm, and agony, redeeming the people with himself so they might be saved thereby from the punishment of the Hereafter and eternal perdition. He is the atonement (كفارة) for the sins of those who believe in him, rather, for the sins of the whole world. (1) This is what they have said.

The Christians have made this word—meaning the issue of the crucifixion and redemption (الفداء) — the act of delivering from sin through sacrifice — the foundation of their mission; they neither begin except with it, nor conclude except upon it. This is while the Quran makes the foundation of the Islamic mission monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — as Allah said, addressing His Messenger (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم): قُلْ هَٰذِهِ سَبِيلِي أَدْعُو إِلَى اللَّهِ عَلَىٰ بَصِيرَةٍ أَنَا وَمَنِ اتَّبَعَنِي وَسُبْحَانَ اللَّهِ وَمَا أَنَا مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ — Say, ‘This is my way. I summon to Allah with insight—I and he who follows me. Immaculate is Allah, and I am not one of the polytheists’ (12:108). This is despite the fact that the Messiah (according to what the Gospels state, as previously cited) used to make the first of the commandments monotheism (التوحيد) and the love of Allah, the Glorified.

Other Muslims and researchers have debated them regarding the aspects of corruption and falsehood contained in this claim of theirs. Books and treatises have been authored, and pages and scrolls filled with the explanation of its contradiction to the necessity of reason (ضرورة العقل) and its inconsistency with the books of the Two Testaments. What concerns us and accords with the purpose for which this book was established is the explanation of the aspects of its contradiction to the principles of the Quranic teaching, concluding with an explanation of the difference between what the Quran establishes of intercession (الشفاعة) — the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah — and what they establish of redemption (الفداء).

Furthermore, the Quran explicitly mentions that it only addresses people and speaks to them by explaining what is near the horizon of their intellects (عقولهم) and what its explanations can reach of their comprehension and understanding. This is the matter by which man distinguishes truth (الحق) from falsehood (الباطل), thus submitting to the former and rejecting the latter, and differentiates between good and evil, and the beneficial and the harmful, thus taking the former and leaving the latter. What we have mentioned regarding the Quran's consideration of the judgment of sound reason (العقل السليم) in its explanations is something beyond doubt for anyone who refers to the Mighty Book.

As for what they mentioned, it contains firstly: their mention of the disobedience (معصية) of Adam (عليه‌السلام) by eating from the forbidden tree. The Quran refutes this from two perspectives:

The first: that the prohibition there was an advisory prohibition (نهياً إرشادياً) intended for the welfare of the one prohibited and the direction of rectitude (الرشد) in his affair, not the exercise of mastership (المولوية). A command of this nature does not result in reward or masterly punishment (عقاب مولوي) upon its fulfillment or abandonment, like the commands and prohibitions of an advisor to the one who consults him, or the commands and prohibitions of a doctor to a patient. Rather, what results from the fulfillment of an advisory obligation is the rectitude intended for the benefit of the subject, and from its violation, falling into the corruption of the violation and the harm of the act insofar as it is an act. In short, nothing befell Adam (عليه‌السلام) except that he was removed from Paradise and missed the comfort of proximity and the joy of [divine] pleasure. As for the punishment of the Hereafter, no, because he did not commit a masterly disobedience (معصية مولوية) such that it would entail punishment. Refer to the commentary (تفسير) of verses 35–39 of Surah al-Baqarah.

The second: that he (عليه‌السلام) was a prophet, and the Quran sanctifies the station of the prophets (عليهم السلام) and absolves their noble souls from committing acts of disobedience or transgressing (الفسق) the command of Allah, the Glorified. Rational proof (البرهان العقلي) also supports this; refer to what we mentioned in the discussion on the infallibility (عصمة) of the prophets in the commentary of verse 213 of Surah al-Baqarah.

Secondly: their saying that the sin (الخطيئة) became attached to Adam. The Quran refutes this with His saying: ثُمَّ اجْتَبَاهُ رَبُّهُ فَتَابَ عَلَيْهِ وَهَدَىٰ — Then his Lord chose him, and He turned to him clemently, and guided him (20:122), and His saying: فَتَلَقَّىٰ آدَمُ مِن رَّبِّهِ كَلِمَاتٍ فَتَابَ عَلَيْهِ إِنَّهُ هُوَ التَّوَّابُ الرَّحِيمُ — Then Adam received certain words from his Lord, and He turned to him clemently. Indeed He is the All-clement, the All-merciful (2:37).

Rational consideration supports this, rather it clarifies it; for sin and the consequence of guilt (تبعة الذنب) are only a cautioned and feared matter that reason or the Master considers necessary for violation and rebellion to solidify the matter of obligation (التكليف). Were it not for punishment and reward, the matter of mastership (المولوية) would not be established, and neither command nor prohibition would be obeyed. Just as it is among the functions of mastership to extend punishment over criminals for their crimes, like reward for the obedient for their acts of obedience, it is also among the functions of mastership to have absolute discretion within the sphere of its mastership. Thus, the Master has the right to overlook the sin of the sinners and the disobedience of the disobedient through pardon (العفو) and forgiveness (المغفرة), for it is a type of discretion and governance (حكومة), just as He has the right to take them to account for it, which is also a type of governance. The goodness of pardon and forgiveness from masters and those of power and authority is generally beyond doubt, and the rational among humans have employed it until this time. Therefore, the notion that every sin originating from a human is necessarily attached to the human is entirely groundless; otherwise, the principle of pardon and forgiveness would have no reality. For forgiveness and pardon only exist to erase the sin and nullify the effect of the guilt. Given the assumption that the sin is necessarily attached and inseparable, no subject remains for pardon and forgiveness. This is while Divine Revelation is filled with the discourse of pardon and forgiveness, and the books of the Two Testaments are likewise, such that even this speech quoted from them is not devoid of it. In short, the claim that a sin among sins or an error among errors is necessarily attached and inherently incapable of being forgiven or erased—even through repentance (التوبة), turning back (الإنابة), return, and remorse—is something that neither sound reason nor a straight nature (طبع مستقيم) accepts.

Thirdly: their saying that Adam's sin, just as it was attached to him, was likewise attached to his progeny until the Day of Resurrection, necessitates that the consequence of a guilt originating from one person also encompasses others who did not sin in terms of masterly disobediences. In other words, that an act originates from one person and its disobedience and consequence generalize to someone other than its doer just as it encompasses its doer. This is different from a group committing a disobedience and others from their successors being pleased with it, such that the disobedience is counted against all. In short, it is the bearing of a burden (الوزر) without the commission of the guilt, and the Quran rejects this as in His saying: أَلَّا تَزِرُ وَازِرَةٌ وِزْرَ أُخْرَىٰ — that no bearer shall bear another’s burden — وَأَن لَّيْسَ لِلْإِنسَانِ إِلَّا مَا سَعَىٰ — and that nothing belongs to man except what he strives for (53:38–39). Reason supports this due to the ugliness of punishing one who did not sin for a guilt that did not originate from him. Refer to the discussions on acts (الأفعال) in the commentary of verses 216–218 of Surah al-Baqarah.

Fourthly: their discourse is based on the consequence of all sins and guilts being eternal perdition (الهلاك الأبدي) without distinction between them. Its necessity is that sins and guilts do not differ in terms of smallness or greatness; rather, all of them would be major, destructive sins (كبائر موبقات). What the Noble Quran views in its teaching is that sins and disobediences are diverse: among them are major sins (كبائر), and among them are minor sins (صغائر); among them are those reached by forgiveness, and among them are those not reached except through repentance, such as polytheism (الشرك). The Exalted said: إِن تَجْتَنِبُوا كَبَائِرَ مَا تُنْهَوْنَ عَنْهُ نُكَفِّرْ عَنكُمْ سَيِّئَاتِكُمْ — If you avoid the major sins that you are forbidden, We will absolve you of your misdeeds (4:31). And the Exalted said: إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَا يَغْفِرُ أَن يُشْرَكَ بِهِ وَيَغْفِرُ مَا دُونَ ذَٰلِكَ لِمَن يَشَاءُ — Indeed Allah does not forgive that any partner be ascribed to Him, but He forgives anything besides that to whomever He wishes (4:48). Thus, the Exalted made among the forbidden prohibited things—which are the sins and guilts—those that are major sins, and those that are misdeeds (سيئات), meaning minor sins, by the evidence of juxtaposition. And the Exalted made among the guilts those that do not accept forgiveness, and those that do. Thus, guilts are diverse in any case, and not every guilt necessitates abiding in the Fire and eternal perdition.

Furthermore, reason rejects the arrangement of all sins and their organization into a single string; for a slap is not like killing, and a suspicious look is not like adultery, and so on. The rational among humans in all eras have not placed every sin and error in the place of another, and they see for different disobediences different consequences and accountings. How then can it be correct to treat all of them in one way despite this glaring difference between them? If their difference is assumed, it would only be correct to assign eternal punishment and eternal perdition to some of them, such as polytheism (الشرك) against Allah, as the Noble Quran says. It is well known that violating a certain prohibition in eating from the tree does not take the place of disbelief (الكفر) in Allah the Great and what resembles that; thus, there is no reason to make its punishment and consequence eternal torment. (Refer to the previously mentioned discussion on acts).

Fifthly: what they mentioned regarding the occurrence of the problem and the emergence of conflict between the attribute of mercy (الرحمة) and the attribute of justice (العدل), then the stratagem to remove it by the descent of the Messiah and his ascent in the manner they described. One who reflects on this discourse and the necessities it entails finds that they view Allah—Exalted and Sanctified is He—as an existing Creator to Whom this created world with all its parts is attributed and terminates; however, He only acts by will (إرادة) and knowledge (علم) within Himself, and His will in its realization depends on a cognitive preference (ترجيح علمي), just as a human only wills something when he prefers it by his knowledge. Thus, there are interests (مصالح) and corruptions (مفاسد) upon which Allah applies His acts and thus performs them. He might err in the application and thus regret (1) the act; He might think about a matter and not be guided to the way of its rectitude; He might be ignorant of a matter. In short, He, the Exalted, in His attributes and acts, is like a human; He only does what He does through reflection and deliberation, seeking therein to apply His act to the interest. Thus, He is governed by the judgment of interests and overcome by their operation upon Him from the outside. It is possible for Him to be guided to rectitude, and it is possible for Him to stray, err, or be heedless; thus, He sometimes knows and sometimes is ignorant, and He sometimes overcomes and is sometimes overcome. His power (قدرته) is limited like His knowledge. If this which was mentioned is permissible for Him, then all else that befalls a reflecting, willing agent in his act—such as joy, sadness, praise, regret, elation, emotion, and so on—is also permissible for Him. One who is of this state would be a material (مادياً), corporeal (جسمانياً) being, falling under the law of motion (الحركة), change (التغير), and perfection-seeking (الاستكمال). One who is such is a contingent (ممكن), created being, rather a manufactured human, and not the Necessary (الواجب), the Exalted, the Creator of everything.

And you, by referring to the books of the Two Testaments, will find the truth of all that we have attributed to them regarding the Necessary (الواجب), the Exalted, in terms of His corporeality and His being characterized by all the attributes of corporeal beings, especially humans.

The Quran, in all these mentioned meanings, sanctifies Allah, the Exalted, from these superstitious delusions; as the Exalted says: سُبْحَانَ اللَّهِ عَمَّا يَصِفُونَ — Immaculate is Allah from what they attribute [to Him] (37:159). Decisive rational proofs (البراهين العقلية) are established that He, the Exalted, is an Essence (ذات) combining all attributes of perfection; thus, He has existence (الوجود) without any taint of non-existence (عدم),

(1) In the sixth chapter of the Book of Genesis in the Torah: "And God regretted having created the son of Adam on the earth" (Arabic Torah printed in the year 1811 AD).

absolute power (القدرة المطلقة) without any taint of weakness, absolute knowledge (العلم المطلق) without the occurrence of ignorance, and absolute life (الحيوة المطلقة) without the possibility of death or annihilation. If He is such, it is not permissible for a change of state to occur in His existence, knowledge, power, or life.

If He is such, He is neither a body (جسماً) nor corporeal (جسمانياً), because bodies and corporeal things are the locus of changes and transformations, and the sites of contingencies, dependencies, and needs. If He is neither a body nor corporeal, then different states and various occurrences do not befall Him: such as heedlessness, oversight, error, regret, confusion, being affected, emotion, humiliation, smallness, being overcome, and the like. We have fully provided the demonstrative research related to these meanings in this book in appropriate places; the researcher will find them if he refers to them.

The discerning critic and the reflecting contemplator must compare the two views: what the Noble Quran says regarding the God of the world—thus establishing for Him every attribute of perfection and sanctifying Him from every attribute of deficiency, and ultimately considering Him greater and more magnificent than for our understandings to judge Him with what they have accompanied from the world of limitation and measure; and what the Two Testaments establish regarding the Creator, the Exalted, which is not found except in the myths of Greece and the superstitions of ancient India and China, and matters that early man used to imagine and thus be affected by what his imagination presented to him.

Sixthly: their saying that Allah sent His son, the Messiah, and commanded him to enter one of the wombs to be born as a human while being God. This is the irrational claim for the explanation of whose falsehood the Noble Quran arose, as we clarified in the previous explanation, so we shall not repeat it.

It is well known that reason also does not support it; for if you reflect on the attributes that must be said to characterize the Necessary (الواجب), the Exalted—such as eternal stability, lack of change, lack of limitation of existence, encompassment of everything, and sanctification from time and space and what follows them—and you reflect on the formation of a human from the time of being a drop of fluid, then a fetus in a womb—whether you consider in its meaning the interpretation of the Melkites for this word, or the interpretation of the Nestorians, or the interpretation of the Jacobites or others—there is no proportion between that which has corporeality and all the attributes and effects of corporeality, and that in which there is no corporeality nor anything of what it is characterized by, such as time, space, motion, or otherwise. How then can the union (الاتحاد) between them be conceived in any way?

The lack of correspondence of the mentioned claim with necessary rational propositions (القضايا الضرورية العقلية) is the secret behind what Paul and others among their saintly leaders mention regarding the disparagement of philosophy and the belittling of rational judgments. Paul says: "For it is written, 'I will destroy the wisdom of the wise, and will bring to nothing the understanding of the prudent.' Where is the wise? Where is the scribe? Where is the disputer of this world? Hath not God made foolish the wisdom of this world? ... For the Jews require a sign, and the Greeks seek after wisdom: But we preach (1) Christ crucified" (Epistle of Paul, Chapter 1). Similar words are many in his speech and the speech of others, and they are only for the policy of dissemination, broadcasting, preaching, and exhortation; one who directs his gaze to these epistles and books and delves into their method of addressing people and delivering their explanations to them will be certain of this.

From what has passed, it becomes clear what is in their saying: that He, the Exalted, is infallible (معصوم) from sins and guilts; for the God they have depicted is not protected from error at all, in the sense of error in perception and error in action, without it terminating in the violation of one whose agreement is mandatory.

As for sin (الذنب) and disobedience (المعصية) in the sense of rebellion in what obedience and submission are mandatory, it is inconceivable in His right, the Exalted; thus, infallibility (العصمة) is also inconceivable in His right, the Glorified.

Seventhly: their saying that after he became a human, he socialized with people as a human socializes with a human, until he was surrendered to his enemies. In this is the permission for the Necessary (الواجب) to be characterized by one of the realities (حقائق) of contingent beings (الممكنات), such that he would be God and man in the same rank. Thus, it would be permissible for the Necessary to become something from His creatures—meaning to be characterized by the reality of every species among these external species. So, at one time He would be a human among humans, at another a horse, at another a bird, at another an insect, at another something else, and at another He would be more than one species among the species, such as a human, a horse, and an insect together.

Likewise, it would be permissible for any assumed act among the acts of existing things to originate from Him, due to the permissibility of Him becoming that species and thus performing its specific act. Similarly, it would be permissible for contradictory acts to originate from Him together, such as justice and injustice, and for Him to be characterized by contradictory attributes, such as knowledge and ignorance, power and weakness, life and death, and wealth and poverty. Exalted is the King, the Truth; and this is other than the previously mentioned difficulty in the sixth point.

(1) Karaza (كرز): to preach and proclaim.

Eighthly: their saying that he bore the crucifixion and the curse (اللعن) as well, because the crucified is cursed. What do they mean by their saying that he bore the curse? And what is meant by this curse? Is it this curse known by convention and language, which is distancing from mercy and dignity, or something else? If it is that which we know and which the language knows, then what is the meaning of Him, the Exalted, distancing Himself from mercy, or someone else distancing Him from mercy? Is mercy anything other than the existential effusion (الفيض الوجودي), the gift of blessing, and the specialization with the advantages of existence? Thus, this distancing and curse would return, according to the meaning, to poverty in wealth or status or the like in this world or the Hereafter or both. At that point, what is the meaning of the curse attaching to Allah—Exalted and Sanctified is He—in whatever way they conceived it? This is while He is the Rich by Essence (الغني بالذات) Who closes the door of poverty for everything.

The Quranic teaching is contrary to this strange teaching in every sense of the word. The Exalted said: يَا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ أَنتُمُ الْفُقَرَاءُ إِلَى اللَّهِ وَاللَّهُ هُوَ الْغَنِيُّ الْحَمِيدُ — O mankind! You are the ones who stand in need of Allah, and Allah—He is the All-sufficient, the All-laudable (35:15). The Quran names Him, the Exalted, with names and describes Him with attributes with which it is impossible for any poverty, destitution, need, deficiency, loss, non-existence, evil, ugliness, humiliation, or lowliness to occur in His courtyard of sanctity and grandeur.

If it is said: His being characterized by humiliation and His bearing the curse was by means of his union (اتحاده) with the human; otherwise, He, the Exalted, in Himself and regarding His Essence, is too magnificent for that to befall Him.

It is said to them: Does this union necessitate his bearing the curse and his being characterized by these difficult matters in reality (حقيقة) and without metaphor (مجاز), or not? If it is the first, the difficulty we mentioned follows; and if it is the second, the problem returns—meaning that the birth of the Messiah did not necessitate the resolution of the problem of the conflict between mercy and justice. For the bearing by someone other than Him, the Exalted, of the calamities and types of torment and curse does not complete the matter of redemption (الفدية)—meaning Allah becoming a ransom for human individuals—and this is obvious.

Ninthly: their saying that this is an atonement (كفارة) for the sins of those who believe in Jesus, rather for the sins of the whole world. This indicates that they have not grasped the reality of the meaning of guilts and sins, and how they entail punishment in the Hereafter, and how this punishment is realized. Nor have they known the reality of the connection between these guilts and sins and legislation (التشريع), and what the position of legislation is regarding that, according to what the Quranic explanation and its teaching guarantee.

We have explained in previous discussions in this book—among them what is in the commentary of His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَا يَسْتَحْيِي أَن يَضْرِبَ مَثَلًا مَّا — Indeed Allah is not ashamed to strike a similitude (2:26), and under His saying, the Exalted: كَانَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً وَاحِدَةً — Mankind were a single community (2:213)—that the rulings and laws in which violation and rebellion, then guilt and sin, occur are only conventional, relative matters (امور وضعية اعتبارية). Their establishment and consideration were intended to preserve the interests of human society through acting upon them and observing them, and that the punishment (العقاب) resulting from disobedience and violation is only an evil consequence they considered and established so that it would be a deterrent for the obligated human from committing disobedience and rebelling against obedience. This is what is held by the rational who build human society.

However, the Quranic teaching gives in this meaning what is higher and more subtle than that, and rational research supports it as has passed. It is that man, through his submission to the law (الشرع) established for him from the side of Allah and his lack of submission to it, prepares in his soul realities of internal attributes—either praiseworthy and virtuous, or vile, base, and wicked. These are what prepare for man a blessing in the Hereafter or a vengeance in the Hereafter, whose representatives are Paradise and the Fire, and whose reality is proximity to and distance from Allah. Thus, good deeds or sins rely upon and terminate in real matters (امور حقيقية) that have a system of reality, not a conventional one.

It is also clear that Divine legislation (التشريع الالهي) is only a completion of the Divine perfecting in creation, and the bringing of existential guidance (الهداية التكوينية) to its goal and objective from creation. In other words, His function, the Exalted, is to bring every species to the perfection of its existence and the objective of its essence. Among the perfection of human existence is the sound social system in this world, and the blissful, happy life in the Hereafter. The way to that is the religion (الدين) which guarantees sound laws for the reform of society and aspects of proximity in the name of acts of worship (العبادات) which man performs. Thereby his livelihood is organized, and he is prepared in his soul and reformed in his essence and action for Divine dignity in the Abode of the Hereafter. All of that is from the perspective of the light placed in his heart and the purity obtained in his soul; this is the truth of the matter.

Thus, for man there is proximity to and distance from Allah, the Glorified, which are the two criteria for his permanent happiness and wretchedness, and for the reform of his civil society in this world. Religion is the sole factor in creating this proximity and distance, and all of these are real matters not based on vanity or randomness.

If we assume that the commission of a single disobedience—like eating from the forbidden tree by Adam—necessitated for him permanent perdition, and not for him alone but for all his progeny, and then there was nothing with which to treat the disease and relieve the distress except the redemption of the Messiah, then what is the benefit of the legislation of religion before the Messiah? And what is the benefit of its legislation with him? And what is the benefit of its legislation after him?!

That is because when it was assumed that permanent perdition and punishment in the Hereafter were inevitable from the perspective of the occurrence of the disobedience—such that neither action nor repentance would benefit in diverting it from man except by way of redemption—there was no meaning for the legislation of laws, the sending down of books, and the sending of messengers from Allah, the Glorified. Promise and threat, warning and glad tidings, would remain devoid of any aspect of correctness. What then could this effort possibly reform after the necessity of torment and the inevitability of corruption?

And if we assume there were those who perfected themselves by acting upon the previous laws (and how many prophets and godly people from previous nations were such, like the noble Prophet Abraham and Moses, peace be upon them, and others) and they passed away and died before reaching the time of redemption, what do you see? Do you see that they concluded life upon wretchedness or happiness? And what did death and the world of the Hereafter receive them with? Did it receive them with punishment and perdition, or with reward and a happy life?

This is while the Messiah explicitly states that he was only sent to save the sinners and the mistaken; as for the righteous and the good, they have no need for that. (1)

In short, there remains for the legislation of Divine laws and the establishment of religious codes before the redemption of the Messiah no sound purpose that would protect it from futility and vanity. Nor is there for this strange act from Allah—Exalted and Sanctified is He—any true interpretation except to say: He, the Exalted, knew that if He did not remove the difficulty of Adam's sin, none of these legislations would ever benefit him. He only legislated these laws by way of precaution, in the hope that He would one day succeed in removing the difficulty and reap the fruit of His legislation after that, reaching His goal and attaining His wish then. So He legislated what He legislated while concealing the matter from the prophets and the people, and hiding that there was a difficulty here which, if not removed, would cause the efforts of the prophets and all the believers to fail, and the laws to go in vain; while manifesting that the legislation and the mission were in earnest and reality.

Thus He deceived the people and deceived Himself: as for the deception of the people, it was by manifesting that acting upon the laws guarantees their forgiveness and happiness; and as for the deception of Himself, it is because legislation after the removal of the difficulty through redemption returns

(1) "And the Pharisees and their scribes murmured against his disciples, saying, 'Why do you eat and drink with tax collectors and sinners?' And Jesus answering said unto them, 'They that are whole need not a physician; but they that are sick. I came not to call the righteous, but sinners to repentance'" (Gospel of Luke, Chapter 5).

to being vain, having no effect on the happiness of the people, just as without the removal of the difficulty it had no effect. This then is the state of the legislation of religion before the arrival of the time of redemption and its realization!

As for during the time of redemption and after it, the matter of the legislation, the religious mission, and the Divine guidance becoming vain is clearer and more evident. For what is the benefit in believing in the true gnoses (المعارف الحقة) and performing righteous deeds (الأعمال الصالحة) after the removal of the difficulty of sin, and the necessity of the descent of forgiveness and mercy upon the people—their believer and their disbeliever, their righteous and their profligate—without distinction between the most God-conscious of the God-conscious and the most wretched of the wretched in that they both share in eternal perdition with the persistence of the sin, and in the necessary mercy with its removal through redemption? And the assumption is that no righteous deed benefits in removing it without redemption.

If it is said: Redemption only benefits in the right of one who believes in the Messiah; thus, the mission has a fruit, as the Messiah explicitly states in his glad tidings. (1)

It is said: In addition to it contradicting what was previously indicated of John's words in his epistle, it is a destruction of all the past principles. For there would remain of the people—from Adam downwards—in the enclosure of salvation and deliverance none but a small group of them, and they are the believers in the Messiah and the Spirit, rather one of their various sects in the principles. As for others, they remain in eternal perdition. I wonder then, to what does the affair of the noble prophets before the Messiah and the affair of the believers from their nations lead? And with what is the mission they brought of book and wisdom characterized—with truth or with falsehood? The Gospels verify the Torah and its mission, and there is not in it a call to the story of the Spirit and redemption! Does it verify what is true, or does it verify the liar?

If it is said: The previous Heavenly Books, as far as we know, give glad tidings of the Messiah; and this from them is a general call to the Messiah even if it does not detail the discourse on the manner of his descent and his redemption. Thus, Allah did not cease giving glad tidings to His prophets of the appearance of the Messiah so they might believe in him and be of good cheer regarding what he would do.

It is said: Firstly: To say so before Moses is a conjecture regarding the Unseen (الغيب); furthermore, if the glad tidings

(1) "I say unto you, Whosoever shall confess me before men, him shall the Son of man also confess before the angels of God: But he that denieth me before men shall be denied before the angels of God. And whosoever shall speak a word against the Son of man, it shall be forgiven him: but unto him that blasphemeth against the Holy Ghost it shall not be forgiven" (Gospel of Luke, Chapter 12).

were given, they are only glad tidings of deliverance and are not a call to faith and practicing religion through it. Secondly: That does not repel the difficulty of the vanity of the mission in the branches of religion (فروع الدين) consisting of ethics and actions, even from the Messiah himself, and the Gospels are full of that. Thirdly: The difficulty of the sin and the frustration of the Divine purpose remains as it was; for Allah, the Exalted, only created them to have mercy on all of them and to spread blessing and happiness over all of them, yet His affair led to their punishment, wrath upon them, and their eternal perdition except for a small group of them.

These are a few of the aspects of its corruption according to reason, and the Noble Quran supports it and follows this course. The Exalted said: الَّذِي أَعْطَىٰ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلْقَهُ ثُمَّ هَدَىٰ — He who gave everything its creation and then guided it (20:50). Thus He explained that everything is guided to its goal and what it seeks by its existence. Guidance encompasses the existential (التكوينية) and the legislative (التشريعية); thus, the Divine custom (السنة الالهية) proceeds upon the spreading of guidance, and among it is the guidance of man—a religious guidance.

Then the Exalted said—and it is the first religious guidance He delivered to Adam and those with him at the time of their descent from Paradise: قُلْنَا اهْبِطُوا مِنْهَا جَمِيعًا فَإِمَّا يَأْتِيَنَّكُم مِّنِّي هُدًى فَمَن تَبِعَ هُدَايَ فَلَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ وَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَكَذَّبُوا بِآيَاتِنَا أُولَٰئِكَ أَصْحَابُ النَّارِ هُمْ فِيهَا خَالِدُونَ — We said, ‘Get down from it, all of you! Should any guidance come to you from Me, those who follow My guidance shall have no fear, nor shall they grieve. But those who are faithless and deny Our signs, they shall be the inmates of the Fire and they shall remain in it [forever]’ (2:38–39). What it contains is in the position of a summary for the details of the laws until the Day of Resurrection; in it is legislation, promise, and threat upon it without hesitation or doubt. And the Exalted has said: الْحَقَّ أَقُولُ — I speak the truth (38:84). And the Exalted said: مَا يُبَدَّلُ الْقَوْلُ لَدَيَّ وَمَا أَنَا بِظَلَّامٍ لِّلْعَبِيدِ — The word is not altered with Me, and I am not tyrannical to My servants (50:29). Thus He explained that He does not hesitate in what He has determined of the matter, nor does He nullify what He has executed of the matter. What He decrees is what He carries out, and He only does what He said. Thus His act does not deviate from the course He intended for it—not from His own side, such that He would intend something and then hesitate in its performance, or intend it and then it would appear differently to Him (يبدو له) so He does not do it; nor from the side of another, such that He would intend something, be certain of it, and resolve upon it, and then a preventer from reason would prevent Him or a difficulty would appear that would confront Him in the way of the act. All of that is from the coercion of a coercer and the overcoming of an external preventer. The Exalted said: وَاللَّهُ غَالِبٌ عَلَىٰ أَمْرِهِ — Allah has full control over His affairs (12:21). And the Exalted said: إِنَّ اللَّهَ بَالِغُ أَمْرِهِ — Allah carries through His command (65:3). And the Exalted said, narrating from Moses: قَالَ عِلْمُهَا عِندَ رَبِّي فِي كِتَابٍ لَّا يَضِلُّ رَبِّي وَلَا يَنسَى — He said, ‘The knowledge of them is with my Lord in a Book. My Lord neither errs nor forgets’ (20:52). And the Exalted said: الْيَوْمَ تُجْزَىٰ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ بِمَا كَسَبَتْ لَا ظُلْمَ الْيَوْمَ إِنَّ اللَّهَ سَرِيعُ الْحِسَابِ — Today every soul shall be recompensed for what it has earned. There shall be no injustice today. Indeed Allah is swift at reckoning (40:17).

These verses and what resembles them indicate that He, the Exalted, only created the creation and was not heedless of its affair, nor was He ignorant of anything that would appear from it, nor did He regret what He did. Then He legislated for them the laws—a serious, decisive legislation without jest, fear, or hope. Then He recompenses every possessor of an act with his act: if good, then good; and if evil, then evil, without any overcomer overcoming Him, the Exalted, or any judge judging over Him—whether a partner, a redemption, a friendship, or an intercession without His permission. All of that contradicts His absolute ownership (ملكه المطلق) of what is other than Him among His creation.

Tenthly: what they mentioned regarding the discourse of redemption (الفداء). The reality of redemption is that an evil effect—of killing or annihilation—attaches to a person or what relates to him of soul or wealth, so he is compensated with something else—whatever it may be—so that he may be protected thereby from that effect attaching to him. Just as a person redeems a captive with a person or wealth, and just as crimes and offenses are redeemed with wealth; the substitute is called a ransom (فدية) and redemption (فداء). Thus, redeeming is a type of transaction (معاملة) by which the right of the possessor of the right and his authority is extracted from the one redeemed for to the redemption; thus the one redeemed for is rescued from the evil attaching to him.

From here it becomes clear that redemption is irrational in what relates to Allah, the Glorified. For Divine authority (السلطنة الإلهية)—contrary to human conventional, relative authority—is a real, actual authority that is not permissible to be exchanged and is impossible to be diverted. Thus, things in their essences and their effects are existing and established by Allah, the Glorified. How then can the change of reality from what it is be conceived? It is nothing but a matter that cannot be conceptualized, let alone be possible of occurrence. This is contrary to ownership (الملك), authority (السلطنة), right (الحق), and their likes circulating among us, the children of society; for they and their likes are conventional, relative matters (امور وضعية اعتبارية) whose rein is in our hands. We, the collective, nullify them once and exchange them another time according to the change of our interests in life and livelihood. (Refer to the previous discussion in the commentary of His saying, the Exalted: مَالِكِ يَوْمِ الدِّينِ — Master of the Day of Retribution (1:4), and His saying, the Exalted: قُلِ اللَّهُمَّ مَالِكَ الْمُلْكِ — Say, ‘O Allah, Master of all sovereignty’ (3:26)).

Allah, the Glorified, has specifically negated the ransom (الفدية) in His saying: فَالْيَوْمَ لَا يُؤْخَذُ مِنكُمْ فِدْيَةٌ وَلَا مِنَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مَأْوَاكُمُ النَّارُ — So today no ransom shall be taken from you or from the faithless. Your abode is the Fire (57:15). It has passed in what preceded that of this type is the saying of the Messiah in what Allah, the Exalted, narrates from him: وَإِذْ قَالَ اللَّهُ يَا عِيسَى ابْنَ مَرْيَمَ أَأَنتَ قُلْتَ لِلنَّاسِ اتَّخِذُونِي وَأُمِّيَ إِلَٰهَيْنِ مِن دُونِ اللَّهِ قَالَ سُبْحَانَكَ مَا يَكُونُ لِي أَنْ أَقُولَ مَا لَيْسَ لِي بِحَقٍّ — And when Allah will say, ‘O Jesus son of Mary! Was it you who said to the people, ‘‘Take me and my mother for two gods besides Allah’’?’ He will say, ‘Immaculate are You! It does not behoove me to say what I have no right to [say]’ — until His saying — مَا قُلْتُ لَهُمْ إِلَّا مَا أَمَرْتَنِي بِهِ أَنِ اعْبُدُوا اللَّهَ رَبِّي وَرَبَّكُمْ وَكُنتُ عَلَيْهِمْ شَهِيدًا مَّا دُمْتُ فِيهِمْ فَلَمَّا تَوَفَّيْتَنِي كُنتَ أَنتَ الرَّقِيبَ عَلَيْهِمْ وَأَنتَ عَلَىٰ كُلشيء شَهِيدٌ إِن تُعَذِّبْهُمْ فَإِنَّهُمْ عِبَادُكَ وَإِن تَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ فَإِنَّكَ أَنتَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ — I did not say to them [anything] except what You had commanded me [to say]: ‘‘Worship Allah, my Lord and your Lord.’’ And I was a witness to them so long as I was among them. But when You had taken me away, You were Yourself the watcher over them, and You are witness to all things. If You punish them, they are indeed Your servants; but if You forgive them, You are indeed the All-mighty, the All-wise’ (5:116–118).

For his saying: "And I was a witness to them..." etc., is in the meaning that I had no function among them except what You assigned to me and designated, which is the delivery of the message and the witnessing over the acts as long as I was among them. As for their perdition, their salvation, their punishment, and their forgiveness, that is only up to You, without anything of that being linked to me or me having any function in it, such that I would possess for them anything from You by which I would remove them from Your punishment or Your authority over them. In that is the negation of redemption (الفداء); for if there were a redemption there, his disavowal of their acts and the returning of both punishment and forgiveness together to Allah, the Glorified, by negating their connection to him entirely, would not be correct.

In the meaning of these verses is His saying, the Exalted: وَاتَّقُوا يَوْمًا لَّا تَجْزِي نَفْسٌ عَن نَّفْسٍ شَيْئًا وَلَا يُقْبَلُ مِنْهَا شَفَاعَةٌ وَلَا يُؤْخَذُ مِنْهَا عَدْلٌ وَلَا هُمْ يُنصَرُونَ — Beware of the day when no soul shall compensate for another, neither any intercession shall be accepted from it, nor any ransom shall be received from it, nor will they be helped (2:48). Likewise His saying, the Exalted: يَوْمٌ لَّا بَيْعٌ فِيهِ وَلَا خُلَّةٌ وَلَا شَفَاعَةٌ — a day in which there will be no bargaining, neither friendship, nor intercession (2:254). And His saying, the Exalted: يَوْمَ تُوَلُّونَ مُدْبِرِينَ مَا لَكُم مِّنَ اللَّهِ مِنْ عَاصِمٍ — the day you will turn to flee, without there being any defender for you against Allah (40:33). For the "ransom" (عدل) in the first verse, the "bargaining" (بيع) in the second verse, and the "defender" (عاصم) from Allah in the third verse are what redemption (الفداء) applies to; thus their negation is the negation of redemption.

Yes, the Noble Quran established in the case of the Messiah intercession (الشفاعة) instead of what they establish of redemption. The difference between them is that intercession (as the discussion regarding it has passed under His saying, the Exalted: وَاتَّقُوا يَوْمًا لَّا تَجْزِي (2:48)) is a type of manifestation of the proximity of the intercessor and his status with the one interceded with, without the intercessor possessing anything from Him, or ownership or authority being stripped from Him, or His judgment which the criminal violated being nullified, or the law of recompense being nullified. Rather, it is only a type of prayer and request from the intercessor for a discretion from the one interceded with—who is the Lord—in what is permissible for Him of discretion in His kingdom. This permissible discretion despite the existence of the right is the pardon (العفو) permissible for the Master despite His having the right to punish him for the place of disobedience and the law of punishment.

Thus, the intercessor urges Him and requests from Him to act with pardon and forgiveness in the case of the desert of punishment for the disobedience, without ownership or authority being stripped from the Master. This is contrary to redemption (الفداء), for it is—as has passed—a transaction by which authority is exchanged from one thing to another thing which is the redemption, and the one redeemed for exits from the authority of the receiver taking the redemption.

What we have mentioned is indicated by His saying, the Exalted: وَلَا يَمْلِكُ الَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِهِ الشَّفَاعَةَ إِلَّا مَن شَهِدَ بِالْحَقِّ وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ — Those whom they invoke besides Him do not possess [the power of] intercession, except those who bear witness to the truth and they know [it] (43:86). For it is explicit in the occurrence of intercession from the excepted [group]. The Messiah (عليه‌السلام) was among those whom they used to invoke besides Allah. The Quran has stated that Allah taught him the Book and wisdom, and that he is among the witnesses on the Day of Resurrection. The Exalted said: وَيُعَلِّمُهُمُ الْكِتَابَ وَالْحِكْمَةَ — and He teaches them the Book and wisdom (3:164). And the Exalted said, narrating from him: وَكُنتُ عَلَيْهِمْ شَهِيدًا مَّا دُمْتُ فِيهِمْ — And I was a witness to them so long as I was among them (5:117). And the Exalted said: وَيَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ يَكُونُ عَلَيْهِمْ شَهِيدًا — and on the Day of Resurrection he will be a witness against them (4:159).

The verses, as you see, indicate that the Messiah (عليه‌السلام) is among the intercessors (الشفعاء). The detailed discourse on this meaning has passed in the commentary of His saying, the Exalted: وَاتَّقُوا يَوْمًا لَّا تَجْزِي نَفْسٌ عَن نَّفْسٍ شَيْئًا — Beware of the day when no soul shall compensate for another... (2:48).

6 — From where did these views originate?

The Quran negates that the Messiah (عليه‌السلام) was the one who delivered these views and beliefs to them and promoted them among them. Rather, they followed their leaders in religion and surrendered the matter to them, and they [the leaders] transferred to them the beliefs of the past pagans (الوثنيين), as the Exalted said: وَقَالَتِ الْيَهُودُ عُزَيْرٌ ابْنُ اللَّهِ وَقَالَتِ النَّصَارَى الْمَسِيحُ ابْنُ اللَّهِ ذَٰلِكَ قَوْلُهُم بِأَفْوَاهِهِمْ يُضَاهِئُونَ قَوْلَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِن قَبْلُ قَاتَلَهُمُ اللَّهُ أَنَّىٰ يُؤْفَكُونَ اتَّخَذُوا أَحْبَارَهُمْ وَرُهْبَانَهُمْ أَرْبَابًا مِّن دُونِ اللَّهِ وَالْمَسِيحَ ابْنَ مَرْيَمَ وَمَا أُمِرُوا إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُوا إِلَٰهًا وَاحِدًا لَّا إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ سُبْحَانَهُ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ — The Jews say, ‘Ezra is the son of Allah,’ and the Christians say, ‘The Messiah is the son of Allah.’ That is an opinion they utter with their mouths, imitating the opinions of the faithless of former times. May Allah assail them, where do they stray? They have taken their scribes and their monks as lords besides Allah, and also the Messiah, son of Mary; though they were not commanded except to worship the One God: there is no god except Him. Immaculate is He from any partners they may ascribe [to Him]! (9:30–31).

These disbelievers to whom the Exalted refers by His saying: "imitating the opinions of the faithless of former times," are not the Arabs of the Jahiliyyah in their paganism where they said: "The angels are the daughters of Allah." For their saying that Allah has a son is older in history than their contact with the Arabs and their mingling with them, especially the saying of the Jews to that effect, while the apparent meaning of His saying: "of former times," is that they preceded the Jews and Christians in it. Furthermore, the taking of idols in the Jahiliyyah was among what was transferred to them from others, and they were not innovators in that. (1)

(1) They mentioned that the first to place idols upon the Kaaba and call the people to them was Amr ibn Luhay. He was in the time of Shapur Dhul-Aktaf, led his people in Mecca, and took over the custodianship of the House. Then he traveled to the city of Balqa in the land of Sham and saw a people worshipping idols. He asked them about them, and they said: "These are lords we have taken in the form of celestial temples and human persons; we seek victory through them and are granted victory, and we seek rain through them and are granted rain." He requested from them an idol from their idols, and they gave him Hubal. He returned to Mecca and placed it upon the Kaaba and called the people to its worship. With him were Isaf and Na'ila in the form of a couple, so he called the people to them and to seeking proximity to Allah through them—mentioned in al-Milal wa al-Nihal and elsewhere. It is a strange matter that the Quran mentions names from the idols of the Arabs in the story of Noah and his complaint of his people; the Exalted said, narrating from him: وَقَالُوا لَا تَذَرُنَّ آلِهَتَكُمْ وَلَا تَذَرُنَّ وَدًّا وَلَا سُوَاعًا وَلَا يَغُوثَ وَيَعُوقَ وَنَسْرًا — and they say, ‘Do not abandon your gods. Do not abandon Wadd, nor Suwa, nor Yaghuth, Ya‘uq and Nasr’ (71:23).

Furthermore, the paganism (الوثنية) of the Romans, Greeks, Egyptians, Syrians, and Indians was closer to the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب) residing in Palestine and its surroundings, and the transfer of beliefs and religious claims to them from them was easier, and the causes for that were more fitting.

Thus, those who disbelieved whom the People of the Book imitated in the claim of sonship are none other than the ancient pagans of India and China, and the pagans of the West from the Romans, Greeks, and North Africa. Just as history narrates from them the likes of these claims found in the People of the Book among the Jews and Christians—of sonship, fatherhood, the Trinity (التثليث), the discourse of the crucifixion and redemption, and so on. This is among the historical realities to which the Noble Quran draws attention.

Similar to the previous verses in indicating this reality is His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ يَا أَهْلَ الْكِتَابِ لَا تَغْلُوا فِي دِينِكُمْ غَيْرَ الْحَقِّ وَلَا تَتَّبِعُوا أَهْوَاءَ قَوْمٍ قَدْ ضَلُّوا مِن قَبْلُ وَأَضَلُّوا كَثِيرًا وَضَلُّوا عَن سَوَاءِ السَّبِيلِ — Say, ‘O People of the Book! Do not exceed the bounds in your religion unduly, and do not follow the desires of a people who went astray before, and led many astray, and strayed from the right way’ (5:77). For the verse explains that their exceeding the bounds (الغلو) in religion unduly only befell them through imitation (التقليد) and following the desires of a people who went astray before them.

What is meant by these people is not their scribes and monks, for the speech is absolute and not restricted, and He did not say: "a people from among you," or "and led many from among you astray." Nor is what is meant by them the Arabs of the Jahiliyyah as previously mentioned; furthermore, He described these people as having led many astray—meaning they were leaders of misguidance who were imitated and followed—and the Arabs at that time were only a small, oppressed, unlettered group who did not possess the knowledge, civilization, and progress for which other nations like Persia, Rome, and India would follow them.

Thus, what is meant by these mentioned people is none other than the pagans of China, India, and the West as previously mentioned.

7 — What is the book attributed to the People of the Book and how is it?

Although the narration counts the Magians among the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب)—and the necessity of that is that they have a specific book or they belong to one of the books mentioned by the Quran like the Book of Noah, the Scrolls of Abraham, the Torah of Moses, the Gospel of Jesus, and the Psalms of David—the Quran does not mention their state, nor does it mention a book for them. That which they have of the "Avesta" has no mention in it, nor do they have the name of any of the other books.

The Quran only uses the term "People of the Book" in its usage to mean the Jews and Christians because of the Book which Allah sent down to them.

That which the Jews have of sacred books are thirty-five books: among them is the Torah of Moses comprising five scrolls (1); among them are the books of the historians, twelve books (2); among them is the Book of Job; among them are the Psalms of David; among them are three books of Solomon (3); and among them are the books of the prophecies, seventeen books (4).

The Quran did not mention among them except the Torah of Moses and the Psalms of David (عليهما السلام).

That which the Christians have of sacred books are the four Gospels: they are the Gospel of Matthew, the Gospel of Mark, the Gospel of Luke, and the Gospel of John; among them is the Book of the Acts of the Apostles; among them are several epistles (5); and among them is the Revelation of John.

The Quran did not mention anything of these sacred books specific to the Christians, except that it mentioned that there is a Heavenly Book which Allah sent down to Jesus son of Mary called the Gospel (الإنجيل), and it is one Gospel, not the Gospels. Although the Christians do not know it nor acknowledge it, yet in the words

(1) They are the Book of Genesis, the Book of Exodus, the Book of Leviticus, the Book of Numbers, and the Book of Deuteronomy. (2) They are the Book of Joshua, the Book of the Judges of Israel, the Book of Ruth, the First Book of Samuel, the Second of them, the First Book of Kings, the Second of them, the First Book of Chronicles, the Second of them, the First Book of Ezra, the Second of it, and the Book of Esther.

(3) These are: the Book of Proverbs (الأمثال), the Book of Ecclesiastes (الجامعة), and the Song of Songs (تسبيح التسابيح).

(4) These are: the Book of the Prophecy of Isaiah, the Book of the Prophecy of Jeremiah, the Lamentations of Jeremiah, the Book of Ezekiel, the Book of the Prophecy of Daniel, the Book of the Prophecy of Hosea, the Book of the Prophecy of Joel, the Book of the Prophecy of Amos, the Book of the Prophecy of Obadiah, the Book of the Prophecy of Jonah, the Book of the Prophecy of Micah, the Book of the Prophecy of Nahum, the Book of the Prophecy of Habakkuk, the Book of the Prophecy of Zephaniah, the Book of the Prophecy of Haggai, the Book of the Prophecy of Zechariah, and the Book of the Prophecy of Malachi.

(5) These are: fourteen epistles of Paul, one epistle of James, two epistles of Peter, three epistles of John, and one epistle of Jude.

Their leaders possess fragments that include an admission that the Messiah had a book named the Gospel (إنجيل) (1).

Furthermore, the Quran is not devoid of indications that a portion of the true Torah (التوراة) exists among what the Jews possess, and likewise, a portion of the true Gospel (الإنجيل) exists in the hands of the Christians. The Exalted says: وَكَيْفَ يُحَكِّمُونَكَ وَعِندَهُمُ التَّوْرَاةُ فِيهَا حُكْمُ اللَّهِ — "But how should they make you a judge while they have the Torah, in which is Allah’s judgement?" (Al-Ma'idah: 43). And the Exalted says: وَمِنَ الَّذِينَ قَالُوا إِنَّا نَصَارَىٰ أَخَذْنَا مِيثَاقَهُمْ فَنَسُوا حَظًّا مِّمَّا ذُكِّرُوا بِهِ — "And from those who say, ‘We are Christians,’ We took their pledge; but they forgot a part of what they were reminded of." (Al-Ma'idah: 14). The indication here is manifest.

(Historical Discussion)

1 — The Story of the Present Torah: The Children of Israel (بنو إسرائيل) are the tribes (الأسباط) from the progeny of Jacob. They initially lived the life of nomadic tribes until the Pharaohs brought them to Egypt, treating them as enslaved captives until Allah delivered them through Moses from Pharaoh and his deeds.

During the time of Moses, they conducted the affairs of their lives under a leader (الإمام), who was Moses, and after him, Joshua (peace be upon them). Then, for a period of time, their affairs were managed by judges (القضاة) such as Ehud, Gideon, and others. After that, the era of kingship began among them; the first of their kings was Saul—whom the Noble Quran calls Talut (طالوت)—then David, then Solomon.

Subsequently, the kingdom was divided and its power fragmented. Nevertheless, many kings ruled over them, such as Rehoboam, Abijam, Jeroboam, Jehoshaphat, Jehoram, and others—totaling some thirty-odd kings.

Their power continued to weaken after the division until the kings of Babylon overcame them and took control of Jerusalem (اورشليم), which is the Holy House (بيت المقدس). This occurred around the year 600 BCE. The king of Babylon at that time was Nebuchadnezzar (بخت نصر). Later, the Jews rebelled against his authority, so he sent his armies against them.

(1) In Paul's Epistle to the Galatians, Chapter One: "I marvel that you are so soon removed from him that called you into the grace of Christ unto another gospel: Which is not another; but there be some that trouble you, and would pervert [i.e., change] the gospel of Christ."
