Al-Najjar, in Stories of the Prophets (قصص الأنبياء), cited the aforementioned and other instances from Paul's words in his epistles as evidence that there was a gospel other than the four, called the "Gospel of Christ."

They besieged them and then conquered the city, plundering the treasures of the king and the treasures of the Temple (الهيكل) — the Al-Aqsa Mosque (المسجد الأقصى). They gathered nearly ten thousand souls from among their wealthy, powerful, and craftsmen and took them to Babylon, leaving only the weak and the destitute in the land. Nebuchadnezzar appointed Zedekiah (صدقيا)—the last of the kings of the Children of Israel—as king over them, under obligation of obedience to Nebuchadnezzar.

This state of affairs lasted for nearly ten years until Zedekiah found some strength and established a connection with one of the Pharaohs of Egypt; he then grew arrogant and rebelled against Nebuchadnezzar's authority.

This enraged Nebuchadnezzar intensely, so he led armies against them and besieged their cities. They fortified themselves in strongholds, and the siege lasted nearly a year and a half until famine and pestilence appeared among them.

Nebuchadnezzar persisted in the siege until he breached the fortifications in the year 586 BCE. He slaughtered them, destroyed their homes, and they demolished the House of Allah (بيت الله). They obliterated every religious sign (آية) and mark (علامة), turning their Temple into a mound of dust. At that time, the Torah and the Ark (التابوت) in which it was kept were lost.

The situation remained as such for approximately fifty years while they resided in Babylon. There was no trace or sign of their book, and nothing remained of their mosque or homes except mounds and ruins.

Then, when Cyrus (كورش), one of the kings of Persia, ascended the throne and his conflict with the Babylonians unfolded as it did, he conquered Babylon and entered it. He released the captives of the Children of Israel from Babylon. Ezra (عزرا), the well-known figure, was among those close to him, so Cyrus appointed him over them and permitted him to rewrite their book, the Torah, rebuild the Temple for them, and restore them to their former way of life. Ezra's return with them to Jerusalem was in the year 457 BCE. After that, Ezra collected the books of the Old Testament (العهد العتيق) and corrected them; these constitute the Torah in circulation today (1).

Upon reflection on this story, you can see that the chain of transmission (سند) for the Torah in circulation today is broken and not continuous back to Moses (peace be upon him), except through one individual (Ezra). Firstly, we do not know who he was; secondly, we do not know the nature of his knowledge or the depth of his insight; thirdly, we do not know the extent of his trustworthiness; fourthly, we do not know from where he obtained the scrolls of the Torah that he collected; and fifthly, we do not know upon what basis he corrected the errors that had occurred or were in circulation.

This ill-fated event produced another unfortunate consequence: several Western historical researchers denied the existence of Moses and what followed him, claiming he was a mythical figure—similar to what has been said regarding the Messiah, Jesus son of Mary (peace be upon them). However, this is not tenable for a Muslim, as the Noble Quran explicitly affirms his existence and specifies it.

2 — The Story of the Messiah and the Gospel:

The Jews are deeply concerned with their national history and the recording of significant events throughout the ages they have passed through. Nevertheless, if you were to search their books and writings, you would find no mention of the Messiah, Jesus son of Mary (peace be upon him): neither regarding the manner of his birth, nor his appearance and mission, nor his biography and the signs (الآيات) Allah manifested at his hands, nor the end of his life—whether by death, killing, crucifixion, or otherwise. What is the reason for this? And what necessitated the concealment of his affair from them, or their concealment of it?

The Quran mentions regarding them that they slandered Mary and accused her of a great falsehood (بهتان) concerning the birth of Jesus, and that they claimed to have killed Jesus. The Exalted says: وَبِكُفْرِهِمْ وَقَوْلِهِمْ عَلَىٰ مَرْيَمَ بُهْتَانًا عَظِيمًا وَقَوْلِهِمْ إِنَّا قَتَلْنَا الْمَسِيحَ عِيسَى ابْنَ مَرْيَمَ رَسُولَ اللَّهِ وَمَا قَتَلُوهُ وَمَا صَلَبُوهُ وَلَٰكِن شُبِّهَ لَهُمْ وَإِنَّ الَّذِينَ اخْتَلَفُوا فِيهِ لَفِي شَكٍّ مِّنْهُ مَا لَهُم بِهِ مِنْ عِلْمٍ إِلَّا اتِّبَاعَ الظَّنِّ وَمَا قَتَلُوهُ يَقِينًا — "And for their faithlessness, and their uttering a monstrous calumny against Mary, and for their saying, ‘We killed the Messiah, Jesus son of Mary, the apostle of Allah’—though they did not kill him nor did they crucify him, but so it was made to appear to them. Indeed those who differ about him are surely in doubt about it: they do not have any knowledge of it beyond following conjectures, and they certainly did not kill him." (An-Nisa: 157).

Was their claim based on a narrative circulating among them, mentioned in their national stories without being deposited in a book? Every nation has circulating narratives—realities and myths—that carry no weight unless they trace back to sound and upright sources.

Or did they hear from the Christians the repeated mention of the Messiah, his birth, appearance, and mission, and thus took it from their mouths to slander Mary and claim the killing of the Messiah? There is no way to ascertain any of this, except that the Quran—as appears from reflecting on the previous verse—does not explicitly attribute to them anything but the claim of killing, not crucifixion, and mentions that they are in doubt about the matter and that there is a disagreement!

As for the reality of what the Christians possess regarding the story of the Messiah, the matter of the Gospel, and the Glad Tidings (البشارة), his story and related matters trace back to their sacred books: the four Gospels—Matthew, Mark, Luke, and John—the Book of Acts by Luke, and several epistles by Paul, Peter, James, John, and Jude. The credibility of all these traces back to the credibility of the Gospels, so let us focus on them:

As for the Gospel of Matthew, it is the oldest of the Gospels in its composition and dissemination. Some have mentioned it was composed in 38 CE; others stated it was written between 50 and 60 CE (1). Thus, it was authored after the Messiah.

Researchers among both their ancients and moderns agree that its original was written in Hebrew and then translated into Greek and other languages. As for the original Hebrew version, it is lost; as for the translation, its status is unknown, and its translator is unidentified (2).

As for the Gospel of Mark: this Mark was a disciple of Peter and was not among the Apostles (الحواريين). They sometimes mention that he only wrote his Gospel at the suggestion and command of Peter. He did not believe in the divinity of the Messiah (3). For this reason, some mentioned that he only wrote his Gospel for the tribes and villagers, thus defining the Messiah as a divine messenger conveying the laws of Allah (4). In any case, he wrote his Gospel in 61 CE.

As for the Gospel of Luke: this Luke was not an Apostle, nor did he see the Messiah; rather, he learned Christianity from Paul. Paul was a Jew who was fanatically opposed to Christianity, harming the believers in the Messiah and subverting their affairs. Then, it happened suddenly that he claimed to have been struck down, and while in that state, the Messiah touched him, blamed him, and rebuked him for mistreating his followers, and that he then believed in the Messiah and the Messiah sent him to preach his Gospel.

This Paul is the one who established the pillars of present-day Christianity as it stands (1). He built the teaching on the premise that faith in the Messiah is sufficient for salvation (النجاة) without deeds (عمل). He permitted them to eat carrion and swine flesh, and forbade circumcision and much of what is in the Torah (2), despite the fact that the Gospel only came to confirm what was before it of the Torah and only permitted a few specific things. In short, Jesus only came to establish the law of the Torah and return the deviant and the transgressors to it, not to nullify deeds and restrict happiness to a void faith.

Luke wrote his Gospel after the Gospel of Mark, following the deaths of Peter and Paul. A group has explicitly stated that his Gospel is not an inspired (إلهامي) book like the other Gospels (3), as indicated by what occurs at the beginning of his Gospel.

As for the Gospel of John, many Christians have mentioned that this John is John son of Zebedee the fisherman, one of the twelve disciples (the Apostles) whom the Messiah loved dearly (4).

They mentioned that when Cerinthus (شيرينطوس), Ebion (أبيسون), and their group held that the Messiah was nothing but a created human being whose existence did not precede that of his mother, the bishops of Asia and others gathered in 96 CE with John and entreated him to write what the others had not written in their Gospels, and to specifically explain the divinity (لاهوت) of the Messiah. He could not refuse their request (1).

Their statements have differed regarding the year this Gospel was composed; some say 65, some say 96, and some say 98.

A group of them said it is not the composition of John the disciple: some hold it is the composition of a student from the Alexandrian school (2); some hold that this entire Gospel, as well as the epistles of John, are not of his composition, but were composed by someone at the beginning of the second century and attributed to John so that people would respect it (3); and some hold that the Gospel of John originally consisted of twenty chapters, and the Church of Ephesus (أفاس) added the twenty-first chapter after John's death (4). This is the state of these four Gospels. If we take what is certain from these paths, they lead back to seven men: Matthew, Mark, Luke, John, Peter, Paul, and Jude. All their reliance traces back to these four Gospels, and the four trace back to one which is the oldest and earliest: the Gospel of Matthew. It has already been mentioned that it is a translation of a lost original, and it is not known who translated it, what its original was like, or upon what it based its teaching—whether on the message of the Messiah or his divinity.

This existing Gospel translates that there appeared among the Children of Israel a man called Jesus son of Joseph the Carpenter, who established the call to Allah. He claimed to be the Son of God, born without a human father, and that his Father sent him to redeem humanity from their sins through crucifixion and killing; that he raised the dead, healed the blind and the leper, and cured the possessed by casting out demons from their bodies; and that he had twelve disciples—one of whom was Matthew, the author of the Gospel—whom he blessed and sent to preach and convey the Christian religion, etc.

(1) Bible Dictionary by Mr. Hawkes, entry: Matthew.

(2) The book Mizan al-Haqq, and an admission of this with hesitation in the Bible Dictionary.

(3) This was narrated by Abd al-Wahhab al-Najjar in Stories of the Prophets from the book Muruj al-Akhbar in the biographies of the news by Peter Qarmaj.

(4) Mentioned in the Bible Dictionary, where it says: The text of the succession of the predecessors is that Mark wrote his Gospel in Rome, and it was disseminated after the deaths of Peter and Paul. However, it does not carry much weight because the outward appearance of his Gospel suggests he wrote it for tribespeople and villagers, not for city dwellers, especially Romans. Reflect on his words!

This is a summary of what the Christian mission leads to, despite its expansion across the East and West of the earth. It amounts to nothing more than a single report (خبر واحد) whose author's name and identity are unknown, and whose person and description are ambiguous.

This extraordinary weakness at the origin of the story is what led some free researchers in Europe to claim that the Messiah, Jesus son of Mary, is a mythical figure fashioned by certain religious tendencies against the governments of the time. This was supported by another mythical subject that closely resembles it in all aspects of the story: the subject of "Krishna," whom the ancient paganism of India claims is the Son of God who descended from his divinity and redeemed humanity with himself through crucifixion to save them from burdens and sins—exactly as is claimed for Jesus the Messiah (as will be mentioned).

It also led other critical researchers to conclude that there are two individuals named the Messiah: the non-crucified Messiah and the crucified Messiah, with more than five centuries between them.

They argue that the Gregorian calendar—of which this year is 1956—does not correspond to either of them. Rather, the first, non-crucified Messiah preceded it by more than 250 years and lived for about sixty years, while the second, crucified Messiah followed it by more than 290 years and lived for about thirty-three years (1).

Furthermore, the fact that the Gregorian calendar does not precisely align with the birth of the Messiah is something Christians cannot deny (2); it is a historical silence.

Moreover, there are other suspicious and suggestive matters. They mentioned that in the first two centuries of the Christian era, many other gospels were written, perhaps numbering over a hundred. The four Gospels were among them, but then the Church prohibited all those gospels except the four that were recognized as canonical (قانونية) because their contents agreed with the Church's teaching (1).

Among the discarded gospels is the Gospel of Barnabas, a copy of which appeared years ago and was translated into Arabic and Persian. In most of its stories, it agrees with what the Quran narrates regarding the Messiah, Jesus son of Mary (2).

It is remarkable that historical materials narrated from non-Jews are also silent regarding the details the Gospel attributes to the Christian mission, such as the narrative of sonship and redemption. The famous American historian Hendrik Willem van Loon mentioned in his work on human history a letter written by the Roman physician Aesculapius Cultellus in 62 CE to his nephew Gladius Ensa, who was a soldier in the Roman army in Palestine. In it, he mentions that he visited a sick man in Rome named Paul, whose words impressed him; Paul had spoken to him about the Christian mission and mentioned some reports of the Messiah and his call.

He then mentions that he left Paul and did not see him again until he heard after a while that he was killed on the road to Ostia. He then asks his nephew to research the news of this Israelite prophet whom Paul used to mention, and the news of Paul himself, and to write to him what he learns of that.

Gladius Ensa wrote back to him six weeks later from the Roman camp in Jerusalem:

(1) The learned leader Behrouz detailed this in a book he recently authored on prophetic glad tidings. I hope to be successful in including a fragment of it in the commentary at the end of Surah an-Nisa in this book. The certain point (which concerns us) is the disruption of Christian history.

(2) See the entry "Messiah" in the Bible Dictionary.

"I asked several of the city's elders and its long-lived residents about Jesus the Messiah, but I found they could not answer my questions well [and this was in the year 62 CE, and they were elders!].

Eventually, I met an olive seller and asked him if he knew him. He answered me affirmatively and then directed me to a man named Joseph, mentioning that he was one of his followers and lovers, and that he was knowledgeable of his stories and insightful regarding his news, capable of answering your questions.

I met Joseph today after searching for days. I found him to be a very old man who had formerly been a fisherman in some of the lakes in this region.

Despite his great age, the man was sound of mind and had an excellent memory. He narrated to me all the news and events that occurred in that time—the time of turmoil and sedition.

He mentioned that Pontius Pilate was the governor of Samaria and Judea during the reign of Emperor Tiberius.

It happened that during his governorship, a sedition occurred in Jerusalem, so Pontius Pilate traveled there to extinguish the fire of sedition. The sedition was what was rumored then: that a carpenter's son from the people of Nazareth was calling people and inciting them against the government.

When they verified his affair, it became clear that the accused carpenter's son was a wise and steadfast young man who had committed nothing necessitating punishment, except that the religious leaders of the Jews opposed him and hated him most intensely. They told Pilate: 'This Nazarene youth says: If a Greek, a Roman, or a Palestinian treats people and associates with them with justice and compassion, he is, in the sight of God, like one who spent his life studying the Book of God and reciting its verses.'

It seems these objections and suggestions did not affect Pilate, but when he heard the crowd gathering in front of the Temple wanting to seize Jesus and his companions and tear them limb from limb, he saw it best to seize this young carpenter himself and imprison him so he would not be killed by the hands of the people in their riot.

Pilate had not fully understood the reason for the people's resentment toward Jesus. Whenever he spoke to the people about his affair, questioned them, and sought clarification, their voices rose, shouting: 'He is a disbeliever,' 'He is an atheist,' 'He is a traitor.' The matter led to nothing substantial.

Eventually, Pilate decided to speak to Jesus himself. He summoned him, spoke to him, and asked him about his intentions regarding the religion reported of him. Jesus replied that he was not concerned with government or politics, nor did he have any purpose in that; rather, he was concerned with spiritual life more than physical life, and he believed that man must do good to people and worship the one and only God, who is like a Father to all living beings among the creatures.

Pilate was experienced in the doctrines of the Stoics and other Greek philosophers, so he saw nothing in what Jesus said to him that warranted objection or punishment. Therefore, he intended a second time to save this peaceful and steadfast prophet from the evil of the Jews, and he delayed the judgment of his execution.

However, the Jews were not satisfied with that and did not leave him be. Instead, they spread rumors that he had been seduced by Jesus's lies and sayings, and that Pilate intended treason against Caesar. They began to testify against him and record scrolls to that effect, wanting his removal from the governorship. Seditions and upheavals had previously broken out in Palestine, and the imperial forces were few in number, unable to fully silence the people.

It was incumbent upon the governors and other officials on behalf of Caesar not to treat the people in a way that would provoke their complaints and dissatisfaction.

For these reasons, Pilate saw no choice but to sacrifice this imprisoned youth for the sake of public security and to grant the people's request for his execution.

As for Jesus, he did not despair at death but met it with magnanimity. Before his death, he forgave those who caused his execution among the Jews, and then he died on the cross while people mocked him, insulted him, and cursed him.

Gladius Ensa said: This is what Joseph narrated to me of the story of Jesus, with tears flowing down his cheeks. When he bid me farewell, I offered him some gold coins, but he refused to take them, saying: 'There are those here who are poorer than I, so give it to them.'

I asked him about your known companion Paul, but he did not know him perfectly. What became clear of his affair was that he was a tentmaker, then he left his work and engaged in preaching this new religion—the religion of the Kind and Merciful Lord, the God between whom and 'Yahweh,' the God of the Jews whom we constantly hear about from Jewish scholars, there is a difference greater than that between heaven and earth.

It appears that Paul first traveled to Asia Minor, then to Greece, and he used to tell the slaves and bondsmen that they were all children of a Father who loves them and is kind to them, and that happiness is not restricted to some people over others but encompasses all people, rich and poor, provided they associate in brotherhood and live in purity and friendship." End of summary.

These are the general points of this letter related to our current discussion.

By reflecting on the contents of this letter, the observer realizes how the Christian mission appeared among the Children of Israel shortly after Jesus (peace be upon him), and that it was nothing but the appearance of a prophetic call with a message from Allah—not the appearance of a divine call through the manifestation of divinity (اللاهوت), its descent to them, and their salvation through redemption!

Furthermore, several of Jesus's disciples or those attributed to him, like Paul and the disciples of their disciples, traveled after the event of the crucifixion to various parts of the earth—India, Africa, Rome, and elsewhere—and spread the Christian mission. However, they soon differed on fundamental issues of teaching, such as the divinity of the Messiah, the sufficiency of faith in the Messiah over acting upon the law of Moses, and whether the religion of the Gospel was an original religion abrogating the religion of Moses or a follower of the law of the Torah, completing it (1). Thus, they split into sects.

What must be carefully considered is that the nations where the Christian mission spread and first appeared, such as Rome, India, and others, were previously adherents of Sabian paganism, Brahmanism, or Buddhism. These contained elements of mysticism (تصوف) on one hand and Brahmanical philosophy on the other. In all of them, there was a significant portion regarding the manifestation of divinity (اللاهوت) in the form of humanity (الناسوت). Moreover, the doctrine of the trinity (تثليث) of unity, the descent of divinity in the garb of humanity, and its enduring crucifixion (1) and torment as redemption, was circulating among the ancients of the pagans of India, China, Egypt, Chaldea, Assyria, and Persia, as well as the ancient pagans of the West like the Romans, Scandinavians, and others, as found in books authored on ancient religions and sects.

Doane mentioned in his book Bible Myths and Their Parallels in Other Religions: "If we turn our gaze to India, we see that their greatest and most famous theological worship is the Trinity. They call this teaching in their language 'Trimurti,' which is a term composed of two words in their Sanskrit language: 'Tri,' meaning three, and 'Murti,' meaning forms or persons (أقانيم). These are Brahma, Vishnu, and Shiva—three united persons that do not separate from unity; they are one god according to their claim."

He then mentioned: "Brahma, according to them, is the Father, Vishnu is the Son, and Shiva is the Holy Spirit."

He further mentioned: "They call Shiva 'Krishna' (2)—the Savior Lord and the Great Spirit from whom was born 'Vishnu,' the god who appeared in humanity on earth to save people. He is one of the three persons who are the one god."

He also mentioned: "They symbolize the third person with the image of a dove, just as the Christians say."

Mr. Faber said in his book The Origin of Pagan Idolatry: "Just as we find among the Hindus a trinity composed of Brahma, Vishnu, and Shiva, we find among the Buddhists a trinity; for they say that Buddha is a god who has three persons. Likewise, the Jaina Buddhists say that Jiva is a trinity of persons."

(1) Execution by crucifixion on a cross is a very ancient practice. They used to kill those whose crimes were severe and whose sins were heinous by crucifixion, which is one of the most agonizing and infamous methods of execution. The method involved making a cross-shaped structure from two intersecting pieces of wood, upon which a person would be placed with arms outstretched. Nails would be driven through the palms into the ends of the crossbeam, and likewise, the feet would be nailed to the wood, or sometimes tied without nailing. Then the wood would be erected with its end in the ground so that the feet were about two cubits from the earth. The crucified person would remain like that for a day or days, then his legs would be broken at the shins and he would be killed on the cross or taken down and killed after removal. The crucified person would be tortured before crucifixion by flogging or mutilation. It was a shameful disgrace for a people to have one of their own killed by crucifixion.

(2) Expressed in English as "Christ," the Savior Messiah.

He said: "The Chinese worship Buddha and call him 'Fo,' and they say he is three persons just as the Hindus say."

Doane said in his aforementioned book: "The priests of the Temple of Memphis in Egypt used to express the Holy Trinity to beginners in religious learning by saying: The first created the second, and the second created the third, and thus the Holy Trinity was completed."

King Thulis of Egypt asked the priest Thulis: "Was there anyone before me greater than me? And will there be anyone after me greater than me?" The priest replied: "Yes, there is one who is greater, and He is God before all things, then the Word, and with them the Holy Spirit. These three have one nature, and they are one in essence (الذات), and from them issued the eternal power. So go, O mortal, O possessor of a short life."

Bonwick said in his book Egyptian Belief and Modern Thought: "The strangest word that gained widespread dissemination in the religion of the Egyptians is their saying regarding the divinity of the Word, and that everything came to be through it, and that it emanated from God, and that it is God." End quote. This is the exact phrasing with which the Gospel of John begins.

Higgins said in the book The Anacalypsis: "The Persians used to call Mithras the Word, the Mediator, and the Savior of the Persians."

It is narrated from the book The First Inhabitants of Europe: "The ancient pagans used to say that God is a trinity of persons."

The aforementioned issue of the Trinity is narrated from the Greeks, Romans, Finns, and Scandinavians, as is the doctrine of the Word from the Chaldeans, Assyrians, and Phoenicians.

Doane said in his book Bible Myths and Their Parallels in Other Religions (pp. 181–182), summarized in translation:

"The concept of salvation through the offering of one of the gods as a sacrifice in redemption for sin is very ancient among the pagan Hindus and others." He mentioned evidence for this:

Among them: "The Hindus believe that Krishna, the firstborn—who is the very god Vishnu who has no beginning and no end according to them—was moved by compassion to save the earth from the weight of its burden, so he came to it and saved man by offering a sacrifice for him."

He mentioned that "Mr. Moor" depicted Krishna crucified as he is depicted in Hindu books, with pierced hands and feet, and on his shirt the image of a human heart hanging. An image of him was found crucified with a crown of gold on his head; the Christians say that Jesus was crucified with a crown of thorns on his head.

Huc said on page 326 of the first volume of his Travels: "The pagan Hindus believe in the incarnation of some gods and the offering of a sacrifice of redemption for people from sin."

Monier-Williams said on page 26 of his book Hinduism: "The pagan Hindus believe in original sin (الخطيئة الأصلية), and evidence for this is found in their supplications and entreaties which they offer after the 'Gayatri,' which is: 'I am a sinner and a committer of sin, my nature is evil, and my mother conceived me in iniquity, so save me, O Thou of the lotus-eye, O Savior of sinners from iniquities and sins.'"

The Reverend George Cox said in his book The Mythology of the Aryan Nations in the context of speaking about the Hindus: "They describe Krishna as the gentle hero full of divinity because he offered his person as a sacrifice."

Higgins narrated from Andrade, the first European to enter the lands of Tibet: "He said regarding the god 'Indra' whom they worship: that he shed his blood by crucifixion and the piercing of nails in order to save humanity from their sins, and that the image of the crucifixion exists in their books."

In the book of the monk Georgius, there is an image of this god "Indra" crucified, in the form of a cross whose arms are equal in width but vary in length; the vertical arm—containing the image of his face—and the lower arm are the longest. Were it not for the image of the face, it would not occur to one who sees the image that it represents a person.

As for what is narrated from the Buddhists regarding Buddha, it is even more applicable to what the Christians narrate regarding the Messiah in all respects, to the point that they call him the Messiah, the Only Begotten, and the Savior of the World. They say he is a perfect man and a perfect god incarnated in humanity, and that he offered himself as a sacrifice to atone for the sins of humanity and save them from their sins so they would not be punished for them, and to make them heirs to the Kingdom of Heaven.

Many Western scholars have explained this: among them Bell in his book, Huc in his Travels, Müller in his book History of Ancient Sanskrit Literature, and others (1).

This is a brief overview or a sample of the belief in the clothing of divinity in humanity, and the narrative of crucifixion and redemption in ancient religions that nations were adhering to and devoted to when the Christian religion began to spread across the earth, and the Christian mission began to captivate hearts in the regions where Christian missionaries traveled. Is this anything other than that the Christian missionaries took the principles of Christianity and cast them into the mold of paganism, thereby winning the hearts of people to accept their call and digest their teaching?

This is supported by what you see in the words of Paul and others regarding the disparagement of the wisdom of the wise and their philosophy, and the belittling of the methods of rational demonstration (الاستدلالات العقلية), and that the Lord God prefers the foolishness of the fool over the intellect of the intellectual.

This is only because they encountered with their teaching the schools of reasoning and demonstration, and its adherents rejected it on the grounds that there was no way to accept it—or even to rationally conceive it correctly—from the perspective of demonstration. So they laid the foundation upon mystical unveiling (المكاشفة) and being filled with the Holy Spirit, thus resembling what the ignorant among the Sufis insist upon: that their way is a realm beyond the realm of the intellect.

Furthermore, the missionaries among them practiced asceticism and traveled through the lands (as narrated by the Book of Acts and histories) and spread the Christian mission. They were received by the common people in various lands. The secret of their success, especially in the Roman Empire, was the spiritual pressure that pervaded the lands due to the spread of injustice and transgression, the pervasiveness of the laws of enslavement and bondage, the vast gap in the lives of the ruling and ruled classes, the commanding and commanded, and the immense separation between the lives of the wealthy and affluent and the poor, the destitute, and the slaves.

The missionaries used to call for brotherhood, love, equality, and beautiful association among people, the rejection of the world and its murky, fleeting life, and the turning toward the pure, happy life in the Kingdom of Heaven. For this very reason, the ruling class of kings and Caesars did not concern themselves much with their state, nor did they target them with harm, punishment, or expulsion.

They continued to increase in number without ostentation or competition, growing in strength and intensity until they had a vast multitude in the Roman Empire, Africa, India, and other lands. Whenever they built a church and opened its door to the people, they thereby demolished one of the houses of idols and closed its door.

They did not care about competing with the leaders of paganism in demolishing their foundation, nor with the kings and governors of the time in rising above submission to them and in opposing their laws and decrees. Sometimes this led them to destruction, killing, imprisonment, and torment; thus, one group would be killed, another imprisoned, and a third displaced.

The matter remained in this state until the time of the reign of Emperor Constantine. He believed in the Christian faith and proclaimed it, so Christianity became official. Churches were built in Rome and the kingdoms following its empire. This was in the latter half of the fourth century CE.

Christianity then became centered in the Church of Rome, which began sending priests to the corners of the earth in the subordinate lands, building churches, monasteries, and schools where they taught the Gospel's instruction.

What must be brought to attention is that they based their research on established Gospel principles. They took Gospel teachings—such as the issue of the Father, the Son, and the Spirit, and the issue of crucifixion and redemption, etc.—as established principles and built their research and investigation upon them.

This was the first weakness and frailty to affect their religious research. For the strength of a building, no matter how great it reaches, and its uprightness, does not compensate for the weakness of the foundation upon which it is built. What they built upon—the issue of the trinity of unity, the crucifixion, and redemption—is an irrational matter.

Several of their researchers into the Trinity admitted that it is an irrational matter, but they excused it by saying it is among the religious issues that must be accepted through devotional submission (تعبداً), for how many devotional issues are there in religions that intellects find impossible?

This is among the corrupt conjectures branching from their corrupt foundation. How can an impossible issue occur in a true religion? We only accept religion and distinguish its being a true religion through the intellect (العقل). How can it be possible for the intellect that a true belief contains a matter that the intellect nullifies and finds impossible? Is this not a blatant contradiction?

Yes, a religion may contain a possible matter that breaks the ordinary habit and the established natural law, but as for the essentially impossible (المحال الذاتي), never.

This aforementioned method of research is what caused the occurrence of disagreement and dispute among the thinking researchers among them in the early days of Christianity's fame and the devotion of students to sectarian research in the schools of Rome, Alexandria, and elsewhere.

The Church increased its monitoring of the unity of the word every day, preparing a council formed upon the appearance of every new saying or innovation (بدعة) from the patriarchs and bishops to convince them of the general doctrine, and to excommunicate, exile, expel, and kill them if they were not convinced.

The first council they held was the Council of Nicaea, when Arius said: "The person (اقنوم) of the Son is not equal to the person of the Father; the Eternal is Allah, and the Messiah is created."

The patriarchs, metropolitans, and bishops gathered in Constantinople in the presence of Emperor Constantine. They were 313 men, and they agreed upon this statement: "We believe in one God, the Father, the possessor of all things, and the maker of what is seen and what is not seen; and in the one Son, Jesus Christ, the one Son of God, the firstborn of all creation, not made, true God from true God, from the essence (جوهر) of his Father by whose hand the worlds and all things were perfected; who for us and for our salvation descended from heaven, and was incarnated from the Holy Spirit, and was born of the Virgin Mary, and was crucified in the days of Pilate, and was buried, then rose on the third day, and ascended to heaven, and sat at the right hand of his Father, and he is prepared to come once more to judge between the dead and the living; and we believe in the one Holy Spirit, the Spirit of Truth who proceeds from his Father; and in one baptism (1) for the forgiveness of sins; and in one holy Christian-Catholic assembly; and in the resurrection of our bodies (1); and life forever and ever (2)."

This was the first council, and how many councils did they hold after that to disavow newly originated sects, such as Nestorianism, Jacobitism, Arianism, Hilarianism, Macedonianism, Sabellianism, Noetianism, Paulicianism, and others.

Despite this, the Church performed its duty of monitoring, never flagging or weakening in its mission, increasing every day in its power and dominance until it succeeded in bringing the rest of the nations of Europe to Christianity—such as France, England, Austria, Prussia, Spain, Portugal, Belgium, Holland, and others—except Russia, toward the end of the fifth century CE in the year 496.

The Church continued to advance and progress on one hand, while on the other, the northern nations and nomadic tribes were attacking Rome, and wars and seditions were weakening the authority of the Caesars. The matter culminated in the people of Rome and the conquering nations agreeing to hand over the reins of the kingdom's affairs to the Church, just as the reins of religious affairs were in its hand. Thus, spiritual and physical authority were united for the head of the Church at that time, who was "Pope Gregory," and that was in the year 590 CE.

The Church of Rome gained absolute leadership of the Christian world. However, since the Roman Empire had split into the Western Roman Empire with its capital Rome, and the Eastern Roman Empire with its capital Constantinople, the Caesars of the Eastern Roman Empire considered themselves religious heads of their kingdom without following the Church of Rome. This was the beginning of the division of Christianity into Catholics—followers of the Church of Rome—and Orthodox, who were others.

The situation remained as such until Constantinople was conquered at the hands of the House of Osman, and Emperor Palaiologos—the last of the Eastern Roman Caesars and the priest of the Church at that time—was killed (he was killed in the Hagia Sophia church).

The Caesars of Russia claimed the inheritance of this religious position—meaning the leadership of the Church—due to a marital relationship between them and the Caesars of Rome. Russia had converted to Christianity in the tenth century CE, so the kings of Russia became the priests of the church of their land, not following the Church of Rome. This was in the year 1454 CE.

The situation remained as such for nearly five centuries until "Tsar Nicholas"—the last of the Russian Caesars—was killed along with all his family in 1918 CE at the hands of the Communists. Thus, the Church of Rome returned approximately to its state before the division.

However, as a result of its leaders attempting to control all aspects of people's lives during the Middle Ages—when the Church was at the peak of its rise and elevation—masses of religious people rebelled against it to free themselves from the restrictions the Church imposed upon them.

One group departed from following the rulings of the Church leaders and Popes while remaining obedient to the Gospel teachings as understood by their councils and determined by the consensus of their scholars and priests; these are the Orthodox.

Another group departed from following the Church of Rome altogether; they do not follow the Church of Rome in Gospel teaching, nor do they pay heed to the orders issued by it; these are the Protestants.

Thus, the Christian world today is divided into three sects: the Catholics, who follow the Church of Rome and its teachings; the Orthodox, who follow the teaching of the Church rather than the institution itself—their branch emerged with the schism in the Church, especially after the seat of the Church of Constantinople moved to Moscow in Russia (as previously mentioned); and the Protestants, who have departed from following both the Church and its teachings entirely. Their way became independent and manifest in the fifteenth century CE.

This is a summary of the course of the Christian mission over a period of nearly twenty centuries. One who is discerning of the purpose for which this book was written knows that the intent behind mentioning these historical summaries is:

First: That the researcher may have insight into the historical transformations in their creed and the meanings that may have transitioned into their religious beliefs by way of inheritance, contagion, or the influence of syncretism, or through familiarity and habit from pagan beliefs and ideas inherited from them or taken from them.

Second: That the power of the Church, especially the Church of Rome, gradually reached its ultimate zenith in the Middle Ages until they possessed authority over both religion and the world. The thrones of kings in Europe submitted to them, and they had the power to depose whomever they wished and appoint whomever they wished (1).

It is narrated that the Pope once ordered the Emperor of Germany to stand barefoot for three days at the gate of his palace in the winter season for a slip he had committed, so that the Pope might forgive him (2).

The Pope once kicked the King's crown with his foot when the King came to him kneeling to seek forgiveness (3).

They had described the Muslims to their followers in a manner that left them no choice but to view the religion of Islam as a pagan religion. This is gathered from the slogans and poems they composed to incite the Christians and rouse them against the Muslims during the Crusades, which broke out between them and the Muslims for many long years.

They used to believe (4) that Muslims worshiped idols and that they had three gods, whose names in order were: "Mahum"—also called Baphomet and Mahomend—who is the first of the gods, and he is "Muhammad"; after him is "Aylin," who is the second; and after him is "Tervagan," who is the third. It sometimes appears from some of their words that the Muslims had two other gods, "Martuan" and "Jubin," but they were lower in rank than the aforementioned three. They used to say: Muhammad built his call on the claim of divinity; and they sometimes said: he had taken for himself an idol of gold.

In the poems of Richard, which he recited to incite the Franks against the Muslims: "Rise and overturn Mahomend and Tervagan and cast them into the fire to draw near to your God."

In the poems of Roland describing "Mahum," the god of the Muslims: "He is made entirely of gold and silver. If you saw him, you would be certain that no craftsman could imagine anything more beautiful than him and then create it. His stature is great, his craftsmanship is excellent, and the marks of majesty are manifest in his features. Mahum is made of gold and silver; the brilliance of his lightning almost takes away the sight. He has been seated upon an elephant that is among the finest and most excellent of fabrications. Its belly is hollow, and the onlooker might perceive a light from its belly; it is encrusted with shimmering precious stones. Its interior can be seen from its exterior, and there is no equal to it in the quality of craftsmanship."

Since the gods of the Muslims would supposedly reveal to them in times of distress, and the Muslims were defeated in some of their battles, the leader of the people sent someone to seek their god who was in Mecca (meaning Muhammad صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم). Someone who witnessed the event narrates: that the god (meaning Muhammad) came to them surrounded by a vast multitude of his followers, while they were beating drums, lutes, flutes, and trumpets made of silver, singing and dancing until they brought him to the camp with joy, welcome, and exultation. His caliph was waiting for his arrival, and when he saw him, he stood on his feet and engaged in his worship with humility and submissiveness.

"Richard" also mentions in describing the revelation of the god (Yahum) whose description you heard, saying: "The sorcerers subjugated one of the jinn and placed him in the belly of that idol. That jinni would first thunder and clamor, then begin speaking to the Muslims while they listened to him."

Similar curiosities are found in abundance in their books written during the years of the Crusades or those dealing with its affairs, even if they might baffle and astonish the reader with wonder and bewilderment. One might almost disbelieve the accuracy of the report when it relates matters that no Muslim has ever witnessed while awake, nor seen in a dream or a slumber.

Third: That the contemplative researcher may realize the manner in which the Christian mission evolved in its course through the past centuries until today. Pagan beliefs entered it through a subtle creeping—first by exaggeration (الغلو) regarding the status of the Messiah (peace be upon him), then they became entrenched, casting the mission into the mold of the Trinity (التثليث): the Father, the Son, and the Spirit, and the doctrine of the crucifixion and atonement (الفداء). This necessitated the doctrine of rejecting works and sufficing with belief.

This was initially in the form of religion, and the Church held their reins through the performance of things like fasting, prayer, and baptism. However, atheism (الإلحاد) continued to grow in body and strengthen in spirit, giving rise to schisms until Protestantism appeared. Formal laws took the place of the chaos in politics, codified on the basis of freedom beyond the law (practical rulings whose implementation is guaranteed). Thus, religious teaching continued to weaken in effect and fail in effort until the pillars of ethics and human virtues were gradually breached following the spread of materialism (المادية) that resulted from absolute freedom.

Communism and socialism emerged based on the philosophy of dialectical materialism (ماترياليسم ديالكتيك) and the rejection of divinity (اللاهوت), fixed virtuous ethics, and religious acts. Thus, spiritual humanity was demolished, and it was inherited by material animality, composed of predatory and bestial natures, and the world rose to march toward it at a rapid pace.

As for the religious revivals that have recently swept the world, they are nothing but political playgrounds played by politicians to use them as means to their ends and desires. Sophisticated politics today knocks on every door and creeps into every hole and crevice.

Dr. Joseph Sittler, Professor of Religious Sciences at Lutheran College in Chicago, mentioned: "The new religious revival in America is nothing but the application of religion to the array of life affairs in modern civilization, and the confirmation that present civilization does not contradict religion. There is a danger that the general public will believe they are religious with the true religion because of the results of present civilization in their hands, until they feel no need to join a true religious revival should it one day appear among them, and thus they would pay no heed to it" (1).

Dr. Georges Florovsky, the greatest defender of the Russian Orthodox in America, mentioned that religious instructions in America are nothing but a false solace for hearts, because if it were a true, living religious revival, it would necessarily have to rely on deep, realistic instructions (2).

So look from where the delegation of religion departed and where it landed. The mission began in the name of reviving religion (creed), ethics (virtuous traits), and the law (acts), and it ended with the abolition of all of them and the placement of animalistic enjoyment in their stead.

All of this is nothing but the evolution of the initial deviation committed by Paul, called the Saint—Paul the Apostle—and his supporters. If they had named this present civilization, which the world admits threatens humanity with extinction, "Pauline civilization," it would have been more worthy of belief than their saying: that the Messiah is the leader of present civilization and culture and the bearer of its banner.

(Discussion regarding Narrations)

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: مَا كَانَ لِبَشَرٍ أَن يُؤْتِيَهُ اللَّهُ الْكِتَابَ "It does not behoove any human that Allah should give him the Book..." [QARAI TRANSLATION NEEDED: Surah 3, Ayah 79], it is mentioned: "Jesus did not say to the people: 'I created you, so be servants to me instead of Allah,' but he said to them: 'Be God-oriented (ربانيين),' meaning scholars."

I say: In the preceding commentary (البيان), there passed that which supports this from the contextual clues. His saying: "He did not say to the people: 'I created you,'" is in the position of an argument for his not having said that; i.e., had he said that to them, it would have been necessary for him to inform them that he created them, yet he did not inform them nor did he do so.

Also therein, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَلَا يَأْمُرَكُمْ أَن تَتَّخِذُوا الْمَلَائِكَةَ وَالنَّبِيِّينَ أَرْبَابًا "Nor would he bid you to take the angels and the prophets for lords..." [QARAI TRANSLATION NEEDED: Surah 3, Ayah 80], he said: "There was a people who worshiped the angels, and a group of Christians claimed that Jesus is a Lord, and the Jews said: 'Ezra is the son of Allah.' So Allah said: 'Nor would he bid you to take the angels and the prophets for lords.'"

I say: Its explanation has preceded.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Ishaq, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and al-Bayhaqi in al-Dala'il narrated from Ibn Abbas, who said: "Abu Rafi' al-Qurazi said—when the rabbis of the Jews and the Christians of the people of Najran gathered with the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم and he invited them to Islam—'Do you intend, O Muhammad, that we should worship you as the Christians worship Jesus son of Mary?' Then a Christian man from the people of Najran called al-Ra'is said: 'Is that what you want from us, O Muhammad?'

The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم said: 'Allah forbid that we should worship other than Allah or command the worship of other than Him. Not for that did He send me, nor with that did He command me.' So Allah revealed regarding their words: مَا كَانَ لِبَشَرٍ أَن يُؤْتِيَهُ اللَّهُ الْكِتَابَ (It does not behoove any human that Allah should give him the Book...) until His saying: بَعْدَ إِذْ أَنتُم مُّسْلِمُونَ (...after you have submitted [to Allah]?) [QARAI TRANSLATION NEEDED: Surah 3, Ayah 79-80]."

Also therein, Abd ibn Humayd narrated from al-Hasan, who said: "It reached me that a man said: 'O Messenger of Allah, we greet you as we greet one another; shall we not then prostrate to you?' He said: 'No, but honor your Prophet and recognize the truth for its people, for it is not fitting that anyone should prostrate to anyone besides Allah.' So Allah revealed: مَا كَانَ لِبَشَرٍ أَن يُؤْتِيَهُ اللَّهُ الْكِتَابَ (It does not behoove any human that Allah should give him the Book...) until His saying: بَعْدَ إِذْ أَنتُم مُّسْلِمُونَ (...after you have submitted [to Allah]?)."

I say: Other than these two reasons have been narrated regarding the occasion of revelation (شأن النزول), and it appears that this is from theoretical inference. Detailed discussion on this has preceded, and it is possible for several reasons to converge in the revelation of a single verse; and Allah knows best.

Ayahs

وَإِذْ أَخَذَ ٱللَّهُ مِيثَـٰقَ ٱلنَّبِيِّـۧنَ لَمَآ ءَاتَيْتُكُم مِّن كِتَـٰبٍ وَحِكْمَةٍ ثُمَّ جَآءَكُمْ رَسُولٌ مُّصَدِّقٌ لِّمَا مَعَكُمْ لَتُؤْمِنُنَّ بِهِۦ وَلَتَنصُرُنَّهُۥ ۚ قَالَ ءَأَقْرَرْتُمْ وَأَخَذْتُمْ عَلَىٰ ذَٰلِكُمْ إِصْرِى ۖ قَالُوٓا۟ أَقْرَرْنَا ۚ قَالَ فَٱشْهَدُوا۟ وَأَنَا۠ مَعَكُم مِّنَ ٱلشَّـٰهِدِينَ (٨١)
When Allah took a compact concerning the prophets, [He said,] ‘Inasmuch as I have given you [the knowledge] of the Book and wisdom, should an apostle come to you thereafter confirming what is with you, you shall believe in him and help him.’ He said, ‘Do you pledge and accept My covenant on this condition?’ They said, ‘We pledge.’ He said, ‘Then be witnesses, and I, too, am among the witnesses along with you.’ (81)
فَمَن تَوَلَّىٰ بَعْدَ ذَٰلِكَ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْفَـٰسِقُونَ (٨٢)
Then whoever turns away after that—it is they who are the transgressors. (82)
أَفَغَيْرَ دِينِ ٱللَّهِ يَبْغُونَ وَلَهُۥٓ أَسْلَمَ مَن فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ طَوْعًا وَكَرْهًا وَإِلَيْهِ يُرْجَعُونَ (٨٣)
Do they seek a religion other than that of Allah, while to Him submits whoever there is in the heavens and the earth, willingly or unwillingly, and to Him they will be brought back? (83)
قُلْ ءَامَنَّا بِٱللَّهِ وَمَآ أُنزِلَ عَلَيْنَا وَمَآ أُنزِلَ عَلَىٰٓ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ وَإِسْمَـٰعِيلَ وَإِسْحَـٰقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ وَٱلْأَسْبَاطِ وَمَآ أُوتِىَ مُوسَىٰ وَعِيسَىٰ وَٱلنَّبِيُّونَ مِن رَّبِّهِمْ لَا نُفَرِّقُ بَيْنَ أَحَدٍ مِّنْهُمْ وَنَحْنُ لَهُۥ مُسْلِمُونَ (٨٤)
Say, ‘We have faith in Allah and in what has been sent down to us, and what was sent down to Abraham, Ishmael, Isaac, Jacob and the Tribes, and that which Moses, Jesus and the prophets were given by their Lord. We make no distinction between any of them, and to Him do we submit.’ (84)
وَمَن يَبْتَغِ غَيْرَ ٱلْإِسْلَـٰمِ دِينًا فَلَن يُقْبَلَ مِنْهُ وَهُوَ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ مِنَ ٱلْخَـٰسِرِينَ (٨٥)
Should anyone follow a religion other than Islam, it shall never be accepted from him, and he will be among the losers in the Hereafter. (85)

thought

(Commentary)

The verses are not devoid of connection to what preceded them; the context is a single, continuous flow maintaining its unity. It is as if He, the Exalted, having explained that the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب) persisted in seeking transgression regarding the knowledge of the Book they were entrusted with—

And the religion (الدين), by distorting words from their proper places, seeking to deceive people by obscuring the matter, differentiating between the Prophets, and denying the signs of the prophethood of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم); and having negated that any Prophet among the Prophets, such as Moses and Jesus (عليهما السلام), would command them to take himself or anyone else from among the Prophets and Angels as lords (أرباباً), contrary to the explicit claims of the Christians and the apparent claims of the Jews—

He intensified the denunciation against them regarding this. How could such a thing occur when Allah took the covenant (الميثاق) from the Prophets that they must believe in every Prophet who comes to them, whether preceding them or succeeding them, and that they must aid him? This is achieved by each of them confirming those Prophets who preceded him and giving glad tidings of those who would succeed him—such as Jesus (عليه السلام) confirming Moses and his law, and giving glad tidings of Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم). Likewise, He, the Exalted, took the covenant from them to take a pledge (العهد) to that effect from their nations and made them witnesses over them. He explained that this is Islam (الإسلام) — the total submission to Allah — whose authority encompasses everyone in the heavens and the earth.

Then He commanded His Prophet to proceed upon this covenant in a manner of acceptance and obedience, believing in Allah and in all that He revealed to His Prophets without differentiating between them, and to submit (يسلم) to Allah, the Glorified, and to represent himself and his nation in this. This is the meaning of taking the covenant from him directly and from his nation through him, as the explanation will follow.

His saying, the Exalted: وإذ أخذ الله ميثاق النبيين لما آتيتكم من كتاب وحكمة ثم جاءكم رسول مصدق لما معكم لتؤمنن به ولتنصرنه "When Allah took the covenant of the prophets: ‘Whatever I give you of the Book and wisdom, and then there comes to you an apostle confirming what is with you, you shall surely believe in him and help him.’" (3:81) The verse informs of a covenant that was taken. Allah took this covenant for the benefit of the Prophets, as indicated by His saying: ثم جاءكم رسول "and then there comes to you an apostle..."; just as He, the Exalted, took it from the Prophets, as indicated by His saying: أأقررتم وأخذتم على ذلكم إصري "‘Do you pledge and accept My covenant on this condition?’" and His subsequent saying: قل آمنا بالله "Say, ‘We believe in Allah...’" (3:84). Thus, the covenant is one taken for the Prophets and taken from them, even if it was also taken from others through their mediation.

Accordingly, it is permissible that "the covenant of the Prophets" (ميثاق النبيين) refers to the covenant taken from them or the one taken for them, as the covenant is one and the same. In other words, "the Prophets" could refer to those for whom the covenant was taken and those from whom it was taken. However, the context of His saying: ما كان لبشر أن يؤتيه الله "It does not behoove any human that Allah should give him..." (3:79) until the end of the two verses, in its connection to this verse, supports the view that "the Prophets" refers to those from whom the covenant was taken. The unity of context implies that after Allah gave the Prophets the Book, judgment, and prophethood, it is inconceivable for them to call toward a partner [with Allah]. How could they do so when the covenant was taken from them to believe in and aid other Prophets who call toward the monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — of Allah, the Glorified? Thus, it is more appropriate to begin by mentioning the covenant in terms of its being taken from the Prophets.

Regarding His saying: لما آتيتكم من كتاب وحكمة "Whatever I give you of the Book and wisdom," the famous recitation—which is the recitation of all except Hamzah—is with a fatha on the lam and without doubling (تخفيف) in لما. According to this, ma (ما) is a relative pronoun (موصولة) and آتيتكم (and it has been recited as آتيناكم) is its relative clause (صلته). The pronoun [referring back to the relative pronoun] is omitted, indicated by His saying: من كتاب وحكمة. The relative pronoun is the subject (مبتدأ), and its predicate (خبر) is His saying: لتؤمنن به "you shall surely believe in him...". The lam in لما is the introductory lam (ابتدائية), and the lam in لتؤمنن به is the lam of the oath (لام القسم). The whole phrase is an explanation of the covenant taken. The meaning is: "For that which I have given you of Book and wisdom, and then there comes to you a messenger confirming what is with you, you shall certainly believe in him and aid him."

It is also possible that ma (ما) is conditional (شرطية) and its apodosis (جزاؤها) is His saying لتؤمنن به. The meaning would be: "Whatever I give you of Book and wisdom, and then there comes to you a messenger confirming what is with you, you must believe in him and aid him." This is better because the entry of the lam (whose oath is implied) into the apodosis is more common, the meaning is smoother and clearer, and the conditional form is more familiar in the context of covenants. As for the recitation with a kasra on the lam in لما, the lam there is for causation (للتعليل) and ma is a relative pronoun; however, the recitation with fatha is preferred.

The address in His saying آتيتكم "I give you" and جاءكم "comes to you," although appearing at first glance to be directed at the Prophets, is qualified by His subsequent saying: أأقررتم وأخذتم على ذلكم إصري "‘Do you pledge and accept My covenant on this condition?’" This serves as a context (قرينة) that the address is to the Prophets and their nations together. That is, the address is specific to them, but its ruling encompasses them and all their nations. Thus, it is incumbent upon the nations to believe and aid, just as it is incumbent upon the Prophets to believe and aid.

The apparent meaning of His saying: ثم جاءكم رسول مصدق لما معكم "and then there comes to you an apostle confirming what is with you," implies a temporal sequence (التراخي الزماني); meaning that the preceding Prophet must believe in and aid the succeeding Prophet. As for what appears from His saying: قل آمنا بالله "Say, ‘We believe in Allah...’" (3:84)—that the covenant is taken from both the predecessor and the successor for one another, and that the successor must believe in and aid the predecessor just as the reverse is true—this is a matter sensed through the implication (فحوى) of the address rather than the literal wording of the verse, as will be explained, Allah the Mighty willing.

Regarding His saying: لتؤمنن به ولتنصرنه "you shall surely believe in him and help him," although it is possible for the first pronoun [in "believe in him"] to refer to the Messenger just like the second pronoun—since there is no harm in a Prophet believing in another Prophet, as the Exalted said: آمَنَ الرَّسُولُ بِمَا أُنزِلَ إِلَيْهِ مِن رَّبِّهِ وَالْمُؤْمِنُونَ كُلٌّ آمَنَ بِاللَّهِ وَمَلَائِكَتِهِ وَكُتُبِهِ وَرُسُلِهِ "The Apostle has faith in what has been sent down to him from his Lord, and all the faithful. Each [of them] has faith in Allah, His angels, His scriptures and His apostles..." (2:285)—

However, what is apparent from His saying: قل آمنا بالله وبما أُنزل علينا وما أُنزل علی إبراهيم "Say, ‘We believe in Allah and what was sent down to us, and what was sent down to Abraham...’" (3:84) is that it refers back to what they were given of the Book and wisdom, while the second pronoun refers to the Messenger. The meaning would be: "You shall surely believe in what I have given you of Book and wisdom, and you shall surely aid the Messenger who comes to you confirming what is with you."

His saying, the Exalted: قال أأقررتم وأخذتم على ذلكم إصري قالوا أقررنا "He said, ‘Do you pledge and accept My covenant on this condition?’ They said, ‘We pledge.’" (3:81) The interrogative is for affirmation (للتقرير). Acknowledgment (الإقرار) is well-known. The isr (الإصر) is the pledge or covenant (العهد), and it is the object of "you accept" (أخذتم). Accepting a covenant necessitates a party from whom it is taken other than the one taking it, and these are none other than the nations of the Prophets. Thus, the meaning is: "Do you acknowledge the covenant, and have you taken My pledge from your nations?" They said: "We acknowledge."

It has been said that "accepting the pledge" refers to the Prophets accepting it for themselves. In this case, His saying وأخذتم على ذلكم إصري "and accept My covenant on this condition" would be an explanatory apposition (عطف بيان) for His saying أأقررتم "Do you pledge." This is supported by their reply قالوا أقررنا "They said, ‘We pledge’" without mentioning "accepting" in the response. According to this, the covenant would not extend beyond the Prophets to their nations. However, this is rendered unlikely by His saying: قال فاشهدوا "He said, ‘Then bear witness...’" because witnessing (الشهادة) apparently occurs against others. Likewise, His subsequent saying: قل آمنا بالله "Say, ‘We believe in Allah...’" without saying "Say, ‘I believe’" (قل آمنت) suggests that it is the faith of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) on behalf of himself and his nation. Unless it is said that the participation of the nations with the Prophets is only inferred from these two phrases—namely His saying فاشهدوا and قل آمنا بالله—without His saying وأخذتم providing any indication of that.

His saying, the Exalted: قال فاشهدوا وأنا معكم من الشاهدين "He said, ‘Then bear witness, and I am with you among the witnesses.’" (3:81) The apparent meaning of witnessing, as mentioned, is that it be against others. Thus, it is a witnessing by the Prophets and their nations together. This is supported, as mentioned, by His saying: قل آمنا بالله. It is also supported by the context, as the verses are structured to argue against the People of the Book for their failure to respond to the call of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم), just as they argue against them regarding what they attributed to Jesus, Moses (عليهما السلام), and others, as indicated by His saying: أفغير دين الله يبغون "Do they seek a religion other than Allah’s...?" (3:83) and other verses.

It is sometimes said that the meaning of فأشهدوا "Then bear witness" is the witnessing of some Prophets over others. It is also said that those addressed by فاشهدوا are the Angels rather than the Prophets.

While both meanings are possible in themselves, the wording does not clearly indicate either of them without a context (قرينة), and you have seen that the context points to the contrary.

Among the subtle points (اللطائف) occurring in the verse is that the covenant is taken from the Prophets for the sake of the Messengers (الرسل), as suggested by His saying: وإذ أخذنا من النبيين "And when We took from the prophets..." until His saying: ثم جاءكم رسول "and then there comes to you an apostle...". It was previously mentioned in the commentary on His saying: كَانَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً وَاحِدَةً "Mankind were a single community..." (2:213) the difference between prophethood (النبوة) and messengership (الرسالة), and that the Messenger (الرسول) is more specific in its instance than the Prophet (النبي).

Thus, according to the apparent meaning of the wording, the covenant is taken from the station of prophethood for the station of messengership, without indicating the reverse.

By this, one may critique what some have mentioned: that the summary of the verse's meaning is that the covenant was taken from all Prophets to confirm one another and command belief in one another—meaning that the religion is one, to which all Prophets call. This is obvious.

The summary of the verse's meaning, based on what has preceded, is: Allah took the covenant from the Prophets and their nations that if Allah gave them the Book and wisdom and a Messenger came to them confirming what was with them, they must believe in what He gave them and aid the Messenger. From the Prophets, this entails the successor confirming the predecessor and contemporary, and the predecessor giving glad tidings of the successor and instructing the nation. From the nation, it entails faith, confirmation, and aid. The necessary consequence of this is the unity of the divine religion.

As for what some exegetes mentioned—that the meaning of the verse is that Allah took the covenant from the Prophets to confirm Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) and give glad tidings to their nations of his advent—while this is correct, it is a matter indicated by the context of the verses as previously noted, rather than the verse in itself due to the generality of the wording. Rather, it is inferred from the verse's occurrence within the argument against the People of the Book and the blame and reproach directed at them for their preoccupation with distorting their books, concealing the signs of prophethood, and their stubbornness and rebellion against the explicit truth.

His saying, the Exalted: فمن تولی بعد ذلك "Then whoever turns away after this..." (3:82) is an emphasis of the aforementioned covenant taken, and the meaning is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: أفغير دين الله يبغون وله أسلم "Do they seek a religion other than Allah’s, while to Him submits...?" (3:83) is a derivation from the preceding verse involving the taking of the covenant from the Prophets. The meaning is: If the religion of Allah is one—and it is that upon which the covenant was taken from all Prophets and their nations—and it was incumbent upon the preceding Prophets and nations to give glad tidings of the succeeding Messenger and believe in what he brings and confirm him, then what do these People of the Book intend when they have disbelieved in you? Their apparent state is that they seek religion; do they then seek other than Islam, which is the only religion of Allah?

Therefore, they do not believe you nor do they hold fast to the religion of Islam, even though it was mandatory for them to seek refuge in Islam because it is the religion based upon the primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being. Religion must be so. The proof for this is that whoever is in the heavens and the earth among those possessed of intellect and perception are submissive (مسلمون) to Allah in the realm of creation (التكوين); thus, they must submit to Him in the realm of legislation (التشريع).

His saying, the Exalted: وله أسلم من في السموات والأرض طوعاً وكرها "while to Him submits whoever is in the heavens and the earth, willingly or unwillingly" (3:83). This submission (الإسلام) encompasses whoever is in the heavens and the earth, including the People of the Book who are mentioned as not being submissive. The word أسلم (submitted) is in the past tense, apparently indicating past occurrence and certain realization. This is the existential submission (التسليم التكويني) to the command of Allah, rather than Islam in the sense of devotional humility (الخضوع العبودي). This is supported or indicated by His saying: طوعاً وكرهاً "willingly or unwillingly."

Accordingly, His saying وله أسلم is a case of sufficing with the mention of the proof and cause instead of the thing proven and the effect. The implied meaning is: "Do they seek other than Islam? It is the religion of Allah because whoever is in the heavens and the earth is submissive to Him, yielding to His command. If they are pleased with it, their yielding is willing (طوعاً) from themselves; and if they dislike what He willed and desired something else, the command remains His command and it occurs upon them unwillingly (كرهاً) without their volition."

From here it becomes clear that the "and" (الواو) in طوعاً وكرهاً is for division (للتقسيم), and that "willingly" and "unwillingly" refer to their pleasure with what Allah willed for them of what they love, and their dislike for what He willed for them of what they do not love, such as death, poverty, illness, and the like.

His saying, the Exalted: وإليه يرجعون "and to Him they will be returned" (3:83). This is another reason for the obligation of seeking Islam as a religion, for their return is to Allah, their true Master, not to that toward which their disbelief and polytheism (الشرك) — the association of partners with Allah — lead them.

His saying, the Exalted: قل آمنا بالله وما أُنزل علينا "Say, ‘We believe in Allah and what was sent down to us...’" (3:84). The Prophet was commanded to proceed upon the covenant taken from him and others, saying on behalf of himself and the believers of his nation: "We believe in Allah and what was sent down to us..."

This is among the evidences that the covenant was taken from the Prophets and their nations together, as alluded to just now.

His saying, the Exalted: وما أُنزل علی إبراهيم وإسماعيل "and what was sent down to Abraham and Ishmael..." (3:84) until the end of the verse. Those mentioned by name are the Prophets from the progeny of Abraham. The verse is not devoid of an indication that the "Tribes" (الأسباط) refers to the Prophets from the progeny of Jacob or from the tribes of the Children of Israel, such as David, Solomon, Jonah, Job, and others.

His saying: والنبيون من ربهم "and what was given to the prophets from their Lord" (3:84) generalizes the discourse to include Adam, Noah, and those besides them. Then He grouped them all by saying: لا نفرق بين أحد منهم ونحن له مسلمون "we make no distinction between any of them, and to Him do we submit."

His saying, the Exalted: ومن يبتغ غير الإسلام ديناً فلن يقبل منه "Should anyone follow a religion other than Islam, it shall never be accepted from him..." (3:85) is a negation of anything other than the case established by the covenant taken. In it is an emphasis on the obligation of proceeding upon the covenant.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Majma', narrated from Amir al-Mu'minin (peace be upon him): "Allah took the covenant from the Prophets before our Prophet that they should inform their nations of his advent and his description, give them glad tidings of him, and command them to confirm him."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Jarir narrated from Ali ibn Abi Talib (may Allah be pleased with him), who said: "Allah did not send any Prophet, from Adam onwards, except that He took from him a pledge (العهد) regarding Muhammad: that if he were sent while the Prophet was alive, he must believe in him and aid him; and He commanded him to take the pledge from his people." Then he recited: وإذ أخذ الله ميثاق النبيين لما آتيتكم من كتاب وحكمة "When Allah took the covenant of the prophets: ‘Whatever I give you of the Book and wisdom...’"

I say: The two narrations explain the verse through the totality of what the wording and context indicate, as previously mentioned.

In al-Majma' and al-Jawami', narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding the verse: "Its meaning is: 'When Allah took the covenant of the nations of the Prophets, every nation to confirm its Prophet and act upon what he brought them.' But they did not fulfill it; they abandoned many of their laws and distorted much."

I say: What is mentioned in the narration is of the nature of mentioning an instance (مصداق) to which the verse applies; thus, it does not contradict the fact that the intended meaning of the verse encompasses the Prophets and their nations together.

Also in al-Majma', from Amir al-Mu'minin (peace be upon him) regarding His saying, the Exalted: أأقررتم وأخذتم "‘Do you pledge and accept...’": "He said: 'Do you acknowledge and have you taken the pledge (العهد) for that from your nations?' The Prophets and their nations said: 'We acknowledge that which You commanded us to acknowledge.' Allah said: 'Then bear witness to that against your nations, and I am with you among the witnesses against you and against your nations.'"

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Jarir narrated from Ali ibn Abi Talib regarding His saying: قال فاشهدوا "He said, ‘Then bear witness...’": "He says: 'Then bear witness against your nations regarding that, and I am with you among the witnesses against you and against them. So whoever turns away from you, O Muhammad, after this pledge from all nations, those are the transgressors (الفاسقون)—they are the ones disobedient in disbelief.'"

I say: The justification for the meaning of the narration has already been mentioned.

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "He said to them in the [realm of] particles (الذر): أأقررتم وأخذتم علی ذلكم إصري 'Do you pledge and accept My covenant (عهدي) on this condition?' They said: 'We pledge.' Allah said to the Angels: 'Then bear witness.'"

I say: The wording of the verse does not preclude this, even if it is not inferred from its apparent meaning, as previously stated.

In al-Durr al-Manthur regarding His saying, the Exalted: ومن يبتغ غير الإسلام ديناً "Should anyone follow a religion other than Islam..." (3:85), Ahmad and al-Tabarani in al-Awsat narrated from Abu Hurayrah, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) said: 'Deeds will come on the Day of Resurrection. Prayer will come and say: "O Lord, I am Prayer." He will say: "You are upon good." Charity will come and say: "O Lord, I am Charity." He will say: "You are upon good." Then Fasting will come and say: "I am Fasting." He will say: "You are upon good." Then [other] deeds will come, and for all of them Allah will say: "You are upon good; by you today I take and by you I give."' Allah said in His Book: ومن يبتغ غير الإسلام ديناً فلن يقبل منه وهو في الآخرة من الخاسرين 'Should anyone follow a religion other than Islam, it shall never be accepted from him, and he will be among the losers in the Hereafter.'"

In al-Tawhid and the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi regarding the verse, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "It is their monotheism (توحيدهم) of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic."

I say: The mentioned monotheism necessitates submission (التسليم) in all that Allah, the Exalted, desires from His servants; thus, it returns to the meaning we provided in the commentary.

If it were intended as merely the negation of a partner, then "willingly" and "unwillingly" would refer to voluntary and involuntary indication [of His oneness].

Know that there are several other narrations narrated by al-Ayyashi and al-Qummi in their commentaries and others regarding the meaning of His saying: وإذ أخذ الله ميثاق النبيين "When Allah took the covenant of the prophets...". In them, it is mentioned: "You shall surely believe in the Messenger of Allah, and you shall surely aid the Commander of the Faithful (عليهما الصلوة والسلام)." Their apparent sense is an interpretation of the verse by referring the pronoun in لتؤمنن به "you shall surely believe in him" to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم), and the pronoun in ولتنصرنه "and help him" to the Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام), without any evidence from the wording to indicate this.

However, in what al-Ayyashi narrated from Sallam ibn al-Mustanir, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), he said: "They have named themselves with a name that Allah did not name anyone with except Ali ibn Abi Talib, and its esoteric interpretation (تأويله) has not yet come." I said: "May I be your ransom, when will its esoteric interpretation come?" He said: "When it comes, Allah will gather before him the Prophets and the believers so that they may aid him. That is the saying of Allah: وإذ أخذ الله ميثاق النبيين لما آتيتكم من كتاب وحكمة until His saying: وأنا معكم من الشاهدين."

By this, the difficulty is eased. For the objection would only arise if the narrations were intended as exegesis (تفسير); as for esoteric interpretation (التأويل), you have already learned that it is not of the nature of [literal] meaning, nor is it linked to the wording, in what preceded in the commentary of His saying: هُوَ الَّذِي أَنزَلَ عَلَيْكَ الْكِتَابَ "It is He who has sent down to you the Book..." (3:7).

Ayahs

كَيْفَ يَهْدِى ٱللَّهُ قَوْمًا كَفَرُوا۟ بَعْدَ إِيمَـٰنِهِمْ وَشَهِدُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱلرَّسُولَ حَقٌّ وَجَآءَهُمُ ٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتُ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٨٦)
How shall Allah guide a people who have disbelieved after their faith and [after] bearing witness that the Apostle is true, and [after] manifest proofs had come to them? Allah does not guide the wrongdoing lot. (86)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ جَزَآؤُهُمْ أَنَّ عَلَيْهِمْ لَعْنَةَ ٱللَّهِ وَٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ وَٱلنَّاسِ أَجْمَعِينَ (٨٧)
Their requital is that there shall be upon them the curse of Allah, the angels, and all mankind. (87)
خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا لَا يُخَفَّفُ عَنْهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابُ وَلَا هُمْ يُنظَرُونَ (٨٨)
They will remain in it [forever], and their punishment will not be lightened, nor will they be granted any respite, (88)
إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ تَابُوا۟ مِنۢ بَعْدِ ذَٰلِكَ وَأَصْلَحُوا۟ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٨٩)
except such as repent after that and make amends, for Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (89)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بَعْدَ إِيمَـٰنِهِمْ ثُمَّ ٱزْدَادُوا۟ كُفْرًا لَّن تُقْبَلَ تَوْبَتُهُمْ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلضَّآلُّونَ (٩٠)
Indeed those who turn faithless after their faith, and then advance in faithlessness, their repentance will never be accepted, and it is they who are the astray. (90)
