إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَمَاتُوا۟ وَهُمْ كُفَّارٌ فَلَن يُقْبَلَ مِنْ أَحَدِهِم مِّلْءُ ٱلْأَرْضِ ذَهَبًا وَلَوِ ٱفْتَدَىٰ بِهِۦٓ ۗ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ وَمَا لَهُم مِّن نَّـٰصِرِينَ (٩١)
Indeed those who turn faithless and die while they are faithless, a world of gold will not be accepted from any of them should he offer it for ransom. For such there will be a painful punishment, and they will have no helpers. (91)

(Commentary)

The verses are potentially connected to the preceding discourse concerning the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب), although it is also possible for them to stand independently and be distinct from what preceded them; this is apparent.

His saying, the Exalted: كَيْفَ يَهْدِي اللَّهُ قَوْمًا كَفَرُوا بَعْدَ إِيمَانِهِمْ "How shall Allah guide a people who have disbelieved after their belief" (3:86). The interrogation here denotes improbability (الاستبعاد) and denunciation (الإنكار), and what is intended is the impossibility of guidance (الهداية) — the divine leading toward truth and the straight path. He concluded the verse by saying: وَاللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الظَّالِمِينَ "and Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people." It has been previously mentioned regarding similar phrases that the description (الوصف) intimates causality (العلية); meaning, He does not guide them as long as this description exists within them. This does not preclude His guiding them should they return and offer repentance (التوبة) — the act of turning back to Allah from sin.

As for His saying: وَشَهِدُوا أَنَّ الرَّسُولَ حَقٌّ "and [after] they had borne witness that the Apostle is true," if the intended ones are the People of the Book, then their witnessing is their observation that the signs of prophethood (آيات النبوة) in their possession correspond to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم, as suggested by His saying: وَجَاءَهُمُ الْبَيِّنَاتُ "and manifest proofs had come to them." If the intended ones are the apostates (أهل الردة) from among the Muslims, then their witnessing is their acknowledgment of the Messenger—not a formal acknowledgment based on ignorance or prejudice (الحمية) and the like, but rather an acknowledgment based on the clarity of the matter, as suggested by His saying: وَجَاءَهُمُ الْبَيِّنَاتُ.

In any case, the conjunction of His saying: "and [after] they had borne witness..." with the beginning of the discourse indicates that the intended disbelief (الكفر) is disbelief after the manifestation of the truth and the completion of the proof (تمام الحجة). Thus, it is disbelief arising from stubbornness (عناد) against the truth and persistence (لجاج) against its people. This constitutes transgression (البغي) without right and wrongdoing (الظلم) whose perpetrator is not guided toward salvation and success.

It has been said regarding His saying: "and [after] they had borne witness..." that it is coordinated (معطوف) with His saying: "their belief" (إيمانهم) because of the verbal sense it contains. The implied meaning would be: "they disbelieved after they had believed and witnessed..." Alternatively, the "and" (الواو) denotes the circumstantial state (الحال), making the clause a circumstantial one with the implicit particle "قد".

His saying, the Exalted: أُولَٰئِكَ جَزَاؤُهُمْ أَنَّ عَلَيْهِمْ لَعْنَةَ اللَّهِ "As for such, their retribution shall be that there shall be upon them the curse of Allah" (3:87) until His saying: وَلَا هُمْ يُنْظَرُونَ "nor shall they be granted any respite" (3:88). The discussion regarding the meaning of the entirety of the curse returning upon them has already passed in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: أُولَٰئِكَ يَلْعَنُهُمُ اللَّهُ وَيَلْعَنُهُمُ اللَّاعِنُونَ "They are the ones whom Allah curses, and who are cursed by the cursers" (2:159).

His saying, the Exalted: إِلَّا الَّذِينَ تَابُوا مِنْ بَعْدِ ذَٰلِكَ وَأَصْلَحُوا "excepting those who repent after that and reform" (3:89); meaning, they entered into righteousness (الصلاح). What is intended is that their repentance be sincere (نصوحاً), washing away the filth of disbelief and purifying their inner being (الباطن) with faith (الإيمان). As for performing righteous deeds (الصالحات) — acts of virtue performed in accordance with divine command — although it is something that branches out from this and is a necessary consequence of it, it is not a constitutive element (مقوّم) of this repentance nor a pillar (ركن) of it; nor is there any indication of that in the verse.

In His saying: فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ "for Allah is indeed all-forgiving, all-merciful," the cause (العلة) is placed in the position of the effect (المعلول). The implied meaning is: "then Allah will forgive him and have mercy on him, for Allah is All-Forgiving, Most Merciful."

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بَعْدَ إِيمَانِهِمْ ثُمَّ ازْدَادُوا كُفْرًا "Indeed those who disbelieve after their belief and then go on adding to their disbelief" (3:90) until the end of the two verses, is a justification (تعليل) for what was contained in His initial saying: "How shall Allah guide a people who have disbelieved..." It is of the nature of justification by applying a general universal (الكلي العام) to a specific particular (الفرد الخاص). The meaning is that he who disbelieves after the manifestation of the truth and the completion of the proof against him, and does not repent thereafter with a reforming repentance, is one of two men:

Either a disbeliever (كافر) who disbelieves and then increases in disbelief, thereby becoming tyrannical (يطغي), such that there is no way for reform (الصلاح) to reach him. Such a person Allah does not guide, nor does He accept his repentance, because he does not truly return; rather, he is immersed in misguidance (الضلال), and there is no hope for his guidance.

Or a disbeliever who dies upon his disbelief and stubbornness without offering a repentance. Allah will not guide him in the Hereafter by entering him into Paradise, since he did not return to his Lord, and there is no substitute for that which he could offer as a ransom (يفتدي به), nor any intercessor (شفيع) or helper to intercede for him or help him.

From here it becomes clear that His saying: وَأُولَٰئِكَ هُمُ الضَّالُّونَ "and it is they who are the astray" (3:90), through its use of a nominal sentence (اسمية الجملة), the distal demonstrative (الإشارة البعيدة) in "those" (أولئك), the separating pronoun (ضمير الفصل), and the definite predicate with the "lam" (اللام), indicates the intensification of misguidance in them such that their guidance is not to be expected.

Likewise, it becomes clear that the intended meaning of His saying: وَمَا لَهُمْ مِنْ نَاصِرِينَ "and they shall have no helpers" (3:91) is the negation of their benefiting from intercessors (الشفعاء) — those who mediate on behalf of others before Allah — who are the "helpers" on the Day of Resurrection. The use of the plural form indicates the existence of helpers on the Day of Resurrection, as previously mentioned in the deduction regarding intercession (الشفاعة) from His saying, the Exalted: فَمَا لَنَا مِنْ شَافِعِينَ "So now we have no intercessors" (26:100) in the discussion on intercession (under verse 48 of Surah al-Baqarah); so refer back to it.

The second verse included the mention of the negation of ransom (الفداء) and helpers because they are like a substitute (البدل), and a substitute is only for something lost (فائت) that a person misses. They have missed repentance in this world, and there is no substitute for it that can take its place in the Hereafter.

From here it becomes clear that His saying: وَمَاتُوا وَهُمْ كُفَّارٌ "and die while they are faithless" (3:91) is in the sense of: "and they missed [the opportunity for] repentance." Thus, this explanation, which appears to be an exclusive restriction (الحصر), is not contradicted by what Allah the Exalted mentioned in His saying: وَلَيْسَتِ التَّوْبَةُ لِلَّذِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ السَّيِّئَاتِ حَتَّىٰ إِذَا حَضَرَ أَحَدَهُمُ الْمَوْتُ قَالَ إِنِّي تُبْتُ الْآنَ وَلَا الَّذِينَ يَمُوتُونَ وَهُمْ كُفَّارٌ أُولَٰئِكَ أَعْتَدْنَا لَهُمْ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا "But repentance is not for those who go on committing misdeeds until, when death visits one of them, he says, ‘I repent now,’ nor for those who die while they are faithless. For such We have prepared a painful punishment" (4:18). For what is intended by the "presence of death" (حضور الموت) is the appearance of the signs of the Hereafter and the severance of this world; at that point, repentance is missed.

The "fill" (الملء) in His saying: مِلْءُ الْأَرْضِ ذَهَبًا "an earthful of gold" (3:91) is the amount of something that a vessel can contain. Thus, the earth is treated as a vessel filled with gold; the sentence is of the nature of an imaginary metaphor (استعارة تخييلية) and an implicit metaphor (استعارة بالكناية).

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Majma', regarding His saying, the Exalted: كَيْفَ يَهْدِي اللَّهُ قَوْمًا "How shall Allah guide a people...": It is said that the verses were revealed concerning a man from the Ansar called Harith bin Suwayd bin al-Samit. He had treacherously killed al-Mujadhdhar bin Ziyad al-Balawi, fled, and apostatized from Islam, joining [the people of] Mecca. Later he felt regret and sent word to his people to ask the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم: "Is there any repentance for me?" They asked, and the verse was revealed until His saying: إِلَّا الَّذِينَ تَابُوا "excepting those who repent." A man from his people brought it to him, and he said: "I know that you are indeed truthful, and the Messenger of Allah is more truthful than you, and Allah is the most truthful of the three." He returned to Medina, repented, and his Islam became good. This is narrated from Mujahid and al-Suddi, and it is what is narrated from Abu Abdillah عليه‌السلام.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Ishaq and Ibn al-Mundhir narrated from Ibn Abbas: That al-Harith bin Suwayd killed al-Mujadhdhar bin Ziyad and Qays bin Zayd, one of the Banu Dabiah, on the day of Uhud, then joined Quraysh and stayed in Mecca. Then he sent word to his brother al-Julas seeking repentance so he could return to his people, so Allah revealed concerning him: كَيْفَ يَهْدِي اللَّهُ قَوْمًا until the end of the story.

I say: The story has been narrated through other chains with variations. Among them is what is narrated from Ikrimah: That it was revealed concerning Abu Amir al-Rahib, al-Harith bin Suwayd bin al-Samit, and Wahwah bin al-Aslat among twelve men who turned back from Islam and joined Quraysh. Then they wrote to their families: "Is there any repentance for us?" So the verses إِلَّا الَّذِينَ تَابُوا مِنْ بَعْدِ ذَٰلِكَ were revealed.

Among them is what is in al-Majma' regarding His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بَعْدَ إِيمَانِهِمْ ثُمَّ ازْدَادُوا "Indeed those who disbelieve after their belief and then go on adding...": That it was revealed concerning eleven companions of al-Harith bin Suwayd. When al-Harith returned, they said: "We will remain in Mecca upon disbelief as long as we please, and whenever we wish to return, we shall return, and there will be revealed concerning us what was revealed concerning al-Harith." When the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم conquered Mecca, those of them who entered Islam did so and their repentance was accepted. Then the verse إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَمَاتُوا وَهُمْ كُفَّارٌ "Indeed those who turn faithless and die while they are faithless" was revealed concerning those of them who died as disbelievers. He attributed this to some [commentators].

It was also said that it was revealed concerning the People of the Book. And it was said: His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بَعْدَ إِيمَانِهِمْ ثُمَّ ازْدَادُوا كُفْرًا was revealed specifically concerning the Jews, as they believed, then disbelieved in Jesus, then increased in disbelief in Muhammad صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم and upon them both. Other things have also been said.

Reflection upon these views and narrations suggests that all of them are analytical opinions (أنظار اجتهادية) from the early commentators, as some have noted.

As for the narration from Imam al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام, it is disconnected (مرسلة) and weak (ضعيفة). Furthermore, it is possible for the occasions of revelation (أسباب النزول) to be multiple for a single verse or set of verses. And Allah knows best.

Ayahs

لَن تَنَالُوا۟ ٱلْبِرَّ حَتَّىٰ تُنفِقُوا۟ مِمَّا تُحِبُّونَ ۚ وَمَا تُنفِقُوا۟ مِن شَىْءٍ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِهِۦ عَلِيمٌ (٩٢)
You will never attain piety until you spend out of what you hold dear, and whatever you may spend of anything, Allah indeed knows it. (92)
۞ كُلُّ ٱلطَّعَامِ كَانَ حِلًّا لِّبَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ إِلَّا مَا حَرَّمَ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلُ عَلَىٰ نَفْسِهِۦ مِن قَبْلِ أَن تُنَزَّلَ ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةُ ۗ قُلْ فَأْتُوا۟ بِٱلتَّوْرَىٰةِ فَٱتْلُوهَآ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (٩٣)
All food was lawful to the Children of Israel except what Israel had forbidden himself before the Torah was sent down. Say, ‘Bring the Torah, and read it, if you are truthful.’ (93)
فَمَنِ ٱفْتَرَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ٱلْكَذِبَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ ذَٰلِكَ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ (٩٤)
So whoever fabricates a lie against Allah after that—it is they who are the wrongdoers. (94)
قُلْ صَدَقَ ٱللَّهُ ۗ فَٱتَّبِعُوا۟ مِلَّةَ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ حَنِيفًا وَمَا كَانَ مِنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ (٩٥)
Say, ‘Allah has spoken the truth; so follow the creed of Abraham, a Hanif, and he was not one of the polytheists. (95)

(Commentary)

The connection of the first verse to what precedes it is not entirely clear. It is possible that it was not revealed alongside the rest of the verses, whose interconnectedness is beyond doubt. You have already encountered a similar issue regarding the timing of revelation in His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ يَا أَهْلَ الْكِتَابِ تَعَالَوْا — "Say, ‘O People of the Book, come...’" (3:64).

It is sometimes said that the address in the verse is directed toward the Children of Israel (بني إسرائيل) and continues to be so. The gist of the meaning—following the previous rebuking and blaming of them for their love of the world and their preference for wealth and possessions over the religion of Allah—is: You are untruthful in your claim of being related to Allah, the Glorified, and His prophets, and that you are people of righteousness and godwariness (التقوى). For you love the most precious of your possessions and are stingy in spending them; you only spend that which is mediocre and to which the soul is not attached—things whose loss or absence is of no consequence. This is despite the fact that righteousness (البر) cannot be attained except by a person spending what he loves from the most precious of his wealth, and its preservation is never missed by Allah, the Glorified. This is the summary of what has been said, but it contains an obvious forced interpretation (تمحل).

As for the rest of the verses, their connection to the preceding explanations is clear and beyond doubt.

His saying, the Exalted: لَن تَنَالُوا الْبِرَّ حَتَّىٰ تُنفِقُوا مِمَّا تُحِبُّونَ — You will never attain piety until you spend out of what you hold dear (3:92). "Attaining" (النيل) is reaching or arriving at something. Righteousness (البر) — the expansive performance of good deeds — is as al-Raghib stated: "Righteousness (البر) is the opposite of the sea (البحر). The concept of expansiveness was envisioned from it, and thus 'righteousness' was derived, meaning expansiveness in doing good." End quote.

His intent by "doing good" is broader than just internal acts, such as true belief (الاعتقاد الحق) and pure intention (النية الطاهرة), or external acts of the limbs, such as worship (العبادة) of Allah and spending in the way of Allah, the Exalted. Both categories are included in His saying, the Exalted: لَّيْسَ الْبِرَّ أَن تُوَلُّوا وُجُوهَكُمْ قِبَلَ الْمَشْرِقِ وَالْمَغْرِبِ وَلَٰكِنَّ الْبِرَّ مَنْ آمَنَ بِاللَّهِ وَالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ وَالْمَلَائِكَةِ وَالْكِتَابِ وَالنَّبِيِّينَ وَآتَى الْمَالَ عَلَىٰ حُبِّهِ ذَوِي الْقُرْبَىٰ وَالْيَتَامَىٰ وَالْمَسَاكِينَ وَابْنَ السَّبِيلِ وَالسَّائِلِينَ وَفِي الرِّقَابِ وَأَقَامَ الصَّلَاةَ وَآتَى الزَّكَاةَ وَالْمُوفُونَ بِعَهْدِهِمْ إِذَا عَاهَدُوا وَالصَّابِرِينَ فِي الْبَأْسَاءِ وَالضَّرَّاءِ وَحِينَ الْبَأْسِ — Piety is not to turn your faces to the east or the west; rather, piety is [personified by] those who have faith in Allah and the Last Day, the angels, the Book, and the prophets, and who give their wealth, for the love of Him, to relatives, orphans, the needy, the traveller and the beggar, and for [the freeing of] slaves, and maintain the prayer and give the zakat, and those who keep their pledges when they pledge, and those who are patient in adversity and hardship, and in the battlefront (2:177).

By joining this verse with His saying: لَن تَنَالُوا الْبِرَّ (You will never attain righteousness...), it becomes clear that the intent is that spending wealth despite one's love for it is one of the pillars of righteousness (البر), which is not complete except by the gathering of all its components. Indeed, making spending the ultimate goal for attaining righteousness indicates a particular concern and emphasis on this specific part, due to the human instinct (غريزة) of heart-felt attachment to the wealth one has accumulated. Man considers it as if it were a part of his own self; if he loses it, it is as if he has lost a part of his own life's vitality—unlike other acts of worship and deeds where no such loss or disappearance from him is apparent.

From here, the error in the statement of some becomes clear—those who claimed that "righteousness" is the act of spending what you love. It is as if this speaker treated it like saying: "You will not be saved from the pain of hunger until you eat," and the like. However, this is refuted by the previously mentioned verse.

It also becomes clear from the aforementioned verse of al-Baqarah that the intent of righteousness (البر) is its literal linguistic meaning, namely expansiveness in good, for the verse explained it through the totality of creedal and practical goodness. From this, the weakness of the view of some becomes apparent: that righteousness refers to Allah's favor and blessing; as well as the view of others: that it refers to Paradise (الجنة).

His saying, the Exalted: وَمَا تُنفِقُوا مِن شَيْءٍ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ بِهِ عَلِيمٌ — and whatever you spend, Allah indeed knows it (3:92). This is a reassurance for the souls of the spenders that whatever they spend of the wealth dear to them will not go to waste without reward, for Allah, who commanded them to do so, is All-Knowing of their spending and what they spend.

His saying, the Exalted: كُلُّ الطَّعَامِ كَانَ حِلًّا لِّبَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ إِلَّا مَا حَرَّمَ إِسْرَائِيلُ عَلَىٰ نَفْسِهِ مِن قَبْلِ أَن تُنَزَّلَ التَّوْرَاةُ — All food was lawful to the Children of Israel except what Israel had forbidden himself before the Torah was sent down (3:93). Food (الطعام) refers to everything that is eaten and used for nourishment. Among the people of the Hijaz, it was used specifically for wheat (البُر), and their minds would turn to it when the term was used absolutely. Lawfulness (الحل) is the opposite of prohibition (الحرمة); it seems to be derived from "untying" (الحل) as opposed to "tying" (العقد) or "binding" (العقل), thus conveying the meaning of being released or permitted. Israel (إسرائيل) is the Prophet Jacob (يعقوب), peace be upon him (عليه السلام). He was named so because he was a striver for the sake of Allah and victorious through Him. The People of the Book say its meaning is "the victor who prevailed over Allah," because he wrestled with Allah in a place called Peniel and defeated Him (according to the Torah)—this is among the things the Quran deems false and which reason (العقل) considers impossible.

His saying: إِلَّا مَا حَرَّمَ إِسْرَائِيلُ عَلَىٰ نَفْسِهِ (except what Israel had forbidden himself) is an exception from the "food" mentioned just before. His saying: مِن قَبْلِ أَن تُنَزَّلَ التَّوْرَاةُ (before the Torah was sent down) relates to the verb "was" (كان) in the first clause. The meaning is: Allah did not prohibit any food for the Children of Israel before the revelation of the Torah, except what Israel had prohibited for himself.

In His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ فَأْتُوا بِالتَّوْرَاةِ فَاتْلُوهَا إِن كُنتُمْ صَادِقِينَ — Say, ‘Bring the Torah and read it, if you are truthful’ (3:93), there is an indication that they used to deny this—meaning the lawfulness of all food for them before the Torah. This is further evidenced by the fact that they used to deny abrogation (النسخ) — the replacement of one religious ruling with another — in divine laws and considered it impossible, as previously mentioned in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: مَا نَنَسَخْ مِنْ آيَةٍ أَوْ نُنْسِهَا — "Any verse that We abrogate or cause to be forgotten..." (2:106). Naturally, they would deny His saying, the Exalted: فَبِظُلْمٍ مِّنَ الَّذِينَ هَادُوا حَرَّمْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ طَيِّبَاتٍ أُحِلَّتْ لَهُمْ — "Due to the wrongdoing of the Jews, We prohibited them certain good things that were permitted to them" (4:160).

Similarly, His saying afterward: قُلْ صَدَقَ اللَّهُ فَاتَّبِعُوا مِلَّةَ إِبْرَاهِيمَ حَنِيفًا — Say, ‘Allah has spoken the truth; so follow the creed of Abraham, a Hanif’ (3:95), indicates that they were using what they denied (regarding the lawfulness of all food for them before the Torah, and that the prohibition was only sent down upon them due to their wrongdoing as an abrogation of lawfulness with prohibition) as a means to cast doubt upon the Muslims. They used it to object to what the Messenger of Allah, صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم, was reporting from his Lord: that his religion is the creed of Abraham, the Hanif (الحنيف) — the primordial monotheistic faith that inclines toward truth and away from all falsehood — which is a natural creed (ملة فطرة) containing neither excess nor deficiency. For how could it be otherwise when they used to say that Abraham was a Jew following the law of the Torah? How then could his creed include the lawfulness of what the Torah prohibited, given that abrogation is not permissible?

Thus, it has become clear that the verse is concerned with repelling a doubt raised by the Jews. It appears from the fact that the verse does not explicitly quote the doubt from them—as the Quran usually does in most instances, such as His saying: وَقَالَتِ الْيَهُودُ يَدُ اللَّهِ مَغْلُولَةٌ — "The Jews say, ‘Allah’s hand is tied’" (5:64), and His saying: وَقَالُوا لَن تَمَسَّنَا النَّارُ إِلَّا أَيَّامًا مَّعْدُودَةً — "And they say, ‘The Fire shall not touch us except for a number of days’" (2:80), and His saying: وَقَالُوا قُلُوبُنَا غُلْفٌ — "And they say, ‘Our hearts are uncircumcised’" (2:88), among many other verses.

This is also supported by His saying several verses later: قُلْ يَا أَهْلَ الْكِتَابِ لِمَ تَصُدُّونَ عَن سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ مَنْ آمَنَ — "Say, ‘O People of the Book! Why do you bar those who have faith from the way of Allah...’" until He says: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِن تُطِيعُوا فَرِيقًا مِّنَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتَابَ يَرُدُّوكُم بَعْدَ إِيمَانِكُمْ كَافِرِينَ — "O you who have faith! If you obey a group of those who were given the Book, they will turn you back into faithless ones after your having found faith" (3:99-100).

In short, it appears from this that it was a doubt the Jews were casting not upon the Messenger of Allah, صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم, but upon the believers during their meetings and conversations.

The gist of it was: How can the Prophet be truthful when he reports abrogation (النسخ), and that Allah only prohibited good things for the Children of Israel because of their wrongdoing? This would be an abrogation of a prior lawfulness, which they claimed is not permissible for Allah, the Glorified; rather, prohibited things are prohibited eternally without the possibility of changing Allah's judgment. The summary of the response from the Prophet, صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم, by the teaching of Allah the Exalted, is: The Torah itself speaks of all food being lawful before its revelation; so bring the Torah and read it if you are truthful in your claim. This is His saying: كُلُّ الطَّعَامِ كَانَ حِلًّا لِّبَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ (All food was lawful to the Children of Israel...) until His saying: إِن كُنتُمْ صَادِقِينَ (if you are truthful).

If you refuse to bring the Torah and read it, then admit that you are the ones fabricating lies against Allah and that you are the wrongdoers (الظالمون). That is His saying: فَمَنِ افْتَرَىٰ (So whoever fabricates...) until His saying: ظَالِمُونَ (wrongdoers).

It has thereby become clear that I am truthful in my call; so follow my creed, which is the creed of Abraham, a Hanif. That is His saying: قُلْ صَدَقَ اللَّهُ فَاتَّبِعُوا مِلَّةَ إِبْرَاهِيمَ (Say, ‘Allah has spoken the truth; so follow the creed of Abraham...’) to the end of the verse.

The commentators have various explanations for clarifying the meaning of the verse, but in any case, they mentioned that the verse addresses a doubt raised by the Jews related to abrogation, as mentioned.

The most peculiar thing said in this regard is what some mentioned: that the verse addresses a doubt raised by the Jews regarding abrogation, the formulation of which was: The Jews supposedly said, "If you, O Muhammad, are upon the creed of Abraham and the prophets after him—as you claim—then how do you permit what was prohibited for him and them, such as camel meat? Since you have permitted what was prohibited for them, you should not claim to be a confirmer of them or in agreement with their religion, nor should you single out Abraham by saying: 'I am the most worthy of him.'"

The summary of the response [according to this view] is: All food was lawful for people in general, including the Children of Israel, but the Children of Israel prohibited things for themselves by the sins and evil deeds they committed, as He said: فَبِظُلْمٍ مِّنَ الَّذِينَ هَادُوا حَرَّمْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ طَيِّبَاتٍ أُحِلَّتْ لَهُمْ — "Due to the wrongdoing of the Jews, We prohibited them certain good things that were permitted to them" (4:160). Thus, the intent of "Israel" is the people of Israel, as is used among them, not Jacob alone. The meaning of their prohibiting it for themselves is that they committed wrongdoing and earned sins, which became the cause for the prohibition. The phrase مِن قَبْلِ أَن تُنَزَّلَ التَّوْرَاةُ (before the Torah was sent down) relates to His saying: حَرَّمَ إِسْرَائِيلُ (Israel had forbidden). If the intent of "Israel" were Jacob himself, then the phrase "before the Torah was sent down" would be redundant and superfluous speech, as it is self-evident that Jacob lived long before the Torah, so there would be no reason to mention it.

This is the summary of what he mentioned. Another person mentioned something similar, except he said: The intent of the Children of Israel prohibiting it for themselves is their prohibiting it through their own legislation without it being based on a revelation from Allah, the Glorified, to one of their prophets, just as the Arabs of the Jahiliyyah (الجاهلية) — the period of ignorance before Islam — used to do, as Allah recounted in His Book.

Both of them have engaged in a level of forced interpretation (التكلف) that no expert would accept, taking the speech out of its proper course. The main thing that drove them to this was their interpreting His saying: مِن قَبْلِ أَن تُنَزَّلَ التَّوْرَاةُ (before the Torah was sent down) as relating to His saying: حَرَّمَ إِسْرَائِيلُ (Israel had forbidden), whereas it actually relates to His saying: كَانَ حِلًّا (was lawful) at the beginning of the sentence, and the phrase إِلَّا مَا حَرَّمَ (except what [Israel] had forbidden) is an parenthetical exception.

From this, it becomes clear that there is no need to take "Israel" to mean the Children of Israel, as they imagined, based on the supposed lack of soundness in the meaning otherwise.

Furthermore, while using "Israel" to mean the Children of Israel is permissible—similar to how they say "Bakr," "Taghlib," "Nizar," and "Adnan" meaning the sons of Bakr, Taghlib, Nizar, and Adnan—its occurrence regarding the Children of Israel is a usage not known among the Arabs at the time of revelation. Nor did the Quran follow this path with this word (outside of this instance they claim), despite the Children of Israel being mentioned in nearly forty places; among them is this very verse: كُلُّ الطَّعَامِ كَانَ حِلًّا لِّبَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ إِلَّا مَا حَرَّمَ إِسْرَائِيلُ عَلَىٰ نَفْسِهِ (All food was lawful to the Children of Israel except what Israel had forbidden himself). What, according to their view, is the difference between the two instances in the verse? Why would they be referred to first as "the Children of Israel" and then immediately followed by "Israel," especially since this is a context most prone to confusion? It is enough to note that the vast majority of commentators understood it to mean Jacob, not his sons.

One of the best proofs that it refers to Jacob is His saying: عَلَىٰ نَفْسِهِ (for himself), using the singular masculine pronoun referring back to Israel. If the intent were the Children of Israel, it would have been necessary to say "for itself" (على نفسها) or "for themselves" (على أنفسهم).

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ فَأْتُوا بِالتَّوْرَاةِ فَاتْلُوهَا إِن كُنتُمْ صَادِقِينَ — Say, ‘Bring the Torah and read it, if you are truthful’ (3:93). That is, so the truth may become clear as to which of the two parties is in the right: I or you. This is a response provided by Allah, the Exalted, to His Prophet, صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم.

His saying, the Exalted: فَمَنِ افْتَرَىٰ عَلَى اللَّهِ الْكَذِبَ مِن بَعْدِ ذَٰلِكَ فَأُولَٰئِكَ هُمُ الظَّالِمُونَ — So whoever fabricates a lie against Allah after that, it is they who are the wrongdoers (3:94). Its apparent meaning is that it is the speech of Allah, the Glorified, addressing His Prophet, صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم. Based on this, it contains a reassurance for the soul of the Prophet, صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم, that his enemies among the Jews are the wrongdoers (الظالمون) after this clarification of their fabrication of lies against Allah. It is also an allusion toward the Jews, and the speech follows the course of metonymy (الكناية).

As for the possibility of the speech being a continuation of the Prophet's words, it is not suited by the apparent singular form of the demonstrative address in His saying: مِن بَعْدِ ذَٰلِكَ (after that). On this basis as well, the speech follows the course of metonymy and veiling the defeated opponent so that the speech may be accepted, as in His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنَّا أَوْ إِيَّاكُمْ لَعَلَىٰ هُدًى أَوْ فِي ضَلَالٍ مُّبِينٍ — "Indeed either we or you are rightly guided or in manifest error" (34:24). The thing pointed to by "that" (ذلك) is the clarification and the proof.

He only said: مِن بَعْدِ ذَٰلِكَ (after that)—even though the fabricator is a wrongdoer in any case—because wrongdoing (الظلم) is not fully realized until after clarification, as has been said. The restriction in His saying: فَأُولَٰئِكَ هُمُ الظَّالِمُونَ (it is they who are the wrongdoers) is a restriction of reversal (قصر قلب) in any case.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ صَدَقَ اللَّهُ فَاتَّبِعُوا مِلَّةَ إِبْرَاهِيمَ حَنِيفًا — Say, ‘Allah has spoken the truth; so follow the creed of Abraham, a Hanif’ (3:95). That is: Since the truth is with me in what I have informed you of and called you toward, then follow my religion and admit the lawfulness of camel meat and other good things that Allah has permitted. He had only prohibited them for you as a punishment for your transgression and wrongdoing, as He, the Exalted, informed.

So His saying: فَاتَّبِعُوا (so follow...) is like a metonymy for following his religion. He did not mention it explicitly because they were already confessing to the creed of Abraham. This serves as an indication that what he calls to of religion is Hanif (حنيف) and natural (فطري), because the primordial nature (الفطرة) does not prevent man from eating good meats and other provisions.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Kafi and the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "When Israel ate camel meat, it would aggravate a pain in his side, so he prohibited camel meat for himself, and that was before the Torah was sent down. When the Torah was sent down, he did not prohibit it, nor did he eat it."

I say: Something close to this is narrated through the chains of the Ahl al-Sunnah wa al-Jama'ah.

In the narration, the phrase "he did not prohibit it, nor did he eat it"—the two subject pronouns refer to Moses, as indicated by the context. The meaning is: Moses did not prohibit it, nor did he eat it. It is also possible that "did not eat it" (لم يأكله) comes from the form of "feeding" (التأكيل), meaning to allow or enable eating. It appears from al-Taj that the second and third verbal forms carry the same meaning here.

Ayahs

إِنَّ أَوَّلَ بَيْتٍ وُضِعَ لِلنَّاسِ لَلَّذِى بِبَكَّةَ مُبَارَكًا وَهُدًى لِّلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٩٦)
Indeed the first house to be set up for mankind is the one at Bakkah, blessed and a guidance for all nations. (96)
فِيهِ ءَايَـٰتٌۢ بَيِّنَـٰتٌ مَّقَامُ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ ۖ وَمَن دَخَلَهُۥ كَانَ ءَامِنًا ۗ وَلِلَّهِ عَلَى ٱلنَّاسِ حِجُّ ٱلْبَيْتِ مَنِ ٱسْتَطَاعَ إِلَيْهِ سَبِيلًا ۚ وَمَن كَفَرَ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَنِىٌّ عَنِ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٩٧)
In it are manifest signs [and] Abraham’s Station, and whoever enters it shall be secure. And it is the duty of mankind toward Allah to make pilgrimage to the House—for those who can afford the journey to it—and should anyone renege [on his obligation], Allah is indeed without need of the creatures. (97)

(Commentary)

The two verses are a response to another doubt that the Jews used to raise against the believers regarding abrogation (النسخ) — the divine replacement of one religious ruling with another. This specifically concerned the matter of the qiblah (القبلة) — the direction faced during prayer — and its change from Jerusalem (بيت المقدس) to the Kaaba (الكعبة). It was previously mentioned in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: فَوَلِّ وَجْهَكَ شَطْرَ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرَامِ — "so turn your face towards the Holy Mosque" (2:144), that the change of the qiblah was a significant matter that had deep material and spiritual effects on the lives of the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب) — those to whom a divinely revealed scripture was given — especially the Jews. Additionally, it was contrary to their doctrine regarding abrogation; therefore, disputes and disturbances between them and the Muslims persisted for a long time after the ruling of the qiblah was revealed.

What is understood from the verse—"Indeed, the first House..."—is that they combined in their objection the doubt regarding abrogation with the attribution of the ruling to the creed (ملة) of Abraham. Thus, the gist of the doubt was: How can the Kaaba be the qiblah in the creed of Abraham when Allah had made Jerusalem the qiblah? Is this anything other than claiming an abrogative ruling in the true creed of Abraham, while abrogation is [according to them] impossible and false?

The answer is: The Kaaba was established for worship before any other place, such as Jerusalem. Abraham undoubtedly built it and established it for worship, and in it are clear signs (آيات بينات) indicating this, such as the Station of Abraham (مقام إبراهيم). As for Jerusalem, its builder was Solomon, who came centuries after Abraham.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ أَوَّلَ بَيْتٍ وُضِعَ لِلنَّاسِ لَلَّذِي بِبَكَّةَ — "Indeed the first house to be set up for mankind is the one at Bakkah" until the end of the verse. The "House" (البيت) is well-known. What is meant by "setting up the House for mankind" is setting it up for their worship (عبادتهم); that is, making it a means (ذريعة) through which one seeks access to the worship of Allah, the Glorified, and an aid therein, such that Allah is worshipped within it, by intending it, traveling toward it, and so forth. The evidence for this is what the discourse contains regarding it being "blessed" (مباركاً) and a "guidance for all the worlds" (هدى للعالمين), among other things. This is also suggested by the expression of the Kaaba as "the one at Bakkah" (الذي ببكة), for it alludes to the crowding (ازدحام) of people around it during circumambulation (الطواف), prayer (الصلوة), and other acts of worship and rituals (المناسك). As for it being the first house built on earth for the general benefit of people, there is no linguistic evidence for that in the wording.

What is meant by "Bakkah" (بكة) is the land of the House; it was named Bakkah because of the crowding of people therein. It is often said that Bakkah is Mecca (مكة), and that it is a case of replacing the mim with a ba, as in their sayings: lazim and lazib, or ratim and ratib, and the like. It has also been said that it is a name for the Sanctuary (الحرم), or the Mosque (المسجد), or the place of circumambulation (المطاف).

"Blessed" (المباركة) is the verbal noun of mufa'alah from "blessing" (البركة) — the presence of enduring and abundant divine goodness. Thus, being blessed means the pouring of abundant goodness upon it and placing it within it. Although this encompasses both worldly and otherworldly blessings, its apparent contrast with His saying, "a guidance for all the worlds," suggests that what is meant here is the pouring of worldly blessings. The core of these is the abundance of provisions and the abundance of motivation and incentives to populate it through pilgrimage (الحج) to it, presence at it, and showing it respect and honor. Thus, the meaning leads back to what is contained in the saying of the Exalted regarding the supplication of Abraham: رَّبَّنَا إِنِّي أَسْكَنتُ مِن ذُرِّيَّتِي بِوَادٍ غَيْرِ ذِي زَرْعٍ عِندَ بَيْتِكَ الْمُحَرَّمِ رَبَّنَا لِيُقِيمُوا الصَّلَاةَ فَاجْعَلْ أَفْئِدَةً مِّنَ النَّاسِ تَهْوِي إِلَيْهِمْ وَارْزُقْهُم مِّنَ الثَّمَرَاتِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَشْكُرُونَ — "Our Lord! I have settled some of my descendants in a barren valley near Your Sacred House, our Lord, that they may maintain the prayer. So make the hearts of some people fond of them, and provide them with fruits, so that they may give thanks" (14:37).

Its being a "guidance" (هدى) — the divine leading toward truth and the straight path — is its showing people the felicity of their Hereafter and bringing them to honor, proximity, and nearness through what Allah has established of worship and what He has legislated there of various types of obedience and rituals. It has never ceased, since Abraham built it, to be a destination for seekers and a place of worship for worshippers.

The Quran has indicated that the pilgrimage (الحج) — the prescribed journey to the House of Allah — was first legislated in the time of Abraham (peace be upon him) after he finished building it. The Exalted said: وَعَهِدْنَا إِلَىٰ إِبْرَاهِيمَ وَإِسْمَاعِيلَ أَن طَهِّرَا بَيْتِيَ لِلطَّائِفِينَ وَالْعَاكِفِينَ وَالرُّكَّعِ السُّجُودِ — "and We charged Abraham and Ishmael [with this]: ‘Purify My House for those who go around it, [for] those who make it a retreat and [for] those who bow and prostrate’" (2:125). And He said, addressing Abraham: وَأَذِّن فِي النَّاسِ بِالْحَجِّ يَأْتُوكَ رِجَالًا وَعَلَىٰ كُلِّ ضَامِرٍ يَأْتِينَ مِن كُلِّ فَجٍّ عَمِيقٍ — "And proclaim the Hajj to the people: they shall come to you on foot and on every lean camel coming from every distant pass" (22:27). This verse, as you see, indicates that this call and invitation would be met with a general response from people, near and far, from various clans and tribes.

It also indicates that this divine rite (الشعار الإلهي) was established and well-known in the time of Shu'ayb among the people, as Allah narrated from him in his saying to Moses (peace be upon them both): إِنِّي أُرِيدُ أَنْ أُنكِحَكَ إِحْدَى ابْنَتَيَّ هَاتَيْنِ عَلَىٰ أَن تَأْجُرَنِي ثَمَانِيَ حِجَجٍ فَإِنْ أَتْمَمْتَ عَشْرًا فَمِنْ عِندِكَ — "He said, ‘I desire to marry you to one of these two daughters of mine, on condition that you hire yourself to me for eight years (حجج). And if you complete ten, that will be of your own accord’" (28:27). He meant by hijjaj (حجج) years, and this is only because years were counted by the pilgrimage (الحج) due to their recurrence with its recurrence.

Similarly, in the call of Abraham (peace be upon him), there is much that indicates that the House has never ceased to be populated with worship, serving as a sign of guidance (refer to Surah Ibrahim).

The Arabs of the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية) used to venerate it and perform the pilgrimage under the title that it was from the legislation of Abraham. History mentions that other people also used to venerate it. This in itself is a type of guidance, as it involves turning toward Allah, the Glorified, and remembering Him. As for after the appearance of Islam, the matter is even clearer; its mention has filled the easts and wests of the earth. It presents itself to the understandings and hearts of people through its very existence and its mention, and through the worship of Muslims, their obedience, their standing and sitting, their places of sacrifice, and all their other affairs.

Thus, it is a guidance in all levels of guidance, starting from a mental passing thought to total detachment (الانقطاع التام) which is only touched by the purified among the sincere servants of Allah.

Furthermore, it guides the world of Muslims to their worldly felicity, which is the unity of the word, the harmony of the nation (الامة), and the witnessing of their benefits. It also guides the world of others by awakening them and alerting them to the fruits of this unity and the harmony of diverse, scattered forces.

From here, it becomes clear firstly: that it is a guidance to the felicity of this world and the Hereafter, just as it is a guidance in all levels of guidance; thus, the guidance is absolute.

Secondly: that it is a guidance for all the worlds (العالمين), not for a specific world or a particular group like the progeny of Abraham, the Arabs, or the Muslims. This is due to the vastness of its guidance.

His saying, the Exalted: فِيهِ آيَاتٌ بَيِّنَاتٌ مَّقَامُ إِبْرَاهِيمَ — "In it are clear signs: the station of Abraham." Although the signs (الآيات) are described as "clear" (بينات), which provides some specification to the described, they nonetheless remain somewhat ambiguous (الإبهام). The context is one of explaining the merits and glories of the House by which it surpasses others in nobility, and this only suits a clear explanation and a description that is beyond doubt, rather than ambiguity or brevity. This is one of the evidences that His saying: "the station of Abraham, and whoever enters it shall be secure, and pilgrimage to the House is a duty to Allah upon mankind..." until the end of the verse, is an explanation (بيان) for His saying: "clear signs." Thus, the signs are: the station of Abraham, the establishment of security within it, and the obligation of its pilgrimage upon those who are able.

However, it is not as it appears in some commentaries—that these three sentences are a substitute (بدل) or an explanatory apposition (عطف بيان) for the word "signs" (آيات). It is clear that such a construction would require the speech to be interpreted as: "They are the station of Abraham, security for whoever enters it, and its pilgrimage for whoever is able." This would involve reducing the saying "and whoever enters it"—whether it is a creative decree (إنشاء) or a report (إخبار)—to a single noun by assuming the particle an (أن), and reducing the saying "and to Allah upon mankind"—which is a creative sentence—to a declarative one, then conjoining it to the previous sentence and interpreting it as a single noun through that or by assuming an there as well. All of this is something the speech does not support at all.

Rather, these three sentences—namely, His saying: "the station of Abraham," etc.—were brought each for a specific purpose of reporting or legislating a ruling, and then the signs are clarified through them, thus providing the benefit of explanation. It is like saying: "So-and-so is a noble man: he is the son of so-and-so, he hosts guests, and it is obligatory upon us to follow him."

His saying, the Exalted: مَّقَامُ إِبْرَاهِيمَ — "the station of Abraham" is a subject (مبتدأ) for a deleted predicate, and the estimation is: "In it is the station of Abraham." It is the stone upon which are the footprints of Abraham the Friend (peace be upon him). Reports are widespread that the stone is buried in the place called today "the Station of Abraham" (مقام إبراهيم) at the edge of the circumambulation area (المطاف) facing the Multazam (الملتزم). Abu Talib, the uncle of the Prophet, alluded to it in his Lamiyyah poem:

And the footprint of Abraham in the rock is moist, Upon his two feet, barefoot and without sandals.

It may also be understood from His saying: "the station of Abraham" that the House, or within the House, is the place where Abraham stood for the worship of Allah, the Glorified.

It is possible that the estimation of the speech is: "They are the station of Abraham, security, and pilgrimage," and then He placed His saying: "and whoever enters it," and His saying: "and to Allah upon mankind"—which are two sentences containing a creative ruling—in the place of the two predicates. This is among the wonders of the Quranic style, where speech intended for one purpose is used for another, being placed in its stead to transition from one to the other, thereby providing two benefits and preserving both aspects. This is like narrating speech in the place of reporting, such as His saying: كُلٌّ آمَنَ بِاللَّهِ وَمَلَائِكَتِهِ وَكُتُبِهِ وَرُسُلِهِ لَا نُفَرِّقُ بَيْنَ أَحَدٍ مِّن رُّسُلِهِ — "Each [of them] has adequate faith in Allah, His angels, His books and His apostles. [Saying,] ‘We make no distinction between any of His apostles’" (2:285). And as previously mentioned in His saying, the Exalted: أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِي حَاجَّ إِبْرَاهِيمَ فِي رَبِّهِ — "Have you not regarded him who argued with Abraham about his Lord" (2:258), and His saying: أَوْ كَالَّذِي مَرَّ عَلَىٰ قَرْيَةٍ — "Or him who came upon a township" (2:259). We explained the subtle point in that in the commentary of the second verse. It is also like His saying, the Exalted: يَوْمَ لَا يَنفَعُ مَالٌ وَلَا بَنُونَ إِلَّا مَنْ أَتَى اللَّهَ بِقَلْبٍ سَلِيمٍ — "the day when neither wealth nor children will avail, except him who comes to Allah with a sound heart" (26:88-89). And as in His saying, the Exalted: وَلَٰكِنَّ الْبِرَّ مَنْ آمَنَ بِاللَّهِ — "but righteousness is [the quality of] one who has faith in Allah" (2:177), where the "possessor of righteousness" was placed in the stead of "righteousness." And as in His saying, the Exalted: وَمَثَلُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا كَمَثَلِ الَّذِي يَنْعِقُ بِمَا لَا يَسْمَعُ — "The parable of the faithless is that of someone who shouts after that which does not hear [anything]" (2:171); and similar to this are most of the parables mentioned in the Noble Quran.

Accordingly, the weight of His saying: "In it are clear signs: the station of Abraham..." until His saying "...from all the worlds," in its oscillation between creation (الإنشاء) and reporting (الإخبار), is the same as the weight of His saying: وَاذْكُرْ عَبْدَنَا أَيُّوبَ إِذْ نَادَىٰ رَبَّهُ أَنِّي مَسَّنيَ الشَّيْطَانُ بِنُصْبٍ وَعَذَابٍ ارْكُضْ بِرِجْلِكَ هَٰذَا مُغْتَسَلٌ بَارِدٌ وَشَرَابٌ وَوَهَبْنَا لَهُ أَهْلَهُ وَمِثْلَهُم مَّعَهُمْ رَحْمَةً مِّنَّا وَذِكْرَىٰ لِأُولِي الْأَلْبَابِ وَخُذْ بِيَدِكَ ضِغْثًا فَاضْرِب بِّهِ وَلَا تَحْنَثْ إِنَّا وَجَدْنَاهُ صَابِرًا نِّعْمَ الْعَبْدُ إِنَّهُ أَوَّابٌ — "And remember Our servant Job [in the Quran], when he called out to his Lord, ‘The Devil has visited me with hardship and torment.’ ‘Stamp your foot on the ground: here is a cool spring for washing and for drink.’ And We gave him [back] his family and others like them along with them, as a mercy from Us and an admonition for those who possess intellect. ‘Take a bunch of herbs in your hand and strike with it, and do not break your oath.’ Indeed We found him to be patient. What an excellent servant! Indeed he was a penitent [soul]" (38:41-44).

What we have mentioned is different from what some have mentioned regarding the discussion of substitution (البدلية). If it must be a substitute, then it is better to make His saying: "the station of Abraham" a substitute, and to make the two following sentences newly commenced (مستأنفتين) indicating two deleted substitutes. The estimation would be: "In it are clear signs: the station of Abraham, the security of the enterer, and the pilgrimage of the able to the House."

There is no doubt that each of these matters is a clear sign (آية بينة) indicating Allah, the Glorified, by its occurrence and reminding of His station. For a sign (الآية) is nothing but a mark (العلامة) that indicates a thing in some way. And what mark indicating Him, the Exalted, and reminding of His station is greater and clearer in the eyes of the people of the world than the standing place of Abraham, a secure sanctuary (حرم آمن) where whoever enters it is safe, and rituals and acts of worship performed by thousands upon thousands of people, recurring with the recurrence of years, and not being abrogated by the passing of nights and days? As for each sign being a supernatural event (أمراً خارقاً للعادة) that breaks the law of nature, this is not necessary. Neither does the word "sign" in its concept indicate this, nor is its usage in the Quran restricted to it. The Exalted said: مَا نَنسَخْ مِنْ آيَةٍ أَوْ نُنسِهَا — "For any verse (آية) that We abrogate or cause to be forgotten" (2:106), and it certainly includes the abrogated rulings in the Law (الشرع). And the Exalted said: أَتَبْنُونَ بِكُلِّ رِيعٍ آيَةً تَعْبَثُونَ — "Do you build a monument (آية) on every high ground for vain delight?" (26:128), among other verses.

From here, the error becomes clear in the insistence of some commentators to justify the Station being a supernatural sign, and that the security and pilgrimage are mentioned for a purpose other than explaining the signs.

Similarly, the insistence of others that the "clear signs" refer to other matters from the properties of the Kaaba (we have refrained from mentioning them; whoever desires them should refer to some of the lengthy commentaries) is based on the idea that "signs" refers to miracles (المعجزة) and supernatural events, for which there is no evidence, as previously mentioned.

The truth is that His saying: وَمَن دَخَلَهُ كَانَ آمِنًا — "and whoever enters it shall be secure" is brought to explain a legislative ruling (حكم تشريعي), not a creative property (خاصة تكوينية). However, it appears that the sentence is a report (إخبارية) informing of a prior legislation of security, as might be understood from the supplication of Abraham mentioned in Surahs Ibrahim and al-Baqarah. This right was preserved for the House before the Prophetic mission among the Arabs of the Age of Ignorance, connecting back to the time of Abraham (peace be upon him).

As for the claim that the report of security means that great trials and events do not occur nor extend their reach into the Sanctuary, this is refuted by the occurrence of wars, killings, and the breach of security within it, especially what occurred before the revelation of this verse. His saying, the Exalted: أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا أَنَّا جَعَلْنَا حَرَمًا آمِنًا وَيُتَخَطَّفُ النَّاسُ مِنْ حَوْلِهِمْ — "Have they not seen that We have appointed a secure sanctuary, while the people are despoiled all around them?" (24:67), does not indicate more than the establishment and continuity of security in the Sanctuary. This is only because of what people see of the sanctity of this House and the obligation of its veneration, which was established in the law of Abraham (peace be upon him) and ultimately traces back to His, the Glorified's, decree and legislation.

Likewise, what occurred in the supplication of Abraham narrated in His saying, the Exalted: رَبِّ اجْعَلْ هَٰذَا الْبَلَدَ آمِنًا — "My Lord! Make this city secure" (14:35), and His saying: رَبِّ اجْعَلْ هَٰذَا بَلَدًا آمِنًا — "My Lord, make this a city of security" (2:126), where he asked for security for the city of Mecca, and Allah answered him by legislating security and driving people's hearts toward submitting to and accepting that, time after time.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلِلَّهِ عَلَى النَّاسِ حِجُّ الْبَيْتِ مَنِ اسْتَطَاعَ إِلَيْهِ سَبِيلًا — "and pilgrimage to the House is a duty to Allah upon mankind, for those who can afford the journey to it." Al-Hajj (الحج) with a kasrah (and it has been recited with a fathah) means "the intention" (القصد); then its usage became specialized for intending the House in a specific manner explained by the Law. The word "a way" (سبيلاً) is a specification (تمييز) for His saying "can afford" (استطاع).

The verse contains the legislation of the pilgrimage as an endorsement (إمضاء) of what was legislated for Abraham (peace be upon him), as indicated by His saying, the Exalted, narrating what Abraham was addressed with: وَأَذِّن فِي النَّاسِ بِالْحَجِّ — "And proclaim the Hajj to the people" (22:27). From here, it becomes clear that the weight of His saying: "and to Allah upon mankind..." etc., is the same as His saying: "and whoever enters it shall be secure," in that it is a report of a prior legislation, even if it is possible for it to be a creative decree by way of endorsement. However, the former is more apparent from the context, as is not hidden.

His saying, the Exalted: وَمَن كَفَرَ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ غَنِيٌّ عَنِ الْعَالَمِينَ — "And should anyone disbelieve, Allah is indeed independent of all the worlds." Disbelief (الكفر) here is from the disbelief in the branches (الفروع) — the practical laws of religion — similar to the disbelief [in a relative sense] by abandoning prayer and zakat; thus, what is meant by disbelief is abandonment (الترك). The speech is of the type of placing the effect (المسبب) or the trace (الأثر) in the place of the cause (السبب) or the source (المنشأ), just as His saying: "Allah is indeed independent..." is of the type of placing the cause in the place of the effect. The estimation is: "And whoever abandons the pilgrimage, he does not harm Allah in the least, for Allah is independent of all the worlds."

(Narrative Discussion)

From Ibn Shahrashub, narrated from the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of the Exalted: إِنَّ أَوَّلَ بَيْتٍ وُضِعَ لِلنَّاسِ — "Indeed the first house to be set up for mankind..." A man said to him: "Is it the first house?" He replied: لا قد كان قبله بيوت ، ولكنه أول بيت وضع للناس مباركاً ، فيه الهدى والرحمة والبركة . وأول من بناه إبراهيم ، ثم بناه قوم من العرب من جرهم ثم هدم فبنته العمالقة ثم هدم فبناه قريش — "No, there were houses before it, but it was the first house set up for mankind as a blessed place, in which is guidance, mercy, and blessing. The first to build it was Abraham, then a group of Arabs from Jurhum built it, then it was destroyed and the Amalekites built it, then it was destroyed and Quraysh built it."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn al-Mundhir and Ibn Abi Hatim narrated through al-Sha'bi from Ali ibn Abi Talib regarding His saying: "Indeed the first house to be set up for mankind is the one at Bakkah," he said: كانت البيوت قبله ولكنه كان أول بيت وضع لعبادة الله — "There were houses before it, but it was the first house set up for the worship of Allah."

I say: It was also narrated from Ibn Jarir from Matar similarly, and the narrations regarding these meanings are numerous.

In al-Ilal, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): موضع البيت بكة ، والقرية مكة — "The site of the House is Bakkah, and the town is Mecca."

Also in it, from him (peace be upon him): إنما سميت بكة بكة لأن الناس يبكون فيها — "Bakkah was only named Bakkah because people yabukkun (crowd) in it."

I say: He means they crowd together (يزدحمون).

In it, from al-Baqir (peace be upon him): إنما سميت مكة بكة لأنه يبك بها الرجال والنساء ، المرأة تصلي بين يديك ، وعن يمينك ، وعن شمالك ومعك ولا بأس بذلك إنما يكره ذلك في سائر البلدان — "Mecca was only named Bakkah because men and women yabukku (crowd) therein; a woman may pray in front of you, to your right, to your left, and with you, and there is no harm in that, whereas it is disliked in all other lands."

In it, from al-Baqir (peace be upon him), he said: لما أراد الله أن يخلق الأرض أمر الرياح فضربن متن الماء حتى صار موجاً ثم أزبد فصار زبداً واحداً فجمعه في موضع البيت ثم جعله جبلاً من زبد ثم دحى الأرض من تحته وهو قول الله : إن أول بيت وضع للناس للذي ببكة مباركاً ، فأول بقعة خلقت من الأرض الكعبة ثم مدت الأرض منها — "When Allah intended to create the earth, He commanded the winds and they struck the surface of the water until it became waves, then it foamed and became a single foam. He gathered it at the site of the House, then made it a mountain of foam, then He expanded (دحى) the earth from beneath it. This is the saying of Allah: 'Indeed the first house to be set up for mankind is the one at Bakkah, blessed.' Thus, the first spot created from the earth was the Kaaba, then the earth was extended from it."

I say: The reports regarding the expansion of the earth (دحو الأرض) from beneath the Kaaba are numerous. They do not contradict the Book, nor is there any proof that refutes them, other than what the ancient natural philosophers used to claim—that the earth is a simple, eternal element. The falsity of this claim has become clear beyond the need for explanation.

This is the interpretation of what has been mentioned in the narrations that the Kaaba is the first house (meaning spot) on the earth, although the apparent meaning of the verse is what is contained in the first two narrations.

In al-Kafi and the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of the Exalted: "In it are clear signs": he was asked: "What are these clear signs?" He said: مقام إبراهيم حيث قام على الحجر فأثرت فيه قدماه ، والحجر الأسود ، ومنزل إسماعيل — "The Station of Abraham where he stood on the stone and his feet left an impression in it, the Black Stone, and the dwelling of Ishmael."

I say: There are other narrations with this meaning, and perhaps his mention of these matters is by way of enumeration, even if the verse does not explicitly contain some of them.

In the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, from Abd al-Samad, he said: "Abu Ja'far [al-Mansur] sought to buy the houses of the people of Mecca to expand the Mosque, but they refused. He offered them more, but they still declined, and he became distressed by this. He came to Abu Abdillah [al-Sadiq] (peace be upon him) and said to him: 'I asked these people for some of their dwellings and courtyards to expand the Mosque, and they have refused, which has caused me great distress.' Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) said: 'Do not be distressed, for your argument against them is clear.' He asked: 'With what shall I argue against them?' He replied: 'With the Book of Allah.' He asked: 'In which place?' He said: 'The saying of Allah: "Indeed the first house to be set up for mankind is the one at Bakkah." Allah has informed you that the first house set up for mankind is the one at Bakkah. If they settled there before the House, then they have their courtyards; but if the House was ancient among them, then it has its courtyard.' So Abu Ja'far called them and argued against them with this, and they said to him: 'Do as you wish.'"

In it, from al-Hasan ibn Ali ibn al-Nu'man, he said: "When al-Mahdi [the Abbasid] was building in the Sacred Mosque, a house remained in the square of the Mosque. He sought it from its owners, but they refused. He asked the jurists (الفقهاء) about this, and each of them said to him: 'It is not proper to include anything in the Sacred Mosque by force (غصباً).' Ali ibn Yaqtin said to him: 'O Commander of the Faithful, I shall write to Musa ibn Ja'far (peace be upon them both) to inform you of the way in this matter.' So he wrote to the governor of Medina to ask Musa ibn Ja'far (peace be upon them both) about a house we intended to include in the Sacred Mosque, but its owner refused; what is the way out of this?

This was mentioned to Abu al-Hasan (peace be upon him), and he said: 'Is an answer necessary for this?' He was told: 'The matter is necessary.' So he said: 'Write: In the Name of Allah, the All-Beneficent, the Most Merciful. If the Kaaba is the one that descended upon the people, then the people have more right to its courtyard; but if the people are the ones who descended upon the courtyard of the Kaaba, then the Kaaba has more right to its courtyard.'

When the letter reached al-Mahdi, he took the letter and kissed it, then ordered the demolition of the house. The owners of the house came to Abu al-Hasan (peace be upon him) and asked him to write a letter to al-Mahdi regarding the price of their house. So he wrote to him to ardakh (give generously) to them, and he satisfied them."

I say: The two narrations contain a subtle deduction. It seems that Abu Ja'far al-Mansur was the one who began the expansion of the Sacred Mosque, and then the matter was completed for al-Mahdi.

In al-Kafi, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of the Exalted: "and to Allah upon mankind is the pilgrimage to the House..." etc., he said: يعني به الحج والعمرة جميعاً لأنهما مفروضان — "He means by it both the Hajj and the Umrah, for both are obligatory."

I say: Al-Ayyashi narrated it in his Tafsir, and the pilgrimage (الحج) was interpreted therein according to its linguistic meaning, which is "the intention."

In the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "And should anyone disbelieve," he said: ترك — "abandoned."

I say: Al-Shaykh [al-Tusi] narrated it in al-Tahdhib. You have already learned that disbelief (الكفر) has levels like faith (الإيمان), and that what is meant here is disbelief in the branches (الفروع).

In al-Kafi, from Ali ibn Ja'far from his brother Musa (peace be upon him) in a tradition, he said: "I said: 'So whoever among us does not perform the pilgrimage, has he disbelieved?' He said: لا ، ولكن من قال : ليس هذا هكذا فقد كفر — 'No, but whoever says: "This is not so," has disbelieved.'"

I say: The narrations regarding these meanings are numerous. Disbelief in the narration means rejection (الرد), and the verse allows for it. Thus, disbelief therein is according to its linguistic meaning, which is "covering the truth," and its instances are determined according to the context.

(Historical Discussion)

It is among the mass-transmitted (المتواتر) and certain facts that the one who built the Kaaba was Abraham the Friend (peace be upon him). Those residing around it at that time were his son Ishmael and Jurhum from the tribes of Yemen. It is a nearly square building, and its four corners face the four directions so that the winds break against them and do not harm it, no matter how strong they are.

The Kaaba remained according to the building of Abraham until the Amalekites (العمالقة) renewed it, then the tribe of Jurhum (or vice versa), as mentioned in the narration from the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him).

Then, when the matter of the Kaaba fell to Qusay ibn Kilab, one of the ancestors of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) (second century before the Hijrah), he demolished it and rebuilt it, making its construction firm. He roofed it with doum wood and palm trunks and built beside it the Dar al-Nadwah (دار الندوة) — the assembly house. In this house was his government and his council with his companions. Then he divided the sides of the Kaaba among the clans of Quraysh, so they built their houses on the circumambulation area around the Kaaba and opened the doors of their houses toward it.

Five years before the Prophetic mission, a flood demolished the Kaaba, so the clans divided the work of rebuilding it. The one who built it was Yaqum the Roman, assisted by an Egyptian carpenter. When they reached the point of placing the Black Stone, they disputed among themselves as to which of them would have the honor of placing it. They decided to seek the judgment of Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), who was thirty-five years old at the time, because they knew of his abundant intellect and sound judgment. He asked for a cloak and placed the stone upon it, then commanded the tribes to hold its edges and lift it. When it reached its place in the building at the Eastern Corner, he took it and placed it with his own hand in its position.

The expenses had burdened them, so they restricted its building to what it is now. Some of its area remained outside the building on the side of the Hijr (حجر إسماعيل) because they made the building smaller.

The building remained in this state until Abdullah ibn al-Zubayr took control of the Hijaz during the reign of Yazid ibn Mu'awiyah. Al-Husayn, the commander of Yazid, fought him in Mecca and struck the Kaaba with catapults, so it was demolished and its covering and some of its wood were burned. Then the siege was lifted due to the death of Yazid. Ibn al-Zubayr decided to demolish the Kaaba and rebuild it. He brought pure gypsum for it from Yemen and built it with it, included the Hijr into the House, attached the door to the ground, and made another door opposite it so that people could enter from one door and exit from the other. He made the height of the House twenty-seven cubits. When he finished its building, he perfumed it with musk and ambergris inside and out and covered it with silk brocade. His completion of its building was on the 17th of Rajab, 64 AH.

Then, when Abd al-Malik ibn Marwan assumed the Caliphate, he sent his commander al-Hajjaj ibn Yusuf, who fought Ibn al-Zubayr until he defeated and killed him. He entered the House and informed Abd al-Malik of what Ibn al-Zubayr had introduced in the Kaaba. He commanded him to return it to its original form. So al-Hajjaj demolished six cubits and a span from its northern side and built that wall on the foundation of Quraysh. He raised the eastern door and blocked the western one, then filled its floor with the stones that remained from it.

When the Ottoman Sultan Suleiman assumed power in the year 960 AH, he changed its roof. When Sultan Ahmed the Ottoman assumed power in the year 1021 AH, he carried out renovations. When the great flood occurred in the year 1039 AH, it demolished some of its northern, eastern, and western walls. So Sultan Murad IV of the House of Osman ordered its renovation. It has remained in that state until today, which is the year 1375 Lunar Hijri and 1338 Solar Hijri.

The Shape of the Kaaba: The shape of the Kaaba is nearly square, and it is built with hard blue stones. Its height reaches sixteen meters. In the time of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), it was much lower than that, as understood from the narration of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) lifting Ali (عليه السلام) onto his shoulders on the day of the Conquest to take the idols that were atop the Kaaba and break them.

The length of the side containing the waterspout (الميزاب) and the one opposite it is ten meters and ten centimeters. The length of the side containing the door and the one opposite it is twelve meters. The door is at a height of two meters from the ground. In the corner to the left of the door for one entering is the Black Stone, at a height of one and a half meters from the floor of the circumambulation area. The Black Stone is a heavy, irregularly shaped oval stone, blackish-red in color, with red spots and yellow streaks, which are traces of the soldering of the pieces that had broken from it. Its diameter is about thirty centimeters.

The corners of the Kaaba have been called Arkan (الأركان) since ancient times. The northern one is called the Iraqi Corner, the western the Syrian, the southern the Yemeni, and the eastern—which contains the Black Stone—the Black Corner. The distance between the door and the corner of the Stone is called the Multazam (الملتزم) because the circumambulator clings to it in his supplication and plea. As for the waterspout on the northern wall, called the Waterspout of Mercy (ميزاب الرحمة), it was introduced by al-Hajjaj ibn Yusuf, then Sultan Suleiman changed it in 954 AH to a silver waterspout, then Sultan Ahmed replaced it in 1021 AH with another of silver engraved with blue enamel interspersed with gold engravings. Then Sultan Abd al-Majid of the House of Osman sent a golden waterspout in 1273 AH, which was installed in its place and is what exists now.

Opposite the waterspout is a curved wall called the Hatim (الحطيم), which is an arc of construction whose two ends point toward the northern and western corners of the House, being about two meters and three centimeters away from them. Its height is one meter and its thickness is one and a half meters; it is lined with engraved marble. The distance from the center of this arc on the inside to the center of the side of the Kaaba is eight meters and forty-four centimeters.

The space between the Hatim and the wall of the House is what is called the Hijr Isma'il (حجر إسماعيل). About three meters of it were included in the Kaaba in the building of Abraham, and the rest was a pen for the sheep of Hagar and her son. It is said that Hagar and Ishmael are buried in the Hijr.

As for the details of the changes and renovations that occurred inside the House, and the traditions and honors pertaining to the House, we do not find it necessary to address them.

The Covering of the Kaaba: It was previously mentioned in what we narrated of traditions in Surah al-Baqarah regarding the story of Hagar and Ishmael and their settling in the land of Mecca, that Hagar hung her cloak on the door of the Kaaba after its building was completed.

As for the covering (كسوة) of the House itself, it is said that the first to cover it was Tubba' Abu Bakr As'ad, who covered it with striped cloths embroidered with silver threads. His successors followed him, and then people began covering it with various cloaks, placing them one over another. Whenever a cloth wore out, another was placed over it until the time of Qusay. Qusay imposed a tax (رفادة) on the Arabs for its covering annually, and this continued among his descendants. Abu Rabi'ah ibn al-Mughirah used to cover it one year and the clans of Quraysh the next.

The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) covered it with Yemeni cloths, and it remained so until the Abbasid Caliph al-Mahdi performed the pilgrimage. The custodians of the Kaaba complained to him about the accumulation of coverings on the roof of the Kaaba, mentioning that they feared it might collapse. So he ordered the removal of those coverings and their replacement with a single covering every year. This practice has continued until today. The Kaaba also has an internal covering; the first to cover it from the inside was the mother of al-Abbas ibn Abd al-Muttalib due to a vow she made regarding her son al-Abbas.

The Status of the Kaaba: The Kaaba was sacred and venerated among various nations. The Hindus used to venerate it, saying that the soul of "Shiva"—who is the third person of their trinity—dwelt in the Black Stone when he visited the land of Hijaz with his wife.

The Sabians among the Persians and Chaldeans considered it one of the seven venerated houses; it was sometimes said to be the House of Saturn due to its antiquity and long endurance.

The Persians also respected the Kaaba, claiming that the soul of Hormizd dwelt in it, and they would sometimes perform pilgrimage to it as visitors.

The Jews used to venerate it and worship Allah therein according to the religion of Abraham. There were images and statues in it, including the statues of Abraham and Ishmael with divining arrows in their hands, and images of the Virgin and Christ. This also testifies to the Christians' veneration of it, like the Jews.

The Arabs also venerated it with all veneration and considered it the House of Allah, the Exalted. They would perform pilgrimage to it from every direction, considering the House to be the building of Abraham and the pilgrimage to be from his religion that remained among them through inheritance.

The Guardianship of the Kaaba: The guardianship (الولاية) over the Kaaba belonged to Ishmael, then to his children after him, until Jurhum overcame them and seized its guardianship. Then the Amalekites—a group from the Banu Karkar—possessed it after wars that occurred between them. They used to reside in the lower part of Mecca, while Jurhum resided in the upper part of Mecca, where their kings were.

Then the turn came for Jurhum over the Amalekites, so the guardianship returned to them, and they held it for about three hundred years. They increased the building of the House and its height beyond what it was in the building of Abraham.

Then, when the children of Ishmael grew and multiplied and became possessors of strength and defense, and the land became narrow for them, they fought Jurhum, defeated them, and expelled them from Mecca. The leader of the Ishmaelites at that time was Amr ibn Luhay, the chief of Khuza'ah. He took control of Mecca and the affairs of the House. He was the one who placed idols on the Kaaba and called people to worship them. The first idol he placed on it was "Hubal," which he brought with him from Syria to Mecca and placed upon it. Then he followed it with others until they multiplied and their worship became widespread among the Arabs, and the Hanifiyyah (الحنيفية) — the primordial monotheism of Abraham — was abandoned.

Regarding this, Shuhnah ibn Khalaf al-Jurhumi said, addressing Amr ibn Luhay:

O Amr, you have introduced various gods In Mecca, around the House, as idols. The House had only one Lord forever, But you have made for Him lords among the people. You shall surely know that Allah, in due time, Will choose guardians for the House other than you.

The guardianship remained in Khuza'ah until the time of Hulayl al-Khuza'i. Hulayl bequeathed it after him to his daughter, who was married to Qusay ibn Kilab. He gave the opening and closing of the door to a man from Khuza'ah named Abu Ghubshan al-Khuza'i. Abu Ghubshan sold it to Qusay ibn Kilab for a camel and a skin of wine. Regarding this, the famous proverb is struck: "More losing than the deal of Abu Ghubshan."

Thus, the guardianship transferred to Quraysh, and Qusay renewed the building of the House as we previously mentioned. The matter remained so until the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) conquered Mecca. He entered the Kaaba and ordered the images and statues to be erased, and the idols to be demolished and broken. The Station of Abraham—which is the stone with the footprints of Abraham—was placed in a basin near the Kaaba, then it was buried in its place where it is known today, which is a dome standing on four pillars that circumambulators intend for prayer.

The reports concerning the Kaaba and the religious sites related to it are many and lengthy. We have restricted ourselves to what is necessary for the researcher reflecting upon the verses of the pilgrimage and the Kaaba.

Among the properties of this House, which Allah has blessed and made a guidance, is that no group among the sects of Islam has differed regarding its status.

Ayahs

قُلْ يَـٰٓأَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لِمَ تَكْفُرُونَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ وَٱللَّهُ شَهِيدٌ عَلَىٰ مَا تَعْمَلُونَ (٩٨)
Say, ‘O People of the Book! Why do you deny the signs of Allah, while Allah is witness to what you do?’ (98)
قُلْ يَـٰٓأَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لِمَ تَصُدُّونَ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ مَنْ ءَامَنَ تَبْغُونَهَا عِوَجًا وَأَنتُمْ شُهَدَآءُ ۗ وَمَا ٱللَّهُ بِغَـٰفِلٍ عَمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ (٩٩)
Say, ‘O People of the Book! why do you bar the faithful from the way of Allah, seeking to make it crooked, while you are witnesses [to its truthfulness]? And Allah is not oblivious of what you do.’ (99)
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِن تُطِيعُوا۟ فَرِيقًا مِّنَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ يَرُدُّوكُم بَعْدَ إِيمَـٰنِكُمْ كَـٰفِرِينَ (١٠٠)
O you who have faith, if you obey a part of those who were given the Book, they will turn you back, after your faith, into faithless ones. (100)
وَكَيْفَ تَكْفُرُونَ وَأَنتُمْ تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْكُمْ ءَايَـٰتُ ٱللَّهِ وَفِيكُمْ رَسُولُهُۥ ۗ وَمَن يَعْتَصِم بِٱللَّهِ فَقَدْ هُدِىَ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ (١٠١)
And how would you be faithless while the signs of Allah are recited to you and His Apostle is in your midst? Whoever takes recourse in Allah is certainly guided to a straight path. (101)

(Commentary)

As you can see from the continuity of the context (سياق), the verses indicate that the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب) — either a group among them, namely the Jews, or a specific faction of the Jews — used to disbelieve in the signs of Allah and bar the believers from the way of Allah by presenting it to them as crooked (عوجاً) rather than straight, and by portraying the crooked, deviant path of misguidance as the way of Allah.

This was achieved by casting doubts (شبهات) upon the believers, through which they would perceive the truth as falsehood and the falsehood to which they were being invited as truth. The preceding verses indicate the matters in which they had deviated, such as their denial of the lawfulness of all food prior to the Torah, and their denial of the abrogation (نسخ) of facing Jerusalem as the direction of prayer (القبلة). Thus, these verses are complementary to the previous ones that addressed the lawfulness of food before the Torah and the fact that the Kaaba was the first house established for mankind. They contain a denunciation and a rebuke of the Jews for casting doubts and creating discord (تفتين) among the believers regarding their religion. They also serve as a warning to the believers against obeying them in what they invite them toward, lest they disbelieve in the religion, and an encouragement and exhortation for them to hold fast (يعتصموا) to Allah so that they may be guided to the path of faith and their guidance may endure.

It has been narrated from Zayd ibn Aslam, as recorded by al-Suyuti in Lubab al-Nuqul according to what has been said: "Shash ibn Qays — who was a Jew — passed by a group from the tribes of al-Aws and al-Khazraj who were conversing. He was enraged by the harmony (تآلف) he saw among them after their former enmity. He ordered a Jewish youth with him to sit among them and remind them of the Day of Bu'ath. The youth did so, and they began to dispute and boast until two men jumped up: Aws ibn Qarzi from al-Aws and Jabbar ibn Sakhr from al-Khazraj. They exchanged harsh words, both factions became angry, and they rushed to fight. This reached the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم, who came and exhorted them and reconciled them. They listened and obeyed. Then Allah revealed concerning Aws and Jabbar: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِن تُطِيعُوا فَرِيقًا مِّنَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتَابَ يَرُدُّوكُم بَعْدَ إِيمَانِكُمْ كَافِرِينَ (O you who have faith! If you obey a part of those who were given the Book, they will turn you back, after your faith, into faithless ones. [3:100]), and concerning Shash ibn Qays: قُلْ يَا أَهْلَ الْكِتابِ لِمَ تَصُدُّونَ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ (Say, ‘O People of the Book! Why do you bar the faithful from the way of Allah...’ [3:99])."

This report is a summary extracted from what was narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from Zayd ibn Aslam in detail, and something similar has been narrated from Ibn Abbas and others.
