In any case, the verses are more closely applicable to what we have mentioned than to the specific occasion mentioned in the narration, as is apparent. Furthermore, the verses mention disbelief (الكفر), faith (الإيمان), the testimony (شهادة) of the Jews, and the recitation of the signs of Allah to the believers, and the like; all of this is more suited to what we have stated. This is supported by His saying, the Exalted: وَدَّ كَثِيرٌ مِّنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتَابِ لَوْ يَرُدُّونَكُم مِّن بَعْدِ إِيمَانِكُمْ كُفَّارًا حَسَدًا مِّنْ عِندِ أَنفُسِهِم (Many of the People of the Book desire to turn you back into faithless ones after your faith, out of envy from themselves... [2:109]). Thus, the truth is as we have stated: that these verses are a completion of the preceding ones.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ يَا أَهْلَ الْكِتَابِ لِمَ تَكْفُرُونَ بِآيَاتِ اللَّهِ (Say, ‘O People of the Book! Why do you deny the signs of Allah...’ [3:98]). What is meant by the signs (الآيات), by the evidence of the unity of the context (سياق), is the lawfulness of food before the revelation of the Torah, and the fact that the direction of prayer (القبلة) in Islam is the Kaaba.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ يَا أَهْلَ الْكِتَابِ لِمَ تَصُدُّونَ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ (Say, ‘O People of the Book! Why do you bar the faithful from the way of Allah...’ [3:99]) until His saying: عِوَجًا (crooked). Barring (الصد) means turning away (الصرف). His saying: تَبْغُونَهَا means you seek the way. His saying: عِوَجًا (crookedness) refers to that which is bent or distorted (المحرف). The meaning is seeking the way of Allah as something crooked, devoid of straightness.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَنتُمْ شُهَدَاءُ (while you are witnesses). That is, you know that food was lawful before the revelation of the Torah and that one of the characteristics of prophethood is the changing of the direction of prayer (القبلة) to the Kaaba. In considering them witnesses (شهداء) in this verse, He has paralleled what was in the previous verse where He, the Exalted, considered Himself a witness over their actions and their disbelief. In this, there is a subtle point (لطف) that is not hidden: they are witnesses to the truth of what they deny, and Allah is a witness to their denial and disbelief. Since He attributed testimony to them in this verse, He replaced the conclusion of the previous verse—namely His saying: وَاللَّهُ شَهِيدٌ عَلَىٰ مَا تَعْمَلُونَ (and Allah is witness to what you do [3:98])—with His saying at the end of this verse: وَمَا اللَّهُ بِغَافِلٍ عَمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ (and Allah is not oblivious of what you do [3:99]). This conveys that they are witnesses to the truth, and Allah, the Glorified, is a witness over all.

His saying, the Exalted: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا (O you who have faith!) until His saying: وَفِيكُمْ رَسُولُهُ (and His Apostle is among you [3:101]). The "group" (الفريق) refers, as previously mentioned, to the Jews or a faction among them. His saying, the Exalted: وَأَنتُمْ تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْكُمْ آيَاتُ اللَّهِ وَفِيكُمْ رَسُولُهُ (while the signs of Allah are recited to you and His Apostle is among you) means that it is possible for you to hold fast to the truth that becomes manifest to you by listening to the signs of Allah and contemplating (التدبر) them, and then referring what remains obscure to you—due to a lack of contemplation—back to Him, or by referring initially to His Messenger who is among you, not veiled from you nor distant. It means seeking the manifestation of truth by referring to him and then nullifying the doubts cast by the Jews, and clinging to the signs of Allah and His Messenger. Holding fast to these two is holding fast (الاعتصام) to Allah; and whoever holds fast to Allah has indeed been guided to a straight path.

Thus, the meaning of disbelief in His saying: وَكَيْفَ تَكْفُرُونَ (And how would you disbelieve...) is disbelief after faith. His saying: وَأَنتُمْ تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْكُمْ (while [the signs of Allah] are recited to you) is an allusion (كناية) to the possibility of holding fast (الاعتصام) in order to avoid disbelieving in the signs of Allah and His Messenger. His saying: وَمَن يَعْتَصِم بِاللَّهِ (And whoever holds fast to Allah...) serves as a universal major premise (الكبرى الكلية) for that. The meaning of guidance to a straight path (الصراط المستقيم) — the direct and unerring way to Allah — is the attainment of a firm faith, which is the path that neither differs nor fails in its purpose; it unites those who walk upon its level surface and does not allow them to exit the way and go astray.

The use of the past tense in His saying: فَقَدْ هُدِيَ (he has certainly been guided), while omitting the agent (الفاعل), indicates the certainty of the action's occurrence without focusing on the specific agent.

It becomes clear from the verse that the Book and the Sunnah (السنة) — the recorded words, actions, and silent approvals of the Prophet — are sufficient in indicating every truth in which one might potentially go astray.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ حَقَّ تُقَاتِهِۦ وَلَا تَمُوتُنَّ إِلَّا وَأَنتُم مُّسْلِمُونَ (١٠٢)
O you who have faith! Be wary of Allah with the wariness due to Him and do not die except as Muslims. (102)
وَٱعْتَصِمُوا۟ بِحَبْلِ ٱللَّهِ جَمِيعًا وَلَا تَفَرَّقُوا۟ ۚ وَٱذْكُرُوا۟ نِعْمَتَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ إِذْ كُنتُمْ أَعْدَآءً فَأَلَّفَ بَيْنَ قُلُوبِكُمْ فَأَصْبَحْتُم بِنِعْمَتِهِۦٓ إِخْوَٰنًا وَكُنتُمْ عَلَىٰ شَفَا حُفْرَةٍ مِّنَ ٱلنَّارِ فَأَنقَذَكُم مِّنْهَا ۗ كَذَٰلِكَ يُبَيِّنُ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَهْتَدُونَ (١٠٣)
Hold fast, all together, to Allah’s cord, and do not be divided [into sects]. Remember Allah’s blessing upon you when you were enemies, then He brought your hearts together, so you became brothers with His blessing. And you were on the brink of a pit of Fire, whereat He saved you from it. Thus does Allah clarify His signs for you so that you may be guided. (103)
وَلْتَكُن مِّنكُمْ أُمَّةٌ يَدْعُونَ إِلَى ٱلْخَيْرِ وَيَأْمُرُونَ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ وَيَنْهَوْنَ عَنِ ٱلْمُنكَرِ ۚ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُفْلِحُونَ (١٠٤)
There has to be a nation among you summoning to the good, bidding what is right, and forbidding what is wrong. It is they who are the felicitous. (104)
وَلَا تَكُونُوا۟ كَٱلَّذِينَ تَفَرَّقُوا۟ وَٱخْتَلَفُوا۟ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا جَآءَهُمُ ٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتُ ۚ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ عَظِيمٌ (١٠٥)
Do not be like those who became divided [into sects] and differed after manifest signs had come to them. For such there will be a great punishment (105)
يَوْمَ تَبْيَضُّ وُجُوهٌ وَتَسْوَدُّ وُجُوهٌ ۚ فَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ٱسْوَدَّتْ وُجُوهُهُمْ أَكَفَرْتُم بَعْدَ إِيمَـٰنِكُمْ فَذُوقُوا۟ ٱلْعَذَابَ بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَكْفُرُونَ (١٠٦)
on the day when [some] faces will turn white and [some] faces will turn black. As for those whose faces turn black [it will be said to them], ‘Did you disbelieve after your faith? So taste the punishment because of what you used to disbelieve.’ (106)
وَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ٱبْيَضَّتْ وُجُوهُهُمْ فَفِى رَحْمَةِ ٱللَّهِ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ (١٠٧)
But as for those whose faces become white, they shall dwell in Allah’s mercy, and they will remain in it [forever]. (107)
تِلْكَ ءَايَـٰتُ ٱللَّهِ نَتْلُوهَا عَلَيْكَ بِٱلْحَقِّ ۗ وَمَا ٱللَّهُ يُرِيدُ ظُلْمًا لِّلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (١٠٨)
These are the signs of Allah, which We recite to you in truth, and Allah does not desire any wrong for the creatures. (108)
وَلِلَّهِ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ وَإِلَى ٱللَّهِ تُرْجَعُ ٱلْأُمُورُ (١٠٩)
To Allah belongs whatever there is in the heavens and the earth, and to Allah all matters are returned. (109)
كُنتُمْ خَيْرَ أُمَّةٍ أُخْرِجَتْ لِلنَّاسِ تَأْمُرُونَ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ وَتَنْهَوْنَ عَنِ ٱلْمُنكَرِ وَتُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ ۗ وَلَوْ ءَامَنَ أَهْلُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لَكَانَ خَيْرًا لَّهُم ۚ مِّنْهُمُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ وَأَكْثَرُهُمُ ٱلْفَـٰسِقُونَ (١١٠)
You are the best nation [ever] brought forth for mankind: you bid what is right and forbid what is wrong, and have faith in Allah. And if the People of the Book had believed, it would have been better for them. Among them [some] are faithful, but most of them are transgressors. (110)

(Commentary)

The verses are a continuation of the address to the believers (المؤمنين), warning them against the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب) and their attempts to sow discord. They emphasize that the believers possess that which enables them to hold fast (يعتصموا) so they do not stray or fall into the pits of destruction. Furthermore, this discourse follows the preceding one without changing the prior context; that is, the discussion regarding the state of the People of the Book has not yet concluded. The evidence for this is the saying of the Exalted after these verses: لن يضروكم إلا أذى — "They will never harm you except for an annoyance" [QARAI TRANSLATION NEEDED: Surah Ali 'Imran, Ayah 111].

His saying, the Exalted: يا أيها الذين آمنوا اتقوا الله حق تقاته — "O you who have faith! Be wary of Allah with the wariness due to Him" [QARAI TRANSLATION NEEDED: Surah Ali 'Imran, Ayah 102]. It has been mentioned previously that godwariness (التقوى) — the innate caution and protection against harm — when directed toward Allah, the Glorified, means avoiding and guarding oneself against His punishment, as the Exalted said: فَاتَّقُوا النَّارَ الَّتِي وَقُودُهَا النَّاسُ وَالْحِجَارَةُ — "beware the Fire whose fuel is humans and stones" (2:24). This is only realized by acting according to what He wills and approves; thus, it is the fulfillment of His commands, the avoidance of His prohibitions, gratitude (الشكر) for His blessings, and patience (الصبر) during His trials. Both of the latter return to gratitude in the sense of putting a thing in its proper place. In short, godwariness (تقوى) toward Allah, the Glorified, is that He be obeyed and not disobeyed, and that one submits to Him in what He grants or withholds.

However, if godwariness is taken as the "due wariness" (حق التقوى) which is not tainted by any falsehood of its own kind, it becomes pure servitude (العبودية) — the absolute submission of the soul to its Creator — untainted by egoism (إنية) or heedlessness (غفلة). It is obedience without disobedience, gratitude without ingratitude (كفر), and remembrance (الذكر) without forgetfulness. This is true Islam (الإسلام الحق) — the total submission to the Divine Will — meaning the highest of its degrees. Accordingly, the meaning of His saying: ولا تموتن إلا وأنتم مسلمون — "and do not die except as Muslims" [QARAI TRANSLATION NEEDED: Surah Ali 'Imran, Ayah 102] refers to something like our saying: "Continue in this state (of due wariness) until you die."

This meaning differs from what is understood from the saying of the Exalted: فَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ مَا اسْتَطَعْتُمْ — "So be wary of Allah as much as you can" (64:16). This verse means not to abandon godwariness in anything you are capable of, except that capability (الاستطاعة) varies according to the strengths, understandings, and resolves of individuals. There is no doubt that "due wariness" (حق التقوى) in the sense we mentioned is not within the capacity of many people. In this internal journey (المسير الباطني), there are stations, landmarks, and perils that none comprehend except the learned (العالمون), and subtleties and nuances that none notice except the sincere (المخلصون). Many a stage of godwariness is such that the common understanding (الفهم العامي) would not believe it to be within the soul's capability, thus judging it impossible, even though the people of true godwariness have left it behind them and turned their resolves toward what is even more arduous and difficult.

Thus, His saying: فاتقوا الله ما استطعتم — "So be wary of Allah as much as you can" — is a discourse received by different understandings in different ways, according to how each understanding applies it to what its possessor is capable of. This then becomes a means to understand from this verse—meaning His saying: اتقوا الله حق تقاته ولا تموتن إلا وأنتم مسلمون — that the intent is for them to set out upon the path of "due wariness" and aim to attain this station and stand firmly within it. This is similar to being guided to the straight path (الصراط المستقيم) — the direct and unerring way to Allah — which none can fully master except the elite (الأوحديون), yet all people are called toward it. Thus, the sum of the two verses—"Be wary of Allah with the wariness due to Him" and "Be wary of Allah as much as you can"—is that all people are exhorted and called to "due wariness," then commanded to journey toward this goal as much as they are able and capable. The result is that everyone falls upon the path of godwariness, though they are at different stages and degrees according to their understanding and resolve, and according to the success (توفيق), support (تأييد), and guidance (تسديد) bestowed upon them by Allah. This is what reflection (التدبر) upon the meaning of the two verses yields.

From this, it becomes clear that the two verses do not differ in substance, nor is the first verse—"Be wary of Allah with the wariness due to Him"—intended to mean exactly what is intended by "Be wary of Allah as much as you can." Rather, the first verse calls toward the destination, while the second explains the manner of the journey (السلوك).

His saying, the Exalted: ولا تموتن إلا وأنتم مسلمون — "and do not die except as Muslims." Death is among the ontological (تكوينية) matters that lie outside the domain of our choice. Therefore, commands and prohibitions related to it and its like are ontological commands and prohibitions, such as His saying: فَقَالَ لَهُمُ اللَّهُ مُوتُوا — "so Allah said to them, ‘Die!’" (2:243), and His saying: أَن يَقُولَ لَهُ كُن فَيَكُونُ — "that He says to it, ‘Be!’ and it is" (36:82). However, a non-voluntary matter is sometimes joined to a voluntary matter such that they form a composite attributed to choice. In such cases, conventional (اعتباري) commands and prohibitions become applicable, such as the saying of the Exalted: فَلَا تَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْمُمْتَرِينَ — "so do not be among the skeptics" (2:147), and His saying: وَلَا تَكُن مَّعَ الْكَافِرِينَ — "and do not be with the faithless" (11:42), and His saying: وَكُونُوا مَعَ الصَّادِقِينَ — "and be with the truthful" (9:119), and others. The essence of "being" (الكون) is an ontological necessity for man in which his choice has no effect, but through its connection to a voluntary matter—such as skepticism, disbelief, or commitment to truthfulness—it is considered a voluntary matter, and thus commanded or prohibited with a masterly (مولوي) command or prohibition.

In short, the prohibition against dying except in a state of Islam is only because it is considered voluntary, and it ultimately serves as a metonymy (كناية) for the necessity of adhering to Islam in all states so that death occurs in one of these states, and thus the deceased dies in the state of Islam.

His saying, the Exalted: واعتصموا بحبل الله جميعاً ولا تفرقوا — "Hold fast, all together, to Allah’s cord, and do not be divided" [QARAI TRANSLATION NEEDED: Surah Ali 'Imran, Ayah 103]. The Glorified mentioned previously in His saying: وكيف تكفرون وأنتم تتلی عليكم آيات الله وفيكم رسوله ومن يعتصم بالله — "And how would you disbelieve while Allah’s signs are recited to you and His Apostle is among you? Whoever holds fast to Allah..." [QARAI TRANSLATION NEEDED: Surah Ali 'Imran, Ayah 101] that holding fast to the signs of Allah and to His Messenger (the Book and the Sunnah) is a holding fast to Allah, whereby the one holding fast is safe and guaranteed guidance (الهدى). Holding fast to the hem of the Messenger is holding fast to the hem of the Book, for the Book is what commands that in verses such as: وَمَا آتَاكُمُ الرَّسُولُ فَخُذُوهُ وَمَا نَهَاكُمْ عَنْهُ فَانتَهُوا — "Take whatever the Apostle gives you, and relinquish whatever he forbids you" (59:7).

In this verse, the "holding fast" (الاعتصام) exhorted in the previous verse has been replaced with "holding fast to the cord of Allah" (بحبل الله). This results in the "cord of Allah" being the Book revealed from Allah, which connects the servant to the Lord and links the heavens to the earth. If you wish, you could say the cord of Allah is the Quran and the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم), for you have seen that the ultimate reality of all is one.

Although the Quran only calls to "due wariness" and firm Islam, the purpose of this verse differs from the purpose of the previous verse commanding "due wariness" and dying upon Islam. The previous verse addresses the ruling of the individual (الفرد), while this verse addresses the ruling of the collective society (الجماعة المجتمعة). The evidence for this is His saying: "all together" (جميعاً) and His saying: "and do not be divided" (ولا تفرقوا). Thus, the verses command the Islamic society to hold fast to the Book and the Sunnah just as they command the individual to do so.

His saying, the Exalted: واذكروا نعمة الله عليكم إذ كنتم أعداء فألف بين قلوبكم فأصبحتم بنعمته إخواناً — "and remember Allah’s blessing upon you when you were enemies, then He brought your hearts together, so you became brothers by His blessing" [QARAI TRANSLATION NEEDED: Surah Ali 'Imran, Ayah 103]. The phrase "when you were" (إذ كنتم) is an explanation of the mentioned blessing (النعمة), and His saying: وكنتم على شفا حفرة من النار فأنقذكم منها — "and you were on the brink of a pit of Fire, whereat He saved you from it" [QARAI TRANSLATION NEEDED: Surah Ali 'Imran, Ayah 103] is coordinated with it.

The command to remember this blessing is based on the custom of the Quran to base its teaching upon the explanation of causes (العلل) and reasons (الأسباب), calling to goodness and guidance through its proper avenue without commanding blind, common imitation (التقليد). Far be it from Divine teaching to guide people to felicity (السعادة) — the state of ultimate well-being through beneficial knowledge and righteous deeds — and then command them to fall into the wilderness of imitation and the darkness of ignorance.

However, the matter must not be confused, nor the situation blurred for the reflecting researcher. Allah, the Glorified, teaches people the reality of their felicity and explains the reason for it so they may gain insight into the connection of truths to one another, and that all flow from the fountainhead of monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — along with the necessity of their submission (إسلام) to Allah because He is Allah, the Lord of all the worlds (رب العالمين), and their holding fast to His cord because it is the cord of Allah, the Lord of all the worlds. This is alluded to at the end of the verses in His saying: تلك آيات الله نتلوها عليك — "These are the signs of Allah which We recite to you" [QARAI TRANSLATION NEEDED: Surah Ali 'Imran, Ayah 108].

In short, He commanded them not to accept any word or obey any command except through knowledge of its reason, then He commanded them to absolute submission (التسليم المطلق) to Himself and explained the reason: that He is Allah who possesses them absolutely; they have nothing except what He wills for them and how He disposes of them. He commanded them to absolute obedience to what His Messenger conveys and explained the reason: that he is a Messenger whose only task is the clear delivery (البلاغ). Then He speaks to them of the realities of knowledge (المعارف) and the explanation of the paths to felicity, and He explains the general reason in all of that so they may be guided to the connections of knowledge and the paths to felicity, thereby realizing the principle of monotheism. He does this so they may be disciplined by this Divine etiquette, gaining mastery over the path of correct reflection and knowing the way of true discourse, thus being alive through knowledge and free from imitation. The result of this is that if they recognize the reason for a command in any of the established religious sciences or what pertains to them, they adopt it; and if they do not recognize it, they refrain from rejection and return to seeking it through research and reflection without rejection or objection after its establishment.

This is different from saying that religion is based on not accepting anything—even from Allah and His Messenger—except with a proof (دليل), for that is the most foolish of opinions and the worst of speech. Its implication is that Allah wants His servants to demand a proof after its existence, for His Lordship (ربوبيته) and Sovereignty are the root of every proof for the necessity of submission and the effectiveness of the decree. The messengership of His Messenger is the proof that what he conveys is from Allah, the Glorified—so understand this. Otherwise, its implication is the nullification of His Lordship in that which He disposes through His Lordship, which is nothing but a contradiction. In conclusion, the Islamic path and the Prophetic way are nothing but the call to knowledge (العلم) rather than imitation (التقليد), contrary to what these imitators who call themselves "critics" imagine.

Perhaps the reason for mentioning that this is a blessing (نعمة الله عليكم) is to point to what we have mentioned: that the proof for what We have exhorted you toward—unity and social cohesion—is what you have witnessed of the bitterness of enmity and the sweetness of love, intimacy, and brotherhood, and being on the brink of the pit of Fire and escaping it. We remind you of this proof not because We must support Our word with that without which it would not be true—for Our word is true whether We provide proof for it or not—but so that you may know that it is a blessing from Us upon you, and thus recognize that in this social cohesion, as in everything else We exhort you toward, lies your felicity, your comfort, and your success.

Of the two proofs mentioned by the Exalted, the first—His saying: "when you were enemies"—is based on the principle of experience (التجربة), and the second—His saying: "and you were on the brink of a pit"—is based on the method of rational explanation (البيان العقلي), as is apparent.

In His saying: فأصبحتم بنعمته إخواناً — "so you became brothers by His blessing," there is a repetition of the bestowal of favor indicated by His saying: "and remember Allah’s blessing upon you." The "blessing" intended is the bringing together (التأليف), so the "brotherhood" (الاخوّة) which this blessing creates and realizes is also the intimacy of hearts; thus, brotherhood here is a metaphorical reality (حقيقة ادعائية).

It is also possible that it refers to what is contained in His saying: إِنَّمَا الْمُؤْمِنُونَ إِخْوَةٌ — "The faithful are indeed brothers" (49:10), regarding the legislation of brotherhood among them; for between the believers, there is a legislated brotherhood to which important rights are attached.

His saying, the Exalted: وكنتم علی شفا حفرة من النار فأنقذكم منها — "and you were on the brink of a pit of Fire, whereat He saved you from it." The "brink" (شفا) of a pit is its edge, which overlooks the fall for whoever is upon it.

If the "Fire" (النار) intended is the Fire of the Hereafter, then their being on the brink of its pit means they were disbelievers, with nothing between them and falling into it except death, which is closer to man than the black of the eye to its white; so Allah saved them from it through faith (الإيمان).

If the intent is to describe their state in the corrupt society they were in before their faith and the bringing together of their hearts, and the "Fire" intended is wars and conflicts—which is a common metaphorical usage—then the meaning is that a society built upon the scattering of hearts and the divergence of goals and desires will inevitably not be guided by a single proof toward a single end. Rather, it is guided by various proofs that differ according to personal inclinations and arbitrary individual dictates, leading them to the most intense disagreement and discord—bringing them to the brink of the worst conflict—and constantly threatening them with fighting and combat, promising them annihilation and cessation. This is the Fire that "spares nothing and leaves nothing" (74:28) upon the pit of ignorance (الجهالة) from which there is no escape or salvation for the one who falls.

These people—a group of Muslims who had believed before the revelation of the verse after their disbelief, and who are the primary addressees of these verses—did not live their lives before Islam except in a state of being threatened by wars and battles moment after moment. There was no security, no comfort, and no leisure. They did not comprehend the reality of public security (الأمن العام) that encompasses society in all its aspects: status, wealth, honor, life, and so on.

Then, when they gathered to hold fast to the cord of Allah, and the signs of felicity appeared to them, and they tasted something of the sweetness of blessings, they found the truth of what Allah reminds them of regarding the pleasant blessing and the delicious felicity. Thus, the address was more impactful upon their souls and the souls of others.

For this reason, the discourse was constructed and the call placed upon the foundation of observation and direct experience (الوجدان) rather than mere estimation and hypothesis. Seeing is not like hearing, and experience is not like hypothesis and estimation. For this very reason, in the upcoming warning in His saying: ولا تكونوا كالذين تفرقوا واختلفوا — "Do not be like those who became divided and differed..." [QARAI TRANSLATION NEEDED: Surah Ali 'Imran, Ayah 105], He pointed to the state of those before them; for the outcome of their state was within the sight and hearing of the believers. They must take a lesson from them and what their affair led to, so they do not follow their course or walk their path.

Then Allah alerted them to the specificity of this explanation, saying: كذلك يبين الله لكم آياته لعلكم تهتدون — "Thus does Allah clarify His signs for you so that you may be guided" [QARAI TRANSLATION NEEDED: Surah Ali 'Imran, Ayah 103].

His saying, the Exalted: ولتكن منكم امة يدعون إلی الخير ويأمرون بالمعروف وينهون عن المنكر — "There has to be a nation among you summoning to good, bidding what is right, and forbidding what is wrong..." [QARAI TRANSLATION NEEDED: Surah Ali 'Imran, Ayah 104]. Definitive experience (التجربة القطعية) shows that the information a person prepares for himself in his life—and one only prepares and stores for himself what benefits him—no matter how he prepared it or in what way he stored it, will vanish from him if he does not remember it and persist in repeating it through action (العمل). We do not doubt that action in all its affairs revolves around knowledge (العلم); it strengthens with its strength, weakens with its weakness, becomes sound with its soundness, and corrupt with its corruption. Allah, the Glorified, has likened their state in His saying: الْبَلَدُ الطَّيِّبُ يَخْرُجُ نَبَاتُهُ بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِ وَالَّذِي خَبُثَ لَا يَخْرُجُ إِلَّا نَكِدًا — "As for the good land, its vegetation comes out by its Lord’s leave; and as for that which is bad, it does not come out except scantily" (7:58).

We do not doubt that knowledge and action are reciprocal in their influence; knowledge is the strongest caller to action, and the witnessed, actual action is the strongest teacher that instructs man.

This is what calls a righteous society that possesses beneficial knowledge and righteous deeds to preserve their knowledge and culture, to return the one who deviates from the path of recognized good (الخير المعروف) back to it, and not to let the one who leans away from the path of recognized good—falling into the descent of unrecognized evil (الشر المنكر)—fall into the destruction of evil, but rather to forbid him from it.

This is the "summoning through teaching" (الدعوة بالتعليم), "bidding what is right" (الأمر بالمعروف), and "forbidding what is wrong" (النهي عن المنكر), which Allah mentions in this verse by His saying: "summoning to good, bidding what is right, and forbidding what is wrong."

From here, the secret appears in His expression of good and evil as "the recognized" (المعروف) and "the unrecognized" (المنكر); for the discourse is built upon what was in the previous verse: "Hold fast, all together, to Allah’s cord, and do not be divided." It is known that in a society of this nature, the "recognized" is the good and the "unrecognized" is the evil. Were it not for the consideration of this point, the reason for naming good and evil as "recognized" and "unrecognized" would be that good and evil are recognized and unrecognized according to the view of religion, not according to external action.

As for His saying: "There has to be a nation among you" (ولتكن منكم امة), it has been said that "among" (من) is for partitivity (التبعيض), based on the fact that bidding what is right and forbidding what is wrong, as well as summoning, are among the collective obligations (الواجبات الكفائية).

It has also been said that "among" (من) is explanatory (بيانية), and the meaning is: "You should be, through this righteous social cohesion, a nation summoning to good." Thus, the discourse follows the pattern of our saying: "Let there be for me from you a friend," meaning "Be a friend to me." It appears that the intent of "among" being explanatory is that it is originative (نشوئية) and introductory (ابتدائية).

What ought to be said is that the discussion of whether "among" is partitive or explanatory does not yield a practical result. Summoning, bidding what is right, and forbidding what is wrong are matters that, if obligatory, would by their nature be collective obligations; for there is no meaning to summoning, bidding, and forbidding after the purpose has been achieved. Even if we assume the entire nation is summoning to good, bidding what is right, and forbidding what is wrong, it means that among them are those who perform these duties. Thus, the command is fulfilled by some in any case. If the address is to some, then that is it; and if it is to all, it is also in consideration of some. In other words, the responsibility lies with all, but the reward is for those who act. Therefore, He followed it with His saying: وأولئك هم المفلحون — "and it is they who are the felicitous" [QARAI TRANSLATION NEEDED: Surah Ali 'Imran, Ayah 104]. Thus, it appears that "among" is partitive, which is the apparent meaning of such a construction in the language of speakers, and one does not deviate from it except with a proof.

Know that these three subjects—summoning, bidding what is right, and forbidding what is wrong—possess long and deep exegetical discussions that we shall address in another suitable place, God willing. Likewise, the scientific, psychological, and sociological discussions related to them.

His saying, the Exalted: ولا تكونوا كالذين تفرقوا واختلفوا من بعد ما جائهم البينات — "Do not be like those who became divided and differed after manifest proofs had come to them" [QARAI TRANSLATION NEEDED: Surah Ali 'Imran, Ayah 105]. It is not unlikely that His saying: "after manifest proofs had come to them" relates only to His saying: "and differed." In that case, "differing" (الاختلاف) would mean division in terms of belief (الاعتقاد), while "division" (التفرق) would mean disagreement and scattering in terms of bodies (الأبدان). Division was mentioned before differing because it is like the prelude leading to it; for as long as a people are united and connected, the beliefs of some connect to others and unite through contact and interaction, and this preserves them from differing. But when they divide and some are cut off from others, it leads them to a difference of tastes and paths. They do not remain long before their thoughts and opinions become independent of one another, sects emerge among them, and the staff of unity is split. It is as if the Exalted is saying: "Do not be like those who divided in bodies first and left the collective, which ultimately led them to a difference of beliefs and opinions."

The Exalted has attributed this differing in several places in His speech to "rebellion" (البغي). The Exalted said: وَمَا اخْتَلَفَ فِيهِ إِلَّا الَّذِينَ أُوتُوهُ مِن بَعْدِ مَا جَاءَتْهُمُ الْبَيِّنَاتُ بَغْيًا بَيْنَهُمْ — "and none differed about it except those who had been given it, after manifest proofs had come to them, out of envy among themselves" (2:213). Although the emergence of differing in beliefs and opinions is necessary among individuals due to the difference in understandings, just as the emergence of this differing is necessary, so too is the social cohesion to repel it and the return of the differers to the arena of unity also necessary. Thus, removing differing is possible and within power through mediation, and the nation's turning away from that is a rebellion on their part and a casting of themselves into the destruction of discord.

The Quran has emphasized the call to unity and gone to extremes in prohibiting discord. This is only because of what was foreseen regarding the affair of this nation: that they would differ like those before them, or even exceed them in that. It has been mentioned repeatedly that it is the custom of the Quran that when it goes to extremes in warning against something and prohibiting its commission, it is a sign of its occurrence and perpetration. This is a matter that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) also foretold, just as the Quran foretold it: that discord would creep into his nation and then appear in the form of various sects, and that his nation would differ just as the Jews and Christians differed before. The narration will come in the "Traditions Research."

The course of events has confirmed this Quranic prophecy (الملحمة القرآنية). The nation did not remain long after the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) before they scattered in all directions and differed into various schools of thought, some declaring others faithless, from the era of the Companions until our day. Whenever someone intended to reconcile between different groups among them, it only gave birth to a third school of thought.

What research through analysis and decomposition guides us to is that the root of this discord goes back to the hypocrites (المنافقين), against whom the Quran speaks harshly and whose plotting and deception it deems immense. If you were to reflect upon what Allah, the Exalted, mentions regarding them in the Surahs of al-Baqarah, al-Tawbah, al-Ahzab, al-Munafiqun, and others, you would see something wondrous. This was their state during the time of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) while revelation had not yet ceased. Then, when Allah took him, their mention vanished and their bells fell silent suddenly.

As if between al-Hajun and al-Safa there was never A companion, and no storyteller ever spent the night in Mecca.

People did not remain long before they found themselves scattered like the people of Saba, separated by various schools of thought, enslaved by governments of arbitrary rule and despotism, having exchanged the felicity of life for the misery of misguidance and error. Allah is the one whose help is sought, and it is hoped from the grace of Allah that He will grant us success to complete this research in the commentary of Surah al-Bara'ah, God willing.

His saying, the Exalted: يوم تبيض وجوه وتسود وجوه — "The day when some faces will turn white and some faces will turn black..." [QARAI TRANSLATION NEEDED: Surah Ali 'Imran, Ayah 106] until the end of the two verses. Since the context is one of ingratitude for blessings (الكفر بالنعمة), which is similar to betrayal (الخيانة) that necessitates the baseness of embarrassment and shame, the Glorified mentioned among the types of punishment in the Hereafter that which suits it according to representation (التمثيل): the blackening of the face, which is used as a metonymy in this world for embarrassment, shame, and the like. This is sensed or indicated by the saying of the Exalted: فأما الذين اسودت وجوههم أكفرتم بعد إيمانكم — "As for those whose faces turn black: ‘Did you disbelieve after your faith?’" [QARAI TRANSLATION NEEDED: Surah Ali 'Imran, Ayah 106].

Likewise, He mentioned for the reward of those grateful for this blessing that which suits gratitude: the whitening of the face, which is used as a metonymy in this world for approval and satisfaction (الرضا).

His saying, the Exalted: تلك آيات الله نتلوها عليك بالحق — "These are the signs of Allah which We recite to you in truth" [QARAI TRANSLATION NEEDED: Surah Ali 'Imran, Ayah 108]. The adverbial phrase "in truth" (بالحق) relates to His saying: "We recite them," and the meaning is that the recitation is a recitation of truth without being a satanic falsehood. Or it relates to "the signs" (الآيات) by sensing the meaning of a description within it, or it is a settled phrase relating to something omitted. The meaning is that these signs, which reveal what Allah does with the two groups—the faithless and the grateful—are accompanied by truth without proceeding in the manner of falsehood or injustice. This view is more in accord with what follows it: وما الله يريد ظلماً — "and Allah does not desire any injustice."

His saying, the Exalted: وما الله يريد ظلماً للعالمين — "and Allah does not desire any injustice for all the worlds" [QARAI TRANSLATION NEEDED: Surah Ali 'Imran, Ayah 108]. The indefiniteness of "injustice" (ظلماً) in the context of negation implies exhaustiveness (الاستغراق), and the appearance of His saying: "for all the worlds" (للعالمين), which is a plural defined by the article, also implies exhaustiveness. The meaning based on this is that Allah does not desire any injustice—meaning any supposed injustice—for all the worlds and all collectives. And so it is; for discord among people is a matter whose ill effect returns to all the worlds and all people.

His saying, the Exalted: ولله ما في السموات والأرض وإلی الله ترجع الامور — "To Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth, and to Allah all matters are returned" [QARAI TRANSLATION NEEDED: Surah Ali 'Imran, Ayah 109]. When He mentioned that Allah does not desire injustice, He justified that with what removes any illusion of the occurrence of injustice. He mentioned that Allah, the Exalted, possesses all things from every aspect; thus, He has the right to dispose of them as He wills. Therefore, the disposal of what He does not possess—which would be injustice and transgression—is inconceivable in His regard.

Furthermore, a person only tends toward injustice if he has a need that he cannot fulfill except by transgressing against what he does not possess. Allah is the All-Sufficient (الغني) to whom belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth. This is what some have established, but it does not suit the apparent meaning of the verse; for this answer is actually based on His self-sufficiency (غناه) rather than His sovereignty (ملكه), and what is mentioned in the verse is sovereignty, not self-sufficiency. In any case, His sovereignty is proof that He, the Exalted, is not unjust.

There is another proof: that the return of all matters, whatever they may be, is to Him, the Exalted. No one other than Allah, the Exalted, has any share in the command such that Allah would strip it from him, wrest it from his hand, and execute His own will upon it, thereby being unjust. This is what His saying: "and to Allah all matters are returned" points toward.

The two reasons, as you see, are inseparable: one is based on the fact that everything belongs to Him, the Exalted, and the second is based on the fact that none of the matters belong to anyone other than Him, the Exalted.

His saying, the Exalted: كنتم خير أُمة أُخرجت للناس — "You are the best nation [ever] brought forth for mankind" [QARAI TRANSLATION NEEDED: Surah Ali 'Imran, Ayah 110]. The meaning of "bringing forth the nation for mankind" (إخراج الامة للناس)—and Allah knows best—is manifesting it to them. The distinction of this word "bringing forth" (الاخراج) is that it contains an allusion to occurrence and formation (التكون). The Exalted said: وَالَّذِي أَخْرَجَ الْمَرْعَىٰ — "and who brought forth the pasture" (87:4). The address is to the believers, which serves as a context that the "mankind" (الناس) intended is all of humanity. The verb—meaning His saying: "You were" (كنتم)—is stripped of time, as has been said. A "nation" (الامة) is only applied to a collective or an individual because they possess a goal and a purpose (مقصد) that they aim for and intend. The mention of faith in Allah after bidding what is right and forbidding what is wrong is like mentioning the whole after the part, or the root after the branch.

Thus, the meaning of the verse is: "You, O community of Muslims, are the best nation Allah has manifested to mankind through its guidance, because as a collective you believe in Allah and perform the two obligations of bidding what is right and forbidding what is wrong." It is known that the extension of this honor to the entire nation is because some are characterized by the reality of faith and the fulfillment of the duty of bidding what is right and forbidding what is wrong. This is the summary of what they have mentioned in this regard.

What is apparent—and Allah knows best—is that His saying: "You were" (كنتم) is not stripped of time. The verse praises the state of the believers at the beginning of the emergence of Islam, from the "foremost ones" (السابقين الأولين) among the Emigrants (المهاجرين) and the Helpers (الأنصار). The "faith" intended is faith in the call to unite upon holding fast to the cord of Allah and not being divided therein, in contrast to the disbelief in it indicated by His saying before: "Did you disbelieve after your faith?" Likewise, the "faith" of the People of the Book refers to that as well. Thus, the meaning results in: "You, O community of the nation of Islam, were—in the first of your formation and appearance to mankind—the best nation that appeared, because you bid what is right, forbid what is wrong, and hold fast to the cord of Allah, in agreement and unity as a single soul. Had the People of the Book also possessed this description, it would have been better for them; but they differed: among them are a faithful nation, but most of them are transgressors (فاسقون)."

Know that in the verses, there are instances of transition (الالتفات) from the third person to the second person, and from the plural address to the singular address and vice versa. There are also instances of placing a manifest noun in the place of a pronoun, such as the repetition of the Name of Majesty (لفظ الجلالة) in several places. The point in all of them is apparent to the one who reflects.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Ma'ani and the Tafsir of al-'Ayyashi, narrated from Abu Basir, who said: "I asked Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: 'Be wary of Allah with the wariness due to Him.' He said: 'He is obeyed and not disobeyed, remembered and not forgotten, and thanked and not shown ingratitude.'"

In al-Durr al-Manthur, al-Hakim and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from another path from Ibn Mas'ud, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) said: 'Be wary of Allah with the wariness due to Him: that He be obeyed and not disobeyed, and remembered and not forgotten.'"

In the same source, al-Khatib narrated from Anas, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) said: 'A servant does not be wary of Allah with the wariness due to Him until he knows that what befell him was not to miss him, and what missed him was not to befall him.'"

I say: It has been mentioned in the preceding commentary how the meaning of the first two hadiths is derived from the verse. As for the third hadith, it is an explanation through the necessary implication of the meaning, and that is apparent.

In Tafsir al-Burhan, from Ibn Shahrashub, from the Tafsir of Waki', from 'Abd Khayr, who said: "I asked 'Ali ibn Abi Talib regarding His saying: 'O you who have faith! Be wary of Allah with the wariness due to Him.' He said: 'By Allah, none acted upon it except the House of the Messenger of Allah. We remembered Him and did not forget Him, we thanked Him and will never show Him ingratitude, and we obeyed Him and did not disobey Him.' When this verse was revealed, the Companions said: 'We cannot endure that.' So Allah revealed: 'So be wary of Allah as much as you can.'" Waki' said: "As much as you are able."

In the Tafsir of al-'Ayyashi, from Abu Basir, who said: "I asked Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah: 'Be wary of Allah with the wariness due to Him.' He said: 'It is abrogated (منسوخة).' I said: 'And what abrogated it?' He said: 'The saying of Allah: "So be wary of Allah as much as you can."'"

I say: It is understood from the narration of Waki' that the "abrogation" (النسخ) intended in the narration of al-'Ayyashi is the explanation of the levels of godwariness. As for abrogation in its technical sense, as reported from some exegetes, it is a meaning rejected by the apparent text of the Book.

In al-Majma', from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding the verse: وأنتم مسلمون (and you are Muslims), with the doubling (of the sin).

In al-Durr al-Manthur, regarding the saying of the Exalted: واعتصموا بحبل الله جميعاً (Hold fast, all together, to Allah’s cord), Ibn Abi Shaybah and Ibn Jarir narrated from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) said: 'The Book of Allah is the cord of Allah extended from the heaven to the earth.'"

In the same source, Ibn Abi Shaybah narrated from Abu Shurayh al-Khuza'i, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) said: 'Indeed, this Quran is a means (سبب); one end of it is in the hand of Allah, and the other end is in your hands. So hold fast to it, for you shall never perish nor stray after it ever.'"

In al-Ma'ani, from al-Sajjad (peace be upon him) in a hadith: "And the cord of Allah is the Quran."

I say: There are other narrations with this meaning from the paths of both parties.

In the Tafsir of al-'Ayyashi, from al-Baqir (peace be upon him): "The Progeny of Muhammad (آل محمد) are the cord of Allah whom He commanded to hold fast to, saying: 'Hold fast, all together, to Allah’s cord, and do not be divided.'"

I say: There are other narrations with this meaning; and in the commentary, that which supports their meaning has been presented, and it is also supported by the narrations that follow.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, al-Tabarani narrated from Zayd ibn Arqam, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) said: 'I am your predecessor (فرط), and you shall meet me at the Pond (الحوض). So look to how you succeed me regarding the two weighty things (الثقلين)?' It was asked: 'And what are the two weighty things, O Messenger of Allah?' He said: 'The greater one is the Book of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, a means whose one end is in the hand of Allah and whose other end is in your hands; so hold fast to it, and you shall never perish nor stray. And the lesser one is my Progeny (عترتي). Indeed, the two shall never separate until they meet me at the Pond. I have asked that for them from my Lord, so do not precede them lest you perish, and do not [try to] teach them, for they are more learned than you.'"

I say: The Hadith of the Two Weighty Things (حديث الثقلين) is among the mutawatir (متواترات) — narrations transmitted by so many chains that their authenticity is certain — which both parties have agreed to narrate. It was mentioned at the beginning of the Surah that some scholars of hadith traced its narrators among the Companions to thirty-five men and women; and a vast multitude of narrators and people of hadith have narrated it from them.

In al-Durr al-Manthur as well, Ibn Majah, Ibn Jarir, and Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Anas, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) said: 'The Children of Israel divided into seventy-one sects, and indeed my nation shall divide into seventy-two sects, all of them in the Fire except one.' They said: 'O Messenger of Allah, and who is this one?' He said: 'The collective (الجماعة).' Then he said: 'Hold fast, all together, to Allah’s cord.'"

I say: The narration is also among the famous ones. The Shi'a have narrated it in another way, as in al-Khisal, al-Ma'ani, al-Ihtijaj, al-Amali, the Book of Sulaym ibn Qays, and the Tafsir of al-'Ayyashi. The wording is from al-Khisal, with its chain to Sulayman ibn Mehran, from Ja'far ibn Muhammad, from his fathers, from the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon them), who said: "I heard the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) saying: 'Indeed, the nation of Moses divided after him into seventy-one sects; one sect among them is saved and seventy are in the Fire. And the nation of Jesus divided after him into seventy-two sects; one sect among them is saved and seventy-one are in the Fire. And indeed, my nation shall divide after me into seventy-three sects; one sect among them is saved and seventy-two are in the Fire.'"

I say: This is in agreement with what follows.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Abu Dawud, al-Tirmidhi, Ibn Majah, and al-Hakim (who authenticated it) narrated from Abu Hurayrah, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him) said: 'The Jews divided into seventy-one sects, and the Christians divided into seventy-two sects; and my nation shall divide into seventy-three sects.'"

I say: This meaning is narrated through other paths from Mu'awiyah and others.

In the same source, al-Hakim narrated from Ibn 'Umar, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) said: 'There shall come upon my nation what came upon the Children of Israel, step by step (حذو النعل بالنعل), such that if there were among them one who married his mother openly, there would be in my nation one like him. Indeed, the Children of Israel divided into seventy-one creeds, and my nation shall divide into seventy-three creeds, all of them in the Fire except one creed.' It was said to him: 'What is the one?' He said: 'That which I and my companions are upon today.'"

I say: From Jami' al-Usul by Ibn al-Athir, from al-Tirmidhi, from Ibn 'Amr ibn al-'As, from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم), the like of it.

In Kamal al-Din, with its chain to Ghiyath ibn Ibrahim, from al-Sadiq, from his fathers (peace be upon them), who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) said: 'Everything that was in the past nations, its like shall be in this nation, step by step (حذو النعل بالنعل) and feather by feather (القذة بالقذة).'"

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم): "You shall surely follow the custom (سنة) of those before you, step by step and feather by feather; you shall not miss their path nor shall it miss you, span by span, cubit by cubit, and fathom by fathom, such that if those before you had entered the hole of a lizard, you would enter it." They said: "Do you mean the Jews and Christians, O Messenger of Allah?" He said: "Who else do I mean? You shall surely break the handles of Islam one by one; the first of what you shall break of your religion is trustworthiness (الأمانة), and the last of it is prayer (الصلوة)."

From Jami' al-Usul in what he extracted from the Sahihs, and from the Sahih of al-Tirmidhi, from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) that he said: "By the One in whose hand is my soul, you shall surely follow the customs of those before you (and Razin added) step by step and feather by feather, such that if there were among them one who came to his mother, there would be among you [the like]; so I do not know whether you will worship the calf or not."

I say: This narration is also among the famous ones; the Sunnis narrated it in their Sahihs and others, and the Shi'a narrated it in their collections.

In the two Sahihs, from Anas: "That the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) said: 'Men from among those who accompanied me shall surely be brought to me at the Pond, until when they are raised, they will be snatched away from me. I shall say: "O Lord, my companions!" It will be said: "Indeed, you do not know what they innovated after you."'"

In the two Sahihs as well, from Abu Hurayrah: "That the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) said: 'A group of my companions—or he said: of my nation—shall be brought to me on the Day of Resurrection, and they will be driven away from the Pond. I shall say: "O Lord, my companions!" He will say: "You have no knowledge of what they innovated after you; they turned back upon their heels in retreat," and they will be driven away.'"

I say: This hadith is also among the famous ones; both parties narrated it in their Sahihs and collections from several of the Companions, such as Ibn Mas'ud, Anas, Sahl ibn Sa'id, Abu Hurayrah, Abu Sa'id al-Khudri, 'Aishah, Umm Salamah, Asma' bint Abi Bakr, and others, and from some of the Imams of the People of the House (peace be upon them).

The narrations, despite their abundance and variety, confirm what we derived from the apparent meaning of the noble verses, and the succession of events and tribulations confirms the narrations.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, al-Hakim narrated (and authenticated it) from Ibn 'Umar that the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) said: "Whoever leaves the collective (الجماعة) by the measure of a span has indeed cast off the yoke of Islam from his neck until he returns to it. And whoever dies without having upon him an Imam of a collective, his death is a death of ignorance (ميتة جاهلية)."

I say: The narration is also among the famous ones in substance; both parties have narrated from him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) that he said: "Whoever dies and does not know the Imam of his time, dies a death of ignorance."

From Jami' al-Usul, from al-Tirmidhi and the Sunan of Abu Dawud, from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم): "A group of my nation shall not cease to be upon the truth."

In al-Majma', regarding the saying of the Exalted: أكفرتم بعد إيمانكم (Did you disbelieve after your faith?), from the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him): "They are the people of innovations (أهل البدع), desires, and false opinions from this nation."

In it and in the Tafsir of al-'Ayyashi, regarding the saying of the Exalted: كنتم خير امة اخرجت للناس (You are the best nation brought forth for mankind), from Abu 'Amr al-Zubayri, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), who said: "It means the nation for whom the prayer of Abraham was mandatory; they are the nation in whom, from whom, and to whom Allah sent [the Messenger]; they are the middle nation (الامة الوسطى), and they are the best nation brought forth for mankind."

I say: The discussion clarifying the meaning of the narration has passed in the commentary of the saying of the Exalted: وَمِن ذُرِّيَّتِنَا أُمَّةً مُّسْلِمَةً لَّكَ — "and of our descendants a Muslim nation of Thine" (2:128).

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Abu Ja'far regarding "You are the best nation brought forth for mankind," he said: "The People of the House of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم)."

In the same source, Ahmad narrated with a good chain from 'Ali, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) said: 'I have been given what none of the Prophets were given: I have been aided by terror [in the hearts of enemies], I have been given the keys of the earth, I have been named Ahmad, the soil has been made a purification for me, and my nation has been made the best of nations.'"

Ayahs

لَن يَضُرُّوكُمْ إِلَّآ أَذًى ۖ وَإِن يُقَـٰتِلُوكُمْ يُوَلُّوكُمُ ٱلْأَدْبَارَ ثُمَّ لَا يُنصَرُونَ (١١١)
They will never do you any harm, except for some hurt; and if they fight you, they will turn their backs [to flee], then they will not be helped. (111)
ضُرِبَتْ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلذِّلَّةُ أَيْنَ مَا ثُقِفُوٓا۟ إِلَّا بِحَبْلٍ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَحَبْلٍ مِّنَ ٱلنَّاسِ وَبَآءُو بِغَضَبٍ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَضُرِبَتْ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْمَسْكَنَةُ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ يَكْفُرُونَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ وَيَقْتُلُونَ ٱلْأَنۢبِيَآءَ بِغَيْرِ حَقٍّ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ بِمَا عَصَوا۟ وَّكَانُوا۟ يَعْتَدُونَ (١١٢)
Wherever they are found, abasement is stamped upon them, except for an asylum from Allah and an asylum from the people. They have earned the wrath of Allah, and poverty has been stamped upon them. That, because they would deny the signs of Allah and kill the prophets unjustly. That, because they would disobey and commit transgression. (112)
۞ لَيْسُوا۟ سَوَآءً ۗ مِّنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ أُمَّةٌ قَآئِمَةٌ يَتْلُونَ ءَايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ ءَانَآءَ ٱلَّيْلِ وَهُمْ يَسْجُدُونَ (١١٣)
Yet they are not all alike. Among the People of the Book is an upright nation; they recite Allah’s signs in the watches of the night and prostrate. (113)
يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ وَيَأْمُرُونَ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ وَيَنْهَوْنَ عَنِ ٱلْمُنكَرِ وَيُسَـٰرِعُونَ فِى ٱلْخَيْرَٰتِ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ (١١٤)
They have faith in Allah and the Last Day, and bid what is right and forbid what is wrong, and they are active in [performing] good deeds. Those are among the righteous. (114)
وَمَا يَفْعَلُوا۟ مِنْ خَيْرٍ فَلَن يُكْفَرُوهُ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌۢ بِٱلْمُتَّقِينَ (١١٥)
Whatever good they do, they will not go unappreciated for it, and Allah knows well the Godwary. (115)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لَن تُغْنِىَ عَنْهُمْ أَمْوَٰلُهُمْ وَلَآ أَوْلَـٰدُهُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ شَيْـًٔا ۖ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ ۚ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ (١١٦)
As for the faithless, neither their wealth nor their children will avail them anything against Allah. They shall be the inmates of the Fire and remain in it [forever]. (116)
مَثَلُ مَا يُنفِقُونَ فِى هَـٰذِهِ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا كَمَثَلِ رِيحٍ فِيهَا صِرٌّ أَصَابَتْ حَرْثَ قَوْمٍ ظَلَمُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ فَأَهْلَكَتْهُ ۚ وَمَا ظَلَمَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ وَلَـٰكِنْ أَنفُسَهُمْ يَظْلِمُونَ (١١٧)
The parable of what they spend in the life of this world is that of a cold wind that strikes the tillage of a people who wronged themselves, destroying it. Allah does not wrong them, but they wrong themselves. (117)
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَتَّخِذُوا۟ بِطَانَةً مِّن دُونِكُمْ لَا يَأْلُونَكُمْ خَبَالًا وَدُّوا۟ مَا عَنِتُّمْ قَدْ بَدَتِ ٱلْبَغْضَآءُ مِنْ أَفْوَٰهِهِمْ وَمَا تُخْفِى صُدُورُهُمْ أَكْبَرُ ۚ قَدْ بَيَّنَّا لَكُمُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ ۖ إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ (١١٨)
O you who have faith! Do not take your confidants from others than yourselves; they will spare nothing to ruin you. They are eager to see you in distress. Hatred has already shown itself from their mouths, and what their breasts hide [within] is yet worse. We have certainly made the signs clear for you, should you exercise your reason. (118)
هَـٰٓأَنتُمْ أُو۟لَآءِ تُحِبُّونَهُمْ وَلَا يُحِبُّونَكُمْ وَتُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱلْكِتَـٰبِ كُلِّهِۦ وَإِذَا لَقُوكُمْ قَالُوٓا۟ ءَامَنَّا وَإِذَا خَلَوْا۟ عَضُّوا۟ عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلْأَنَامِلَ مِنَ ٱلْغَيْظِ ۚ قُلْ مُوتُوا۟ بِغَيْظِكُمْ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلِيمٌۢ بِذَاتِ ٱلصُّدُورِ (١١٩)
Ah! You are the ones who bear love towards them, while they do not love you, though you believe in all the Books. When they meet you, they say, ‘We believe,’ but when they are alone, they bite their fingertips at you out of rage. Say, ‘Die of your rage!’ Indeed Allah knows well what is in the breasts. (119)
إِن تَمْسَسْكُمْ حَسَنَةٌ تَسُؤْهُمْ وَإِن تُصِبْكُمْ سَيِّئَةٌ يَفْرَحُوا۟ بِهَا ۖ وَإِن تَصْبِرُوا۟ وَتَتَّقُوا۟ لَا يَضُرُّكُمْ كَيْدُهُمْ شَيْـًٔا ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِمَا يَعْمَلُونَ مُحِيطٌ (١٢٠)
If some good should befall you, it upsets them, but if some ill befalls you, they rejoice at it. Yet if you are patient and Godwary, their guile will not harm you in any way. Indeed Allah encompasses what they do. (120)

(Commentary)

The noble verses—as you can see—return to the previous discourse concerning the state of the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب), specifically the Jews, regarding their disbelief in the signs of Allah, their leading of themselves into error, and their barring of the believers from the path of Allah (سبيل الله). The preceding ten verses were merely a parenthetical discourse within the main speech; thus, the continuity of the verses remains intact.

His saying, the Exalted: Should they harm you, they will cause you no more than a [fleeting] hurt... (3:111). Hurt (الأذى) refers to the harm that reaches a living being, whether in its soul, its body, or its circumstances, be it worldly or otherworldly, as mentioned by al-Raghib in Mufradat al-Quran.

His saying, the Exalted: Abasement has been stamped upon them wherever they are faced, except [when they take hold of] a tether from Allah and a tether from the people... (3:112). Abasement (الذلة) is a noun denoting a specific type of humiliation. Humiliation (الذل), with a damma on the first letter, refers to that which arises from subjugation, while with a kasra, it refers to that which arises from difficulty and recalcitrance, according to al-Raghib. Its general meaning is a state of brokenness and compliance, and its opposite is might (العز), which is resistance.

His saying: they are faced (ثقفوا) means they are found. The tether (الحبل) is the means (السبب) that necessitates protection (العصمة) by holding onto it. It has been used metaphorically for everything that necessitates a type of security, protection, and safeguard, such as a covenant (العهد), a treaty of protection (الذمة), and a guarantee of safety (الأمان). The meaning—and Allah knows best—is that abasement is stamped upon them like the stamping of a coin upon metal, or like the pitching (ضرب) of a tent over a person; thus, abasement is decreed for them or imposed upon them, except by a tether and a means from Allah, and a tether and a means from the people.

The word "tether" (الحبل) is repeated by attributing it once to Allah and once to the people due to the difference in meaning resulting from the attribution. From Allah, it is the decree and judgment, whether existential (تكوينياً) or legislative (تشريعاً), while from the people, it is the implementation and action.

What is meant by "stamping abasement upon them" is the legislative decree (القضاء التشريعي) of their humiliation. The evidence for this is His saying: wherever they are faced (أينما ثقفوا), for its apparent meaning is wherever the believers find them—that is, wherever they gain mastery over them. This is only consistent with legislative abasement, one of the effects of which is the tribute (الجزية).

Thus, the meaning of the verse resolves to the fact that they are abased according to the judgment of Islamic law (الشرع الإسلامي), unless they come under the treaty of protection (الذمة) or a guarantee of safety from the people in some manner.

The view of some commentators is that His saying: Abasement has been stamped upon them is not in the context of legislating a judgment, but rather an informative report (إخبار) of what has befallen them by the decree and measure (قضاء وقدر) of Allah. For Islam encountered the Jews while they were paying the tribute to the Magians, and some of their tribes were under the authority of the Christians.

There is no harm in this meaning, and it is perhaps supported by the end of the verse, as it clearly indicates that the reason for stamping abasement and wretchedness (المسكنة) upon them is what their hands earned of disbelief in the signs of Allah, the killing of the prophets, and continuous transgression. However, the necessary implication of this meaning is that the discourse in the verse is restricted to the Jews, yet there is no apparent reason for such restriction. A discussion regarding this will follow in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: “And We have cast enmity and hatred among them...” (5:64).

His saying, the Exalted: ...and they have earned the wrath of Allah, and wretchedness has been stamped upon them (3:112). They have earned (بائوا) means they have taken a dwelling and a place, or they have returned. Wretchedness (المسكنة) is the severest form of poverty. It appears that wretchedness is when a person finds no way to escape or be delivered from the poverty or any lack that threatens him. Based on this, the meaning of the beginning and the end of the verse harmonize.

His saying, the Exalted: That, because they would disobey and used to transgress (3:112). The meaning is that they disobeyed and, prior to that, they used to persist in transgression.

His saying, the Exalted: They are not all alike... until His saying ...and Allah knows well the God-conscious (3:113-115). Alike (السواء) is an infinitive used here with the meaning of an adjective (الوصف); that is, they are not equal in description and judgment. For among them is an upright community (امة قائمة) who recite the signs of Allah... etc. From this, it becomes clear that His saying: of the People of the Book... is in the position of providing the reason (التعليل), explaining why the People of the Book are not all alike.

There is a difference of opinion regarding His saying: upright (قائمة). It is said to mean: firm upon the command of Allah; it is also said to mean: just; and it is said to mean: possessing an upright path. The truth is that the term is absolute and can encompass all these meanings, but the mention of the Book and their righteous deeds (الأعمال الصالحة) specifies that what is meant is being upright in faith and obedience.

The word al-ana (الآناء) is the plural of iny (إنی) with a kasra or fatha on the hamza; it is also said to be inw (إنو), meaning time.

Hastening (المسارعة) means taking the initiative (المبادرة). It is on the morphological pattern of mufa'alah from speed (السرعة). It is stated in al-Majma': "The difference between speed (السرعة) and haste (العجلة) is that speed is to move forward in that which is permissible to move forward in, and it is praiseworthy; its opposite is slowness (الإبطاء), which is blameworthy. Haste is to move forward in that which is not appropriate to move forward in, and it is blameworthy; its opposite is deliberation (الأناة), which is praiseworthy." End quote. It appears that speed is originally a description of the motion (الحركة), while haste is a description of the mover (المتحرك).

Good works (الخيرات) refers to righteous deeds in an absolute sense, whether worship (عبادة), spending (إنفاق), justice, or fulfilling a need. It is a plural noun defined by the definite article al-, which denotes exhaustiveness (الاستغراق). It is frequently applied to financial good deeds, just as "good" (الخير) is frequently applied to wealth.

Allah, the Glorified, has enumerated for them a group of important righteous deeds: faith, enjoining the good and forbidding the wrong, and hastening toward every good. Then He described them as being among the righteous (الصالحون). Thus, they are the people of the straight path (الصراط المستقيم) — the direct and unerring way to Allah — and the companions of the prophets, the truthful, and the witnesses, according to His saying, the Exalted: اهْدِنَا الصِّرَاطَ الْمُسْتَقِيمَ صِرَاطَ الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَيْهِمْ غَيْرِ الْمَغْضُوبِ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا الضَّالِّينَ "Guide us on the straight path, the path of those whom You have blessed — such as have not incurred Your wrath, nor are astray" (1:7), and His saying, the Exalted: فَأُولَٰئِكَ مَعَ الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِم مِّنَ النَّبِيِّينَ وَالصِّدِّيقِينَ وَالشُّهَدَاءِ وَالصَّالِحِينَ "...they will be with those whom Allah has blessed of the prophets, the truthful, the martyrs and the righteous..." (4:69). It is said that those intended by this praise are Abdullah ibn Salam and his companions.

His saying, the Exalted: Whatever good they do, they will not be denied [the reward of] it... (3:115). This is from ingratitude (الكفران), the opposite of gratitude (الشكر). That is, Allah will show gratitude to them and return it to them without it being lost, as He, the Exalted, said: وَمَن تَطَوَّعَ خَيْرًا فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ شَاكِرٌ عَلِيمٌ "...and whoever puts himself out to do good, then Allah is indeed appreciative, all-knowing" (2:158), and He said: وَمَا تُنفِقُوا مِنْ خَيْرٍ فَلِأَنفُسِكُمْ "...And whatever wealth you spend, it is for your own sake..." until He said: وَمَا تُنفِقُوا مِنْ خَيْرٍ يُوَفَّ إِلَيْكُمْ وَأَنتُمْ لَا تُظْلَمُونَ "...And whatever wealth you spend will be repaid to you in full, and you will not be wronged" (2:272).

His saying, the Exalted: As for the faithless, neither their possessions nor their children shall avail them anything... (3:116). The apparent unity of the context suggests that those intended by "the faithless" (الذين كفروا) are the other group of the People of the Book who did not respond to the call of prophecy, who were plotting against Islam, and who spared no effort in extinguishing its light.

It is sometimes said that the verse refers to the state of the polytheists (المشركين), serving as a preamble to what He will later point to regarding the story of Uhud. However, this does not harmonize with what follows in His saying: ...while you believe in all the Books. When they meet you, they say, ‘We believe’... (3:119), for that is an explanation of the state of the Jews with the Muslims, not the state of the polytheists. From this, it becomes clear that the continuity of the context has not been broken.

Some commentators have attempted to apply this verse to the polytheists while applying the other to the Jews, but this is an error.

His saying, the Exalted: The parable of what they spend in the life of this world... (3:117). Sirr (الصر) is intense cold. The object of the parable is restricted by His saying: in the life of this world (في هذه الحيوة الدنيا) to indicate that they are cut off from the Abode of the Hereafter; thus, their spending relates only to this life. He restricted the tilth of the people by saying: who have wronged themselves (ظلموا أنفسهم) to facilitate the connection with His subsequent saying: and Allah did not wrong them.

The summary of the discourse is that their spending in this life—while they intend by it to rectify their affairs and achieve their corrupt goals—yields nothing for them but misery and the ruin of what they desire and consider to be happiness for themselves. It is like a wind containing intense cold that destroys the tilth of the wrongdoers. This is nothing but their own wrongdoing against themselves, for a corrupt deed produces nothing but a corrupt effect.

His saying, the Exalted: O you who have faith! Do not take an intimate confidant from others than yourselves... (3:118). An outsider (الوليجة) is called an "intimate confidant" (بطانة) — a close associate who is privy to one's secrets — which refers to the inner lining of a garment that touches the body, as opposed to the outer fabric (الظهارة), because such a person becomes aware of a person's inner state and what he conceals and keeps secret. His saying: they will spare no effort [to ruin] you (لا يألونكم) means they will not fall short in harming you. His saying: ruin (خبالاً) means evil and corruption; from it comes khabal for madness, because it is the corruption of the intellect. His saying: they choose for you what distresses you (ودوا ما عنتّم), where ma is an infinitive particle; that is, they love and desire your distress and severe harm. His saying: Enmity has already appeared from their mouths (قد بدت البغضاء من أفواههم) intends the manifestation of hatred and enmity from the tone of their speech and their slips of the tongue; this contains a subtle metaphor (استعارة) and an allusion (كناية). He did not specify what is in their breasts, but rather left it vague in His saying: and what their breasts conceal is greater (وما تخفي صدورهم أكبر) to intimate that it cannot be described due to its variety and magnitude, and by this, His saying "greater" (أكبر) is emphasized.

His saying, the Exalted: Ah! It is you who love them... (3:119). It appears that ula'i (اولاء) is a demonstrative pronoun and the word ha (ها) is for drawing attention (التنبيه). The word "you" (أنتم) has been placed between ha and ula'i. The meaning is: "You are these people," similar to the Arabic expression: "Zayd is this one" or "Hind is this one," meaning such and such.

And His saying: ...while you believe in all the Books (وتؤمنون بالكتاب كله). The definite article al- denotes the genus; that is, you believe in all the heavenly Books revealed by Allah: their Book and your Book, while they do not believe in your Book. His saying: When they meet you, they say, ‘We believe’ means they are hypocrites. His saying: but when they are alone, they bite their finger-tips at you out of rage (وإذا خلوا عضوا عليكم الأنامل من الغيظ). Biting (العض) is to grip with the teeth while pressing. Al-Anamil (الأنامل) is the plural of anmulah, which is the fingertip. Rage (الغيظ) is intense anger. Biting the fingertips over something is a proverb struck for regret and sorrow out of anger and fury.

And His saying: Say, ‘Die of your rage!’ (قل موتوا بغيظكم) is a supplication against them in the form of a command. By this, the sentence connects to His saying: Indeed Allah knows well what is in the breasts (إن الله عليم بذات الصدور); that is: O Allah, cause them to die in their rage, for You are all-knowing of what is in the breasts—meaning the hearts or the souls.

His saying, the Exalted: If some good befalls you, it grieves them... (3:120). Grief (المسائة) is the opposite of joy. In the verse is an indication that safety from their plotting is conditional upon patience (الصبر) and God-consciousness (التقوى).

Ayahs

وَإِذْ غَدَوْتَ مِنْ أَهْلِكَ تُبَوِّئُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ مَقَـٰعِدَ لِلْقِتَالِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ (١٢١)
When you left your family at dawn to settle the faithful in their positions for battle —and Allah is all-hearing, all-knowing. (121)
إِذْ هَمَّت طَّآئِفَتَانِ مِنكُمْ أَن تَفْشَلَا وَٱللَّهُ وَلِيُّهُمَا ۗ وَعَلَى ٱللَّهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ (١٢٢)
When two groups among you were about to lose courage—though Allah is their guardian, and in Allah let all the faithful put their trust. (122)
وَلَقَدْ نَصَرَكُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِبَدْرٍ وَأَنتُمْ أَذِلَّةٌ ۖ فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ (١٢٣)
Certainly Allah helped you at Badr, when you were weak [in the enemy’s eyes]. So be wary of Allah so that you may give thanks. (123)
إِذْ تَقُولُ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ أَلَن يَكْفِيَكُمْ أَن يُمِدَّكُمْ رَبُّكُم بِثَلَـٰثَةِ ءَالَـٰفٍ مِّنَ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ مُنزَلِينَ (١٢٤)
When you were saying to the faithful, ‘Is it not enough for you that your Lord should aid you with three thousand angels sent down?’ (124)
بَلَىٰٓ ۚ إِن تَصْبِرُوا۟ وَتَتَّقُوا۟ وَيَأْتُوكُم مِّن فَوْرِهِمْ هَـٰذَا يُمْدِدْكُمْ رَبُّكُم بِخَمْسَةِ ءَالَـٰفٍ مِّنَ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ مُسَوِّمِينَ (١٢٥)
Yes, if you are steadfast and Godwary, and should they come at you suddenly, your Lord will aid you with five thousand angels sent in [to the scene of battle]. (125)
وَمَا جَعَلَهُ ٱللَّهُ إِلَّا بُشْرَىٰ لَكُمْ وَلِتَطْمَئِنَّ قُلُوبُكُم بِهِۦ ۗ وَمَا ٱلنَّصْرُ إِلَّا مِنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْحَكِيمِ (١٢٦)
Allah did not appoint it but as a good news for you and to reassure with it your hearts; and victory comes only from Allah, the All-mighty, the All-wise, (126)
لِيَقْطَعَ طَرَفًا مِّنَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ أَوْ يَكْبِتَهُمْ فَيَنقَلِبُوا۟ خَآئِبِينَ (١٢٧)
that He may cut down a section of the faithless, or subdue them, so that they retreat disappointed. (127)
لَيْسَ لَكَ مِنَ ٱلْأَمْرِ شَىْءٌ أَوْ يَتُوبَ عَلَيْهِمْ أَوْ يُعَذِّبَهُمْ فَإِنَّهُمْ ظَـٰلِمُونَ (١٢٨)
You have no hand in the matter, whether He accepts their repentance or punishes them, for they are indeed wrongdoers. (128)
وَلِلَّهِ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ يَغْفِرُ لِمَن يَشَآءُ وَيُعَذِّبُ مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (١٢٩)
To Allah belongs whatever there is in the heavens and the earth: He forgives whomever He wishes and punishes whomever He wishes, and Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (129)

(Commentary)

This is a return to what the Surah began with: alerting the believers to the difficult situation they find themselves in, reminding them of Allah’s favors upon them—such as faith, victory, and sufficiency—teaching them that by which they may excel toward their noble objective, and guiding them to that which will bring them happiness in their lives and after their deaths.

Contained herein is the story of the Battle of Uhud. As for the verses referring to the Battle of Badr, they are included as a supplementary attachment (ضميمة متممة), serving as a testimonial witness (محل شاهد) for the story, and are not the primary objective (مقصودة بالأصالة), as will be explained.

His saying, the Exalted: "And when you left your family at dawn to settle the believers in their positions for battle" (وَإِذْ غَدَوْتَ مِنْ أَهْلِكَ تُبَوِّئُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ مَقاعِدَ لِلْقِتالِ) [Qarai: 3:121]. The word "when" (إذ) is an adverb (ظرف) related to a deleted verb such as "remember" (اذكر) or its like. "You left at dawn" (غدوت) is derived from al-ghuduww (الغدو), which is to go out in the early morning. "Settling" (التبوئة) means preparing a place for another, or housing them and establishing them in a location. "Positions" (المقاعد) is a plural. The "family of a man" (أهل الرجل)—as mentioned by al-Raghib—refers to those joined with him by lineage (نسب), a house (بيت), or other factors such as religion (دين), country (بلد), or profession (صناعة). It is said: a man's ahl are his wife and those in his house including children, servants, and others; it also refers to those attributed to him from his kinsfolk (عشيرته) and progeny (عترته). It is said: the ahl of such-and-less city for its inhabitants, the ahl of such-and-such religion for its adherents, and the ahl of such-and-such craft for its practitioners and masters. The term is used equally for masculine, feminine, singular, and plural, and its usage is specific to human beings; thus, the ahl of a thing are those humans specifically associated with it.

What is meant by the "family" (أهل) of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) are his close associates (خاصته), and they are a group. It does not refer here to a single person, as evidenced by His saying: "you left from your family" (غَدَوْتَ مِنْ أَهْلِكَ), for it is permissible to say: "You went out from your inner circle" or "from your group," but it is not [linguistically] permissible to say: "You went out from your wife" or "You went out from your mother" [in the sense of leaving a location]. Therefore, some exegetes felt compelled to assume an omitted word in the verse, suggesting the meaning is: "You went out from the house of your family," because they interpreted "family" as a singular person; however, there is no evidence in the speech to support this.

The context of the verses is based on addressing the collective (خطاب الجمع), which is the address to the believers, as indicated by the preceding and succeeding verses. Thus, in His saying: "And when you left your family at dawn to settle the believers" (وَإِذْ غَدَوْتَ مِنْ أَهْلِكَ تُبَوِّئُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ), there is a transition (التفات) from addressing them to addressing the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). The reason for this is the tone of reprimand (لحن العتاب) apparent in the verses of the story; they are not devoid of a trace of blame, reproach, and regret over what occurred and what became manifest from the believers in terms of failure (الفشل) and weakness (الوهن) in resolve and fighting. For this reason, He turned away from addressing them in the midst of the story and shifted to addressing the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) regarding what specifically concerned him.
