He said: "And when you left your family at dawn" (وَإِذْ غَدَوْتَ مِنْ أَهْلِكَ); and He said: "When you were saying to the faithful, ‘Is it not enough for you...’" (إِذْ تَقُولُ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ أَلَنْ يَكْفِيَكُمْ); and He said: "You have no hand in the matter" (لَيْسَ لَكَ مِنَ الْأَمْرِ شَيْءٌ); and He said: "Say, ‘Indeed the entire matter belongs to Allah’" (قُلْ إِنَّ الْأَمْرِ كُلَّهُ لِلَّهِ); and He said: "It is by Allah’s mercy that you are gentle to them; had you been harsh and hardhearted, they would have surely scattered from around you. So excuse them" (فَبِما رَحْمَةٍ مِنَ اللهِ لِنْتَ لَهُمْ وَلَوْ كُنْتَ فَظًّا غَلِيظَ الْقَلْبِ لَانْفَضُّوا مِنْ حَوْلِكَ فَاعْفُ عَنْهُمْ); and He said: "Do not suppose those who were slain in the way of Allah to be dead" (وَلا تَحْسَبَنَّ الَّذِينَ قُتِلُوا فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ أَمْواتاً) [to the end of the verse].

Thus, He changed the collective address in these instances to a singular address. These are instances where a speaker, in the flow of his speech, is restrained from continuing in the same manner due to what enrages him or stirs his grief. This is unlike His saying within the verses: "Muhammad is but an apostle; [other] apostles have passed before him. If he dies or is slain, will you turn back on your heels?" (وَما مُحَمَّدٌ إِلَّا رَسُولٌ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِنْ قَبْلِهِ الرُّسُلُ أَفَإِنْ ماتَ أَوْ قُتِلَ انْقَلَبْتُمْ), and His saying: "while the Apostle was calling you from your rear" (وَالرَّسُولُ يَدْعُوكُمْ فِي أُخْراكُمْ), because the reprimand in these two is more effective through the collective address than the singular. It is also unlike His saying within the verses: "Allah certainly favoured the faithful when He raised up among them an apostle from among themselves" (لَقَدْ مَنَّ اللهُ عَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ إِذْ بَعَثَ فِيهِمْ رَسُولاً مِنْ أَنْفُسِهِمْ), because expressing favor (الامتنان) regarding the sending of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) while referring to him in the third person (غائبا) is more impactful and has a deeper effect on the souls, and is further removed from [unworthy] illusions or passing thoughts. Reflect upon the verses, and you will find the correctness of what we have mentioned.

The meaning of the verse is: And remember when you went out in the early morning from your family, preparing for the believers positions for battle, or housing them and stationing them therein. And Allah is All-Hearing (سميع) of what was said there, All-Knowing (عليم) of what their hearts concealed. What is understood from His saying: "And when you left your family at dawn" (وَإِذْ غَدَوْتَ مِنْ أَهْلِكَ) is the proximity of the battlefield to his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) house. This necessitates that the two verses refer to the Battle of Uhud; thus, these two verses connect to the subsequent verses revealed regarding Uhud, as the contents correspond to the events of this battle. Through this, the weakness of the opinion that these two verses are about the Battle of Badr becomes apparent, as does the opinion that they are about the Battle of the Trench (غزوة الأحزاب); the reason for this is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: "and Allah is all-hearing, all-knowing" (وَاللهُ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ) [Qarai: 3:121]. That is, All-Hearing, hearing what was said there; All-Knowing, knowing what was concealed in your hearts. In this is an indication of speech that took place there among them, and matters they harbored in their hearts. It appears that His saying: "When [two groups...]" (إِذْ هَمَّتْ) is related to these two attributes.

His saying, the Exalted: "When two groups among you were about to lose heart, though Allah was their guardian" (إِذْ هَمَّتْ طائِفَتانِ مِنْكُمْ أَنْ تَفْشَلا وَاللهُ وَلِيُّهُما) [Qarai: 3:122]. "Concern" (الهم) is what you concern yourself with in your soul, which is intention (القصد). "Losing heart" (الفشل) is weakness accompanied by cowardice.

His saying: "though Allah was their guardian" (وَاللهُ وَلِيُّهُما) is a circumstantial clause (حال), and the governing agent (العامل) for it is His saying: "were about to" (هَمَّتْ). The speech is intended for reprimand and blame. Likewise is His saying: "and in Allah let the faithful put their trust" (وَعَلَى اللهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ). The meaning is: they were about to lose heart despite Allah being their Guardian (الولي) — the protecting friend and supporter — and it is not fitting for a believer to lose heart while he sees that Allah is his Guardian, and while it is fitting for the believers to entrust their affair to Allah, for whoever puts his trust in Allah, He is sufficient for him.

From this, the weakness of the claim that this "concern" (الهم) was a passing thought (هم خطرة) and not a firm intention (هم عزيمة) becomes clear. [The proponents of this claim argue] that Allah the Exalted praised them and informed that He was their Guardian, and had it been a firm intention and purpose, then blaming them would have been more appropriate than praising them.

I do not know what is meant by saying it was a "passing thought." Is it merely the occurrence in the mind and the conceptualization of the notion of failure? Everyone present would have had that occur to their minds, and there is certainly no meaning in mentioning such a thing in the story. Nor is that called hamm (هم) in the language. Or was it a conceptualization accompanied by some degree of assent (تصديق), and a thought tinged with intention? This is indicated by the manifestation of their state to others; for if it were a mere thought without any effect, it would not have been apparent that they were about to lose heart. Furthermore, the mention of Allah’s guardianship over them and the necessity of trust (التوكل) — the act of relying upon Allah's agency — for the believer only suits this [intentional] concern, not a mere passing thought. Moreover, His saying: "though Allah was their guardian" (وَاللهُ وَلِيُّهُما) is not praise, but rather blame and exhortation, as dictated by the context, as previously mentioned.

Perhaps the origin of this [erroneous] claim is what was narrated from Jabir ibn Abdillah al-Ansari, who said: "It was revealed concerning us, and I would not have liked for it not to have been [revealed], because of His saying: 'though Allah was their guardian' (وَاللهُ وَلِيُّهُما)." Thus, it was understood from the narration that Jabir understood the verse as praise.

Even if the narration is authentic, Jabir only meant that Allah the Exalted accepted their faith and the truth of their being believers when He counted Himself as their Guardian—and Allah is the Guardian of those who believe, while the guardians of those who disbelieve are the Taghut (الطاغوت) — the false deities and powers that lead away from Allah. It does not mean that the sentence is placed for the purpose of praise in this context, which is clearly one of reprimand.

His saying, the Exalted: "Allah certainly helped you at Badr, when you were abject" (وَلَقَدْ نَصَرَكُمُ اللهُ بِبَدْرٍ وَأَنْتُمْ أَذِلَّةٌ) [Qarai: 3:123] to the end of the verse. The apparent context is that the verse is presented as a testimonial witness (شاهد) to complete and emphasize the reprimand. Thus, it functions as a circumstantial clause (حال), like His saying: "though Allah was their guardian" (وَاللهُ وَلِيُّهُما). The meaning is: It was not fitting for the concern of losing heart to appear from you when Allah had already helped you at Badr while you were abject. It is not far-fetched that it is an independent statement presented as an expression of favor (الامتنان) by mentioning a wondrous victory from Allah through the sending down of angels to reinforce and help them on the day of Badr.

When Allah the Exalted mentioned His helping them on the day of Badr, He contrasted that with the state they were in—and it is well known that whoever is empowered (اعتز) is only empowered by the help and aid of Allah, for man possesses nothing from himself except poverty and abasement (الذلة). Therefore, He said: "when you were abject" (وَأَنْتُمْ أَذِلَّةٌ).

From here, it is known that His saying: "when you were abject" (وَأَنْتُمْ أَذِلَّةٌ) does not contradict such verses as His saying: "yet all might belongs to Allah and His Apostle, and the faithful" (وَلِلَّهِ الْعِزَّةُ وَلِرَسُولِهِ وَلِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ) [Qarai: 63:8], for their might (عزة) is only through the might of Allah. He, the Exalted, said: "for all might belongs to Allah" (فَإِنَّ الْعِزَّةَ لِلَّهِ جَمِيعاً) [Qarai: 4:139]. This is through Allah’s helping the believers, as He, the Exalted, said: "Certainly We sent apostles before you to their people, and they brought them manifest proofs. Then We took vengeance upon those who were guilty, and it was a must for Us to help the faithful" (وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنا مِنْ قَبْلِكَ رُسُلاً إِلى قَوْمِهِمْ فَجاؤُهُمْ بِالْبَيِّناتِ فَانْتَقَمْنا مِنَ الَّذِينَ أَجْرَمُوا وَكانَ حَقًّا عَلَيْنا نَصْرُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ) [Qarai: 30:47]. Since this is the case, if the state of the believers were considered from the perspective of their own selves, they would have nothing but abasement.

Furthermore, the outward state of the believers on the day of Badr also dictated their being abject in the face of the strength, power, and pomp of the polytheists. There is no harm in attributing relative abasement (الذلة النسبية) to those who are mighty (الأعزة), and Allah, the Glorified, attributed it to a people whom He praised most highly when He said: "Allah will soon bring a people whom He loves and who love Him, [who will be] humble towards the faithful, mighty against the faithless" (فَسَوْفَ يَأْتِي اللهُ بِقَوْمٍ يُحِبُّهُمْ وَيُحِبُّونَهُ أَذِلَّةٍ عَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ أَعِزَّةٍ عَلَى الْكافِرِينَ) [Qarai: 5:54].

His saying, the Exalted: "When you were saying to the faithful, ‘Is it not enough for you...’" (إِذْ تَقُولُ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ أَلَنْ يَكْفِيَكُمْ) [Qarai: 3:124]. "Reinforcement" (الإمداد) is from al-madd (المد), which is the continuous provision of aid.

His saying, the Exalted: "Yes, if you are steadfast and God-conscious, and they come at you in this heat of theirs" (بَلى إِنْ تَصْبِرُوا وَتَتَّقُوا وَيَأْتُوكُمْ مِنْ فَوْرِهِمْ هذا) [Qarai: 3:125]. "Yes" (بلى) is a word of affirmation. "Heat" (الفور) and "boiling" (الفوران) refer to the bubbling of a liquid; it is said: "the pot boiled" (فار القدر) when it bubbled and surged. Then it was used metaphorically for speed and haste, applied to a matter in which there is no delay or respite. Thus, the meaning of "in this heat of theirs" (من فورهم هذا) is: in this very hour of theirs.

It appears that the actualization (مصداق) of the verse is the day of Badr, and it is a promise contingent upon a condition, which is what is contained in His saying: "if you are steadfast and God-conscious, and they come at you in this heat of theirs" (إِنْ تَصْبِرُوا وَتَتَّقُوا وَيَأْتُوكُمْ مِنْ فَوْرِهِمْ هذا).

As for what appears from some exegetes—that it is a promise to send down angels if they were to come after this "heat" of theirs, meaning the day of Badr, such that the meaning of "in this heat of theirs" (مِنْ فَوْرِهِمْ هذا) refers to the time of the day of Badr and not on the day of Badr—and similarly what appears from others—that it is a promise to send them down in other battles after Badr, such as Uhud, Hunayn, and the Trench—there is no evidence for this in the wording of the verse.

As for the day of Uhud, there is no basis for inferring the descent of angels from the verses, as is apparent. As for the day of the Trench and the day of Hunayn, although the Quran explicitly mentions the descent of angels in them—for He said in the story of the Trench: "when there came against you hosts, and We sent against them a gale and hosts you did not see" (إِذْ جاءَتْكُمْ جُنُودٌ فَأَرْسَلْنا عَلَيْهِمْ رِيحاً وَجُنُوداً لَمْ تَرَوْها) [Qarai: 33:9], and He said: "and on the day of Hunayn" (وَيَوْمَ حُنَيْنٍ) until He said: "and He sent down hosts you did not see" (وَأَنْزَلَ جُنُوداً لَمْ تَرَوْها) [Qarai: 9:26]—the wording of this verse: "Yes, if you are steadfast and God-conscious, and they come at you in this heat of theirs" (بَلى إِنْ تَصْبِرُوا وَتَتَّقُوا وَيَأْتُوكُمْ مِنْ فَوْرِهِمْ هذا) falls short of conveying a general promise.

As for the descent of three thousand on the day of Badr, it does not contradict His saying, the Exalted, in Surah al-Anfal: "whereupon He answered you, ‘I will aid you with a thousand angels in succession’" (فاسْتَجابَ لَكُمْ أَنِّي مُمِدُّكُمْ بِأَلْفٍ مِنَ الْمَلائِكَةِ مُرْدِفِينَ) [Qarai: 8:9], because of His saying: "in succession" (مُرْدِفِينَ), meaning following others, which are the remaining two thousand that complete the number mentioned in these verses.

His saying, the Exalted: "Allah did not ordain it but as good news for you" (وَما جَعَلَهُ اللهُ إِلَّا بُشْرى لَكُمْ) [Qarai: 3:126]. The pronoun refers to the reinforcement (الإمداد). The word "from" (عند) is an adverb (ظرف) conveying the meaning of presence (الحضور). It was originally used for spatial proximity and presence specific to physical bodies, then its use was expanded to temporal proximity, and then to absolute spiritual proximity and presence in whatever form. It has been used in the Quran in various ways.

What it conveys in this context—namely His saying: "and help comes only from Allah, the All-mighty, the All-wise" (وَمَا النَّصْرُ إِلَّا مِنْ عِنْدِ اللهِ الْعَزِيزِ الْحَكِيمِ)—considering what preceded it in His saying: "Allah did not ordain it but as good news for you and that your hearts might be at rest with it" (وَما جَعَلَهُ اللهُ إِلَّا بُشْرى لَكُمْ وَلِتَطْمَئِنَّ قُلُوبُكُمْ بِهِ), is the Divine Station (المقام الربوبي) to which every matter and decree returns, and for which no cause is sufficient or independent without Him. Thus, the meaning is: the reinforcing angels have no part in the matter of victory; rather, they are outward causes (أسباب ظاهرية) that bring you good news and tranquility of heart. The reality of victory is only from Allah, the Glorified, and nothing can substitute for Him. He is Allah to Whom every matter returns, the All-Mighty (العزيز) Who is never overcome, the All-Wise (الحكيم) Who is never ignorant.

His saying, the Exalted: "that He may cut off a part of the faithless or abase them" (لِيَقْطَعَ طَرَفاً مِنَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا أَوْ يَكْبِتَهُمْ) [Qarai: 3:127] to the end of the verses. The "that" (اللام) is related to His saying: "Allah certainly helped you" (وَلَقَدْ نَصَرَكُمُ اللهُ). "Cutting off a part" (قطع الطرف) is a metaphor for reducing their number and weakening their strength through killing and capture, as occurred on the day of Badr, where seventy of the polytheists were killed and seventy were captured. "Abasement" (الكبت) is humiliation and enragement.

His saying: "You have no hand in the matter" (لَيْسَ لَكَ مِنَ الْأَمْرِ شَيْءٌ) [Qarai: 3:128] is a parenthetical clause (معترضة). Its benefit is to clarify that the matter of "cutting off" and "abasing" belongs to Allah, and the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) has no hand in it such that they should praise him and approve of his management when they triumph over their enemy and gain from them, or blame and reproach him when the tide turns against them and they lose heart and grieve, as happened with them on the day of Uhud, as narrated by Allah the Exalted.

His saying: "or He may turn to them" (أَوْ يَتُوبَ عَلَيْهِمْ) is coordinated (معطوف) with His saying: "that He may cut off" (لِيَقْطَعَ), and the speech is continuous. His saying: "To Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth" (وَلِلَّهِ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَما فِي الْأَرْضِ) [Qarai: 3:129] is a clarification that the matter of repentance (التوبة) and forgiveness (المغفرة) returns to Allah. The meaning is: this precise management from Him, the Exalted, is only to cut off a part of the polytheists through killing and capture, or to humiliate them and frustrate them in their efforts, or to turn to them in mercy, or to punish them. As for the "cutting off" and "abasing," it is because the matter belongs to Him, not to you, such that you should be praised or blamed. As for the "repentance" and "punishment," it is because Allah is the Owner of everything; thus, He forgives whomever He wishes and punishes whomever He wishes. Nevertheless, His forgiveness and mercy precede His punishment and wrath, for He is the All-Forgiving, the Most Merciful.

We have taken His saying: "To Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth" (وَلِلَّهِ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَما فِي الْأَرْضِ) as the rationale (التعليل) for the last two phrases—namely His saying: "or He may turn to them" (أَوْ يَتُوبَ) etc.—because the conclusion of the verse specifically clarifies them, namely His saying: "He forgives whomever He wishes and punishes whomever He wishes" (يَغْفِرُ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ وَيُعَذِّبُ مَنْ يَشاءُ).

The exegetes have mentioned other views regarding the connection of His saying: "that He may cut off a part" (لِيَقْطَعَ طَرَفاً), and the meaning of the coordination in His saying: "or He may turn to them or punish them" (أَوْ يَتُوبَ عَلَيْهِمْ أَوْ يُعَذِّبَهُمْ), as well as what is rationalized by His saying: "You have no hand in the matter" (لَيْسَ لَكَ مِنَ الْأَمْرِ شَيْءٌ), and what is rationalized by His saying: "To Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth" (وَلِلَّهِ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَما فِي الْأَرْضِ). We have refrained from addressing or discussing them due to their limited benefit, as they contradict what the apparent context of the verses conveys. Whoever wishes to examine them should refer to the extensive commentaries.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Majma': Narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) that he said: "The cause of the Battle of Uhud was that when Quraysh returned from Badr to Mecca—having suffered what they suffered of killing and capture, for seventy of them were killed and seventy were captured—Abu Sufyan said: 'O people of Quraysh, do not let your women weep for your slain, for if the tear comes out, it removes grief and enmity toward Muhammad.' When they set out to fight the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) on the day of Uhud, they permitted their women to weep and wail. They went out from Mecca with three thousand horsemen and two thousand footmen, and they took the women with them.

When the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) heard of that, he gathered his companions and urged them to Jihad (الجهاد) — the struggle in the way of Allah. Abdullah ibn Ubayy ibn Salul said: 'O Messenger of Allah, do not go out from Medina so that we may fight in its alleys; then the weak man, the woman, the male slave, and the female slave can fight at the entrances of the streets and on the rooftops. No people have ever intended [to attack] us and triumphed over us while we were in our fortresses and houses, and we have never gone out to an enemy of ours but that victory was theirs over us.'

Then Sa'd ibn Mu'adh and others from the Aws stood up and said: 'O Messenger of Allah, none of the Arabs dared to covet [attacking] us while we were polytheists (مشركون) worshipping idols; how then can they covet us while you are among us? No, rather we shall go out to them and fight them; whoever among us is killed will be a martyr (شهيد), and whoever among us survives will have struggled in the way of Allah.'

So the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) accepted their view and went out with a group of his companions to prepare the place of battle—as He, the Exalted, said: 'And when you left your family at dawn...' (وَإِذْ غَدَوْتَ مِنْ أَهْلِكَ) the verse. Abdullah ibn Ubayy ibn Salul stayed behind, as did a group from the Khazraj who followed his view.

Quraysh arrived at Uhud. The Messenger of Allah had arrayed his companions, and they were seven hundred men. He placed Abdullah ibn Jubayr in charge of fifty archers (الرماة) at the mouth of the mountain pass (الشعب), fearing that their ambush might come from that place. He said to Abdullah ibn Jubayr and his companions: 'If you see us having defeated them until we have driven them into Mecca, do not leave this place; and if you see them having defeated us until they have driven us into Medina, do not leave and stick to your positions.'

Abu Sufyan had placed Khalid ibn al-Walid with two hundred horsemen in ambush, saying: 'When you see us intermingled [in battle], then come out against them from this pass so that you are behind them.'

The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) arrayed his companions and gave the banner (الراية) to the Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام). The Ansar (الأنصار) — the Helpers of Medina — charged the polytheists of Quraysh, and they were defeated in a disgraceful rout. The companions of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) began taking their spoils (سوادهم). Khalid ibn al-Walid descended with two hundred horsemen upon Abdullah ibn Jubayr, but they met them with arrows and he retreated. The companions of Abdullah ibn Jubayr looked at the companions of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) plundering the spoils of the people and said to Abdullah ibn Jubayr: 'Our companions have gained booty (غنم), shall we remain without booty?' Abdullah said to them: 'Fear Allah, for the Messenger of Allah commanded us not to leave.' They did not accept this from him and began to slip away, man by man, until they vacated their positions, and Abdullah ibn Jubayr remained with twelve men.

The banner of Quraysh was with Talhah ibn Abi Talhah al-Abdi—from the Banu Abd al-Dar—and Ali killed him. Then Abu Sa'id ibn Abi Talhah took the banner, and Ali killed him, and the banner fell. Then Musafi' ibn Abi Talhah took it, and Ali killed him—until he had killed nine men from the Banu Abd al-Dar—until their banner ended up with a black slave of theirs called Sawab. Ali reached him and cut off his right hand; he took the banner with his left, so Ali struck his left and cut it off. He then embraced it with his two stumps to his chest, then turned to Abu Sufyan and said: 'Have I fulfilled my duty for the Banu Abd al-Dar?' Ali struck him on his head and killed him. The banner fell, and Ghamrah bint Alqamah al-Kinaniyyah took it and raised it.

Khalid ibn al-Walid descended upon Abdullah ibn Jubayr—whose companions had fled, leaving him with a few men—and killed them at the mouth of the pass, then came upon the Muslims from their rear. Quraysh, in their defeat, saw the banner had been raised and rallied to it. The companions of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) were defeated in a great rout and began ascending the mountains and fleeing in every direction.

When the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) saw the defeat, he uncovered the helmet (البيضة) from his head and said: 'To me! I am the Messenger of Allah! Where are you fleeing from Allah and from His Messenger?' Hind bint Utbah was in the middle of the army; whenever a man from Quraysh fled, she would hand him a kohl-needle and a kohl-container and say: 'You are but a woman, so apply kohl with this.'

Hamzah ibn Abd al-Muttalib was charging the people; whenever they saw him, they fled, and no one stood firm against him. Hind had given a promise to Wahshi: 'If you kill Muhammad, or Ali, or Hamzah, I will give you such-and-such.' Wahshi was an Abyssinian slave belonging to Jubayr ibn Mut'im. Wahshi said: 'As for Muhammad, I could not get to him. As for Ali, I saw him cautious and looking around much, so there was no hope in him. So I lay in wait for Hamzah and saw him mowing down the people. He passed by me and stepped on the edge of a riverbank and fell. I took my spear, shook it, and threw it at him; it hit him in his flank and came out from his groin. He fell, so I went to him, split open his belly, took his liver, and brought it to Hind. I said: "This is the liver of Hamzah." She took it in her mouth and chewed it, but Allah made it in her mouth like a kneecap (الداعضة)—which is the bone at the head of the knee—so she spat it out and threw it away.' The Messenger of Allah (عليه السلام) said: 'Allah sent an angel who carried it and returned it to its place.' He said: 'She then went to him and cut off his genitals, his ears, his hand, and his foot.' No one remained with the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) except Abu Dujanah Simak ibn Kharashah and Ali. Whenever a group charged the Messenger of Allah, Ali met them and repelled them from him until his sword broke. Then the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) gave him his sword, Dhu al-Fiqar. The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) withdrew to a side of Uhud and stood there. Ali (عليه السلام) continued to fight them until he was struck in his head, face, body, belly, and legs with seventy wounds." Thus it was narrated by Ali ibn Ibrahim in his Tafsir. "Then Gabriel said: 'This is indeed true consolation (المواساة), O Muhammad.' Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'He is from me and I am from him.' Gabriel said: 'And I am from both of you.'

Abu Abdillah said: 'The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) looked at Gabriel between the heaven and the earth on a throne of gold, saying: لا سيف إلا ذو الفقار ولا فتى إلا علي — "There is no sword but Dhu al-Fiqar, and no youth but Ali."'

In the narration of al-Qummi: "Nusaybah bint Ka'b al-Maziniyyah remained with the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). She used to go out with the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in his battles to treat the wounded. Her son was with her, and he wanted to flee and retreat, so she charged him and said: 'O my son, where are you fleeing from Allah and from His Messenger?' She sent him back, and a man charged him and killed him. She took her son's sword, charged the man, struck him on his thigh, and killed him. The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'May Allah bless you, O Nusaybah.' She was shielding the Messenger of Allah with her chest and breasts until she suffered many wounds.

Ibn Qami'ah charged the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and said: 'Show me Muhammad! May I not be saved if he is saved!' He struck him on the vein of his shoulder and called out: 'I have killed Muhammad, by al-Lat and al-Uzza!'"

I say: There are other narrations in the story that may differ from this narration in some of its details.

Among them: what is in this narration that the number of polytheists was five thousand, whereas most narrations state they were three thousand men.

Among them: what is in it that Ali (عليه السلام) killed the banner-bearers, and they were nine. Other narrations agree with this; Ibn al-Athir narrated it in al-Kamil from Abu Rafi'. The rest of the narrations attribute the killing of some of them to others besides him (عليه السلام). Reflection on the story supports what is in this narration.

Among them: what is in it that Hind gave a promise to Wahshi regarding the killing of Hamzah, whereas what the Sunnis narrated is that the one who gave him the promise was his master Jubayr ibn Mut'im, promising him manumission upon that condition. His bringing the liver of Hamzah to Hind rather than Jubayr supports what is in this narration.

Among them: what is in it that all the Muslims scattered from the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) except Ali and Abu Dujanah, which is what the narrations agree upon. In some of them, others are mentioned, such that those who stood firm with the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) are numbered up to thirty men. However, these narrations negate one another. You must reflect on the core of the story and the clues that clarify the circumstances until the truth becomes clear to you, for these stories and narrations have witnessed positions of agreement and disagreement and passed through bright and dark atmospheres until they reached us.

Among them: what is in it that Allah sent an angel who carried the liver of Hamzah and returned it to its place. This is not in most narrations. In some of them, as in al-Durr al-Manthur from Ibn Abi Shaybah, Ahmad, and Ibn al-Mundhir from Ibn Mas'ud in a hadith, he said: "Then Abu Sufyan said: 'There has been mutilation (مثلة) among the people; although it was not from our leaders, I neither commanded it nor forbade it, neither liked it nor disliked it, and it neither saddened me nor pleased me.' He said: 'They looked and found that Hamzah's belly had been ripped open, and Hind had taken his liver and chewed it but could not eat it.' The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'Did she eat anything?' They said: 'No.' He said: 'Allah would not enter anything of Hamzah into the Fire.'" [The hadith].

In the narrations of our companions and others: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was struck that day with a wound (شجة) on his forehead, his incisor (رباعيته) was broken, and his front tooth (ثنيته) was injured." Narrated by Mughirah.

In al-Durr al-Manthur: Ibn Ishaq, Abd ibn Humayd, Ibn Jarir, and Ibn al-Mundhir narrated from Ibn Shihab, Muhammad ibn Yahya ibn Hayyan, Asim ibn Amr ibn Qatadah, al-Husayn ibn Abd al-Rahman ibn Amr ibn Sa'd ibn Mu'adh, and others, each of whom narrated part of the account of the day of Uhud.

They said: "When Quraysh, or those of the disbelievers of Quraysh who were afflicted, suffered what they did on the day of Badr and their remnants returned to Mecca, and Abu Sufyan returned with his caravan, Abdullah ibn Abi Rabi'ah, Ikrimah ibn Abi Jahl, and Safwan ibn Umayyah walked among men of Quraysh whose fathers, sons, and brothers had been afflicted at Badr. They spoke to Abu Sufyan ibn Harb and those of Quraysh who had trade in that caravan, saying: 'O people of Quraysh, Muhammad has wronged you and killed your best men, so help us with this wealth to fight him, that we may perhaps take revenge for those he afflicted.' They did so. Quraysh then gathered to fight the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and went out with their full strength and equipment. They went out with women (الظعن) to incite protective zeal and so they would not flee. Abu Sufyan went out as the leader of the people. They approached until they camped at Aynayn, a mountain in the belly of al-Sabkhah from Qanat on the edge of the valley toward Medina.

When the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and the Muslims heard of the polytheists having camped where they did, the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'I saw [in a dream] cows being slaughtered, and I saw a notch in the tip of my sword, and I saw that I had inserted my hand into a sturdy coat of mail, which I interpreted as Medina. If you think it best to stay in Medina and leave them where they have camped—for if they stay, they stay in an evil place, and if they enter upon us, we will fight them therein.'

Quraysh camped at their position at Uhud on Wednesday. They stayed that day, Thursday, and Friday. The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) set out after he prayed Friday prayer and reached the pass of Uhud in the morning. They met on Saturday, the middle of Shawwal, in the third year [of the Hijrah].

The view of Abdullah ibn Ubayy was with the view of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم); he held the same view that they should not go out to them. The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) disliked going out of Medina. However, men from the Muslims—among those whom Allah honored with martyrdom on the day of Uhud and others who had missed the day of Badr and its attendance—said: 'O Messenger of Allah, lead us out to our enemies so they do not think we have become cowardly or weak before them.' Abdullah ibn Ubayy said: 'O Messenger of Allah, stay in Medina and do not go out to them. By Allah, we have never gone out from it to an enemy of ours but that they afflicted us, and they have never entered it upon us but that we afflicted them. So leave them, O Messenger of Allah; if they stay, they stay in an evil state, and if they enter, the women, children, and men will fight them with stones from above them. If they return, they return disappointed as they came.' The people who desired to meet the enemy continued to urge the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) until he entered and put on his armor (لأمته). That was on Friday when he finished the prayer. Then he came out to them, and the people had regretted [their insistence] and said: 'We have coerced the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and we should not have done that. If you wish, then stay.' The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'It is not fitting for a prophet, once he has put on his armor, to take it off until he fights.'

The Messenger of Allah went out with a thousand men of his companions until they were at al-Shawt between Medina and Uhud. Abdullah ibn Ubayy turned back with a third of the people. The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) continued until he passed through the volcanic tract (حرة) of Banu Harithah. A horse flicked its tail and hit the tip of his sword, so he unsheathed it. The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)—who loved good omens (الفال) and did not practice augury (يعتاف)—said to the owner of the sword: 'Sheathe your sword, for I see that swords will be unsheathed today.' The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) continued until he camped at the pass of Uhud on the side of the valley toward the mountain. He placed his back and his camp toward Uhud. The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) arrayed for battle with seven hundred men.

The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) appointed Abdullah ibn Jubayr over the archers—who were fifty men—and said: 'Repel the mountain from us with arrows so they do not come from behind us. Whether it is against us or for us, stay in your place, for we shall only be approached from your direction.' The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) wore two coats of mail, one over the other."

Also in al-Durr al-Manthur, from Ibn Jarir from al-Suddi in a hadith: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) went out to Uhud with a thousand men, and he had promised them victory if they were steadfast. Abdullah ibn Ubayy returned with three hundred. Abu Jabir al-Salami followed them, calling them back, but they refused him and said to him: 'We do not know of any fighting, and if you obey us, you will return with us.'

And He said: 'When two groups among you were about to lose heart' (إِذْ هَمَّتْ طائِفَتانِ مِنْكُمْ أَنْ تَفْشَلا). They were the Banu Salimah and the Banu Harithah. They were about to return when Abdullah ibn Ubayy returned, but Allah protected them. The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) remained with seven hundred."

I say: Banu Salimah and Banu Harithah are two clans of the Ansar; Banu Salimah are from the Khazraj and Banu Harithah are from the Aws.

In al-Majma': Ibn Abi Ishaq, al-Suddi, al-Waqidi, Ibn Jarir, and others narrated and said: "The polytheists camped at Uhud on Wednesday in Shawwal of the third year of the Hijrah. The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) went out to them on Friday, and the fighting was on Saturday, the middle of the month. The incisor of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was broken and his face was wounded. Then the Muhajirun (المهاجرون) — the Emigrants from Mecca — and the Ansar returned after the defeat. Seventy of the Muslims had been killed. The Messenger of Allah pressed on with those with him until he drove them back. The polytheists had mutilated a group, and Hamzah suffered the greatest mutilation."

I say: The narrations regarding the story of Uhud are very numerous. We have only narrated among them, in what preceded and what follows, the small portion upon which the understanding of the meanings of the verses revealed concerning it depends. The verses regarding the story are of several types:

Among them: those that address the failure of those who failed among the people, and their dispute, or their being about to lose heart that day.

Among them: those revealed with a tone of reprimand and blame for those who were defeated and scattered from the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) when Allah had forbidden that for them.

Among them: those that contain praise for those who were martyred before the people were defeated, and those who stood firm, did not flee, and fought until they were killed.

Among them: those that include beautiful praise for those who stood firm until the end of the battle, fought, and were not killed.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَأْكُلُوا۟ ٱلرِّبَوٰٓا۟ أَضْعَـٰفًا مُّضَـٰعَفَةً ۖ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ (١٣٠)
O you who have faith! Do not exact usury, twofold and severalfold, and be wary of Allah so that you may be felicitous. (130)
وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱلنَّارَ ٱلَّتِىٓ أُعِدَّتْ لِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (١٣١)
Beware of the Fire, which has been prepared for the faithless, (131)
وَأَطِيعُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱلرَّسُولَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ (١٣٢)
and obey Allah and the Apostle so that you may be granted [His] mercy. (132)
۞ وَسَارِعُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ مَغْفِرَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ وَجَنَّةٍ عَرْضُهَا ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتُ وَٱلْأَرْضُ أُعِدَّتْ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ (١٣٣)
Hasten towards your Lord’s forgiveness and a paradise as vast as the heavens and the earth, prepared for the Godwary (133)
ٱلَّذِينَ يُنفِقُونَ فِى ٱلسَّرَّآءِ وَٱلضَّرَّآءِ وَٱلْكَـٰظِمِينَ ٱلْغَيْظَ وَٱلْعَافِينَ عَنِ ٱلنَّاسِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (١٣٤)
—those who spend in ease and adversity, and suppress their anger, and excuse [the faults of] the people, and Allah loves the virtuous; (134)
وَٱلَّذِينَ إِذَا فَعَلُوا۟ فَـٰحِشَةً أَوْ ظَلَمُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ ذَكَرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ فَٱسْتَغْفَرُوا۟ لِذُنُوبِهِمْ وَمَن يَغْفِرُ ٱلذُّنُوبَ إِلَّا ٱللَّهُ وَلَمْ يُصِرُّوا۟ عَلَىٰ مَا فَعَلُوا۟ وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ (١٣٥)
and those who, when they commit an indecent act or wrong themselves, remember Allah, and plead [to Allah seeking His] forgiveness for their sins—and who forgives sins except Allah?—and who knowingly do not persist in what [sins] they have committed. (135)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ جَزَآؤُهُم مَّغْفِرَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّهِمْ وَجَنَّـٰتٌ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا ۚ وَنِعْمَ أَجْرُ ٱلْعَـٰمِلِينَ (١٣٦)
Their reward is forgiveness from their Lord, and gardens with streams running in them, to remain in them [forever]. How excellent is the reward of the workers! (136)
قَدْ خَلَتْ مِن قَبْلِكُمْ سُنَنٌ فَسِيرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَٱنظُرُوا۟ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلْمُكَذِّبِينَ (١٣٧)
Certain [Divine] precedents have passed before you. So travel through the land and observe how was the fate of the deniers. (137)
هَـٰذَا بَيَانٌ لِّلنَّاسِ وَهُدًى وَمَوْعِظَةٌ لِّلْمُتَّقِينَ (١٣٨)
This is an explanation for mankind, and a guidance and advice for the Godwary. (138)

(Commentary)

These are verses calling toward goodness and restraining from evil and wickedness. Despite this, they do not lose their connection with the preceding and succeeding verses that explain the account of the Battle of Uhud (غزوة أحد) and describe the ill states and blameworthy traits present among the believers on that day—traits with which Allah, the Glorified, is not pleased. These traits were the cause of the frailty (الوهن), weakness, and disobedience to Allah and His Messenger that crept into them. Thus, these verses are a continuation of the verses revealed concerning the Battle of Uhud.

Furthermore, they guide them toward that which will secure them from falling into these destructive entanglements and ruinous obstacles, calling them to godwariness (تقوى الله) — the divine leading toward truth and the straight path — trust in Him, and steadfastness in obeying the Messenger. These nine verses specifically contain both encouragement and warning. They encourage the believers to hasten toward goodness, which is spending in the way of Allah in ease and adversity, restraining anger, and pardoning people; all of which are encompassed by the spreading of excellence (الإحسان) and goodness in society, patience in enduring harm and evil, and overlooking ill-treatment in the face of offense. This is the only way by which the life of society is preserved and its strength is fortified so that it may stand firm. Among the requirements of this spending and excellence is the abandonment of usury (الربا), which is why He began with it; it serves as a preamble to the call toward excellence and spending. It has already been mentioned in the verses of spending and usury in Surah al-Baqarah that spending in all its forms is among the greatest pillars upon which the structure of society relies, and that it breathes the spirit of unity into human society, thereby uniting its scattered forces so that it attains happiness in life and gains the strength to repel every destructive or harmful affliction that targets it. Conversely, usury is among the greatest things that oppose spending in this specific characteristic.

This is what Allah encourages them toward. Then He encourages them not to be severed from their Lord by the severing ties of sins and acts of disobedience. If they commit that which does not please their Lord, they should rectify it through repentance (التوبة) and returning to Him a second and third time without becoming sluggish or negligent. Through these two matters, their journey upon the path of a happy life remains straight, so they neither stray nor halt and perish.

This explanation, as you see, is the best way by which a human being is guided to perfect his soul after the appearance of deficiency, and the finest path in treating psychological vices (الرذائل النفسانية) that may creep into souls adorned with virtues, causing degradation and fall, and threatening them with destruction and ruin.

(The Teaching of the Quran and the Pairing of Knowledge with Action)

This is the custom of the Quran in its divine teaching; throughout the period of its revelation—which was twenty-three years—it consistently established primary materials for its universal teachings (كليات تعاليمه). When any of these were acted upon, the form of the actual deed was taken as material for teaching them a second time. He would then present it to them after rectifying the corrupt parts of its composition with the remaining sound parts, censuring the corrupt, praising the sound and upright, and offering a beautiful promise and abundant gratitude to the doer. Thus, the Mighty Book of Allah is a book of knowledge (علم) and action (عمل), not a book of mere hypothesis and estimation, nor a book of obfuscation and blind imitation (تقليد).

Its likeness is that of a teacher who imparts universal scientific principles (الكليات العلمية) to his students in the briefest explanation and shortest phrasing, commanding them to act upon them. Then he takes what they have performed and analyzes it into its primary components—the sound and the corrupt—explaining to them the instances of deficiency and shortcoming, accompanied by exhortation and warning. He praises the instances of uprightness and correctness, pairing them with promise and gratitude, and commands them to act a second time. This is his method until they become perfected in their craft and succeed in their endeavor.

This which we have mentioned is among the Quranic truths that appear to the meticulous contemplator at first glance. You see Him, the Glorified, revealing the universals (كليات) of Jihad, for example, in His verses for the first time: كُتِبَ عَلَيْكُمُ الْقِتالُ "Fighting is prescribed for you" (al-Baqarah: 216), and He commands the believers therein. Then He takes the account of Badr a second time and commands them with what He explains to them therein, then the account of Uhud, then another account, and so on. You see Him, the Glorified, narrating the stories of the predecessors among the Prophets and their nations, then making them—after rectifying them and explaining the aspect of truth therein—a lesson for the successors and a constitution for their conduct, and so forth. Among what has been revealed in these verses of this nature is His saying: فَسِيرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ "So travel through the land," and His saying: وَكَأَيِّنْ مِنْ نَبِيٍ "How many a prophet..."

His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَأْكُلُوا الرِّبَوا "O you who have faith! Do not exact usury" until the end of the three verses. The reason for using the term "eating" (الأكل) while intending "taking" (الأخذ) has passed previously. His saying: أَضْعافاً مُضاعَفَةً "doubled and multiplied" refers to the dominant characteristic of usury, for by its nature it multiplies, such that the wealth becomes doubled and multiplied by exhausting the wealth of others and adding it to the usurious capital.

In His saying: وَاتَّقُوا النَّارَ الَّتِي أُعِدَّتْ لِلْكافِرِينَ "and beware of the Fire which has been prepared for the faithless," there is an allusion to the disbelief (كفر) of the consumer of usury, as mentioned in Surah al-Baqarah in the verses of usury: وَاللهُ لا يُحِبُّ كُلَّ كَفَّارٍ أَثِيمٍ "and Allah does not like any sinful ingrate" (al-Baqarah: 276).

His saying, the Exalted: سارِعُوا إِلى مَغْفِرَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ وَجَنَّةٍ "Hasten towards your Lord’s forgiveness and a paradise." Hastening (المسارعة) is the intensification of speed; it is praised in good deeds and censured in evil ones.

In the Noble Quran, forgiveness (المغفرة) is paired with Paradise (الجنة) in most instances. This is only because Paradise is an abode of purity (طهارة) where the filth of acts of disobedience and sins and their stains do not enter; nor does anyone defiled by them enter except after forgiveness and removal.

The forgiveness and Paradise mentioned in this verse correspond to what is in the following two verses. As for forgiveness, it corresponds to His saying: وَالَّذِينَ إِذا فَعَلُوا فاحِشَةً "and those who, when they commit an indecency," and as for Paradise, it corresponds to His saying: الَّذِينَ يُنْفِقُونَ فِي السَّرَّاءِ وَالضَّرَّاءِ "those who spend in ease and adversity."

As for His saying: جَنَّةٍ عَرْضُهَا السَّماواتُ وَالْأَرْضُ "a paradise whose width is [as] the heavens and the earth," what is meant by "width" (العرض) is vastness (السعة), which is a common usage. The expression was a metaphor for its reaching the ultimate limit in vastness, or that which human imagination cannot bound. It has another meaning that we shall point to in the coming discussion of narrations.

His saying: أُعِدَّتْ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ "prepared for the God-conscious" is like a preamble to mentioning the attributes of the God-conscious (المتقين) — those who possess godwariness and protect themselves from Allah's punishment by fulfilling duties and avoiding sins — that He mentions afterward. The purpose is to explain the attributes related to the state of the believers in this context—meaning at the time of the revelation of these verses. They were revealed after the Battle of Uhud, following the weakness, frailty, and opposition that occurred to them and from them. Despite this, they were on the verge of other similar battles and resembling events, and they were in need of unity, agreement, and harmony.

His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِينَ يُنْفِقُونَ فِي السَّرَّاءِ وَالضَّرَّاءِ "those who spend in ease and adversity" until the end of the verse. Ease and adversity (السراء والضراء) are what pleases a person and what grieves him, or prosperity and hardship. Restraining (الكظم) originally means tying the neck of a water-skin after filling it; it was then used metaphorically for a person when he is filled with grief or rage. Rage (الغيظ) is the agitation of the nature toward revenge upon witnessing an abundance of what one does not approve of, unlike anger (الغضب), which is the will for revenge or punishment. Therefore, it is said: "the anger of Allah," but it is not said: "He became enraged (اغتاظ)."

In His saying: وَاللهُ يُحِبُّ الْمُحْسِنِينَ "and Allah loves the virtuous," there is an indication that the attributes He mentioned define them. It only defines the virtuous (المحسنين) — those who attain the station of excellence (إحسان) by worshipping Allah as if they see Him and doing good to His creation — in relation to people through doing good to them. As for their relation to Allah, their definition is found in His saying, the Exalted: وَبُشْرى لِلْمُحْسِنِينَ إِنَّ الَّذِينَ قالُوا رَبُّنَا اللهُ ثُمَّ اسْتَقامُوا فَلا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ "and a binary for the virtuous. Indeed those who say, ‘Our Lord is Allah,’ and then remain steadfast, no fear shall be on them, nor shall they grieve" (al-Ahqaf: 12-13). Rather, this excellence mentioned in these verses is the source for that mentioned in His saying: الَّذِينَ يُنْفِقُونَ فِي السَّرَّاءِ وَالضَّرَّاءِ "those who spend in ease and adversity," for spending and its like, if not for the sake of Allah, have no status with Allah, the Glorified, as indicated by His saying in the preceding verses: مَثَلُ ما يُنْفِقُونَ فِي هذِهِ الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا "The parable of what they spend in the life of this world" (Al Imran: 117) and others.

Evidence for what we have mentioned is His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ جاهَدُوا فِينا لَنَهْدِيَنَّهُمْ سُبُلَنا وَإِنَّ اللهَ لَمَعَ الْمُحْسِنِينَ "As for those who strive in Us, We shall surely guide them in Our ways, and Allah is indeed with the virtuous" (al-Ankabut: 69). This striving (الجهاد) is the exertion of effort and only occurs in what opposes the desire of the soul and the requirement of nature. It only occurs if they possess faith in matters that necessitate acting according to their requirements and remaining steadfast upon them in resistance against what the human nature loves and the soul desires. Its necessary consequence in terms of speech and belief is that they say "Our Lord is Allah" and remain upright upon it. In terms of action, it is that they establish this speech through striving in the worship of Allah between them and Allah, and through spending and good conduct between them and people. It follows from what we have mentioned that excellence (الإحسان) is performing deeds in a beautiful manner (الوجه الحسن) by way of uprightness and steadfastness upon faith in Allah, the Glorified.

His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِينَ إِذا فَعَلُوا فاحِشَةً أَوْ ظَلَمُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ "and those who, when they commit an indecency or wrong themselves" until His saying: وَنِعْمَ أَجْرُ الْعامِلِينَ "and excellent is the reward of those who act!" Indecency (الفاحشة) is that which includes foulness and ugliness among deeds; its usage became common for adultery (الزنا). Thus, what is meant by "wronging" (الظلم), by the evidence of contrast, is all other sins, major and minor, or specifically minor sins if "indecency" is intended to mean the abominable among sins, which are the major ones. In His saying: ذَكَرُوا اللهَ "remember Allah" etc., there is evidence that the criterion in seeking forgiveness (الاستغفار) is that the remembrance of Allah, the Exalted, should prompt it, rather than mere verbalization out of habit and the like. His saying: وَمَنْ يَغْفِرُ الذُّنُوبَ إِلَّا اللهُ "and who forgives sins except Allah?" is an encouragement and an awakening of the innate disposition toward seeking refuge and sanctuary in the human being.

His saying: وَلَمْ يُصِرُّوا عَلى ما فَعَلُوا وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ "and do not persist in what they have committed while they know." He only conditioned seeking forgiveness with this because persistence (الإصرار) produces a state in the soul with which the remembrance of the station of the Lord, the Exalted, is of no benefit; that state is the belittling of Allah's command, indifference toward violating His sanctities, and arrogance toward Him, the Exalted. With it, no servitude (عبودية) remains and no remembrance benefits. For this very reason, He conditioned it with His saying: وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ "while they know." This is an indication that "wronging" (الظلم) at the beginning of the verse also includes minor sins. This is because persistence in a sin necessitates belittling Allah's command and disparaging His station, whether the mentioned sin is minor or major. Thus, His saying: ما فَعَلُوا "what they have committed" is more general than major sins; what is meant by "what they have committed" is what was mentioned at the beginning of the verse. Since a minor sin is not an "indecency," it is inevitably the "wronging of the soul."

His saying: أُولئِكَ جَزاؤُهُمْ مَغْفِرَةٌ "Their reward is forgiveness" is an explanation of their abundant reward. What He, the Exalted, mentioned in this verse is the very thing He commanded hastening toward in His saying: وَسارِعُوا إِلى مَغْفِرَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ وَجَنَّةٍ "Hasten towards your Lord’s forgiveness and a paradise" etc. From this, it is known that the command was to hasten toward spending, restraining anger, pardoning people, and seeking forgiveness.

His saying, the Exalted: قَدْ خَلَتْ مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ سُنَنٌ فَسِيرُوا "Certain customs have passed before you; so travel." Customs (السنن) is the plural of sunnah, which is the path followed in society. The command to travel through the land is for the sake of taking heed (الاعتبار) from the remnants of past nations, kings, and tyrannical Pharaohs, where their lofty palaces, stored treasures, thrones, and throngs did not benefit them. Allah has made them accounts from which the mindful take heed and the heedless take amusement.

As for preserving their remnants, guarding their statues, and striving to uncover their outward worldly greatness and glory during their days, it is something the Quran does not care for. Indeed, that is but idolatry (الوثنية) which continues to appear in different guises at every moment. We shall discuss this meaning, if Allah wills, in an independent inquiry in which we analyze the meaning of idolatry.

His saying, the Exalted: هذا بَيانٌ لِلنَّاسِ "This is an explanation for mankind" etc. The division is based on the effect; thus, it is a proclamation and clarification for some, and guidance and advice for others.

(Discussion of Narrations)

In al-Majma', regarding His saying, the Exalted: جَنَّةٍ عَرْضُهَا السَّماواتُ وَالْأَرْضُ (a paradise whose width is [as] the heavens and the earth): It is narrated from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله that he was asked: "If Paradise has a width of the heavens and the earth, then where would the Fire be?" He صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله replied: "Glory be to Allah! When the day comes, where is the night?"

I say: It was also narrated by al-Suyuti in al-Durr al-Manthur from al-Tanukhi in a letter he brought from Heraclius to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله asking him about this verse, and he answered it with that. It was also narrated through another chain from Abu Hurayrah: that a man asked him about that and he answered with that.

As for the interpretation of his saying صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله that it means the Fire is in the knowledge of Allah, the Exalted—just as the night, upon the arrival of day, is in the knowledge of Allah, the Exalted—if it is intended that the Fire is not hidden from His knowledge, then it is known that this answer does not repel the objection. The question is about the place of the Fire, not about Allah's knowledge of it. If it is intended that it is possible for there to be another place beyond the heavens and the earth where the Fire is situated, then although this is not far-fetched in itself, the comparison of Paradise and the Fire to day and night would then be misplaced. For the night does not leave the ambit of the heavens and the earth upon the arrival of day. Thus, the truth is that this is an unsatisfactory interpretation.

I think the narration looks toward another meaning, the clarification of which is: The Hereafter (الآخرة), with its bliss and its hellfire, although it resembles the world (الدنيا) and its pleasures and pains—and likewise the human being therein is the very same human being who was in the world, as necessitated by the outward meanings of the Book and the Sunnah—nevertheless, the system (النظام) governing the Hereafter is different from the system governing the world. The Hereafter is an abode of eternity and permanence, while the world is an abode of cessation and annihilation. Therefore, the human being eats, drinks, marries, and enjoys in Paradise without being subject to what these acts are subject to in the world. Likewise, the human being is burned by the fire of Hell and endures pains and calamities in his food, drink, dwelling, and companion in the Fire, yet what happens to him with them while in the world does not happen to him there. He lives for eternity, and that does not affect him with middle age, gray hair, or senility, and so on. This is only because the aforementioned accidents and occurrences are among the requirements of the worldly system, not the absolute system that is more general than it and the otherworldly system. The world is the abode of mutual crowding (التزاحم) and mutual exclusion (التمانع), unlike the Hereafter.

Among the evidences for this is that what we find in the context of our observation of occurring events disappears from us when we observe something else afterward, such as the events of yesterday and the events of today, night and day, and so on. As for Allah, the Glorified, that which we observe first and then disappears from us does not disappear from Him, nor does that which we find after it, and there is no mutual crowding between them. Thus, night and day, as well as the events concurrent with them, are mutually crowding and mutually exclusive according to the system of matter and motion; yet they themselves do not crowd or exclude one another according to another system. This is derived from His saying, the Exalted: أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلى رَبِّكَ كَيْفَ مَدَّ الظِّلَّ وَلَوْ شاءَ لَجَعَلَهُ ساكِناً ثُمَّ جَعَلْنَا الشَّمْسَ عَلَيْهِ دَلِيلاً ثُمَّ قَبَضْناهُ إِلَيْنا قَبْضاً يَسِيراً "Have you not regarded your Lord, how He extends the shadow? Had He wished, He would have made it still. Then We made the sun a guide for it. Then We draw it in toward Ourselves gently" (al-Furqan: 45-46).

If that is possible in the case of night and day, which are mutually crowding, it is permissible for the heavens and the earth to encompass what equals them in vastness, and to encompass along with that something else equal to it in measure, such as Paradise and the Fire, for example—but not according to the system of this abode, but according to the system of the Hereafter. There are parallels to this in the reports, such as what has been narrated: "The grave is either a garden from the gardens of Paradise or a pit from the pits of the Fire," and what has been narrated: "The believer has his grave widened for him as far as his eye can see."

Accordingly, his saying صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: "Glory be to Allah! When the day comes, where is the night?" should be understood in this way. For it is evident that if the meaning were that Allah, the Glorified, is not ignorant of the night when He knows the day, it would not relate to the question. Likewise, if the meaning were that the night remains externally with the arrival of day, the questioner would object that the night is nullified with the existence of day when compared to a single location among the regions of the earth. If they are considered in themselves, the night is in reality a conical shadow resulting from the sun's illumination, and it revolves around the terrestrial globe according to the daily motion; thus, night and day are always traveling around the earth without nullification or identity.

The narration has parallels among the narrations, such as what was narrated in the interpretation of His saying, the Exalted: لِيَمِيزَ اللهُ الْخَبِيثَ مِنَ الطَّيِّبِ "so that Allah may separate the bad from the good" (al-Anfal: 37), in his saying عليه‌السلام: "When the sun sets, where does this ray spread upon the earth go?" the hadith. The discussion of it will come.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَالْكاظِمِينَ الْغَيْظَ وَالْعافِينَ عَنِ النَّاسِ (and those who restrain their anger and excuse the people) the verse:

Al-Bayhaqi narrated from Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه‌السلام: That a slave-girl began pouring water for him as he was preparing for prayer. The pitcher fell from her hand onto his face and bruised it. He raised his head toward her, and she said: "Indeed Allah says: وَالْكاظِمِينَ الْغَيْظَ (and those who restrain their anger)." He said: "I have restrained my anger." She said: "وَالْعافِينَ عَنِ النَّاسِ (and excuse the people)." He said: "May Allah excuse you." She said: "وَاللهُ يُحِبُّ الْمُحْسِنِينَ (and Allah loves the virtuous)." He said: "Go, for you are free."

I say: It is also narrated through Shi'i paths. The outward meaning of the narration is that he عليه‌السلام interprets excellence (الإحسان) as that which exceeds these attributes, and it is indeed so according to the generality of its concept; however, the attributes mentioned before it are among the requirements of its meaning, so it is possible to define excellence through them.

Know that there are very many narrations regarding good character and other noble ethics such as spending, restraining anger, pardoning, and the like, narrated from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه‌السلام, which we have deferred to another place more suitable for them.

In al-Majalis, from Abd al-Rahman ibn Ghanam al-Dawsi: That His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ إِذا فَعَلُوا فاحِشَةً (and those who, when they commit an indecency) etc., was revealed concerning Bahlul the Grave-robber. He used to dig up graves. He dug up the grave of one of the daughters of the Ansar, took her out, and removed her shrouds—she was fair and beautiful. Satan enticed him and he committed adultery with her, then he regretted it. He came to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, who turned him away. Then he withdrew from people and devoted himself to worship and asceticism in some of the mountains of Medina until Allah accepted his repentance and the Quran was revealed concerning him.

I say: The narration is detailed; we have quoted it in summary. If the narration were authentic, it would be another occasion of revelation (شأن النزول) for the verse, other than the single cause that encompasses the entirety of the verses of the account.

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Imam al-Baqir عليه‌السلام: Regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَلَمْ يُصِرُّوا عَلى ما فَعَلُوا (and do not persist in what they have committed) the verse—he said: "Persistence (الإصرار) is that a sinner commits a sin and does not seek Allah's forgiveness, nor does he speak to himself of repentance; that is persistence."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ahmad narrated from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, who said: "Iblis said: 'O Lord, by Your Might, I shall not cease to mislead the children of Adam as long as their souls are in their bodies.' Allah said: 'By My Might, I shall not cease to forgive them as long as they seek My forgiveness.'"

In al-Kafi, from Imam al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام: "There is no minor sin with persistence, and no major sin with seeking forgiveness."

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Imam al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام in a hadith, he said: "And in the Book of Allah is salvation from ruin, insight from blindness, and a healing for what is in the breasts—concerning what Allah has commanded you of seeking forgiveness and repentance. Allah said: وَالَّذِينَ إِذا فَعَلُوا فاحِشَةً أَوْ ظَلَمُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ ذَكَرُوا اللهَ فَاسْتَغْفَرُوا لِذُنُوبِهِمْ وَمَنْ يَغْفِرُ الذُّنُوبَ إِلَّا اللهُ وَلَمْ يُصِرُّوا عَلى ما فَعَلُوا وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ (and those who, when they commit an indecency or wrong themselves, remember Allah and plead for forgiveness for their sins—and who forgives sins except Allah?—and do not persist in what they have committed while they know), and He said: وَمَنْ يَعْمَلْ سُوءاً أَوْ يَظْلِمْ نَفْسَهُ ثُمَّ يَسْتَغْفِرِ اللهَ يَجِدِ اللهَ غَفُوراً رَحِيماً (Whoever commits evil or wrongs himself and then pleads to Allah for forgiveness, will find Allah all-forgiving, all-merciful). This is what Allah has commanded of seeking forgiveness, and He conditioned repentance and desisting from what Allah has forbidden with it; for He says: إِلَيْهِ يَصْعَدُ الْكَلِمُ الطَّيِّبُ وَالْعَمَلُ الصَّالِحُ يَرْفَعُهُ (To Him ascends the good word, and He elevates righteous deeds). By this verse, it is proven that seeking forgiveness is not elevated to Allah except by righteous deeds and repentance."

I say: He عليه‌السلام derived desisting and not returning after repentance from the negation of persistence. Likewise, the need of repentance and seeking forgiveness for righteous deeds afterward was derived from the generality of the "good word" in His saying: إِلَيْهِ يَصْعَدُ الْكَلِمُ الطَّيِّبُ (To Him ascends the good word) the verse.

In al-Majalis, from Imam al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام, he said: "When this verse was revealed: وَالَّذِينَ إِذا فَعَلُوا فاحِشَةً (and those who, when they commit an indecency), Iblis ascended a mountain in Mecca called Thawr. He cried out at the top of his voice to his demons, and they gathered to him. They said to him: 'O our master, why have you called us?' He said: 'This verse has been revealed; who is for it?' A demon from among the devils stood up and said: 'I am for it by such and such.' He said: 'You are not for it.' Another stood up and said the like of that. He said: 'You are not for it.' Then the Slinking Whisperer (الوسواس الخناس) said: 'I am for it.' He asked: 'By what?' He replied: 'I will make promises to them and arouse desires in them until they commit the sin. When they commit it, I will make them forget to seek forgiveness.' He said: 'You are for it,' and he put him in charge of it until the Day of Resurrection."

I say: The narration is also narrated through Sunni paths.

Ayahs

وَلَا تَهِنُوا۟ وَلَا تَحْزَنُوا۟ وَأَنتُمُ ٱلْأَعْلَوْنَ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ (١٣٩)
Do not weaken or grieve: you shall have the upper hand, should you be faithful. (139)
إِن يَمْسَسْكُمْ قَرْحٌ فَقَدْ مَسَّ ٱلْقَوْمَ قَرْحٌ مِّثْلُهُۥ ۚ وَتِلْكَ ٱلْأَيَّامُ نُدَاوِلُهَا بَيْنَ ٱلنَّاسِ وَلِيَعْلَمَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَيَتَّخِذَ مِنكُمْ شُهَدَآءَ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (١٤٠)
If wounds afflict you, like wounds have already afflicted those people; and We make such vicissitudes rotate among mankind, so that Allah may ascertain those who have faith, and that He may take martyrs from among you, and Allah does not like the wrongdoers. (140)
وَلِيُمَحِّصَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَيَمْحَقَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (١٤١)
And so that Allah may purge [the hearts of] those who have faith and that He may wipe out the faithless. (141)
أَمْ حَسِبْتُمْ أَن تَدْخُلُوا۟ ٱلْجَنَّةَ وَلَمَّا يَعْلَمِ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِينَ جَـٰهَدُوا۟ مِنكُمْ وَيَعْلَمَ ٱلصَّـٰبِرِينَ (١٤٢)
Do you suppose that you would enter paradise, while Allah has not yet ascertained those of you who have waged jihad and He has not ascertained the steadfast? (142)
وَلَقَدْ كُنتُمْ تَمَنَّوْنَ ٱلْمَوْتَ مِن قَبْلِ أَن تَلْقَوْهُ فَقَدْ رَأَيْتُمُوهُ وَأَنتُمْ تَنظُرُونَ (١٤٣)
Certainly you were longing for death before you had encountered it. Then certainly, you saw it, as you were looking on. (143)
وَمَا مُحَمَّدٌ إِلَّا رَسُولٌ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِن قَبْلِهِ ٱلرُّسُلُ ۚ أَفَإِي۟ن مَّاتَ أَوْ قُتِلَ ٱنقَلَبْتُمْ عَلَىٰٓ أَعْقَـٰبِكُمْ ۚ وَمَن يَنقَلِبْ عَلَىٰ عَقِبَيْهِ فَلَن يَضُرَّ ٱللَّهَ شَيْـًٔا ۗ وَسَيَجْزِى ٱللَّهُ ٱلشَّـٰكِرِينَ (١٤٤)
Muhammad is but an apostle; [other] apostles have passed before him. If he dies or is slain, will you turn back on your heels? Anyone who turns back on his heels, will not harm Allah in the least, and soon Allah will reward the grateful. (144)
وَمَا كَانَ لِنَفْسٍ أَن تَمُوتَ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ كِتَـٰبًا مُّؤَجَّلًا ۗ وَمَن يُرِدْ ثَوَابَ ٱلدُّنْيَا نُؤْتِهِۦ مِنْهَا وَمَن يُرِدْ ثَوَابَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ نُؤْتِهِۦ مِنْهَا ۚ وَسَنَجْزِى ٱلشَّـٰكِرِينَ (١٤٥)
No soul may die except by Allah’s leave, at an appointed time. Whoever desires the reward of this world, We will give him of it; and whoever desires the reward of the Hereafter, We will give him of it; and soon We will reward the grateful. (145)
وَكَأَيِّن مِّن نَّبِىٍّ قَـٰتَلَ مَعَهُۥ رِبِّيُّونَ كَثِيرٌ فَمَا وَهَنُوا۟ لِمَآ أَصَابَهُمْ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَمَا ضَعُفُوا۟ وَمَا ٱسْتَكَانُوا۟ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ يُحِبُّ ٱلصَّـٰبِرِينَ (١٤٦)
How many a prophet there has been with whom a multitude of godly men fought. They did not falter for what befell them in the way of Allah, neither did they weaken, nor did they abase themselves; and Allah loves the steadfast. (146)
وَمَا كَانَ قَوْلَهُمْ إِلَّآ أَن قَالُوا۟ رَبَّنَا ٱغْفِرْ لَنَا ذُنُوبَنَا وَإِسْرَافَنَا فِىٓ أَمْرِنَا وَثَبِّتْ أَقْدَامَنَا وَٱنصُرْنَا عَلَى ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (١٤٧)
All that they said was, ‘Our Lord, forgive us our sins and our excesses in our affairs, make our feet steady, and help us against the faithless lot.’ (147)
فَـَٔاتَىٰهُمُ ٱللَّهُ ثَوَابَ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَحُسْنَ ثَوَابِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (١٤٨)
So Allah gave them the reward of this world and the fair reward of the Hereafter; and Allah loves the virtuous. (148)

(Commentary)

As you can see, these verses are a continuation of the preceding ones that began with His saying: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا (O you who have faith). Just as the previous verses, with their commands and prohibitions, served as a preamble, these verses contain the core objective—comprising command, prohibition, praise, and rebuke.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلا تَهِنُوا وَلا تَحْزَنُوا وَأَنْتُمُ الْأَعْلَوْنَ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ — "Do not become hollow, and do not grieve, for you shall be the uppermost, if you are believers" (3:139). Weakness (الوهن) — a deficiency in physical constitution or character, as mentioned by al-Raghib. What is meant here is their weakness in terms of resolve (العزيمة) and commitment to establishing the religion and fighting its enemies. Grief (الحزن) is the opposite of joy; it afflicts a human being upon losing something they possess and love, or something they consider themselves to possess.

In His saying, the Exalted: وَأَنْتُمُ الْأَعْلَوْنَ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ إِنْ يَمْسَسْكُمْ قَرْحٌ فَقَدْ مَسَّ الْقَوْمَ قَرْحٌ مِثْلُهُ — "for you shall be the uppermost, if you are believers. If a wound afflicts you, a like wound has already afflicted those people" (3:139-140), there is an indication that the cause of their weakness and grief was what they witnessed of the wounds (القرح) that afflicted them and the apparent superiority of the disbelievers over them. Although the polytheists did not achieve total victory and triumph over the believers, and the battle did not end in a complete rout of the faithful, what befell the believers was severe and painful: the martyrdom of seventy of their leaders and brave men, occurring in their very own territory. This was the cause of their weakness and grief. The placement of His saying, "for you shall be the uppermost," as a justification (التعليل) is the reason why these two prohibitions are directed at an existing weakness and grief, rather than hypothetical or expected ones.

The phrase "the uppermost" (الأعلون) is mentioned without restriction, but it is conditioned upon faith (الإيمان). The sum of the meaning is: It is not fitting for you to weaken in your resolve, nor to grieve over the victory and triumph over your enemies that escaped you, if faith is present within you. For faith is a quality that necessarily entails your superiority, as it is inseparable from godwariness (التقوى) and patience (الصبر), and in these two lies the essence of victory and triumph. As for the wound that afflicted you, you are not alone in this; the other people—the polytheists—have been afflicted by the like of it. Thus, they have not surpassed you in anything that would necessitate your weakness or grief.

Conditioning their superiority upon faith, despite the address being directed to "those who have faith," is to point out that although the group does not lack faith, they are not acting according to its requirements—such as patience and godwariness—otherwise, it would have produced its effect.

This is the state of any group with varying levels of faith; among them are the true believers, those weak in faith, and those with sickness in their hearts. Such discourse serves as an encouragement for the souls of the true believers, an exhortation for the weak, and a rebuke and reprimand for those with diseased hearts.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنْ يَمْسَسْكُمْ قَرْحٌ فَقَدْ مَسَّ الْقَوْمَ قَرْحٌ مِثْلُهُ — "If a wound afflicts you, a like wound has already afflicted those people" (3:140). Wound (القرح) — with a fatha on the qaf — refers to the mark of a wound caused by something external. Wound (القرح) — with a damma — refers to its mark from within, like a blister or the like, as stated by al-Raghib. It seems to be a metaphor for what befell them on the day of Uhud, treating the collective body of Muslims as a single person afflicted by a wound from his enemy—this being the killing of those who were killed, the wounding of those who were wounded, and the loss of victory and triumph after they had appeared imminent.

This sentence, "If a wound afflicts you..." and the subsequent coordinated sentences up to His saying, "and that He may annihilate the faithless," are in the position of justification (التعليل)—as previously mentioned—for His saying, "Do not become hollow, and do not grieve." Similarly, His saying, "for you shall be the uppermost," is another justification.

The difference between the two types of justification is that the first—"for you shall be the uppermost"—is a justification by way of correcting their erroneous assumption. They only weakened and grieved because they thought the polytheists had gained superiority over them; so Allah corrected them, stating that the criterion for superiority is with you if you are believers, not with the polytheists. As the Exalted has said: وَكانَ حَقًّا عَلَيْنا نَصْرُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ — "and it was a must for Us to help the faithful" (30:47).

As for the second, it is by way of explaining the state of the two parties—the believers and the polytheists—or explaining the wisdoms and benefits that trace back to a single principle: the divine sunnah (السنة الإلهية) — the established way of Allah — which proceeds by the alternation of days among people.

His saying, the Exalted: وَتِلْكَ الْأَيَّامُ نُداوِلُها بَيْنَ النَّاسِ — "and such days We alternate among the people" (3:140). Day (اليوم) is a significant measure of time required for events to occur; thus, it varies according to the events. It is commonly used for the period between sunrise and sunset, and sometimes it is used for dominion, authority, power, and the like, by way of the relationship between the container and the contained. Thus, one speaks of "the day of such a group" or "the day of the family of so-and-so," meaning their precedence and rule over others. It may also refer to the time itself in which that occurred. This is the meaning intended by "days" in the verse. Alternation (المداولة) is to make something be taken by one after another. The meaning is: the divine sunnah has proceeded to alternate the days among people without them being halted for one group or defended against another, due to general benefits following this sunnah, of which your understandings can only encompass some, not all.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلِيَعْلَمَ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَيَتَّخِذَ مِنْكُمْ شُهَداءَ — "and so that Allah may ascertain those who have faith, and that He may take witnesses from among you" (3:140). This is coordinated with a deleted clause, omitted to intimate that it is something that understandings cannot fully encompass nor intellects perceive except from certain aspects. What benefits the believers to know is what He mentioned in His saying: "and so that Allah may ascertain those who have faith, and that He may take witnesses from among you..." and in His saying: "and so that Allah may purge those who have faith and that He may annihilate the faithless."

As for His saying: وَلِيَعْلَمَ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا — "and so that Allah may ascertain those who have faith," what is meant is the manifestation of the believers' faith after its being hidden and concealed. For His knowledge, the Exalted, of events and things in the external world is identical to their existence therein; things are known to Him, the Exalted, by their very existence, not by a form taken from them—similar to our knowledge and perceptions—and this is evident. The necessary consequence of this is that His will, the Exalted, to "know" something is the will for its actualization and manifestation. Since He said, "and so that Allah may ascertain those who have faith," taking their existence as a realized fact, this implies the will for the manifestation of their faith. Since this occurs according to the sunnah of causes and effects, it was inevitable that events would occur necessitating the manifestation of the believer's faith after its concealment. Understand this well.

As for His saying: وَيَتَّخِذَ مِنْكُمْ شُهَداءَ — "and that He may take witnesses from among you," the witnesses (الشهداء) here are the witnesses of deeds (شهداء الأعمال). As for "witnesses" in the sense of those killed in the battlefield (martyrs), its usage is not established in the Qur'an; rather, it is among the newly introduced Islamic terms, as previously mentioned regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَكَذلِكَ جَعَلْناكُمْ أُمَّةً وَسَطاً لِتَكُونُوا شُهَداءَ — "Thus We have made you a middle nation that you may be witnesses" (2:143). Furthermore, His saying "and that He may take" (ويتخذ) does not fit well with "witnesses" in the sense of those killed in battle. It is not said, "Allah took so-and-so as one killed in His way and a martyr," as one says, "Allah took Ibrahim as a friend (خليلاً)," "Allah took Musa as one spoken to (كليماً)," or "Allah took the Prophet as a witness (شهيداً) to testify over his community on the Day of Resurrection."

He changed the context, saying, "and that He may take witnesses from among you," and did not say, "and that He may take them as witnesses," because although witnessing is attributed to the community in His saying, "Thus We have made you a middle nation that you may be witnesses over the people" (2:143), it is of the nature of describing the part attributed to the whole; the witnesses are some of the community, not all of them. This was explained in Surah al-Baqarah. This view may be supported by His subsequent saying: وَاللهُ لا يُحِبُّ الظَّالِمِينَ — "and Allah does not like the wrongdoers" (3:140).

As for His saying: وَلِيُمَحِّصَ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَيَمْحَقَ الْكافِرِينَ — "and so that Allah may purge those who have faith and that He may annihilate the faithless" (3:141). Purging (التمحيص) — the process of purifying something from external impurities. Annihilation (المحق) — the gradual depletion and removal of something bit by bit. This purging is among the wisdoms and benefits of the alternation of days. It is distinct from "ascertaining those who have faith," which is also among the wisdoms of the alternation of days. For distinguishing the believer from the non-believer is one thing, and purifying his faith after distinction from the impurities of disbelief, hypocrisy, and transgression is another. Therefore, it was contrasted with the annihilation of the faithless.
