Thus, Allah, the Glorified, removes the elements of disbelief and the like from the believer bit by bit until nothing remains but his faith, making it sincere for Allah; and He destroys the elements of disbelief, polytheism, and plotting from the disbeliever bit by bit until nothing remains.

These are aspects of wisdom in His alternation, the Exalted, of days among people, and the lack of continuous dominion for a specific group. To Allah belongs the entire command; He does what He wills, and He only does what is most beneficial and appropriate, as He said: كَذلِكَ يَضْرِبُ اللهُ الْحَقَّ وَالْباطِلَ فَأَمَّا الزَّبَدُ فَيَذْهَبُ جُفاءً وَأَمَّا ما يَنْفَعُ النَّاسَ فَيَمْكُثُ فِي الْأَرْضِ — "Thus does Allah draw the parable of the truth and the falsehood. As for the scum, it leaves as dross, and as for what profits the people, it remains in the earth" (13:17). And Allah said shortly before these verses: لِيَقْطَعَ طَرَفاً مِنَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا أَوْ يَكْبِتَهُمْ فَيَنْقَلِبُوا خائِبِينَ لَيْسَ لَكَ مِنَ الْأَمْرِ شَيْءٌ أَوْ يَتُوبَ عَلَيْهِمْ أَوْ يُعَذِّبَهُمْ فَإِنَّهُمْ ظالِمُونَ — "that He may cut off a part of the faithless, or humiliate them so that they retreat disappointed. You have no hand in the matter, whether He accepts their repentance or punishes them, for they are indeed wrongdoers" (3:127-128). Thus, He denied His Prophet any hand in the matter and restricted the command to Himself, judging among His creation as He wills.

This discourse—which explains that the days are divided among people for the purpose of trial (الامتحان), distinguishing the believer from the disbeliever, purging the believers, and annihilating the disbelievers, along with the aforementioned denial of the matter returning to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)—reveals that most of the believers thought that their being upon the true religion was a sufficient cause for their victory wherever they fought and their triumph over falsehood regardless of their state. They thought they possessed the command and would not be repelled from it. They were emboldened in this assumption by what they witnessed on the day of Badr of their extraordinary triumph over their enemy and the descent of the angels of victory. This is a corrupt assumption that would necessitate the nullification of the system of trial and purging, and therein lies the nullification of the benefit of command, prohibition, reward, and punishment. This would lead to the collapse of the foundation of religion; for religion is the religion of the primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — not built upon the breaking of established habits and the divine sunnah standing in existence, which bases victory and defeat upon their customary causes.

The Glorified began—after explaining that the days are alternating turns for the purpose of trial and tribulation—to blame them for entertaining this false view and to explain the reality of the situation, saying: أَمْ حَسِبْتُمْ (Did you suppose...) until the end of the verses.

His saying, the Exalted: أَمْ حَسِبْتُمْ أَنْ تَدْخُلُوا الْجَنَّةَ وَلَمَّا يَعْلَمِ اللهُ — "Did you suppose that you would enter paradise while Allah has not yet ascertained..." (3:142). This—namely, their thought that they would enter Paradise without being tried—is a necessary consequence of the aforementioned assumption: that since they were upon the truth, and truth is never overcome, the matter of victory and triumph belonged to them, and they would never be defeated or overcome. It is well known that the consequence of this assumption is that everyone who believes in the Prophet and joins the group of believers would be happy in this world through victory and spoils, and happy in the Hereafter through forgiveness and Paradise. This would nullify the difference between the outward appearance of faith and its reality, and the distinction between degrees would vanish; the faith of the mujahid and the faith of the patient mujahid would be one, and he who wished for good and did it when its time came would be like him who wished for good and then turned away when it reached him.

Accordingly, His saying: "Did you suppose that you would enter..." is of the nature of placing the effect in the place of the cause. That is: Did you suppose that dominion was decreed for you, so you would not be tested, but rather enter Paradise without the one deserving it being distinguished from the one who is not, and the possessor of a high degree from others?

As for His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ كُنْتُمْ تَمَنَّوْنَ الْمَوْتَ — "Certainly you used to wish for death..." (3:143), it contains a confirmation that their assumption was corrupt. They used to wish for death before the battle occurred, but when it arrived and they saw it with their own eyes, they did not step forward nor take what they had been wishing for; rather, they lost heart and turned away from the fight. Was it then permissible for them to enter Paradise by merely this wishing, without being tried or purged? Or was it not mandatory that they be tested?

From this, it becomes clear that there is an implicit meaning in the discourse: "You have seen it while you were looking, yet you did not advance toward it." It is possible that His saying "you were looking" (تنظرون) is a metaphor for their lack of advancement; that is, you were content with merely looking without taking action. This contains rebuke and reproach.

(A Discourse on Trial and its Reality)

There is no doubt that the Qur'an attributes the matter of guidance (الهداية) to Allah, the Glorified. However, guidance therein is not restricted to the volitional guidance toward the happiness of the Hereafter or this world. He, the Exalted, said among other things: الَّذِي أَعْطى كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلْقَهُ ثُمَّ هَدى — "He who gave everything its creation and then guided it" (20:50). Thus, He generalized guidance to everything—those with consciousness and intellect and others—and left it unrestricted in terms of its goal. He also said: الَّذِي خَلَقَ فَسَوَّى وَالَّذِي قَدَّرَ فَهَدى — "who created and proportioned, who determined and guided" (87:2-3). This verse, in its lack of restriction, is like the previous one.

From here, it appears that this guidance is different from the specific guidance that is contrasted with misguidance (الإضلال); for Allah, the Glorified, denied the latter and established misguidance in its place for certain groups, while general guidance is not denied to any of His creation. The Exalted said: وَاللهُ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الظَّالِمِينَ — "and Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people" (62:5), and He said: وَاللهُ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الْفاسِقِينَ — "and Allah does not guide the transgressing people" (61:5), among many other verses.

Similarly, it appears that the aforementioned guidance is different from guidance in the sense of "showing the way" (إراءة الطريق) which is general to the believer and the disbeliever, as in His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّا هَدَيْناهُ السَّبِيلَ إِمَّا شاكِراً وَإِمَّا كَفُوراً — "Indeed We have guided him to the way, whether he be grateful or ungrateful" (76:3), and His saying: وَأَمَّا ثَمُودُ فَهَدَيْناهُمْ فَاسْتَحَبُّوا الْعَمى عَلَى الْهُدى — "As for Thamud, We guided them, but they preferred blindness to guidance" (41:17). For the guidance in these two verses and their likes does not encompass other than those possessed of consciousness and intellect, whereas you have seen that what is in His saying "then guided" and His saying "who determined and guided" is general in terms of both subject and goal. Furthermore, the second verse makes guidance a branch of determination (التقدير), and specific guidance does not align with determination, which is the preparation of causes and conditions to lead a thing to the goal of its creation—even if that guidance is also, from the perspective of the general system of the world, within the scope of determination, but the perspective is different. Understand this well.

In any case, this general guidance is His guidance, the Exalted, of every thing to the perfection of its existence and bringing it to the goal of its creation. It is through this that every thing yearns for what the constitution of its essence requires of growth, completion, actions, movements, and so on. This discourse has a long sequel which we shall explain, if success is granted, Allah willing.

The point is that His word indicates that things are led to their goals and terms (آجالها) by a general divine guidance from which nothing deviates. Allah, the Exalted, has made this a right for them upon Himself, and He does not break His promise, as the Exalted said: إِنَّ عَلَيْنا لَلْهُدى وَإِنَّ لَنا لَلْآخِرَةَ وَالْأُولى — "Indeed, it is for Us to guide, and to Us belong the Hereafter and the first [life]" (92:12-13). This verse, as you see, encompasses by its generality social guidance for societies and individual guidance, in addition to what the two previous verses indicate.

Thus, it is the right of things upon Allah to guide them ontologically (تکويناً) to their determined perfection, and to guide them to their legislated (المشرع) perfection. You have already learned in the previous discussions on Prophethood how legislation enters into the ontological realm and how it is encompassed by divine decree and determination (القضاء والقدر). For the human species has a type of existence whose affairs are not completed except through a series of volitional, intentional acts that only occur based on theoretical and practical beliefs. Thus, it must live under laws, whether true or false, good or bad. Therefore, the driver of the ontological realm must prepare for it a series of commands and prohibitions (the Sharia) and another series of social and individual events, so that through its encounter with them, what is in its potentiality may emerge into actuality, resulting in happiness or wretchedness, and revealing what lies in the depths of its existence. At that point, the name "trial" (المحنة) and "tribulation" (البلاء) and the like apply to these events and this legislation.

To clarify: whoever does not follow the divine call and earns wretchedness for themselves, the word of punishment has become due for them if they remain in that state. Every event they encounter—to which divine commands and prohibitions are attached and through which they move from potentiality to actuality—completes for them a new actuality of wretchedness, even if they are pleased with what they have and deluded by what they find. This is nothing but a divine plot (مكر إلهي), for He makes them wretched through the very things they consider to be happiness for themselves, and He frustrates their efforts in what they think is success. The Exalted said: وَمَكَرُوا وَمَكَرَ اللهُ وَاللهُ خَيْرُ الْماكِرِينَ — "And they plotted, and Allah plotted, and Allah is the best of plotters" (3:54); and He said: وَلا يَحِيقُ الْمَكْرُ السَّيِّئُ إِلَّا بِأَهْلِهِ — "but the evil plot enmeshes only its authors" (35:43); and He said: لِيَمْكُرُوا فِيها وَما يَمْكُرُونَ إِلَّا بِأَنْفُسِهِمْ وَما يَشْعُرُونَ — "that they may plot therein; but they do not plot except against themselves, and they are not aware" (6:123); and He said: سَنَسْتَدْرِجُهُمْ مِنْ حَيْثُ لا يَعْلَمُونَ وَأُمْلِي لَهُمْ إِنَّ كَيْدِي مَتِينٌ — "We will draw them on [to ruin] degree by degree from whence they do not know. And I will grant them a respite; indeed My stratagem is firm" (7:182-183). Thus, what the deluded one, ignorant of Allah's command, boasts of—that he has outstripped his Lord in what He intended for him through opposition and rebellion—is actually helping against himself in what He intended. The Exalted said: أَمْ حَسِبَ الَّذِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ السَّيِّئاتِ أَنْ يَسْبِقُونا ساءَ ما يَحْكُمُونَ — "Do those who commit misdeeds suppose that they can outstrip Us? Evil is the judgment that they make" (29:4). Among the most wondrous verses in this regard is His saying, the Exalted: فَلِلَّهِ الْمَكْرُ جَمِيعاً — "to Allah belongs all plotting" (13:42).

All these plots, oppositions, injustices, and transgressions that manifest from these people regarding religious duties, and all the events of the days that encounter them and through which they manifest what they concealed in their hearts and what their desires called them to, are divine plotting, respite, and gradual drawing to ruin (استدراج). For it is their right upon Allah that He guide them to the consequence and end of their affair, and He has done so; and Allah is dominant over His affair.

These same matters, when attributed to Satan, are the types of disbelief and sins as a seduction (إغواء) from him to them; the yearning for them is a call, whispering (وسوسة), impulse, inspiration, and misguidance. The inviting events and what proceeds like them are an adornment (زينة) for him, and means, snares, and nets from him, as will be explained in Surah al-A'raf, Allah willing.

As for the believer in whose heart faith has taken root, the acts of obedience and worship that manifest from him, as well as the events that encounter him and through which these manifest, the concepts of success (التوفيق), divine guardianship (الولاية الإلهية), and guidance in the more specific sense apply to them. The Exalted said: وَاللهُ يُؤَيِّدُ بِنَصْرِهِ مَنْ يَشاءُ — "and Allah strengthens with His help whomever He wishes" (3:13); and He said: وَاللهُ وَلِيُّ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ — "and Allah is the Guardian of the faithful" (3:68); and He said: اللهُ وَلِيُّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا يُخْرِجُهُمْ مِنَ الظُّلُماتِ إِلَى النُّورِ — "Allah is the Guardian of the faithful: He brings them out of darkness into light" (2:257); and He said: يَهْدِيهِمْ رَبُّهُمْ بِإِيمانِهِمْ — "their Lord guides them because of their faith" (10:9); and He said: أَوَمَنْ كانَ مَيْتاً فَأَحْيَيْناهُ وَجَعَلْنا لَهُ نُوراً يَمْشِي بِهِ فِي النَّاسِ — "Is he who was lifeless, then We gave him life and maintained for him a light by which he walks among the people..." (6:122). This is when these matters are attributed to Allah, the Glorified. If they are attributed to the angels, they are called strengthening (تأييد) and directing (تسديد) from them. The Exalted said: أُولئِكَ كَتَبَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمُ الْإِيمانَ وَأَيَّدَهُمْ بِروحٍ مِنْهُ — "He has written faith into their hearts and strengthened them with a spirit from Him" (58:22).

Furthermore, just as general guidance accompanies things from the beginning of their being until the last moments of their existence as long as they traverse the path of return to Allah, the Glorified, so too do the determinations (المقادير) push them from behind, as is evident from His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِي قَدَّرَ فَهَدى — "who determined and guided" (87:3). For the determinations carried by the causes and conditions surrounding the existence of a thing are what transform it from a first state to a second state, and so on; they continue to push things from behind.

And just as the determinations push them from behind, so too do the terms (الآجال) — the final point to which the existence of things reaches — pull them from the front, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: ما خَلَقْنَا السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ وَما بَيْنَهُما إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ وَأَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى — "We did not create the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them except with the truth and for a stated term" (46:3). For the verse links things to their goals, which are the terms. When two linked things have one stronger than the other, its state relative to its companion is called "pulling" (جذب). The stated terms are fixed, unchanging matters; thus, they pull things from the front, and this is evident.

Thus, things are surrounded by divine powers: a power that pushes them, a power that pulls them, and a power that accompanies and nurtures them. these are the primary powers established by the Qur'an, besides the preserving powers, the watchers (الرقباء), and the companions (القرناء) such as angels, satans, and others.

Furthermore, we call the type of actions performed on a thing—when a goal is intended but its state relative to it is not apparent: does it have the aptitude for it or not?—trial (الامتحان) and testing (الاختبار). For if you are ignorant of the state of a thing—whether it is fit for such an affair or not—or you know its inner state but want it to manifest, you apply to it things that align with the mentioned goal until its state becomes apparent: does it accept them for itself or repel them? You call this trial, testing, and ascertaining its state, or similar terms.

This very meaning applies to the divine action regarding the legislations and events He brings upon those possessed of consciousness and intellect, such as humans. For through these matters, the state of the human becomes apparent relative to the goal to which they are called by the religious invitation; thus, they are divine trials.

The only difference between divine trial and our trial is that we are mostly not free from ignorance regarding the inner reality of things, so we intend by trial to ascertain their state which is unknown to us. As for Allah, the Glorified, ignorance is impossible for Him, and with Him are the keys of the Unseen. Thus, the general divine nurturing of the human—from the perspective of calling them to a good end and happiness—is a trial because through it, the state of the thing becomes manifest and determined: to which of the two abodes does it belong, the abode of reward or the abode of punishment?

Therefore, Allah called this divine action from Himself—namely, legislation and the direction of events—tribulation (بلاء), trial (ابتلاء), and ordeal (فتنة). He said in a general sense: إِنَّا جَعَلْنا ما عَلَى الْأَرْضِ زِينَةً لَها لِنَبْلُوهُمْ أَيُّهُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلاً — "Indeed We have made whatever is on the earth an adornment for it that We may test them [to see] which of them is best in conduct" (18:7); and He said: إِنَّا خَلَقْنَا الْإِنْسانَ مِنْ نُطْفَةٍ أَمْشاجٍ نَبْتَلِيهِ فَجَعَلْناهُ سَمِيعاً بَصِيراً — "Indeed We created man from a drop of mixed fluid so that We may test him. So We made him endowed with hearing and sight" (76:2); and He said: وَنَبْلُوكُمْ بِالشَّرِّ وَالْخَيْرِ فِتْنَةً — "and We test you with evil and good by way of trial" (21:35). It is as if He intends what is detailed by His saying: فَأَمَّا الْإِنْسانُ إِذا مَا ابْتَلاهُ رَبُّهُ فَأَكْرَمَهُ وَنَعَّمَهُ فَيَقُولُ رَبِّي أَكْرَمَنِ وَأَمَّا إِذا مَا ابْتَلاهُ فَقَدَرَ عَلَيْهِ رِزْقَهُ فَيَقُولُ رَبِّي أَهانَنِ — "As for man, whenever his Lord tests him and honors him and blesses him, he says, ‘My Lord has honored me.’ But whenever He tests him and tightens for him his provision, he says, ‘My Lord has humiliated me.’" (89:15-16). And He said: إِنَّما أَمْوالُكُمْ وَأَوْلادُكُمْ فِتْنَةٌ — "Your possessions and your children are only a trial" (64:15); and He said: وَلكِنْ لِيَبْلُوَا بَعْضَكُمْ بِبَعْضٍ — "but [He did so] that He may test some of you by means of others" (47:4); and He said: كَذلِكَ نَبْلُوهُمْ بِما كانُوا يَفْسُقُونَ — "Thus did We test them because of the transgressions they used to commit" (7:163); and He said: وَلِيُبْلِيَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ مِنْهُ بَلاءً حَسَناً — "and that He might test the faithful with a good test from Himself" (8:17); and He said: أَحَسِبَ النَّاسُ أَنْ يُتْرَكُوا أَنْ يَقُولُوا آمَنَّا وَهُمْ لا يُفْتَنُونَ وَلَقَدْ فَتَنَّا الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ فَلَيَعْلَمَنَّ اللهُ الَّذِينَ صَدَقُوا وَلَيَعْلَمَنَّ الْكاذِبِينَ — "Do the people suppose that they will be let off because they say, ‘We have faith,’ and they will not be tested? Certainly We tested those who were before them. So Allah shall surely ascertain those who are truthful, and He shall surely ascertain the liars" (29:2-3).

He said regarding the like of Ibrahim: وَإِذِ ابْتَلى إِبْراهِيمَ رَبُّهُ بِكَلِماتٍ — "And when his Lord tested Abraham with certain words" (2:124); and He said in the story of the sacrifice of Isma'il: إِنَّ هذا لَهُوَ الْبَلاءُ الْمُبِينُ — "This was indeed a manifest testing" (37:106); and He said regarding Musa: وَفَتَنَّاكَ فُتُوناً — "and We tested you with various trials" (20:40), among other verses.

The verses, as you see, generalize trial and tribulation to everything the human is linked to—their existence and parts of their existence like hearing, sight, and life; and what is external to their existence but linked to it in some way, like children, spouses, kin, friends, wealth, status, and everything they benefit from in any way. Similarly, the opposites of these things, such as death and other calamities directed at them. In short, the verses count everything the human is linked to from the parts of the world and their states as an ordeal and tribulation from Allah, the Glorified, relative to them.

There is another generalization in terms of individuals: everyone is tried and tested, whether believer or disbeliever, righteous or wicked, prophet or otherwise. It is an established sunnah from which no one is exempt.

It has become clear that the sunnah of trial is an established divine sunnah, and it is a practical sunnah resting upon another ontological sunnah, which is the sunnah of general divine guidance in its relation to those charged with duties (المكلفين) like humans, and what precedes and follows it—namely, determination and the term, as previously explained.

From here, it appears that it is not subject to abrogation (النسخ); for its abrogation would be the very corruption of the ontological realm, which is impossible. This is indicated by the verses showing that creation is based on truth, and those showing that the Resurrection is a truth, such as His saying, the Exalted: ما خَلَقْنَا السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ وَما بَيْنَهُما إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ وَأَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى — "We did not create the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them except with the truth and for a stated term" (46:3); and His saying, the Exalted: أَفَحَسِبْتُمْ أَنَّما خَلَقْناكُمْ عَبَثاً وَأَنَّكُمْ إِلَيْنا لا تُرْجَعُونَ — "Did you suppose that We created you aimlessly, and that you will not be brought back to Us?" (23:115); and His saying, the Exalted: وَما خَلَقْنَا السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ وَما بَيْنَهُما لاعِبِينَ ما خَلَقْناهُما إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ وَلكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ — "We did not create the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them for play. We did not create them except with the truth, but most of them do not know" (44:38-39); and His saying, the Exalted: مَنْ كانَ يَرْجُوا لِقاءَ اللهِ فَإِنَّ أَجَلَ اللهِ لَآتٍ — "Whoever expects to encounter Allah [should know that] Allah’s [appointed] term will surely come" (29:5), among others. All of them indicate that creation is with truth and is not vain or severed from a goal. If there are true goals and terms ahead of things, true determinations behind them, and true guidance with them, then there is no escape from their general collision, and specifically the tribulation of those charged with duties through matters by which their potential perfection, deficiency, happiness, and wretchedness emerge into actuality. This meaning in the human charged with the duty of religion is trial and tribulation. Understand this well.

From what we have mentioned, the meaning of annihilation (المحق) and purging (التمحيص) also becomes clear. For if trial comes upon the believer and necessitates the distinction of his latent virtues from vices, or comes upon the group and requires the distinction of believers from hypocrites and those with diseased hearts, the name "purging"—which is distinction—applies to it. Similarly, if divine trials follow one another upon the disbeliever and the hypocrite—who outwardly possess enviable good qualities and states—and gradually necessitate the manifestation of the impurities in their inner selves, and every time an impurity appears it removes an outward virtue, that is an "annihilation" of them; that is, a gradual depletion of their good qualities. The Exalted said: وَتِلْكَ الْأَيَّامُ نُداوِلُها بَيْنَ النَّاسِ وَلِيَعْلَمَ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَيَتَّخِذَ مِنْكُمْ شُهَداءَ وَاللهُ لا يُحِبُّ الظَّالِمِينَ وَلِيُمَحِّصَ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَيَمْحَقَ الْكافِرِينَ — "and such days We alternate among the people, so that Allah may ascertain those who have faith, and that He may take witnesses from among you, and Allah does not like the wrongdoers; and so that Allah may purge those who have faith and that He may annihilate the faithless" (3:140-141).

For the faithless, there is another annihilation from the perspective of what He, the Exalted, informs that the cosmos is led toward the welfare of humanity and the sincerity of religion for Allah. The Exalted said: وَالْعاقِبَةُ لِتَّقْوى — "and the outcome will be in favor of godwariness" (20:132); and He said: أَنَّ الْأَرْضَ يَرِثُها عِبادِيَ الصَّالِحُونَ — "that My righteous servants shall inherit the earth" (21:105).

His saying, the Exalted: وَما مُحَمَّدٌ إِلَّا رَسُولٌ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِنْ قَبْلِهِ الرُّسُلُ — "Muhammad is but an apostle; [other] apostles have passed before him" (3:144). Death (الموت) is the departure of the spirit and the cessation of the body's life. Killing (القتل) is death when it is attributed to an intentional cause or the like. When death and killing are mentioned separately, death is more general than killing; when they are mentioned together, death refers to dying naturally (بحتف الأنف) and killing is its opposite.

"Turned back on his heels" (انقلب على عقبيه) means returned. Al-Raghib said: "He returned on his heels" when he turned back, and "he turned back on his heels" is like "he returned to his starting point" (رجع على حافرته), and like "they both turned back, retracing their footsteps" (ارتدا على آثارهما قصصاً), and their saying "he returned from where he started" (رجع عوده إلى بدئه). End quote.

Since turning back on the heels was made the consequence of the condition—which is the death or killing of the Messenger—it implies that what is meant is turning back from the religion, rather than turning away from the fight. For fleeing from the battlefield has no connection to the death or killing of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم); rather, the relationship and link is between his death or killing and the return to disbelief after faith.

That turning back from the religion is intended is indicated by what He, the Exalted, mentioned in His saying: وَطائِفَةٌ قَدْ أَهَمَّتْهُمْ أَنْفُسُهُمْ يَظُنُّونَ بِاللهِ غَيْرَ الْحَقِّ ظَنَّ الْجاهِلِيَّةِ — "while another party, preoccupied with their own souls, entertained wrong thoughts about Allah, thoughts of [pagan] ignorance" (3:154). Furthermore, instances similar to their fleeing from the battlefield and turning away from the fight in Uhud occurred elsewhere, such as the battles of Hunayn, Khaybar, and others, yet Allah did not address them with such a discourse, nor did He describe their turning away from the fight with such a phrase. The Exalted said: وَيَوْمَ حُنَيْنٍ إِذْ أَعْجَبَتْكُمْ كَثْرَتُكُمْ فَلَمْ تُغْنِ عَنْكُمْ شَيْئاً وَضاقَتْ عَلَيْكُمُ الْأَرْضُ بِما رَحُبَتْ ثُمَّ وَلَّيْتُمْ مُدْبِرِينَ — "and on the day of Hunayn, when your great number tickled your fancy, but did not avail you anything, and the earth became narrow for you despite its expanse; then you turned your backs [to flee]" (9:25). Thus, the truth is that turning back on the heels means returning to the previous disbelief.

The sum of the verse's meaning, given its context of rebuke and reproach, is: Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) is but a messenger from Allah like other messengers; his role is only to convey the message of his Lord, and he possesses no hand in the matter. The matter belongs to Allah, and the religion is His religion, remaining as long as He remains. So what is the meaning of your faith being dependent on his life, such that it appears from you that if he were to die or be killed, you would abandon the establishment of the religion, return to your heels in retreat, and take up error after guidance?

This context is the strongest evidence that on the day of Uhud, after the heat of battle intensified, they thought the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) had been killed, so they slipped away and turned from the fight. This supports what is mentioned in narration and history—as in what Ibn Hisham narrated in the Sira: that Anas ibn al-Nadr—the uncle of Anas ibn Malik—came upon Umar ibn al-Khattab and Talhah ibn Ubaydillah among men of the Emigrants and Helpers—who had given up—and said: "What keeps you?" They said: "The Messenger of Allah has been killed." He said: "Then what will you do with life after him? Die upon what the Messenger of Allah died upon." Then he faced the people and fought until he was killed.

In short, the meaning of this slipping away and giving up is that their faith was dependent on the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), remaining with his survival and vanishing with his death; this is the desire for the reward of this world through faith, and this is what Allah rebuked them for. This meaning is supported by His subsequent saying: وَسَيَجْزِي اللهُ الشَّاكِرِينَ — "and Allah will soon reward the grateful" (3:144). For Allah, the Glorified, repeated this phrase in the following verse after His saying: وَمَنْ يُرِدْ ثَوابَ الدُّنْيا نُؤْتِهِ مِنْها وَمَنْ يُرِدْ ثَوابَ الْآخِرَةِ نُؤْتِهِ مِنْها — "Whoever desires the reward of this world, We will give him of it; and whoever desires the reward of the Hereafter, We will give him of it" (3:145). Understand this well.

His saying: "and Allah will soon reward the grateful" is in the position of an exception to what preceded it, as given by the context. It is the evidence that among the people were those from whom this turning back did not manifest, nor anything suggesting it like slipping away and turning back; these are the grateful (الشاكرون).

The reality of gratitude (الشكر) is the manifestation of the blessing, just as disbelief/ingratitude (الكفر) which opposes it is its concealment and covering. Manifesting the blessing is to use it in its proper place intended by its Bestower, and to mention the Bestower of it verbally—which is praise—and in the heart without forgetting. Thus, His gratitude, the Exalted, for one of His blessings is to remember Him when using it and to place the blessing in the position He intended for it and not to exceed that. There is nothing but that it is a blessing from His blessings, the Exalted, and He intends for none of His blessings except that it be used in the way of His worship. The Exalted said: وَآتاكُمْ مِنْ كُلِّ ما سَأَلْتُمُوهُ وَإِنْ تَعُدُّوا نِعْمَتَ اللهِ لا تُحْصُوها إِنَّ الْإِنْسانَ لَظَلُومٌ كَفَّارٌ — "And He gave you all that you had asked Him. If you enumerate Allah’s blessings, you will not be able to count them. Indeed man is most unfair and ungrateful" (14:34). Thus, gratitude for His blessing is to obey Him regarding it and to remember the station of His Lordship in its presence.

Accordingly, His absolute gratitude, without restriction, is to remember Him without forgetting, and to obey Him without disobedience. Thus, the meaning of His saying: وَاشْكُرُوا لِي وَلا تَكْفُرُونِ — "and be grateful to Me, and do not be ungrateful to Me" (2:152) is: Remember Me with a remembrance unmixed with forgetfulness, and obey My command with an obedience untainted by disobedience. One should not listen to the saying of those who say: "He commanded what is beyond capacity," for this arises from a lack of reflection on these realities and distance from the arena of servitude.

You have already learned in the preceding parts of the book that the absolute form of the verb only indicates some engagement with the action, unlike the description (الوصف), which indicates the stability of the engagement and the descriptive meaning becoming a faculty (ملكة) that does not leave the person. Thus, there is a difference between our saying: "those who associated partners," "those who were patient," "those who did wrong," and "those who transgress," and our saying: "the polytheists," "the patient," "the wrongdoers," and "the transgressors." The grateful (الشاكرون) are those in whom the quality of gratitude has become established and this virtue has taken root. It has become clear that absolute gratitude is that the servant does not mention anything—which is a blessing—except that he mentions Allah with it, and does not touch anything—which is a blessing—except that he obeys Allah regarding it.

It has thus become clear that gratitude is not completed except with sincerity (الإخلاص) for Allah, the Glorified, in knowledge and action. Thus, the grateful are the sincere ones (المخلصون) for Allah, for whom Satan has no hope.

This reality appears from what Allah, the Exalted, narrated from Iblis. The Exalted said: قالَ فَبِعِزَّتِكَ لَأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ إِلَّا عِبادَكَ مِنْهُمُ الْمُخْلَصِينَ — "He said, ‘By Your might, I will surely pervert them all, except Your devoted servants among them’" (38:82-83); and the Exalted said: قالَ رَبِّ بِما أَغْوَيْتَنِي لَأُزَيِّنَنَّ لَهُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَلَأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ إِلَّا عِبادَكَ مِنْهُمُ الْمُخْلَصِينَ — "He said, ‘My Lord! As You have consigned me to perversity, I will surely glamorize [evil] for them on the earth, and I will surely pervert them all, except Your devoted servants among them’" (15:39-40). He did not exempt anyone from his perversion except the sincere ones, and Allah, the Glorified, affirmed this without rejection. And the Exalted said: قالَ فَبِما أَغْوَيْتَنِي لَأَقْعُدَنَّ لَهُمْ صِراطَكَ الْمُسْتَقِيمَ ثُمَّ لَآتِيَنَّهُمْ مِنْ بَيْنِ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِمْ وَعَنْ أَيْمانِهِمْ وَعَنْ شَمائِلِهِمْ وَلا تَجِدُ أَكْثَرَهُمْ شاكِرِينَ — "He said, ‘As You have consigned me to perversity, I will surely lie in wait for them on Your straight path. Then I will come at them from their front and from their rear, and from their right and from their left, and You will not find most of them to be grateful’" (7:16-17). His saying "and You will not find..." is in the position of an exception; thus, he replaced "the sincere ones" with "the grateful." This is only because the grateful are the sincere ones for whom Satan has no hope, and he has no influence over them; his influence and plot consist of making one forget the station of Lordship and calling to disobedience.

This is supported by these verses revealed regarding the battle of Uhud, in His saying, the Exalted, in the upcoming verses: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ تَوَلَّوْا مِنْكُمْ يَوْمَ الْتَقَى الْجَمْعانِ إِنَّمَا اسْتَزَلَّهُمُ الشَّيْطانُ بِبَعْضِ ما كَسَبُوا وَلَقَدْ عَفَا اللهُ عَنْهُمْ إِنَّ اللهَ غَفُورٌ حَلِيمٌ — "Those of you who turned last on the day when the two hosts met, it was only Satan who made them slip because of some of what they had earned. But Allah has certainly excused them; indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-forbearing" (3:155), along with His saying in this verse we are considering: "and Allah will soon reward the grateful," and His saying in the one after it: "and We will soon reward the grateful." You have already learned that it is in the sense of an exception.

Reflect upon this and wonder at what is sometimes said: that the verse—namely His saying: "Those of you who turned back..."—refers to what was narrated: that Satan called out on the day of Uhud, "Behold, Muhammad has been killed," which caused the believers to weaken and disperse from the battle! Consider to what depth the Book of Allah has been lowered from the height of its realities and the level of its sublime gnosis.

The verse indicates the existence of a number of them on the day of Uhud who did not weaken, nor flag, nor fail in their duty toward Allah, the Glorified; Allah called them "grateful," and it is true that Satan has no way to them nor hope in them—not only in this battle, but it is a description for them, fixed in them and stable with them. The name "the grateful" (الشاكرون) is not applied to anyone in the Qur'an as a title by way of description except in these two verses: His saying, "Muhammad is but an apostle..." and His saying, "No soul may die except by Allah’s leave..." and He did not mention what He rewards them with in either place, as an intimation of its greatness and preciousness.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما كانَ لِنَفْسٍ أَنْ تَمُوتَ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ اللهِ كِتاباً مُؤَجَّلاً — "No soul may die except by Allah’s leave, [according to] a term inscribed" (3:145). This is an allusion to them regarding what they said about their killed brothers, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَكُونُوا كَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَقالُوا لِإِخْوانِهِمْ إِذا ضَرَبُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ أو كانُوا غُزًّى لَوْ كانُوا عِنْدَنا ما ماتُوا وَما قُتِلُوا — "O you who have faith! Do not be like the faithless who say of their brethren when they travel in the land or go forth to fight, ‘Had they been with us, they would not have died or been killed’" (3:156), and the saying of a group of them: لَوْ كانَ لَنا مِنَ الْأَمْرِ شَيْءٌ ما قُتِلْنا هاهُنا — "Had we a say in the matter, we would not have been killed here" (3:154). These were among the believers, not the hypocrites who abandoned the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and sat back from the fight.

The necessary consequence of this saying of theirs is that the death of souls is not by leave from Allah and a firm sunnah issuing from a definitive decree; its consequence is the nullification of divine sovereignty and the precise Lordly management. The discussion on the meaning of the inscription of terms (كتابة الآجال) will come, Allah willing, at the beginning of Surah al-An'am.

Since the consequence of this saying for those who said it was that they believed because they thought the matter belonged to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and the believers, they desired this world, as previously explained. Whoever avoided this desired the Hereafter, so He, the Exalted, said: وَمَنْ يُرِدْ ثَوابَ الدُّنْيا نُؤْتِهِ مِنْها وَمَنْ يُرِدْ ثَوابَ الْآخِرَةِ نُؤْتِهِ مِنْها — "Whoever desires the reward of this world, We will give him of it; and whoever desires the reward of the Hereafter, We will give him of it" (3:145). He only said "We will give him of it" (نؤته منها) and did not say "We will give it to him" (نؤتها) because the desire might not align with all the causes leading to the entirety of what he intended, so he is not provided with the entirety of what he desired. However, it is never free from some alignment with the causes in general; if it aligns with all, he is provided with all, and if it aligns with some, he is provided with some only. Allah, the Exalted, said: مَنْ كانَ يُرِيدُ الْعاجِلَةَ عَجَّلْنا لَهُ فِيها ما نَشاءُ لِمَنْ نُرِيدُ ثُمَّ جَعَلْنا لَهُ جَهَنَّمَ يَصْلاها مَذْمُوماً مَدْحُوراً وَمَنْ أَرادَ الْآخِرَةَ وَسَعى لَها سَعْيَها وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَأُولئِكَ كانَ سَعْيُهُمْ مَشْكُوراً — "Whoever desires that which is transitory, We expedite for him therein whatever We wish, for whomever We desire. Then We appoint hell for him, to enter it, blamed and spurned. Whoever desires the Hereafter and strives for it with an effort worthy of it, should he be faithful—the endeavor of such shall be appreciated" (17:18-19). And the Exalted said: وَأَنْ لَيْسَ لِلْإِنْسانِ إِلَّا ما سَعى — "and that nothing belongs to man except what he strives for" (53:39).

Then He singled out the grateful for mention by excluding them from the two groups, saying: وَسَنَجْزِي الشَّاكِرِينَ — "and We will soon reward the grateful" (3:145). This is only because they desire nothing but the face of Allah, being occupied neither with this world nor the Hereafter, as previously mentioned.

His saying, the Exalted: وَكَأَيِّنْ مِنْ نَبِيٍّ قاتَلَ مَعَهُ رِبِّيُّونَ كَثِيرٌ — "How many a prophet there has been with whom a multitude of godly men fought" (3:146) until the end of the verses. How many (كأين) is a word of multiplicity, and the word "of" (من) is explanatory. Godly men (الربيون) is the plural of ribbi, which is like rabbani: one who is devoted to his Lord, the Exalted, and is not occupied with other than Him. It is also said: what is meant is thousands, and al-ribbi is a thousand. Abasement (الاستكانة) is supplication and humility.

In the verse is an exhortation and a lesson mixed with rebuke and encouragement for the believers to follow the example of these godly men, so that Allah may give them the reward of this world and the fair reward of the Hereafter as He gave them, and love them for their excellence as He loved them for it.

Allah has narrated of their actions and words that which the believers should take as a lesson and make their motto, so that they are not tried with what they were tried with on the day of Uhud of actions and words not pleasing to Allah, the Exalted, and so that Allah may gather for them the reward of this world and the Hereafter as He gathered for those godly men.

He described the reward of the Hereafter as "fair" (حسن) but not that of this world, as an indication of the loftiness of its station and value relative to it.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِن تُطِيعُوا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ يَرُدُّوكُمْ عَلَىٰٓ أَعْقَـٰبِكُمْ فَتَنقَلِبُوا۟ خَـٰسِرِينَ (١٤٩)
O you who have faith! If you obey the faithless, they will turn you back on your heels, and you will become losers. (149)
بَلِ ٱللَّهُ مَوْلَىٰكُمْ ۖ وَهُوَ خَيْرُ ٱلنَّـٰصِرِينَ (١٥٠)
Indeed, Allah is your Master, and He is the best of helpers. (150)
سَنُلْقِى فِى قُلُوبِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ٱلرُّعْبَ بِمَآ أَشْرَكُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ مَا لَمْ يُنَزِّلْ بِهِۦ سُلْطَـٰنًا ۖ وَمَأْوَىٰهُمُ ٱلنَّارُ ۚ وَبِئْسَ مَثْوَى ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (١٥١)
We shall cast terror into the hearts of the faithless because of their ascribing partners to Allah, for which He has not sent down any authority, and their refuge shall be the Fire, and evil is the [final] abode of the wrongdoers. (151)
وَلَقَدْ صَدَقَكُمُ ٱللَّهُ وَعْدَهُۥٓ إِذْ تَحُسُّونَهُم بِإِذْنِهِۦ ۖ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا فَشِلْتُمْ وَتَنَـٰزَعْتُمْ فِى ٱلْأَمْرِ وَعَصَيْتُم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ مَآ أَرَىٰكُم مَّا تُحِبُّونَ ۚ مِنكُم مَّن يُرِيدُ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَمِنكُم مَّن يُرِيدُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةَ ۚ ثُمَّ صَرَفَكُمْ عَنْهُمْ لِيَبْتَلِيَكُمْ ۖ وَلَقَدْ عَفَا عَنكُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ ذُو فَضْلٍ عَلَى ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (١٥٢)
Allah certainly fulfilled His promise to you when you were slaying them with His leave, until you lost courage, disputed about the matter, and disobeyed after He showed you what you loved. Some among you desire this world, and some among you desire the Hereafter. Then He turned you away from them so that He might test you. Certainly He has excused you, for Allah is gracious to the faithful. (152)
۞ إِذْ تُصْعِدُونَ وَلَا تَلْوُۥنَ عَلَىٰٓ أَحَدٍ وَٱلرَّسُولُ يَدْعُوكُمْ فِىٓ أُخْرَىٰكُمْ فَأَثَـٰبَكُمْ غَمًّۢا بِغَمٍّ لِّكَيْلَا تَحْزَنُوا۟ عَلَىٰ مَا فَاتَكُمْ وَلَا مَآ أَصَـٰبَكُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ خَبِيرٌۢ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ (١٥٣)
When you were fleeing without paying any attention to anyone, while the Apostle was calling you from your rear, He requited you with grief upon grief, so that you may not grieve for what you lose nor for what befalls you, and Allah is well aware of what you do. (153)
ثُمَّ أَنزَلَ عَلَيْكُم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ ٱلْغَمِّ أَمَنَةً نُّعَاسًا يَغْشَىٰ طَآئِفَةً مِّنكُمْ ۖ وَطَآئِفَةٌ قَدْ أَهَمَّتْهُمْ أَنفُسُهُمْ يَظُنُّونَ بِٱللَّهِ غَيْرَ ٱلْحَقِّ ظَنَّ ٱلْجَـٰهِلِيَّةِ ۖ يَقُولُونَ هَل لَّنَا مِنَ ٱلْأَمْرِ مِن شَىْءٍ ۗ قُلْ إِنَّ ٱلْأَمْرَ كُلَّهُۥ لِلَّهِ ۗ يُخْفُونَ فِىٓ أَنفُسِهِم مَّا لَا يُبْدُونَ لَكَ ۖ يَقُولُونَ لَوْ كَانَ لَنَا مِنَ ٱلْأَمْرِ شَىْءٌ مَّا قُتِلْنَا هَـٰهُنَا ۗ قُل لَّوْ كُنتُمْ فِى بُيُوتِكُمْ لَبَرَزَ ٱلَّذِينَ كُتِبَ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْقَتْلُ إِلَىٰ مَضَاجِعِهِمْ ۖ وَلِيَبْتَلِىَ ٱللَّهُ مَا فِى صُدُورِكُمْ وَلِيُمَحِّصَ مَا فِى قُلُوبِكُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌۢ بِذَاتِ ٱلصُّدُورِ (١٥٤)
Then He sent down to you safety after grief—a drowsiness that came over a group of you—while another group, anxious only about themselves, entertained false notions about Allah, notions of [pagan] ignorance. They say, ‘Do we have any role in the matter?’ Say, ‘Indeed the matter belongs totally to Allah.’ They hide in their hearts what they do not disclose to you. They say, ‘Had we any role in the matter, we would not have been slain here.’ Say, ‘Even if you had remained in your houses, those destined to be slain would have set out toward the places where they were laid to rest, so that Allah may test what is in your breasts, and that He may purge what is in your hearts, and Allah knows well what is in the breasts.’ (154)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ تَوَلَّوْا۟ مِنكُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْتَقَى ٱلْجَمْعَانِ إِنَّمَا ٱسْتَزَلَّهُمُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ بِبَعْضِ مَا كَسَبُوا۟ ۖ وَلَقَدْ عَفَا ٱللَّهُ عَنْهُمْ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ حَلِيمٌ (١٥٥)
Those of you who fled on the day when the two hosts met, only Satan had made them stumble because of some of their deeds. Certainly, Allah has excused them, for Allah is all-forgiving, all-forbearing. (155)

(Commentary)

These verses are a continuation of those revealed specifically regarding the Battle of Uhud. They contain an exhortation and encouragement for the believers (المؤمنين) not to obey anyone other than their Lord, for He alone is their Master (المولى) and Helper. They also serve as a testimony to the truth of His promise, clarifying that the defeat and abandonment they suffered on the day of Uhud stemmed only from themselves—due to their transgression of the limits Allah had commanded them to observe and the call of His Messenger—and that Allah, the Glorified, despite this, pardoned their crimes because He is All-Forgiving (غفور), All-Clement (حليم).

His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِنْ تُطِيعُوا الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا — "O you who have faith! If you obey the faithless..." (3:149) until the end of the two verses. It is not improbable to infer from the context that during the days these verses were revealed following the Battle of Uhud, the disbelievers (الكفار) were casting suggestions to the believers—under the guise of advice—intended to discourage them from fighting, to sow discord and division, and to scatter their unity. This is perhaps supported by what appears at the end of these verses: الَّذِينَ قالَ لَهُمُ النَّاسُ إِنَّ النَّاسَ قد جَمَعُوا لَكُمْ فَاخْشَوْهُمْ — "Those to whom the people said, 'All the people have mustered against you, so fear them'..." until His saying: ذلِكُمُ الشَّيْطانُ يُخَوِّفُ أَوْلِياءَهُ فَلا تَخافُوهُمْ وَخافُونِ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ — "That is only Satan frightening his allies. So do not fear them, but fear Me, if you are believers" (3:173–175).

It has been suggested that the verse refers to the statement of the Jews and the hypocrites (المنافقين) on the day of Uhud: "Muhammad has been killed, so return to your kinsmen," but this is of no consequence.

Then, after explaining that their obedience to the disbelievers and leaning toward their guardianship would lead them to loss (الخسران)—which is their turning back on their heels as disbelievers—He turns away from that by saying: بَلِ اللهُ مَوْلاكُمْ وَهُوَ خَيْرُ النَّاصِرِينَ — "Rather Allah is your master, and He is the best of helpers" (3:150).

His saying, the Exalted: سَنُلْقِي فِي قُلُوبِ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا الرُّعْبَ بِما أَشْرَكُوا بِاللهِ — "We will cast terror into the hearts of the faithless because of their ascribing to Allah partners..." (3:151) etc. This is a beautiful promise to the believers that they will be aided by terror [cast into the hearts of their enemies]. The Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم used to mention this among the gifts and distinctions Allah the Exalted bestowed upon him over other prophets, as narrated by both schools [Sunni and Shi'a].

His saying: بِما أَشْرَكُوا — "because of their ascribing partners," means: they took as partners to Him that for which they have no proof (برهان). It is a recurring theme in the Quran that there is no authority (سلطان) for establishing a partner to Allah. Included in "establishing a partner" is the denial of the Creator (الصانع) and attributing influence and governance (التدبير) to other than Him, such as time (الدهر) or matter (المادة).

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ صَدَقَكُمُ اللهُ وَعْدَهُ إِذْ تَحُسُّونَهُمْ — "Allah certainly fulfilled His promise to you when you were slaying them..." (3:152) until the end of the verse. The term al-hass (الحس)—with a fatha—means killing in a manner of total eradication.

The narrations are in agreement, and history has recorded regarding the Battle of Uhud, that the believers initially overcame and triumphed over the enemy, putting them to the sword and beginning to plunder their wealth. This continued until the archers abandoned their positions in the ambush point. At that moment, Khalid ibn al-Walid, with those with him, charged at Abdullah ibn Jubayr and the remaining archers, killing them. They then attacked the believers from behind. The polytheists (المشركون) returned from their retreat, put the companions of the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم to the sword, killed seventy of them, and then defeated them severely.

Thus, His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ صَدَقَكُمُ اللهُ وَعْدَهُ — "Allah certainly fulfilled His promise to you," is an affirmation of the truth of His promise of victory, conditional upon godwariness (التقوى) and patience (الصبر). His saying: إِذْ تَحُسُّونَهُمْ بِإِذْنِهِ — "when you were slaying them by His leave," is applicable to the triumph over their enemy that they were granted at the beginning of the day of Uhud. His saying: حَتَّى إِذا فَشِلْتُمْ وَتَنازَعْتُمْ فِي الْأَمْرِ وَعَصَيْتُمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما أَراكُمْ ما تُحِبُّونَ — "...until you lost heart and disputed about the matter, and disobeyed after He had shown you what you loved," applies to what the archers did when they disputed among themselves about leaving their positions to join those with the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم to obtain the spoils. Thus, they lost heart (فشلتم), disputed the matter, and disobeyed the Prophet's command not to leave their positions under any circumstances. Accordingly, "losing heart" (الفشل) must be interpreted here as a weakness of judgment (ضعف الرأي). As for it meaning cowardice (الجبن), it does not apply to them, as their action was not out of cowardice but out of greed for the spoils. If "losing heart" (الفشل) meant cowardice, it would apply to the state of the entire group, and in that case, the word "then" (ثم) in His saying: ثُمَّ صَرَفَكُمْ — "Then He turned you away," would indicate a rank-based sequence (التراخي الرتبي) rather than a temporal one.

The word "disputed" (التنازع) indicates that not all of them were unanimous in losing heart and disobedience; rather, some of them insisted on obedience and remaining at their posts. Therefore, Allah the Exalted said afterward: مِنْكُمْ مَنْ يُرِيدُ الدُّنْيا وَمِنْكُمْ مَنْ يُرِيدُ الْآخِرَةَ — "Among you were those who desired this world, and among you were those who desired the Hereafter."

His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ صَرَفَكُمْ عَنْهُمْ لِيَبْتَلِيَكُمْ — "Then He turned you away from them so that He might test you," means He restrained you from the polytheists after the appearance of failure, dispute, and disobedience—in short, after the occurrence of discord (الاختلاف) among you—to try you and test your faith and your patience for the sake of Allah. Discord in the hearts is the strongest factor necessitating the expansion of trial (الابتلاء), so that the believer may be distinguished from the hypocrite, and the believer who is firm in faith and steadfast in resolve may be distinguished from the fickle one whose resolve quickly vanishes. Despite this, Allah the Glorified pardoned them by His grace, as He said: وَلَقَدْ عَفا عَنْكُمْ — "and He has certainly excused you."

His saying, the Exalted: إِذْ تُصْعِدُونَ وَلا تَلْوُونَ عَلى أَحَدٍ وَالرَّسُولُ يَدْعُوكُمْ فِي أُخْراكُمْ — "When you were fleeing without paying any attention to anyone, while the Apostle was calling you from your rear..." (3:153). The term al-is'ad (الإصعاد) means going away and distancing oneself in the land, as opposed to al-su'ud (الصعود), which is ascending to a high place. It is said: "He as'ada in the side of the desert," meaning he went far into it; and "He sa'ada the ladder," meaning he climbed it. It is also said that al-is'ad is sometimes used in the sense of al-su'ud.

The adverbial phrase [When you were fleeing] relates to an implied verb, i.e., "Remember when you were fleeing," or to His saying: صَرَفَكُمْ — "He turned you away," or to His saying: لِيَبْتَلِيَكُمْ — "so that He might test you," as has been suggested. His saying: وَلا تَلْوُونَ — "without paying any attention," is from al-layy (اللي), meaning turning or leaning toward. It is mentioned in al-Majma' that it is only used in the negative; one does not say, "I leaned (lawaytu) toward such-and-such."

His saying: وَالرَّسُولُ يَدْعُوكُمْ فِي أُخْراكُمْ — "while the Apostle was calling you from your rear." The "rear" (al-ukhra) is the opposite of the "front" (al-ula). The fact that the Messenger was calling while in their rear indicates that they had scattered away from him صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم, forming a long line of groups: the foremost were far from him, and the hindmost were near him. He was calling them while they paid no attention to him—neither the foremost nor the hindmost. They left him صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم amidst the masses of the polytheists while they were fleeing (yus'idun) to escape death.

Indeed, His saying shortly before this: وَسَيَجْزِي اللهُ الشَّاكِرِينَ — "and Allah will soon reward the grateful" (3:144)—the commentary of which has passed—indicates that among them were those whose resolve did not waver and who did not flee, neither at the start of the retreat nor after the rumor of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم death spread, as indicated by His saying: أَفَإِنْ ماتَ أَوْ قُتِلَ انْقَلَبْتُمْ — "If he dies or is slain, will you turn back on your heels?" (3:144).

What is also indicated by His saying: وَلا تَلْوُونَ عَلى أَحَدٍ وَالرَّسُولُ يَدْعُوكُمْ فِي أُخْراكُمْ — "without paying any attention to anyone, while the Apostle was calling you from your rear," is that the news of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم death only spread among them after their retreat and flight.

His saying, the Exalted: فَأَثابَكُمْ غَمًّا بِغَمٍّ لِكَيْلا تَحْزَنُوا عَلى ما فاتَكُمْ وَلا ما أَصابَكُمْ — "So He requited you with grief upon grief, so that you may not grieve for what you lose, nor for what befalls you..." (3:153). That is, He recompensed you with grief for grief to divert you from grieving over such-and-such. This grief with which they were requited, whatever it may have been, is a blessing from Him, the Exalted, as evidenced by His saying: لِكَيْلا تَحْزَنُوا عَلى ما فاتَكُمْ وَلا ما أَصابَكُمْ — "so that you may not grieve for what you lose, nor for what befalls you." For Allah the Exalted has censured this type of grief in His Book, as He said: لِكَيْلا تَأْسَوْا عَلى ما فاتَكُمْ — "so that you may not grieve for what has escaped you" (57:23). Thus, this grief that diverts them from that censured grief is a blessing and a gift. It would then be the grief that came upon them due to regret for what they had done and sorrow for the victory they missed because of their failure. In this case, the second "grief" in His saying: بِغَمٍ — "upon grief," would be the grief arising from the aforementioned sorrow. The preposition bi (ب) here denotes substitution (البدلية). The meaning would be: He recompensed you with the grief of regret and sorrow over the loss of victory in exchange for the grief of sorrow over what you lost and what befell you.

It is also possible that His saying: فَأَثابَكُمْ — "So He requited you," implies the meaning of "replacement," so the meaning would be: He replaced the grief of sorrow with the grief of regret and remorse as a reward for you. In this case, the meanings of the two griefs would be reversed relative to the previous interpretation.

According to either of the two meanings, His saying: فَأَثابَكُمْ — "So He requited you," is a result of His saying: وَلَقَدْ عَفا عَنْكُمْ — "and He has certainly excused you," and what follows it—namely His saying: ثُمَّ أَنْزَلَ عَلَيْكُمْ — "Then He sent down to you"—connects to it perfectly. The sequence is: He pardoned you, then He requited you with grief upon grief to protect you from the grief He does not approve for you, then He sent down upon you, after the grief, security in the form of slumber.

There is another view supported by the apparent context, which makes His saying: فَأَثابَكُمْ — "So He requited you," a result of what it is immediately connected to. This means the [first] grief is what is contained in His saying: إِذْ تُصْعِدُونَ — "When you were fleeing," and the meaning of His saying: بِغَمٍ — "upon grief," is what the dispute and disobedience led to, which was the polytheists' overlooking them from behind. The preposition bi (ب) here would denote causality (السببية). This is a fine meaning. Accordingly, the meaning of His saying: لِكَيْلا تَحْزَنُوا — "so that you may not grieve," etc., would be: We explain to you the reality of the matter so that you may not grieve, as in His saying, the Exalted: ما أَصابَ مِنْ مُصِيبَةٍ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَلا فِي أَنْفُسِكُمْ إِلَّا فِي كِتابٍ مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ نَبْرَأَها إِنَّ ذلِكَ عَلَى اللهِ يَسِيرٌ لِكَيْلا تَأْسَوْا عَلى ما فاتَكُمْ وَلا تَفْرَحُوا بِما آتاكُمْ — "No affliction visits the earth or your souls but it is in a Book before We bring it about—that is indeed easy for Allah—so that you may not grieve for what has escaped you, nor be exultant at what He has given you" (57:22–23).

This is what makes the arrangement of the verse and the sequence of the consecutive sentences sound. The commentators have many conjectures regarding the verse—concerning what His saying: فَأَثابَكُمْ — "So He requited you," is coordinated with, the meaning of the first and second grief, the meaning of the preposition bi (ب), and the meaning of His saying: لِكَيْلا — "so that [you may] not." However, these are not sound, and there is no benefit in quoting or discussing them.

Based on the two meanings we have suggested, the meaning of "what you lose" in His saying: لِكَيْلا تَحْزَنُوا عَلى ما فاتَكُمْ — "so that you may not grieve for what you lose," refers to victory and spoils; and "what befalls you" refers to the killing and wounding that the people suffered.

His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ أَنْزَلَ عَلَيْكُمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ الْغَمِّ أَمَنَةً نُعاساً يَغْشى طائِفَةً مِنْكُمْ — "Then He sent down to you, after the grief, security—a slumber overtaking a part of you..." (3:154). The term al-amanah (الأمنة)—with a fatha on the mim—means security (الأمن). Al-nu'as (النعاس) is the lethargy that precedes sleep; it is a light sleep. The word "slumber" (nu'asan) is a substitute (بدل) for "security" (amanatan) because they are usually concomitant. It has also been suggested that amanah is the plural of amin (one who is secure), like talabah is the plural of talib; in that case, it would be a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for the pronoun "you" ('alaykum), and "slumber" (nu'asan) would be the object of His saying: "He sent down." "Overtaking" (al-ghashiyan) means surrounding.

The verse indicates that this descending slumber only overtook a group of the people and did not encompass everyone, as evidenced by His saying: طائِفَةً مِنْكُمْ — "a part of you." These are the ones who returned to the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم after the retreat and flight, when they felt regret and sorrow. It is far from Allah's way that He would pardon them with a pardon of mercy while they were in the state of fleeing from the battlefield—which is among the major sins (كبائر المعاصي)—whereas He said: وَلَقَدْ عَفا عَنْكُمْ وَاللهُ ذُو فَضْلٍ عَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ — "and He has certainly excused you, and Allah is gracious to the faithful." It is also far from His way that His care would encompass the perpetrator of indecency and evil while he is committing it, before he repents. He showed care for them when He requited them with grief upon grief so that they would not grieve, lest their hearts be defiled by what Allah, the Glorified, does not approve, as previously explained.

So, these were some of the people: those who regretted what they had done, returned to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم, and gathered around him. This occurred when he صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم left the masses of the polytheists and returned to the mountain pass, even if their return to him was gradual after learning that he had not been killed.

As for the other part of the people, they are those whom Allah mentions by His saying: وَطائِفَةٌ قَدْ أَهَمَّتْهُمْ أَنْفُسُهُمْ — "while another part was solicitous only about themselves."

His saying, the Exalted: وَطائِفَةٌ قَدْ أَهَمَّتْهُمْ أَنْفُسُهُمْ — "while another part was solicitous only about themselves." This is another group of the believers. By calling them "believers," we mean they were not the hypocrites whom Allah mentioned later by His saying: وَلِيَعْلَمَ الَّذِينَ نافَقُوا وَقِيلَ لَهُمْ تَعالَوْا قاتِلُوا فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ أَوِ ادْفَعُوا قالُوا لَوْ نَعْلَمُ قِتالاً لَاتَّبَعْناكُمْ — "and so that He may know those who are hypocrites. And it was said to them, 'Come, fight in the way of Allah, or defend [yourselves].' They said, 'If we knew any fighting, we would have surely followed you'" (3:167). These were the ones who separated from the body of the believers at the beginning, before the fighting, and withdrew. These hypocrites have another story that Allah will soon relate.

This second group, described as being solicitous only about themselves, was not honored by Allah with what He honored the first group—pardon, the requital of grief, and then security and slumber. Rather, He left them to themselves, so they were solicitous only about themselves and forgot everything else besides them.

Allah the Exalted has mentioned two of their characteristics, even though one is a necessary consequence and branch of the other. He mentioned that they were solicitous only about themselves. This does not mean they desired the true happiness of their souls, for the [true] believers also desire nothing but the happiness of their souls; indeed, man—rather, every being with purpose and will—desires nothing but himself. Rather, it means they had no concern except preserving their worldly life and avoiding falling into the trap of death. They did not desire religion or anything else except to enjoy themselves in this world. They only affiliated themselves with the religion thinking it was an invincible factor and that Allah would not allow His enemies to prevail over it, even if the apparent causes were in their favor. These people milk the religion as long as it yields; but if the situation turns and fortune does not favor them, they turn back on their heels.

His saying, the Exalted: يَظُنُّونَ بِاللهِ غَيْرَ الْحَقِّ ظَنَّ الْجاهِلِيَّةِ — "thinking of Allah quite unjustly with the thoughts of [the age of] ignorance..." (3:154) until His saying: بِاللهِ — "of Allah." That is, they thought of Allah something that is not true; rather, it was from the thoughts of the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية). They described Him with an attribute that is not true, but rather one of the attributes with which the people of the Age of Ignorance used to describe Him. This thought, whatever it may be, is something suited and accompanied by their saying: هَلْ لَنا مِنَ الْأَمْرِ مِنْ شَيْءٍ — "Is there anything for us in the matter?" It is revealed by what the Prophet was commanded to answer them with, which is His saying: قُلْ إِنَّ الْأَمْرَ كُلَّهُ لِلَّهِ — "Say, 'Indeed the matter belongs wholly to Allah.'" The apparent sense of this answer is that they thought some of the matter belonged to them; therefore, when they were defeated and killing spread among them, they doubted and said: "Is there anything for us in the matter?"

From this, it becomes clear that the "matter" they saw as belonging to them was triumph and victory. They only thought it belonged to them because of their Islam. They thought that the true religion cannot be defeated and that those who profess it cannot be overcome, because it is incumbent upon Allah to aid it without any condition or restriction, and He had promised them this.

This is the "unjust thought" (بغير الحق), which is the "thought of the Age of Ignorance." For the paganism of the Age of Ignorance believed that Allah the Exalted is the Creator of all things, but that for every category of events—such as sustenance, life, death, love, war, and others—and likewise for every type of cosmic entity—such as man, the earth, the seas, and others—there is a lord (رب) who governs its affairs and whose will cannot be overcome. They used to worship these lords so that they would provide them with sustenance, bring them happiness, and protect them from evils and calamities. Allah, the Glorified, was like a great king who delegates every category of his subjects and every part of his kingdom to a governor with full choice, who may do as he pleases within his zone of influence and domain of authority.

If a person thinks that the true religion cannot be defeated in its outward progress, and that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم—who is the first to receive it from his Lord and carry its burdens—cannot be conquered in his outward mission, or that he cannot be killed or die, then he has thought of Allah quite unjustly with the thoughts of the Age of Ignorance. He has taken peers (أنداد) for Allah and made the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم a pagan lord to whom the matter of victory and spoils has been delegated. Yet Allah, the Glorified, is One, having no partner; to Him the matter belongs wholly, and no one has anything of the matter. Therefore, when He said in the preceding verses: لِيَقْطَعَ طَرَفاً مِنَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا أَوْ يَكْبِتَهُمْ فَيَنْقَلِبُوا خائِبِينَ — "that He may cut off a part of the faithless or humiliate them so that they retreat disappointed" (3:127), He interrupted the speech with a parenthetical address to His Prophet, saying: لَيْسَ لَكَ مِنَ الْأَمْرِ شَيْءٌ — "You have no hand in the matter" (3:128), lest it be imagined that he صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم had any role in the "cutting off" or "humiliating." Allah, the Glorified, is the one who established the law of causes and effects (الأسباب والمسببات). Whichever cause is stronger, its effect is more likely to occur, whether in truth or falsehood, good or evil, guidance or misguidance, justice or injustice. There is no difference in this between the believer and the disbeliever, the beloved and the hated, or Muhammad and Abu Sufyan.

Yes, Allah the Glorified has a special care for His religion and His friends (أولياء), because of which He conducts the system of the universe in a way that leads to the triumph of the religion and the preparation of the earth for His friends, and the outcome is for the God-conscious (المتقين).

The matter of prophethood and the mission is not an exception to this prevailing law. Therefore, whenever the ordinary causes coincided with the progress of this religion and the triumph of the believers—as in some of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم battles—that occurred. And where the causes did not coincide—such as the occurrence of hypocrisy, or disobedience to the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم command, or losing heart, or anxiety—victory and triumph were for the polytheists over the believers. The same is true for the case of other prophets with the people. Since the enemies of the prophets were people of this world, limiting their efforts to the cultivation of the world, the expansion of power, the intensification of strength, and the gathering of masses, the outward victory and triumph were theirs over the prophets. Thus, some were killed like Zechariah, some slaughtered like John, and some displaced like Jesus, and so on.

Yes, if the triumph of the truth—by virtue of its being the truth—depends on the suspension of the system of habit (نظام العادة) rather than the actual law (السنة الواقعية)—in other words, if the matter of the truth hangs between life and death—then it is incumbent upon Allah the Glorified to uphold the pillar of the religion and not let its proof be refuted. A portion of this discussion has already passed in the discourse on miracles (الإعجاز) in the first volume of this book, and in the discourse on the rulings of actions in the second volume.

Let us return to our subject: the statement of this group who were solicitous only about themselves: هَلْ لَنا مِنَ الْأَمْرِ مِنْ شَيْءٍ — "Is there anything for us in the matter?" was a doubt regarding the truth of the religion, and they had inserted into its structure the spirit of paganism, as previously explained. So Allah, the Glorified, commanded His Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم to answer them, saying: قُلْ إِنَّ الْأَمْرَ كُلَّهُ لِلَّهِ — "Say, 'Indeed the matter belongs wholly to Allah.'" He had addressed His Prophet before that by saying: لَيْسَ لَكَ مِنَ الْأَمْرِ شَيْءٌ — "You have no hand in the matter." Thus, He clarified that the primordial creed (ملة الفطرة) and the religion of monotheism (دين التوحيد) is that in which no one owns the matter except Allah, majestic is His state. All other things, including the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم, have no independent influence; rather, they are within the domain of causes and effects and the divine law (السنة الإلهية) that leads to the operation of the principle of trial and testing (الابتلاء والامتحان).

His saying, the Exalted: يُخْفُونَ فِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ ما لا يُبْدُونَ لَكَ يَقُولُونَ لَوْ كانَ — "They hide in their hearts what they do not reveal to you. They say, 'Had we...'" (3:154) etc. This is a description of them that is more severe than their saying: "Is there anything for us in the matter?" For that was a doubt in the form of a question, while this—namely their saying: لَوْ كانَ لَنا مِنَ الْأَمْرِ شَيْءٌ ما قُتِلْنا هاهُنا — "Had we any say in the matter, we would not have been slain here"—is a preference in the form of an argument. Therefore, they revealed their first statement to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم but hid their second statement because it contained a preference for disbelief over Islam.

So Allah the Exalted commanded His Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم to answer them, saying: قُلْ لَوْ كُنْتُمْ فِي بُيُوتِكُمْ لَبَرَزَ الَّذِينَ كُتِبَ عَلَيْهِمُ الْقَتْلُ إِلى مَضاجِعِهِمْ وَلِيَبْتَلِيَ اللهُ ما فِي صُدُورِكُمْ وَلِيُمَحِّصَ ما فِي قُلُوبِكُمْ — "Say, 'Had you been in your houses, those whose death was decreed would have surely gone forth to their deathbeds, so that Allah may test what is in your breasts, and that He may purge what is in your hearts'" (3:154). Thus, He explained to them:

First: That the killing of those among you who were killed in the battle was not because you were not on the truth, nor because the matter was not yours as you claim. Rather, it was because the divine decree (القضاء الإلهي)—which must inevitably be executed and passed—ran its course so that these slain ones would lie in these deathbeds. Even if you had not gone out to fight, those for whom death was decreed would have gone forth to their deathbeds. There is no escape from the appointed term (الأجل المسمى), which you can neither delay by an hour nor hasten.

Second: That the law of Allah (سنة الله) has run its course regarding the universality of trial (الابتلاء) and purging (التمحيص), and it will inevitably occur to them and to you. Thus, it was necessary for you to go out and for this fighting to occur, so that the slain could reach their stations and attain their ranks, and you could reach your stations, whereby one of the two sides—happiness or wretchedness—would be determined for you through the testing of the thoughts in your breasts and the purification of the faith or polytheism in your hearts.

Among the strange things mentioned regarding this verse is the statement of several commentators that the group whose state the verse describes are the hypocrites, despite the clear context of the verses showing that they describe the state of the believers. As for the hypocrites—meaning the companions of Abdullah ibn Ubayy who withdrew at the start of the event before the fighting—their state will be addressed later.

Unless, of course, they mean by "hypocrites" those weak in faith whose contradictory beliefs necessarily lead to denying the truth in the heart while professing it with the tongue. These are the ones whom Allah calls "those in whose hearts is a sickness" (الذين في قلوبهم مرض). He, the Exalted, said: إِذْ يَقُولُ الْمُنافِقُونَ وَالَّذِينَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ غَرَّ هؤُلاءِ دِينُهُمْ — "When the hypocrites and those in whose hearts is a sickness said, 'Their religion has deluded them'" (8:49). And He said: وَفِيكُمْ سَمَّاعُونَ لَهُمْ — "and among you are some who would listen to them" (9:47). Or they might mean that not all the hypocrites returned to Medina with the companions of Abdullah ibn Ubayy.

Even stranger is the statement of some others that this group were believers, and that they thought the matter of victory and triumph belonged to them because they were on the true religion of Allah, having seen the victory and the descent of the angels on the day of Badr. Thus, their saying: "Is there anything for us in the matter?" and "Had we any say in the matter..." etc., was an admission from them that the matter belongs to Allah and not to them; otherwise, death would not have eradicated them.

This is refuted by the fact that the answer would then be inconsistent—namely His saying, the Exalted: "Say, 'Indeed the matter belongs wholly to Allah,'" and His saying: "Say, 'Had you been in your houses...'" etc. Some of these [commentators] sensed this problem and answered it with something even worse than their original statement. You have already learned the true meaning.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ تَوَلَّوْا مِنْكُمْ يَوْمَ الْتَقَى الْجَمْعانِ إِنَّمَا اسْتَزَلَّهُمُ الشَّيْطانُ بِبَعْضِ ما كَسَبُوا — "Those of you who turned back on the day when the two hosts met, it was only Satan who made them stumble because of some of what they had performed..." (3:155). Satan's "making them stumble" (istizlal) means his desire for them to fall into a slip (الزلة). He only desired this from them because of some of what they had performed (kasabu) in their souls and through their actions. For evil deeds lead to one another, as they are based on following the whim of the soul (هوى النفس), and the soul's whim for something is a whim for what resembles it.

As for the possibility that the preposition bi (ب) denotes the instrument and that "what they performed" is the very act of their turning back on the day of the meeting, this is far from the apparent wording. For the apparent sense of "what they performed" (ma kasabu) is that the performance preceded the turning back and the stumbling.

In any case, the apparent sense of the verse is that some of the sins and offenses they had previously committed enabled Satan to beguile them into turning back and fleeing. From here, it becomes clear that the possibility of the verse referring to Satan's cry on the day of Uhud that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم had been killed—as mentioned in some narrations—is of no consequence, as there is no indication of it in the wording.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ عَفَا اللهُ عَنْهُمْ إِنَّ اللهَ غَفُورٌ حَلِيمٌ — "and Allah has certainly excused them. Indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-clement" (3:155). This pardon (العفو) is for those who turned back, mentioned at the beginning of the verse. The verse is general, encompassing all who turned back that day; thus, it includes both groups—the group overtaken by slumber and the group solicitous only about themselves. The two groups differ in being honored with Allah's special favor or not. Because they are different, He did not mention with this general pardon—which includes both of them—the aspects of honor that were included in the pardon specifically for the first group, as previously explained.

From this, it becomes clear that this pardon mentioned in this verse is different from the pardon mentioned in His saying: وَلَقَدْ عَفا عَنْكُمْ — "and He has certainly excused you" (3:152). Evidence for the difference between the two pardons is the difference in tone in the two verses. There is a clear difference between His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ عَفا عَنْكُمْ وَاللهُ ذُو فَضْلٍ عَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ — "and He has certainly excused you, and Allah is gracious to the faithful," as it is a statement suggesting grace and compassion, and He called them "believers," then mentioned requiting them with grief upon grief so they would not grieve, then sending down security and slumber upon them; and His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ عَفَا اللهُ عَنْهُمْ إِنَّ اللهَ غَفُورٌ حَلِيمٌ — "and Allah has certainly excused them. Indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-clement," where He mentioned the pardon but remained silent about all that He honored the first group with, then concluded the speech by mentioning His clemency (hilm)—which is not to hasten the punishment. A pardon accompanied by clemency is an overlooking that conceals an underlying displeasure. If you say: "Those who equated the two groups did so because the word 'pardon' was used for both," I say: the meaning of pardon differs in the two cases according to the specific instance (المصداق), even if the concept (مفهوم) of pardon applies to all equally. There is no proof that pardon, forgiveness (المغفرة), and the like are of the same type in all instances. We have already explained the reason for the difference.

(The Meaning of Pardon and Forgiveness in the Quran)

Pardon (العفو), according to what al-Raghib mentioned—and it is the meaning derived from its instances of usage—is the intention to take or reach something. It is said: "He 'afahu and i'tafahu," meaning he sought him out to take what he had. "The wind 'afat the house," meaning it sought it out, taking away its traces. He said: The saying "The house 'afat" when it has decayed is based on a subtle nuance, which is that the house, as it were, sought its own traces and the outward signs of its beauty and took them, so it disappeared from the eyes of beholders. By this nuance, pardon is attributed to Him, the Exalted, as if He, the Exalted, attends to the servant, takes away the sin he has, and leaves him without sin.

From this, it becomes clear that forgiveness (المغفرة)—which is "veiling" (الستر)—is a branch of it in terms of conceptualization. For a thing, like a sin for example, is first taken and removed, then it is veiled so that the sinner's sin does not appear, neither to himself nor to others. He, the Exalted, said: وَاعْفُ عَنَّا وَاغْفِرْ لَنا — "and excuse us, and forgive us" (2:286), and He said: وَكانَ اللهُ عَفُوًّا غَفُوراً — "and Allah is all-excusing, all-forgiving" (4:99).

It has thus become clear that although pardon and forgiveness are different—one being a branch of the other in terms of mental nuance—they are one in terms of the actual instance (المصداق). Furthermore, their meaning is not among the meanings exclusive to Him, the Exalted; rather, it is correct to apply them to others besides Him, the Exalted, according to their meaning, as He, the Exalted, said: إِلَّا أَنْ يَعْفُونَ أَوْ يَعْفُوَا الَّذِي بِيَدِهِ عُقْدَةُ النِّكاحِ — "...unless they forgo [it] or he in whose hand is the marriage tie forgoes [it]" (2:237).
