And He, the Exalted, said: قُلْ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا يَغْفِرُوا لِلَّذِينَ لا يَرْجُونَ أَيَّامَ اللهِ — "Say to the faithful to forgive those who do not expect Allah’s days..." (45:14). And He, the Exalted, said: فَاعْفُ عَنْهُمْ وَاسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ وَشاوِرْهُمْ فِي الْأَمْرِ — "So excuse them and plead for [Allah’s] forgiveness for them, and consult them in the affairs" (3:159). Thus, He commanded His Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم to pardon them—meaning he should not apply the consequences of their disobedience, such as reprimand, censure, or turning away—and to seek forgiveness—meaning he should ask Allah to forgive them, and He, the Exalted, will inevitably do so regarding the consequences of the sin that pertain to Him.

It has also become clear that the meaning of pardon and forgiveness can relate to ontological (تكوينية), legislative (تشريعية), worldly, and otherworldly consequences altogether. He, the Exalted, said: وَما أَصابَكُمْ مِنْ مُصِيبَةٍ فَبِما كَسَبَتْ أَيْدِيكُمْ وَيَعْفُوا عَنْ كَثِيرٍ — "Whatever affliction may visit you is because of what your hands have earned, and He excuses many [an offense]" (42:30). The verse certainly includes worldly consequences and outcomes. Similar to it is His saying, the Exalted: وَالْمَلائِكَةُ يُسَبِّحُونَ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّهِمْ وَيَسْتَغْفِرُونَ لِمَنْ فِي الْأَرْضِ — "and the angels celebrate the praise of their Lord and plead for forgiveness for those who are on the earth" (42:5), according to its apparent meaning. Likewise is the statement of Adam and his spouse in what Allah related from them: رَبَّنا ظَلَمْنا أَنْفُسَنا وَإِنْ لَمْ تَغْفِرْ لَنا وَتَرْحَمْنا لَنَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْخاسرِينَ — "They said, 'Our Lord, we have wronged ourselves! If You do not forgive us and have mercy on us, we will surely be among the losers'" (7:23), based on the view that their wrongdoing was a disobedience to an advisory (إرشادي) prohibition rather than a masterly (مولوي) one.

Numerous Quranic verses indicate that proximity (القرب) and station (الزلفى) with Allah, and enjoying the blessings of Paradise, depend on the precedence of divine forgiveness and the removal of the rust (رين) of polytheism and sins through repentance (توبة) and the like, as He, the Exalted, said: كَلَّا بَلْ رانَ عَلى قُلُوبِهِمْ ما كانُوا يَكْسِبُونَ — "No indeed! Rather their hearts have been sullied by what they have been earning" (83:14). And He, the Exalted, said: وَمَنْ يُؤْمِنْ بِاللهِ يَهْدِ قَلْبَهُ — "and whoever has faith in Allah, He guides his heart" (64:11).

In short, pardon and forgiveness are of the nature of removing the obstacle (إزالة المانع) and lifting the contradictory opposite. Allah the Glorified has counted faith (الإيمان) and the Abode of the Hereafter (الدار الآخرة) as "life," and the effects of faith and the actions and vital course of the people of the Hereafter as "light," as He said: أَوَمَنْ كانَ مَيْتاً فَأَحْيَيْناهُ وَجَعَلْنا لَهُ نُوراً يَمْشِي بِهِ فِي النَّاسِ كَمَنْ مَثَلُهُ فِي الظُّلُماتِ لَيْسَ بِخارِجٍ مِنْها — "Is he who was lifeless, then We gave him life and maintained for him a light by which he walks among the people, like one whose likeness is [that of one] in a manifold darkness from which he cannot emerge?" (6:122). And He, the Exalted, said: وَإِنَّ الدَّارَ الْآخِرَةَ لَهِيَ الْحَيَوانُ — "and the abode of the Hereafter is indeed the [true] life" (29:64). Thus, polytheism is death and sins are darknesses. He, the Exalted, said: أَوْ كَظُلُماتٍ فِي بَحْرٍ لُجِّيٍّ يَغْشى طائِفَةً مِنْكُمْ يَغْشاهُ مَوْجٌ مِنْ فَوْقِهِ مَوْجٌ مِنْ فَوْقِهِ سَحابٌ ، ظُلُماتٌ بَعْضُها فَوْقَ بَعْضٍ إِذا أَخْرَجَ يَدَهُ لَمْ يَكَدْ يَراها وَمَنْ لَمْ يَجْعَلِ اللهُ لَهُ نُوراً فَما لَهُ مِنْ نُورٍ — "Or like manifold darkness in a deep sea, covered by billow upon billow, overcast by clouds; manifold darkness, one atop another; when he stretches out his hand, he can hardly see it. One whom Allah has not granted any light has no light" (24:40). Thus, forgiveness is the removal of death and darkness, and it only occurs through life—which is faith—and light—which is divine mercy.

The disbeliever has neither life nor light. The believer who is forgiven has life and light. If the believer has evil deeds with him, he is alive but his light is not complete; it is only completed through forgiveness. He, the Exalted, said: نُورُهُمْ يَسْعى بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَبِأَيْمانِهِمْ يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنا أَتْمِمْ لَنا نُورَنا وَاغْفِرْ لَنا — "...their light moving on before them and on their right, they say, 'Our Lord! Perfect our light for us, and forgive us'" (66:8).

It has appeared from all the foregoing that the instance (مصداق) of pardon and forgiveness, when attributed to Him, the Exalted, in ontological matters, is the removal of the obstacle by bringing about a cause that repels it; in legislative matters, it is the removal of the cause that prevents kindness and the like; and in the case of happiness and wretchedness, it is the removal of the obstacle to happiness.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَكُونُوا۟ كَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَقَالُوا۟ لِإِخْوَٰنِهِمْ إِذَا ضَرَبُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ أَوْ كَانُوا۟ غُزًّى لَّوْ كَانُوا۟ عِندَنَا مَا مَاتُوا۟ وَمَا قُتِلُوا۟ لِيَجْعَلَ ٱللَّهُ ذَٰلِكَ حَسْرَةً فِى قُلُوبِهِمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ يُحْىِۦ وَيُمِيتُ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ (١٥٦)
O you who have faith! Do not be like the faithless who say of their brethren, when they travel in the land or go into battle, ‘Had they stayed with us they would not have died or been killed,’ so that Allah may make it a regret in their hearts. But Allah gives life and brings death, and Allah watches what you do. (156)
وَلَئِن قُتِلْتُمْ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ أَوْ مُتُّمْ لَمَغْفِرَةٌ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَرَحْمَةٌ خَيْرٌ مِّمَّا يَجْمَعُونَ (١٥٧)
If you are slain in the way of Allah, or die, forgiveness and mercy from Allah are surely better than what they amass. (157)
وَلَئِن مُّتُّمْ أَوْ قُتِلْتُمْ لَإِلَى ٱللَّهِ تُحْشَرُونَ (١٥٨)
If you die or are slain, you will surely be mustered toward Allah. (158)
فَبِمَا رَحْمَةٍ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ لِنتَ لَهُمْ ۖ وَلَوْ كُنتَ فَظًّا غَلِيظَ ٱلْقَلْبِ لَٱنفَضُّوا۟ مِنْ حَوْلِكَ ۖ فَٱعْفُ عَنْهُمْ وَٱسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ وَشَاوِرْهُمْ فِى ٱلْأَمْرِ ۖ فَإِذَا عَزَمْتَ فَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُتَوَكِّلِينَ (١٥٩)
It is by Allah’s mercy that you are gentle to them; had you been harsh and hardhearted, they would have surely scattered from around you. So excuse them and plead for forgiveness for them, and consult them in the affairs, and once you are resolved, put your trust in Allah. Indeed Allah loves those who trust in Him. (159)
إِن يَنصُرْكُمُ ٱللَّهُ فَلَا غَالِبَ لَكُمْ ۖ وَإِن يَخْذُلْكُمْ فَمَن ذَا ٱلَّذِى يَنصُرُكُم مِّنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ ۗ وَعَلَى ٱللَّهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ (١٦٠)
If Allah helps you, no one can overcome you, but if He forsakes you, who will help you after Him? So in Allah let all the faithful put their trust. (160)
وَمَا كَانَ لِنَبِىٍّ أَن يَغُلَّ ۚ وَمَن يَغْلُلْ يَأْتِ بِمَا غَلَّ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۚ ثُمَّ تُوَفَّىٰ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ مَّا كَسَبَتْ وَهُمْ لَا يُظْلَمُونَ (١٦١)
A prophet may not breach his trust, and whoever breaches his trust will bring his breaches on the Day of Resurrection; then every soul shall be recompensed fully for what it has earned, and they will not be wronged. (161)
أَفَمَنِ ٱتَّبَعَ رِضْوَٰنَ ٱللَّهِ كَمَنۢ بَآءَ بِسَخَطٍ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَمَأْوَىٰهُ جَهَنَّمُ ۚ وَبِئْسَ ٱلْمَصِيرُ (١٦٢)
Is he who follows [the course of] Allah’s pleasure like him who earns Allah’s displeasure and whose refuge is hell, an evil destination? (162)
هُمْ دَرَجَـٰتٌ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ بَصِيرٌۢ بِمَا يَعْمَلُونَ (١٦٣)
They have ranks with Allah, and Allah watches what they do. (163)
لَقَدْ مَنَّ ٱللَّهُ عَلَى ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ إِذْ بَعَثَ فِيهِمْ رَسُولًا مِّنْ أَنفُسِهِمْ يَتْلُوا۟ عَلَيْهِمْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ وَيُزَكِّيهِمْ وَيُعَلِّمُهُمُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَٱلْحِكْمَةَ وَإِن كَانُوا۟ مِن قَبْلُ لَفِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ (١٦٤)
Allah certainly favoured the faithful when He raised up among them an apostle from among themselves to recite to them His signs and to purify them and teach them the Book and wisdom, and earlier they had indeed been in manifest error. (164)

(Commentary)

The verses are a continuation of those revealed specifically regarding the Battle of Uhud. They involve addressing another matter that occurred to them, namely the grief and regret that entered their hearts due to the killing of their leading men and the elite of their people. Most of those killed were from the Ansar (الأنصار) — the "Helpers" from the inhabitants of Medina who supported the Prophet — as it is said that only four of the Emigrants (المهاجرين) — those who migrated from Mecca to Medina for the sake of Allah — were killed. This strengthens the intuition that the bulk of the resistance was from the side of the Ansar, and that defeat overtook the Emigrants before them.

In short, the verses clarify the error and confusion inherent in this grief and regret. They then turn to another matter resulting from this sorrow and lamentation: their ill-supposition (سوء ظنهم) regarding the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, thinking that it was he who led them to this fate and cast them into this destruction. This is indicated by their saying, as alluded to in these verses: لَوْ كانُوا عِنْدَنا ما ماتُوا وَما قُتِلُوا "Had they been with us, they would not have died and would not have been killed" (3:156), and the saying of the hypocrites (المنافقين) — those who outwardly profess Islam while concealing disbelief in their hearts — in what follows: لَوْ أَطاعُونا ما قُتِلُوا "Had they obeyed us, they would not have been killed" (3:168); meaning, had they obeyed us and not obeyed the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, for it was he who caused their destruction. Thus, the verses clarify that it is not for him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to betray anyone; rather, he is a Messenger from Him, the Exalted, noble of soul, honorable of lineage, and great in character (عظيم الخلق), being gentle with them by a mercy from Allah. He pardons them, seeks forgiveness for them, and consults them in the matter by a command from Him, the Exalted. Furthermore, Allah has favored them by sending him to bring them out of misguidance (الضلال) into guidance (الهدى) — the divine leading toward truth and the straight path.

His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَكُونُوا كَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا "O you who have faith! Do not be like the faithless" (3:156). What is meant by "those who disbelieve" (الذين كفروا) is the literal meaning of the term, namely the disbelievers, rather than the hypocrites—as has been suggested—because hypocrisy (النفاق), qua hypocrisy, is not the source of this statement—even if the hypocrites say it—rather, its source is disbelief (الكفر); thus, it must be attributed to the disbelievers.

"Journeying" (الضرب في الأرض) is a metaphor for traveling. The word ghuzza (غزي) is the plural of ghazin (غاز), meaning "warrior," similar to the plural forms of talib (طالب) and darib (ضارب). His saying: لِيَجْعَلَ اللهُ ذلِكَ حَسْرَةً "so that Allah may make that a regret [in their hearts]" (3:156), means so that He may punish them by it; this is an instance of placing the consequence (المغيا) in the position of the objective (الغاية). His saying: وَاللهُ يُحْيِي وَيُمِيتُ "and it is Allah who gives life and makes to die" (3:156), is an explanation of the reality of the matter regarding which the disbelievers erred when they said: "Had they been..." This "death" includes both natural death and being killed, as necessitated by the absolute use of "death" (الموت) alone, as previously mentioned. His saying: وَاللهُ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ "and Allah sees best what you do" (3:156), serves as the justification for the prohibition in His saying: لا تَكُونُوا "Do not be..."

In His saying: ما ماتُوا وَما قُتِلُوا "they would not have died and would not have been killed" (3:156), death was mentioned before being killed so that the distribution (النشر) follows the order of the preceding mention (اللف) in His saying: إِذا ضَرَبُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ أَوْ كانُوا غُزًّى "when they journey in the land or go forth to fight" (3:156). Also, because death is a matter that proceeds according to nature and familiar custom, unlike being killed, which is an exceptional matter; thus, the familiar was mentioned before the unfamiliar.

The summary of the verse is a prohibition for the believers against being like the disbelievers, who say regarding those of them who die outside their city or people, or those killed in a battle: "Had they been with us, they would not have died and would not have been killed." For such a statement leads a person to spiritual torment and divine wrath, which is the regret (الحسرة) cast into their hearts. This is despite the fact that it stems from ignorance, for being near or far from them does not give life or cause death; rather, giving life (الإحياء) and causing death (الإماتة) are among the affairs exclusive to Allah alone, without partner. Therefore, let them be God-conscious (ليتقوا الله) and not be like them, for Allah is All-Seeing of what they do.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَئِنْ قُتِلْتُمْ فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ أَوْ مُتُّمْ لَمَغْفِرَةٌ مِنَ اللهِ وَرَحْمَةٌ خَيْرٌ مِمَّا يَجْمَعُونَ "If you are slain in the way of Allah, or die, forgiveness and mercy from Allah are surely better than what they amass" (3:157). It appears that what is meant by "what they amass" (مما يجمعون) is wealth and its related worldly goods, which are the primary objects of desire in the life of this world.

Being killed (القتل) was mentioned here before death (الموت) because being killed in the way of Allah is closer to forgiveness (المغفرة) than natural death; this subtle point (النكتة) is what necessitated the precedence of being killed over death. For this reason, He returned in the following verse: وَلَئِنْ مُتُّمْ أَوْ قُتِلْتُمْ لَإِلَى اللهِ تُحْشَرُونَ "And if you die or are slain, you will surely be mustered toward Allah" (3:158), to the natural order by mentioning death before being killed, due to the absence of that additional subtle point.

His saying, the Exalted: فَبِما رَحْمَةٍ مِنَ اللهِ لِنْتَ لَهُمْ "It is by Allah’s mercy that you are gentle to them" (3:159) to the end of the verse. The rough (الفظ) is the coarse and harsh person. "Hard-heartedness" (غلظ القلب) is a metaphor for the lack of tenderness and compassion. In fidad (الانفضاض) means dispersing.

In the verse, there is a transition (التفات) from addressing them to addressing the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. The original meaning is: Our Messenger has been gentle with you by a mercy from Us; therefore, We commanded him to pardon you, seek forgiveness for you, consult you in the matter, and to rely upon Us when he has decided.

The subtle point of this transition is what was mentioned at the beginning of the verses concerning the battle: that the discourse contains a trace of reproach and rebuke. For this reason, it included certain instances of turning away (الإعراض) in appropriate places, including this instance which addresses the explanation of one of their states related to the objection against the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. Their grieving for those killed among them might have led them to question the Prophet's صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله actions and to accuse him of leading them to slaughter and extermination. Thus, Allah, the Exalted, turned away from addressing them and turned toward His Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, addressing him with His saying: فَبِما رَحْمَةٍ مِنَ اللهِ لِنْتَ لَهُمْ "It is by Allah’s mercy that you are gentle to them."

The speech is branched (متفرع) from another discourse indicated by the context, and the implied meaning is: Since their state is as you see—imitating those who disbelieve and grieving over their slain—then by a mercy from Us, you were gentle with them; otherwise, they would have dispersed from around you. And Allah knows best.

His saying: فَاعْفُ عَنْهُمْ وَاسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ وَشاوِرْهُمْ فِي الْأَمْرِ "so pardon them and plead for [Allah’s] forgiveness for them, and consult them in the affairs" (3:159), was brought forth to serve as an endorsement of his صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله conduct, for that is how he used to act. He had consulted them regarding the matter of fighting shortly before the day of Uhud. In this, there is an intimation that he only does what he is commanded, and that Allah, the Glorified, is pleased with his actions.

Allah, the Exalted, commanded His Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to pardon them so that he does not apply the consequences of disobedience to their actions, and to seek forgiveness (الاستغفار) so that he asks Allah to forgive them—and He, the Exalted, will undoubtedly do so. Although the wording is absolute (مطلق) and not restricted to this specific instance, it does not include cases of legal punishments (الحدود الشرعية) and their likes; otherwise, legislation would be rendered futile. Furthermore, following it with His saying: وَشاوِرْهُمْ فِي الْأَمْرِ "and consult them in the affairs," is not devoid of the intimation that these two matters belong to the sphere of governance (الولاية) and the management of public affairs, wherein consultation with them takes place.

His saying: فَإِذا عَزَمْتَ فَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى اللهِ إِنَّ اللهَ يُحِبُّ الْمُتَوَكِّلِينَ "and when you have decided, trust in Allah; indeed Allah loves those who trust [in Him]" (3:159). When He loves you, He becomes your Guardian (وليا) and Helper (ناصرا), never forsaking you. Therefore, He followed the verse with this meaning and called the believers also to trust (التوكل) — the act of relying upon Allah while taking the necessary means — saying: إِنْ يَنْصُرْكُمُ اللهُ فَلا غالِبَ لَكُمْ وَإِنْ يَخْذُلْكُمْ فَمَنْ ذَا الَّذِي يَنْصُرُكُمْ مِنْ بَعْدِهِ "If Allah helps you, no one can overcome you, but if He forsakes you, who is it that will help you after Him?" (3:160). Then He commanded them to trust by placing its cause in its proper place, saying: وَعَلَى اللهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ "and in Allah let the faithful put their trust" (3:160); that is, because of their faith in Allah, besides Whom there is no helper or sustainer.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما كانَ لِنَبِيٍّ أَنْ يَغُلَّ "A prophet may not be false [to his trust]" (3:161). Ghall (الغل) is treachery or breach of trust (الخيانة). It was previously mentioned regarding the saying of the Exalted: ما كانَ لِبَشَرٍ أَنْ يُؤْتِيَهُ اللهُ الْكِتابَ "It does not behoove any human that Allah should give him the Book" (3:79), that this context means declaring the Prophet's station free from evil and indecency by virtue of his purity (الطهارة). The meaning is: Far be it from a Prophet to be false or betray his Lord or the people (which is also a betrayal of Allah), while the one who betrays shall meet his Lord with his betrayal, then every soul shall be paid in full what it has earned.

Then He mentioned that accusing the Prophet of betrayal is an unjust analogy with a clear distinction (الفارق); for he is a follower of Allah's pleasure (رضوان الله), never deviating from the pleasure of his Lord, whereas the betrayer has incurred a great wrath from Allah, and his abode is Hell—an evil destination. This is the meaning of His saying: أَفَمَنِ اتَّبَعَ رِضْوانَ اللهِ كَمَنْ باءَ بِسَخَطٍ مِنَ اللهِ "Is he who follows Allah’s pleasure like him who incurs Allah’s wrath?" (3:162).

It is possible that the intended meaning is to address the believers, suggesting that these states involve exposure to Allah's wrath, while Allah calls you through these exhortations to His pleasure, and the two are not equal.

Then He mentioned that these groups—those who follow Allah's pleasure and those who incur His wrath—occupy different ranks (درجات), and Allah is All-Seeing of their deeds. So do not imagine that the smallest amount of good or evil escapes Him, such that you might be negligent in following His pleasure or incurring His wrath.

His saying, the Exalted: لَقَدْ مَنَّ اللهُ عَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ "Allah certainly favoured the faithful" (3:164). In this verse, there is another transition (التفات) from addressing the believers to treating them as absent (الغيبة). The general reason for such transitions in these instances has been mentioned, and the specific reason here is that the verse is framed as an expression of favor (الامتنان) and grace upon the believers due to the attribute of their faith (الإيمان); hence it was said: "upon the believers" (على المؤمنين). Nothing other than the descriptive attribute (الوصف) provides this sense—even if it were said "those who believe" (الذين آمنوا)—because that which intimates causality (العلية), as it is said, is the attribute, or it is what is most complete in this intimation. The meaning is clear.

In the verse, there are other discussions, a portion of which will follow in their appropriate places, if Allah, the Mighty, wills.

Ayahs

أَوَلَمَّآ أَصَـٰبَتْكُم مُّصِيبَةٌ قَدْ أَصَبْتُم مِّثْلَيْهَا قُلْتُمْ أَنَّىٰ هَـٰذَا ۖ قُلْ هُوَ مِنْ عِندِ أَنفُسِكُمْ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (١٦٥)
What, when an affliction visits you—while you have inflicted twice as much—do you say, ‘How is this?’! Say, ‘This is from your own souls.’ Indeed Allah has power over all things. (165)
وَمَآ أَصَـٰبَكُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْتَقَى ٱلْجَمْعَانِ فَبِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ وَلِيَعْلَمَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (١٦٦)
What befell you on the day when the two hosts met, was by Allah’s permission, so that He may ascertain the faithful, (166)
وَلِيَعْلَمَ ٱلَّذِينَ نَافَقُوا۟ ۚ وَقِيلَ لَهُمْ تَعَالَوْا۟ قَـٰتِلُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ أَوِ ٱدْفَعُوا۟ ۖ قَالُوا۟ لَوْ نَعْلَمُ قِتَالًا لَّٱتَّبَعْنَـٰكُمْ ۗ هُمْ لِلْكُفْرِ يَوْمَئِذٍ أَقْرَبُ مِنْهُمْ لِلْإِيمَـٰنِ ۚ يَقُولُونَ بِأَفْوَٰهِهِم مَّا لَيْسَ فِى قُلُوبِهِمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا يَكْتُمُونَ (١٦٧)
and ascertain the hypocrites. [When] they were told: ‘Come, fight in the way of Allah, or defend [yourselves], they said, ‘If we knew any fighting, we would have surely followed you.’ That day they were nearer to unfaith than to faith. They say with their mouths what is not in their hearts, and Allah knows well whatever they conceal. (167)
ٱلَّذِينَ قَالُوا۟ لِإِخْوَٰنِهِمْ وَقَعَدُوا۟ لَوْ أَطَاعُونَا مَا قُتِلُوا۟ ۗ قُلْ فَٱدْرَءُوا۟ عَنْ أَنفُسِكُمُ ٱلْمَوْتَ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (١٦٨)
Those who said of their brethren, while they themselves sat back: ‘Had they obeyed us, they would not have been killed.’ Say, ‘Then keep death off from yourselves, if you are truthful.’ (168)
وَلَا تَحْسَبَنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ قُتِلُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ أَمْوَٰتًۢا ۚ بَلْ أَحْيَآءٌ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ يُرْزَقُونَ (١٦٩)
Do not suppose those who were slain in the way of Allah to be dead; no, they are living and provided for near their Lord, (169)
فَرِحِينَ بِمَآ ءَاتَىٰهُمُ ٱللَّهُ مِن فَضْلِهِۦ وَيَسْتَبْشِرُونَ بِٱلَّذِينَ لَمْ يَلْحَقُوا۟ بِهِم مِّنْ خَلْفِهِمْ أَلَّا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ (١٧٠)
exulting in what Allah has given them out of His grace, and rejoicing for those who have not yet joined them from [those left] behind them, that they will have no fear, nor will they grieve. (170)
۞ يَسْتَبْشِرُونَ بِنِعْمَةٍ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَفَضْلٍ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُضِيعُ أَجْرَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (١٧١)
They rejoice in Allah’s blessing and grace, and that Allah does not waste the reward of the faithful. (171)

(Commentary)

These verses are a continuation of the verses revealed specifically regarding the Battle of Uhud. Within them is an address concerning the state of a number of hypocrites (المنافقين) who abandoned the group of believers (المؤمنين) when they went out from Medina to Uhud. They also contain a response to what they said regarding those who were killed, and a description of the state of the martyrs (المستشهدين) after their death—that they are favored in the Presence of Proximity (حضرة القرب), rejoicing in those of their brothers left behind.

His saying, the Exalted: أَوَلَمَّا أَصابَتْكُمْ مُصِيبَةٌ قَدْ أَصَبْتُمْ مِثْلَيْها — "What! When a misfortune smote you, while you had already smitten [the enemy] with twice as much..." (3:165). When He forbade them from being like those who disbelieve in grieving for their slain and feeling regret over them—by explaining that the matter of life and death belongs to Allah alone and not to them, such that it would revolve around their proximity, distance, going out to fight, or staying behind—He returned a second time to explain its proximate cause (سببه القريب) according to the established custom of causes (سنة الأسباب). He explained that its cause was simply the disobedience (المعصية) that occurred from them on the day of Uhud; namely, the disobedience of the archers by abandoning their positions, and the disobedience of those among them who turned away from the fight thereafter. In short, the cause was their disobedience to the Messenger—who was their leader—their loss of courage, and their disputing the command. This is a cause for defeat according to the law of nature and custom.

Thus, the verse carries the meaning of saying: "Do you know from where this misfortune smote you, when you had already smitten [the enemy] with twice as much? It only smote you from your own selves (من عند أنفسكم); it was your own ruining of the cause of victory and triumph with your own hands, your opposition to your leader, your loss of courage, and the discord in your word."

The misfortune was described by His saying: قَدْ أَصَبْتُمْ مِثْلَيْها — "while you had already smitten [the enemy] with twice as much" (3:165). This is an allusion to a comparison between what the disbelievers (الكفار) inflicted upon them on the day of Uhud—which was the killing of seventy of their men—and what they had inflicted upon the disbelievers on the day of Badr, which was twice that seventy. For on the day of Badr, they killed seventy of their men and took seventy as captives.

In this description, there is a calming of their agitated hearts and a belittling of the misfortune; for they were afflicted by their enemies with only half of what they had inflicted upon them. Therefore, it is not fitting for them to grieve or despair.

It has also been said that the meaning of the verse is: "You yourselves chose this misfortune." This is because they chose to take ransom (الفداء) from the captives on the day of Badr, while the judgment regarding them was execution. It was stipulated to them: "If you accept the ransom, an equal number of you will be killed in the coming year." They said: "We agree; for we shall take the ransom and benefit from it, and if any of us are killed later, we shall be martyrs (شهداء)."

This view is supported—indeed, indicated—by the conclusion of the verse, namely His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ — "Indeed Allah has power over all things" (3:165). This phrase does not fit the previous view at all except through strained interpretation. The narration of this view from the Imams of the People of the House (أئمة أهل البيت) صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم will follow in the upcoming "Discussion on Traditions."

His saying, the Exalted: وَما أَصابَكُمْ يَوْمَ الْتَقَى الْجَمْعانِ — "What smote you on the day the two hosts met..." (3:166) until the end of the two verses. The first verse supports what was previously mentioned: that the meaning of His saying: قُلْ هُوَ مِنْ عِنْدِ أَنْفُسِكُمْ — "Say, ‘It is from your own souls’" (3:165) refers to their choosing the ransom from the captives of the day of Badr and their stipulating upon themselves before Allah what they stipulated. Thus, the occurrence of this misfortune was by the permission of Allah (بإذن الله). As for the first mentioned view—that the proximate cause of the misfortune was their disobedience—there is no apparent harmony between it and the attribution of the misfortune to the permission of Allah, as is evident.

According to what we have mentioned, the mention of attributing the misfortune to the permission of Allah serves as an explanation for His saying: هُوَ مِنْ عِنْدِ أَنْفُسِكُمْ — "It is from your own souls" (3:165), and as a prelude to the addition of His saying: وَلِيَعْلَمَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ — "and that He may ascertain the faithful" (3:166). By this addition, the way is paved to address the state of the hypocrites, what they spoke, the response to it, and the clarification of the reality of this death, which is being killed in the way of Allah.

His saying: أو ادفعوا — "or defend" (3:167) means: "If you do not fight in the way of Allah, then defend your sanctuary and yourselves." His saying: هم للكفر يومئذ أقرب منهم للإيمان — "That day they were nearer to unfaith than to faith" (3:167); the preposition (اللام) here carries the meaning of "toward" (إلى). This was their state in relation to explicit disbelief (الكفر الصريح); as for hypocrisy (النفاق), they had already fallen into it by that action of theirs.

His saying: يَقُولُونَ بِأَفْواهِهِمْ ما لَيْسَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ — "saying with their mouths what was not in their hearts" (3:167). The "mouths" (الأفواه) are mentioned for emphasis and to create a contrast between them and the "hearts" (القلوب).

His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِينَ قالُوا لِإِخْوانِهِمْ وَقَعَدُوا لَوْ أَطاعُونا ما قُتِلُوا — "Those who said of their brethren, while they themselves sat back: ‘Had they obeyed us, they would not have been killed’" (3:168). What is meant by "their brethren" (إخوانهم) is their brothers in lineage, namely the slain. Their brotherhood was mentioned so that, when joined with His saying: وَقَعَدُوا — "while they themselves sat back", it would be a more effective reproach and censure against them; for they sat back from aiding their brothers until they were afflicted by such terrible slaughter. His saying: قل فادرءوا — "Say, ‘Then ward off...’" (3:168) is a response to that statement of theirs. Warding off (الدرء) means to repel (الدفع).

His saying, the Exalted: وَلا تَحْسَبَنَّ الَّذِينَ قُتِلُوا فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ أَمْواتاً — "Do not suppose those who were slain in the way of Allah to be dead" (3:169). In this verse, there is a transition (التفات) from addressing the believers to addressing the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم. The reason for this is what has been repeatedly mentioned throughout these verses. It is also possible that the address is a continuation of the address in His saying: قُلْ فَادْرَؤُا عَنْ أَنْفُسِكُمُ الْمَوْتَ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ — "Say, ‘Then ward off death from yourselves, if you are truthful’" (3:168).

What is meant by death (الموت) here is the cessation of consciousness (الشعور) and action (الفعل). For this reason, He mentioned both in His saying: بَلْ أَحْياءٌ — "no, they are alive" (3:169) etc., where He mentioned "receiving provision" (الارتزاق), which is an action, and "rejoicing" (الفرح) and "being glad" (الاستبشار), which are accompanied by consciousness.

His saying, the Exalted: فَرِحِينَ بِما آتاهُمُ اللهُ — "rejoicing in what Allah has given them..." (3:170). Joy (الفرح) is the opposite of grief (الحزن). Good tidings (البشارة) or (البشرى) is news that gladdens you, and being glad (الاستبشار) is the seeking of joy through good tidings. The meaning is: they are joyful because of the divine grace (الفضل الإلهي) they have found present and witnessed with them, and they seek joy through the good tidings that reach them regarding the good state of those who have not yet joined them from behind—that no fear shall be upon them, nor shall they grieve.

From this, several points become clear: First, that news of the best of the believers remaining after them in the world reaches and connects with those killed in the way of Allah.

Second, that these good tidings consist of the reward for the deeds of the believers, which is that "no fear shall be upon them, nor shall they grieve." This is only through their witnessing this reward in the abode where they reside; for their state is one of witnessing (المشاهدة) rather than inference (الاستدلال). Thus, the verse contains an indication of the survival of the human being after death in the period between death and the Day of Resurrection. We have detailed the discussion on this in the discourse on the intermediate realm (البرزخ) — the state between death and resurrection — under His saying, the Exalted: وَلا تَقُولُوا لِمَنْ يُقْتَلُ فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ أَمْواتٌ — "Do not call those who were slain in Allah’s way ‘dead’" (2:154).

His saying, the Exalted: يَسْتَبْشِرُونَ بِنِعْمَةٍ مِنَ اللهِ وَفَضْلٍ — "They rejoice in Allah’s blessing and grace..." (3:171). This being glad (الاستبشار) is more general than being glad for the state of others or for their own state. The proof for this is His saying: وَأَنَّ اللهَ لا يُضِيعُ أَجْرَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ — "and that Allah does not waste the reward of the faithful" (3:171), for in its absolute sense, it encompasses everyone. Perhaps this is the subtle point in the repetition of "being glad" (الاستبشار) as well as the repetition of "grace" (الفضل); so reflect upon the verse.

"Grace" (الفضل) and "blessing" (النعمة) are mentioned in the indefinite form, and "provision" (الرزق) is left vague in the verses so that the mind of the listener may go in every possible direction. Similarly, "fear" (الخوف) and "grief" (الحزن) are left vague to indicate generality within the context of negation.

Reflection on the verses reveals that they are concerned with: first, explaining the reward of the believers; second, that this reward is their provision (رزقهم) with Allah the Glorified; third, that this provision is a blessing (نعمة) from Allah and a grace (فضل); and fourth, that what characterizes this blessing and grace is that "no fear shall be upon them, nor shall they grieve."

This sentence—namely His saying: أن لا خوف عليهم ولا هم يحزنون — "no fear shall be upon them, nor shall they grieve"—is a wondrous statement. The more one reflects upon it, the more its meaning expands in subtlety, delicacy, and ease of expression. The first thing that appears from its meaning is that fear and grief are removed from them. Fear (الخوف) only arises from a possible, anticipated matter that would cause the loss of some aspect of human happiness that one considers oneself to possess. Similarly, grief (الحزن) only arises from an occurred matter that causes such a loss. Thus, a calamity or any dreaded thing is feared as long as it has not yet occurred; once it occurs, fear vanishes and grief sets in. Therefore, there is no fear after the occurrence, and no grief before it.

The removal of absolute fear from a person only happens when the various blessings (النعم) they possess are not subject to cessation. The removal of absolute grief is only possible for them if they do not lose any type of their happiness, neither initially nor after possessing it. Thus, Allah's removal of absolute fear and grief from a person means that He pours upon them everything they can possibly enjoy and find pleasure in, and that this shall not be subject to cessation. This is the eternity of happiness (خلود السعادة) for the human being and their eternity within it.

From here, it becomes clear that the negation of fear and grief is the very same thing as the human being "receiving provision with Allah." For He, the Glorified, says: وَما عِنْدَ اللهِ خَيْرٌ — "and what is with Allah is better" (3:198), and He says: وَما عِنْدَ اللهِ باقٍ — "and what is with Allah is enduring" (16:96). These two verses indicate that what is with Allah is an enduring blessing (نعمة باقية) that is not mixed with any affliction, nor subject to any annihilation.

It also becomes clear that their negation is the very same thing as the affirmation of blessing (النعمة) and grace (الفضل), which is the gift (العطية). However, it was mentioned at the beginning of the book, and will follow in His saying, the Exalted: مَعَ الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمَ اللهُ عَلَيْهِمْ — "with those whom Allah has blessed" (4:69), that when "blessing" (النعمة) is used in the parlance of the Quran, it refers to the divine guardianship (الولاية الإلهية). Accordingly, the meaning is: Allah takes charge of their affairs and singles them out with a gift from Him.

As for the possibility that "grace" (الفضل) refers to a bestowal exceeding what they deserve by their deeds, while "blessing" (النعمة) refers to what corresponds to their deeds—this is not harmonized with His saying: وَأَنَّ اللهَ لا يُضِيعُ أَجْرَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ — "and that Allah does not waste the reward of the faithful" (3:171), for "reward" (الأجر) implies desert. You have already seen that these phrases—namely His saying: عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ يُرْزَقُونَ — "receiving provision from their Lord" (3:169), His saying: فَرِحِينَ بِما — "rejoicing in what..." (3:170), His saying: يَسْتَبْشِرُونَ بِنِعْمَةٍ — "They rejoice in Allah’s blessing..." (3:171), and His saying: وَأَنَّ اللهَ لا يُضِيعُ أَجْرَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ — "and that Allah does not waste the reward of the faithful" (3:171)—all return to a single reality.

There are other discussions regarding these verses, some of which preceded in the commentary on His saying: وَلا تَقُولُوا لِمَنْ يُقْتَلُ فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ أَمْواتٌ — "Do not call those who were slain in Allah’s way ‘dead’" (2:154). Perhaps Allah will grant us success to exhaust what we can of the discussion regarding them in the appropriate places to come, if Allah the Exalted wills.

Ayahs

ٱلَّذِينَ ٱسْتَجَابُوا۟ لِلَّهِ وَٱلرَّسُولِ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَآ أَصَابَهُمُ ٱلْقَرْحُ ۚ لِلَّذِينَ أَحْسَنُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ وَٱتَّقَوْا۟ أَجْرٌ عَظِيمٌ (١٧٢)
Those who responded to Allah and the Apostle [even] after they had been wounded—for those of them who have been virtuous and Godwary there shall be a great reward. (172)
ٱلَّذِينَ قَالَ لَهُمُ ٱلنَّاسُ إِنَّ ٱلنَّاسَ قَدْ جَمَعُوا۟ لَكُمْ فَٱخْشَوْهُمْ فَزَادَهُمْ إِيمَـٰنًا وَقَالُوا۟ حَسْبُنَا ٱللَّهُ وَنِعْمَ ٱلْوَكِيلُ (١٧٣)
—Those to whom the people said, ‘All the people have gathered against you, so fear them.’ That only increased them in faith, and they said, ‘Allah is sufficient for us, and He is an excellent trustee.’ (173)
فَٱنقَلَبُوا۟ بِنِعْمَةٍ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَفَضْلٍ لَّمْ يَمْسَسْهُمْ سُوٓءٌ وَٱتَّبَعُوا۟ رِضْوَٰنَ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ ذُو فَضْلٍ عَظِيمٍ (١٧٤)
So they returned with Allah’s blessing and grace, untouched by any harm. They pursued the pleasure of Allah, and Allah is dispenser of a great grace. (174)
إِنَّمَا ذَٰلِكُمُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ يُخَوِّفُ أَوْلِيَآءَهُۥ فَلَا تَخَافُوهُمْ وَخَافُونِ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ (١٧٥)
That is only Satan frightening [you] of his followers! So fear them not, and fear Me, should you be faithful. (175)

(Commentary)

The verses are connected to the verses regarding the Battle of Uhud (غزوة أحد), as suggested by His saying: مِنْ بَعْدِ ما أَصابَهُمُ الْقَرْحُ — "after they had been smitten by the wound" (3:172), while He had said regarding that battle: إِنْ يَمْسَسْكُمْ قَرْحٌ فَقَدْ مَسَّ الْقَوْمَ قَرْحٌ مِثْلُهُ — "If a wound afflicts you, a like wound has already afflicted those people" (3:140).

His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِينَ اسْتَجابُوا لِلَّهِ وَالرَّسُولِ — "Those who responded to Allah and the Apostle" (3:172). The terms istijabah (الاستجابة) and ijabah (الإجابة) carry the same meaning—as has been said—which is to be asked for something and to respond with acceptance.

Perhaps the mention of both Allah and the Messenger, despite the sufficiency of mentioning only one in this context, is because during the Battle of Uhud they had disobeyed both Allah and the Messenger. As for Him, the Exalted, they disobeyed Him by fleeing and turning their backs, which Allah had forbidden them from doing while commanding them to engage in jihad (الجهاد) — the striving in the way of Allah. As for the Messenger, they disobeyed him by violating the order he issued to the archers to remain at their posts, and when they were climbing the mountain while he was calling them from their rear, yet they did not respond to his call. Thus, when they responded in this subsequent encounter, their response to Allah and the Messenger was placed in contrast to that previous encounter.

His saying: لِلَّذِينَ أَحْسَنُوا مِنْهُمْ وَاتَّقَوْا أَجْرٌ عَظِيمٌ — "For such of them as were virtuous and God-conscious, there shall be a great reward" (3:172). He restricted the promise to only some of those who responded, because responding is an outward act (فعل ظاهري) that does not necessarily entail the reality of virtue (الإحسان) and godwariness (التقوى) — the innate state of being conscious of Allah's presence — upon which the great reward is predicated. This is part of the wondrous precision of the Quranic discourse, where one matter does not distract it from another. From this, it becomes clear that this group was not entirely sincere toward Allah in their affair; rather, there were those among them who were not virtuous or God-conscious (متقياً) enough to deserve the great reward from Allah, the Glorified. It is sometimes said that the word "of" (مِنْ) in His saying "of them" (مِنْهُمْ) is explanatory (بيانية), as has been said regarding His saying, the Exalted: مُحَمَّدٌ رَسُولُ اللهِ وَالَّذِينَ مَعَهُ أَشِدَّاءُ عَلَى الْكُفَّارِ — "Muhammad, the Apostle of Allah, and those who are with him are hard against the faithless..." until He says: وَعَدَ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ مِنْهُمْ مَغْفِرَةً وَأَجْراً عَظِيماً — "Allah has promised those of them who have faith and do righteous deeds forgiveness and a great reward" (48:29). However, this is an interpretation (تأول) that is refuted by the context (السياق).

It also becomes clear that the praise Allah, the Glorified, bestows upon them in His saying: الَّذِينَ قالَ لَهُمُ النَّاسُ — "Those to whom the people said..." (3:173) until the end of the verses, is of the nature of attributing the description of a part to the whole through a linguistic consideration (عناية لفظية).

His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِينَ قالَ لَهُمُ النَّاسُ إِنَّ النَّاسَ قَدْ جَمَعُوا لَكُمْ — "Those to whom the people said, 'All the people have mustered against you...'" (3:173). The term "people" (الناس) refers to human individuals without considering the specific characteristics that distinguish one from another. The first "people" mentioned are different from the second. The second refers to the enemy who was gathering forces. As for the first, they were the discouragers and hinderers who said what they said to dissuade the believers from going out to fight the polytheists (المشركين). Thus, the second "people" refers to the polytheists, while the first "people" were those whose hands were against the believers and whose eyes were upon them. The apparent meaning of the verse is that they were a group, not a single individual. This supports the view that the verses were revealed concerning the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) expedition with the remaining companions after Uhud in pursuit of the polytheists, rather than the story of the "Lesser Badr" (بدر الصغرى). Both accounts will be discussed in the forthcoming "Traditionary Discourse."

His saying: قَدْ جَمَعُوا لَكُمْ — "have mustered against you," meaning they have gathered their forces to fight you a second time (and Allah knows best).

His saying: فَزادَهُمْ إِيماناً — "it only increased them in faith" (3:173). This is because it is in the nature of a human being that when he is forbidden from something he desires and is determined upon, if he does not have a good opinion of the one forbidding him, it acts as an incitement. It triggers his faculties and strengthens his resolve. The more he is insistently barred, the more he insists on proceeding with what he intends. This effect is even stronger if the one being forbidden sees himself as being in the right and justified in his actions. Therefore, whenever a blamer censured the believers regarding the command of Allah or a hinderer tried to stop them, they only increased in the strength of their faith (الإيمان) and the intensity of their resolve and courage.

It is also possible that the increase in their faith was because such reports confirmed the revelation they had received—that they would be persecuted for the sake of Allah until their affair was completed by Allah's permission, and He had promised them victory, and victory only comes through encounter and combat.

His saying: وَقالُوا حَسْبُنَا اللهُ وَنِعْمَ الْوَكِيلُ — "and they said, 'Allah is sufficient for us, and He is an excellent trustee'" (3:173). Meaning, Allah is enough for us. The root of hasb (الحسب) comes from hisab (الحساب) — calculation — because sufficiency is according to the calculation of need. This is a reliance on Allah based on faith (الإيمان), setting aside the external causes (الأسباب الخارجية) that operate within the Divine Custom (السنة الإلهية). The "Trustee" (الوكيل) is the one who manages affairs on behalf of a person. Thus, the content of the verse returns to the meaning of His saying: وَمَنْ يَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى اللهِ فَهُوَ حَسْبُهُ إِنَّ اللهَ بالِغُ أَمْرِهِ — "And whoever puts his trust in Allah, He will suffice him. Indeed Allah carries through His command" (65:3). For this reason, He followed the saying وَقالُوا حَسْبُنَا اللهُ وَنِعْمَ الْوَكِيلُ with His saying: فَانْقَلَبُوا بِنِعْمَةٍ مِنَ اللهِ وَفَضْلٍ لَمْ يَمْسَسْهمْ سُوءٌ — "So they returned with Allah’s blessing and grace, and no evil touched them" (3:174), so that it might be a confirmation of His promise. Then He praised them for following His pleasure, saying: "and they followed Allah’s pleasure; and Allah is dispenser of a boundless grace."

(A Discourse on Trust in Allah)

The reality of the matter is that the execution of will and the attainment of the goal in the material realm (نشأة المادة) require both natural causes (أسباب طبيعية) and spiritual causes (أسباب روحية). When a person intends to enter into an important matter and prepares the necessary natural causes, nothing stands between him and his objective except the failure of spiritual causes—such as weakness of will, fear, grief, recklessness, greed, folly, or ill-suspicion. These are significant and pervasive factors. However, when he puts his trust (التوكل) in Allah, the Glorified, he connects himself to a cause that is absolutely invincible—the Cause that is above every cause. Consequently, his will becomes so strong that it cannot be overcome by any opposing or conflicting spiritual causes, leading to attainment and felicity.

There is another dimension to trust in Allah (التوكل) that relates to its effect through supernatural means (خوارق العادة), as is apparent from His saying: وَمَنْ يَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى اللهِ فَهُوَ حَسْبُهُ إِنَّ اللهَ بالِغُ أَمْرِهِ — "And whoever puts his trust in Allah, He will suffice him. Indeed Allah carries through His command" (65:3). A portion of the discussion related to this was previously covered in the discourse on miracles (الإعجاز).

His saying, the Exalted: ذلِكُمُ الشَّيْطانُ يُخَوِّفُ أَوْلِياءَهُ — "That is only Satan frightening his friends" (3:175). The apparent meaning of the verse is that the reference is to the "people" who said what they said to them. Thus, this is one of the instances where the Quran applies the term "Satan" (الشيطان) to a human being, as is evident from His saying: مِنْ شَرِّ الْوَسْواسِ الْخَنَّاسِ الَّذِي يُوَسْوِسُ فِي صُدُورِ النَّاسِ مِنَ الْجِنَّةِ وَالنَّاسِ — "from the evil of the sneaking whisperer, who whispers into the breasts of mankind, from among the jinn and the humans" (114:4-6). This is supported by His saying immediately after: فَلا تَخافُوهُمْ — "so do not fear them" (3:175), meaning the people who spoke to you, because "that is Satan." We shall discuss this meaning in a way that unveils its true reality, if Allah wills.

(Narrative Discussion)

The narrations regarding the Battle of Uhud (غزوة أحد) are extremely numerous, yet they differ significantly in various aspects of the story, which may lead to skepticism regarding them. The greatest discrepancies are found in the narrations concerning the occasions of revelation (أسباب النزول) for many of the verses of this story—which are nearly sixty verses—for their matter is indeed strange. A contemplative observer cannot help but conclude that different schools of thought (المذاهب) have breathed their own spirits into these narrations to speak through them for their own benefit. This is our excuse for omitting them in this discussion; whoever seeks them should consult the compendiums of hadith (جوامع الحديث) and the extensive commentaries (مطولات التفاسير).

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Abu al-Duha who said: "The verse وَيَتَّخِذَ مِنْكُمْ شُهَداءَ — 'and that He may take martyrs from among you' (3:140) was revealed, and seventy of them were killed on that day: four from the Emigrants (المهاجرين)—including Hamzah ibn Abd al-Muttalib, Mus'ab ibn Umayr of the Banu Abd al-Dar, Shammas ibn Uthman al-Makhzumi, and Abd Allah ibn Jahsh al-Asadi—and the rest were from the Helpers (الأنصار)."

I say: The apparent meaning of the narration is that Abu al-Duha took the term "martyrs" (الشهداء) in the verse to mean those killed in battle. Most commentators have followed this view. However, it was mentioned in the previous commentary that there is no evidence for this in the apparent text of the Book; rather, the apparent meaning is that "martyrs" refers to the witnesses of deeds (شهداء الأعمال).

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: أَمْ حَسِبْتُمْ أَنْ تَدْخُلُوا الْجَنَّةَ وَلَمَّا يَعْلَمِ اللهُ — "Do you suppose that you will enter paradise though Allah has not yet known..." (3:142), it is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) that he said: "Indeed, Allah knew what He would bring into existence before He brought it into existence—while they were in the realm of particles (ذر)—and He knew who would strive from those who would not, just as He knew He would cause His creation to die before He caused them to die, yet He did not see their death while they were alive."

I say: This is an allusion to what was previously mentioned—that there is a distinction between knowledge before creation and actual knowledge (العلم الفعلي), which is the act itself, and that the intended meaning is not the knowledge before creation.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ كُنْتُمْ تَمَنَّوْنَ الْمَوْتَ — "You certainly used to wish for death..." (3:143): "When Allah informed the believers of what He had prepared for their martyrs at Badr in their stations in Paradise, they yearned for that and said: 'O Allah, show us a battle in which we may be martyred.' So Allah showed them the day of Uhud, but they did not stand firm except for those whom Allah willed. That is His saying: وَلَقَدْ كُنْتُمْ تَمَنَّوْنَ الْمَوْتَ." I say: This meaning is also narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from Ibn Abbas, Mujahid, Qatadah, al-Hasan, and al-Suddi.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, he (peace be upon him) said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) went out on the day of Uhud, and someone saw him in that state and began saying to whomever he met: 'The Messenger of Allah has been killed, save yourselves!' When they returned to Medina, Allah revealed: وَما مُحَمَّدٌ إِلَّا رَسُولٌ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِنْ قَبْلِهِ الرُّسُلُ — 'Muhammad is but an apostle; [other] apostles have passed before him...' (3:144) until His saying: انْقَلَبْتُمْ عَلى أَعْقابِكُمْ — 'will you turn back on your heels?' (3:144), meaning to disbelief (الكفر); وَمَنْ يَنْقَلِبْ عَلى عَقِبَيْهِ فَلَنْ يَضُرَّ اللهَ شَيْئاً — 'Whoever turns back on his heels will not harm Allah in the least' (3:144)."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Jarir and Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from al-Rabi' regarding the verse: "That was on the day of Uhud when they were afflicted by killing and wounds, and they called out for the Prophet of Allah, saying: 'He has been killed.' Some people among them said: 'If he were a prophet, he would not have been killed.' But some of the elite companions of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'Fight for what your Prophet fought for until Allah grants you victory or you join him.' It was mentioned to us that a man from the Emigrants passed by a man from the Helpers who was wallowing in his blood and said: 'O so-and-so, do you know that Muhammad has been killed?' The Helper replied: 'If Muhammad has been killed, he has delivered the message; so fight for your religion.' Then Allah revealed: وَما مُحَمَّدٌ إِلَّا رَسُولٌ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِنْ قَبْلِهِ الرُّسُلُ أَفَإِنْ ماتَ أَوْ قُتِلَ انْقَلَبْتُمْ عَلى أَعْقابِكُمْ — 'Muhammad is but an apostle; [other] apostles have passed before him. If he dies or is slain, will you turn back on your heels?' (3:144), meaning: you would apostatize as disbelievers after your faith."

In the same source, Ibn Jarir narrated from al-Suddi: "It spread among the people on the day of Uhud that the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) had been killed. Some of the 'people of the rock' said: 'If only we had a messenger to go to Abd Allah ibn Ubayy so he could get us a guarantee of safety from Abu Sufyan. O people, Muhammad has been killed, so return to your people before they come and kill you.' Anas ibn al-Nadr said: 'O people, if Muhammad has been killed, the Lord of Muhammad has not been killed. So fight for what Muhammad fought for. O Allah, I apologize to You for what these people say, and I disassociate myself from what they have brought.' Then he drew his sword and fought until he was killed. Then Allah revealed: وَما مُحَمَّدٌ إِلَّا رَسُولٌ."

I say: These meanings have been narrated through many other chains.

In al-Kafi, from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him): "Ali was afflicted with sixty wounds on the day of Uhud. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) ordered Umm Sulaym and Umm Atiyyah to treat him. They said: 'We no sooner treat one place than another bursts open, and we fear for him.' The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and the Muslims entered to visit him while he was one continuous wound. He began wiping him with his hand and saying: 'A man who has met this for the sake of Allah has indeed been tested and has fulfilled his duty.' Every wound the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) wiped would heal. Ali said: 'Praise be to Allah that I did not flee nor turn my back.' Allah thanked him for that in two places in the Quran: وَسَيَجْزِي اللهُ الشَّاكِرِينَ — 'and Allah will soon reward the grateful' (3:144), and سَنَجْزِي الشَّاكِرِينَ — 'We will soon reward the grateful' (3:145)."

I say: This means Allah thanked him for his steadfastness, not for his saying "Praise be to Allah."

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) that he read: وكأين من نبي قتل معه ربيون كثير — "How many a prophet there has been with whom many a godly man fought" (3:146). He said: "Thousands upon thousands." Then he said: "Yes, by Allah, they are killed."

I say: This reading and meaning are narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from Ibn Mas'ud and others. It is narrated from Ibn Abbas that he was asked about the word ribbiyyun (ربيون) and he said: "Multitudes."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Abd ibn Humayd and Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Mujahid regarding مِنْ بَعْدِ ما أَراكُمْ ما تُحِبُّونَ — "after He had shown you what you love" (3:152): "Allah gave the believers victory over the polytheists until the women of the polytheists were riding every mount, easy or difficult [to flee]. Then the turn of fortune went against them because of their disobedience to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)."

In the same source, Ibn Ishaq, Ibn Rahwayh, Abd ibn Humayd, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and al-Bayhaqi in al-Dala'il narrated from al-Zubayr: "I saw myself with the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) when fear intensified upon us. Allah sent down sleep upon us, such that there was not a man among us but his chin was on his chest. By Allah, I could hear the words of Mu'attib ibn Qushayr—I heard them as if in a dream—'If we had any say in the matter, we would not have been killed here.' I memorized it from him. Regarding that, Allah revealed: ثُمَّ أَنْزَلَ عَلَيْكُمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ الْغَمِّ أَمَنَةً نُعاساً — 'Then He sent down to you, after the grief, a security—a slumber...' (3:154) until His saying: ما قُتِلْنا هاهُنا — 'we would not have been killed here' (3:154), referring to the words of Mu'attib ibn Qushayr."

I say: This meaning has been narrated from al-Zubayr ibn al-Awwam through many chains.

In the same source, Ibn Mandah in Ma'rifat al-Sahabah narrated from Ibn Abbas regarding His saying: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ تَوَلَّوْا مِنْكُمْ يَوْمَ الْتَقَى الْجَمْعانِ — "Those of you who turned their backs on the day when the two hosts met..." (3:155): "It was revealed concerning Uthman, Rafi' ibn al-Mu'alla, and Harithah ibn Zayd."

I say: Something similar is narrated through several chains from Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf, Ikrimah, and Ibn Ishaq, and in some of them, Abu Hudhayfah ibn Utbah, al-Walid ibn Utbah, Sa'd ibn Uthman, and Utbah ibn Uthman are added.

In any case, the mention of Uthman and those counted with him by name is by way of mentioning an instance (المصداق); otherwise, the verse was revealed concerning all the companions who turned back and disobeyed the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). What specifically pertains to Uthman is that he and those with him fled until they reached al-Jal'ab (a mountain in the direction of Medina near al-Aghwas), where they stayed for three days before returning to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). He said to them: "You went far and wide in it."

As for his companions in general, narrations are numerous that they turned back almost to the last man, and none remained with the Messenger of Allah except two men from the Emigrants and seven from the Helpers. Then the polytheists attacked the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and the Helpers were killed one by one defending him until none of them remained with him.

It is narrated that those who stood firm with him were eleven, or eighteen, and even thirty has been narrated, which is the weakest of the narrations.

Perhaps this discrepancy is due to the different information available to the narrators and other factors. What is indicated by the narrations of Nusaybah al-Maziniyyah's defense of him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) is that there was no one with him at that moment; those who had stood firm and not fled were occupied with fighting. The narrators do not agree on anyone except Ali (peace be upon him), and perhaps Abu Dujanah al-Ansari Simak ibn Kharashah as well, though he first fought with the sword of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and then shielded the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) with his own body when his companions had scattered, warding off arrows with his shield and his back until he was severely wounded (may Allah be pleased with him).

As for the rest of his companions, some joined him once they recognized him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and knew he had not been killed, and others joined him after a while. These are the ones upon whom Allah sent down slumber, though Allah, the Exalted, pardoned them all. You have already learned the meaning of "pardon" (العفو) in the previous commentary. Some commentators mentioned that the meaning of pardon in this verse is that He, the Exalted, turned the polytheists away from them so they did not annihilate them or kill them to the last man.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Adi and al-Bayhaqi in al-Shu'ab narrated with a good chain from Ibn Abbas: "When وَشاوِرْهُمْ فِي الْأَمْرِ — 'and consult them in the matter' (3:159) was revealed, the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'As for Allah and His Messenger, they are certainly independent of it, but Allah made it a mercy for my community. Whoever among them consults will not lack right guidance, and whoever abandons it will not lack error.'"

In the same source, al-Tabarani in al-Awsat narrated from Anas: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'He who seeks the best from Allah (استخار) will not fail, and he who consults (استشار) will not regret.'"

In Nahj al-Balaghah: "He who is autocratic in his opinion perishes, and he who consults men shares in their intellects."

Also in it: "Consultation is the very essence of guidance, and he who is autocratic in his opinion takes a risk."

In al-Safi, from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): "No loneliness is more desolate than vanity, and no support is more reliable than consultation."

I say: The narrations regarding consultation (المشاورة) are very numerous. Their scope is what is permissible for the consultant to do or leave according to prevailing considerations. As for the established Divine laws (الأحكام الإلهية), there is no room for consultation in them, nor is there permission for anyone to change them; otherwise, the variation of occurring events would abrogate the speech of Allah, the Exalted.

In al-Majalis, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "The pleasure of people cannot be possessed, and their tongues cannot be controlled. Did they not accuse him on the day of Badr of taking for himself a red velvet cloak from the spoils? Until Allah revealed the cloak and cleared His Prophet of treachery, and revealed in His Book: وَما كانَ لِنَبِيٍّ أَنْ يَغُلَ — 'It is not for a prophet to be untrustworthy' (3:161)."

I say: al-Qummi mentioned this in his commentary, adding: "A man came to the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him) and said: 'So-and-so has hidden a red velvet cloak, so dig it up there.' The Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him) ordered that place to be dug, and the cloak was brought out."

This meaning and similar ones have been narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur through many chains. Perhaps the meaning of the verse being revealed regarding it is that the verse alludes to it; otherwise, the context of the verses indicates they were revealed after the Battle of Uhud, as previously explained.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him): "Whoever misappropriates (غل) something will see it on the Day of Resurrection in the Fire; then he will be tasked to enter it and bring it out of the Fire."

I say: This is a subtle derivation from His saying, the Exalted: وَمَنْ يَغْلُلْ يَأْتِ بِما غَلَّ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ — "and whoever is untrustworthy will bring his trust on the Day of Resurrection" (3:161).

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: هُمْ دَرَجاتٌ عِنْدَ اللهِ — "They have [various] degrees with Allah" (3:163), from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "Those who followed the pleasure of Allah are the Imams (الأئمة); they are, by Allah, degrees with Allah for the believers. Through their guardianship (ولاية) and their love for us, Allah multiplies their deeds for them and raises for them the high degrees. And those who earned the wrath of Allah are those who denied the right of Ali and the right of the Imams from us, the People of the House (أهل البيت); thus they earned the wrath of Allah."

I say: This is of the nature of "conceptual flow and application" (الجري والانطباق).

In the same source, from Imam al-Rida (peace be upon him): "A degree is [the distance] between the heaven and the earth."

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi as well, regarding His saying, the Exalted: أَوَلَمَّا أَصابَتْكُمْ مُصِيبَةٌ قَدْ أَصَبْتُمْ مِثْلَيْها — "What! When a misfortune smote you, while you had already smitten [the enemy] with twice as much..." (3:165), from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "The Muslims had smitten one hundred and forty men at Badr: they killed seventy and took seventy captive. When the day of Uhud came, seventy men from the Muslims were smitten. They were grieved by that, so the verse was revealed."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Abi Shaybah, al-Tirmidhi—who graded it as hasan—Ibn Jarir, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ali: "Gabriel came to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and said: 'O Muhammad, Allah has disliked what your people did in taking captives, and He has commanded you to give them a choice between two things: either they come forward and their necks are struck, or they take ransom on the condition that an equal number of them will be killed.' The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) called the people and mentioned that to them. They said: 'O Messenger of Allah, they are our kinsmen and our people; we will take their ransom so we may be strengthened for fighting our enemy, and an equal number of us will be martyred later—there is nothing in that we dislike.' So seventy men were killed from them on the day of Uhud, the number of the captives of the people of Badr."

I say: It was narrated in al-Majma' from Ali (peace be upon him), and al-Qummi recorded it in his commentary.

In al-Majma', regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَلا تَحْسَبَنَّ الَّذِينَ قُتِلُوا فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ — "Do not suppose those who were slain in the way of Allah..." (3:169), from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him): "It was revealed concerning the martyrs of Badr and Uhud together."

I say: There are many narrations to this effect in al-Durr al-Manthur and elsewhere. You have already learned that the meaning of the verses is general, encompassing everyone truly or legally killed in the way of Allah. It is sometimes said that the verses were revealed concerning the martyrs of Bi'r Ma'unah, who were seventy or forty men from the companions of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) whom he sent to invite Amir ibn al-Tufayl and his people. They were at that water when they sent Abu Milhan al-Ansari to them with the message; they killed him first, then they fell upon the companions of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and fought them until they killed them all (may Allah be pleased with them).

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Imam al-Sadiq: "They are, by Allah, our Shia (شيعتنا) when their souls reached Paradise and they received honor from Allah, the Mighty and Majestic. They knew and were certain that they were upon the truth and upon the religion of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic. So they rejoiced in those who had not yet joined them from their brothers among the believers left behind."

I say: This is of the nature of "conceptual flow." Their knowing and being certain that they were upon the truth means they attained this through the "eye of certainty" (عين اليقين) after having attained it in the world through the "knowledge of certainty" (علم اليقين), not that they were in doubt or suspicion while in the world.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ahmad, Hannad, Abd ibn Humayd, Abu Dawud, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, al-Hakim—who graded it as sahih—and al-Bayhaqi in al-Dala'il narrated from Ibn Abbas: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'When your brothers were smitten at Uhud, Allah placed their souls in the crops of green birds that frequent the rivers of Paradise and eat of its fruits, then return to golden lamps suspended in the shade of the Throne.

When they found the goodness of their food, drink, and resting place, they said: "If only our brothers knew what Allah has done for us."' In another wording: 'They said: "We are alive in Paradise, being provided for, so that they may not lose interest in jihad nor recoil from war." Allah said: "I shall convey it to them on your behalf." So Allah revealed these verses: وَلا تَحْسَبَنَّ الَّذِينَ قُتِلُوا and what follows.'"

I say: There are many narrations with this meaning from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri, Abd Allah ibn Mas'ud, Abu al-Aliyah, Ibn Abbas, and others. In some: "in the forms of green birds" like the narration of Abu al-Aliyah; in some: "in green birds" like the narration of Abu Sa'id; and in some: "as green birds" like the narration of Ibn Mas'ud. The wordings are similar.

It has been narrated through the chains of the Imams of the People of the House (أهل البيت) that when this narration was presented to them, they denied it being from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). In some reports, they interpreted it. There is no doubt—considering the established and accepted principles—of the necessity of interpreting the narration if it is not rejected.

Furthermore, the narrations are not in the position of describing their state in the Paradise of the Hereafter; rather, what is intended is the Paradise of the intermediate realm (البرزخ) — the state between death and resurrection. The evidence for this is in the narration of Ibn Jarir from Mujahid: "They are provided from the fruits of Paradise and find its scent, but they are not in it." And in the narration of Ibn Jarir from al-Suddi: "The souls of the martyrs are in the crops of green birds in golden lamps suspended from the Throne; they graze morning and evening in Paradise and spend the night in the lamps."

You have already learned in the previous discussion on the intermediate realm (البرزخ) that the content of these two narrations is only consistent with the Paradise of this world, which is the Barzakh, not the Paradise of the Hereafter.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, regarding His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِينَ اسْتَجابُوا لِلَّهِ (3:172), Ibn Ishaq, Ibn Jarir, and al-Bayhaqi in al-Dala'il narrated from Abd Allah ibn Abi Bakr ibn Muhammad ibn Amr ibn Hazm: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) went out to Hamra' al-Asad. Abu Sufyan had resolved to return to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and his companions, saying: 'We returned before we could eradicate them; we shall certainly attack their remnants.' He was informed that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) had come out with his companions seeking them. This deterred Abu Sufyan and his companions. A caravan from Abd al-Qays passed by, and Abu Sufyan said to them: 'Inform Muhammad that we have resolved to return to his companions to eradicate them.'

When the caravan passed by the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) at Hamra' al-Asad, they informed him of what Abu Sufyan had said. The Messenger of Allah and the believers with him said: 'Allah is sufficient for us, and He is an excellent trustee.' Then Allah revealed regarding that: الَّذِينَ اسْتَجابُوا لِلَّهِ وَالرَّسُولِ."

I say: al-Qummi narrated this in detail in his commentary, mentioning that he (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) took with him to Hamra' al-Asad those of his companions who were wounded. In some narrations, he only took those who had been at Uhud, and the outcome is the same.

In the same source, Musa ibn Uqbah narrated in his Maghazi and al-Bayhaqi in al-Dala'il from Ibn Shihab: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) called the Muslims to the appointment with Abu Sufyan at Badr. Satan carried his friends among the people, and they went among the people frightening them, saying: 'We have been informed that they have gathered against you people like the night, hoping to fall upon you and plunder you; so beware, beware!' Allah protected the Muslims from Satan's intimidation, so they responded to Allah and His Messenger. They went out with their merchandise, saying: 'If we meet Abu Sufyan, he is the one we went out for; and if we do not meet him, we will sell our merchandise.' Badr was a marketplace frequented every year. They set out until they reached the season of Badr and fulfilled their needs. Abu Sufyan broke the appointment and did not come out, nor did his companions. Ibn Hammam passed by them and asked: 'Who are these?' They said: 'The Messenger of Allah and his companions waiting for Abu Sufyan and those of the Quraysh with him.' He went to the Quraysh and informed them, and Abu Sufyan was terrified and returned to Mecca. The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) returned to Medina with blessing and grace from Allah. That expedition was considered the 'Expedition of the Army of Sawiq' (جيش السويق), and it was in Sha'ban of the third year."

I say: It has been narrated through other than this chain, and al-Majma' narrated it in detail from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him). In it, it is stated that the verses were revealed regarding the "Lesser Badr." The "Army of Sawiq" refers to the army of Abu Sufyan, for he came out of Mecca with an army of Quraysh carrying loads of sawiq (parched grain). They camped outside Mecca, ate the sawiq, and then returned to Mecca when terror seized them from meeting the Muslims at Badr. The people called them the "Army of Sawiq" in mockery and derision.

Also in it, al-Nasa'i, Ibn Abi Hatim, and al-Tabarani narrated with a sound chain from Ikrimah from Ibn Abbas: "When the polytheists returned from Uhud, they said: 'You neither killed Muhammad nor took the young women; what a wretched thing you have done! Return!' The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) heard of this and summoned the Muslims. They responded until they reached Hamra' al-Asad or Bi'r Abi Utbah—Sufyan was in doubt. The polytheists said: 'We shall return next year.' The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) returned, and it was counted as an expedition. Then Allah revealed: الَّذِينَ اسْتَجابُوا لِلَّهِ وَالرَّسُولِ. Abu Sufyan had said to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): 'Your appointment is the season of Badr where you killed our companions.' As for the coward, he returned; as for the brave, he took the equipment for fighting and trade. They went there but found no one, so they traded. Then Allah revealed: فَانْقَلَبُوا بِنِعْمَةٍ مِنَ اللهِ وَفَضْلٍ."

I say: We have only cited this narration despite its contradictions for the sake of brevity and summary, which is effective in traditionary discourses by providing a comprehensive sample from every category. This is so the contemplative researcher may realize that what they mention as "occasions of revelation" (أسباب النزول) are all or mostly theoretical. This means they often narrate historical events and then join them with whatever verses of the Noble Quran can be applied to them, counting them as occasions of revelation. This sometimes leads to the fragmentation of a single verse or verses with a single context, then attributing each part to a separate, independent revelation, even if this causes the disruption of the verses' order and the invalidation of their context. This is one of the reasons for the weakness in the genre of narrations concerning the occasions of revelation.

Add to this what we mentioned at the beginning of this discourse—that the differences between schools of thought have an impact on the tone of these narrations and their direction toward justifying specific doctrines.

Furthermore, the political climates and the ruling environments in every age have a powerful effect on truths in terms of concealing or obscuring them. Therefore, the contemplative researcher must not neglect these factors that play a role in understanding the truths. And Allah is the Guide.

(Historical Discourse)

The martyrs of the Muslims on the day of Uhud were seventy men. Here is the list of their names:

  1. Hamzah ibn Abd al-Muttalib ibn Hashim.
  2. Abd Allah ibn Jahsh.
  3. Mus'ab ibn Umayr.
  4. Shammas ibn Uthman. These four were the martyrs from the Emigrants (المهاجرين).
  5. Amr ibn Mu'adh ibn al-Nu'man.
  6. al-Harith ibn Anas ibn Rafi'.
  7. Umarah ibn Ziyad ibn al-Sakan.
  8. Salamah ibn Thabit ibn Waqsh.
  9. Amr ibn Thabit ibn Waqsh.
  10. Thabit ibn Waqsh.
  11. Rifa'ah ibn Waqsh.
  12. Husayl ibn Jabir Abu Hudhayfah al-Yaman.
  13. Sayfi ibn Qayzi.
  14. Hubab ibn Qayzi.
  15. Abbad ibn Sahl.
  16. al-Harith ibn Aws ibn Mu'adh.
  17. Iyas ibn Aws.
  18. Ubayd ibn al-Tayyihan.
  19. Habib ibn Yazid ibn Taym.
  20. Yazid ibn Hatib ibn Umayyah ibn Rafi'.
  21. Abu Sufyan ibn al-Harith ibn Qays ibn Zayd.
  22. Hanzalah ibn Abi Amir, who is "the one washed by the angels" (غسيل الملائكة).
  23. Anis ibn Qatadah.
  24. Abu Habbah ibn Amr ibn Thabit.
  25. Abd Allah ibn Jubayr ibn al-Nu'man, the commander of the archers.
  26. Abu Sa'd Khaythamah ibn Khaythamah.
  27. Abd Allah ibn Salamah.
  28. Subay' ibn Hatib ibn al-Harith.
  29. Amr ibn Qays.
  30. Qays ibn Amr ibn Qays.
  31. Thabit ibn Amr ibn Yazid.
  32. Amir ibn Mukhallad.
  33. Abu Hubayrah ibn al-Harith ibn Alqamah ibn Amr.
  34. Amr ibn Mutarrif ibn Alqamah ibn Amr.
  35. Aws ibn Thabit ibn al-Mundhir, brother of Hassan ibn Thabit.
  36. Anas ibn al-Nadr, uncle of Anas ibn Malik, the servant of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).
  37. Qays ibn Mukhallad.
  38. Kaysan, a slave of the Banu al-Najjar.
  39. Sulaym ibn al-Harith.
  40. Nu'man ibn Abd Amr.
  41. Kharijah ibn Zayd ibn Abi Zuhayr.
  42. Sa'd ibn al-Rabi' ibn Amr ibn Abi Zuhayr.
  43. Aws ibn al-Arqam.
  44. Malik ibn Sinan of the Banu Khudrah, the father of Abu Sa'id al-Khudri.
  45. Sa'id ibn Suwayd.
  46. Utbah ibn Rabi'.
  47. Tha'labah ibn Sa'd ibn Malik.
  48. Saqf ibn Farwah ibn al-Badi.
  49. Abd Allah ibn Amr ibn Wahb.
  50. Damrah, an ally of the Banu Tarif.
  51. Nawfal ibn Abd Allah.
  52. Abbas ibn Ubadah.
  53. Nu'man ibn Malik ibn Tha'labah.
  54. al-Mujadhdhar ibn Ziyad.
  55. Ubadah ibn al-Hashas. Nu'man, al-Mujadhdhar, and Ubadah were buried in a single grave.
  56. Rifa'ah ibn Amr.
  57. Abd Allah ibn Amr of the Banu Haram.
  58. Amr ibn al-Jamuh of the Banu Haram. They were buried in a single grave.
  59. Khallad ibn Amr ibn al-Jamuh.
  60. Abu Ayman, the client (مولى) of Amr ibn al-Jamuh.
  61. Sulaym ibn Amr ibn Hadidah.
  62. Antarah, the client of Sulaym.
  63. Sahl ibn Qays ibn Abi Ka'b.
  64. Dhakwan ibn Abd Qays.
  65. Ubayd ibn al-Mu'alla.
  66. Malik ibn Tumaylah.
  67. Harith ibn Adi ibn Kharashah.
  68. Malik ibn Iyas.
  69. Iyas ibn Adi.
  70. Amr ibn Iyas.

These are seventy men according to what Ibn Hisham mentioned in the Sirah of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

Ayahs

وَلَا يَحْزُنكَ ٱلَّذِينَ يُسَـٰرِعُونَ فِى ٱلْكُفْرِ ۚ إِنَّهُمْ لَن يَضُرُّوا۟ ٱللَّهَ شَيْـًٔا ۗ يُرِيدُ ٱللَّهُ أَلَّا يَجْعَلَ لَهُمْ حَظًّا فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۖ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ عَظِيمٌ (١٧٦)
Do not grieve for those who are active in unfaith; they will not hurt Allah in the least: Allah desires to give them no share in the Hereafter, and there is a great punishment for them. (176)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱشْتَرَوُا۟ ٱلْكُفْرَ بِٱلْإِيمَـٰنِ لَن يَضُرُّوا۟ ٱللَّهَ شَيْـًٔا وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (١٧٧)
Those who have bought unfaith for faith will not hurt Allah in the least, and there is a painful punishment for them. (177)
وَلَا يَحْسَبَنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ أَنَّمَا نُمْلِى لَهُمْ خَيْرٌ لِّأَنفُسِهِمْ ۚ إِنَّمَا نُمْلِى لَهُمْ لِيَزْدَادُوٓا۟ إِثْمًا ۚ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ مُّهِينٌ (١٧٨)
Let the faithless not suppose that the respite that We grant them is good for their souls: We give them respite only that they may increase in sin, and there is a humiliating punishment for them. (178)
مَّا كَانَ ٱللَّهُ لِيَذَرَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ عَلَىٰ مَآ أَنتُمْ عَلَيْهِ حَتَّىٰ يَمِيزَ ٱلْخَبِيثَ مِنَ ٱلطَّيِّبِ ۗ وَمَا كَانَ ٱللَّهُ لِيُطْلِعَكُمْ عَلَى ٱلْغَيْبِ وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَجْتَبِى مِن رُّسُلِهِۦ مَن يَشَآءُ ۖ فَـَٔامِنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرُسُلِهِۦ ۚ وَإِن تُؤْمِنُوا۟ وَتَتَّقُوا۟ فَلَكُمْ أَجْرٌ عَظِيمٌ (١٧٩)
Allah will not leave the faithful in your present state, until He has separated the bad ones from the good. Allah will not acquaint you with the Unseen, but Allah chooses whomever He wishes from His apostles. So have faith in Allah and His apostles; and if you are faithful and Godwary, there shall be a great reward for you. (179)
وَلَا يَحْسَبَنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَبْخَلُونَ بِمَآ ءَاتَىٰهُمُ ٱللَّهُ مِن فَضْلِهِۦ هُوَ خَيْرًا لَّهُم ۖ بَلْ هُوَ شَرٌّ لَّهُمْ ۖ سَيُطَوَّقُونَ مَا بَخِلُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۗ وَلِلَّهِ مِيرَٰثُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ (١٨٠)
Let the stingy not suppose that [their grudging] what Allah has given them out of His bounty is good for them; no, it is bad for them.
