They will be collared with what they grudge on the Day of Resurrection. To Allah belongs the heritage of the heavens and the earth, and Allah is well aware of what you do. (180)

(Commentary)

The verses are connected to the preceding verses revealed concerning the Battle of Uhud. It is as if they—especially the first four verses—are a completion of them, because the most important theme addressed by those verses is the matter of divine trial (الابتلاء) and testing (الامتحان) of His servants. Accordingly, these verses serve as a summary (الفذلكة) of the verses of Uhud, wherein Allah, the Glorified, explains that the sunnah (السنة) — the established divine way — of trial and testing is an ongoing process from which there is no escape, neither for the disbeliever nor the believer. Thus, Allah, the Glorified, tests both of them to bring forth what is in the interior (الباطن) of each into the realm of manifestation (الظهور), so that the disbeliever becomes purely designated for the Fire, and the base (الخبيث) is distinguished from the good (الطيب) among the believers.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلا يَحْزُنْكَ الَّذِينَ يُسارِعُونَ فِي الْكُفْرِ "Do not let those who hasten into unfaith grieve you" (3:176) until the end of the verse, is a consolation and a removal of grief by explaining the reality of the matter. Indeed, their hastening into disbelief, their outward cooperation to extinguish the light of Allah, and their occasional apparent victory might cause the believer to grieve, as if they had overcome Allah, the Glorified, in His will to exalt the word of Truth. However, if he contemplates the matter of the general testing (الامتحان العام), he will become certain that Allah is the Victor and that they are all situated within the path of the goals toward which they are directed, so that the ontological guidance (الهداية التكوينية) and legislative guidance (الهداية التشريعية) toward the ends of their affairs may be completed for them. Thus, the disbeliever is directed—by means of being satiated with well-being, blessing, and power, which is the divine "gradual leading to ruin" (الاستدراج) and "stratagem" (المكر الإلهي)—toward the furthest extent of rebellion and disobedience he can commit. Meanwhile, the believer continues to be rubbed against the touchstone of trial (محك الامتحان) to purify the faith (الإيمان) in his interior from any mixture with other than it, so that he becomes sincere for Allah, or his polytheism (الشرك) therein becomes pure, whereby he descends into the falling-place of others among the friends of the Taghut (أولياء الطاغوت) and the leaders of disbelief (أئمة الكفر).

The meaning of the verse is: Do not let those who hasten—and whose speed continues to intensify—into disbelief grieve you. For if you grieve, you only grieve because of what you think they might harm Allah with, but it is not so; they will not harm Allah in the least. This is because they are subjected (مسخرون) to Allah; He leads them through the course of their lives to where no portion remains for them in the Hereafter (which is the ultimate limit of their disbelief), and for them is a painful punishment. Thus, His saying: لا يَحْزُنْكَ "Do not let... grieve you" is an advisory command (أمر إرشادي), and His saying: إِنَّهُمْ "Indeed they..." etc., is the justification for the prohibition, and His saying: يُرِيدُ اللهُ "Allah desires..." etc., is the justification and explanation for their inability to cause harm.

Then the Exalted mentioned the negation of harm from all disbelievers in relation to Him, more general than those hastening into disbelief and others. This is like a universal explanation (البيان الكلي) following a particular explanation (البيان الجزئي), which can serve as the reason for the prohibition (لا يَحْزُنْكَ) and as the reason for its cause (أَنَّهُمْ لَنْ يَضُرُّوا "that they will never harm..."), because the more general serves as the reason for the more specific. The meaning is: We only said that these who hasten do not harm Allah in the least because all disbelievers do not harm Him in the least.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلا يَحْسَبَنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا "Let the faithless not suppose..." (3:178). When He comforted the soul of His Prophet regarding the disbelievers' hastening into their disbelief—explaining that this is, in reality, a divine subjection of them so they may be driven to where no portion remains for them in the Hereafter—He turned the discourse toward the disbelievers themselves. He explained that it is not fitting for them to rejoice in the "granting of respite" (الإملاء) and "deferment" (الإمهال) they find from Allah, for that is a driving of them through "gradual leading to ruin" (الاستدراج) toward an increase in sin. Beyond that is a dominant punishment accompanied by nothing but humiliation—all of this according to the requirements of the sunnah of perfection (سنة التكميل).

His saying, the Exalted: ما كانَ اللهُ لِيَذَرَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ "Allah will not leave the faithful..." (3:179) etc. Then He turned the discourse toward the believers, explaining that the sunnah of trial (سنة الابتلاء) is ongoing among them as well, so that their perfection may also be completed, whereby the sincere believer is purified from others, and the base (الخبيث) is distinguished from the good (الطيب).

Since it might be imagined that there is another way to distinguish the base from the good—namely, that He informs them of the base ones so they may be distinguished from them, thus sparing them all these tribulations and trials they endure due to the mixing of the hypocrites and those in whose hearts is a sickness with them—He repelled this notion by stating that knowledge of the Unseen (الغيب) — that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge — is among that which Allah has reserved for Himself. He does not disclose it to anyone except those He chooses from His messengers, for He may disclose it to them through revelation (الوحي). That is His saying, the Exalted: وَما كانَ اللهُ لِيُطْلِعَكُمْ عَلَى الْغَيْبِ وَلكِنَّ اللهَ يَجْتَبِي مِنْ رُسُلِهِ مَنْ يَشاءُ "and Allah will not inform you of the Unseen, but Allah chooses whomever He wishes of His apostles."

Then He mentioned that since there is no escape from trial and perfection, then believe in Allah and His messengers so that you may be included in the ranks of the good rather than the base. However, faith alone is not sufficient for the persistence of the "goodness of life" (طيب الحياة) until the reward is completed, except through righteous deeds (العمل الصالح) which raise faith to Allah and preserve its goodness. Therefore, He first said: فَآمِنُوا بِاللهِ وَرُسُلِهِ "So believe in Allah and His apostles," then He completed it secondly by saying: وَإِنْ تُؤْمِنُوا وَتَتَّقُوا فَلَكُمْ أَجْرٌ عَظِيمٌ "and if you believe and are God-conscious, there shall be a great reward for you."

From the verse, several points have become clear: First: That the matter of the perfection of souls (تكميل النفوس) and bringing them to their ultimate end and destination of felicity (السعادة) or wretchedness (الشقاء) is something from which there is no escape.

Second: That goodness (الطيب) and baseness (الخباثة), while being attributed to the essences (ذوات) of individuals, revolve around faith (الإيمان) and disbelief (الكفر), which are two matters of choice (أمران اختياريان) for them. This is among the subtle Quranic realities from which many secrets of monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — branch out. This is indicated by His saying, the Exalted: وَلِكُلٍّ وِجْهَةٌ هُوَ مُوَلِّيها فَاسْتَبِقُوا الْخَيْراتِ "Everyone has a direction to which he turns; so hasten towards all that is good" (2:148), when joined with His saying: وَلكِنْ لِيَبْلُوَكُمْ فِي ما آتاكُمْ فَاسْتَبِقُوا الْخَيْراتِ "but [He desired] to test you in what He has given you. So take the lead in all that is good" (5:48). A full discussion of this will come under His saying, the Exalted: لِيَمِيزَ اللهُ الْخَبِيثَ مِنَ الطَّيِّبِ وَيَجْعَلَ الْخَبِيثَ بَعْضَهُ عَلى بَعْضٍ "so that Allah may separate the bad from the good, and put the bad on top of one another" (8:37).

Third: That faith in Allah and His messengers is the substance for the goodness of life, which is the "goodness of the essence" (طيب الذات). As for the reward, it depends upon godwariness (التقوى) and righteous deeds. Therefore, the Exalted first mentioned the discourse of the distinction between the good and the base, then branched from it His saying: فَآمِنُوا بِاللهِ وَرُسُلِهِ "So believe in Allah and His apostles." Then, when He intended to mention the reward, He added godwariness to faith, saying: وَإِنْ تُؤْمِنُوا وَتَتَّقُوا فَلَكُمْ أَجْرٌ عَظِيمٌ "and if you believe and are God-conscious, there shall be a great reward for you."

By this, it becomes clear in His saying, the Exalted: مَنْ عَمِلَ صالِحاً مِنْ ذَكَرٍ أَوْ أُنْثى وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَلَنُحْيِيَنَّهُ حَياةً طَيِّبَةً وَلَنَجْزِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْرَهُمْ بِأَحْسَنِ ما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ "Whoever acts righteously, [whether] male or female, should he be faithful, We shall surely revive him with a good life and pay them their reward by the best of what they used to do" (16:97), that the mentioned revival is a fruit of faith and branched from it, while the compensation with reward is branched from righteous deeds. Thus, faith is the spirit (روح) of the good life, but its persistence until its effects are produced requires righteous deeds—just like natural life, which requires an animal spirit (روح حيواني) for its formation and actualization, while its persistence requires the use of faculties and limbs; if they all became still, it would perish and life would be nullified.

The Majestic Name (لفظ الجلالة) has been repeated several times in the verse. The last three instances are cases of placing the manifest noun (الاسم الظاهر) in the place of the pronoun (المضمر), and this is only to indicate the source of Majesty (الجلال) and Beauty (الجمال) in matters that none but He is characterized by in His divinity: namely, testing, informing of the Unseen, choosing messengers, and the worthiness of faith in Him.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلا يَحْسَبَنَّ الَّذِينَ يَبْخَلُونَ بِما آتاهُمُ اللهُ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ "Let not those who are niggardly with what Allah has given them out of His bounty suppose..." (3:180). When He explained the state of the "granting of respite" (إملاء) to the disbelievers, and the case of miserliness (البخل) with wealth and the failure to spend it in the way of Allah was similar to it—for the miser is joyful and boastful of the wealth he amasses—the Exalted turned the discourse toward them and explained that it is an evil for them. In expressing wealth as بِما آتاهُمُ اللهُ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ "what Allah has given them out of His bounty," there is an intimation of the reason for their blame and censure. His saying: سَيُطَوَّقُونَ "They shall be collared..." etc., is in the position of justifying why miserliness is an evil for them. His saying: وَلِلَّهِ مِيراثُ السَّماواتِ "To Allah belongs the heritage of the heavens..." (3:180), apparently describes the state (حال) of the Day of Resurrection. Likewise His saying: وَاللهُ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ "and Allah is well aware of what you do."

It is possible, though remote, that His saying: وَلِلَّهِ مِيراثُ "To Allah belongs the heritage..." is a circumstantial clause (حال) referring to the subject of "they are niggardly" (يبخلون), and His saying: وَاللهُ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ is also a circumstantial clause referring to it, or an independent sentence (جملة مستأنفة).

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, narrated from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him) that he was asked about the disbeliever: is death better for him or life?

He (peace be upon him) said: الموت خير للمؤمن والكافر ـ لأن الله يقول : ( وَما عِنْدَ اللهِ خَيْرٌ لِلْأَبْرارِ ) ، ويقول : ( لا يَحْسَبَنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا أَنَّما نُمْلِي لَهُمْ خَيْرٌ ) ، الآية. — "Death is better for the believer and the disbeliever—because Allah says: 'and what is with Allah is better for the pious' (3:198), and He says: 'Let the faithless not suppose that the respite We grant them is good for them' (3:178)."

I say: The aforementioned inference in the narration does not entirely accord with the taste of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them), for the pious (الأبرار) are a specific group among the believers, not all of them. Unless it is said that what is meant by "the pious" is all believers by virtue of the element of piety (البر) present in each of them. This meaning was narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from Ibn Mas'ud.

Ayahs

لَّقَدْ سَمِعَ ٱللَّهُ قَوْلَ ٱلَّذِينَ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ فَقِيرٌ وَنَحْنُ أَغْنِيَآءُ ۘ سَنَكْتُبُ مَا قَالُوا۟ وَقَتْلَهُمُ ٱلْأَنۢبِيَآءَ بِغَيْرِ حَقٍّ وَنَقُولُ ذُوقُوا۟ عَذَابَ ٱلْحَرِيقِ (١٨١)
Allah has certainly heard the remark of those who said, ‘Allah is poor and we are rich.’ We will record what they have said, and their killing of the prophets unjustly, and We shall say, ‘Taste the punishment of the burning. (181)
ذَٰلِكَ بِمَا قَدَّمَتْ أَيْدِيكُمْ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَيْسَ بِظَلَّامٍ لِّلْعَبِيدِ (١٨٢)
That is because of what your hands have sent ahead, and because Allah is not tyrannical to His servants.’ (182)
ٱلَّذِينَ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَهِدَ إِلَيْنَآ أَلَّا نُؤْمِنَ لِرَسُولٍ حَتَّىٰ يَأْتِيَنَا بِقُرْبَانٍ تَأْكُلُهُ ٱلنَّارُ ۗ قُلْ قَدْ جَآءَكُمْ رُسُلٌ مِّن قَبْلِى بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ وَبِٱلَّذِى قُلْتُمْ فَلِمَ قَتَلْتُمُوهُمْ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (١٨٣)
Tell those who say, ‘Allah has pledged us not to believe in any apostle unless he brings us an offering consumed by fire,’ ‘Apostles before me certainly did bring you manifest signs and what you speak of. Then why did you kill them, if you are truthful?’ (183)
فَإِن كَذَّبُوكَ فَقَدْ كُذِّبَ رُسُلٌ مِّن قَبْلِكَ جَآءُو بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ وَٱلزُّبُرِ وَٱلْكِتَـٰبِ ٱلْمُنِيرِ (١٨٤)
But if they deny you, [other] apostles have been denied before you, who came with manifest signs, holy writs, and an illuminating scripture. (184)
كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ذَآئِقَةُ ٱلْمَوْتِ ۗ وَإِنَّمَا تُوَفَّوْنَ أُجُورَكُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۖ فَمَن زُحْزِحَ عَنِ ٱلنَّارِ وَأُدْخِلَ ٱلْجَنَّةَ فَقَدْ فَازَ ۗ وَمَا ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَآ إِلَّا مَتَـٰعُ ٱلْغُرُورِ (١٨٥)
Every soul shall taste death, and you will indeed be paid your full rewards on the Day of Resurrection. Whoever is delivered from the Fire and admitted to paradise has certainly succeeded. The life of this world is nothing but the wares of delusion. (185)
۞ لَتُبْلَوُنَّ فِىٓ أَمْوَٰلِكُمْ وَأَنفُسِكُمْ وَلَتَسْمَعُنَّ مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ مِن قَبْلِكُمْ وَمِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوٓا۟ أَذًى كَثِيرًا ۚ وَإِن تَصْبِرُوا۟ وَتَتَّقُوا۟ فَإِنَّ ذَٰلِكَ مِنْ عَزْمِ ٱلْأُمُورِ (١٨٦)
You will surely be tested in your possessions and your souls, and you will surely hear much affront from those who were given the Book before you and from the polytheists; but if you are patient and Godwary, that is indeed the steadiest of courses. (186)
وَإِذْ أَخَذَ ٱللَّهُ مِيثَـٰقَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ لَتُبَيِّنُنَّهُۥ لِلنَّاسِ وَلَا تَكْتُمُونَهُۥ فَنَبَذُوهُ وَرَآءَ ظُهُورِهِمْ وَٱشْتَرَوْا۟ بِهِۦ ثَمَنًا قَلِيلًا ۖ فَبِئْسَ مَا يَشْتَرُونَ (١٨٧)
When Allah made a covenant with those who were given the Book: ‘You shall explain it for the people, and you shall not conceal it,’ they cast it behind their backs and sold it for a paltry gain. How evil is what they buy! (187)
لَا تَحْسَبَنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَفْرَحُونَ بِمَآ أَتَوا۟ وَّيُحِبُّونَ أَن يُحْمَدُوا۟ بِمَا لَمْ يَفْعَلُوا۟ فَلَا تَحْسَبَنَّهُم بِمَفَازَةٍ مِّنَ ٱلْعَذَابِ ۖ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (١٨٨)
Do not suppose those who brag about what they have done, and love to be praised for what they have not done—do not suppose them saved from punishment and there is a painful punishment for them. (188)
وَلِلَّهِ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (١٨٩)
To Allah belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth, and Allah has power over all things. (189)

(Commentary)

The verses are connected to what preceded them. Most of the previous verses were concerned with rousng the people and encouraging them toward struggle in the way of Allah (الجهاد في سبيل الله) with their wealth and their lives, and warning them against weakness (الوهن), failure (الفشل), and stinginess (البخل). Related to this is the saying of the Jews: "Indeed, Allah is poor and we are rich," their turning of matters against the Muslims, their denial of the signs of the Message (الرسالة), and their concealment of that which the covenant (الميثاق) was taken from them to clarify. These are the matters the verses undertake to explain, along with what they contain of strengthening the hearts of the believers (المؤمنين) toward steadfastness, patience, and firmness, and the incitement toward spending in the way of Allah.

His saying, the Exalted: لَقَدْ سَمِعَ اللهُ قَوْلَ الَّذِينَ قالُوا إِنَّ اللهَ فَقِيرٌ وَنَحْنُ أَغْنِياءُ "Allah has certainly heard the speech of those who said, ‘Allah is poor and we are rich’" (3:181). The speakers are the Jews, based on the context provided in the conclusion of the discourse regarding their killing of the prophets and other matters.

They only said this when they heard the likes of His saying, the Exalted: مَنْ ذَا الَّذِي يُقْرِضُ اللهَ قَرْضاً حَسَناً "Who is it that will lend Allah a good loan..." (2:245). This is partially evidenced by its connection to the previous verse: وَلا يَحْسَبَنَّ الَّذِينَ يَبْخَلُونَ "Let not those who are stingy..." (3:180).

Alternatively, they said this when they saw the poverty and destitution (الفاقة) of the generality of the believers. They said it as an insinuation (تعريضاً) that if their Lord were rich, He would have been protective of them and enriched them; thus, He is nothing but poor and we are rich.

His saying, the Exalted: سَنَكْتُبُ ما قالُوا وَقَتْلَهُمُ الْأَنْبِياءَ بِغَيْرِ حَقٍّ "We will write down what they have said, and their killing of the prophets unjustly" (3:181). What is meant by writing (الكتابة) is preservation and recording, or writing in the scrolls of their deeds; the ultimate outcome is the same. What is meant by "killing the prophets unjustly" is killing with recognition and intent, rather than by oversight, error, or ignorance. Allah paired this saying of theirs with their killing of the prophets because it is a momentous statement. His saying: عَذابَ الْحَرِيقِ "the punishment of the burning" (3:181)—the burning (الحريق) is the fire or the flame; it is also said to mean that which causes burning (المحرق).

His saying, the Exalted: ذلِكَ بِما قَدَّمَتْ أَيْدِيكُمْ "That is because of what your hands have sent ahead" (3:182). That is, because of the deeds you have sent before you. It is attributed to the hands (الأيدي) because they are usually the instrument of sending forth. His saying: وَأَنَّ اللهَ لَيْسَ بِظَلَّامٍ لِلْعَبِيدِ "and that Allah is not tyrannical to [His] servants" (3:182) is coordinated with His saying: بِما قَدَّمَتْ "because of what [your hands] have sent ahead," and it serves as a justification for the recording and the punishment. For if there were no such preservation and recompense, it would be a neglect of the system of deeds, and in that would be much injustice (ظلم) due to the multiplicity of deeds; thus, He would be a tyrant (ظلام) to His servants—exalted is He far above that.

His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِينَ قالُوا إِنَّ اللهَ عَهدَ إِلَيْنا "Those who said, ‘Allah has pledged to us...’" (3:183). This is an attribute of those mentioned before. The pledge (العهد) is the command. The offering (القربان) is that by which one seeks nearness, such as livestock or other things. The "fire consuming it" is a metaphor for the fire burning it. What is meant by His saying: قَدْ جاءَكُمْ رُسُلٌ مِنْ قَبْلِي "Messengers have certainly come to you before me" (3:183) refers to the likes of Zechariah and John from among the prophets of the Children of Israel who were killed by their hands.

His saying, the Exalted: فَإِنْ كَذَّبُوكَ فَقَدْ كُذِّبَ "If they deny you, then [other] messengers have certainly been denied [before you]" (3:184). This is a consolation for the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله regarding their denial of him. The scriptures (الزبر) is the plural of zabur, which is a book of wisdom and exhortations. By "the scriptures" (الزبر) and "the luminous Book" (الكتاب المنير), the likes of the Book of Noah, the Scrolls of Abraham, the Torah, and the Gospel are intended.

His saying, the Exalted: كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ذائِقَةُ الْمَوْتِ "Every soul shall taste death" (3:185). The verse contains a promise for the one who confirms the truth and a threat for the one who denies it. It begins with the general decree (الحكم العام) ordained for every possessor of a soul. Full payment (التوفية) is the complete giving. Some have used this verse as evidence for the reality of the intermediate realm (البرزخ) — the state between death and resurrection — because it indicates that some giving precedes [the Day of Resurrection], and that what occurs on the Day of Resurrection is the complete giving; this is a fine deduction. Being "delivered" (الزحزحة) is being distanced; its linguistic origin is the repeated pulling with haste. Success (الفوز) is the attainment of one's desire. Illusion (الغرور) is the verbal noun of gharra (to deceive), or it is the plural of gharr (deceiver).

His saying, the Exalted: لَتُبْلَوُنَّ فِي أَمْوالِكُمْ وَأَنْفُسِكُمْ "You will surely be tested in your possessions and your souls" (3:186). Testing (الإبلاء) is trial (الاختبار). After the Glorified mentioned the course of trial and testing upon the believers, and then mentioned the saying of the Jews—which is of the nature that would weaken the resolve of the believers—He informed them that this divine testing and the hurtful sayings from the People of the Book and the polytheists will be repeated upon the believers. They will frequently encounter these things and they will strike their ears; therefore, they must be patient and God-conscious (يتقوا) so that their Lord may protect them from slipping and failing, and so they may be masters of resolve and will. This is an announcement before the occurrence so they may prepare for it and accustom their souls to it.

In His saying: وَلَتَسْمَعُنَ "and you will surely hear" until His saying: أَذىً كَثِيراً "much hurt" (3:186), "much hurt" (الأذى الكثير) has been placed in the position of the "saying." This is of the category of placing the effect (الأثر) in the position of the cause (المؤثر) metaphorically.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذْ أَخَذَ اللهُ مِيثاقَ "And when Allah took the covenant..." (3:187). Flinging (النبذ) is throwing away. "Flinging it behind one's back" is like a proverb intended to mean abandonment and lack of concern, just as their saying "he placed it before his eyes" is like a proverb intended to mean taking hold and adherence.

His saying, the Exalted: لا تَحْسَبَنَّ الَّذِينَ يَفْرَحُونَ بِما أَتَوْا "Do not suppose those who exult in what they have done..." (3:188) until the end of the two verses. That is, in what they were given of wealth, the necessary consequence of which is the love of wealth and stinginess with it. Safety (المفازة) is salvation. These people only perished because their hearts were attached to falsehood (الباطل), so the Truth (الحق) has no authority over them.

Then the Exalted mentioned the matter of His sovereignty over the heavens and the earth and His power over all things. These two attributes are suitable to serve as the justification for the contents of all the preceding verses.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Jarir and Ibn al-Mundhir narrated from Qatadah regarding His saying: لَقَدْ سَمِعَ اللهُ "Allah has certainly heard..." (3:181). He said: "It was mentioned to us that it was revealed concerning Huyayy ibn Akhtab when there was revealed: مَنْ ذَا الَّذِي يُقْرِضُ اللهَ قَرْضاً حَسَناً فَيُضاعِفَهُ لَهُ أَضْعافاً كَثِيرَةً 'Who is it that will lend Allah a good loan, so He may multiply it for him many times over?' (2:245). He said: 'Our Lord asks us for a loan? Only the poor asks the rich for a loan.'"

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, regarding the verse, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام, he said: "By Allah, they did not see Allah so as to know He is poor, but they saw the friends (أولياء) of Allah poor and said: 'If He were rich, He would have enriched His friends,' so they boasted of wealth over Allah."

In al-Manaqib, from Imam al-Baqir عليه‌السلام: "They are those who claim that the Imam is in need of what they bring to him."

I say: As for the first two narrations, their meaning's correspondence to the verse has already been mentioned. As for the third, it is of the category of conceptual application (الجري).

In al-Kafi, from Imam al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام, he said: "There were five hundred years between those who said [the words] and those who killed [the prophets]—yet Allah bound the killing to them because of their satisfaction with what [their ancestors] did."

I say: The number of years mentioned does not align with the existing Common Era history; refer back to the previous historical discussion.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, regarding His saying, the Exalted: كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ذائِقَةُ الْمَوْتِ "Every soul shall taste death" (3:185), Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Ali ibn Abi Talib عليه‌السلام, who said: "When the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله passed away and the condolences arrived, a comer came to them whose sound they could hear but whose person they could not see. He said: 'Peace be upon you, O People of the House (أهل البيت), and the mercy of Allah and His blessings. كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ذائِقَةُ الْمَوْتِ وَإِنَّما تُوَفَّوْنَ أُجُورَكُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ (Every soul shall taste death, and you will indeed be paid your full rewards on the Day of Resurrection). Indeed, in Allah there is consolation for every calamity, a successor for everyone who perishes, and a recovery for everything that is lost. So in Allah put your trust, and in Him place your hope, for the truly afflicted is the one who is deprived of reward.'" Ali said: "This was al-Khidr."

In the same source, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Sahl ibn Sa'd, who said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: 'The place of one of your whips in Paradise is better than the world and all that is in it.' Then he recited this verse: فَمَنْ زُحْزِحَ عَنِ النَّارِ وَأُدْخِلَ الْجَنَّةَ فَقَدْ فازَ (So whoever is delivered from the Fire and admitted into Paradise has certainly succeeded) (3:185)."

I say: It is also narrated therein through some other chains from others. Know that there are many narrations regarding the occasions of revelation (أسباب النزول) for these verses which we have omitted because it is evident they are of the nature of theoretical application.

Ayahs

إِنَّ فِى خَلْقِ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَٱخْتِلَـٰفِ ٱلَّيْلِ وَٱلنَّهَارِ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّأُو۟لِى ٱلْأَلْبَـٰبِ (١٩٠)
Indeed in the creation of the heavens and the earth and the alternation of night and day, there are signs for those who possess intellect. (190)
ٱلَّذِينَ يَذْكُرُونَ ٱللَّهَ قِيَـٰمًا وَقُعُودًا وَعَلَىٰ جُنُوبِهِمْ وَيَتَفَكَّرُونَ فِى خَلْقِ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ رَبَّنَا مَا خَلَقْتَ هَـٰذَا بَـٰطِلًا سُبْحَـٰنَكَ فَقِنَا عَذَابَ ٱلنَّارِ (١٩١)
Those who remember Allah standing, sitting, and lying on their sides, and reflect on the creation of the heavens and the earth [and say], ‘Our Lord, You have not created this in vain! Immaculate are You! Save us from the punishment of the Fire. (191)
رَبَّنَآ إِنَّكَ مَن تُدْخِلِ ٱلنَّارَ فَقَدْ أَخْزَيْتَهُۥ ۖ وَمَا لِلظَّـٰلِمِينَ مِنْ أَنصَارٍ (١٩٢)
Our Lord, whoever that You make enter the Fire will surely have been disgraced by You, and the wrongdoers will have no helpers. (192)
رَّبَّنَآ إِنَّنَا سَمِعْنَا مُنَادِيًا يُنَادِى لِلْإِيمَـٰنِ أَنْ ءَامِنُوا۟ بِرَبِّكُمْ فَـَٔامَنَّا ۚ رَبَّنَا فَٱغْفِرْ لَنَا ذُنُوبَنَا وَكَفِّرْ عَنَّا سَيِّـَٔاتِنَا وَتَوَفَّنَا مَعَ ٱلْأَبْرَارِ (١٩٣)
Our Lord, we have indeed heard a summoner calling to faith, declaring, ‘‘Have faith in your Lord!’’ So we believed. Our Lord, forgive us our sins and absolve us of our misdeeds, and make us die with the pious. (193)
رَبَّنَا وَءَاتِنَا مَا وَعَدتَّنَا عَلَىٰ رُسُلِكَ وَلَا تُخْزِنَا يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۗ إِنَّكَ لَا تُخْلِفُ ٱلْمِيعَادَ (١٩٤)
Our Lord, give us what You have promised us through Your apostles, and do not disgrace us on the Day of Resurrection. Indeed You do not break Your promise.’ (194)
فَٱسْتَجَابَ لَهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ أَنِّى لَآ أُضِيعُ عَمَلَ عَـٰمِلٍ مِّنكُم مِّن ذَكَرٍ أَوْ أُنثَىٰ ۖ بَعْضُكُم مِّنۢ بَعْضٍ ۖ فَٱلَّذِينَ هَاجَرُوا۟ وَأُخْرِجُوا۟ مِن دِيَـٰرِهِمْ وَأُوذُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِى وَقَـٰتَلُوا۟ وَقُتِلُوا۟ لَأُكَفِّرَنَّ عَنْهُمْ سَيِّـَٔاتِهِمْ وَلَأُدْخِلَنَّهُمْ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ ثَوَابًا مِّنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عِندَهُۥ حُسْنُ ٱلثَّوَابِ (١٩٥)
Then their Lord answered them, ‘I do not waste the work of any worker among you, whether male or female; you are all on the same footing. So those who migrated and were expelled from their homes, and were tormented in My way, and those who fought and were killed—I will surely absolve them of their misdeeds and I will admit them into gardens with streams running in them, as a reward from Allah, and Allah—with Him is the best of rewards.’ (195)
لَا يَغُرَّنَّكَ تَقَلُّبُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْبِلَـٰدِ (١٩٦)
Never be misled by the bustle of the faithless in the towns. (196)
مَتَـٰعٌ قَلِيلٌ ثُمَّ مَأْوَىٰهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ ۚ وَبِئْسَ ٱلْمِهَادُ (١٩٧)
It is a trivial enjoyment; then their refuge is hell, and it is an evil resting place. (197)
لَـٰكِنِ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّقَوْا۟ رَبَّهُمْ لَهُمْ جَنَّـٰتٌ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا نُزُلًا مِّنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَمَا عِندَ ٱللَّهِ خَيْرٌ لِّلْأَبْرَارِ (١٩٨)
But those who are wary of their Lord—for them shall be gardens with streams running in them, to remain in them [forever], a hospitality from Allah; and what is with Allah is better for the pious. (198)
وَإِنَّ مِنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لَمَن يُؤْمِنُ بِٱللَّهِ وَمَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكُمْ وَمَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْهِمْ خَـٰشِعِينَ لِلَّهِ لَا يَشْتَرُونَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ ثَمَنًا قَلِيلًا ۗ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمْ أَجْرُهُمْ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَرِيعُ ٱلْحِسَابِ (١٩٩)
Indeed among the People of the Book there are some who have faith in Allah and in what has been sent down to you, and in what has been sent down to them. Humble toward Allah, they do not sell the signs of Allah for a paltry gain. They shall have their reward near their Lord; indeed Allah is swift at reckoning. (199)

(Commentary)

These verses serve as a summary of what has preceded in this Surah regarding the description of the believers (المؤمنين), the polytheists (المشركين), and the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب). They clarify that the state of the righteous (الأبرار) among the believers is the remembrance of Allah, the Glorified, reflecting upon His signs, seeking refuge in Allah from the punishment of the Fire, and asking for forgiveness and Paradise. Furthermore, they show that Allah has responded to them and will grant them what they asked for—this being their general state—whereas the state of those who disbelieve is that they fluctuate within a meager enjoyment, after which their resting place is the Fire. Thus, the state of the believers is not to be compared to theirs. An exception is made for those among the People of the Book who follow the truth; they are joined with the believers.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ فِي خَلْقِ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ "Indeed in the creation of the heavens and the earth" (3:190). What is meant by creation (الخلق) is the mode of their existence, their effects, and their activities—such as motion, rest, change, and transformation. Thus, the creation of the heavens and the earth and the alternation of night and day encompass the majority of the perceptible signs (الآيات المحسوسة). The explanation of this has already been provided in Surah al-Baqarah (1). Similarly, the meaning of "those possessors of intellect" (أولي الألباب) has been previously discussed (2).

His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِينَ يَذْكُرُونَ اللَّهَ قِيَامًا وَقُعُودًا "Those who remember Allah standing and sitting" (3:191), etc. That is, they remember Allah in all their states—whether standing, sitting, or lying on their sides. The discussion regarding the meaning of remembrance (الذكر) and reflection (التفكر) has already passed. The sum total of the meaning of these two verses is that observing the signs of the heavens and the earth and the alternation of night and day has bequeathed to them a perpetual remembrance of Allah, such that they do not forget Him in any state. It has also granted them a reflection upon the creation of the heavens and the earth through which they remember that Allah will surely resurrect them for recompense; at that point, they seek His mercy and ask for the fulfillment of His promise.

His saying, the Exalted: رَبَّنَا مَا خَلَقْتَ هَٰذَا بَاطِلًا "Our Lord, You have not created this in vain!" (3:191). The demonstrative pronoun "this" (هذا) is used even though the object referred to is plural and feminine, because the purpose is not related to distinguishing their individual entities or names; rather, they are all considered as a single creation. This is similar to what Allah, the Exalted, narrated of the words of Ibrahim: فَلَمَّا رَأَى الشَّمْسَ بَازِغَةً قَالَ هَٰذَا رَبِّي هَٰذَا أَكْبَرُ "When he saw the sun rising, he said, ‘This is my Lord! This is bigger!’" (6:78), due to his lack of prior knowledge of its reality or name other than it being a "thing."

The "vain" or "void" (الباطل) is that which has no ultimate purpose (غاية) to which an objective is attached. The Exalted says: فَأَمَّا الزَّبَدُ فَيَذْهَبُ جُفَاءً ۖ وَأَمَّا مَا يَنْفَعُ النَّاسَ فَيَمْكُثُ فِي الْأَرْضِ "As for the scum, it leaves as dross, and as for what profits mankind, it stays on the earth" (13:17). Therefore, when they negated vanity from the creation, it became clear to them that Allah would gather mankind for recompense, and that He, the Exalted, would there requite the wrongdoers (الظالمين) with a recompense of disgrace (خزي), which is the Fire. There is no one to repel the wisdom of punishment, for otherwise, creation would be in vain. This is the meaning of their saying: "So save us from the punishment of the Fire. Our Lord, whoever You admit into the Fire, You have surely disgraced him, and the wrongdoers have no helpers."

His saying, the Exalted: رَبَّنَا إِنَّنَا سَمِعْنَا مُنَادِيًا "Our Lord, we have indeed heard a summoner" (3:193). The "summoner" (المنادي) refers to the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله. His saying: أَنْ آمِنُوا "calling to faith" (3:193) clarifies the nature of the summons, and the particle "that" (أن) is explanatory (تفسيرية). Since they mentioned their faith in the summoner—who is the Messenger—and he informs them of matters from Allah, the Exalted, warning them against some, such as sins (الذنوب), evil deeds (السيئات), and dying in a state of disbelief or sin, and encouraging them toward others, such as forgiveness (المغفرة), mercy (الرحمة), and the details of Paradise which Allah has promised His righteous (الأبرار) believing servants, they asked their Lord to forgive them, to absolve them of their evil deeds, and to take their souls with the righteous. They also asked Him to fulfill for them what He promised of Paradise and mercy through what the Messengers guaranteed them by Allah's permission. Thus they said: فَاغْفِرْ لَنَا ذُنُوبَنَا "so forgive us our sins" (3:193), etc. His saying, the Exalted: عَلَىٰ رُسُلِكَ "through Your apostles" (3:194) means: what You charged Your Messengers with and what the Messengers guaranteed on Your behalf. His saying: وَلَا تُخْزِنَا "and do not disgrace us" (3:194) means: by breaking the promise. For this reason, He followed it with His saying: إِنَّكَ لَا تُخْلِفُ الْمِيعَادَ "Indeed You do not break Your promise" (3:194).

It has become clear from the verses that they only attained conviction in Allah, the Last Day, and that Allah has Messengers, by observing the signs. As for the details of what the Prophet brought, that was through the path of faith in the Messenger. Thus, they follow the primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — in what the primordial nature judges, and they follow hearing and obedience in what pertains to that.

His saying, the Exalted: فَاسْتَجَابَ لَهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ "Then their Lord answered them" (3:195), etc. The use of the term "Lord" (الرب) and its annexation to them indicates the surging of Divine Mercy. This is also indicated by the generalization in His saying: أَنِّي لَا أُضِيعُ عَمَلَ عَامِلٍ مِنْكُمْ "I do not leave to waste the work of any worker among you" (3:195). Thus, with Allah, there is no distinction between one deed and another, nor between one worker and another.

Accordingly, His saying, the Exalted, in the position of branching out (التفريع): فَالَّذِينَ هَاجَرُوا وَأُخْرِجُوا مِنْ دِيَارِهِمْ وَأُوذُوا "So those who migrated and were expelled from their homes and were persecuted" (3:195), etc., is in the position of detailing righteous deeds (صالحات الأعمال) to affirm their reward. The conjunction "and" (الواو) is for detailing (التفصيل) rather than mere grouping (الجمع), so that it does not merely state the reward of the martyrs among the migrants alone.

Furthermore, the verse only details those deeds to which this Surah invites and in which it intensely encourages and exhorts—namely, preferring religion over homeland, enduring harm in the way of Allah, and struggle (الجهاد).

It appears that what is meant by migration (المهاجرة) includes migrating away from polytheism, kinsfolk, and homeland due to the absolute nature of the term, and because it is contrasted with His saying: وَأُخْرِجُوا مِنْ دِيَارِهِمْ "and were expelled from their homes," which is a specific type of migration. It is also supported by His subsequent saying: لَأُكَفِّرَنَّ عَنْهُمْ سَيِّئَاتِهِمْ "I will surely absolve them of their misdeeds" (3:195), for the apparent meaning of "misdeeds" (السيئات) in the Quran refers to minor sins (صغائر المعاصي); thus, they "migrated" from major sins through avoidance and repentance. Therefore, the mentioned migration is more general—so understand this.

His saying, the Exalted: لَا يَغُرَّنَّكَ تَقَلُّبُ "Never be misled by the bustle..." (3:196), etc. This is in the position of repelling a potential objection (دفع الدخل). The implied meaning is: this is the state of the righteous believers and this is their reward; as for the prosperity, luxurious living, and abundant livelihood you see the disbelievers enjoying, let that not deceive you (the address is to the Prophet, but the intended audience is the people), for it is a meager enjoyment (متاع قليل) that has no permanence.

His saying, the Exalted: لَٰكِنِ الَّذِينَ اتَّقَوْا رَبَّهُمْ "But those who are wary of their Lord..." (3:198), etc. The "hospitality" (النزل) refers to what is prepared for a guest (النازل) of food, drink, and other things. The intended people are the righteous (الأبرار), as evidenced by what is at the end of the verse. This supports what we mentioned—that the previous verse was to repel an objection.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنَّ مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتَابِ "And indeed among the People of the Book..." (3:199), etc. The meaning is that they share with the believers in the excellence of reward. The purpose is to show that otherworldly felicity is not based on ethnicity such that the People of the Book would be barred from it even if they believe; rather, the matter revolves around faith in Allah and His Messengers. If they believe, they and the believers are equal.

Those praised among the People of the Book are exonerated from what Allah censured them for in previous verses—namely, differentiating between the Messengers of Allah, and concealing what they were covenanted to clarify, bartering Allah's signs for a paltry price.

(A Philosophical Discussion and Comparison)

Observation and experience dictate that man and woman are two individuals of a single substantial species (نوع جوهري), which is the human being (الإنسان). All the effects observed in the category of man are observed in the category of woman without difference. The manifestation of the effects of a species necessitates the realization of its subject (الموضوع) without doubt. Yes, the categories may differ in the intensity or weakness of some shared effects, but this does not necessitate the nullification of the species-nature (النوعية) in the individual. From this, it becomes clear that the species-perfections (الاستكمالات النوعية) possible for one of the two categories are possible for the other. Among these are the spiritual perfections (الاستكمالات المعنوية) attained through faith, acts of obedience, and means of proximity. Thus, it will be clear to you that the most beautiful and comprehensive word in conveying this meaning is His saying, the Glorified: أَنِّي لَا أُضِيعُ عَمَلَ عَامِلٍ مِنْكُمْ مِنْ ذَكَرٍ أَوْ أُنْثَىٰ ۖ بَعْضُكُمْ مِنْ بَعْضٍ "I do not leave to waste the work of any worker among you, whether male or female; you are all on the same footing" (3:195).

If you compare this to what is mentioned in the Torah, the difference between the positions of the two books will be manifest to you. In the Book of Ecclesiastes in the Torah, it says: "I applied my heart to know, to search and seek out wisdom and the reason of things, and to know that wickedness is folly and that foolishness is madness. And I find more bitter than death the woman whose heart is snares and nets, and whose hands are fetters... One man among a thousand I have found, but a woman among all these I have not found." Most ancient nations did not believe her deeds were acceptable to Allah, the Glorified. In Greece, she was called an abomination from the work of Satan. The Romans and some Greeks believed she had no soul, while the man possessed a human, immaterial soul (نفس مجردة إنسانية). The Council of Mâcon in France in 586 CE, after much debate regarding her, decided that she is a human being but created to serve man. In England, approximately one hundred years ago, she was not considered part of human society. Refer to books on opinions, creeds, and the customs of nations, and you will find wonders among their views.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Abu Nu’aym narrated in al-Hilyah from Ibn Abbas, who said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله said: تفكروا في خلق الله ولا تفكروا في الله — 'Reflect upon the creation of Allah, and do not reflect upon Allah.'"

I say: This meaning has also been narrated through other chains from several companions, such as Abdullah ibn Salam and Ibn Umar, from him صلى الله عليه وآله. The narration is also reported through Shi'a chains. What is meant by "reflecting upon Allah" or "upon the essence (الذات) of Allah"—depending on the variation of the reports—is reflecting upon His inner reality (كنهه), whereas He, the Exalted, has said: وَلَا يُحِيطُونَ بِهِ عِلْمًا "while they do not comprehend Him in [their] knowledge" (20:110). As for His attributes, the Quran is the most just witness that He, the Exalted, is known through them, and it has encouraged knowing Him through them in many verses.

In the same source, Abu al-Shaykh narrated in al-Azamah from Abu Hurayrah, who said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله said: فكرة ساعة خير من عبادة ستين سنة — 'An hour's reflection is better than sixty years of worship.'"

I say: In some narrations, it says: "than a night's worship," and in others: "than a year's worship." It is also narrated through Shi'a chains.

It has been narrated through Sunni chains that His saying, the Exalted: فَاسْتَجَابَ لَهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ "Then their Lord answered them" (3:195), was revealed concerning Umm Salamah—when she said to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "O Messenger of Allah, I do not hear Allah mentioning women regarding the migration (الهجرة) at all"—so Allah revealed: فَاسْتَجَابَ لَهُمْ "Then their Lord answered them," the verse.

It has been narrated through Shi'a chains that His saying: فَالَّذِينَ هَاجَرُوا وَأُخْرِجُوا "So those who migrated and were expelled" (3:195), was revealed concerning Ali عليه السلام when he migrated accompanied by the "Fatimahs": Fatimah bint Asad, Fatimah bint Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله, and Fatimah bint al-Zubayr. They were later joined in Dajnan by Umm Ayman and a group of the weak among the believers. They traveled while remembering Allah in all their states until they reached the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and the verses were revealed.

It has been narrated through Sunni chains that it was revealed concerning the Migrants (المهاجرين). It is also narrated that His saying: لَا يَغُرَّنَّكَ تَقَلُّبُ "Never be misled by the bustle..." (3:196), was revealed when some believers wished for the prosperous state of the disbelievers. It is also narrated that His saying: وَإِنَّ مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتَابِ "And indeed among the People of the Book..." (3:199), was revealed concerning the Negus (النجاشي) and a group of his companions; when he died, the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله prayed over him while in Medina, and some hypocrites criticized him for praying over someone not of his religion, so Allah revealed: وَإِنَّ مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتَابِ "And indeed among the People of the Book," the verse.

All of these are narrations that apply the verses to specific stories; they are not the actual occasions of revelation (أسباب النزول) in the true sense.

(1) Commentary on verse 164 of Surah al-Baqarah. (2) In the commentary of the seventh verse of this Surah.

Ayah

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱصْبِرُوا۟ وَصَابِرُوا۟ وَرَابِطُوا۟ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ (٢٠٠)
O you who have faith! Be patient, stand firm, and close [your] ranks, and be wary of Allah so that you may be felicitous. (200)

(Commentary)

The verse serves as a summary (الفذلكة) of the detailed explanation provided in the Surah, concluding it by extracting the result and presenting it.

His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اصْبِرُوا وَصابِرُوا — "O you who have faith! Be patient, and stand firm" (3:200) etc. The commands are absolute; thus, patience (الصبر) refers to patience in the face of hardships, patience in the obedience of Allah, and patience in refraining from His disobedience. In any case, it refers to the patience of the individual, as evidenced by the term contrasted with it.

Standing firm (المصابرة) — the act of collective endurance and bearing harm as a group — is based on the patience of some relying on the patience of others. Through this, the state is strengthened, the quality intensified, and its impact multiplied. This is a perceptible matter regarding the influence of the individual when his personality is considered in isolation versus when considered in a state of social gathering and cooperation, where forces are joined together. We shall discuss this extensively in its proper place, Allah willing.

His saying, the Exalted: وَرابِطُوا — "and be ever ready" (3:200) — is broader in meaning than standing firm (المصابرة). It refers to the formation of a community (الجماعة) and the interconnection (الارتباط) between their powers and actions in all affairs of their religious life, whether in times of hardship or ease. Since the objective of this is to attain the reality of the intended happiness (السعادة) for this world and the Hereafter — otherwise, only a partial worldly happiness would be achieved, which is not true happiness — He followed these commands with His saying, the Exalted: وَاتَّقُوا اللهَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ — "and be God-conscious, so that you may be felicitous" (3:200), meaning complete and true felicity (الفلاح).

(A Discourse on Social Bond in Islamic Society)

1 — Man and Society: The fact that the human species is a social species (نوع اجتماعي) does not require much research to prove. Every individual of this species is created with an innate disposition (مفطور) toward this. Man has always lived in a state of social gathering, as narrated by history and the observable remains that tell of the most ancient eras in which this species lived and ruled upon this earth.

The Quran has informed us of this in the best possible manner in many verses, such as His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ إِنَّا خَلَقْناكُمْ مِنْ ذَكَرٍ وَأُنْثى وَجَعَلْناكُمْ شُعُوباً وَقَبائِلَ لِتَعارَفُوا — "O mankind! Indeed, We created you from a male and a female, and made you nations and tribes that you may identify with one another" (49:13). And He, the Exalted, said: نَحْنُ قَسَمْنا بَيْنَهُمْ مَعِيشَتَهُمْ فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَرَفَعْنا بَعْضَهُمْ فَوْقَ بَعْضٍ دَرَجاتٍ لِيَتَّخِذَ بَعْضُهُمْ بَعْضاً سُخْرِيًّا — "It is We who dispense their livelihood between them in the present life, and We elevate some of them above others in rank, so that some of them may employ others in service" (43:32). And He, the Exalted, said: بَعْضُكُمْ مِنْ بَعْضٍ — "You are all [members] of one another" (3:195). And He, the Exalted, said: وَهُوَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ مِنَ الْماءِ بَشَراً فَجَعَلَهُ نَسَباً وَصِهْراً — "It is He who created the human being from water, then He established for him lineage and kinship" (25:54), among other verses. (1)

2 — Man and His Growth within Society: Human society, like all other human spiritual properties (الخواص الروحية) and what pertains to them, did not come into existence in a complete and perfect state that accepts no growth or increase. Rather, like all other human spiritual and cognitive matters, it has continued to perfect itself alongside the perfection of man in his material and spiritual attainments. In truth, it was not to be expected that this property would be an exception among all human properties, appearing at its first emergence as complete and perfect as it could possibly be. Instead, like other human properties linked to the faculties of knowledge (العلم) and will (الإرادة), it attains perfection gradually in man. What appears from reflecting upon the state of this species is that the first form of social gathering to appear was domestic society (الاجتماع المنزلي) through marriage. This is because its natural driver — the reproductive system — is the strongest factor of social gathering, as it cannot be realized by a single individual at all, unlike nutrition and other needs.

Subsequently, there emerged from it the property we named in previous discussions of this book as "utilization" (الاستخدام) — the act of man employing others as intermediaries to fulfill his needs by extending his authority and imposing his will upon them. This then manifested in the form of leadership (الرئاسة), such as the head of the household, the head of the clan, the head of the tribe, and the head of the nation. Naturally, the designated leader among a group was initially the strongest and bravest, then the bravest and most abundant in wealth and children, and so on, until it culminated in the one most knowledgeable in the arts of governance and politics. This was the primary cause for the emergence of idolatry (الوثنية) and its persistence to this day. We shall provide a full discussion of this in what is to come, Allah the Mighty willing.

Although the property of social gathering in all its forms (domestic and otherwise) never left humanity during these stages for even a moment, it was not consciously recognized by man in detail. Rather, it lived and grew as a byproduct of other properties that man was concerned with, such as utilization (الاستخدام) and defense (الدفاع) and the like.

The Noble Quran informs us that the first to alert man to social gathering in detail and to care for its preservation independently was Prophethood (النبوة). He, the Exalted, said: وَما كانَ النَّاسُ إِلَّا أُمَّةً واحِدَةً فَاخْتَلَفُوا — "Mankind were but a single community; then they differed" (10:19). And He said: كانَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً واحِدَةً فَبَعَثَ اللهُ النَّبِيِّينَ مُبَشِّرِينَ وَمُنْذِرِينَ وَأَنْزَلَ مَعَهُمُ الْكِتابَ بِالْحَقِّ لِيَحْكُمَ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ فِيمَا اخْتَلَفُوا فِيهِ — "Mankind were but one community; then Allah sent the prophets as bearers of good news and as warners, and He sent down with them the Book with the truth, that it may judge between the people concerning that about which they differed" (2:213). This informs us that man in his most ancient eras was a single, simple community (أمة واحدة) with no differences among them, until differences emerged and disputes became apparent. Then Allah sent the prophets and sent down the Book with them to remove the difference and return them to a social unity preserved by legislated laws.

He, the Exalted, said: شَرَعَ لَكُمْ مِنَ الدِّينِ ما وَصَّى بِهِ نُوحاً وَالَّذِي أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ وَما وَصَّيْنا بِهِ إِبْراهِيمَ وَمُوسى وَعِيسى أَنْ أَقِيمُوا الدِّينَ وَلا تَتَفَرَّقُوا فِيهِ — "He has prescribed for you the religion which He had enjoined upon Noah and which We have revealed to you, and which We had enjoined upon Abraham, Moses and Jesus: 'Maintain the religion, and do not be divided regarding it'" (42:13). Thus, He informed us that the removal of differences among people and the creation of unity in their word was only in the form of a call to establish the religion and not to be divided in it. Therefore, religion (الدين) was the guarantor of their righteous social gathering.

The verse — as you see — attributes this call (the call to social gathering and unity) to Noah (peace be upon him), who is the most ancient of the prophets possessing a law (شريعة) and a Book, then to Abraham, then to Moses, then to Jesus (peace be upon them).

The laws of Noah and Abraham contained a small number of rulings. The most extensive of these four was the law of Moses, followed by the law of Jesus, as reported by the Quran and as is evident in the Gospels. It is said that the law of Moses contains only about six hundred rulings.

Thus, the call to social gathering did not begin as an independent and explicit call except from the direction of Prophethood (النبوة) in the mold of religion, as the Quran explicitly states and as history confirms, as will be discussed.

3 — Islam and Its Concern for Social Gathering: There is no doubt that Islam is the only religion that explicitly founded its structure upon social gathering (الاجتماع). It did not neglect the matter of social gathering in any of its affairs. If you wish for further insight into this, look at the vastness of human actions that the mind is unable to count, and their branching into genera, species, and categories. Then look at how this divine law (الشريعة) has enumerated them, encompassed them, and extended its rulings over them; you will see something wondrous. Then look at how it cast all of that into the mold of social gathering; you will see that it infused the spirit of social gathering (روح الاجتماع) into them to the greatest possible extent.

Then proceed to compare what you have found with other true laws that the Quran cares for — namely the laws of Noah, Abraham, Moses, and Jesus — so that you may witness the proportion and know the status.

As for the laws that the Noble Quran does not care for, such as the religions of idolatry (الوثنية), the Sabians (الصابئة), Manichaeism (المانوية), Dualism (الثنوية), and others, the matter is even more evident and clear.

As for the civilized nations and others, history mentions nothing of their affairs except that they followed what they inherited from the most ancient eras of humanity: the subordination of social gathering to utilization (الاستخدام), and the gathering of individuals under the umbrella of autocratic government and monarchical power. Thus, ethnic, national, and regional social gathering lived under the banner of the king and leadership, guided by the factors of heredity, geography, and others, without any of these nations giving independent concern to its affair or making it a subject of research and action. Even the great nations that held world sovereignty when the sun of religion rose and began its illumination — namely the Roman and Persian Empires — were nothing but Caesarism and Khosrowism. Their nations gathered under the banner of the king and sultanate, and social gathering followed in its growth and development, remaining as long as they remained.

Yes, there exist in what they inherited social discussions in the writings of their philosophers, such as Socrates, Plato, Aristotle, and others. However, these were merely papers and scrolls that did not enter the realm of action; they were mental ideals (مثلا ذهنية) that did not descend to the stage of external reality (العين والخارج). Inherited history is the fairest witness to the truth of what we have mentioned.

The first call to strike the ears of the human species, inviting this species to care for the matter of social gathering by making it an independent subject removed from the corner of neglect and the rule of subordination, was that proclaimed by the Proclaimer of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). He called the people, through the verses of his Lord revealed to him, to the happiness of life and a goodly existence in a state of social gathering. He, the Exalted, said: وَأَنَّ هذا صِراطِي مُسْتَقِيماً فَاتَّبِعُوهُ وَلا تَتَّبِعُوا السُّبُلَ فَتَفَرَّقَ بِكُمْ — "This is My straight path, so follow it, and do not follow other ways, for they will separate you from His way" (6:153). And He said: وَاعْتَصِمُوا بِحَبْلِ اللهِ جَمِيعاً وَلا تَفَرَّقُوا — "Hold fast, all together, to Allah’s cord, and do not be divided" (3:103), until He said: وَلْتَكُنْ مِنْكُمْ أُمَّةٌ يَدْعُونَ إِلَى الْخَيْرِ وَيَأْمُرُونَ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ وَيَنْهَوْنَ عَنِ الْمُنْكَرِ — "There has to be a nation among you summoning to good, bidding what is right, and forbidding what is wrong" (3:104) — referring to preserving society from division and branching — وَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الْمُفْلِحُونَ وَلا تَكُونُوا كَالَّذِينَ تَفَرَّقُوا وَاخْتَلَفُوا مِنْ بَعْدِ ما جاءَهُمُ الْبَيِّناتُ — "it is they who are the felicitous. Do not be like those who became divided [into sects] and differed after manifest signs had come to them" (3:104-105). And He said: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ فَرَّقُوا دِينَهُمْ وَكانُوا شِيَعاً لَسْتَ مِنْهُمْ فِي شَيْءٍ — "Indeed those who split up their religion and became sects, you will not have anything to do with them" (6:159), among other absolute verses calling to the principle of social gathering and unity.

He, the Exalted, said: إِنَّمَا الْمُؤْمِنُونَ إِخْوَةٌ فَأَصْلِحُوا بَيْنَ أَخَوَيْكُمْ — "The faithful are indeed brothers. Therefore, make peace between your brothers" (49:10). And He said: وَلا تَنازَعُوا فَتَفْشَلُوا وَتَذْهَبَ رِيحُكُمْ — "and do not dispute, or you will lose heart and your power will be gone" (8:46). And He said: وَتَعاوَنُوا عَلَى الْبرِّ وَالتَّقْوى — "and help one another in piety and God-consciousness" (5:2). And He said: وَلْتَكُنْ مِنْكُمْ أُمَّةٌ يَدْعُونَ إِلَى الْخَيْرِ وَيَأْمُرُونَ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ وَيَنْهَوْنَ عَنِ الْمُنْكَرِ — "There has to be a nation among you summoning to good, bidding what is right, and forbidding what is wrong" (3:104), among other verses commanding the building of Islamic society upon agreement and unity in acquiring its spiritual and material benefits and advantages, and defending it, as we shall clarify to some extent.

4 — Islam's Consideration of the Bond between the Individual and Society: Creation and origination (الصنع والإيجاد) first produce primary parts with specific effects and properties, then combine and harmonize them despite their points of distinction. From this, new benefits are derived in addition to the observable benefits of the parts. For example, man has parts, portions, limbs, and faculties that have diverse material and spiritual benefits. Sometimes they harmonize, becoming strong and great, such as the weight of each part and the weight of the whole, or the ability to move from one direction to another. Sometimes they do not harmonize and remain in a state of disparity and separation, such as hearing, sight, taste, will, and movement. However, they are all, from the perspective of unity in composition, under the control of the single emergent entity — which is the human being. At that point, benefits arise that do not exist in any of his individual parts; these are numerous benefits of the nature of action, passion (الفعل والانفعال), and spiritual and material advantages. Among his benefits is the occurrence of a wondrous multiplicity within those benefits while maintaining unity. For instance, human matter, such as the sperm (النطفة), when its development is complete, is capable of secreting a portion of matter from itself and nurturing it into another complete human being who performs material and spiritual acts similar to those of his origin and source. Thus, human individuals, despite their multiplicity, are "man," which is one; and their actions are many in number but one in species (نوع). They gather and harmonize like water divided into vessels; they are many waters of one species, possessing many properties of one species. Whenever the waters are gathered in one place, the property is strengthened and the effect becomes great.

In the education of the individuals of this species and their guidance toward their true happiness, Islam has considered this real meaning within them, and there is no escape from considering it. He, the Exalted, said: وَهُوَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ مِنَ الْماءِ بَشَراً فَجَعَلَهُ نَسَباً وَصِهْراً — "It is He who created the human being from water, then He established for him lineage and kinship" (25:54). And He said: يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ إِنَّا خَلَقْناكُمْ مِنْ ذَكَرٍ وَأُنْثى — "O mankind! Indeed, We created you from a male and a female" (49:13). And He said: بَعْضُكُمْ مِنْ بَعْضٍ — "You are all [members] of one another" (3:195).

This real bond (الرابطة الحقيقية) between the person and society inevitably leads to another mode of being (كينونة) in society, according to what individuals provide of their existence, powers, properties, and effects. Thus, there is formed in society a nature similar to that of the individual's existence and the properties of existence, which is clearly observable. Therefore, the Quran has attributed to the nation (الأمة) an existence, a term (أجل), a book, consciousness (شعور), understanding, action, obedience, and disobedience. He said: وَلِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ أَجَلٌ فَإِذا جاءَ أَجَلُهُمْ لا يَسْتَأْخِرُونَ ساعَةً وَلا يَسْتَقْدِمُونَ — "There is a time for every nation: when their time comes, they shall not defer it by a single hour nor shall they advance it" (7:34). And He said: كُلُّ أُمَّةٍ تُدْعى إِلى كِتابِهَا — "Every nation will be summoned to its book" (45:28). And He said: زَيَّنَّا لِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ عَمَلَهُمْ — "We have made every nation’s deeds seem goodly to them" (6:108). And He said: مِنْهُمْ أُمَّةٌ مُقْتَصِدَةٌ — "Among them are a moderate community" (5:66). And He said: أُمَّةٌ قائِمَةٌ يَتْلُونَ آياتِ اللهِ — "an upright community, who recite Allah’s signs" (3:113). And He said: وَهَمَّتْ كُلُّ أُمَّةٍ بِرَسُولِهِمْ لِيَأْخُذُوهُ وَجادَلُوا بِالْباطِلِ لِيُدْحِضُوا بِهِ الْحَقَّ فَأَخَذْتُهُمْ فَكَيْفَ كانَ عِقابِ — "Every nation conspired against its messenger to seize him, and they disputed by means of falsehood to refute the truth therewith; so I seized them, and how was My retribution!" (40:5). And He said: وَلِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ رَسُولٌ فَإِذا جاءَ رَسُولُهُمْ قُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ بِالْقِسْطِ — "There is a messenger for every nation: when their messenger comes, judgment is made between them with justice" (10:47).

From here, we see that the Quran cares for the histories of nations just as it cares for the stories of individuals, or even more so, at a time when histories only recorded the conditions of famous kings and great men. Historians did not engage with the histories of nations and societies until after the revelation of the Quran. Some individuals, such as al-Mas'udi and Ibn Khaldun, engaged with it to some extent, until the final transformation occurred in transmitted history by replacing individuals with nations. The first to establish this, as is said, was the Frenchman Auguste Comte, who died in 1857 AD.

In short, the necessary consequence of this, as previously indicated, is the formation of powerful social forces and properties that overwhelm individual forces and properties when they conflict or contradict. Moreover, sense perception and experience testify to this in both active and passive forces and properties. The resolve and will of a group in a matter — as in cases of mobs and social surges — cannot be stood against by any opposing or contradicting will from any of its individuals or parts. The part has no choice but to follow its whole and proceed as it proceeds, to the point that consciousness and thought are stripped from its individuals and parts. Similarly, general fear and general panic — as in cases of defeat, loss of security, earthquakes, famine, and plague, or lesser things like customary rituals and national fashions — compel the individual to follow and strip him of the power of perception and thought.

This is the criterion for Islam's intense concern for the matter of social gathering — a concern the likes of which we do not find, and shall never find, in any other religion or in the traditions of civilized nations (and perhaps you can hardly believe that). For the cultivation of ethics and instincts in the individual — which is the basis for the existence of society — can hardly succeed when opposing and contradicting ethics and instincts are powerful and dominant in society, except to a negligible degree when measured and estimated.

Thus, it placed its most important rulings and laws, such as the Pilgrimage (الحج), Prayer (الصلاة), Struggle (الجهاد), and Spending (الإنفاق) — in short, religious God-consciousness (التقوي الديني) — upon the foundation of social gathering. It maintained this, in addition to the powers of the Islamic government that preserve the public religious rites and their boundaries, and in addition to the obligation of summoning to good and bidding what is right and forbidding what is wrong, which is general to the entire nation. It made the goal of Islamic society — and no society can do without a common goal — true happiness and proximity and status with Allah. This is an internal sentinel (رقيب باطني) from whom nothing in the human conscience or secret is hidden — let alone what is manifest — even if it is hidden from the group of callers and those who bid what is right and forbid what is wrong. This is what we meant when we said that Islam's concern for social gathering surpasses all other traditions and ways.

5 — Is the Islamic Social Tradition Capable of Implementation and Survival? You might say: If what has been mentioned is true — that Islam's vision in forming a righteous society is of a higher structure and more solid foundation than even the societies formed by truly advanced civilized nations — then why was it only implemented for a short period, and then could not prevent itself from being transformed into Caesarism and Khosrowism, turning into an empire whose actions were more tragic and hideous than what preceded it, unlike Western civilization which sustains its survival?

This is the evidence that their civilization is more advanced and their social tradition more solid and robust. They have placed their social tradition and current laws upon the foundation of the nation's will and the suggestion of natural dispositions and inclinations. Then they considered the will of the majority and their suggestion, due to the habitual impossibility of the entire group agreeing on a single will. The dominance of the majority is a current tradition in nature that is observable; for we find that each of the material causes and natural reasons is effective in the majority of cases, not always. Similarly, among various conflicting factors, only the majority are effective, not all and not the minority. Thus, it is appropriate that the structure of society be built, according to its purpose and the traditions and laws current within it, upon the will of the majority. As for the religious hypothesis, in the present world, it is nothing but a wish that does not exceed the stage of hypothesis and a mental ideal that is not permissible to attain.

Modern civilization has guaranteed, in the countries where it has appeared, the strength of society and its happiness, and the refinement of individuals and their purification from vices — those matters that society does not approve of, such as lying, betrayal, injustice, harshness, and the like.

This which we have presented is the summary of what occurs in the minds of many of our Eastern researchers, especially the educated among our thinkers who reflect on social and psychological matters. However, they approached this research from the wrong direction, and thus the truth of the matter became confused for them. To clarify this, we say:

As for their saying that the Islamic social tradition is not capable of being practiced in the world, unlike the traditions of modern civilization in the context of existing conditions — meaning that present world conditions do not harmonize with the laws legislated in Islam — this is granted, but it yields nothing. For all the traditions current in the human community only came into being after they were not, and appeared at a time when the general existing conditions and circumstances were nothing but contradictory to them and repelling them. They then rose up and contended with the preceding, long-standing, and deep-rooted traditions. Sometimes they were oppressed and defeated in their first uprising, then returned a second and third time until they triumphed, became established, and held sway. Sometimes they perished and became extinct if factors and conditions did not support them afterward. History testifies (1) to this in all traditions, religious and worldly, even in things like democracy and socialism. To the likes of this, His saying, the Exalted, refers: قَدْ خَلَتْ مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ سُنَنٌ فَسِيرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ فَانْظُروا كَيْفَ كانَ عاقِبَةُ الْمُكَذِّبِينَ — "Certain customs have passed before you; so travel through the land and observe how was the fate of the deniers" (3:137), indicating that the tradition accompanied by the denial of Allah's signs does not end in a good or praiseworthy outcome.

Thus, the mere lack of correspondence of a tradition with the present human condition does not reveal its falsehood or corruption. Rather, it is among the natural traditions current in the world for completing the formation of new events following action, passion, and the conflict of various factors.

Islam, like other traditions from the perspective of natural and social observation, is not an exception to this universal rule. Its state in terms of advancement, delay, and seeking support from factors and conditions is the state of all other traditions. The state of Islam today — having become established in the souls of more than four hundred million human individuals and taken root in their hearts — is not weaker than its state in the world during the time of the calls of Noah, Abraham, and Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), when the call of each of them stood by a single person and the world at that time knew nothing but corruption. Then it expanded, took root, lived, and joined one with another, and has not been severed until today.

The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) stood with the call when there was no one to support him that day except a man and a woman. Then they continued to be joined by one after another, and today is a day of great hardship, until the help of Allah came to them. They formed a righteous society whose individuals were dominated by righteousness and God-consciousness. They remained in social righteousness for a period until the trials occurred after the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

This small model, despite its short life and narrow scope, did not delay in expanding in less than half a century over the east and west of the earth. It transformed history in a fundamental way whose significant effects are witnessed to our day and shall continue.

Social and psychological research in theoretical history cannot refrain from admitting that the proximate origin and complete factor for the contemporary transformation witnessed in the world is the emergence and rising of the Islamic tradition. Most researchers from Europe have only neglected to fully investigate its impact on the human community due to religious prejudice or political reasons. How can an expert researcher — if he were to look fairly — call the modern civilized movement a "Christian movement" and consider Jesus (peace be upon him) its leader and standard-bearer, when Jesus explicitly states (1) that he only cares for the matter of the spirit and does not engage in the matter of the body, nor does he interfere in the affairs of the state and politics? Yet here is Islam, calling to social gathering and harmony, and intervening in all affairs of human society and its individuals without exception. Is this overlooking and ignoring on their part anything other than an attempt to extinguish the light of Allah — وَيأْبَى اللهُ إِلَّا أَنْ يُتِمَّ نُورَهُ — "but Allah refuses but to perfect His light" (9:32) — and to dampen its fire in the hearts out of envy and enmity, until it becomes a mere ethnicity with no effect except that of branched lineages?

In short, Islam has proven its suitability for guiding people to their happiness and a goodly life. That which possesses this quality is not called a hypothesis incapable of being applied to human life, nor is it to be despaired of its governance over the affairs of the world one day (given that its objective is the true happiness of man). It has already been mentioned in the commentary on the verse: كانَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً واحِدَةً — "Mankind were but one community" (2:213) — that deep research into the conditions of cosmic existents leads to the conclusion that the human species will reach its goal and attain its desire, which is the perfection of the manifestation of Islam in its reality in the world and its complete governance over the affairs of human society. Allah the Exalted has promised this according to this theory in His Noble Book, saying: فَسَوْفَ يَأْتِي اللهُ بِقَوْمٍ يُحِبُّهُمْ وَيُحِبُّونَهُ أَذِلَّةٍ عَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ أَعِزَّةٍ عَلَى الْكافِرِينَ يُجاهِدُونَ فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ وَلا يَخافُونَ لَوْمَةَ لائِمٍ — "Allah will soon bring a people whom He loves and who love Him, [who will be] humble towards the faithful, dignified before the faithless, waging jihad in the way of Allah, not fearing the blame of any blamer" (5:54). And He said: وَعَدَ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنْكُمْ وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ لَيَسْتَخْلِفَنَّهُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ كَمَا اسْتَخْلَفَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ وَلَيُمَكِّنَنَّ لَهُمْ دِينَهُمُ الَّذِي ارْتَضى لَهُمْ وَلَيُبَدِّلَنَّهُمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ خَوْفِهِمْ أَمْناً يَعْبُدُونَنِي لا يُشْرِكُونَ بِي شَيْئاً — "Allah has promised those of you who have faith and do righteous deeds that He will surely make them successors in the earth, even as He made those who were before them successors, and He will surely establish for them their religion which He has approved for them, and that He will surely replace their fear with security: 'They shall worship Me, not ascribing any partners to Me'" (24:55). And He said: أَنَّ الْأَرْضَ يَرِثُها عِبادِيَ الصَّالِحُونَ — "that My righteous servants shall inherit the earth" (21:105), among other verses.

There is another aspect that these people have overlooked in their research, which is that the sole slogan of Islamic society is the following of truth (الحق) in thought and action, while the slogan of modern civilized society is the following of what the majority sees and desires. These two slogans necessitate a difference in the goal of the formed society. The goal of Islamic society is true intellectual happiness (السعادة الحقيقية العقلية), in the sense that man adopts moderation in the requirements of his faculties, giving the body its desires to the extent that it does not hinder him from knowing Allah through the path of servitude (العبودية); rather, it becomes a preliminary that leads to it. In this lies the happiness of man through the happiness of all his faculties, and it is the great rest (though we do not truly perceive it today due to the disruption of Islamic education among us). Therefore, Islam placed its laws upon the foundation of observing the side of the intellect (العقل), which is naturally disposed to follow the truth. It strictly prohibited what corrupts the sound intellect and placed the guarantee for the implementation of all actions, ethics, and primary knowledge upon the responsibility of society, in addition to what the Islamic government and guardianship (الولاية) maintain in terms of implementing policies, legal punishments (الحدود), and others. This, in any case, does not suit the natural dispositions of the common people and is repelled by this wondrous immersion in whims and desires that we witness from all the affluent and the destitute alike. It strips them of their freedom in pleasure-seeking, amusement, predatory behavior, and aggression, except after intense struggle in spreading the call and extending education, just like all other high matters that man needs a decisive resolve, sufficient training, and constant preservation to adopt.

As for the goal of modern civilized society, it is the enjoyment of matter (المادة). It is clear that this entails a sensory life (حياة إحساسية) that follows what the natural disposition inclines toward, whether it agrees with what is true according to the intellect or not. Rather, the intellect is only followed in what does not contradict its goal and purpose.

Therefore, the laws in their formulation and implementation follow what the whims of the majority of society and their natural inclinations demand. The guarantee of implementation is confined to the articles of law pertaining to actions. As for ethics and primary knowledge, there is no guarantor for their implementation; rather, people are free to adopt them or not, unless they interfere with the law in its course, in which case they are prevented.

The necessary consequence of this is that the society whose state is such becomes accustomed to what agrees with its whims of the vices of lust and anger. Thus, it deems good much of what religion used to deem ugly. It indulges in playing with virtuous ethics and high knowledge, supported by legal freedom.

The consequence of this consequence is that the mode of thought transforms from the intellectual course to the sensory and emotional course. Thus, what might be debauchery and profligacy in the course of the intellect becomes piety in the course of inclinations and sensations, and is called gallantry, cheerfulness, and good character — like most of what occurs in Europe among the youth, between men and chaste women or virgins, between women and dogs, between men and their children and close relatives, and what occurs in celebrations, dance halls, and other things that the tongue of one disciplined by the etiquette of religion shrinks from mentioning.

Sometimes the common practices of the religious path become strange and laughably wondrous to them, and vice versa.
