What is most important here is to turn the reins of the discussion toward another direction: the social nature of Islam in its fundamental doctrines (معارفه الأساسية), after recognizing that it observes the social dimension in everything it calls people to—be it laws of action (devotional, transactional, or political), noble ethics, or primary doctrines.

We see that Islam calls people to the religion of nature (دين الفطرة) with the claim that it is the manifest truth (الحق) about which there is no doubt. The Quranic verses stating this are numerous and need no citation. This is the first step toward harmony and intimacy among different understandings; for understandings, despite their differences and their being bound by the constraints of ethics and instincts, do not differ in the principle that "the truth must be followed."

Furthermore, we see that He excuses those to whom the clear proof (البينة) has not been established and for whom the path has not become clear, even if the argument (الحجة) has reached their ears. The Exalted said: لِيَهْلِكَ مَنْ هَلَكَ عَنْ بَيِّنَةٍ وَيَحْيى مَنْ حَيَّ عَنْ بَيِّنَةٍ "so that he who perishes might perish by a manifest proof, and he who lives might live by a manifest proof" (Al-Anfal: 42). And the Exalted said: إِلَّا الْمُسْتَضْعَفِينَ مِنَ الرِّجالِ وَالنِّساءِ وَالْوِلْدانِ لا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ حِيلَةً وَلا يَهْتَدُونَ سَبِيلاً فَأُولئِكَ عَسَى اللهُ أَنْ يَعْفُوَ عَنْهُمْ وَكانَ اللهُ عَفُوًّا غَفُوراً "except the oppressed among men, women and children, who have neither access to any means nor are guided to any way. Maybe Allah will excuse them, for Allah is all-excusing, all-forgiving" (Al-Nisa: 98-99). Observe the absolute nature of the verse and the placement of His saying: "who have neither access to any means nor are guided to any way." This grants complete freedom to every thinker who finds himself capable of reflection and prepared for research and investigation to reflect upon what pertains to the doctrines of religion and to delve deeply into understanding and contemplating them. Moreover, the Quranic verses are replete with urging and encouragement toward reflection (التفكر), intellection (التعقل), and remembrance (التذكر).

It is well known that the variation of mental and external factors influences the variation of understandings in terms of their conceptualization (تصور), assent (تصديق), attainment, and judgment. This leads to variation in the principles upon which the Islamic society was built, as previously mentioned.

However, the divergence in understanding between two individuals—as dictated by the sciences of psychology (فن معرفة النفس), ethics (فن الأخلاق), and sociology (فن الاجتماع)—is attributable to one of several factors. It may stem from a difference in psychological dispositions (الأخلاق النفسانية) and internal traits, whether they be virtuous or base habits (الملكات). These have a profound impact on human sciences and knowledge due to the varying aptitudes they instill in the mind. The perception and mental judgment of a fair-minded person are not like those of a stubborn, arbitrary individual. Nor is the attainment of knowledge by a balanced and dignified person like that of one who is hasty, partisan, a follower of whims, or a barbarian who follows every caller. Nor is it like the straying soul (الغوي) who knows neither where he intends to go nor what is intended for him. Religious education (التربية الدينية) suffices to address the burden of this divergence, for it is established in a manner that accords with the religious principles of knowledge and science, and it engenders the character traits that suit those principles—namely, the noble virtues (مكارم الأخلاق). Allah the Exalted says: كِتاباً أُنْزِلَ مِنْ بَعْدِ مُوسى مُصَدِّقاً لِما بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ يَهْدِي إِلَى الْحَقِّ وَإِلى طَرِيقٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ "a Book sent down after Moses, confirming what was before it, guiding to the truth and to a straight path" (Al-Ahqaf: 30). And He, the Exalted, says: يَهْدِي بِهِ اللهُ مَنِ اتَّبَعَ رِضْوانَهُ سُبُلَ السَّلامِ وَيُخْرِجُهُمْ مِنَ الظُّلُماتِ إِلَى النُّورِ بِإِذْنِهِ وَيَهْدِيهِمْ إِلى صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ "With it Allah guides those who follow [the course of] His pleasure to the ways of peace, and brings them out from darkness into light by His will, and guides them to a straight path" (Al-Ma'idah: 16). And He, the Exalted, says: وَالَّذِينَ جاهَدُوا فِينا لَنَهْدِيَنَّهُمْ سُبُلَنا وَإِنَّ اللهَ لَمَعَ الْمُحْسِنِينَ "As for those who strive in Us, We shall surely guide them in Our ways, and Allah is indeed with the virtuous" (Al-Ankabut: 69). The applicability of these verses to the subject at hand is evident.

Alternatively, the divergence may be attributed to a difference in actions. An act that opposes the truth—such as sins and various forms of human caprice (التهوسات), including types of misguidance and whisperings (الوساوس)—indoctrinates the human being, especially the simple commoner, with corrupt ideas. it prepares his mind for the creeping in of doubts and the infiltration of false opinions. Consequently, understandings diverge and fail to follow the truth. Islam has also sufficed the burden of this issue: first, by commanding the society to maintain the religious call (الدعوة الدينية) perpetually; second, by charging the society with the duty of enjoining the good and forbidding the evil (الأمر بالمعروف والنهي عن المنكر); and third, by commanding the avoidance of those who harbor deviance and doubts. Allah the Exalted says: وَلْتَكُنْ مِنْكُمْ أُمَّةٌ يَدْعُونَ إِلَى الْخَيْرِ وَيَأْمُرُونَ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ وَيَنْهَوْنَ عَنِ الْمُنْكَرِ "There should be a nation among you summoning to virtue and enjoining what is right and forbidding what is wrong" (Al-Imran: 104). The call to virtue reinforces right belief and settles it in the hearts through instruction and reminder, while enjoining the good and forbidding the evil prevent the emergence of obstacles that hinder the rooting of true beliefs in the souls. Allah the Exalted also says: وَإِذا رَأَيْتَ الَّذِينَ يَخُوضُونَ فِي آياتِنا فَأَعْرِضْ عَنْهُمْ حَتَّى يَخُوضُوا فِي حَدِيثٍ غَيْرِهِ وَإِمَّا يُنْسِيَنَّكَ الشَّيْطانُ فَلا تَقْعُدْ بَعْدَ الذِّكْرى مَعَ الْقَوْمِ الظَّالِمِينَ وَما عَلَى الَّذِينَ يَتَّقُونَ مِنْ حِسابِهِمْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ وَلكِنْ ذِكْرى لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَّقُونَ وَذَرِ الَّذِينَ اتَّخَذُوا دِينَهُمْ لَعِباً وَلَهْواً وَغَرَّتْهُمُ الْحَياةُ الدُّنْيا وَذَكِّرْ بِهِ أَنْ تُبْسَلَ نَفْسٌ بِما كَسَبَتْ "When you see those who gossip about Our signs, avoid them until they engage in some other discourse. And if Satan causes you to forget, then, after remembering, do not sit with the wrongdoing lot. Those who are God-conscious are not accountable for them in any way, but [this is] a reminder so that they may be God-conscious. Leave alone those who take their religion for play and diversion and whom the life of this world has deceived, and remind with it lest any soul should be delivered up [to destruction] for what it has earned" (Al-An'am: 68-70). Allah the Exalted forbids participating in discourse that involves delving into divine knowledge or religious truths through doubt, objection, or mockery—even if by way of implication or allusion. He mentions that such behavior stems from a person's lack of seriousness regarding his knowledge, his resort to jest, play, and diversion, and that its origin is being deceived by the worldly life. Its remedy lies in sound education and reminding one of His Exalted station.

Or the divergence may arise from external factors, such as geographical distance and the failure of religious knowledge to reach people except in a meager or distorted form, or the inadequacy of a person's intellect to correctly conceptualize religious truths—such as excessive cleverness (الجربزة) or dullness (البلادة) rooted in a particular temperament (المزاج). The remedy for this is the universalization of the message and gentleness in invitation and education. These two are among the characteristics of the methodology of propagation (السلوك التبليغي) in Islam. Allah the Exalted says: قُلْ هذِهِ سَبِيلِي أَدْعُوا إِلَى اللهِ عَلى بَصِيرَةٍ أَنَا وَمَنِ اتَّبَعَنِي "Say, ‘This is my way. I summon to Allah with insight, I and he who follows me’" (Yusuf: 108). It is well known that one who possesses insight (البصير) into a matter knows the extent of its impact on hearts and the various ways it affects different recipients and listeners; thus, he does not impart to anyone more than what they can comprehend. The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said, as narrated by both sects: إنا معاشر الأنبياء نكلم الناس على قدر عقولهم "We, the assembly of Prophets, speak to people according to the capacity of their intellects." Allah the Exalted also says: فَلَوْ لا نَفَرَ مِنْ كُلِّ فِرْقَةٍ مِنْهُمْ طائِفَةٌ لِيَتَفَقَّهُوا فِي الدِّينِ وَلِيُنْذِرُوا قَوْمَهُمْ إِذا رَجَعُوا إِلَيْهِمْ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَحْذَرُونَ "Yet why should not a group from every section of them go forth to acquire a deep understanding of the religion and to warn their people when they return to them, so that they may be on their guard?" (At-Tawbah: 122). These are the general means by which the occurrence of divergence in beliefs is prevented, or treated if it occurs.

Furthermore, Islam has established for its society a social constitution (دستور اجتماعي) beyond this, which protects it from the creeping in of divergence that leads to corruption and dissolution. Allah the Exalted says: وَأَنَّ هذا صِراطِي مُسْتَقِيماً فَاتَّبِعُوهُ وَلا تَتَّبِعُوا السُّبُلَ فَتَفَرَّقَ بِكُمْ عَنْ سَبِيلِهِ ذلِكُمْ وَصَّاكُمْ بِهِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَّقُونَ "‘This is My straight path, so follow it, and do not follow [other] ways, for they will separate you from His way.’ This is what He has enjoined upon you so that you may be God-conscious" (Al-An'am: 153). He clarified that their gathering upon the straight path (الصراط المستقيم) — the direct and unerring way to Allah — and their caution against following other ways preserves them from division and maintains their unity and agreement. Then He said: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اتَّقُوا اللهَ حَقَّ تُقاتِهِ وَلا تَمُوتُنَّ إِلَّا وَأَنْتُمْ مُسْلِمُونَ وَاعْتَصِمُوا بِحَبْلِ اللهِ جَمِيعاً وَلا تَفَرَّقُوا "O you who have faith! Be wary of Allah with the wariness due to Him, and do not die except as Muslims. And hold fast to the rope of Allah, all together, and do not be divided" (Al-Imran: 102-103). It has been previously mentioned that the "rope of Allah" (حبل الله) refers to the manifest Quran which explains the realities of religious knowledge, or it refers to both the Quran and the Messenger صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, as appears from the preceding verse: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِنْ تُطِيعُوا فَرِيقاً مِنَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتابَ يَرُدُّوكُمْ بَعْدَ إِيمانِكُمْ كافِرِينَ وَكَيْفَ تَكْفُرُونَ وَأَنْتُمْ تُتْلى عَلَيْكُمْ آياتُ اللهِ وَفِيكُمْ رَسُولُهُ وَمَنْ يَعْتَصِمْ بِاللهِ فَقَدْ هُدِيَ إِلى صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ "O you who have faith! If you obey a group of those who were given the Book, they will turn you into faithless after your faith. And how would you be faithless while the signs of Allah are recited to you and His Apostle is among you? Whoever holds fast to Allah is certainly guided to a straight path" (Al-Imran: 100-101).

The verses indicate the necessity of gathering upon the knowledge of religion, linking their thoughts together (يرابطوا أفكارهم), and blending in the process of teaching and learning. Thus, in every intellectual event or presented doubt, they should find repose in the verses recited to them and in contemplating them to sever the root of divergence. Allah the Exalted says: أَفَلا يَتَدَبَّرُونَ الْقُرْآنَ وَلَوْ كانَ مِنْ عِنْدِ غَيْرِ اللهِ لَوجَدُوا فِيهِ اخْتِلافاً كَثِيراً "Do they not contemplate the Quran? Had it been from [someone] other than Allah, they would surely have found much discrepancy in it" (An-Nisa: 82). And He says: وَتِلْكَ الْأَمْثالُ نَضْرِبُها لِلنَّاسِ وَما يَعْقِلُها إِلَّا الْعالِمُونَ "We draw these parables for mankind; but no one grasps them except those who possess knowledge" (Al-Ankabut: 43). And He says: فَسْئَلُوا أَهْلَ الذِّكْرِ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ لا تَعْلَمُونَ "Ask the People of the Reminder if you do not know" (An-Nahl: 43). Thus, He informed that contemplating the Quran or referring to those who contemplate it removes divergence from among them.

The verses also indicate that referring back to the Messenger—who carries the weight of the religion—removes divergence from among them and clarifies for them the truth they must follow. Allah the Exalted says: وَأَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكَ الذِّكْرَ لِتُبَيِّنَ لِلنَّاسِ ما نُزِّلَ إِلَيْهِمْ وَلَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ "We have sent down the Reminder to you so that you may clarify for the people what has been sent down to them, and so that they may reflect" (An-Nahl: 44). Similar to this is His saying: وَلَوْ رَدُّوهُ إِلَى الرَّسُولِ وَإِلى أُولِي الْأَمْرِ مِنْهُمْ لَعَلِمَهُ الَّذِينَ يَسْتَنْبِطُونَهُ مِنْهُمْ "and had they referred it to the Apostle or to those in authority among them, those of them who are alert to such matters would have known it" (An-Nisa: 83). And His saying: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَطِيعُوا اللهَ وَأطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ وَأُولِي الْأَمْرِ مِنْكُمْ فَإِنْ تَنازَعْتُمْ فِي شَيْءٍ فَرُدُّوهُ إِلَى اللهِ وَالرَّسُولِ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ تُؤْمِنُونَ بِاللهِ وَالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ ذلِكَ خَيْرٌ وَأَحْسَنُ تَأْوِيلاً "O you who have faith! Obey Allah and obey the Apostle and those vested with authority among you. And if you dispute concerning anything, refer it to Allah and the Apostle, if you believe in Allah and the Last Day. That is better and more favorable in outcome" (An-Nisa: 59). This, then, is the form of social reflection (التفكر الاجتماعي) in Islam.

From this, it becomes clear that just as this religion relies fundamentally on preserving its specific divine knowledge, it also allows people complete freedom of thought (الحرية في الفكر). The sum of this is that it is obligatory for Muslims to reflect upon the realities of religion and to strive (يجتهدوا) in its knowledge through collective reflection and mutual connection. If a doubt arises regarding any of its truths or knowledge, or if something appearing to contradict them occurs to them, there is no harm in that. Rather, it is incumbent upon the one with the doubt or the opposing view to present what he has to the Book of Allah through contemplation in a social inquiry. If his ailment is not cured, he should present it to the Messenger or the one appointed in his place until his doubt is resolved or the falsehood of what appeared to him is made manifest, if it was indeed false. Allah the Exalted says: الَّذِينَ يَسْتَمِعُونَ الْقَوْلَ فَيَتَّبِعُونَ أَحْسَنَهُ أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ هَداهُمُ اللهُ وَأُولئِكَ هُمْ أُولُوا الْأَلْبابِ "who listen to the word and follow the best [interpretation] of it. They are the ones whom Allah has guided, and it is they who possess intellect" (Az-Zumar: 18).

Freedom of belief and thought in the manner we have explained is distinct from calling to such a view and disseminating it among the people before presenting it [to the authorities of knowledge], for that leads to the divergence that corrupts the foundation of a sound society.

This is the best possible way to manage the affairs of society in opening the door to intellectual advancement (الارتقاء الفكري) in its proper manner, while simultaneously preserving its personal life. As for imposing beliefs upon souls, sealing hearts, and forcibly or coercively extinguishing the instinct of thought (غريزة الفكرة) in man—resorting to the whip, the sword, excommunication (التكفير), social boycott (الهجرة), or the cessation of social interaction—the sanctuary of Truth and the upright religion (الدين القويم) are far above consenting to such things or legislating what supports them. Rather, this is a Christian characteristic; the history of the Church is filled with such actions and arbitrary impositions—especially between the fifth and sixteenth centuries CE—the likes of which cannot be found even among the most heinous and cruel deeds committed by the hands of tyrants and despots (الطواغيت).

However, it is regrettable that we, the community of Muslims, have been stripped of this blessing and its necessary concomitants—namely, intellectual sociality (الاجتماع الفكري) and freedom of belief (حرية العقيدة)—just as we have been stripped of many of the great blessings Allah, the Glorified, had bestowed upon us when we were negligent regarding the side of Allah. إِنَّ اللهَ لا يُغَيِّرُ ما بِقَوْمٍ حَتَّى يُغَيِّرُوا ما بِأَنْفُسِهِمْ "Indeed Allah does not change a people’s lot, unless they change what is in their souls" (13:11). Consequently, the conduct of the Church came to rule over us, leading to the division of hearts, the appearance of lethargy, and the fragmentation of schools of thought (المذاهب) and paths. May Allah forgive us, grant us success in attaining His pleasure, and guide us to His straight path (الصراط المستقيم).

15 – The True Religion is the Victor over the World in the End: The ultimate outcome belongs to godwariness (التقوى), for the human species, by the primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — deposited within it, seeks its true happiness. This happiness consists of man ascending the throne of his spiritual and physical life together, in a social life, by giving his soul its share of both worldly and otherworldly conduct. You have already learned that this is Islam and the religion of monotheism (التوحيد).

As for the deviations occurring in the journey of humanity toward its goal and its ascent to the pinnacle of its perfection, they arise from errors in application, not from the invalidity of the judgment of the primordial nature (الفطرة). The goal that follows creation and origination (الإيجاد) must inevitably be realized one day, whether sooner or later. The Exalted says: فَأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ حَنِيفاً فِطْرَتَ اللهِ الَّتِي فَطَرَ النَّاسَ عَلَيْها لا تَبْدِيلَ لِخَلْقِ اللهِ ذلِكَ الدِّينُ الْقَيِّمُ وَلكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لا يَعْلَمُونَ "So set your face on the religion as a Hanif, with the nature of Allah which He inaugurated for mankind. There is no altering Allah’s creation; that is the upright religion, but most people do not know" (30:30). (He means that they do not know this with detailed knowledge, even if their primordial nature knows it in a general sense). Until He says: لِيَكْفُرُوا بِما آتَيْناهُمْ فَتَمَتَّعُوا فَسَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ "Let them be ungrateful for what We have given them! So enjoy yourselves; soon you will know!" (30:34). Until He says: ظَهَرَ الْفَسادُ فِي الْبَرِّ وَالْبَحْرِ بِما كَسَبَتْ أَيْدِي النَّاسِ لِيُذِيقَهُمْ بَعْضَ الَّذِي عَمِلُوا لَعَلَّهُمْ يرجعون "Corruption has appeared in land and sea because of what the hands of men have wrought, that He may make them taste something of what they have done, so that they may return" (30:41). And the Exalted says: فَسَوْفَ يَأْتِي اللهُ بِقَوْمٍ يُحِبُّهُمْ وَيُحِبُّونَهُ أَذِلَّةٍ عَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ أَعِزَّةٍ عَلَى الْكافِرِينَ يُجاهِدُونَ فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ وَلا يَخافُونَ لَوْمَةَ لائِمٍ "Allah will soon bring a people whom He loves and who love Him, [who will be] humble towards the faithful, mighty against the faithless, waging jihad in the way of Allah, not fearing the blame of any blamer" (5:54). And the Exalted says: وَلَقَدْ كَتَبْنا فِي الزَّبُورِ مِنْ بَعْدِ الذِّكْرِ أَنَّ الْأَرْضَ يَرِثُها عِبادِيَ الصَّالِحُونَ "Certainly We wrote in the Psalms, after the Torah: ‘Indeed My righteous servants shall inherit the earth’" (21:105). And the Exalted says: وَالْعاقِبَةُ لِلتَّقْوى "and the outcome will be in favour of Godwariness" (20:132). These and similar verses inform us that Islam will eventually manifest in its complete manifestation and rule over the entire world.

Do not listen to the words of one who says: "Though Islam did manifest to some extent and its days were a link in the chain of history—leaving its general mark on subsequent links and serving as a foundation for modern civilization, whether consciously or unconsciously—its complete manifestation, meaning the governance of the religious hypothesis (فرضية الدين) with all its articles, forms, and goals, is something the nature of the human species does not accept and will never accept. Furthermore, no experiment has occurred in this capacity such that one could trust the validity of its external occurrence and its complete governance over the species."

This is because you have learned that Islam, in the sense we are discussing, is the goal of the human species and its perfection toward which it is instinctively directed, whether it perceives this in detail or not. Definitive experiments (التجارب القطعية) occurring in all types of created beings indicate that they are directed toward goals suitable to their existences (وجوداتها), driven toward them by the system of creation (نظام الخلقة); and man is no exception to this universal (الكلية).

Moreover, none of the established traditions (السنن) and paths circulating in the world and current among human societies relied, in their inception, survival, or governance, upon a prior definitive experiment. Here are the laws (شرائع) of Noah, Abraham, Moses, and Jesus; they appeared when they appeared and then proceeded among the people. Likewise, what was brought by Brahma, Buddha, Mani, and others. Similarly, the traditions of material civilization, such as democracy, communism, and others—all of these proceeded in various human societies through their various courses without any prior experiment.

Rather, social traditions (السنن الاجتماعية), in their emergence and entrenchment within society, require decisive resolve (عزائم قاطعة) and high aspirations (همم عالية) from powerful souls that are not overtaken by fatigue or exhaustion in the path of reaching their objectives. Such souls do not concede that time might not permit the desired outcome or that the effort might fail. In this regard, there is no difference between divine goals and objectives and satanic ones.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Ma'ani, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اصْبِرُوا وَصابِرُوا وَرابِطُوا "O you who have faith! Be patient, and stand firm, and close your ranks" (3:200): "Be patient (اصبروا) regarding calamities, stand firm (صابروهم) against trials (الفتنة), and close your ranks (رابطوا) around those whom you follow."

In the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, from him (peace be upon him): "Be patient (اصبروا) upon your religion, stand firm (صابروهم) against your enemy, and close your ranks (رابطوا) around your Imam."

I say: Something similar has been narrated through Sunni channels from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

In al-Kafi, from him (peace be upon him): "Be patient (اصبروا) upon the obligatory duties, stand firm (صابروهم) upon calamities, and close your ranks (رابطوا) around the Imams."

In al-Majma', from Ali (peace be upon him): "Close your ranks (رابطوا) for the prayers"—he said: "Meaning, wait for them, because military stationing (المرابطة) [at the frontiers] did not exist at that time."

I say: The variation in the narrations is based on what was previously mentioned regarding the absolute nature (إطلاق) of the commands.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Jarir and Ibn Hayyan narrated from Jabir ibn Abdillah who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: ألا أدلكم على ما يمحو الله به الخطايا ، ويكفر به الذنوب؟ قلنا بلى يا رسول الله ـ قال : إسباغ الوضوء على المكاره ، وكثرة الخطى إلى المساجد ، وانتظار الصلاة بعد الصلاة فذلكم الرباط. "Shall I not point you toward that by which Allah erases sins and expiates misdeeds?" We said: "Yes, O Messenger of Allah." He said: "Performing the ablution (الوضوء) thoroughly despite hardships, taking many steps toward the mosques, and waiting for one prayer after another; that is the stationing (الرباط)."

I say: It is narrated through other channels from him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and the reports regarding the merit of stationing (المرابطة) are too numerous to count.
